This equation is essential for understanding **porous polymer membranes**, **moisture transport in polymers**, and **wicking in polymer composites**. ## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula Name | Equation | Primary Application | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | Young's Equation | $\gamma_{SV} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta$ | Contact angle measurement | | 2 | Work of Cohesion | $W_c = 2\gamma_{LV}$ | Droplet formation | | 3 | Work of Adhesion | $W_a = \gamma_{SV} + \gamma_{LV} - \gamma_{SL}$ | Adhesion strength | | 4 | Young–Dupré | $W_a = \gamma_{LV}(1 + \cos\theta)$ | Wettability from $\theta$ | | 5 | Spreading Coefficient | $S = W_a - W_c = \gamma_{SV} - \gamma_{SL} - \gamma_{LV}$ | Coating / film formation | | 6 | Young–Laplace (Sphere) | $\Delta P = \dfrac{2\gamma_{LV}}{R}$ | Droplet pressure | | 7 | Young–Laplace (General) | $\Delta P = \gamma_{LV}\left(\dfrac{1}{R_1} + \dfrac{1}{R_2}\right)$ | Non-spherical interfaces | | 8 | Jurin's Law (Capillary Rise) | $h = \dfrac{2\gamma_{LV}\cos\theta}{\rho g r}$ | Porous media transport | | 9 | Critical Surface Tension (Zisman) | $\cos\theta = 1 - \dfrac{\gamma_c}{\gamma_{LV}}$ (approx.) | Polymer surface characterization | | 10 | Fowkes Equation (Dispersion) | $\gamma_{SL} = \gamma_{SV} + \gamma_{LV} - 2\sqrt{\gamma_{SV}^d \gamma_{LV}^d}$ | Polar/dispersive components | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Note on Notation:** All $\gamma$ values are in **N/m** (equivalently J/m²). All radii are in **meters (m)**. Pressures are in **Pascals (Pa)**. Heights in **meters (m)**. Density in **kg/m³**. Keep SI units strictly to avoid KTU valuation errors. ## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Membrane Separation (Reverse Osmosis / Ultrafiltration):** Capillary pressure (Young–Laplace) determines the **liquid entry pressure** (LEP) of hydrophobic membranes — critical for membrane distillation. - **Polymer Coating & Adhesives:** The spreading coefficient governs whether a paint or adhesive wets the substrate. - **Biomedical Polymers:** Contact angle $\theta < 60°$ indicates a **hydrophilic** surface, promoting cell adhesion in tissue engineering scaffolds. - **Textile & Packaging:** Surface energy dictates printability, dye uptake, and barrier properties. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Young–Laplace Equation (Sphere) We derive the pressure difference across a spherical liquid interface by considering a reversible expansion of a spherical droplet. **Step 1: Initial State** Consider a spherical droplet of radius $R$ at pressure $P_{in}$ surrounded by vapor at pressure $P_{out}$. The pressure difference is: $$\Delta P = P_{in} - P_{out}$$ **Step 2: Infinitesimal Expansion** Expand the droplet radius by an infinitesimal amount $dR$. The new radius is $R + dR$, and the change in volume is: $$dV = \frac{4}{3}\pi(R + dR)^3 - \frac{4}{3}\pi R^3$$ **Step 3: Binomial Expansion** Applying the binomial expansion $(R + dR)^3 = R^3 + 3R^2 dR + 3R (dR)^2 + (dR)^3$ and neglecting second-order and higher terms (since $dR$ is infinitesimal): $$dV = \frac{4}{3}\pi \left( R^3 + 3R^2 dR - R^3 \right) = 4\pi R^2 \, dR$$ **Step 4: New Surface Area** Similarly, the new surface area is: $$dA = 4\pi(R + dR)^2 - 4\pi R^2 = 8\pi R \, dR$$ **Step 5: Work Done BY the System** The system does work against the pressure difference. The work done **by the system** on expansion is $\Delta P \cdot dV$: $$W_{expansion} = \Delta P \cdot 4\pi R^2 \, dR$$ **Step 6: Work Done AGAINST Surface Tension** Creating new surface area $dA$ requires work equal to $\gamma_{LV} \cdot dA$ (this is the definition of surface free energy): $$W_{surface} = \gamma_{LV} \cdot 8\pi R \, dR$$ **Step 7: Energy Balance** At equilibrium (reversible process), the work done by the pressure difference must equal the work required to create new surface: $$\Delta P \cdot 4\pi R^2 \, dR = \gamma_{LV} \cdot 8\pi R \, dR$$ **Step 8: Final Simplification** Canceling $4\pi R \, dR$ from both sides: $$\Delta P \cdot R = 2\gamma_{LV}$$ $$\boxed{\Delta P = \frac{2\gamma_{LV}}{R}}$$ This is the **Young–Laplace equation for a spherical interface**. --- ## 3.2 Derivation of Capillary Rise (Jurin's Law) **Step 1: Identify the Force Balance** A liquid column of height $h$ rises in a capillary of radius $r$ due to the upward pull of surface tension around the circumference of the meniscus. **Step 2: Upward Force (Surface Tension)** Surface tension acts along the perimeter $2\pi r$ at angle $\theta$ to the vertical. The vertical component is: $$F_{up} = 2\pi r \, \gamma_{LV} \cos\theta$$ **Step 3: Downward Force (Gravity)** The weight of the liquid column of height $h$ is: $$F_{down} = \rho \, g \, \pi r^2 \, h$$ **Step 4: Equilibrium Condition** At equilibrium, $F_{up} = F_{down}$: $$2\pi r \, \gamma_{LV} \cos\theta = \rho \, g \, \pi r^2 \, h$$ **Step 5: Solve for h** Cancel $\pi r$ from both sides: $$2\gamma_{LV} \cos\theta = \rho \, g \, r \, h$$ $$\boxed{h = \frac{2\gamma_{LV} \cos\theta}{\rho g r}}$$ --- ## 3.3 Analytical Worked Example (KTU Exam Style) **Problem:** A polymer membrane has a cylindrical pore of radius $r = 1.0 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}$. Water (with $\gamma_{LV} = 0.072 \text{ N/m}$, $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\theta = 60°$) is in contact with the membrane. Calculate: (a) The capillary rise height $h$. (b) The pressure difference $\Delta P$ across the meniscus. Take $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$. ### Part (a) — Capillary Rise **Given:** $r = 1.0 \times 10^{-6}$ m, $\gamma_{LV} = 0.072$ N/m, $\theta = 60°$, $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s². $$\cos(60°) = 0.5$$ $$h = \frac{2 \times 0.072 \times 0.5}{1000 \times 9.81 \times 1.0 \times 10^{-6}}$$ $$h = \frac{0.072}{9.81 \times 10^{-3}}$$ $$h = \frac{0.072}{0.00981} = 7.339 \text{ m}$$ **Result:** $h \approx 7.34$ m. ### Part (b) — Pressure Difference (Young–Laplace) For a cylindrical meniscus, one radius of curvature is $R_1 = r$ and $R_2 \to \infty$: $$\Delta P = \frac{\gamma_{LV}}{r} = \frac{0.072}{1.0 \times 10^{-6}} = 7.2 \times 10^4 \text{ Pa} = 72 \text{ kPa}$$ **Result:** $\Delta P = 72$ kPa. > [!IMPORTANT] > This 72 kPa value is the **minimum transmembrane pressure** required to force water through a hydrophobic pore. In membrane distillation, this is called the **Liquid Entry Pressure (LEP)**. Below this pressure, water cannot enter the dry hydrophobic pore. --- ## 3.4 Worked Example — Work of Adhesion from Contact Angle **Problem:** A water droplet on a polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE / Teflon) surface shows a contact angle of $\theta = 110°$. The surface tension of water is $\gamma_{LV} = 0.072$ N/m. Calculate the work of adhesion. **Solution:** Using the Young–Dupré equation: $$W_a = \gamma_{LV}(1 + \cos\theta)$$ $$\cos(110°) = -0.342$$ $$W_a = 0.072 \times (1 - 0.342) = 0.072 \times 0.658$$ $$W_a = 0.0474 \text{ J/m}^2 = 47.4 \text{ mJ/m}^2$$ **Interpretation:** Since $W_a < W_c = 2\gamma_{LV} = 0.144$ J/m², the work of cohesion dominates, confirming PTFE is **non-wettable** — exactly why Teflon is used as a non-stick coating. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Surface Property Decision Tree The following flowchart maps the **conceptual decision logic** that a chemical engineer follows when analyzing a polymer–liquid interface. ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Polymer Surface + Liquid Encountered"]:::startNode B["Measure Contact Angle theta"]:::processNode C{"theta less than 90 deg"}:::decisionNode D{"theta greater than 90 deg"}:::decisionNode E["theta approaches 0 deg"]:::decisionNode F["Hydrophilic Surface
Wetting occurs
F_a greater than F_c"]:::resultNode G["Hydrophobic Surface
Beading occurs
F_c greater than F_a"]:::resultNode H["Complete Wetting
Spreading Coefficient S greater than 0"]:::resultNode I["Apply Young Laplace
Compute Capillary Pressure"]:::processNode J["Membrane Pore Transport
Liquid Entry Pressure Calculation"]:::finalNode K["Coating Adhesion
Young Dupre Equation"]:::finalNode A --> B B --> C B --> D B --> E C -->|Yes| F C -->|No| I D -->|Yes| G D -->|No| K E -->|Yes| H F --> J G --> I H --> K I --> J classDef startNode fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e40af,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef processNode fill:#0891b2,stroke:#0e7490,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef decisionNode fill:#d97706,stroke:#b45309,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef resultNode fill:#16a34a,stroke:#15803d,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef finalNode fill:#7c3aed,stroke:#6d28d9,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — The Three-Phase Contact Line Architecture The following diagram visualizes the **force-vector architecture** at the three-phase contact line, which is the physical origin of Young's equation. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph SolidPhase["Solid Substrate Region"] S1["gamma_SV
Solid Vapor Tension"]:::solidNode S2["gamma_SL
Solid Liquid Tension"]:::solidNode end subgraph LiquidPhase["Liquid Droplet Region"] L1["gamma_LV
Liquid Vapor Tension"]:::liquidNode Theta["theta
Contact Angle
Measured Through Liquid"]:::angleNode end subgraph VaporPhase["Vapor Phase Region"] V1["Surrounding Gas / Air"]:::vaporNode end ContactLine["Three Phase Contact Line
Equilibrium Point"]:::contactNode S1 -->|Pulls Tangentially| ContactLine S2 -->|Pulls Along Interface| ContactLine L1 -->|Pulls With Angle theta| ContactLine V1 -.->|Ambient Pressure| ContactLine ContactLine -->|Force Balance| Theta classDef solidNode fill:#374151,stroke:#1f2937,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef liquidNode fill:#2563eb,stroke:#1e3a8a,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef vaporNode fill:#9ca3af,stroke:#6b7280,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef angleNode fill:#dc2626,stroke:#991b1b,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff classDef contactNode fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#b45309,stroke-width:3px,color:#000000 ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Sequence — Capillary Rise Process ```mermaid flowchart TD P1["Step 1: Capillary touches liquid surface"]:::step1 P2["Step 2: Wetting forms concave meniscus
theta less than 90 deg"]:::step2 P3["Step 3: Surface tension pulls liquid upward
Vertical force = 2 pi r gamma cos theta"]:::step3 P4["Step 4: Liquid column rises
Hydrostatic pressure increases"]:::step4 P5["Step 5: Equilibrium reached
Force balance gives Jurin Law"]:::step5 P6["h = 2 gamma cos theta / rho g r"]:::result1 P1 --> P2 P2 --> P3 P3 --> P4 P4 --> P5 P5 --> P6 classDef step1 fill:#0369a1,stroke:#075985,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef step2 fill:#0e7490,stroke:#155e75,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef step3 fill:#0891b2,stroke:#164e63,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef step4 fill:#0d9488,stroke:#115e59,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef step5 fill:#14b8a6,stroke:#0f766e,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef result1 fill:#facc15,stroke:#ca8a04,stroke-width:3px,color:#000000 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define surface tension and surface free energy. Are they numerically equal? Justify. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Surface Tension ($\gamma$):** It is defined as the force per unit length acting on an imaginary line drawn on the liquid surface, the force being perpendicular to the line and tangent to the surface. Units: **N/m**. $$\gamma = \frac{F}{L}$$ **Surface Free Energy ($G^s$):** It is the reversible work required to create a unit area of new surface at constant temperature and pressure. Units: **J/m²**. $$G^s = \frac{W_{reversible}}{A} = \frac{dW}{dA}$$ **Numerical Equivalence:** Since $1 \text{ N} = 1 \text{ J/m}$, it follows that $1 \text{ N/m} = 1 \text{ J/m}^2$. Therefore, surface tension and surface free energy are dimensionally and numerically equivalent, though they arise from different conceptual frameworks (mechanical vs. thermodynamic). > **Mark Distribution:** Definition of $\gamma$: 1 mark; Definition of $G^s$: 1 mark; Justification: 1 mark. --- ### Question 2: Differentiate between cohesive and adhesive forces with a relevant polymer engineering example. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Aspect | Cohesive Forces ($F_c$) | Adhesive Forces ($F_a$) | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Definition | Attractive forces between **like** molecules | Attractive forces between **unlike** molecules | | Effect | Hold the bulk material together | Hold two different materials together | | Determines | Surface tension of a single phase | Wetting, contact angle, bond strength | **Polymer Engineering Example:** In a polymer–metal adhesive joint (e.g., epoxy bonded to aluminum), the **adhesive forces** between epoxy and aluminum must exceed the **cohesive forces** within the epoxy for the joint to fail *cohesively* (within the epoxy) rather than *adhesively* (at the interface). Engineers design surface treatments to enhance $F_a$ and ensure cohesive failure for maximum joint strength. > **Mark Distribution:** Definitions (1 each = 2 marks); Polymer example (1 mark). --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) ### Question A: Comprehensive Problem on Wetting and Capillarity **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** **(a)** Define the contact angle. Derive **Young's equation** for a liquid droplet resting on a solid surface, clearly defining each term. Explain the physical significance of each interfacial tension. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A polymer membrane contains cylindrical pores of radius $1.5 \times 10^{-6}$ m. Calculate: (i) The capillary rise of water ($\gamma_{LV} = 0.072$ N/m, $\theta = 70°$, $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³) in a single pore. (ii) The liquid entry pressure required to push water through the pore. (iii) The work of adhesion between water and the polymer if $\theta = 70°$. Take $g = 9.81$ m/s². **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution for Question A: **(a) Derivation of Young's Equation (7 Marks):** **Definition (1 Mark):** The **contact angle ($\theta$)** is the angle formed at the three-phase contact line, measured through the liquid, between the solid surface and the tangent to the liquid–vapor interface. **Three Interfacial Tensions (2 Marks):** - $\gamma_{SV}$: Solid–vapor interfacial tension (force per unit length at the solid–air interface) - $\gamma_{SL}$: Solid–liquid interfacial tension (force per unit length at the solid–liquid interface) - $\gamma_{LV}$: Liquid–vapor interfacial tension (force per unit length at the liquid–air interface) **Force Balance Derivation (4 Marks):** At the three-phase contact line, three forces act tangentially along their respective interfaces. Projecting all forces onto the horizontal plane and applying equilibrium ($\sum F_x = 0$): $$\gamma_{SV} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV} \cos\theta$$ **Physical Significance:** $\gamma_{SV}$ pulls the contact line outward (along the solid). $\gamma_{SL}$ pulls it inward (along the solid–liquid interface). The horizontal component of $\gamma_{LV}$, which is $\gamma_{LV} \cos\theta$, pulls the contact line along the liquid surface. The vertical component $\gamma_{LV} \sin\theta$ is balanced by the rigidity of the solid. --- **(b) Numerical Solutions (7 Marks):** **(i) Capillary Rise (3 Marks):** Using Jurin's Law: $$h = \frac{2\gamma_{LV} \cos\theta}{\rho g r}$$ $$\cos(70°) = 0.342$$ $$h = \frac{2 \times 0.072 \times 0.342}{1000 \times 9.81 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-6}}$$ $$h = \frac{0.04925}{0.014715} = 3.347 \text{ m}$$ **Answer: $h \approx 3.35$ m** (3 marks: 1 formula, 1 substitution, 1 result) **(ii) Liquid Entry Pressure (2 Marks):** For a cylindrical pore, the Young–Laplace equation reduces to: $$\Delta P_{LEP} = \frac{2\gamma_{LV} \cos\theta}{r} = \frac{2 \times 0.072 \times 0.342}{1.5 \times 10^{-6}}$$ $$\Delta P_{LEP} = \frac{0.04925}{1.5 \times 10^{-6}} = 3.28 \times 10^4 \text{ Pa} \approx 32.8 \text{ kPa}$$ **Answer: $\Delta P_{LEP} \approx 32.8$ kPa** (2 marks: 1 formula, 1 calculation) **(iii) Work of Adhesion (2 Marks):** Using the Young–Dupré equation: $$W_a = \gamma_{LV}(1 + \cos\theta) = 0.072 \times (1 + 0.342) = 0.072 \times 1.342$$ **Answer: $W_a = 0.0966 \text{ J/m}^2$** (2 marks: 1 formula, 1 result) --- ### Question B: Alternative Comprehensive Problem on Young–Laplace and Spreading **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** **(a)** State and derive the **Young–Laplace equation** for the pressure difference across a spherical liquid interface. Explain its significance in membrane separation processes. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A polymer coating is applied to a metal substrate. The following interfacial tensions are measured: $\gamma_{SV} = 45$ mN/m, $\gamma_{SL} = 15$ mN/m, $\gamma_{LV} = 30$ mN/m. Calculate: (i) The contact angle $\theta$ of the coating on the substrate. (ii) The spreading coefficient $S$. Will the coating spread completely? (iii) The work of adhesion and work of cohesion. Compare and comment. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution for Question B: **(a) Derivation of Young–Laplace Equation (7 Marks):** **Statement (1 Mark):** The pressure difference across a curved liquid interface is directly proportional to the surface tension and inversely proportional to the radius of curvature. **Derivation (4 Marks):** As detailed in Section 3.1, considering the reversible expansion of a spherical droplet and equating the work done by the pressure difference to the work required to create new surface: $$\Delta P \cdot 4\pi R^2 \, dR = \gamma_{LV} \cdot 8\pi R \, dR$$ $$\boxed{\Delta P = \frac{2\gamma_{LV}}{R}}$$ **Significance in Membrane Separation (2 Marks):** This equation defines the **Liquid Entry Pressure (LEP)** of hydrophobic membranes used in **membrane distillation** and **membrane contactors**. The smaller the pore radius, the higher the pressure required for liquid penetration. It also governs capillary condensation in porous polymer adsorbents. --- **(b) Numerical Solutions (7 Marks):** **(i) Contact Angle (2 Marks):** Using Young's equation: $$\cos\theta = \frac{\gamma_{SV} - \gamma_{SL}}{\gamma_{LV}} = \frac{45 - 15}{30} = 1.0$$ Since $\cos\theta = 1.0$ exactly: $$\theta = 0°$$ **Answer: $\theta = 0°$ (complete spreading)** (2 marks: 1 substitution, 1 result) **(ii) Spreading Coefficient (2 Marks):** $$S = \gamma_{SV} - \gamma_{SL} - \gamma_{LV} = 45 - 15 - 30 = 0 \text{ mN/m}$$ **Answer: $S = 0$ mN/m — borderline case, marginal spreading** (2 marks: 1 formula, 1 result) > **Critical Insight:** $S = 0$ represents a **limiting equilibrium** state. Theoretically, a thin film can form but is unstable to perturbations. Practical coatings in this regime are sensitive to surface roughness and contamination. **(iii) Work of Adhesion and Cohesion (3 Marks):** $$W_a = \gamma_{SV} + \gamma_{LV} - \gamma_{SL} = 45 + 30 - 15 = 60 \text{ mN/m}$$ $$W_c = 2\gamma_{LV} = 2 \times 30 = 60 \text{ mN/m}$$ **Comparison:** $W_a = W_c$ (both equal 60 mN/m). Therefore, the **spreading coefficient** $S = W_a - W_c = 0$, confirming the borderline behavior. The coating will wet the surface, but spreading will be slow and may not form a uniform film. > **Mark Distribution:** Each part: 1 formula + 1 substitution/result. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in Surface Properties Problems:** > > 1. **Unit Inconsistency Trap:** Mixing cm and m in capillary rise problems. **Always convert all dimensions to SI (meters) before calculation.** A typical error: using $r$ in cm but expecting $h$ in m. > > 2. **Radian vs. Degree Trap:** Trigonometric functions in calculators default to radians. **Always ensure your calculator is in DEGREE mode** for $\theta$ values like 60°, 70°, 110°. > > 3. **Forgetting the "2" in Young–Laplace:** Students often write $\Delta P = \gamma/R$ for a sphere, forgetting the factor of 2. The factor of 2 comes from the two principal radii being equal. > > 4. **Confusing $W_a$ and $W_c$:** Work of adhesion uses two **different** phases; work of cohesion uses one phase split into two surfaces (hence the factor of 2). > > 5. **Sign of Spreading Coefficient:** A **negative** $S$ does NOT mean "no wetting" — it means *partial* wetting (a droplet forms with finite $\theta$). Only $S < 0$ with $\theta > 0$ confirms non-spreading. > > 6. **Young's Equation Validity:** Young's equation is strictly valid for **ideal, smooth, rigid, homogeneous surfaces**. On rough surfaces, the **Wenzel** or **Cassie–Baxter** models apply, and $\theta$ becomes an *apparent* contact angle. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ### Rapid Revision Checklist - [x] **Surface tension = Surface free energy** numerically; both have units N/m or J/m². - [x] **Contact angle $\theta < 90°$** → Wetting (hydrophilic); **$\theta > 90°$** → Non-wetting (hydrophobic). - [x] **Young's equation:** $\gamma_{SV} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV} \cos\theta$ — the *master* equation of surface science. - [x] **Young–Dupré:** $W_a = \gamma_{LV}(1 + \cos\theta)$ — links contact angle to adhesion energy. - [x] **Young–Laplace (sphere):** $\Delta P = 2\gamma_{LV}/R$ — pressure difference across curved interface. - [x] **Capillary rise (Jurin):** $h = 2\gamma_{LV}\cos\theta / (\rho g r)$ — fundamental to porous membrane transport. - [x] **Spreading coefficient:** $S = \gamma_{SV} - \gamma_{SL} - \gamma_{LV}$. If $S \geq 0$ → complete spreading. - [x] **Work of cohesion:** $W_c = 2\gamma_{LV}$ (splitting one phase into two new surfaces). - [x] **Work of adhesion:** $W_a = \gamma_{SV} + \gamma_{LV} - \gamma_{SL}$ (separating two unlike phases). - [x] **Polymer relevance:** Contact angle determines printability, biocompatibility, membrane selectivity, and adhesion strength. - [x] **Engineering applications:** Membrane distillation (LEP), polymer coatings, biomedical implants, textile finishing, adsorption. - [x] **Units must always be SI:** $\gamma$ in N/m, $R$ in m, $P$ in Pa, $h$ in m. ### Key Polymer Examples to Remember for Viva/Exam | Polymer | Typical $\theta$ (Water) | Surface Character | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | PTFE (Teflon) | $\approx 110°$ | Strongly hydrophobic | | Polyethylene (PE) | $\approx 94°$ | Hydrophobic | | Polystyrene (PS) | $\approx 87°$ | Near-neutral | | Poly(methyl methacrylate) (PMMA) | $\approx 70°$ | Moderately hydrophilic | | Poly(vinyl alcohol) (PVA) | $\approx 30°$ | Strongly hydrophilic | > **End of Topic Notes — Surface Properties (Module 1, PEPOT752, KTU 2024 Scheme)**

topics.title: Ionic Properties and Other Special Characteristics of Substances
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT752 - POLYMERS AND SEPARATION PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Ionic Properties and Other Special Characteristics of Substances

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What Are Ionic Properties?

In the context of **Polymers and Separation Processes**, ionic properties refer to the characteristic physicochemical behaviours exhibited by substances that dissociate into positively charged cations and negatively charged anions when dissolved in a polar solvent (most commonly water). These properties govern how charged species migrate, interact, adsorb, and are selectively captured by polymeric membranes, ion-exchange resins, and electrodialysis stacks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PEPOT752 Module 1):**
> *"Ionic properties include ionisation, dissociation, ionic conductivity, ionic strength, ion hydration, ion-dipole interactions, and the activity coefficients that dictate the behaviour of charged species in solution. These properties collectively determine the efficiency and selectivity of separation processes such as ion exchange, electrodialysis, reverse osmosis, and nanofiltration."*

The substances exhibiting these properties are called **electrolytes** and they are broadly classified as:

| Type | Dissociation Behaviour | Conductivity | Examples |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Strong Electrolyte** | ~100 % dissociated in solution | High (constant over concentration range) | NaCl, KCl, HCl, HNO₃ |
| **Weak Electrolyte** | Partially dissociated (equilibrium governed) | Low (rises with dilution) | CH₃COOH, NH₄OH, H₂CO₃ |
| **Non-Electrolyte** | Does not ionise | Negligible | Glucose, urea, sucrose |

## 1.2 Other Special Characteristics

Beyond ionic behaviour, substances possess special molecular characteristics that critically affect their separability:

- **Polarity** of molecules (dipole moment, $\mu$)
- **Hydrogen bonding capacity** (donor/acceptor sites)
- **Hydrophobic / Hydrophilic balance**
- **Molecular weight and size distribution**
- **Acid-base character (Bronsted / Lewis)**
- **Surface charge and zeta potential**

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (The Traffic Signal Analogy):**
> Imagine a busy four-way intersection. Cars = molecules; charged cars = ions. The traffic signal = the electric field applied across an ion-exchange membrane or during electrodialysis. Just as a signal guides cars selectively, the charge on an ion dictates its direction and speed through the separation medium. A **heavy, highly hydrated cation (like Mg²⁺)** moves slowly like a loaded truck, while a **small, lightly hydrated ion (like H⁺)** zips through quickly via the *Grotthuss proton-hopping* mechanism — exactly the kind of selectivity exploited in real separation units.

## 1.3 Physical Constants at a Glance

- **Faraday's Constant:** $F = 96{,}485 \text{ C mol}^{-1}$
- **Avogadro's Number:** $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23} \text{ mol}^{-1}$
- **Universal Gas Constant:** $R = 8.314 \text{ J mol}^{-1}\text{K}^{-1}$
- **Boltzmann Constant:** $k_B = 1.381 \times 10^{-23} \text{ J K}^{-1}$
- **Permittivity of Free Space:** $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F m}^{-1}$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Degree of Dissociation ($\alpha$)

The **degree of dissociation** is the fraction of dissolved electrolyte molecules that exist as free ions at equilibrium.

For a weak electrolyte $AB \rightleftharpoons A^+ + B^-$:

$$\alpha = \dfrac{\text{Number of moles dissociated}}{\text{Initial number of moles}}$$

The **Ostwald Dilution Law** relates $\alpha$, the dissociation constant $K_a$, and the molar concentration $C$:

$$K_a = \dfrac{C\alpha^2}{1 - \alpha}$$

For very weak electrolytes where $\alpha \ll 1$, this simplifies to:

$$\alpha \approx \sqrt{\dfrac{K_a}{C}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** As the solution is diluted ($C$ decreases), $\alpha$ increases. This is why weak electrolytes become more efficient conductors in dilute streams — a critical consideration when designing **demineralisation units** in water treatment plants.

## 2.2 Ionic Strength ($I$)

The **ionic strength** accounts for the total concentration of ions weighted by the square of their charges:

$$I = \dfrac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^{n} c_i z_i^2$$

where $c_i$ is the molar concentration and $z_i$ is the charge of ion $i$. For a simple binary electrolyte like NaCl at concentration $C$:

$$I_{\text{NaCl}} = \dfrac{1}{2}\left[C(+1)^2 + C(-1)^2\right] = C$$

For CaCl₂ at concentration $C$:

$$I_{\text{CaCl}_2} = \dfrac{1}{2}\left[C(+2)^2 + 2C(-1)^2\right] = 3C$$

## 2.3 Mean Ionic Activity Coefficient ($\gamma_{\pm}$)

The **Debye-Hückel Limiting Law** (valid for $I \leq 10^{-3}$ M):

$$\log_{10}\gamma_{\pm} = -A \, z_+ \, z_- \, \sqrt{I}$$

The **Extended Debye-Hückel Equation** (valid for $I \leq 0.1$ M):

$$\log_{10}\gamma_{\pm} = \dfrac{-A \, z_+ \, z_- \sqrt{I}}{1 + B a \sqrt{I}}$$

where:
- $A \approx 0.509$ (mol/kg)$^{-1/2}$ for water at 25 °C
- $B \approx 0.328$ Å$^{-1}$(mol/kg)$^{-1/2}$ for water at 25 °C
- $a$ = effective ion size parameter in Å

## 2.4 Specific, Equivalent, and Molar Conductivity

| Quantity | Symbol | Definition | Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Specific (Conductivity) | $\kappa$ | $\kappa = \dfrac{1}{\rho} = \dfrac{L}{A \cdot R}$ | S cm$^{-1}$ |
| Molar Conductivity | $\Lambda_m$ | $\Lambda_m = \dfrac{\kappa}{C}$ | S cm² mol$^{-1}$ |
| Equivalent Conductivity | $\Lambda_{eq}$ | $\Lambda_{eq} = \dfrac{\kappa}{N} = \dfrac{\Lambda_m}{z}$ | S cm² eq$^{-1}$ |

where $N$ is normality, $C$ is molarity, $L$ is cell length, $A$ is cross-sectional area, and $R$ is measured resistance.

**Kohlrausch's Law** (for strong electrolytes at infinite dilution):

$$\Lambda_m = \Lambda_m^0 - K\sqrt{C}$$

where $\Lambda_m^0$ is the limiting molar conductivity and $K$ is a constant.

**Kohlrausch's Law of Independent Migration of Ions** (additivity at infinite dilution):

$$\Lambda_m^0 = \nu_+ \lambda_+^0 + \nu_- \lambda_-^0$$

## 2.5 Ionic Hydration Energy & Hydration Number

The **enthalpy of hydration** ($\Delta H_{\text{hyd}}$) is the energy released when gaseous ions are surrounded by water molecules. Born equation approximation:

$$\Delta H_{\text{hyd}} = -\dfrac{N_A z^2 e^2}{8\pi \varepsilon_0 r_i}\left(1 - \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_r}\right)$$

The **hydration number** ($n_h$) is the average number of water molecules tightly bound to an ion — strongly influencing **effective ionic radius** and hence membrane permeability.

## 2.6 Ion Exchange Capacity & Selectivity

For **ion-exchange resins**, two key parameters are:

$$\text{Capacity} = \dfrac{\text{mass of ions exchanged}}{\text{mass of dry resin}} \quad (\text{units: meq/g})$$

The **Selectivity Coefficient** for the exchange $A^{z_A} + z_B \cdot \overline{B} \rightleftharpoons \overline{A} + z_A \cdot B^{z_B}$:

$$K_B^A = \dfrac{[\overline{A}]^{z_B}[B]^{z_A}}{[A]^{z_B}[\overline{B}]^{z_A}}$$

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Table — Memorise for KTU ESE**

| Concept | Governing Equation | Validity / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Degree of dissociation | $\alpha = \sqrt{K_a/C}$ (for $\alpha \ll 1$) | Weak electrolytes |
| Ionic strength | $I = \frac{1}{2}\sum c_i z_i^2$ | All electrolyte solutions |
| Debye-Hückel (limiting) | $\log\gamma_{\pm} = -Az_+z_-\sqrt{I}$ | $I \leq 10^{-3}$ M |
| Extended Debye-Hückel | $\log\gamma_{\pm} = \dfrac{-Az_+z_-\sqrt{I}}{1+Ba\sqrt{I}}$ | $I \leq 0.1$ M |
| Kohlrausch's Law | $\Lambda_m = \Lambda_m^0 - K\sqrt{C}$ | Strong electrolytes |
| Independent ion migration | $\Lambda_m^0 = \nu_+\lambda_+^0 + \nu_-\lambda_-^0$ | Infinite dilution |
| Born hydration enthalpy | $\Delta H_{\text{hyd}} = -\dfrac{N_A z^2 e^2}{8\pi\varepsilon_0 r_i}\left(1-\frac{1}{\varepsilon_r}\right)$ | Single ion |
| Selectivity coefficient | $K_B^A = \dfrac{[\overline{A}]^{z_B}[B]^{z_A}}{[A]^{z_B}[\overline{B}]^{z_A}}$ | Ion-exchange resins |
| Faraday's Law of Electrolysis | $m = \dfrac{MIt}{zF}$ | Electrodialysis design |
| Nernst Equation | $E = E^0 - \dfrac{RT}{zF}\ln Q$ | Membrane potential |

**Real-world utility:** Every entry above directly drives the design of an industrial separation unit — from **demineralisers in power plants** (ionic strength/activity) to **electrodialysis for desalination** (Faraday's Law) to **mixed-bed polishers in pharma** (selectivity).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Ostwald Dilution Law

**Starting Point:** Consider a binary weak electrolyte $AB$ of initial molar concentration $C$ that dissociates as:

$$AB \rightleftharpoons A^+ + B^-$$

**Step 1 — Set up the equilibrium concentrations:**

At equilibrium, the degree of dissociation is $\alpha$. Therefore:

$$[AB] = C(1 - \alpha), \quad [A^+] = C\alpha, \quad [B^-] = C\alpha$$

**Step 2 — Write the thermodynamic equilibrium constant:**

$$K_a = \dfrac{[A^+][B^-]}{[AB]} = \dfrac{(C\alpha)(C\alpha)}{C(1-\alpha)}$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the expression:**

$$K_a = \dfrac{C\alpha^2}{1-\alpha}$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange into a quadratic in $\alpha$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
K_a(1-\alpha) &= C\alpha^2 \\
K_a - K_a\alpha &= C\alpha^2 \\
C\alpha^2 + K_a\alpha - K_a &= 0
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Solve the quadratic using the quadratic formula:**

$$\alpha = \dfrac{-K_a + \sqrt{K_a^2 + 4CK_a}}{2C}$$

(we take the positive root because $\alpha \geq 0$)

**Step 6 — Apply the approximation $\alpha \ll 1$ for very weak electrolytes:**

When $K_a \ll C$, then $\alpha \ll 1$, so $1 - \alpha \approx 1$:

$$\begin{aligned}
K_a &\approx C\alpha^2 \\
\alpha^2 &\approx \dfrac{K_a}{C} \\
\alpha &\approx \sqrt{\dfrac{K_a}{C}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** Doubling the dilution (halving $C$) only increases $\alpha$ by a factor of $\sqrt{2}$. This non-linear behaviour is critical in designing **two-stage weak-acid cation exchange** systems.

## 3.2 Derivation of Ionic Strength for a Mixed Electrolyte

**Given:** A solution contains $0.10$ M NaCl, $0.05$ M K₂SO₄, and $0.02$ M FeCl₃.

**Step 1 — Identify all ionic species and their concentrations:**

| Ion | Formula | $c_i$ (M) | $z_i$ | $c_i z_i^2$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Sodium | Na$^+$ | 0.10 | +1 | 0.10 |
| Potassium | K$^+$ | 0.10 | 0.05 $\times$ 2 = 0.10 | +1 |
| Sulphate | SO₄$^{2-}$ | 0.05 | -2 | 0.05 $\times$ 4 = 0.20 |
| Iron(III) | Fe$^{3+}$ | 0.02 | +3 | 0.02 $\times$ 9 = 0.18 |
| Chloride | Cl$^-$ | 0.10 + 0.06 = 0.16 | -1 | 0.16 |

**Step 2 — Apply the ionic strength formula:**

$$I = \dfrac{1}{2}\sum c_i z_i^2$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$I = \dfrac{1}{2}\left(0.10 + 0.10 + 0.20 + 0.18 + 0.16\right)$$

**Step 4 — Compute the sum inside the parentheses:**

$$0.10 + 0.10 + 0.20 + 0.18 + 0.16 = 0.74$$

**Step 5 — Final result:**

$$I = \dfrac{0.74}{2} = 0.37 \text{ M}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A high $I$ value (0.37 M) tells us this is a concentrated brackish stream. The Debye-Hückel Limiting Law will **fail** here, so the engineer must use the Pitzer or Davies equation for accurate activity prediction in any downstream ion-exchange design.

## 3.3 Derivation of Mean Ionic Activity Coefficient from Chemical Potential

**Step 1 — Define chemical potential of an ion in solution:**

$$\mu_i = \mu_i^0 + RT \ln a_i = \mu_i^0 + RT \ln (\gamma_i m_i)$$

where $m_i$ is molality and $\gamma_i$ is the activity coefficient.

**Step 2 — The mean ionic activity coefficient for electrolyte $A_{\nu_+}B_{\nu_-}$:**

$$\gamma_{\pm} = (\gamma_+^{\nu_+} \gamma_-^{\nu_-})^{1/(\nu_+ + \nu_-)}$$

**Step 3 — Mean ionic molality:**

$$m_{\pm} = (m_+^{\nu_+} m_-^{\nu_-})^{1/(\nu_+ + \nu_-)}$$

**Step 4 — Combined mean activity:**

$$a_{\pm} = \gamma_{\pm} m_{\pm}$$

**Step 5 — The mean activity of the electrolyte itself:**

$$a_{AB} = (a_{\pm})^{\nu_+ + \nu_-} = (\gamma_{\pm} m_{\pm})^{\nu_+ + \nu_-}$$

**Step 6 — Substituting back into chemical potential:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\mu_{AB} &= (\nu_+ + \nu_-)\mu_{\pm} \\
\mu_{AB} &= (\nu_+ + \nu_-)\left[\mu_{\pm}^0 + RT \ln(\gamma_{\pm} m_{\pm})\right]
\end{aligned}$$

This is the theoretical foundation for treating non-ideal electrolyte solutions in **electrodialysis and reverse osmosis** simulations.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical: Mean Activity Coefficient

**Problem:** Estimate $\gamma_{\pm}$ for a 0.005 M NaCl solution at 25 °C using (a) the Debye-Hückel Limiting Law and (b) the Extended Debye-Hückel Equation with $a = 3.97$ Å.

**Solution — Step 1: Compute ionic strength.**

$$I = \dfrac{1}{2}\left[(0.005)(+1)^2 + (0.005)(-1)^2\right] = 0.005 \text{ M}$$

**Solution — Step 2: Apply limiting law.**

$$\log_{10}\gamma_{\pm} = -(0.509)(+1)(-1)\sqrt{0.005}$$

$$\sqrt{0.005} = 0.07071$$

$$\log_{10}\gamma_{\pm} = +0.509 \times 0.07071 = 0.03599$$

$$\gamma_{\pm} = 10^{0.03599} = 1.0862$$

Wait — this is greater than 1, which is unphysical. **Correction:** The limiting law strictly applies for $I < 10^{-3}$ M; at $I = 0.005$ M, only the extended form is reliable.

**Solution — Step 3: Apply extended Debye-Hückel.**

$$Ba = (0.328)(3.97) = 1.302 \text{ (mol/kg)}^{-1/2}$$

$$1 + Ba\sqrt{I} = 1 + (1.302)(0.07071) = 1.0921$$

$$\log_{10}\gamma_{\pm} = -\dfrac{(0.509)(1)(0.07071)}{1.0921} = -\dfrac{0.03599}{1.0921} = -0.03296$$

$$\gamma_{\pm} = 10^{-0.03296} = 0.9265$$

**Final Answer:** $\gamma_{\pm}^{\text{extended}} \approx 0.927$ (physically reasonable, $< 1$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This correction is a KTU favourite trap.** Students blindly apply the limiting law and get $\gamma > 1$. Always check whether $I$ lies in the valid range before choosing the formula.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Block Diagram — Ionic Interactions Influencing Separation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Solute in Bulk Solution] --> B{Ionisation State}
    B -->|Dissociates| C[Free Ions in Solvation Shell]
    B -->|Remains Molecular| D[Neutral Species]
    C --> E[Ionic Strength I]
    C --> F[Hydration Shell Size]
    C --> G[Charge Density z_r]
    D --> H[Polarity / Dipole Moment]
    D --> I[Hydrogen Bonding Capacity]
    E --> J[Activity Coefficient gamma_pm]
    F --> K[Effective Ionic Radius]
    G --> K
    J --> L[Driving Force for Transport]
    K --> L
    H --> L
    I --> L
    L --> M{Separation Process Selection}
    M -->|Charge-driven| N[Ion Exchange Resin]
    M -->|Electric field| O[Electrodialysis]
    M -->|Size + charge| P[Nanofiltration]
    M -->|Polarity| Q[Reverse Osmosis]
    M -->|Affinity| R[Adsorption on Polymer]
    N --> S[Purified Stream]
    O --> S
    P --> S
    Q --> S
    R --> S
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Stages of an Ion-Exchange Separation Unit

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Raw[Raw Feed Water with Ions] --> PreT[Pre-Treatment Filtration]
    PreT --> CatCol[Strong Acid Cation Exchanger H+ form]
    CatCol --> CO2Strip[Decarbonator Removes CO2]
    CO2Strip --> AnCol[Strong Base Anion Exchanger OH- form]
    AnCol --> MBed[Mixed Bed Polisher]
    MBed --> Pure[Deionised Water Outlet]
    CatCol -.Regeneration HCl.-> Reg1[Acid Regenerant Tank]
    AnCol -.Regeneration NaOH.-> Reg2[Base Regenerant Tank]
    MBed -.Regeneration.-> Reg3[Polisher Regen Unit]
```

## 4.3 Architecture — How Ion Properties Map onto Polymer Membrane Behaviour

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph ION[ION CHARACTERISTICS]
        A1[Charge Number z]
        A2[Hydrated Radius r_h]
        A3[Polarizability alpha_p]
        A1 --> B1
        A2 --> B1
        A3 --> B1
    end
    B1[Effective Transport through Membrane] --> C1{Dominant Mechanism}
    C1 -->|Donnan exclusion| D1[Co-ion rejection]
    C1 -->|Size sieving| D2[Neutral rejection]
    C1 -->|Sorption diffusion| D3[Organic rejection]
    C1 -->|Electrostatic repulsion| D4[Multivalent rejection]
    D1 --> E1[Selectivity]
    D2 --> E1
    D3 --> E1
    D4 --> E1
    E1 --> F1[Separation Performance]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading these diagrams:** The first flowchart shows how raw solute properties cascade through solution chemistry into process selection. The second models the classical two-bed + mixed-bed **demineralisation train** universally used in KTU-relevant industries (power, pharma, semiconductors). The third links microscopic ion descriptors to macroscopic polymer-membrane selectivity.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]
**Define the term "degree of dissociation" of an electrolyte. How does it vary with dilution for a weak electrolyte?**

**Model Answer:**
The **degree of dissociation ($\alpha$)** is defined as the ratio of the number of molecules of electrolyte that dissociate into ions at equilibrium to the total number of molecules initially dissolved.

$$\alpha = \dfrac{\text{Moles dissociated}}{\text{Moles initially dissolved}}$$

For a weak electrolyte, by the Ostwald Dilution Law:

$$\alpha = \sqrt{\dfrac{K_a}{C}}$$

As the solution is diluted ($C$ decreases), $\alpha$ increases. In the limit of infinite dilution, $\alpha \to 1$. This trend arises because dilution shifts the dissociation equilibrium forward to counter the reduction in ion concentration (Le Chatelier's Principle). *(3 marks — definition 1, equation 1, dilution trend 1)*

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]
**State Kohlrausch's Law of independent migration of ions. Why is the equivalent conductivity of a strong electrolyte lower at higher concentrations?**

**Model Answer:**
**Kohlrausch's Law of Independent Migration of Ions** states that at infinite dilution, the limiting molar conductivity of an electrolyte is the sum of the individual limiting ionic conductivities of its constituent cations and anions:

$$\Lambda_m^0 = \nu_+ \lambda_+^0 + \nu_- \lambda_-^0$$

Equivalent conductivity of a strong electrolyte decreases with concentration because of **interionic effects**: as concentration rises, oppositely charged ions form temporary *ionic atmospheres* that drag along, retarding the motion of each ion (the **electrophoretic effect**) and creating a relaxation asymmetry (**asymmetry / relaxation effect**). Both effects reduce ionic mobility and hence $\Lambda_{eq}$. *(3 marks — statement 1, formula 1, physical reason 1)*

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper]

**(a)** Derive the **Ostwald Dilution Law** for a weak binary electrolyte $AB$ dissociating as $AB \rightleftharpoons A^+ + B^-$. Show the approximation that leads to $\alpha \approx \sqrt{K_a/C}$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Calculate the ionic strength and the mean ionic activity coefficient (using the Extended Debye-Hückel equation, $a = 4$ Å, $T = 298$ K) for a solution containing 0.01 M CaCl₂ and 0.02 M Na₂SO₄. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a):

**Step 1 — Equilibrium concentrations:**

$$[AB] = C(1-\alpha), \quad [A^+] = C\alpha, \quad [B^-] = C\alpha$$

**Step 2 — Equilibrium constant expression:**

$$K_a = \dfrac{[A^+][B^-]}{[AB]} = \dfrac{C\alpha^2}{1-\alpha}$$

**Step 3 — Cross multiply:**

$$K_a(1-\alpha) = C\alpha^2$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange as quadratic in $\alpha$:**

$$C\alpha^2 + K_a\alpha - K_a = 0$$

**Step 5 — Solve (positive root):**

$$\alpha = \dfrac{-K_a + \sqrt{K_a^2 + 4CK_a}}{2C}$$

**Step 6 — Approximation for weak electrolytes ($\alpha \ll 1$, so $1-\alpha \approx 1$):**

$$K_a \approx C\alpha^2 \Rightarrow \alpha \approx \sqrt{\dfrac{K_a}{C}}$$

Hence proved. *(7 marks — equilibrium 2, derivation 3, approximation 2)*

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b):

**Step 1 — Identify ions and their molar concentrations:**

| Ion | $c_i$ (M) | $z_i$ | $c_i z_i^2$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Ca$^{2+}$ | 0.01 | +2 | 0.04 |
| Na$^+$ | 0.04 | +1 | 0.04 |
| Cl$^-$ | 0.02 | -1 | 0.02 |
| SO₄$^{2-}$ | 0.02 | -2 | 0.08 |

**Step 2 — Compute ionic strength:**

$$I = \dfrac{1}{2}(0.04 + 0.04 + 0.02 + 0.08) = \dfrac{0.18}{2} = 0.09 \text{ M}$$

**Step 3 — Check validity of Extended DH:** $I = 0.09 \leq 0.1$ M, so valid.

**Step 4 — Compute denominator:**

$$Ba = (0.328)(4) = 1.312$$

$$\sqrt{I} = \sqrt{0.09} = 0.30$$

$$1 + Ba\sqrt{I} = 1 + (1.312)(0.30) = 1 + 0.3936 = 1.3936$$

**Step 5 — Compute $\log\gamma_{\pm}$:**

$$\log_{10}\gamma_{\pm} = -\dfrac{(0.509)(2)(1)(0.30)}{1.3936} = -\dfrac{0.3054}{1.3936} = -0.2192$$

**Step 6 — Final answer:**

$$\gamma_{\pm} = 10^{-0.2192} = 0.603$$

The mean ionic activity coefficient is approximately **0.603**, indicating significant deviation from ideality. *(7 marks — ions 2, ionic strength 2, calculation 2, interpretation 1)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**

**(a)** Explain **Debye-Hückel Theory** of interionic attraction. Discuss its limitations and state the Davies equation as an improvement. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An ion-exchange resin in H$^+$ form is used to remove Na$^+$ from a 0.05 M NaCl solution. The selectivity coefficient $K_{Na}^H = 1.5$. If the total capacity of the resin is 4.0 meq/g, calculate the mass of resin needed to treat 5 L of feed solution and determine the equivalent fraction of H$^+$ remaining on the resin after the first loading cycle. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a):

**Debye-Hückel Theory (1923):**

1. **Central premise:** In an electrolyte solution, any ion is surrounded by a statistically averaged *ionic atmosphere* of oppositely charged ions.

2. **Mathematical outcome:** The electrostatic potential energy of the central ion due to its atmosphere lowers its chemical potential, leading to $\gamma_{\pm} < 1$.

3. **Limiting Law (for very dilute solutions, $I \leq 10^{-3}$ M):**

$$\log_{10}\gamma_{\pm} = -A \, z_+ \, z_- \, \sqrt{I}$$

**Limitations:**

- Fails above $I = 10^{-3}$ M (predicts $\gamma > 1$ for higher I).
- Assumes ions are point charges; ignores finite size.
- Assumes solvent is a continuum (ignores specific ion-solvent interactions).
- Does not account for ion association / ion-pairing.

**Davies Equation (improved, valid up to $I \approx 0.5$ M):**

$$\log_{10}\gamma_{\pm} = -A z_+ z_-\left[\dfrac{\sqrt{I}}{1+\sqrt{I}} - 0.3 I\right]$$

The empirical $-0.3 I$ term extends validity into the moderately concentrated regime. *(7 marks — theory 2, equations 2, limitations 2, Davies 1)*

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b):

**Step 1 — Moles of Na$^+$ in feed:**

$$n_{Na^+} = 0.05 \text{ mol/L} \times 5 \text{ L} = 0.25 \text{ mol} = 250 \text{ mmol}$$

In meq: $250 \text{ mmol} \times 1 = 250 \text{ meq}$ (since $z = +1$).

**Step 2 — Mass of resin required if fully loaded:**

$$m_{\text{resin}} = \dfrac{250 \text{ meq}}{4.0 \text{ meq/g}} = 62.5 \text{ g}$$

**Step 3 — At equilibrium, selectivity governs distribution:**

For exchange $H^+ + Na^+(\text{sol}) \rightleftharpoons H^+(\text{sol}) + Na^+(\text{res})$:

$$K_{Na}^H = \dfrac{[\overline{Na}^+][H^+]}{[Na^+][\overline{H}^+]} = 1.5$$

**Step 4 — Set up equivalent fractions:**

Let $x$ = equivalent fraction of Na$^+$ on the resin. Then $(1-x)$ = equivalent fraction of H$^+$ on the resin.

In solution, after exchange (assuming most Na$^+$ removed initially to maintain charge balance): $[\overline{Na}^+]/[Na^+]$ becomes large.

For a rough estimate using the **equivalent-fraction form of the selectivity equation:**

$$K_{Na}^H = \dfrac{x_{Na}/x_H}{y_{Na}/y_H} = \dfrac{x/(1-x)}{(1-x_{Na})/x_{Na,\text{sol}}}$$

Assuming 90 % removal of Na$^+$ from solution initially ($y_{Na} \approx 0.1$):

$$1.5 = \dfrac{x/(1-x)}{0.1/0.9} = \dfrac{x}{1-x} \times 9$$

$$\dfrac{x}{1-x} = \dfrac{1.5}{9} = 0.1667$$

$$x = 0.1667(1-x) \Rightarrow x = 0.1667 - 0.1667x$$

$$1.1667x = 0.1667 \Rightarrow x = 0.143$$

**Step 5 — Equivalent fraction of H$^+$ on resin:**

$$x_H = 1 - 0.143 = 0.857$$

**Final Answer:** Resin mass = **62.5 g** (for full stoichiometric loading); H$^+$ remaining on resin after cycle ≈ **85.7 %** equivalent fraction. *(7 marks — moles 2, mass 1, K-equation 2, calculation 2)*

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit errors** in ionic strength (must be mol/kg or M, but consistent with $A$, $B$ constants).
> 2. **Forgetting the factor ½** in $I = \frac{1}{2}\sum c_i z_i^2$.
> 3. **Applying the limiting Debye-Hückel Law beyond $10^{-3}$ M** — examiners instantly deduct 2 marks for unphysical $\gamma > 1$.
> 4. **Confusing molar and equivalent conductivity** — remember $\Lambda_{eq} = \Lambda_m/z$.
> 5. **Not checking the validity range** of Kohlrausch's Law.
> 6. **Skipping units in numerical answers** — guaranteed ½ mark deduction per subpart.
> 7. **Mixing up Born equation sign conventions** — $\Delta H_{\text{hyd}}$ is negative (exothermic).

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Electrolytes** are classified as strong, weak, or non-electrolytes based on $\alpha$ in solution.
- **Ostwald Dilution Law:** $K_a = C\alpha^2/(1-\alpha)$; for $\alpha \ll 1$, $\alpha \approx \sqrt{K_a/C}$.
- **Ionic strength** weights ions by the **square of charge**, so multivalent ions dominate.
- **Debye-Hückel Limiting Law** → valid only for $I \leq 10^{-3}$ M; **Extended DH** → $I \leq 0.1$ M; **Davies** → up to 0.5 M.
- **Kohlrausch's Law:** $\Lambda_m = \Lambda_m^0 - K\sqrt{C}$; at infinite dilution $\Lambda_m^0 = \nu_+ \lambda_+^0 + \nu_- \lambda_-^0$.
- **Equivalent conductivity** decreases with concentration for strong electrolytes due to **electrophoretic + relaxation effects**.
- **Hydration enthalpy** (Born equation) depends on $z^2/r_i$; small, highly charged ions hydrate strongly.
- **Ion-exchange selectivity** depends on charge, size, polarisability, and hydration energy.
- **Faraday's Constant** $F = 96{,}485$ C mol$^{-1}$ is essential for electrodialysis calculations.
- **Selectivity coefficient** $K_B^A > 1$ means resin prefers A; $K_B^A < 1$ means resin prefers B.
- Always **state validity range** when applying an empirical equation — examiners reward this awareness.
- **Engineering link:** Every concept above directly drives the design of ion-exchange columns, electrodialysis stacks, reverse osmosis plants, and nanofiltration membrane modules used across KTU-relevant Kerala industries (Cochin Shipyard water treatment, FACT effluent plants, Brahmapuram waste management).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rate Based Versus Equilibrium Separation Processes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT752 - POLYMERS AND SEPARATION PROCESSES > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Rate Based Versus Equilibrium Separation Processes # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition ### Equilibrium-Based Separation Processes An **equilibrium-stage separation process** is a separation technique whose design and ultimate performance are governed by **thermodynamic phase equilibrium** between the contacting phases. The exiting streams from each ideal stage are assumed to be in complete thermodynamic equilibrium with each other, meaning no further net inter-phase mass transfer would occur given additional contact time. The number of ideal (theoretical) stages required is calculated using material balances combined with equilibrium relationships (e.g., VLE, LLE, SLE). ### Rate-Based Separation Processes A **rate-based (rate-governed) separation process** is a separation technique whose design and performance are governed by the **rate of mass, momentum, or energy transport** across a non-equilibrium interface, membrane, or barrier. Thermodynamic equilibrium is never truly attained within the equipment because the rate of transport through the separating medium (membrane pore, sorbent particle, barrier film) is the rate-limiting step. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Distinguish clearly between the *thermodynamic limit* (set by equilibrium) and the *kinetic rate* (set by transport phenomena). Equilibrium processes are *thermodynamically limited*, while rate-based processes are *kinetically controlled*. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition **The Classroom Analogy (Equilibrium Process):** Imagine two adjacent classrooms with a wide-open connecting door. If you keep the door open for a very long time, students will eventually distribute themselves (in terms of noise levels, energy) equally between the two rooms — they reach an *equilibrium* state. You don't care *how fast* they crossed the door; you only care about the *final distribution*. This is like **distillation or absorption** — eventually vapor and liquid reach equilibrium. **The Toll-Booth Analogy (Rate-Based Process):** Now imagine the same two rooms, but connected by a *narrow turnstile* that only lets one student through per second. No matter how long you wait, the *final distribution* will be limited by how many students the turnstile can process per second — the **rate** dictates the outcome. This is like a **membrane separation** or **adsorption** — the membrane/sorbent acts as a rate-limiting barrier. > [!NOTE] > **Memory Trick:** > - **E**quilibrium → "**E**nd state matters" → distillation, extraction, absorption > - **R**ate → "**R**ace matters" → membrane, chromatography, ion exchange --- ## 1.3 Standard Physical Quantities & Metrics | Quantity | Symbol | Standard Unit | |----------|--------|---------------| | Mass transfer coefficient | $k_c$ | m/s | | Permeability | $P$ | mol·m / (m²·s·Pa) | | Diffusivity | $D_{AB}$ | m²/s | | Stage efficiency (Murphree) | $E_M$ | dimensionless | | Overall mass transfer resistance | $1/K$ | s/m | | Henry's constant | $H$ | Pa·m³/mol | > [!NOTE] > Equilibrium processes are characterized by **dimensionless equilibrium ratios** ($K$-value, relative volatility $\alpha$, distribution coefficient $m$), while rate-based processes are characterized by **dimensional transport coefficients** ($k_c$, $P$, $D_{AB}$). # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown ### A. Equilibrium Separation Processes — How They Work 1. **Two phases are brought into intimate contact** (vapor-liquid, liquid-liquid, solid-liquid, or vapor-solid). 2. **Mass transfer occurs spontaneously** until thermodynamic equilibrium is reached. 3. The **thermodynamic equilibrium condition** is described by: - Raoult's Law: $y_i P = x_i P_i^{sat}$ - Henry's Law: $y_i P = H_i x_i$ - Distribution Law: $K_D = C_1 / C_2$ (liquid-liquid) 4. **Stages are counted** (theoretical stages via McCabe-Thiele, Ponchon-Savarit, etc.) to achieve the desired separation. 5. **Real performance** is converted from ideal stages using stage efficiency (Murphree $E_M$, Overall $\eta_O$). **Examples:** Distillation, Absorption, Stripping, Liquid-Liquid Extraction, Crystallization, Leaching, Drying, Flash Vaporization. ### B. Rate-Based Separation Processes — How They Work 1. A **selective barrier or medium** (membrane, sorbent, electrical field, thermal gradient) is interposed. 2. **Mass transport occurs at a finite rate** governed by a driving force (concentration, pressure, electrical potential, temperature). 3. The **rate of mass transfer** is described by: - Fick's Law: $J = -D_{AB} \dfrac{dC}{dx}$ - Permeation: $J = \dfrac{P}{l}(p_1 - p_2)$ - Convective: $N_A = k_c (C_A^* - C_A)$ 4. The **equipment is sized** to provide the residence time / area needed for the desired flux. 5. The **separation factor** depends on the relative rates, not on true equilibrium. **Examples:** Membrane separation (RO, NF, UF, MF, Gas Permeation, Pervaporation), Adsorption (fixed bed, simulated moving bed), Ion Exchange, Chromatography, Electrodialysis, Electrophoresis, Foam Separation, Field-Flow Fractionation. ### C. The Crucial "Why" and "How" - **Why equilibrium works for distillation:** Vapor and liquid can be brought into contact very rapidly, and the time to reach vapor-liquid equilibrium is short compared to residence time. Therefore, the *rate* of mass transfer is fast enough to assume equilibrium at each stage. - **Why rate-based is needed for membranes:** In a membrane, the *transport through the polymer matrix or pores* is slow and rate-limiting. The upstream and downstream sides may never come to true equilibrium within the device. So the **flux equation** (not an equilibrium equation) is what determines equipment size. --- ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | **#** | **Process Type** | **Governing Equation** | **Key Variable** | **Units** | |-------|------------------|------------------------|------------------|-----------| | 1 | VLE (Raoult) | $y_i = \dfrac{x_i P_i^{sat}}{P}$ | $P_i^{sat}$ (saturation pressure) | Pa | | 2 | VLE (Henry) | $y_i = \dfrac{H_i x_i}{P}$ | $H_i$ (Henry's constant) | Pa | | 3 | Relative Volatility | $\alpha_{AB} = \dfrac{K_A}{K_B} = \dfrac{y_A/x_A}{y_B/x_B}$ | $\alpha_{AB}$ | dimensionless | | 4 | Distribution Coef. | $K_D = \dfrac{C_{phase\,1}}{C_{phase\,2}}$ | $K_D$ | dimensionless | | 5 | Murphree Efficiency | $E_{M,V} = \dfrac{y_n - y_{n-1}}{y_n^* - y_{n-1}}$ | $E_{M,V}$ | dimensionless | | 6 | Fick's First Law | $J_A = -D_{AB} \dfrac{dC_A}{dx}$ | $D_{AB}$ (diffusivity) | m²/s | | 7 | Permeation Flux | $J_A = \dfrac{P_M}{\delta}(p_{A,feed} - p_{A,perm})$ | $P_M$ (permeability) | mol·m/(m²·s·Pa) | | 8 | Convective Flux | $N_A = k_c (C_{A,bulk} - C_{A,interface})$ | $k_c$ (mass transfer coef.) | m/s | | 9 | Membrane Selectivity | $\alpha_{A/B} = \dfrac{P_M^A}{P_M^B}$ | $\alpha_{A/B}$ | dimensionless | | 10 | Kremser Equation (Absorber) | $\dfrac{x_N - x_F}{x_N - y_{N+1}/m} = \dfrac{A^{N+1} - A}{A^{N+1} - 1}$ | $A = L/mV$ (absorption factor) | dimensionless | | 11 | Fenske Equation (Min. Stages) | $N_{min} = \dfrac{\ln[(x_{LK}/x_{HK})_{dist}/(x_{LK}/x_{HK})_{bot}]}{\ln \alpha_{LK,HK}}$ | $N_{min}$ | dimensionless | | 12 | Resistance-in-Series | $\dfrac{1}{K} = \dfrac{1}{k_f} + \dfrac{l}{P_M} + \dfrac{1}{k_p}$ | $K$ (overall coef.) | s/m | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Note:** The **Fenske Equation** (min. stages at total reflux) and the **Kremser Equation** (groups of equilibrium stages) are heavily tested in KTU 2024 ESE — master both! --- ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Petroleum Refining:** Crude oil distillation uses equilibrium stages; hydrocracker recycle gas uses rate-based membrane H₂ recovery. - **Pharmaceuticals:** Membrane ultrafiltration (rate-based) for protein concentration; crystallization (equilibrium) for API purity. - **Water Treatment:** Reverse osmosis (rate-based, pressure-driven) vs. air stripping (equilibrium) for VOC removal. - **Polymer Industry:** Pervaporation (rate-based) for dehydration of ethanol; devolatilization (equilibrium + rate) for residual monomer removal. - **Biotechnology:** Chromatography (rate-based, adsorption kinetics) for protein purification; extraction (equilibrium) for antibiotics. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Analytical Comparison Matrix | **Parameter** | **Equilibrium-Based Process** | **Rate-Based Process** | |---------------|-------------------------------|-------------------------| | **Governing Principle** | Thermodynamic phase equilibrium | Rate of mass/energy transport | | **Driving Force** | Chemical potential gradient → equilibrium | Concentration, pressure, electrical, or thermal gradient | | **Design Basis** | Number of theoretical stages + efficiency | Transfer area, residence time, flux | | **Key Equation** | $y_i = K_i x_i$ (VLE) | $J_i = \dfrac{P_M}{\delta}\Delta p_i$ (permeation) | | **Scale-up Parameter** | Number of stages, stage efficiency | Membrane area, bed volume, time | | **Limiting Factor** | Equilibrium constraint ($K$, $\alpha$, $K_D$) | Transport rate ($k_c$, $D_{AB}$, $P_M$) | | **Energy Intensity** | High (phase change often required) | Moderate to low | | **Examples** | Distillation, Extraction, Absorption | Membrane, Adsorption, Chromatography | | **Selectivity Source** | Vapor pressure, solubility, partition coef. | Membrane structure, sorbent affinity, pore size | | **KTU Application Module** | Mass transfer with chemical reaction | Polymer membrane design | ## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Murphree Vapor-Stage Efficiency (Equilibrium Concept) The Murphree efficiency quantifies how closely an actual stage approaches equilibrium. It is a *bridge* between the ideal equilibrium stage model and real rate-limited equipment. ### Step 1: Define the Streams Entering and Leaving Stage $n$ Consider a vapor stream entering stage $n$ with composition $y_{n+1}$ and leaving with composition $y_n$. The corresponding liquid compositions are $x_n$ (leaving) and $x_{n-1}$ (entering). ### Step 2: Define the Equilibrium Vapor Composition If the actual liquid leaving stage $n$ ($x_n$) were in equilibrium with the vapor, the vapor composition would be $y_n^*$, obtained from the VLE relationship: $$y_n^* = K_n x_n$$ ### Step 3: Define the Murphree Vapor-Stage Efficiency The efficiency is the ratio of the *actual change* in vapor composition to the *change that would occur* if the stage operated at equilibrium: $$E_{M,V} = \frac{\text{Actual change in vapor composition}}{\text{Change at equilibrium}} = \frac{y_n - y_{n+1}}{y_n^* - y_{n+1}}$$ ### Step 4: Show Limiting Behavior - **Perfect stage:** $y_n = y_n^*$ → $E_{M,V} = 1.0$ (100% approach to equilibrium) - **No change in composition:** $y_n = y_{n+1}$ → $E_{M,V} = 0$ (zero approach, no mass transfer) ### Step 5: Mass Transfer Basis (Rate-Governed) If we want to *derive* $E_{M,V}$ from a rate-based (mass transfer coefficient) viewpoint, we use the two-film theory on the vapor side: $$\frac{dy}{dt} = \frac{K_y \, a \, \mathcal{H}_V}{G}(y^* - y)$$ Integrating over the residence time $\tau = \mathcal{H}_V / G$ of vapor in the stage: $$E_{M,V} = 1 - \exp\!\left(-K_y a \tau\right) = 1 - \exp\!\left(-\frac{K_y a \mathcal{H}_V}{G}\right)$$ where: - $K_y$ = overall vapor-side mass transfer coefficient (m/s) - $a$ = interfacial area per unit volume (m²/m³) - $\mathcal{H}_V$ = volumetric liquid holdup on the stage (m³) - $G$ = molar vapor flow rate (mol/s) > [!NOTE] > **Critical Insight:** This equation beautifully **bridges the two paradigms** — it shows that *equilibrium efficiency* ($E_{M,V}$) emerges from a *rate-based* description (mass transfer coefficient $K_y$). This is a favorite KTU question! ## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation: Membrane Permeation (Rate-Based Concept) ### Step 1: State the Physical Setup A dense polymer membrane of thickness $\delta$ separates a high-pressure feed side at pressure $p_{feed}$ from a low-pressure permeate side at $p_{perm}$. The component $A$ dissolves at the upstream face, diffuses through, and desorbs at the downstream face. ### Step 2: Apply Fick's First Law The steady-state flux through the membrane is: $$J_A = -D_A \frac{dC_A}{dx}$$ ### Step 3: Apply Henry's Law at the Interfaces At the upstream face ($x = 0$): $C_A(0) = S_A \, p_{A,feed}$ At the downstream face ($x = \delta$): $C_A(\delta) = S_A \, p_{A,perm}$ where $S_A$ is the solubility coefficient. ### Step 4: Integrate Across the Membrane Assuming constant $D_A$ and $S_A$: $$J_A = \frac{D_A S_A}{\delta} (p_{A,feed} - p_{A,perm}) = \frac{P_M}{\delta}(p_{A,feed} - p_{A,perm})$$ where $P_M = D_A S_A$ is the **permeability** of component $A$ in the polymer. ### Step 5: Define Membrane Area Required For a desired molar flow $\dot{n}_A$ of permeate: $$A_{mem} = \frac{\dot{n}_A}{J_A} = \frac{\dot{n}_A \, \delta}{P_M (p_{A,feed} - p_{A,perm})}$$ ### Step 6: Define Selectivity (The "Equivalence" of an Equilibrium Metric) For a binary mixture: $$\alpha_{A/B} = \frac{P_M^A}{P_M^B} = \frac{D_A S_A}{D_B S_B}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Insight:** In a rate-based process, **selectivity emerges from the ratio of permeabilities**, while in an equilibrium process, selectivity comes from the ratio of $K$-values or relative volatility. The mathematical structure is analogous, but the physical origin differs fundamentally. ## 3.4 The Fundamental Decision: Equilibrium vs. Rate The choice between an equilibrium-stage approach and a rate-based approach depends on whether the rate of approach to equilibrium is fast or slow relative to the residence time in the equipment. Define the **Damköhler-type ratio**: $$Da = \frac{\text{Rate of mass transfer}}{\text{Rate of convective flow}} = \frac{k_c \, a \, \mathcal{H}}{G}$$ - If $Da \gg 1$: Mass transfer is fast → **equilibrium assumption is valid** (e.g., distillation, absorption) - If $Da \approx 1$ or $Da < 1$: Mass transfer is rate-limiting → **rate-based model required** (e.g., membrane, chromatography) # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: Classification of Separation Processes ```mermaid flowchart TD A[SEPARATION PROCESSES] --> B[Equilibrium Based] A --> C[Rate Based] A --> D[Hybrid Combined] B --> B1[Distillation] B --> B2[Absorption and Stripping] B --> B3[Liquid Liquid Extraction] B --> B4[Crystallization] B --> B5[Leaching and Drying] B --> B6[Flash Vaporization] C --> C1[Membrane Separation] C --> C2[Adsorption] C --> C3[Ion Exchange] C --> C4[Chromatography] C --> C5[Electrodialysis] C --> C6[Electrophoresis] C1 --> C1a[Reverse Osmosis] C1 --> C1b[Nanofiltration] C1 --> C1c[Ultrafiltration] C1 --> C1d[Microfiltration] C1 --> C1e[Gas Permeation] C1 --> C1f[Pervaporation] D --> D1[Reactive Distillation] D --> D2[Membrane Distillation] D --> D3[Membrane Extraction] D --> D4[Simulated Moving Bed SMB] D --> D5[Reactive Extraction] ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Block-Level Functional Architecture — Mass Transfer Pathway Comparison ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph EQ[Equilibrium Based Process] F1[Feed Phase] --> M1[Intimate Contact Mixer] M1 --> EQ1[Equilibrium Interface] EQ1 --> P1[Product Phase 1] EQ1 --> P2[Product Phase 2] EQ1 -.-> EQ2[Thermodynamic equilibrium assumed y equals K times x] end subgraph RB[Rate Based Process] F2[Feed Phase] --> MB[Selective Barrier] MB --> IF1[Upstream Interface] IF1 --> MED[Transport Medium Membrane Sorbent] MED --> IF2[Downstream Interface] IF2 --> PR[Permeate Product] MED -.-> RT[Rate limiting step J equals P divided by delta times delta P] end ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Decision Tree: Selecting the Right Model ```mermaid flowchart TD START[New Separation Problem] --> Q1{Is contact time long
compared to mass transfer timescale?} Q1 -->|YES| Q2{Are phases well mixed
at molecular level?} Q1 -->|NO| RB1[Use Rate Based Model] Q2 -->|YES| EQ1[Use Equilibrium Stage Model
McCabe Thiele Kremser] Q2 -->|NO| RB2[Use Rate Based Model
Two Film Theory] EQ1 --> Q3{Need real equipment design?} Q3 -->|YES| EQ2[Apply Stage Efficiency
Murphree E sub M] Q3 -->|NO| END1[Use Theoretical Stages] RB1 --> Q4{Is medium a membrane?} Q4 -->|YES| RB3[Solve Diffusion Permeation Equation] Q4 -->|NO| RB4[Solve Adsorption Kinetic Equation] RB2 --> Q5{Is system dilute?} Q5 -->|YES| RB5[Use Linear Driving Force LDF] Q5 -->|NO| RB6[Use Stefan Diffusion Maxwell Stefan] ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid Comparison Chart: Equilibrium Stage vs. Rate Transfer Unit ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph ES[Equilibrium Stage Concept] ES1[Stream In y sub n plus 1] --> ES2[Ideal Stage
y sub n star equals K x sub n] ES2 --> ES3[Stream Out y sub n] end subgraph RT[Rate Transfer Unit Concept] RT1[Stream In y sub n plus 1] --> RT2[Finite Rate Contact
dY over dZ equals minus K sub y a over G times Y star minus Y] RT2 --> RT3[Stream Out y sub n] end ES -.-> ESnote[Number of stages from
material balance plus VLE] RT -.-> RTnote[Height of transfer unit HTU
Number of transfer units NTU] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Conceptual Cross-Walk:** The "**NTU/HTU**" (Number/Height of a Transfer Unit) in rate-based design is the **continuous-contact analog** of the "**N-theoretical stages**" in equilibrium design. Both express the *amount of contact* required for a given separation. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Define equilibrium-based and rate-based separation processes. Give two examples of each.** **Model Answer:** **Equilibrium-Based Separation Processes:** These are separation techniques whose design and performance are governed by *thermodynamic phase equilibrium*. The exiting streams from an ideal stage are assumed to be in equilibrium with each other, so no further net mass transfer would occur with additional contact time. Examples: (i) Distillation, (ii) Liquid-Liquid Extraction. **Rate-Based Separation Processes:** These are separation techniques whose design and performance are governed by the *rate of mass transport* across a barrier or interface. Thermodynamic equilibrium is rarely reached because the rate of transport through the separating medium is the limiting step. Examples: (i) Membrane separation (e.g., reverse osmosis), (ii) Adsorption in a fixed bed. --- ### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **State the difference between Murphree efficiency and overall column efficiency. Why is efficiency important in equilibrium-stage design?** **Model Answer:** - **Murphree Efficiency ($E_M$):** It is the efficiency of a *single stage*, defined for the vapor phase as: $$E_{M,V} = \frac{y_n - y_{n+1}}{y_n^* - y_{n+1}}$$ - **Overall Column Efficiency ($E_O$):** It is the ratio of the *number of ideal (theoretical) stages* to the *number of real (actual) stages*: $$E_O = \frac{N_{ideal}}{N_{actual}}$$ - **Importance:** In equilibrium-stage design, we first calculate the number of *ideal* stages from material balances + VLE. Real equipment cannot achieve perfect equilibrium at every stage due to finite mass-transfer rates. The efficiency is the *correction factor* that converts ideal stages to real stages, allowing the column to be physically sized. --- ## PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > **KTU ESE Pattern (2024 Scheme):** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split into two sub-parts: **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Two internal-choice alternatives are provided. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]` #### Part (a) — 7 Marks A packed absorption column is used to remove acetone from air using water as the solvent. The inlet gas contains **2.0 mol% acetone** and the outlet gas must contain **≤ 0.05 mol% acetone**. The gas flow rate is $G = 30$ kmol/h and the pure water inlet is $L = 90$ kmol/h. The equilibrium relation is $y = 1.2 x$. Using the **Kremser equation**, determine the number of ideal stages required. #### Part (b) — 7 Marks A reverse osmosis membrane is to recover water from a **3000 ppm NaCl** feed at a flux of $J = 1.0 \times 10^{-5}$ m/s. The membrane has a water permeability of $P_M = 1.5 \times 10^{-9}$ m²·s·Pa and thickness $\delta = 1.0 \times 10^{-6}$ m. The osmotic pressure difference is $\Delta \pi = 2.5 \times 10^{5}$ Pa. Determine the **required transmembrane pressure difference**. --- ### **MODEL SOLUTION TO QUESTION A** #### Part (a) Solution **Step 1: Identify the Kremser equation for an absorber** For an absorber with $N$ ideal stages, fresh solvent ($y_{N+1} = 0$ for pure water), and $A = L/(mV)$: $$\frac{x_N - x_F}{x_N - y_{N+1}/m} = \frac{A^{N+1} - A}{A^{N+1} - 1}$$ Since fresh water is used, $x_F = 0$, and $y_{N+1} = 0$ (pure water contains no acetone). Therefore: $$x_N = x_F = 0 \text{ (water inlet composition of acetone)}$$ **Step 2: Calculate the absorption factor $A$** Given: $m = 1.2$, $L = 90$ kmol/h, $V = G = 30$ kmol/h: $$A = \frac{L}{mV} = \frac{90}{1.2 \times 30} = \frac{90}{36} = 2.5$$ **Step 3: Calculate the exit liquid composition $x_N$** Material balance on acetone: $$V(y_{F} - y_1) = L(x_N - x_F)$$ $$30 (0.02 - 0.0005) = 90 (x_N - 0)$$ $$30 \times 0.0195 = 90 x_N$$ $$x_N = \frac{0.585}{90} = 0.0065$$ **Step 4: Apply Kremser equation to find $N$** $$\frac{x_N - 0}{x_N - 0/m} = \frac{A^{N+1} - A}{A^{N+1} - 1}$$ Since $y_{N+1} = 0$ and $m \neq 0$, the LHS reduces to: $$\frac{x_N}{x_N} = 1 = \frac{A^{N+1} - A}{A^{N+1} - 1}$$ This implies $A^{N+1} \to \infty$, which physically means that for $A > 1$, the solute is completely absorbed. To find a finite $N$, we use the more general form for finite outlet gas: $$N = \frac{\ln\!\left[\left(\frac{y_{N+1} - m x_N}{y_1 - m x_N}\right)\left(1 - \frac{1}{A}\right) + \frac{1}{A}\right]}{\ln A}$$ **Step 5: Substitute the values** $$y_1 = 0.0005, \quad y_{N+1} = 0, \quad x_F = 0, \quad x_N = 0.0065, \quad m = 1.2, \quad A = 2.5$$ $$y_1 - m x_N = 0.0005 - 1.2 \times 0.0065 = 0.0005 - 0.0078 = -0.0073$$ $$y_{N+1} - m x_N = 0 - 0.0078 = -0.0078$$ $$\frac{y_{N+1} - m x_N}{y_1 - m x_N} = \frac{-0.0078}{-0.0073} = 1.0685$$ $$1 - \frac{1}{A} = 1 - 0.4 = 0.6$$ Numerator inside ln: $$1.0685 \times 0.6 + 0.4 = 0.6411 + 0.4 = 1.0411$$ $$N = \frac{\ln(1.0411)}{\ln(2.5)} = \frac{0.0403}{0.9163} = 0.044$$ Since $N$ must be a positive integer, and $A = 2.5 > 1$, the operation is "**absorption-factor dominated**" — practically, **$N = 1$ ideal stage is sufficient** to achieve the required removal. > **Answer (a):** $N = 1$ ideal stage --- #### Part (b) Solution **Step 1: Write the solution-diffusion flux equation for RO** For a reverse osmosis membrane treating water as the permeating species: $$J_w = \frac{P_M}{\delta} (\Delta P - \Delta \pi)$$ **Step 2: Identify the known quantities** - $J_w = 1.0 \times 10^{-5}$ m/s - $P_M = 1.5 \times 10^{-9}$ m²·s·Pa (water permeability) - $\delta = 1.0 \times 10^{-6}$ m - $\Delta \pi = 2.5 \times 10^{5}$ Pa **Step 3: Calculate $P_M / \delta$** $$\frac{P_M}{\delta} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^{-9}}{1.0 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m/(s·Pa)}$$ **Step 4: Solve for $\Delta P$** $$\Delta P = \frac{J_w \cdot \delta}{P_M} + \Delta \pi$$ $$\Delta P = \frac{1.0 \times 10^{-5}}{1.5 \times 10^{-3}} + 2.5 \times 10^{5}$$ $$\Delta P = 6.667 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Pa} + 2.5 \times 10^{5} \text{ Pa}$$ $$\Delta P \approx 2.5 \times 10^{5} \text{ Pa} = 2.5 \text{ bar}$$ > **Answer (b):** $\Delta P \approx 2.5$ bar (≈ **250 kPa**) --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]` #### Part (a) — 7 Marks Define **selectivity** for (i) a distillation column and (ii) a membrane separation process. Explain with equations how the separation factor emerges from fundamentally different physical mechanisms in each case. #### Part (b) — 7 Marks A binary mixture of benzene (B) and toluene (T) is to be separated in a distillation column. The feed is **40 mol% benzene, 60 mol% toluene**, and the distillate is **95 mol% benzene**. The bottoms is **5 mol% benzene**. The relative volatility $\alpha_{BT} = 2.47$ is constant. Using the **Fenske equation**, calculate the **minimum number of stages at total reflux**. --- ### **MODEL SOLUTION TO QUESTION B** #### Part (a) Solution **(i) Distillation Column — Selectivity (Relative Volatility):** In distillation, selectivity is governed by the difference in vapor pressures (or equilibrium $K$-values) of the components: $$\alpha_{A/B} = \frac{K_A}{K_B} = \frac{y_A/x_A}{y_B/x_B} = \frac{P_A^{sat}}{P_B^{sat}}$$ The **physical mechanism** is *thermodynamic*: the more volatile component preferentially partitions into the vapor phase at equilibrium. The selectivity arises from differences in the *Gibbs free energy of vaporization*. **(ii) Membrane Separation — Selectivity (Permeability Ratio):** In a rate-based membrane process, selectivity is governed by the ratio of permeabilities: $$\alpha_{A/B}^{mem} = \frac{P_M^A}{P_M^B} = \frac{D_A S_A}{D_B S_B}$$ The **physical mechanism** is *kinetic + thermodynamic*: - The **solubility** $S_A$ reflects the *thermodynamic* affinity of the penetrant for the polymer. - The **diffusivity** $D_A$ reflects the *kinetic* mobility of the penetrant in the polymer matrix. **Key Difference:** | Aspect | Distillation | Membrane | |--------|-------------|----------| | Selectivity source | Purely thermodynamic (VLE) | Combined solubility + diffusivity | | Number of stages / area | Number of stages (discrete) | Membrane area (continuous) | | Limitation | Azeotrope (selectivity = 1) | Robeson upper bound | --- #### Part (b) Solution **Step 1: State the Fenske Equation** The Fenske equation gives the minimum number of stages at total reflux for a binary mixture: $$N_{min} = \frac{\ln \left[ \left(\dfrac{x_{LK}}{x_{HK}}\right)_D \Big/ \left(\dfrac{x_{LK}}{x_{HK}}\right)_B \right]}{\ln \alpha_{LK,HK}}$$ **Step 2: Identify the Light and Heavy Keys** - **Light Key (LK):** Benzene (B) — the more volatile component we want in the distillate - **Heavy Key (HK):** Toluene (T) — the less volatile component we want in the bottoms **Step 3: Compute composition ratios** **Distillate (D):** $x_{LK,D} = 0.95$, $x_{HK,D} = 1 - 0.95 = 0.05$ $$\left(\frac{x_{LK}}{x_{HK}}\right)_D = \frac{0.95}{0.05} = 19.0$$ **Bottoms (B):** $x_{LK,B} = 0.05$, $x_{HK,B} = 1 - 0.05 = 0.95$ $$\left(\frac{x_{LK}}{x_{HK}}\right)_B = \frac{0.05}{0.95} = 0.05263$$ **Step 4: Compute the ratio of ratios** $$\frac{(x_{LK}/x_{HK})_D}{(x_{LK}/x_{HK})_B} = \frac{19.0}{0.05263} = 361.0$$ **Step 5: Apply Fenske** $$N_{min} = \frac{\ln(361.0)}{\ln(2.47)} = \frac{5.886}{0.9046} = 6.51$$ Since the number of stages must be an integer (we always round up), the reboiler is included as a stage: $$N_{min, total} = 6.51 \approx 7 \text{ stages (including reboiler)}$$ If we exclude the reboiler: $N_{min, plates} = 7 - 1 = 6$ ideal plates. > **Answer (b):** $N_{min} = 7$ total ideal stages (6 plates + 1 reboiler) --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting units in $P_M / \delta$:** Many students drop units and write $J = P_M \cdot \Delta P$ instead of $J = (P_M/\delta)\Delta P$. This causes a **factor-of-1000 error**. > 2. **Confusing Murphree and Overall Efficiency:** Murphree is *per stage*; overall is *per column*. They are **not** the same. > 3. **Sign convention in Fenske:** Always use **light-key/heavy-key** ratio — putting them upside-down is the #1 mistake. > 4. **Forgetting the reboiler as a stage:** In Fenske/Kremser, the reboiler is **counted as one stage** in the total $N$. > 5. **Assuming equilibrium in membrane problems:** Never apply Raoult's Law or VLE to a membrane problem — use the solution-diffusion or pore-flow model. --- ## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER ### 🔑 Quick-Recall Bullet Points - ✅ **Equilibrium processes** are governed by **thermodynamic phase equilibrium** (VLE, LLE, SLE); examples include distillation, absorption, extraction, crystallization. - ✅ **Rate-based processes** are governed by **mass transfer kinetics**; examples include membrane separation, adsorption, ion exchange, chromatography. - ✅ The **Murphree efficiency** $E_{M,V} = (y_n - y_{n+1})/(y_n^* - y_{n+1})$ bridges ideal equilibrium stages and real equipment. - ✅ The **Fenske equation** gives the **minimum number of stages** at total reflux; uses the **light-key/heavy-key** composition ratio and relative volatility. - ✅ The **Kremser equation** handles **finite stages** in absorbers, strippers, and extractors, using the **absorption factor** $A = L/(mV)$. - ✅ The **solution-diffusion model** gives the membrane flux: $J = (P_M/\delta)(\Delta p)$ where $P_M = D \cdot S$. - ✅ **Membrane selectivity** is the **ratio of permeabilities**: $\alpha_{A/B} = P_M^A / P_M^B$. - ✅ When the **Damköhler number is large** (mass transfer fast vs. flow), use **equilibrium model**; when small, use **rate-based model**. - ✅ **HTU-NTU** (rate-based) is the continuous analog of **theoretical stages** (equilibrium-based). - ✅ **Azeotropes** are the *fundamental limit* of equilibrium processes; **Robeson upper bound** is the *fundamental limit* of membrane processes. - ✅ For KTU 2024 ESE: focus on **deriving equations, units, and applying the right model** to the right problem.

topics.title: Selection of Separation Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT752 - POLYMERS AND SEPARATION PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Selection of Separation Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In chemical and polymer engineering, a **Separation Process** is defined as a unit operation or unit process that transforms a mixture of substances into two or more distinct products that differ in composition. The **Selection of a Separation Process** is the systematic, engineering decision-making procedure used to identify the most technically feasible, economically viable, and operationally safe technique for separating a given feed mixture into desired products, based on quantifiable property differences, thermodynamic equilibria, and transport rate considerations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** The selection of a separation process is fundamentally governed by the **"Property Difference"** between the components to be separated. If a property difference does not exist, no separation is possible. If the property difference is too small, separation is impractical regardless of the technique employed.
