### 1.2 Classification Hierarchy of Separation Techniques

Separation processes are broadly classified into two master categories based on the nature of the **separating agent** used:

1. **Equilibrium-Staged Processes (Differential Contactors):** Operate based on thermodynamic phase equilibrium between two immiscible phases.
2. **Rate-Governed Processes:** Operate based on differences in the rate of mass (or heat/charge) transport across a barrier or interface.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have a mixture of **iron filings, sand, salt, and water** in a single glass beaker. How would you separate them?

- **Magnet** pulls out the iron filings → exploits **magnetic susceptibility** difference.
- **Filter paper** catches the sand → exploits **particle size** difference.
- **Evaporation** recovers the salt → exploits **volatility** difference.

The **Selection of a Separation Process** is essentially the engineering version of this kitchen experiment. Instead of trial-and-error, chemical engineers use a structured decision framework to ask: *"What physical or chemical property makes these components different?"* Once the dominant property difference is identified, the engineer selects the corresponding unit operation. For example:
- If components differ in **volatility** → **Distillation**
- If components differ in **solubility** → **Liquid-Liquid Extraction**
- If components differ in **molecular size** → **Membrane Separation**
- If components differ in **charge** → **Electrodialysis**

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb (C.J. King, 1980):** A well-known framework by C. Judson King classifies separation processes into **12 generic methods** grouped by the underlying physical/chemical property exploited. This forms the backbone of selection logic taught in KTU.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics Used in Selection

- **Separation Factor (α):** The ratio of equilibrium concentrations of two components between two phases. A value of **α = 1** indicates **impossible separation**.
- **Recovery (R):** The fraction of a key component in the feed that ends up in the desired product, typically expressed as a **percentage (%)**.
- **Purity:** The mass (or mole) fraction of the target component in the final product stream.
- **Reflux Ratio / Solvent-to-Feed Ratio:** Operating parameter that governs separation sharpness.
- **Energy Duty (Q):** The energy input per unit mass of feed, measured in **kJ/kg**.

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU Board Exams:** Always start a "selection of separation process" question by **identifying the property difference first**, then justify the process choice. This is the standard valuation pattern examiners expect.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Fundamental Question: Why is Selection Important?

There are often **multiple valid techniques** to separate the same mixture. For instance, dilute aqueous ethanol can be separated by:
- Conventional distillation (limited by the azeotrope at 95.6 wt% ethanol)
- Azeotropic distillation (using benzene as entrainer)
- Extractive distillation (using ethylene glycol)
- Adsorption (using molecular sieves - 3Å type)
- Reverse osmosis (membrane process)

The **engineer's job** is to evaluate these options systematically using criteria such as:
1. **Feed Composition & Phase** (gas, liquid, solid)
2. **Product Specifications** (purity, recovery)
3. **Thermodynamic Constraints** (azeotropes, miscibility gaps)
4. **Capital Cost (CAPEX)** and **Operating Cost (OPEX)**
5. **Safety, Environmental, and Regulatory Factors**

### 2.2 The Decision Logic Hierarchy (Step-by-Step)

A rigorous selection follows this structured logic:

**Step 1 — Identify the Feed State**
- Gaseous feed → Lean towards absorption, adsorption, membrane gas separation, cryogenic distillation.
- Liquid feed → Distillation, extraction, distillation variants, liquid membranes.
- Solid feed → Leaching, crystallization, sublimation, solid-liquid extraction.

**Step 2 — Quantify the Property Difference**
- Compute the **relative volatility (α)** for distillation candidates.
- Compute the **distribution coefficient (K_D)** for extraction candidates.
- Compute the **permeability ratio** for membrane candidates.

**Step 3 — Apply the King/Seader Selection Matrix**
- Match the dominant property to the unit operation.
- Reject any process where the property difference is < a practical threshold (e.g., α < 1.05 for distillation is rarely economical).

**Step 4 — Verify Thermodynamic Feasibility**
- Check for **azeotropes**, **eutectics**, and **miscibility limits**.
- Confirm that equilibrium data exists in literature (DECHEMA, DIPPR, NIST).

**Step 5 — Economic and Energy Analysis**
- Estimate minimum energy duty using reversible thermodynamic limits.
- Compare annualized CAPEX + OPEX for top 2–3 candidates.

**Step 6 — Final Selection and Safety Review**

### 2.3 Separating Agents: MSA vs ESA

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mass Separating Agent (MSA):** A second phase (liquid or solid) is added to selectively bind/carry one component. Example: Solvent in extraction, absorbent in absorption.
>
> **Energy Separating Agent (ESA):** Only energy (heat, work) is added; no second mass stream. Example: Distillation, evaporation, crystallization.

| Process | Type of Separating Agent | Property Exploited |
|---|---|---|
| Distillation | ESA (Heat) | Volatility |
| Absorption | MSA (Liquid solvent) | Solubility in liquid |
| Adsorption | MSA (Solid adsorbent) | Surface affinity |
| Liquid-Liquid Extraction | MSA (Immiscible liquid) | Solubility difference |
| Membrane Separation | MSA or ESA | Size, charge, permeability |
| Crystallization | ESA (Cooling/heat removal) | Melting point |
| Drying | ESA (Heat) | Humidity / Vapor pressure |
| Ion Exchange | MSA (Resin) | Charge |
| Electrodialysis | ESA (Electrical work) | Ionic charge |
| Leaching | MSA (Solvent) | Solubility |
| Sublimation | ESA (Heat) | Vapor pressure of solid |
| Zone Refining | ESA (Heat zone) | Phase change |

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Rule for LaTeX:** No vertical pipes `|` are used inside table cells. The notation `\vert` is used for absolute values to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Separation Factor (Distillation) | $\alpha_{ij} = \dfrac{K_i}{K_j} = \dfrac{P_i^{sat}}{P_j^{sat}}$ | Dimensionless | Measure of ease of separation by distillation |
| 2 | Minimum Number of Stages (Fenske) | $N_{min} = \dfrac{\ln \left[ \left( \dfrac{x_D}{x_B} \right)_{LK} \left( \dfrac{x_B}{x_D} \right)_{HK} \right]}{\ln \alpha_{LK,HK}}$ | Stages | Theoretical stages at total reflux |
| 3 | Distribution Coefficient | $K_D = \dfrac{C_{organic}}{C_{aqueous}}$ | Dimensionless | Liquid-Liquid Extraction |
| 4 | Selectivity (Extraction) | $\beta = \dfrac{K_{solute}}{K_{impurity}}$ | Dimensionless | Solvent screening for extraction |
| 5 | Recovery | $R = \dfrac{\text{Component in product}}{\text{Component in feed}} \times 100\%$ | % | Universal |
| 6 | Relative Selectivity (Membrane) | $\alpha_{A/B} = \dfrac{P_A}{P_B} = \dfrac{Q_A \, l}{Q_B \, l}$ | Dimensionless | Gas/Liquid membrane separation |
| 7 | Minimum Work of Separation (Reversible) | $W_{min,rev} = -RT \sum_i n_i \ln(y_i)$ | J/mol | Thermodynamic energy limit |
| 8 | Azeotrope Condition | $y_i = x_i$ for all $i$ at azeotrope | — | Identifies distillation ceiling |
| 9 | Kremser Equation (Absorption) | $\dfrac{L}{V} = \dfrac{y_{N+1} - y_1}{x_N - x_0}$ | — | Stage requirement for gas absorption |
| 10 | Reflux Ratio (Min vs Operating) | $R_{min} = \dfrac{x_D - y_{q,feed}}{y_{q,feed} - x_{q,feed}}$ | Dimensionless | Column design minimum |
| 11 | Fenske Distribution Function | $\theta_n = \dfrac{x_{n,HK}}{x_{n,LK}} = \alpha^{N-n} \, \theta_D$ | — | Multicomponent stage distribution |
| 12 | Energy of Separation Index (ESI) | $ESI = \dfrac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R T_{bp}}$ | Dimensionless | Predicts distillation energy demand |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The selection framework is used directly in:
- **Petroleum Refining:** Choosing between atmospheric distillation, vacuum distillation, or catalytic cracking based on feed boiling range.
- **Polymer Manufacturing:** Choosing between devolatilization, precipitation, or steam stripping for residual monomer removal.
- **Pharmaceutical Industry:** Selecting crystallization over distillation for heat-sensitive APIs (Active Pharmaceutical Ingredients).
- **Water Treatment:** Selecting reverse osmosis vs electrodialysis based on TDS (Total Dissolved Solids) levels.
- **Food Industry:** Selecting supercritical CO₂ extraction vs solvent extraction for caffeine or essential oils.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Industrial Context:** A frequently asked exam scenario is selecting a separation process for **polymer-solvent systems**. Always consider that polymers are thermally sensitive → **membrane separation, precipitation, or extractive methods** are often preferred over high-temperature distillation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Fenske Equation (Minimum Number of Stages)

**Objective:** Derive the equation for the **minimum number of theoretical stages** $N_{min}$ required to achieve a specified separation at **total reflux** conditions (i.e., no products withdrawn, all condensate returned as reflux).

**Starting Point — Vapor-Liquid Equilibrium for an Ideal Binary System:**

$$y_i = \dfrac{\alpha \, x_i}{1 + (\alpha - 1) x_i}$$

**Step 1 — Define Composition Ratio at Any Stage $n$:**

At any stage $n$, the ratio of light key (LK) to heavy key (HK) in the liquid phase is:

$$\theta_n = \dfrac{x_{n, LK}}{x_{n, HK}}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Equilibrium Relationship at Stage $n$:**

For an ideal stage, the vapor leaving stage $n$ is in equilibrium with the liquid:

$$y_{n, LK} = \alpha \, x_{n, LK} \quad \text{(since } x_{HK} \to 0 \text{ in dilute region)}$$

**Step 3 — Operate at Total Reflux:**

At total reflux, the operating line coincides with the diagonal $y_{n+1} = x_n$. This means the vapor composition leaving stage $n$ equals the liquid composition on stage $n+1$:

$$y_{n, LK} = x_{n+1, LK} \quad \text{and} \quad y_{n, HK} = x_{n+1, HK}$$

**Step 4 — Form the Ratio $\theta_{n+1} / \theta_n$:**

$$\dfrac{\theta_{n+1}}{\theta_n} = \dfrac{x_{n+1, LK} / x_{n+1, HK}}{x_{n, LK} / x_{n, HK}} = \dfrac{y_{n, LK} / y_{n, HK}}{x_{n, LK} / x_{n, HK}}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute the Equilibrium Expression:**

Since $y_{n, LK} / y_{n, HK} = \alpha \cdot (x_{n, LK} / x_{n, HK})$:

$$\dfrac{\theta_{n+1}}{\theta_n} = \alpha$$

**Step 6 — Telescope the Recursion from $n = 1$ to $n = N$:**

$$\theta_{N+1} = \alpha^{N} \, \theta_1$$

**Step 7 — Translate to Product Specifications:**

- $\theta_{N+1}$ corresponds to the **distillate** composition: $\theta_D = \dfrac{x_{D,LK}}{x_{D,HK}}$
- $\theta_1$ corresponds to the **bottoms** composition: $\theta_B = \dfrac{x_{B,LK}}{x_{B,HK}}$

**Step 8 — Final Fenske Equation:**

$$\boxed{\,N_{min} = \dfrac{\ln \left[ \left( \dfrac{x_{D,LK}}{x_{D,HK}} \right) \left( \dfrac{x_{B,HK}}{x_{B,LK}} \right) \right]}{\ln \alpha_{LK,HK}}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for selection:** If the Fenske $N_{min}$ computed for a given separation is **unreasonably large (e.g., > 100 stages)**, then distillation is **not the practical choice** — the engineer should switch to extraction, adsorption, or a hybrid process.

### 3.2 Derivation of Minimum Work of Separation

**Objective:** Show that **no separation process is thermodynamically reversible in practice**; the minimum theoretical energy requirement is given by the change in Gibbs free energy of mixing.

**Step 1 — Consider an Isothermal, Isobaric Separation of an Ideal Mixture:**

The Gibbs free energy of mixing for $N$ components in the feed is:

$$\Delta G_{mix} = RT \sum_{i=1}^{N} n_i \ln(x_i)$$

**Step 2 — The Separated Products Have Pure-Component Gibbs Energies:**

For a feed of $n_i$ moles split into $n_i^{prod}$ moles in pure form, $x_i \to 1$ and $\ln(x_i) \to 0$:

$$\Delta G_{prod} = 0 \quad (\text{pure components})$$

**Step 3 — Net Change in Gibbs Free Energy is the Minimum Reversible Work:**

$$W_{min, rev} = \Delta G_{products} - \Delta G_{feed} = -RT \sum_{i=1}^{N} n_i \ln(x_i)$$

Since $x_i < 1$ for all $i$, $\ln(x_i) < 0$, hence $W_{min, rev} > 0$, confirming that **separation always requires a positive energy input**.

**Step 4 — Engineering Implication for Selection:**

Processes that operate **close to the reversible work limit** are thermodynamically efficient. Real processes always exceed $W_{min, rev}$ by a factor known as the **Second-Law Efficiency** $\eta_{II}$:

$$\eta_{II} = \dfrac{W_{min, rev}}{W_{actual}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Application:** This is why low-temperature distillation (e.g., air separation at cryogenic conditions) and membrane processes are favored for high-volume industrial separations — they approach the reversible limit more closely than high-temperature phase-change processes.

### 3.3 Worked Example: Selection of a Process for Acetone-Water Separation

**Problem:** A feed stream contains **50 mol% acetone, 50 mol% water** at 1 atm. Product specifications: **Distillate ≥ 99 mol% acetone, Bottoms ≤ 1 mol% acetone**. Select the most appropriate separation process and justify with calculations.

**Given:** $\alpha_{acetone/water} = 2.3$ (average over column range)

**Step 1 — Apply Fenske's Equation to Evaluate Distillation Feasibility:**

$$N_{min} = \dfrac{\ln \left[ \left( \dfrac{0.99}{0.01} \right) \left( \dfrac{0.99}{0.01} \right) \right]}{\ln(2.3)} = \dfrac{\ln(99 \times 99)}{\ln(2.3)} = \dfrac{\ln(9801)}{0.8329} = \dfrac{9.19}{0.8329} \approx 11.03$$

Rounded up: **$N_{min} = 12$ stages** — highly feasible for distillation.

**Step 2 — Check for Azeotrope:**

Acetone-water system has **NO azeotrope** at 1 atm (unlike ethanol-water at 95.6 wt%).

**Step 3 — Compare with Alternative Processes:**

- **Liquid-Liquid Extraction:** Acetone and water are **fully miscible** → extraction requires adding a third solvent → adds cost and complexity → rejected.
- **Membrane Pervaporation:** Possible, but with a high water-acetone feed, the flux is low and membrane replacement OPEX is high.
- **Adsorption (Activated Carbon / 3Å Sieves):** Effective but typically used for **trace** impurity removal, not bulk 50-50 splits.

**Step 4 — Final Selection: Conventional Distillation.**

**Justification (for KTU 14-mark answer):**
- Property difference (relative volatility) is large ($\alpha = 2.3$).
- No azeotrope.
- Minimum stages (~12) is economically realizable.
- Distillation uses **only heat as ESA**, no added MSA to recover.
- CAPEX is moderate; OPEX (steam) is acceptable for this feed.
- Alternative processes (extraction, membrane) are either infeasible or uneconomical at this composition.

> [!TIP]
> **For the KTU 14-mark part (a):** Always show the Fenske calculation explicitly. For part (b), provide a comparison table justifying why the chosen process wins over at least 2 alternatives.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Decision Tree for Separation Process Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Feed Mixture Identification] --> B{Feed Phase State?}
    B -- Gas --> C[Check Molecular Size, Volatility, Solubility]
    B -- Liquid --> D[Check Volatility, Solubility, Charge]
    B -- Solid --> E[Check Solubility, Particle Size, Magnetic Properties]
    C --> C1{Absorption Feasible?}
    C1 -- Yes --> C1A[Adsorption: Molecular Sieves, Silica, Activated Carbon]
    C1 -- No --> C2{Cryogenic Distillation Feasible?}
    C2 -- Yes --> C2A[Cryogenic Distillation: Linde, Claude Cycles]
    C2 -- No --> C3[Membrane Gas Separation: Polymeric or Inorganic Membranes]
    D --> D1{Azeotrope Present?}
    D1 -- No --> D1A[Conventional Distillation: Sieve, Bubble Cap, Packed]
    D1 -- Yes --> D2{Azeotrope Homogeneous?}
    D2 -- Yes --> D2A[Azeotropic or Extractive Distillation]
    D2 -- No --> D2B[Pressure-Swing Distillation]
    D1A --> D3{Heat Sensitive Components?}
    D3 -- Yes --> D3A[Evaporation under Vacuum or Falling Film Evaporator]
    D3 -- No --> D1A
    E --> E1{Component Soluble in Liquid?}
    E1 -- Yes --> E1A[Leaching: Batch or Continuous Soxhlet]
    E1 -- No --> E2{Magnetic Property Difference?}
    E2 -- Yes --> E2A[Magnetic Separation]
    E2 -- No --> E3[Sublimation or Zone Refining]
    D1A --> F[Economic Analysis: CAPEX and OPEX]
    C1A --> F
    C2A --> F
    C3 --> F
    D2A --> F
    D2B --> F
    D3A --> F
    E1A --> F
    E2A --> F
    E3 --> F
    F --> G[Final Process Selection and PFD Development]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Selection Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT [INPUT BLOCK]
        IP1[Feed Composition]
        IP2[Feed Conditions T and P]
        IP3[Product Specifications]
    end
    subgraph ANALYSIS [ANALYSIS BLOCK]
        AN1[Property Difference Identification]
        AN2[Thermodynamic Equilibrium Check]
        AN3[Kinetic Rate Check]
        AN4[Azeotrope and Eutectic Detection]
    end
    subgraph SCREENING [SCREENING BLOCK]
        SC1[King 12-Process Matrix Match]
        SC2[Fenske and Kremser Calculations]
        SC3[Solvent Selection if MSA Needed]
    end
    subgraph ECONOMIC [ECONOMIC BLOCK]
        EC1[CAPEX Estimation]
        EC2[OPEX and Energy Duty]
        EC3[Second-Law Efficiency]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT [OUTPUT BLOCK]
        OP1[Ranked Candidate List]
        OP2[Selected Process]
        OP3[Process Flow Diagram]
    end
    IP1 --> AN1
    IP2 --> AN1
    IP3 --> AN1
    AN1 --> AN2
    AN1 --> AN3
    AN2 --> AN4
    AN4 --> SC1
    SC1 --> SC2
    SC1 --> SC3
    SC2 --> EC1
    SC3 --> EC2
    EC1 --> EC3
    EC2 --> EC3
    EC3 --> OP1
    OP1 --> OP2
    OP2 --> OP3
```

### 4.3 Generic Schematic: Distillation Column as the Default Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    FEED[Feed Mixture: 50-50 Acetone-Water] --> COLUMN[Distillation Column]
    COLUMN --> COND[Total Condenser]
    COND --> REF[Reflux Drum]
    REF --> REFLUX[Reflux Return to Column]
    REF --> DIST[Distillate Product: 99% Acetone]
    COLUMN --> REB[Reboiler: Steam or Electric]
    REB --> BOTTOM[Bottoms Product: 1% Acetone in Water]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note for KTU Students:** Mermaid diagrams in exams are not required, but drawing equivalent **Process Flow Diagrams (PFDs)** with standard symbols (ISA 5.1) is highly recommended for full marks in design-oriented questions.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*List any three factors that govern the selection of a separation process for a given binary liquid mixture.*

**Model Answer:**

The three principal factors are:

1. **Property Difference Between Components:** The relative volatility $\alpha$, distribution coefficient $K_D$, or selectivity $\beta$ must exceed a practical threshold (e.g., $\alpha > 1.05$ for distillation to be economical).
2. **Feed Phase and Composition:** A liquid feed with no azeotrope favors distillation; a heat-sensitive feed favors extraction or membrane separation.
3. **Product Specifications:** Required purity and recovery dictate the number of stages, reflux ratio, and ultimately the choice of unit operation (e.g., 99.9% purity may require extractive distillation instead of conventional distillation).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Distinguish between Mass Separating Agent (MSA) and Energy Separating Agent (ESA) with one example each.*

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Mass Separating Agent (MSA) | Energy Separating Agent (ESA) |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | A second mass phase (liquid or solid) is added to selectively bind or carry one component | Only energy (heat, work) is supplied; no second mass stream |
| Example | Absorption of CO₂ in monoethanolamine (MEA) | Distillation of acetone-water mixture |
| Recovery Needed? | Yes — the MSA must be regenerated and recycled | Not applicable |
| Cost Driver | Cost of solvent + regeneration OPEX | Cost of heating/cooling utilities |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** *With the help of a stepwise procedure, explain how a chemical engineer selects an appropriate separation process for a binary liquid mixture of benzene and toluene. The feed is equimolar, and the product specifications are 99 mol% benzene in distillate and 1 mol% benzene in bottoms. Use the Fenske equation. Given: $\alpha_{benzene/toluene} = 2.47$ at 1 atm. (7 marks)*

**(b)** *Compare distillation with liquid-liquid extraction as competing separation techniques. Under what conditions is extraction preferred over distillation? (7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Step-by-Step Selection:**

Step 1 — **Identify property difference:** Benzene (BP = 80.1°C) and toluene (BP = 110.6°C) differ in volatility → Distillation is the natural first candidate.

Step 2 — **Check azeotrope:** No azeotrope exists for benzene-toluene at 1 atm → Conventional distillation feasible.

Step 3 — **Apply Fenske equation:**

$$N_{min} = \dfrac{\ln \left[ \left( \dfrac{0.99}{0.01} \right) \left( \dfrac{0.99}{0.01} \right) \right]}{\ln(2.47)} = \dfrac{\ln(9801)}{0.9046} = \dfrac{9.19}{0.9046} \approx 10.16$$

Rounded up: **$N_{min} = 11$ stages** — economically feasible.

Step 4 — **Verify with minimum reflux (Underwood):**

For a saturated liquid feed at $q = 1$ and equimolar feed, $R_{min} \approx 0.5$ to $1.0$ (from McCabe-Thiele analysis or shortcut Underwood method).

Step 5 — **Decide column type:** For vacuum to atmospheric operation, **sieve tray column** is the standard KTU-recommended choice (efficiency 70–80%).

Step 6 — **Final selection:** Conventional distillation with ~30 actual stages (assuming ~40% overall efficiency), $R/R_{min} = 1.3$ to 1.5.

**(b) Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Distillation | Liquid-Liquid Extraction |
|---|---|---|
| Property exploited | Volatility (BP) | Solubility difference |
| Separating agent | Heat (ESA) | Solvent (MSA) |
| Best for | Wide BP difference (α > 1.5) | Close BP, large solubility difference |
| Energy | High (latent heat) | Moderate (pump + recovery still) |
| Heat-sensitive feeds | Not suitable | Suitable |
| Azeotrope problem | Limiting | Avoids azeotrope |

**Conditions favoring extraction:**
- Components have close boiling points ($\alpha < 1.2$).
- Feed is heat-sensitive (e.g., antibiotics, vitamins).
- Azeotrope exists between the two components.
- Concentration of solute in feed is low (extraction avoids bulk vaporization).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024]**

**(a)** *Define the separation factor. How is it used as a screening tool in process selection? Provide a numerical example. (7 marks)*

**(b)** *A dilute aqueous solution contains 2 wt% acetic acid. Suggest and justify a suitable separation process. Compare it with conventional distillation. (7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Definition and Screening:**

The **separation factor** $\alpha$ is defined as the ratio of concentrations (or mole fractions) of two components between two coexisting phases at equilibrium:

$$\alpha_{ij} = \dfrac{(x_i / x_j)^{Phase\,1}}{(x_i / x_j)^{Phase\,2}}$$

**Screening criteria:**
- $\alpha = 1$: Impossible separation.
- $1 < \alpha < 1.05$: Possible but uneconomical.
- $\alpha > 1.5$: Economical separation likely.

**Numerical Example:** For benzene (1) and toluene (2), $\alpha_{1,2} = 2.47$. For a feed with 50 mol% benzene, the relative volatility indicates a **practical** distillation, confirmed by the Fenske result $N_{min} = 11$.

**(b) Selection for Dilute Acetic Acid:**

**Recommended Process: Liquid-Liquid Extraction using ethyl acetate or methyl isobutyl ketone (MIBK)** followed by a solvent recovery column.

**Justification:**
- The relative volatility of acetic acid to water is $\alpha \approx 1.21$ → distillation requires many stages and high reflux.
- Boiling points: Acetic acid (118°C), Water (100°C) — but acetic acid-water does **not form an azeotrope**, however the relative volatility is too close to 1 for economical distillation.
- **Extraction advantage:** $K_D$ of acetic acid in ethyl acetate is ~0.9 at 25°C → high distribution coefficient → low solvent-to-feed ratio.

**Comparison with Distillation:**

| Parameter | Distillation | Extraction |
|---|---|---|
| Relative volatility | 1.21 (poor) | $K_D$ = 0.9 (good) |
| Number of stages | High (>30) | Moderate (4–6) |
| Energy | High (steam for water vaporization) | Lower (only solvent recovery still) |
| Suitability for dilute feeds | Poor | Excellent |
| Capital cost | Lower (single column) | Higher (extractor + recovery column) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. **Do not write "separation factor = 1 means easy separation."** This is the **most common error** and will cost 1 mark. $\alpha = 1$ is **impossible**, not easy.
> 2. **Forgetting to check for azeotropes** before recommending distillation. Always verify the VLE diagram or check the DECHEMA database.
> 3. **Ignoring the feed state.** Solid feeds require leaching/crystallization, not distillation. Many students default to distillation without verifying.
> 4. **Not justifying the choice with a numerical calculation.** A 14-mark answer without a Fenske/Kremser calculation typically caps at 8–9 marks.
> 5. **Confusing selectivity ($\beta$) with separation factor ($\alpha$).** Selectivity is for extraction; separation factor is a general equilibrium term.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exams:**

- ☐ **Property difference is the foundation** of every separation — if it does not exist, no separation is possible.
- ☐ **King's 12-Process Matrix** is the standard academic reference for matching property differences to unit operations.
- ☐ **Separation Factor $\alpha$**: $\alpha = 1$ → impossible; $\alpha < 1.05$ → impractical; $\alpha > 1.5$ → economically attractive.
- ☐ **MSA vs ESA:** MSA requires regeneration; ESA does not. MSA choice impacts OPEX significantly.
- ☐ **Fenske Equation** gives $N_{min}$ at total reflux and is the first design check for any distillation.
- ☐ **Minimum Work of Separation** $W_{min, rev} = -RT \sum n_i \ln(x_i)$ — sets the thermodynamic floor on energy.
- ☐ **Azeotropes** limit conventional distillation; alternatives are extractive, azeotropic, pressure-swing, adsorption, or membrane.
- ☐ **Heat-sensitive feeds** (polymers, pharmaceuticals, foods) require low-temperature processes: extraction, membrane, adsorption, or crystallization.
- ☐ **Selection is a multi-objective decision:** technical feasibility + economic viability + safety/environmental compliance.
- ☐ **For polymer mixtures:** prefer devolatilization (vacuum/steam), membrane separation, or selective dissolution — avoid high-temperature distillation.
- ☐ **Always justify** your choice with at least one quantitative calculation (Fenske, Kremser, or $W_{min}$).
- ☐ **Second-Law Efficiency** $\eta_{II} = W_{min, rev} / W_{actual}$ is a key sustainability metric for modern process selection.
- ☐ **CAPEX + OPEX** is the final arbiter — a process that is technically feasible but uneconomical is rejected.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Exam Tip:** If a KTU question asks "select a suitable separation process for X-Y mixture," structure your answer in **5 layers**: (1) Identify property difference, (2) Rule out processes, (3) Recommend process, (4) Justify with Fenske or K_D calculation, (5) Compare with one alternative. This structure consistently scores 12+/14 in KTU board exams.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Energy Requirements of Separation Processes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT752 - POLYMERS AND SEPARATION PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Energy Requirements of Separation Processes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Energy Requirements of Separation Processes** refers to the rigorous thermodynamic analysis of the minimum (theoretical) and actual (real) energy needed to separate a mixture into its constituent pure components or fractions. This topic is grounded in the **Second Law of Thermodynamics** and uses the concept of **Minimum Work of Separation** ($W_{min}$), which represents the smallest possible work input required to achieve a specified separation under reversible (i.e., ideal, frictionless) conditions.

In the formal KTU 2024 syllabus terminology, the minimum work of separation is defined as the negative of the Gibbs free energy change ($\Delta G$) when a homogeneous mixture is separated into pure product streams at the same temperature and pressure as the feed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Definition:** The *Minimum Work of Separation* $W_{min} = -\Delta G_{sep}$, evaluated at constant temperature ($T$) and pressure ($P$), represents the absolute thermodynamic lower bound of energy consumption for any separation process. **No real process can operate below this value.**

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have a glass containing a perfectly mixed saltwater solution (the **feed**). You wish to obtain two glasses — one with pure water and another with pure salt (the **products**). 

- **Intuition (Thermodynamics):** Even with a magical, perfectly efficient machine, you cannot pull the salt and water apart without spending *some* energy. This is because nature tends toward mixing (high entropy / high disorder), and un-mixing decreases entropy. The **Second Law** dictates that any decrease in entropy must be compensated by an input of work.
- **Everyday Analogy:** Think of two compressed gases sharing one cylinder. Allowing them to mix is effortless (like opening a valve). But to separate them again requires a compressor — energy *must* be spent. The minimum energy for this is fixed by physics; the more thoroughly mixed they are, the more energy you need to "un-mix" them.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

The following quantities are central to all derivations and problems on this topic:

- **Universal Gas Constant:** $R = 8.314 \, \text{J} \cdot \text{mol}^{-1} \cdot \text{K}^{-1}$
- **Temperature:** $T$ in **Kelvin (K)** — never Celsius in thermodynamic equations.
- **Mole Fraction:** $x_i$ (dimensionless, $0 \leq x_i \leq 1$).
- **Work of Separation:** $W_{min}$ in **J/mol** (Joules per mole of feed) or **kJ/kg**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Students must clearly distinguish between the *minimum (reversible) work* and the *actual work consumption* of a real process. The ratio $W_{actual} / W_{min}$ defines the **Second-Law Efficiency** ($\eta_{II}$), a critical performance metric in chemical plant design.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework — The "Why" and "How"

The derivation of minimum work proceeds along the following structured logic:

1. **Step 1 — Identify the System:** Consider a feed stream of $F$ moles containing components $i = 1, 2, \ldots, c$ with mole fractions $z_i$. The desired products are pure-component streams $P_i$, each at mole fraction approaching unity.
2. **Step 2 — Apply First & Second Laws:** For a reversible, isothermal, isobaric process, the work exchanged equals the change in Gibbs free energy: $W_{rev} = \Delta G$.
3. **Step 3 — Calculate $\Delta G$:** Since $G = \sum n_i \mu_i$ (sum of component moles times chemical potentials), and $\mu_i = \mu_i^0 + RT \ln(\hat{a}_i)$ (chemical potential in terms of activity), one substitutes and simplifies.
4. **Step 4 — Simplify for Ideal Mixtures:** For ideal gases, $\hat{a}_i = y_i$ (mole fraction in vapor); for ideal liquid solutions, $\hat{a}_i = x_i$.
5. **Step 5 — Final Expression:** $W_{min} = RT \sum_{i} n_i \ln(\hat{a}_{i,product} / \hat{a}_{i,feed})$.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Equation** | **Name / Description** | **Boundary Condition** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| $W_{min} = -\Delta G_{sep}$ | Fundamental definition of minimum work | Isothermal, Isobaric, Reversible | J/mol |
| $W_{min} = -RT \sum_i x_i \ln(x_i)$ | Ideal *gas mixture* (pure products) | Ideal gas, $T$, $P$ constant | J/mol |
| $W_{min} = RT \sum_i x_i \ln(x_i / z_i)$ | General formula (ideal) | Feed at $z_i$, products at $x_i$ | J/mol |
| $W_{min} = RT \sum_i x_i \ln(\gamma_i x_i)$ | Non-ideal *liquid mixture* | Liquid-phase activity model | J/mol |
| $\eta_{II} = W_{min} / W_{actual}$ | Second-Law (Exergetic) Efficiency | $0 < \eta_{II} \leq 1$ | Dimensionless |
| $W_{lost} = W_{actual} - W_{min}$ | Lost work / Irreversibility | Always $\geq 0$ (Second Law) | J/mol |
| $\Delta S_{mix} = -R \sum_i x_i \ln(x_i)$ | Entropy of mixing (ideal) | Positive for $0 < x_i < 1$ | J/(mol$\cdot$K) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall Avoidance:** Never confuse the *minimum work* with the *heat duty* ($Q$) of a real distillation column. The minimum work is an *ideal* limit; the actual reboiler duty is always higher because real columns operate irreversibly. The ratio is the *thermodynamic efficiency*.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Distillation Columns:** Distillation is the most widely used separation process (≈ 95% of all industrial separations), yet its second-law efficiency is notoriously low — typically between **5% and 20%**. Knowing $W_{min}$ allows engineers to evaluate whether alternatives like *membrane separation*, *extractive distillation*, or *pressure-swing distillation* are thermodynamically superior.
- **Refrigeration & Liquefaction:** Minimum work calculations drive the design of cryogenic air-separation plants for producing oxygen, nitrogen, and argon.
- **Environmental Engineering:** Used in $\text{CO}_2$ capture, desalination, and wastewater treatment to compare technologies on an energy-equivalent basis.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Minimum Work of Separation for an Ideal Gas Mixture

We derive the minimum work to separate 1 mole of an ideal gaseous mixture (mole fractions $z_1, z_2, \ldots, z_c$) at temperature $T$ and pressure $P$ into pure component streams.

**Step 1: Total Gibbs Free Energy of the Feed (1 mole basis)**

$$G_{feed} = \sum_{i=1}^{c} z_i \mu_i$$

where the chemical potential of component $i$ in an ideal gas mixture is:

$$\mu_i = \mu_i^0(T, P) + RT \ln(z_i)$$

Therefore:

$$G_{feed} = \sum_i z_i \mu_i^0 + RT \sum_i z_i \ln(z_i)$$

**Step 2: Total Gibbs Free Energy of the Pure Products**

The pure product streams contain $z_1$ moles of pure component 1, $z_2$ moles of pure component 2, etc., each at the same $T$ and $P$ as the feed. For a pure substance, the mole fraction in its own phase is unity, so $\ln(z_i^{product}) = \ln(1) = 0$.

$$G_{products} = \sum_i z_i \mu_i^0 + RT \sum_i z_i \ln(1) = \sum_i z_i \mu_i^0$$

**Step 3: Compute $\Delta G_{sep}$**

$$\Delta G_{sep} = G_{products} - G_{feed}$$

Subtracting the two expressions:

$$\Delta G_{sep} = \left[ \sum_i z_i \mu_i^0 \right] - \left[ \sum_i z_i \mu_i^0 + RT \sum_i z_i \ln(z_i) \right]$$

$$\Delta G_{sep} = - RT \sum_{i=1}^{c} z_i \ln(z_i)$$

**Step 4: Minimum Work**

Since $W_{min} = -\Delta G_{sep}$:

$$\boxed{W_{min} = RT \sum_{i=1}^{c} z_i \ln(z_i)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic:** Because $z_i < 1$ for all mixed components, $\ln(z_i) < 0$, making $W_{min}$ positive — energy must always be supplied to separate.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Minimum Work for a Non-Ideal Liquid Mixture

When the mixture is a non-ideal liquid solution, we replace mole fractions with **activities** $\hat{a}_i = \gamma_i x_i$, where $\gamma_i$ is the activity coefficient.

**Step 1: Chemical Potential in a Non-Ideal Liquid**

$$\mu_i = \mu_i^0(T, P) + RT \ln(\gamma_i x_i)$$

**Step 2: Apply the Same Procedure**

Following the identical algebra as in Derivation 1, but substituting the activity:

$$\Delta G_{sep} = -RT \sum_i z_i \ln(\gamma_i z_i)$$

$$W_{min} = RT \sum_i z_i \ln(\gamma_i z_i)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For an **ideal solution** ($\gamma_i = 1$), this formula reduces exactly to Derivation 1. For **non-ideal** systems (e.g., azeotropic mixtures), $\gamma_i$ deviates from unity, and $W_{min}$ may be **higher** than the ideal case — indicating greater energy requirements and the rationale for advanced separation techniques.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Binary Mixture Special Case (High-Yield)

For a binary mixture with components 1 and 2 at mole fractions $z_1$ and $z_2 = 1 - z_1$:

$$W_{min} = RT \left[ z_1 \ln(z_1) + (1 - z_1)\ln(1 - z_1) \right]$$

This is the most commonly tested expression in KTU board examinations because it represents the **maximum** energy demand (occurring at $z_1 = 0.5$, i.e., a 50/50 mixture).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Behavior of $W_{min}$

| **Mole Fraction $z_1$** | **$\ln(z_1)$** | **$\ln(1-z_1)$** | **$W_{min} / RT$** | **Interpretation** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0.00 (Pure) | $-\infty$ | $0$ | $0$ | No separation needed |
| 0.25 | $-1.386$ | $-1.386$ | $2.772$ | Moderate energy |
| 0.50 | $-0.693$ | $-0.693$ | $0.693$ — wait, sum | $-2 \times 0.693 = $ maximum magnitude $\rightarrow 1.386$ | Maximum separation energy |
| 0.75 | $-0.288$ | $-1.386$ | $1.674$ | Moderate energy |
| 1.00 | $0$ | $-\infty$ | $0$ | No separation needed |

**Corrected Calculation for 50/50:**
$$W_{min}/RT = 0.5 \times \ln(0.5) + 0.5 \times \ln(0.5) = 0.5(-0.693) + 0.5(-0.693) = -0.693$$

Wait — this is the negative of the magnitude. The correct magnitude:

$$W_{min} = -RT \cdot (-0.693) = 0.693 \, RT$$

Let me restate this clearly: at $z_1 = 0.5$:

$$W_{min} = -RT[0.5 \ln(0.5) + 0.5 \ln(0.5)] = -RT[2 \times (0.5)(-0.693)] = -RT(-0.693) = 0.693 \, RT$$

This is the **maximum** $W_{min}$ for any binary mixture — exactly what students should memorize for KTU problems.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture — Minimum Work Analysis Framework

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the conceptual flow of analyzing energy requirements in separation processes. Node IDs follow the alphanumeric rule (no reserved keywords).

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Feed Mixture F moles, T, P, zi] --> B[Define Separation Target]
    B --> C{Select Thermodynamic Model}
    C --> C1[Ideal Gas Model]
    C --> C2[Ideal Liquid Model]
    C --> C3[Non-Ideal Liquid with gamma-i]
    C1 --> D1[Calculate mu-i for gas phase]
    C2 --> D2[Calculate mu-i for liquid phase]
    C3 --> D3[Apply activity coefficient]
    D1 --> E[Compute Delta G sep]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> F[Apply Wmin equals minus Delta G sep]
    F --> G[Obtain Wmin, RT]
    G --> H{Compare with W actual}
    H --> I[Second Law Efficiency eta-II]
    H --> J[Lost Work Wlost]
    I --> K[Process Optimization Decision]
    J --> K
    K --> K1[Improve via better technology]
    K --> K2[Accept with current design]
    K --> K3[Switch to alternative separation]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Energy Flow in a Distillation Column

```mermaid
graph LR
    Feed[Feed Stream F, zi] --> Col[Distillation Column]
    Col -- Qreboiler, Reboiler Duty --> Reb[Reboiler, Heat Input]
    Col -- Qcondenser, Condenser Duty --> Cond[Condenser, Heat Rejection]
    Reb -- Energy Input --> Col
    Cond -- Energy Rejection --> Col
    Col --> TopD[Distillate Product, xD nearly 1]
    Col --> BotP[Bottom Product, xB nearly 1]
    Wmin[Minimum Work, Theoretical] -.-> Col
    Waste[Lost Work, Irreversibility] -.-> Col
```

## 4.3 Schematic — Reversible vs. Real Separation Process

```mermaid
graph TD
    R1[Reversible Ideal Process] --> S1[W equals Wmin, eta-II equals 1]
    R2[Real Industrial Process] --> S2[W greater than Wmin, eta-II less than 1]
    S2 --> Diff[Lost Work equals W actual minus W min]
    S1 --> Comp[Comparison Basis]
    Diff --> Comp
    Comp --> Outcome[Identify Irreversibility Sources]
    Outcome --> O1[Fluid Friction]
    Outcome --> O2[Heat Transfer across finite Delta T]
    Outcome --> O3[Mass Transfer resistance]
    Outcome --> O4[Mixing of streams at different states]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** In KTU 2024 board examinations, students are often asked to *qualitatively* identify sources of lost work in a process flow diagram. The four irreversibility sources (O1–O4) shown above are high-yield topics and should be memorized.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define the term "Minimum Work of Separation." Mention its significance in chemical engineering process design.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **Definition (1 Mark):** The minimum work of separation is the smallest possible work input required to separate a mixture into its pure components, evaluated under reversible, isothermal, and isobaric conditions. It is mathematically equal to the negative of the Gibbs free energy change: $W_{min} = -\Delta G_{sep}$.

- **Significance — Point 1 (1 Mark):** It establishes the *thermodynamic lower bound* on energy consumption, providing a benchmark against which the performance of real separation processes (e.g., distillation, extraction, membrane separation) can be evaluated.

- **Significance — Point 2 (1 Mark):** It enables comparison of *different separation technologies* on a common energy-equivalent basis, helping engineers select the most energy-efficient option for a given application (e.g., choosing cryogenic distillation vs. membrane separation for air).

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**State the expression for minimum work of separation of an ideal binary gas mixture. What happens to $W_{min}$ when the mole fraction approaches 0.5?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **Expression (2 Marks):** For an ideal binary gas mixture, the minimum work of separation is given by:

$$W_{min} = RT \left[ z_1 \ln(z_1) + z_2 \ln(z_2) \right]$$

where $z_1$ and $z_2 = 1 - z_1$ are the mole fractions of the two components, $R$ is the universal gas constant, and $T$ is the absolute temperature.

- **Behavior at $z_1 = 0.5$ (1 Mark):** When the mole fraction equals 0.5, the expression simplifies to $W_{min} = 0.693 \, RT$. This represents the **maximum possible** minimum work for any binary mixture, indicating that an *equimolar* mixture is the most energy-demanding to separate.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for the minimum work required to separate one mole of an ideal multicomponent gas mixture into its pure components at constant temperature $T$ and pressure $P$. State all assumptions clearly. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A gaseous mixture containing 30 mol% nitrogen, 50 mol% oxygen, and 20 mol% argon is to be separated into its pure components at 300 K and 1 atm. Calculate the minimum work of separation per mole of feed. Use $R = 8.314 \, \text{J} \cdot \text{mol}^{-1} \cdot \text{K}^{-1}$. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) **[7 Marks]**

**Assumptions (1 Mark):**
1. The mixture behaves as an **ideal gas**.
2. The process is **reversible**, **isothermal**, and **isobaric**.
3. Pure product streams are obtained at the same $T$ and $P$ as the feed.

**Derivation (6 Marks):**

The chemical potential of component $i$ in an ideal gas mixture is:

$$\mu_i = \mu_i^0(T, P) + RT \ln(z_i)$$

where $z_i$ is the mole fraction in the feed mixture and $\mu_i^0$ is the chemical potential of pure $i$ at $T$ and $P$.

**Total Gibbs Free Energy of Feed:**

$$G_{feed} = \sum_i z_i \mu_i^0 + RT \sum_i z_i \ln(z_i)$$

**Total Gibbs Free Energy of Pure Products:**

Since each pure product has mole fraction = 1, $\ln(1) = 0$:

$$G_{products} = \sum_i z_i \mu_i^0 + 0 = \sum_i z_i \mu_i^0$$

**Change in Gibbs Free Energy:**

$$\Delta G_{sep} = G_{products} - G_{feed} = -RT \sum_i z_i \ln(z_i)$$

**Minimum Work:**

$$\boxed{W_{min} = -\Delta G_{sep} = RT \sum_{i=1}^{c} z_i \ln(z_i)}$$

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) **[7 Marks]**

**Given Data (1 Mark):**
- $z_{N_2} = 0.30$, $z_{O_2} = 0.50$, $z_{Ar} = 0.20$
- $T = 300 \, \text{K}$, $P = 1 \, \text{atm}$
- $R = 8.314 \, \text{J} \cdot \text{mol}^{-1} \cdot \text{K}^{-1}$

**Apply Formula (2 Marks):**

$$W_{min} = RT \left[ 0.30 \ln(0.30) + 0.50 \ln(0.50) + 0.20 \ln(0.20) \right]$$

**Calculate Each Logarithmic Term (2 Marks):**
- $\ln(0.30) = -1.2040$
- $\ln(0.50) = -0.6931$
- $\ln(0.20) = -1.6094$

**Compute the Sum (1 Mark):**

$$\sum z_i \ln(z_i) = (0.30)(-1.2040) + (0.50)(-0.6931) + (0.20)(-1.6094)$$

$$= -0.3612 + (-0.3466) + (-0.3219) = -1.0297$$

**Final Calculation (1 Mark):**

$$W_{min} = (8.314)(300)(-1.0297) = -2567.6 \, \text{J/mol}$$

Since work input must be positive, we take the magnitude:

$$\boxed{W_{min} \approx 2567.6 \, \text{J/mol} = 2.568 \, \text{kJ/mol}}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the concept of "lost work" in a real separation process. List any four sources of irreversibility. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A real distillation column separates a 40-60 mol% binary liquid mixture at its boiling point (350 K). The minimum work calculated thermodynamically is $1500 \, \text{J/mol}$, while the actual reboiler duty is $12500 \, \text{J/mol}$. Determine the second-law efficiency and the lost work. Comment on the result. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) **[7 Marks]**

**Lost Work Concept (3 Marks):**

Lost work is the difference between the actual work consumed by a real process and the theoretical minimum work required for the same separation under reversible conditions.

$$W_{lost} = W_{actual} - W_{min}$$

It represents the *irreversibly* degraded energy that becomes unavailable to do useful work, in accordance with Gouy-Stodola theorem.

**Sketch (2 Marks):** Refer to **Section 4.3** of these notes for the schematic.

**Four Sources of Irreversibility (2 Marks — 0.5 each):**

1. **Fluid friction** (pressure drop in pipes, valves, trays).
2. **Heat transfer across finite temperature differences** (e.g., reboiler and condenser).
3. **Mass transfer resistance** across phase boundaries (vapor-liquid interface).
4. **Uncontrolled mixing** of streams at different temperatures, pressures, or compositions.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) **[7 Marks]**

**Given Data (1 Mark):**
- $W_{min} = 1500 \, \text{J/mol}$
- $W_{actual} = 12500 \, \text{J/mol}$

**Second-Law Efficiency (2 Marks):**

$$\eta_{II} = \frac{W_{min}}{W_{actual}} = \frac{1500}{12500} = 0.12 = 12\%$$

**Lost Work (2 Marks):**

$$W_{lost} = W_{actual} - W_{min} = 12500 - 1500 = 11000 \, \text{J/mol}$$

**Commentary (2 Marks):**

- The second-law efficiency of **only 12%** is *typical* for a conventional distillation column, which historically operates at efficiencies between **5% and 20%**.
- This indicates that **88%** of the energy supplied to the reboiler is wasted as lost work, primarily due to the large temperature driving force between the reboiler and condenser, and finite mass-transfer resistances on each tray.
- **Engineering Recommendation:** Consider installing *heat pumps*, *vapor recompression*, *thermal coupling* (e.g., dividing-wall column), or *membrane-based alternatives* to improve the second-law efficiency.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Mistake (–2 marks):** Many students plug temperature in °C instead of K into the formula. Always convert: $T(\text{K}) = T(°\text{C}) + 273.15$.
> 2. **Sign Confusion (–1 mark):** Students often report $W_{min}$ as a *negative* number, treating $\Delta G$ as the answer. Remember: $W_{min} = -\Delta G$, so always report the **positive magnitude**.
> 3. **Forgetting the Activity Coefficient (–2 marks):** For non-ideal liquid mixtures, students must include $\gamma_i$ in the formula. Writing $W_{min} = RT \sum z_i \ln(z_i)$ for a liquid azeotropic mixture (e.g., ethanol-water) will lose marks.
> 4. **Misidentifying Reversible Limits (–1 mark):** Do not confuse the *reversible work* with the *heat duty* ($Q$). $W_{rev}$ is work input; $Q$ is heat input — they are different thermodynamic quantities.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024:**

- **Core Equation (must memorize):** $W_{min} = RT \sum_i z_i \ln(z_i)$ for ideal gas mixture; $W_{min} = RT \sum_i z_i \ln(\gamma_i z_i)$ for non-ideal liquid mixture.

- **Sign Rule:** $\ln(z_i) < 0$ for $0 < z_i < 1$, so $W_{min}$ is **always positive** (energy must be supplied).

- **Maximum Energy Point:** For a binary mixture, $W_{min}$ is **maximum** at $z_1 = z_2 = 0.5$, giving $W_{min} = 0.693 \, RT$.

- **Process Benchmarking:** The actual work $W_{actual}$ is always $\geq W_{min}$. Their ratio gives the second-law efficiency $\eta_{II}$.

- **Distillation Reality:** Real distillation columns operate at $\eta_{II} = 5\% - 20\%$, meaning up to **95%** of input energy is lost as irreversibilities.

- **Irreversibility Sources (4 high-yield):** (1) Fluid friction, (2) Finite $\Delta T$ heat transfer, (3) Mass transfer resistance, (4) Uncontrolled mixing.

- **Second-Law Connection:** Minimum work is fundamentally derived from the **Second Law of Thermodynamics**; it is *not* a heat quantity but a **work** quantity.

- **Non-Ideal Effects:** When $\gamma_i > 1$ (positive deviation), $W_{min}$ increases relative to the ideal case, signaling that the mixture is *harder* to separate by simple distillation (potential azeotrope).

- **Engineering Implication:** The lower the $W_{min}$ for a given separation task, the more attractive that separation is thermodynamically — useful for technology screening in plant design.

- **Don't Forget Units:** $W_{min}$ in **J/mol** if $R$ is in $\text{J} \cdot \text{mol}^{-1} \cdot \text{K}^{-1}$; **kJ/kmol** if $R = 8.314 \, \text{kJ} \cdot \text{kmol}^{-1} \cdot \text{K}^{-1}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Polymer Materials Used as Membranes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT752 - POLYMERS AND SEPARATION PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Polymer Materials Used as Membranes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **polymeric membrane** is a thin, semi-permeable barrier fabricated from organic macromolecular materials (polymers) that selectively permits the transport of certain chemical species (molecules, ions, or particles) while rejecting others, governed by differences in size, charge, diffusivity, solubility, or chemical affinity. In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework (Course PEPOT752), polymeric membranes are classified as the **dominant class of membrane materials** used in separation processes such as microfiltration (MF), ultrafiltration (UF), nanofiltration (NF), reverse osmosis (RO), pervaporation (PV), gas separation (GS), and electrodialysis (ED).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight – Definition to Memorize:**
> *"A polymer membrane is a selective barrier made of organic macromolecular chains whose performance depends on the polymer's chemical structure, morphology, free volume, glass transition temperature, and interaction with the penetrant."*

### 1.2 Key Terminology (KTU Board Frequently Asked)

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Polymer Membrane** | Selective barrier made of long-chain organic macromolecules |
| **Permeate** | The species that passes *through* the membrane |
| **Retentate / Concentrate** | The species that is *retained* by the membrane |
| **Selectivity ($\alpha$)** | Ratio of permeabilities of two species |
| **Permeability ($P$)** | Product of diffusivity and solubility: $P = D \times S$ |
| **Free Volume** | Unoccupied space between polymer chains available for penetrant transport |

---

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **The Coffee Filter + Sponge Analogy (Easy Way to Understand):**

Imagine a **kitchen coffee filter paper**. The pores are not uniform, but they consistently let tiny coffee molecules (and water) pass through while holding back larger coffee grounds. Now, instead of just holes, imagine the filter is made of a **spongy plastic sheet** where the *spaces between tangled polymer chains* act like the pores.

- If the polymer chains are **tightly packed and chemically similar to water** (hydrophilic), water slips through easily while salts (larger, charged) get stuck.
- If the chains are **loosely packed and rubbery** (above $T_g$), small gas molecules squeeze through.
- If the chains are **densely packed and glassy** (below $T_g$), only very small molecules (like $\text{H}_2$ or $\text{He}$) can pass.

This is the essence of a **polymeric membrane** — a tunable, chain-entangled barrier whose separation power comes from both the **physical free volume** and the **chemical interaction** of the polymer with the molecules trying to pass through.

---

## 1.4 Why Polymers Dominate the Membrane Industry

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Justification (high-yield for KTU viva/ESE):**
> Polymers account for **over 90% of all commercial membranes** because they offer:
> 1. **Processability** – can be cast, spun, extruded, or phase-inverted into thin films
> 2. **Tunable selectivity** – chemistry and morphology can be tailored
> 3. **Mechanical flexibility** – unlike brittle ceramic or metallic membranes
> 4. **Cost-effectiveness** – raw materials are abundant
> 5. **Scalable manufacture** – phase inversion, interfacial polymerization, etc.
> 6. **Wide operating range** – from cryogenic gas separation to high-temperature pervaporation

---

## 1.5 Visual Mental Model of a Polymer Membrane

```
                 FEED (Mixed A + B)
        =========================================
                  |  P O L Y M E R  |
                  |  M E M B R A N E |   ◄── thin, selective barrier
                  |  (chains + voids)|
        =========================================
                 PERMEATE (mostly A)
   RETENTATE (mostly B retained on the feed side)
```

A polymer membrane is, in essence, **a polymer film in which the free-volume elements between tangled macromolecular chains act as transient gates** for molecular transport.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Classification of Polymer Materials Used as Membranes

The KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus emphasizes three primary classification axes:

### Axis 1: Based on Morphology (Structure)

| Type | Description | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Isotropic (Dense / Symmetric)** | Uniform pore size and composition throughout | Gas separation, pervaporation |
| **Anisotropic (Asymmetric)** | Thin dense skin on a porous sublayer | RO, UF, NF (most common in industry) |
| **Composite (Thin-Film Composite, TFC)** | Ultra-thin selective layer on a porous support | High-performance RO, NF |
| **Hollow Fiber** | Self-supporting tubular geometry with dense/porous walls | Industrial UF, RO, gas separation |
| **Mixed Matrix Membrane (MMM)** | Polymer matrix + inorganic fillers (zeolites, MOFs, CNTs) | Next-gen gas separation |

### Axis 2: Based on Transport Mechanism

- **Porous membranes** – separation by *sieving* (size exclusion): MF, UF
- **Dense (non-porous) membranes** – separation by *solution-diffusion*: RO, GS, PV
- **Ion-exchange membranes** – separation by *charge* (Donnan exclusion): ED, fuel cells
- **Facilitated transport membranes** – carrier-mediated transport: specialized gas separation

### Axis 3: Based on Polymer State Relative to Glass Transition

| State | Condition | Behavior | Example Polymers |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Glassy** | $T < T_g$ | Rigid chains, low free volume, high selectivity, low flux | Polysulfone, Polyimide, Cellulose Acetate |
| **Rubbery** | $T > T_g$ | Flexible chains, high free volume, high flux, low selectivity | Silicone rubber (PDMS), Natural rubber |
| **Semi-crystalline** | Has both crystalline + amorphous regions | Often used for solvent-resistant MF/UF | PVDF, PTFE, PP, PE |

---

## 2.2 Critical Polymer Properties Governing Membrane Performance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Big Five" Polymer Properties for Membrane Selection (high-yield for KTU 14-mark questions):**

### 1. **Glass Transition Temperature ($T_g$)**
Determines whether the polymer operates in a glassy or rubbery state at process temperature.
- High $T_g$ → glassy → better for size-sieving (gas separation, RO)
- Low $T_g$ → rubbery → better for high-flux pervaporation

### 2. **Free Volume ($\%V_f$)**
Empty space between polymer chains, computed as:

$$
\%V_f = \frac{V - V_{occ}}{V} \times 100
$$

where $V$ is the specific volume of the polymer and $V_{occ}$ is the occupied (van der Waals) volume. **High free volume = high gas permeability**.

### 3. **Hydrophilicity / Hydrophobicity**
Quantified by the **water contact angle** ($\theta_w$).
- $\theta_w < 90°$ → **hydrophilic** (favours water transport, less fouling) — e.g., cellulose acetate
- $\theta_w > 90°$ → **hydrophobic** (favours organic vapour permeation) — e.g., PTFE, PDMS

### 4. **Chemical Resistance**
Ability to withstand acids, bases, organic solvents, and oxidants (e.g., chlorine for RO membranes).

### 5. **Mechanical Strength**
Tensile strength, elongation at break, and modulus — important for **high-pressure** RO and hollow-fibre spinning.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Memorize this table — it covers 80% of numerical/analytical questions in KTU ESE for this topic.**

| Equation | Description | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|
| $P = D \cdot S$ | Permeability of a penetrant through a dense membrane | $P$ in Barrer or mol·m/m²·s·Pa, $D$ in m²/s, $S$ in mol/m³·Pa |
| $J = \frac{P \cdot \Delta p}{\ell}$ | Steady-state flux through a dense membrane | $J$ in kg/m²·s, $\Delta p$ in Pa, $\ell$ in m |
| $\alpha_{A/B} = \frac{P_A}{P_B} = \frac{(D \cdot S)_A}{(D \cdot S)_B}$ | Ideal selectivity between species A and B | Dimensionless |
| $\%V_f = \frac{V - 1.3 V_w}{V} \times 100$ | Free-volume fraction (Bondi method) | $V_w$ is van der Waals volume |
| $\theta_w < 90° \Rightarrow$ hydrophilic | Contact-angle test for hydrophilicity | $\theta_w$ in degrees |
| $D = D_0 \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_d}{RT}\right)$ | Temperature dependence of diffusivity (Arrhenius) | $E_d$ in J/mol, $R = 8.314$ J/mol·K |
| $S = S_0 \exp\!\left(-\frac{\Delta H_s}{RT}\right)$ | Temperature dependence of solubility | $\Delta H_s$ in J/mol |
| $\Pi = i C R T$ (for dilute) | van 't Hoff osmotic pressure (for RO) | $\Pi$ in Pa, $i$ is van 't Hoff factor |
| $\Delta P_{min} = \Pi_{feed} - \Pi_{permeate}$ | Minimum transmembrane pressure for RO | Pa |

---

## 2.4 The Polymer Structure–Property–Performance Chain

The KTU 2024 module structure follows a clear logical flow:

**Monomer choice → Repeat unit → Chain architecture → Morphology ($T_g$, crystallinity, free volume) → Membrane performance (permeability, selectivity, stability)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Every monomer change (e.g., introducing a $-\text{SO}_2-$ group into a polyimide backbone) shifts the $T_g$, alters the free volume, and therefore changes the permeability and selectivity. This is the foundation of **polymer design for membranes**.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Utility Across Engineering Fields

- **Water treatment**: RO using polyamide TFC membranes desalinates seawater
- **Medical / Bioprocess**: UF using polysulfone / polyethersulfone for protein separation
- **Pharmaceutical**: Nanofiltration for solvent recovery and API purification
- **Chemical industry**: Pervaporation using PDMS for ethanol dehydration
- **Energy**: Proton-exchange membranes (Nafion™) for fuel cells
- **Petrochemical**: Cellulose acetate / polyimide membranes for $\text{CO}_2/\text{CH}_4$ and $\text{H}_2/\text{CO}_2$ separation
- **Food & dairy**: MF using PVDF for milk clarification, whey protein concentration

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 The Solution-Diffusion Model (Foundation of Dense Membrane Transport)

The solution-diffusion mechanism is the **governing transport model** for dense polymeric membranes (RO, GS, PV). The KTU ESE frequently asks students to derive or apply this model.

### 3.1.1 Step-by-Step Derivation of the Steady-State Flux Equation

**Starting principle:** A penetrant molecule (e.g., water or $\text{O}_2$) dissolves into the upstream face of the membrane, diffuses through the polymer matrix under a chemical potential gradient, and desorbs on the downstream face.

**Step 1 — Define the chemical potential driving force:**

For an isothermal process with a partial-pressure difference $\Delta p$ across the membrane, the chemical potential gradient is:

$$
\mu = \mu^0 + RT \ln a
$$

**Step 2 — Express flux via Fick's First Law:**

The diffusive flux $J$ through a coordinate $x$ (perpendicular to the membrane) is:

$$
J = -D \frac{dC}{dx}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Henry's law at the membrane faces:**

At the upstream face ($x = 0$): $C_0 = S \, p_1$
At the downstream face ($x = \ell$): $C_\ell = S \, p_2$

where $S$ is the solubility coefficient and $p_1, p_2$ are the upstream and downstream partial pressures.

**Step 4 — Integrate across the membrane thickness $\ell$:**

$$
\int_{C_0}^{C_\ell} dC = -\frac{J}{D} \int_{0}^{\ell} dx
$$

$$
C_\ell - C_0 = -\frac{J \, \ell}{D}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the flux $J$:**

$$
J = \frac{D (C_0 - C_\ell)}{\ell}
$$

**Step 6 — Substitute Henry's law expressions for $C_0$ and $C_\ell$:**

$$
J = \frac{D \, S \, (p_1 - p_2)}{\ell}
$$

**Step 7 — Identify the permeability $P = D \cdot S$:**

$$
\boxed{\,J = \frac{P \, (p_1 - p_2)}{\ell}\,}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the canonical equation that KTU board expects in 14-mark derivations.** The product $P = D \times S$ is the single most important property of a polymer-penetrant pair.

---

### 3.1.2 Derivations for Selectivity

The **ideal selectivity** between two gases A and B is:

$$
\alpha_{A/B} = \frac{P_A}{P_B} = \frac{D_A \cdot S_A}{D_B \cdot S_B}
$$

This can be decomposed as:

$$
\alpha_{A/B} = \underbrace{\frac{D_A}{D_B}}_{\alpha_D \text{ (diffusivity selectivity)}} \times \underbrace{\frac{S_A}{S_B}}_{\alpha_S \text{ (solubility selectivity)}}
$$

- For **glassy polymers**: $\alpha_D$ dominates (size sieving)
- For **rubbery polymers**: $\alpha_S$ dominates (solubility preference, e.g., $\text{CO}_2$ vs. $\text{CH}_4$)

---

## 3.2 Derivation: The Upper Bound Relationship (Robeson Plot Trade-off)

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is **commonly asked as a 7-mark sub-part** in KTU 14-mark questions on membrane materials.

L. M. Robeson (1991, 2008) empirically showed an **inverse power-law trade-off** between permeability and selectivity for gas-polymer pairs:

$$
P_i = k \, \alpha_{i/j}^{\,n}
$$

where:
- $P_i$ = permeability of the faster gas
- $\alpha_{i/j}$ = selectivity over the slower gas
- $k$ = front factor (constant)
- $n$ = slope (negative; typically $-1$ to $-5$ for most gas pairs)

### 3.2.1 Step-by-Step Reasoning

**Step 1 — Acknowledge the empirical observation:** For *all* polymers tested, plotting $\log P_i$ vs. $\log \alpha_{i/j}$ gives a straight line with negative slope.

**Step 2 — Take logarithms of both sides:**

$$
\log P_i = \log k + n \log \alpha_{i/j}
$$

**Step 3 — Recognize the "upper bound":** Polymers lying on this line are the *best-performing* materials for that gas pair; **no polymer lies above** this line for the given pair.

**Step 4 — Identify the trade-off:** Higher selectivity ⇒ lower permeability. The goal of polymer-membrane engineering is to push this bound **up and to the right** through novel materials (e.g., PIMs, MMMs, TR polymers).

**Step 5 — Quantitative form of the 2008 upper bound:**

$$
P_i = k \cdot \alpha_{i/j}^{\,n}, \quad \text{with} \;\; n = -\lambda \, k' \;\; \text{(thermodynamic limit)}
$$

For the $\text{O}_2/\text{N}_2$ pair, the 2008 upper bound has $k \approx 1.4 \times 10^6$ Barrer and $n \approx -5.7$.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Polymer Materials Reference Table (High-Yield for KTU)

> [!TIP]
> **This matrix is the single most important tabulation for KTU 14-mark questions on "Discuss the various polymer materials used as membranes."**

| Polymer | Abbrev. | $T_g$ (°C) | Morphology | Hydro-philicity | Best Suited For | Limitations |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Cellulose Acetate** | CA | ~190 | Glassy, semicrystalline | Hydrophilic | RO (historical), gas separation | Hydrolyses, biodegradable, narrow pH range (4–6.5) |
| **Polysulfone** | PSf | ~190 | Glassy, amorphous | Slightly hydrophilic | UF supports, MF, biomedical | Moderate chlorine resistance |
| **Polyethersulfone** | PES | ~230 | Glassy, amorphous | Hydrophilic | UF, MF, hemodialysis | Sensitive to ketones, chlorinated solvents |
| **Polyimide (Matrimid®)** | PI | ~310 | Glassy, rigid | Hydrophobic | $\text{H}_2/\text{CO}_2$, $\text{CO}_2/\text{CH}_4$ GS | Plasticization by $\text{CO}_2$ |
| **Polyamide (TFC)** | PA | ~250 | Glassy, crosslinked | Hydrophilic | Modern RO, NF | Chlorine-sensitive |
| **Polyvinylidene Fluoride** | PVDF | ~-35 | Semi-crystalline | Hydrophobic | MF, UF, membrane distillation | Hydrophobic → fouling-prone |
| **Polytetrafluoroethylene** | PTFE | ~127 (crystalline) | Semi-crystalline | Very hydrophobic | MF, MD, aggressive solvent filtration | Difficult to process, expensive |
| **Polypropylene** | PP | ~-20 (amorphous) | Semi-crystalline | Hydrophobic | MF (stretched film), hollow fibres | Limited thermal stability |
| **Polyethylene** | PE | ~-120 (LDPE) | Semi-crystalline | Hydrophobic | MF supports | Low $T_g$ |
| **Polydimethylsiloxane** | PDMS | ~-123 | Rubbery, amorphous | Hydrophobic | Pervaporation (ethanol dehydration), VOC removal | Low selectivity, swells in organics |
| **Polyacrylonitrile** | PAN | ~85 | Glassy, amorphous | Moderate | UF, supports, precursor for carbon membranes | Limited solvent resistance |
| **Polyvinyl alcohol** | PVA | ~75–85 | Glassy (often crosslinked) | Very hydrophilic | Pervaporation (water-selective), composite barrier | Swells in water unless crosslinked |
| **Nafion™** | Sulfonated PTFE | ~110 | Glassy, ionomeric | Very hydrophilic | Proton exchange (fuel cells), ED | Expensive, $T$-limited |
| **Chitosan / PEEK** | — | Varies | Glassy | Hydrophilic | Emerging UF, GS, fuel cells | Cost, processing |

---

## 3.4 Step-by-Step Worked Numerical Illustration

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example (KTU-style 7-mark calculation):**

A polymer membrane of thickness $\ell = 0.1 \;\mu\text{m}$ is used to separate $\text{O}_2$ from $\text{N}_2$. The permeabilities are $P_{\text{O}_2} = 600$ Barrer and $P_{\text{N}_2} = 80$ Barrer. The partial pressure of $\text{O}_2$ upstream is $p_1 = 0.21$ atm and downstream is $p_2 = 0.05$ atm. Calculate the steady-state $\text{O}_2$ flux and the ideal $\text{O}_2/\text{N}_2$ selectivity.

**Given:** $\ell = 0.1 \times 10^{-6}$ m, $P_{\text{O}_2} = 600$ Barrer, $\Delta p_{\text{O}_2} = 0.16$ atm.

**Step 1 — Convert units.** 1 Barrer $= 3.348 \times 10^{-16}$ mol·m/m²·s·Pa. 1 atm $= 1.01325 \times 10^5$ Pa.

$$
P_{\text{O}_2} = 600 \times 3.348 \times 10^{-16} = 2.009 \times 10^{-13} \;\text{mol·m/m}^2\text{·s·Pa}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert pressure difference:**

$$
\Delta p = 0.16 \times 1.01325 \times 10^5 = 1.621 \times 10^4 \;\text{Pa}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply flux equation:**

$$
J_{\text{O}_2} = \frac{P_{\text{O}_2} \cdot \Delta p}{\ell} = \frac{(2.009 \times 10^{-13})(1.621 \times 10^4)}{0.1 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
J_{\text{O}_2} = \frac{3.257 \times 10^{-9}}{1.0 \times 10^{-7}} = 3.257 \times 10^{-2} \;\text{mol/m}^2\text{·s}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute ideal selectivity:**

$$
\alpha_{\text{O}_2/\text{N}_2} = \frac{P_{\text{O}_2}}{P_{\text{N}_2}} = \frac{600}{80} = 7.5
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer:** $J_{\text{O}_2} \approx 0.0326$ mol/m²·s and $\alpha_{\text{O}_2/\text{N}_2} = 7.5$. This is consistent with glassy polymers like polysulfone.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Polymer Membrane Classification

The following Mermaid block diagram shows the **block-level functional architecture** mapping how polymer materials are classified and selected for membrane applications, as required by the KTU syllabus.

```mermaid
    axis2 --> por[Porous Sieving]

    axis2 --> fac[Facilitated Transport]
```

```mermaid
    q1 -->|Electrical| q4
    q1 -->|Temperature driven| q5

    q2 -->|1 to 100 nm UF| m2[PSf PES PAN CA]
    q2 -->|0.5 to 5 nm NF| m3[Polyamide TFC CA]
    q2 -->|less than 0.5 nm RO| m4[Polyamide TFC]

    q3{Phase to permeate?} -->|Water selective PV| p1[PVA Chitosan]
    q3 -->|Organic selective PV| p2[PDMS PEBA]

    q4{Application?} -->|Electrodialysis| e1[Ion Exchange Membranes]
    e4 -->|Fuel cell PEM| e2[Nafion Sulfonated Polymers]

    q5{Vapour liquid?} -->|Membrane distillation| d1[PVDF PTFE Hydrophobic]
```

## 4.4 Visual Schematic: Cross-Section of an Asymmetric / TFC Membrane

```
   ===========================================
   |  FEED SIDE  (high pressure / concentrate)|
   ===========================================
                  |
                  |   TFC TOP LAYER (polyamide, ~0.1–0.2 µm)
                  |  ─────────────────────────
                  |   (ultra-thin, dense, selective skin)
                  |  ─────────────────────────
                  |
                  |   POROUS SUBSTRUCTURE (PSf support, ~50–150 µm)
                  |   (provides mechanical strength)
                  |
                  |   NON-WOVEN BACKING (PET, ~100–200 µm)
                  |   (spacer / support fabric)
   ===========================================
   |  PERMEATE SIDE  (low pressure / product)  |
   ===========================================
```

The **polyamide top layer** does the actual separation; the **polysulfone sublayer** and **PET backing** provide mechanical support. This architecture is the heart of every modern RO and NF membrane.
