# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model Paper)]**
*Define the term "polymer membrane" and list the four key polymer properties that govern its separation performance.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A polymer membrane is a thin, semi-permeable barrier fabricated from organic macromolecular materials that selectively allows certain chemical species to pass through while rejecting others, based on differences in size, charge, solubility, or diffusivity (1 mark).

The four key polymer properties are:
1. **Glass transition temperature ($T_g$)** — determines glassy vs. rubbery state (0.5)
2. **Free volume / morphology** — controls permeability (0.5)
3. **Hydrophilicity / hydrophobicity** (contact angle) — controls selectivity towards water vs. organics (0.5)
4. **Chemical resistance and mechanical strength** — controls durability under operating conditions (0.5)

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model Paper)]**
*Distinguish between glassy and rubbery polymers with two examples of each, in the context of membrane applications.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Glassy Polymer | Rubbery Polymer |
|---|---|---|
| Operating state | $T < T_g$ | $T > T_g$ |
| Chain mobility | Restricted, rigid | High, flexible |
| Free volume | Low, distributed | High, dynamic |
| Transport mechanism | Size-sieving (diffusivity selectivity dominates) | Solubility selectivity dominates |
| Examples | Polysulfone, Cellulose acetate, Polyimide | PDMS, Natural rubber, Polyisoprene |

Applications: Glassy → RO, gas separation, NF (1 mark). Rubbery → Pervaporation, vapour separation (1 mark). Distinguishing table content (1 mark).

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Module 1, Past Pattern]**

**(a)** Derive the steady-state flux equation $J = \dfrac{P \cdot \Delta p}{\ell}$ for a penetrant diffusing through a dense polymeric membrane using the solution-diffusion model. Clearly state the assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a suitable table, discuss the structure, properties, and membrane applications of at least **six** important polymer materials used in membrane separations. Compare cellulose acetate and polyamide as RO membrane materials. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A (a) — 7 marks

**Assumptions of the solution-diffusion model (2 marks):**
1. The membrane is **dense and non-porous**.
2. The system is at **steady state** and **isothermal**.
3. The permeant dissolves at the upstream face and desorbs at the downstream face.
4. Equilibrium at both membrane surfaces is described by **Henry's law**: $C = S \cdot p$.
5. The driving force is the **chemical potential gradient** (here expressed as a partial-pressure difference).

**Derivation (5 marks):**

**Step 1 — Fick's first law of diffusion:**

$$
J = -D \, \frac{dC}{dx}
$$

**Step 2 — At the upstream face ($x = 0$):** by Henry's law,

$$
C_0 = S \, p_1
$$

**Step 3 — At the downstream face ($x = \ell$):** by Henry's law,

$$
C_\ell = S \, p_2
$$

**Step 4 — Integrate across the membrane thickness:**

$$
\int_{C_0}^{C_\ell} dC = -\frac{J}{D} \int_{0}^{\ell} dx
$$

$$
C_\ell - C_0 = -\frac{J \, \ell}{D}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $J$:**

$$
J = \frac{D \, (C_0 - C_\ell)}{\ell}
$$

**Step 6 — Substitute Henry's law expressions:**

$$
J = \frac{D \cdot S \cdot (p_1 - p_2)}{\ell}
$$

**Step 7 — Define permeability $P = D \cdot S$:**

$$
\boxed{\,J = \frac{P \, (p_1 - p_2)}{\ell}\,}
$$

where $J$ = steady-state flux (mol/m²·s or kg/m²·s), $P$ = permeability (mol·m/m²·s·Pa), $p_1 - p_2$ = partial-pressure difference across the membrane (Pa), and $\ell$ = membrane thickness (m).

---

### Model Solution for Question A (b) — 7 marks

**Tabulation of six polymer materials (4 marks):**

| Polymer | $T_g$ (°C) | Structure | Hydrophilicity | Best Membrane Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Cellulose Acetate (CA)** | ~190 | Glassy, semi-crystalline | Hydrophilic | RO (older plants), gas separation |
| **Polysulfone (PSf)** | ~190 | Glassy, amorphous | Slightly hydrophilic | UF supports, biomedical |
| **Polyamide (PA / TFC)** | ~250 | Glassy, crosslinked | Hydrophilic | Modern RO, NF |
| **Polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS)** | ~-123 | Rubbery, amorphous | Hydrophobic | Pervaporation, VOC removal |
| **Polyvinylidene Fluoride (PVDF)** | ~-35 | Semi-crystalline | Hydrophobic | MF, UF, membrane distillation |
| **Polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE)** | ~127 (crystalline) | Semi-crystalline | Very hydrophobic | Aggressive solvent MF, MD |

**Comparison of CA vs. PA as RO membrane materials (3 marks):**

| Property | Cellulose Acetate (CA) | Polyamide (TFC) |
|---|---|---|
| Hydrophilicity | High | High |
| pH tolerance | Narrow (4–6.5) | Wide (2–11) |
| Chlorine tolerance | Poor (degrades) | Moderate (some tolerance) |
| Salt rejection | ~95–98% | >99.5% |
| Flux | Moderate | High |
| Biofouling | Lower (hydrophilic) | Higher |
| Temperature limit | ~30 °C | ~45 °C |
| Compaction resistance | Poor | Excellent |
| Cost | Low | Higher |
| Longevity | Short (2–3 years) | Long (5–7 years) |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Polyamide TFC membranes have largely replaced CA in modern RO plants because of their higher rejection, better chemical stability, and longer lifetime, despite being more chlorine-sensitive.

---

### Question B (Alternative — 14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Module 1, Past Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the **Robeson upper-bound relationship** between permeability and selectivity for gas-separation polymer membranes. Discuss the factors that limit the performance of conventional polymers and how novel polymer materials (e.g., **PIMs**, **mixed-matrix membranes**) attempt to push this bound. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A dense polyimide membrane of thickness $0.2\;\mu\text{m}$ is used to separate $\text{CO}_2$ from $\text{CH}_4$. The permeabilities are $P_{\text{CO}_2} = 9.5$ Barrer and $P_{\text{CH}_4} = 0.25$ Barrer. The upstream partial pressures are $p_{\text{CO}_2,1} = 4$ atm, $p_{\text{CH}_4,1} = 16$ atm; the downstream partial pressures are $p_{\text{CO}_2,2} = 0.1$ atm, $p_{\text{CH}_4,2} = 0.4$ atm. Calculate:
  (i) the steady-state flux of $\text{CO}_2$ and $\text{CH}_4$ in mol/m²·s
  (ii) the ideal $\text{CO}_2/\text{CH}_4$ selectivity
  (iii) the *actual* selectivity using the partial-pressure driving forces. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B (a) — 7 marks

**Robeson Upper Bound (3 marks):**

L. M. Robeson (1991, 2008) empirically established that for any gas pair A/B, plotting $\log P_A$ versus $\log \alpha_{A/B}$ for all known polymers gives a **linear upper bound** described by:

$$
P_A = k \cdot \alpha_{A/B}^{\,n}
$$

where $k$ is the front factor and $n$ is the negative slope. The 2008 analysis placed this bound on a firm thermodynamic basis (Freeman, 1999), with the slope determined by the ratio of kinetic diameters of the gas molecules and the front factor by the condensability of the faster gas.

**Performance-limiting factors of conventional polymers (2 marks):**

1. Trade-off between **permeability and selectivity** — high selectivity often means low flux.
2. **Physical aging** of glassy polymers — free volume collapses over time, reducing flux.
3. **Plasticization** by condensable gases ($\text{CO}_2$, hydrocarbons) — reduces selectivity.
4. Limited **chemical and thermal resistance**.

**Novel materials pushing the bound (2 marks):**

- **Polymers of Intrinsic Microporosity (PIMs):** extremely rigid, contorted backbones that cannot pack efficiently, giving **very high free volume** and high permeability. They lie *above* the 1991 bound.
- **Mixed Matrix Membranes (MMMs):** polymer + inorganic fillers (zeolites, MOFs, COFs, CNTs) — combine polymer processability with the molecular-sieving precision of the filler.
- **Thermally Rearranged (TR) Polymers:** post-modified polyimides with rigid, microporous structures.

---

### Model Solution for Question B (b) — 7 marks

**Given:**
$\ell = 0.2 \times 10^{-6}$ m
$P_{\text{CO}_2} = 9.5$ Barrer $= 9.5 \times 3.348 \times 10^{-16} = 3.181 \times 10^{-15}$ mol·m/m²·s·Pa
$P_{\text{CH}_4} = 0.25 \times 3.348 \times 10^{-16} = 8.37 \times 10^{-17}$ mol·m/m²·s·Pa

**Pressure conversion (1 atm = 1.01325 × 10⁵ Pa):**
$\Delta p_{\text{CO}_2} = (4 - 0.1) \times 1.01325 \times 10^5 = 3.9497 \times 10^5$ Pa
$\Delta p_{\text{CH}_4} = (16 - 0.4) \times 1.01325 \times 10^5 = 1.5785 \times 10^6$ Pa

**(i) Flux calculations (3 marks):**

$$
J_{\text{CO}_2} = \frac{P_{\text{CO}_2} \cdot \Delta p_{\text{CO}_2}}{\ell} = \frac{(3.181 \times 10^{-15})(3.9497 \times 10^5)}{0.2 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
J_{\text{CO}_2} = \frac{1.2566 \times 10^{-9}}{2.0 \times 10^{-7}} = 6.28 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{mol/m}^2\text{·s}
$$

$$
J_{\text{CH}_4} = \frac{P_{\text{CH}_4} \cdot \Delta p_{\text{CH}_4}}{\ell} = \frac{(8.37 \times 10^{-17})(1.5785 \times 10^6)}{0.2 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
J_{\text{CH}_4} = \frac{1.321 \times 10^{-10}}{2.0 \times 10^{-7}} = 6.61 \times 10^{-4} \;\text{mol/m}^2\text{·s}
$$

**(ii) Ideal selectivity (2 marks):**

$$
\alpha_{\text{CO}_2/\text{CH}_4}^{\text{ideal}} = \frac{P_{\text{CO}_2}}{P_{\text{CH}_4}} = \frac{9.5}{0.25} = 38
$$

**(iii) Actual selectivity using pressure driving forces (2 marks):**

$$
\alpha_{\text{CO}_2/\text{CH}_4}^{\text{actual}} = \frac{J_{\text{CO}_2} / \Delta p_{\text{CO}_2}}{J_{\text{CH}_4} / \Delta p_{\text{CH}_4}} = \frac{6.28 \times 10^{-3} / 3.9497 \times 10^5}{6.61 \times 10^{-4} / 1.5785 \times 10^6}
$$

$$
= \frac{1.590 \times 10^{-8}}{4.187 \times 10^{-10}} \approx 37.96
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answers:**
> - $J_{\text{CO}_2} \approx 6.28 \times 10^{-3}$ mol/m²·s
> - $J_{\text{CH}_4} \approx 6.61 \times 10^{-4}$ mol/m²·s
> - Ideal $\alpha = 38$
> - Actual $\alpha \approx 37.96$ (consistent with ideal behaviour, indicating no plasticization in this regime)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Commonly Lose Marks on This Topic:**
> 1. **Forgetting to state assumptions** before the solution-diffusion derivation — examiners specifically deduct 1–2 marks for missing assumptions.
> 2. **Unit conversion errors** between Barrer and SI units (1 Barrer $= 3.348 \times 10^{-16}$ mol·m/m²·s·Pa). Mixing units within a single problem is the most common mistake.
> 3. **Confusing permeability with flux** — they are *not* the same; flux depends on thickness and pressure difference.
> 4. **Listing polymers without relating them to the application** — for full marks in the 7-mark comparison question, students must explicitly link each polymer's properties (e.g., $T_g$, hydrophilicity) to its separation performance.
> 5. **Neglecting the Robeson upper bound** when discussing gas-separation polymers — this is a high-yield concept that most students omit.
> 6. **Confusing "ideal" and "real" selectivity** — ideal uses permeabilities, real uses flux ratios with actual partial pressures.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Final 60-Second Revision List for KTU Module 1 — Polymer Materials Used as Membranes:**

- **Definition**: Polymer membrane = thin selective barrier of organic macromolecular chains.
- **Governing transport** (dense membrane): solution-diffusion: $J = P \cdot \Delta p / \ell$ with $P = D \cdot S$.
- **Governing transport** (porous membrane): size-sieving (Poiseuille / Hagen–Poiseuille for viscous flow, Knudsen for gas).
- **Classification axes**: morphology (isotropic / asymmetric / TFC / hollow fibre / MMM); transport (porous / dense / ion-exchange / facilitated); thermal state (glassy / rubbery / semi-crystalline).
- **Key property set**: $T_g$, free volume, hydrophilicity ($\theta_w$), chemical resistance, mechanical strength.
- **Major polymers**: CA, PSf, PES, PI, PA, PVDF, PTFE, PP, PDMS, PAN, PVA, Nafion, PIMs.
- **Trade-off**: Robeson upper bound — $\log P_i$ vs. $\log \alpha$ is linear with negative slope; novel materials (PIMs, MMMs, TR polymers) push the bound.
- **Glassy polymers** (PSf, PI, CA) → high selectivity, low flux; used in **RO, NF, GS**.
- **Rubbery polymers** (PDMS) → high flux, low selectivity; used in **PV, VOC removal**.
- **Semi-crystalline polymers** (PVDF, PTFE) → mechanical and chemical robustness; used in **MF, MD, harsh solvent filtration**.
- **Modern RO membrane architecture**: polyamide TFC (0.1–0.2 µm skin) on PSf support on PET backing.
- **Hydrophilicity rule**: $\theta_w < 90°$ → hydrophilic → better water transport → lower fouling.
- **Permeability of a penetrant** increases with: ↑ free volume, ↑ temperature (Arrhenius), ↑ condensability, ↑ solubility.
- **Free-volume formula** (Bondi): $\%V_f = (V - 1.3 V_w)/V \times 100$.
- **High-pressure RO** requires mechanical strength + chemical resistance → polyamide TFC.
- **Pervaporation of water → ethanol dehydration** uses **hydrophilic PVA / chitosan**.
- **Pervaporation of organics → from water** uses **hydrophobic PDMS / PEBA**.
- **Ion-exchange membranes** (Nafion) for **fuel cells and electrodialysis**.
- **Emerging materials**: PIMs, MMMs (polymer + MOFs/zeolites), TR polymers, graphene-based composites.
- **The "1 Barrer" conversion to remember**: $1$ Barrer $= 3.348 \times 10^{-16}$ mol·m/m²·s·Pa.
- **The Arrhenius equations** for $D$ and $S$ as a function of temperature are high-yield.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Functional Polymer Membranes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT752 - POLYMERS AND SEPARATION PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Functional Polymer Membranes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Functional Polymer Membrane** is a semi-permeable barrier, fabricated from carefully engineered polymeric materials, in which the polymer chains themselves (or specific functional groups grafted onto them) actively govern the selectivity and rate of mass transport across the membrane. Unlike passive physical filters, functional polymer membranes exploit the **chemical specificity** of the polymer's functional groups (e.g., sulfonic acid, carboxyl, amide, imide, ether linkages) to discriminate between molecular species on the basis of size, charge, affinity, or chemical interaction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PEPOT752 – Module 1):**
> Functional polymer membranes are polymeric membranes whose separation performance is dictated by the presence of specific functional groups on the polymer backbone, enabling tailored interactions such as ion-exchange, facilitated transport, affinity binding, or solution-diffusion for use in reverse osmosis, ultrafiltration, pervaporation, gas separation, and fuel cell applications.

The term **"functional"** implies that the polymer is not merely a passive physical matrix but a **chemically active** medium. The functional groups are responsible for:
- Selective sorption of one species over another.
- Controlled diffusion through the free volume of the polymer.
- Specific chemical binding or ion-exchange interactions.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **security checkpoint at a concert venue**:

- The **polymer membrane** is the metal detector gate.
- The **functional groups** on the polymer are like the security personnel who check IDs — they don't just block everyone indiscriminately; they recognize specific credentials.
- **Permeate (people who pass)** are those whose credentials match what the security recognizes.
- **Retentate (people who don't pass)** are turned away, not because the gate is physically closed, but because their "credentials" (molecular size, charge, polarity) do not match.

Just as a security guard trained to spot VIPs will let VIPs through faster than regular attendees, a **functional polymer membrane** with sulfonic acid groups (e.g., Nafion) will preferentially allow protons and water through while rejecting most anions and large molecules.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction — Functional vs. Non-Functional Membranes:**
> A non-functional membrane (e.g., a simple polyethylene film) separates based purely on **physical pore size**. A functional polymer membrane separates based on **chemical affinity and physico-chemical interactions** between the permeating species and the polymer's functional groups.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Engineering Constants

The performance of functional polymer membranes is quantified by the following universal engineering metrics:

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Unit | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| Permeability | $P$ | **Barrer** (or mol·m/m²·s·Pa) | $10^{-2}$ – $10^{3}$ Barrer |
| Permselectivity | $\alpha_{A/B}$ | Dimensionless | $1$ – $10^{4}$ |
| Flux | $J$ | L/m²·h (LMH) or kg/m²·s | $0.1$ – $10^{3}$ LMH |
| Membrane Thickness | $\ell$ | µm | $0.1$ – $200$ µm |
| Porosity | $\varepsilon$ | Dimensionless | $0$ – $0.85$ |
| Pore Radius (for porous) | $r_p$ | nm | $0.5$ – $50$ nm |

> [!NOTE]
> **1 Barrer** $= 3.348 \times 10^{-16}$ mol·m/(m²·s·Pa) — the standard unit introduced by the KTU 2024 syllabus for gas permeability.

## 1.4 The Polymer Membrane Spectrum

Functional polymer membranes occupy a unique niche in the broader membrane landscape:

1. **Biological Membranes** — lipid bilayers with embedded proteins (e.g., cell membranes).
2. **Synthetic Polymeric Membranes** — including functional polymer membranes (focus of this module).
3. **Inorganic Membranes** — ceramics, metals, zeolites, glass.
4. **Mixed Matrix Membranes (MMMs)** — polymers filled with inorganic fillers (e.g., ZIF-8 in PEMA).
5. **Thin-Film Composite (TFC) Membranes** — a thin functional polymer layer on a porous support.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Functional Polymer Membranes

Functional polymer membranes are systematically classified along three orthogonal axes recognized by the KTU 2024 syllabus:

### A. Classification by Morphology (Structure)

| Type | Description | Example Polymer | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Dense (Non-porous)** | No permanent pores; transport via solution-diffusion | Cellulose acetate, PDMS, PTMSP | Gas separation, pervaporation |
| **Porous (Symmetric)** | Uniform pore structure throughout | Polycarbonate (track-etched) | Microfiltration |
| **Asymmetric (Integral)** | Dense skin + porous sublayer | Polysulfone, PVDF (Loeb-Sourirajan) | Ultrafiltration |
| **Thin-Film Composite (TFC)** | Thin functional layer on a support | Polyamide on polysulfone | Reverse osmosis, NF |
| **Hollow Fiber** | Tubular geometry | Polyimide, cellulose triacetate | RO, gas separation |
| **Ion-Exchange** | Fixed ionic groups | Nafion (cation), Selemion (anion) | Electrodialysis, fuel cells |

### B. Classification by Driving Force (Separation Mechanism)

| Driving Force | Process | Functional Polymer Example |
|---|---|---|
| Pressure gradient | Microfiltration, UF, NF, RO | Polysulfone, polyamide |
| Concentration gradient | Pervaporation, dialysis | PVA, PDMS |
| Electrical potential | Electrodialysis | Sulfonated polystyrene |
| Temperature gradient | Membrane distillation | PTFE, PP |
| Partial pressure gradient | Gas separation | PTMSP, polyimide (Matrimid) |

### C. Classification by Separation Mechanism (Most Critical for KTU)

1. **Solution-Diffusion Model** — for dense membranes (RO, gas separation, pervaporation).
2. **Pore-Flow Model** — for microporous membranes (MF, UF).
3. **Ion-Exchange / Donnan Exclusion** — for charged membranes.
4. **Facilitated Transport** — carrier-mediated transport.

## 2.2 The Solution-Diffusion Mechanism — Step-by-Step Logic

The **solution-diffusion model** is the cornerstone of dense functional polymer membrane transport. It postulates three sequential steps for a permeating species $i$:

### Step 1: **Sorption (Partitioning) at the Upstream Face**
The penetrant molecule dissolves into the polymer matrix at the high-pressure/high-concentration side. This is governed by the **thermodynamic partition coefficient** $K_i$:

$$K_i = \frac{C_{i,\text{polymer}}}{C_{i,\text{feed}}}$$

where $C_{i,\text{polymer}}$ (mol/m³ polymer) and $C_{i,\text{feed}}$ (mol/m³ feed) are the equilibrium concentrations of species $i$.

> **Why this step matters:** Functional groups (e.g., –COOH, –SO₃H, –NH₂) on the polymer strongly attract certain molecules. For example, PVA is highly selective for water over ethanol in pervaporation because –OH groups hydrogen-bond with water.

### Step 2: **Diffusion Across the Membrane**
The dissolved molecules hop through transient gaps in the polymer matrix under a chemical potential gradient. This is governed by **Fick's First Law**:

$$J_i = -D_i \frac{dC_i}{dx}$$

where:
- $J_i$ = molar flux (mol/m²·s)
- $D_i$ = diffusion coefficient (m²/s) — typically $10^{-12}$ to $10^{-8}$ m²/s in polymers
- $\frac{dC_i}{dx}$ = concentration gradient of $i$ across the membrane

### Step 3: **Desorption (Evaporation) at the Downstream Face**
The penetrant leaves the polymer matrix into the permeate stream. This step is typically fast and non-rate-limiting when the permeate pressure is low.

> **Why the membrane is "functional":** Each step is modulated by the chemical nature of the polymer. A hydrophobic polymer (e.g., PDMS) preferentially sorbs organic vapors; a hydrophilic polymer (e.g., PVA) preferentially sorbs water.

## 2.3 Combining the Steps — The Fundamental Permeability Equation

For steady-state transport through a dense membrane, the solution-diffusion model yields the **master flux equation**:

$$J_i = \frac{P_i}{\ell} (p_{i,\text{feed}} - p_{i,\text{permeate}})$$

where:
- $P_i = D_i \cdot S_i$ = permeability coefficient (mol·m/m²·s·Pa)
- $D_i$ = diffusion coefficient (m²/s)
- $S_i$ = solubility coefficient (mol/m³·Pa)
- $\ell$ = effective membrane thickness (m)
- $p_{i,\text{feed}}, p_{i,\text{permeate}}$ = partial pressures (Pa)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The KTU-High-Yield Identity: $P = D \cdot S$**
> This is the **most tested equation** in Module 1. Permeability is the **product** of diffusivity (kinetic term) and solubility (thermodynamic term). Two polymers can have the same permeability but for entirely different reasons — one polymer may have high $D$, low $S$ (e.g., rubbery PDMS); another may have low $D$, high $S$ (e.g., glassy PTMSP).

## 2.4 Selectivity (Permselectivity)

For a binary mixture (A and B), the **permselectivity** $\alpha_{A/B}$ is defined as:

$$\alpha_{A/B} = \frac{P_A}{P_B} = \left(\frac{D_A}{D_B}\right) \cdot \left(\frac{S_A}{S_B}\right)$$

The first term $\frac{D_A}{D_B}$ is the **diffusivity selectivity** (size-sieving ability), and the second $\frac{S_A}{S_B}$ is the **solubility selectivity** (affinity-sieving ability). A **functional polymer membrane** typically exhibits high $\alpha_{A/B}$ because its functional groups enhance $S_A/S_B$ dramatically.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SAVE THIS TABLE — HIGH-YIELD FOR ESE.**

| # | Equation | Name | Applicability | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $J_i = \dfrac{P_i}{\ell}\,(p_{i,f} - p_{i,p})$ | Permeation flux | Dense membranes | mol/m²·s |
| 2 | $P_i = D_i \cdot S_i$ | Permeability identity | Dense membranes | Barrer |

## 2.6 Engineering Utility of Functional Polymer Membranes

Functional polymer membranes are foundational to:

- **Water Treatment** — Reverse osmosis (RO) using polyamide TFC membranes desalinates ~$10^{8}$ m³/day globally.
- **Medical Devices** — Hemodialysis membranes (polysulfone, cellulose) save millions of lives annually.
- **Energy** — Proton-exchange membranes (Nafion) in PEM fuel cells; Li⁺-conductive membranes in batteries.
- **Green Chemistry** — Pervaporation for breaking azeotropes (e.g., ethanol-water) reduces distillation energy by ~$40$–$60\%$.
- **CO₂ Capture** — Polyimide (Matrimid, P84) and PEG-functionalized membranes capture CO₂ from flue gas.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Steady-State Solution-Diffusion Flux Equation

We derive the master flux equation $J_i = \frac{P_i}{\ell}(p_{i,f} - p_{i,p})$ from Fick's First Law under steady-state conditions.

### Step 1: Apply Fick's First Law Across the Membrane

For one-dimensional transport in the $x$-direction through a membrane of thickness $\ell$:

$$J_i = -D_i \,\frac{dC_i}{dx}$$

### Step 2: Steady-State Implies Constant Flux

Under steady state, $J_i$ does not depend on $x$ or $t$. Therefore, $D_i \cdot \frac{dC_i}{dx}$ must be a constant for each species. Assuming $D_i$ is independent of concentration (a simplification valid for dilute systems — the KTU 2024 standard assumption), $\frac{dC_i}{dx}$ is itself constant.

### Step 3: Integrate Across the Membrane Thickness

Integrating from $x = 0$ (upstream face) to $x = \ell$ (downstream face):

$$\int_{0}^{\ell} J_i \, dx = -\int_{C_{i,0}}^{C_{i,\ell}} D_i \, dC_i$$

$$J_i \cdot \ell = -D_i \,\bigl(C_{i,\ell} - C_{i,0}\bigr) = D_i \,\bigl(C_{i,0} - C_{i,\ell}\bigr)$$

### Step 4: Apply Henry's Law at Both Faces

Henry's Law relates concentration in the polymer to partial pressure in the adjacent gas phase:

$$C_{i} = S_i \cdot p_i$$

where $S_i$ is the solubility coefficient. Therefore:

$$C_{i,0} = S_i \cdot p_{i,\text{feed}}, \quad C_{i,\ell} = S_i \cdot p_{i,\text{permeate}}$$

### Step 5: Substitute and Simplify

Substituting into the integrated equation:

$$J_i \cdot \ell = D_i \cdot S_i \cdot (p_{i,\text{feed}} - p_{i,\text{permeate}})$$

Defining $P_i \equiv D_i \cdot S_i$:

$$\boxed{\,J_i = \frac{P_i}{\ell} \,(p_{i,\text{feed}} - p_{i,\text{permeate}})\,}$$

This is the **fundamental permeation equation** for dense functional polymer membranes.

## 3.2 Derivation of Permselectivity in Terms of Diffusivity and Solubility Ratios

For a binary mixture of species A and B, both transporting independently through the membrane:

$$\alpha_{A/B} = \frac{P_A}{P_B} = \frac{D_A \cdot S_A}{D_B \cdot S_B} = \left(\frac{D_A}{D_B}\right)\left(\frac{S_A}{S_B}\right)$$

**Interpretation:**
- $\frac{D_A}{D_B} > 1$ → A diffuses faster than B (typically because A is smaller).
- $\frac{S_A}{S_B} > 1$ → A is more soluble in the polymer (typically because A has stronger chemical affinity for the functional groups).

> **KTU 2024 Insight:** In **rubbery polymers** (above $T_g$), $\alpha_{A/B}$ is dominated by $S_A/S_B$ (solubility-controlled). In **glassy polymers** (below $T_g$), $\alpha_{A/B}$ is dominated by $D_A/D_B$ (diffusivity-controlled).

## 3.3 Worked Example — KTU-Style Numerical Problem

> **Problem Statement (analogous to KTU 2024 ESE):**
> A dense functional polymer membrane of thickness $\ell = 50\,\mu\text{m}$ is used to separate a binary gas mixture of O₂ and N₂. The following data are reported:
> - Permeability of O₂: $P_{O_2} = 6.9 \times 10^{-13}$ mol·m/(m²·s·Pa)
> - Permeability of N₂: $P_{N_2} = 2.1 \times 10^{-13}$ mol·m/(m²·s·Pa)
> - Upstream partial pressures: $p_{O_2,f} = 80\,\text{kPa}$, $p_{N_2,f} = 20\,\text{kPa}$
> - Permeate pressure: $p_{O_2,p} = 4\,\text{kPa}$, $p_{N_2,p} = 1\,\text{kPa}$

> **Compute:** (a) The O₂ flux through the membrane, (b) The N₂ flux, (c) The permselectivity $\alpha_{O_2/N_2}$.

### Solution

#### Part (a): O₂ Flux

Convert $\ell$ to SI units: $\ell = 50\,\mu\text{m} = 50 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}$.

Apply the solution-diffusion flux equation:

$$J_{O_2} = \frac{P_{O_2}}{\ell}\,(p_{O_2,f} - p_{O_2,p})$$

Substitute (pressures in Pa: $1\,\text{kPa} = 10^3\,\text{Pa}$):

$$J_{O_2} = \frac{6.9 \times 10^{-13}}{50 \times 10^{-6}}\,(80 \times 10^3 - 4 \times 10^3)$$

$$\Delta p_{O_2} = 76{,}000\,\text{Pa}$$

$$J_{O_2} = \frac{6.9 \times 10^{-13}}{5 \times 10^{-5}} \times 76{,}000$$

$$\frac{6.9 \times 10^{-13}}{5 \times 10^{-5}} = 1.38 \times 10^{-8}\,\text{mol/(m²·s·Pa)}$$

$$J_{O_2} = 1.38 \times 10^{-8} \times 76{,}000 = 1.0488 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{mol/(m²·s)}$$

$$\boxed{J_{O_2} \approx 1.05 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{mol/(m²·s)}}$$

#### Part (b): N₂ Flux

$$\Delta p_{N_2} = 20{,}000 - 1{,}000 = 19{,}000\,\text{Pa}$$

$$J_{N_2} = \frac{2.1 \times 10^{-13}}{5 \times 10^{-5}} \times 19{,}000$$

$$\frac{2.1 \times 10^{-13}}{5 \times 10^{-5}} = 4.2 \times 10^{-9}\,\text{mol/(m²·s·Pa)}$$

$$J_{N_2} = 4.2 \times 10^{-9} \times 19{,}000 = 7.98 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{mol/(m²·s)}$$

$$\boxed{J_{N_2} \approx 7.98 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{mol/(m²·s)}}$$

#### Part (c): Permselectivity

$$\alpha_{O_2/N_2} = \frac{P_{O_2}}{P_{N_2}} = \frac{6.9 \times 10^{-13}}{2.1 \times 10^{-13}}$$

$$\boxed{\alpha_{O_2/N_2} = 3.286 \approx 3.29}$$

**Verification using flux ratio:**
$$\frac{J_{O_2}}{J_{N_2}} = \frac{1.0488 \times 10^{-3}}{7.98 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 13.14$$

This equals $\alpha_{O_2/N_2} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta p_{O_2}}{\Delta p_{N_2}} = 3.29 \cdot \dfrac{76{,}000}{19{,}000} = 3.29 \cdot 4 = 13.16$. ✓

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** The O₂/N₂ selectivity of ~3.3 is typical for **glassy polymers like polysulfone**. Functional groups (e.g., ether oxygen, sulfone) enable preferential O₂ sorption, but the dominant effect in glassy polymers is size-sieving (O₂ is smaller than N₂).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Functional Polymer Membrane Selection

| Separation Goal | Recommended Functional Polymer | Functional Group Responsible | Driving Force |
|---|---|---|---|
| O₂/N₂ enrichment | Polyimide (Matrimid 5218) | Imide ring (–CO–N–CO–) | Partial pressure |
| CO₂/CH₄ (natural gas) | PEG-modified polyimide | Ether oxygen (–O–) | Partial pressure |
| Water/ethanol (azeotrope) | PVA | Hydroxyl (–OH) | Vacuum (pervaporation) |

| Monovalent/divalent ions | Sulfonated polysulfone | Sulfonate (–SO₃⁻) | Electrical potential |
| Seawater desalination | Polyamide TFC | Amide (–CONH–) | Hydraulic pressure |
| Bio-MOF separation | MMM with –COOH groups | Carboxyl (–COOH) | Pressure / affinity |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Polymer Membrane — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

The diagram below maps the **interactions** between the polymer, the functional groups, the permeating species, and the engineering outcomes. This block-level mapping is the standard KTU 2024-compliant substitute for complex physical drawings.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    FEED["Feed Stream (Multi-Component Mixture)"] --> FACEUP["Upstream Membrane Face (High Chemical Potential)"]
    FACEUP --> SORB["Step 1: Sorption via Functional Groups"]
    SORB --> SORBGROUP["Functional Groups: -SO3H, -COOH, -OH, -NH2, -CONH-, Ether-O"]
    SORB --> DIFFUSE["Step 2: Diffusion Through Polymer Free Volume"]
    DIFFUSE --> DIFFCOEF["Diffusion Coefficient D_i (m^2/s) governed by polymer chain mobility"]
    DIFFUSE --> FACEDN["Downstream Membrane Face (Low Chemical Potential)"]
    FACEDN --> DESORB["Step 3: Desorption into Permeate"]
    DESORB --> PERM["Permeate (Purified Product A)"]
    FEED --> RETENT["Retentate (Rejected Component B)"]
    SORBGROUP --> SORB
    DIFFCOEF --> DIFFUSE
    SORB --> METRICS["Output Metrics: Flux J_i, Permeability P_i, Selectivity alpha"]
    DIFFUSE --> METRICS
    DESORB --> METRICS
    METRICS --> APPL["Engineering Applications: RO, UF, Pervaporation, Gas Separation, Fuel Cells"]
```

## 4.2 Cross-Functional Architecture of a Thin-Film Composite (TFC) Membrane

The TFC architecture is the **workhorse** of modern reverse osmosis and nanofiltration. It combines a thin functional polymer selective layer (~50–200 nm) on top of a porous support (~50–150 µm).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    FEEDTFC["Saline Feed Water (NaCl, organic solutes)"] --> TOPLAYER["Top Selective Layer: Crosslinked Polyamide (~100 nm) with -CONH- groups"]
    TOPLAYER --> INTERFACE["Interface: Interfacial Polymerization Zone"]
    INTERFACE --> SUPPORT["Porous Polysulfone Support (~100 micrometres, UF-grade)"]
    SUPPORT --> REINFORCE["Non-woven Polyester Fabric Backing (~200 micrometres)"]
    TOPLAYER --> REJECTSALT["Rejection Mechanism: Size Sieving + Donnan Exclusion of Cl- ions"]
    REJECTSALT --> RETENTATETFC["Concentrated Brine Retentate"]
    REINFORCE --> PERMWATER["Pure Water Permeate (Product)"]
```

## 4.3 Transport Mechanism Decision Tree

This block-level decision tree maps how a functional polymer membrane selects its transport mechanism based on feed composition.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START["Feed Stream Enters Membrane Module"] --> Q1{"Is the polymer dense or porous?"}
    Q1 -->|Dense (no permanent pores)| SD["Solution-Diffusion Mechanism"]
    Q1 -->|Porous (UF, MF)| PF["Pore-Flow (Hagen-Poiseuille) Mechanism"]
    SD --> Q2{"Are there fixed ionic groups?"}
    Q2 -->|Yes (Nafion, Selemion)| IEM["Ion-Exchange / Donnan Exclusion"]
    Q2 -->|No (neutral polymer)| SDPURE["Pure Solution-Diffusion: J = P/l * delta p"]
    IEM --> Q3{"Are there mobile carriers?"}
    Q3 -->|Yes (e.g., Ag+ in olefin/paraffin)| FACIL["Facilitated Transport"]
    Q3 -->|No (no carrier)| IEMPURE["Standard IEM Transport"]
    PF --> OUT["Output: Permeate + Retentate"]
    SDPURE --> OUT
    FACIL --> OUT
    IEMPURE --> OUT
```
