# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]
**Differentiate between symmetric and asymmetric membranes with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Symmetric Membrane | Asymmetric Membrane |
|---|---|---|
| Structure | Uniform throughout thickness | Thin dense skin + porous support |
| Thickness | Entirely active (50–500 µm) | Skin ~0.1–1 µm active; support 100 µm |
| Flux | Lower (thick active layer) | Higher (thin skin) |
| Examples | Cellulose acetate cast film, PTFE | Loeb-Sourirajan RO membrane, PES UF |

(2 marks for clear differentiation with table; 1 mark for at least one example of each.)

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]
**Define "Molecular Weight Cut-Off" (MWCO) of an ultrafiltration membrane. State its significance.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Definition (2 marks):** MWCO is defined as the molecular weight (in Daltons) of a reference solute (typically a globular protein or PEG) that is **90% rejected** by the membrane under standard test conditions. For instance, a 10 kDa MWCO membrane retains 90% of solutes of molecular weight 10,000 Da and above.
- **Significance (1 mark):** It is the **primary selection criterion** for UF membranes. Engineers select a membrane with MWCO **3 to 5 times smaller** than the molecular weight of the solute to be retained, ensuring high rejection while maintaining acceptable flux.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**.

---

### OPTION A — Question A [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Adapted]

**Q. (a)** Classify membranes based on (i) structure, (ii) material, and (iii) configuration. Give at least two examples for each category. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A reverse osmosis plant processes **1500 m³/day** of brackish water (TDS = 3000 ppm) to produce permeate with TDS = 200 ppm. The membrane has a flux of **18 LMH** and operates 22 hours per day. Calculate:
(i) The rejection coefficient.
(ii) The required membrane area in m².
(iii) The daily permeate production per m² of membrane. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to (a)

**(i) By Structure (2.5 marks):**
- **Symmetric (Isotropic):** Porous — e.g., track-etched polycarbonate, sintered ceramic. Dense — e.g., silicone rubber film.
- **Asymmetric (Anisotropic):** Loeb-Sourirajan type — e.g., CA RO membrane, PSf UF membrane.
- **Composite:** Thin film composite (TFC) — e.g., polyamide on polysulfone support.

**(ii) By Material (2.5 marks):**
- **Polymeric:** Cellulose acetate, Polysulfone, Polyamide, PVDF.
- **Ceramic:** Alumina, Zirconia, Titania.
- **Metallic:** Palladium, Silver alloys.
- **Mixed Matrix:** Polymer-zeolite, polymer-CNT composites.

**(iii) By Configuration (2 marks):**
- **Flat Sheet:** Used in lab and small-scale units.
- **Spiral Wound:** Industrial standard for RO/NF.
- **Hollow Fiber:** Used in UF, RO, hemodialysis.
- **Tubular:** Wastewater with high suspended solids.

---

#### Model Solution to (b)

**(i) Rejection (2 marks):**
$$R = 1 - \frac{C_p}{C_f} = 1 - \frac{200}{3000} = 1 - 0.0667 = 0.9333$$

**Rejection = 93.33%**

**(ii) Membrane Area (3 marks):**

Convert daily permeate to hourly, using 22 operating hours:

$$Q_p = \frac{1500 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}}{22 \text{ h/day}} = 68.18 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$$

Convert flux to m³/m²·h:

$$J = 18 \text{ LMH} = 0.018 \, \text{m}^3/\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{h}$$

Required area:

$$A = \frac{Q_p}{J} = \frac{68.18}{0.018} = \boxed{3788 \, \text{m}^2}$$

**(iii) Daily Permeate per m² (2 marks):**

$$J_{\text{day}} = 0.018 \times 22 = 0.396 \, \text{m}^3/\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{day}$$

$$\boxed{J_{\text{day}} = 0.396 \, \text{m}^3/\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{day} \approx 396 \, \text{L/m}^2 \cdot \text{day}}$$

---

### OPTION B — Question B [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Adapted]

**Q. (a)** Discuss the **Loeb-Sourirajan asymmetric membrane** with a neat sketch. Explain how it revolutionized reverse osmosis technology. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An ultrafiltration membrane is being selected for **protein recovery** from a fermentation broth. The protein of interest has a molecular weight of **45 kDa**. State the recommended MWCO range for the membrane, justify your answer, and list **three other key criteria** you would use in the final selection. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to (a)

**Loeb-Sourirajan Membrane (7 marks):**

- **Historical Context (1 mark):** Developed by Sidney Loeb and Srinivasa Sourirajan at UCLA in the 1960s. Prior to this, RO was impractical due to extremely low fluxes in thick dense membranes.
- **Structure (3 marks):** Consists of an **ultra-thin, dense skin layer** (0.1–1 µm) of cellulose acetate supported by a much thicker **porous sub-layer** (50–200 µm) of the same polymer. The skin provides selectivity; the sub-layer provides mechanical strength.
- **Fabrication (1 mark):** Prepared by **phase inversion** — casting a polymer solution, then immersing in a non-solvent bath (water). The skin forms at the air-polymer interface due to rapid solvent evaporation.
- **Impact (2 marks):** The thin skin reduces transport resistance by **2–3 orders of magnitude**, increasing flux by 100–1000× while maintaining the selectivity of the dense polymer. This made **RO economically viable** for desalination, triggering the modern membrane industry.

---

#### Model Solution to (b)

**(i) Recommended MWCO (3 marks):**
- The general engineering rule: choose a membrane with MWCO **3 to 5 times smaller** than the target protein's molecular weight to ensure >90% rejection.
- For 45 kDa protein: MWCO range = $45/5$ to $45/3$ = **9 kDa to 15 kDa**.
- A **10 kDa MWCO UF membrane** is the standard recommendation.

**(ii) Justification (2 marks):**
At 10 kDa, proteins of 45 kDa are well above the cut-off and will be rejected efficiently. Choosing much lower MWCO (e.g., 1 kDa) would unnecessarily reduce flux. Choosing much higher (e.g., 30 kDa) would risk significant protein loss to permeate.

**(iii) Three Other Selection Criteria (2 marks):**
1. **Material Compatibility:** The membrane must resist the fermentation broth's pH, salts, and cleaning chemicals. PES or regenerated cellulose are common choices.
2. **Fouling Resistance:** Hydrophilic, smooth surfaces (e.g., regenerated cellulose) minimize protein adsorption and biofilm formation.
3. **Configuration:** Hollow fiber for high packing density in a clean broth; tubular for broths with high cell debris.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Reasons for Mark Deduction:**
>
> 1. **Unit Omission (–1 to –2 marks):** Always write units with every numerical answer. KTU examiners deduct marks for "10,000" without "m³/h" or "kg/m³".
> 2. **Confusing MWCO with Pore Size (–1 mark):** MWCO is for UF/NF only and is expressed in Daltons; pore size is for MF/UF and is in nm or µm. Mixing these is a frequent error.
> 3. **Forgetting to Convert LMH to SI (–1 mark):** KTU sometimes expects SI units (m³/m²·s). Always show the conversion step.
> 4. **Treating Asymmetric and Composite as Synonyms (–1 mark):** Asymmetric = one material with two morphologies. Composite = two distinct materials in two distinct layers.
> 5. **Sketch with No Labels in Part B (–1 mark):** If you draw a Loeb-Sourirajan membrane, label the **skin, sub-layer, feed side, permeate side, and thicknesses**. Unlabelled diagrams are penalized.
> 6. **Incorrect Rejection Formula Sign (–2 marks):** Rejection is $R = 1 - C_p/C_f$, **never** $C_p/C_f - 1$. A negative rejection is physically meaningless.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] A **membrane** is a selective barrier; performance is measured by **flux, rejection, and selectivity**.
- [ ] Membranes are classified by **structure** (symmetric, asymmetric, composite), **material** (polymeric, ceramic, metallic), **configuration** (flat, spiral, hollow fiber, tubular), and **mechanism** (MF, UF, NF, RO, PV, GS, dialysis, electrodialysis).
- [ ] **Asymmetric membranes** (Loeb-Sourirajan, 1960) revolutionized RO via ultra-thin dense skin + porous support.
- [ ] **TFC composite membranes** decouple selectivity (skin) from mechanical strength (support), enabling independent optimization.
- [ ] **Hagen-Poiseuille flux equation:** $J = \dfrac{\varepsilon \, d_p^2 \, \Delta P}{32 \, \mu \, L \, \tau}$ — flux is **proportional to $d_p^2$**.
- [ ] **Rejection:** $R = 1 - C_p / C_f$. Range = 0 to 1. **MWCO** = 90% rejection point, in Daltons.
- [ ] **Area required:** $A = Q_p / J$. Use consistent units; convert LMH to m³/m²·h or SI.
- [ ] **Selection criteria:** Selectivity, chemical compatibility, thermal stability, mechanical strength, fouling resistance, flux, lifetime, cost, config.
- [ ] **MWCO selection rule:** Choose MWCO = **(1/3 to 1/5) × target molecular weight** for >90% retention.
- [ ] **Packing density order:** Hollow fiber > Spiral wound > Flat sheet > Tubular.
- [ ] **Cost order (per m²):** Polymeric < Ceramic < Metallic.
- [ ] **Solution-diffusion model** governs dense membranes (RO, GS, PV): $P_i = S_i \cdot D_i$.
- [ ] **Fouling control:** Hydrophilic > hydrophobic; smooth > rough; backwash-friendly > non-backwashable.
- [ ] **Always state units, draw labelled sketches, and write the governing formula before substitution in KTU board exams.**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Plate and Frame
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT752 - POLYMERS AND SEPARATION PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Plate and Frame

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plate and Frame Filter Press (PFFP)** is a batch-type, intermittent-pressure solid–liquid separation device in which a slurry is forced under positive pressure through a **filter medium (cloth)** supported on a series of alternating **plates** and **hollow frames**. The solids (filter cake) accumulate within the frames, while the clarified liquid (filtrate) drains out through channels on the plates. It is the most widely used **constant-pressure filtration equipment** in chemical, polymer, pharmaceutical, dye, pigment, and metallurgical industries.

**KTU-Syllabus Terminology (must be reproduced verbatim in exams):**
- **Plate** → a rigid grooved structure covered with filter cloth, supporting the cake and providing filtrate drainage.
- **Frame** → an empty rectangular spacer that defines the **filtration area** and provides the **chamber volume** for cake accumulation.
- **Plate-and-Frame Assembly (Stack)** → the alternating sequence of plates and frames mounted on a horizontal pair of parallel supporting bars.
- **Closing Mechanism** → either manual screw, hydraulic ram, or automatic packer used to seal the press against operating pressure.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Toast Rack Press"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Analogy — A Stack of Hollow Picture Frames**
> Imagine several empty **photo frames** (no glass, no backing) stacked one behind another with a piece of **cheesecloth** between each frame. Now, if you pour a thick, muddy slurry into the stack, water seeps out through every cheesecloth layer into the surrounding gutter, while the mud solids get trapped *inside* each hollow frame. Each frame is "half-full" of solid cake. Open the stack, peel the cloth, and you harvest neat slabs of dewatered solids. That is exactly how a Plate & Frame Filter Press works — only with hydraulics, not hands.

---

### 1.3 Salient Engineering Features

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why PFFP is the Workhorse of Batch Filtration**
> - **High filtration area** in a small footprint (up to **1200 m²** of area in modern presses).
> - **Flexible operation** — handles slurries from dilute to thick (5–500 g/L).
> - **High solids content** in the discharged cake (**30–65 % w/w** dry basis).
> - **Operates at high ΔP** (typically **3–10 bar**, up to **30 bar** in recessed-plate designs).
> - **Excellent wash efficiency** when equipped with dedicated wash plates (>90 % solute recovery).
> - **Low capital cost per unit area** compared to continuous filters (rotary, belt).

### 1.4 Standard Engineering Metrics (Mandatory for KTU Answers)

| Parameter | Typical Range |
|---|---|
| Operating pressure ΔP | **3–10 bar** (recessed up to 30 bar) |
| Cake thickness per frame | **10–50 mm** |
| Frame volume (chamber) | **2–20 L** per frame |
| Filter medium | **woven cloth** (polypropylene, cotton, nylon, polyester) |
| Filtrate clarity | **< 5 ppm** suspended solids |
| Cake moisture (post-press) | **20–35 % w/w** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Sequence — The Six Logical Stages

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Marker Question Favourite — "Explain the working of a Plate & Frame Filter Press."**

A complete **filtration cycle** consists of six distinct logical stages:

1. **Press Closure & Clamping** — the stack of plates + frames is hydraulically or mechanically compressed to seal gaskets against operating pressure.
2. **Slurry Charging (Priming)** — feed slurry is pumped into the central feed channel; it distributes radially into every hollow frame.
3. **Filtration (Cake Build-up)** — filtrate passes through the cloth, drains via plate grooves to the outlet. Solids deposit inside the frame. This is a **constant-pressure** operation because ΔP is held by the pump's pressure regulator.
4. **End-of-Filtration Detection** — when filtrate flow rate drops to a preset minimum (or when the frame is full), the feed pump is stopped.
5. **Cake Washing (Optional but Exam-Relevant)** — if wash plates are installed, wash water enters through a separate channel, percolates **through the entire cake thickness**, and exits as **strong liquor** from the adjacent plates.
6. **Cake Discharge & Cloth Cleaning** — the press is opened, frames separated one by one, and the consolidated cake slabs drop out under gravity. Cloth is washed down before re-assembly.

---

### 2.2 Two Architectural Variants — *Must Know*

#### Variant A — *Recessed-Plate (CGR — Chamber/Gasket Recessed)*
- Single piece of cloth covers a deeply recessed plate.
- Cake forms in the recess (no separate frame).
- **Simpler construction**, higher ΔP tolerance.
- **Disadvantage:** Cake washing is poor because no dedicated wash channel exists.

#### Variant B — *Plate-and-Frame with Wash Plates (Classical PFFP)*
- Three types of plates in alternation: **flush plate (drain only)** and **wash plate (wash water inlet)**.
- Cake accumulates in the **frame**, not on the plate.
- Allows true **through-washing** (most efficient).
- **Most common** in chemical engineering practice.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **board-tested** and **mathematically verifiable** — use them as the primary armoury for any numerical PFFP question.

**Table 2.1 — Master Equation Set for Plate & Frame Filter Press**

| # | Equation | Symbols & Units | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\dfrac{dV}{dt} = \dfrac{A\,\Delta P}{\mu\left(R_c + R_m\right)}$ | $V$: filtrate vol (m³); $t$: time (s); $A$: total area (m²); $\Delta P$: pressure (Pa); $\mu$: viscosity (Pa·s); $R_c$: cake resistance (m⁻¹); $R_m$: medium resistance (m⁻¹) | **Instantaneous Darcy rate** — analogous to Ohm's law. |
| 2 | $R_c = \alpha \cdot \dfrac{c \cdot V}{A}$ | $\alpha$: specific cake resistance (m/kg); $c$: mass of solids per m³ filtrate (kg/m³) | Cake resistance grows **linearly** with filtrate volume. |
| 3 | $\dfrac{dV}{dt} = \dfrac{A^2 \Delta P}{\mu\left(\alpha c V + R_m A\right)}$ | — | **Combined filtration equation** (substituting eq.2 into eq.1). |
| 4 | $V^2 + 2 V V_e = K A^2 t$ | $V_e = \dfrac{R_m A}{\alpha c}$ (m³, equivalent volume); $K = \dfrac{2 \Delta P}{\mu \alpha c}$ (m²/s) | **Constant-pressure integrated form.** |
| 5 | $\dfrac{t}{V} = \dfrac{\mu \alpha c}{2 A^2 \Delta P} \cdot V + \dfrac{\mu R_m}{A \Delta P}$ | — | **Linearised form** — plot $t/V$ vs $V$ → slope $m$, intercept $b$. |
| 6 | $K = \dfrac{2 \cdot \text{slope}}{(\text{intercept})^2 \cdot \text{slope}}$ (from graph) | — | Used when $K$ and $V_e$ are extracted from experimental data. |
| 7 | $\alpha = \alpha_0 (\Delta P)^{1-s}$ | $\alpha_0$: reference $\alpha$ at 1 Pa; $s$: compressibility (0 = incompressible, 1 = fully compressible) | **Cake compressibility** correction. |
| 8 | $V_{\text{frame}} = A_{\text{frame}} \cdot L / 2$ | $L$: frame thickness (m); $A_{\text{frame}}$: plate area (m²) | Cake volume per frame (factor ½ because **2 sides** of each frame filter). |
| 9 | $V_{\text{cycle}} = N \cdot V_{\text{frame}}$ | $N$: number of frames in press | Total filtrate per batch. |
| 10 | $\text{Throughput } Q = \dfrac{V_{\text{cycle}}}{t_{\text{cycle}}}$ | $t_{\text{cycle}}$: total cycle time (s) | **Productivity metric** — optimise to maximise. |
| 11 | $t_{\text{cycle}} = t_f + t_w + t_d + t_a$ | $t_f$: filtration; $t_w$: washing; $t_d$: discharge; $t_a$: assembly | Total batch time. |
| 12 | $\dfrac{t_w}{t_f} = \dfrac{V_w}{V} \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{\eta_w}\right)$ where $\eta_w = \dfrac{V_w}{V_w + V_{\text{displacement}}}$ | $V_w$: wash water volume; $\eta_w$: wash efficiency | **Relative wash time** relation. |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Pitfall — Sign & Factor-of-2 Error**
> Many students forget the factor of **½** when computing cake volume from frame thickness. Each frame deposits cake on **both** faces (two filter cloths), so $V_{\text{cake/frame}} = A_{\text{plate}} \times L / 2$, **not** $A \times L$.

---

### 2.4 Why & How — Physical Insight Behind Each Equation

- **Eq.1 (Darcy form):** Filtration is fluid flow through a porous bed. Cake + cloth behave like a series of resistances — pressure drives flow, viscosity resists it.
- **Eq.2:** As more solids are retained, the cake thickens → resistance rises **linearly** with cumulative solids (= $cV/A$).
- **Eq.4:** Integration of Eq.3 between $t=0,V=0$ and $t,V$ gives a **parabolic** relationship between $V$ and $t$.
- **Eq.5:** Re-arranging Eq.4 into a linear form ($y = mx + c$) is the **graphical method** used to extract $\alpha$ and $R_m$ from pilot data.
- **Eq.7:** Real cakes get **compressed** by ΔP; harder squeeze → denser cake → higher $\alpha$. The exponent $s$ quantifies this.

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Applications of PFFP**
> - **Polymer Industry:** Dewatering of **PVC, polyethylene, polypropylene** slurries from suspension polymerization.
> - **Pharmaceutical:** Recovery of **antibiotics (e.g., penicillin mycelium)**, API crystallization slurries.
> - **Dye & Pigment:** Final dewatering of **azo dyes, titanium dioxide, carbon black**.
> - **Metallurgy & Mining:** Tailings, concentrate dewatering, gold cyanidation slurries.
> - **Sugar Refineries:** Mud clarification in carbonatation process.
> - **Effluent Treatment:** Sludge dewatering in **Sewage Treatment Plants (STPs)**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Constant-Pressure Filtration Equation (Eq.4)

We start from the **instantaneous Darcy filtration law** — flow through two resistances (cake + medium) in series.

**Step 1 — Darcy's Law for Cake + Cloth**

$$
\frac{dV}{dt} = \frac{A\,\Delta P}{\mu\,(R_c + R_m)}
$$

*Logic:* Total resistance is the sum of cake resistance $R_c$ and filter-medium resistance $R_m$. Pressure drop across the combined bed equals $\Delta P$.

**Step 2 — Express Cake Resistance in Terms of Volume**

For a cake of thickness $L$, mass per unit area $w$:

$$
R_c = \alpha \, w = \alpha\,\frac{c\,V}{A}
$$

*Logic:* Specific cake resistance $\alpha$ (m/kg) is intrinsic. Total mass of solids per unit area = $cV/A$, where $c$ is solids concentration per unit filtrate volume.

**Step 3 — Substitute into Darcy's Equation**

$$
\frac{dV}{dt} = \frac{A\,\Delta P}{\mu\left(\dfrac{\alpha c V}{A} + R_m\right)} = \frac{A^2 \Delta P}{\mu\left(\alpha c V + R_m A\right)}
$$

*Logic:* Multiply numerator and denominator by $A$ to obtain a cleaner expression.

**Step 4 — Separate Variables**

$$
\left(\alpha c V + R_m A\right) dV = \frac{A^2 \Delta P}{\mu}\, dt
$$

**Step 5 — Integrate from $t=0, V=0$ to $t, V$**

$$
\int_{0}^{V} (\alpha c V + R_m A)\, dV = \frac{A^2 \Delta P}{\mu}\int_{0}^{t} dt
$$

$$
\frac{\alpha c\,V^2}{2} + R_m A\, V = \frac{A^2 \Delta P}{\mu}\,t
$$

**Step 6 — Multiply Both Sides by $2/(A^2)$**

$$
\frac{\alpha c\,V^2}{A^2} + \frac{2 R_m V}{A} = \frac{2 \Delta P}{\mu}\,t
$$

**Step 7 — Define Compact Variables $K$ and $V_e$**

Let

$$
K \equiv \frac{2\Delta P}{\mu \alpha c} \quad \text{and} \quad V_e \equiv \frac{R_m A}{\alpha c}
$$

Then:

$$
\left(\frac{V}{A}\right)^2 + 2 V_e \left(\frac{V}{A}\right) = K t
$$

Multiplying by $A^2$:

$$
\boxed{V^2 + 2 V V_e = K A^2 t}
$$

*This is the **mother equation** of constant-pressure filtration — derive it on any 14-mark KTU numerical with full marks.*

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Linearised Form (Eq.5)

Starting from the integrated form:

$$
V^2 + 2 V V_e = K A^2 t
$$

Divide throughout by $V$:

$$
V + 2 V_e = K A^2 \,\frac{t}{V}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
\frac{t}{V} = \frac{1}{K A^2}\,V + \frac{2 V_e}{K A^2}
$$

Substituting $K = 2\Delta P/(\mu \alpha c)$ and $V_e = R_m A / (\alpha c)$:

$$
\boxed{\frac{t}{V} = \frac{\mu \alpha c}{2 A^2 \Delta P}\,V + \frac{\mu R_m}{A \Delta P}}
$$

This **straight-line equation** in $(V,\, t/V)$ is used to determine $\alpha$ and $R_m$ experimentally:

- Slope: $m = \dfrac{\mu \alpha c}{2 A^2 \Delta P}$
- Intercept: $b = \dfrac{\mu R_m}{A \Delta P}$

Hence:

$$
\alpha = \frac{2 A^2 \Delta P\, m}{\mu c}, \qquad R_m = \frac{A \Delta P\, b}{\mu}
$$

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical — "KTU Model 14-Mark Question"

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem [KTU University Exam — July 2024 Model]**
> A **Plate and Frame Filter Press** has **20 frames**, each with an effective filtration area of **0.5 m²** per face and frame thickness of **40 mm**. A slurry containing **50 kg/m³** of solids is filtered at a constant pressure of **3.5 bar**. The viscosity of filtrate is **1.1 × 10⁻³ Pa·s** and the specific cake resistance is **2.0 × 10¹¹ m/kg**. The filter medium resistance is **1.2 × 10¹⁰ m⁻¹**. Calculate:
> (a) Total filtrate volume per cycle.
> (b) Time required for one filtration cycle.
> (c) Total cycle time if washing takes **25 %** of filtration time and discharging + assembly takes **600 s**.

#### Step 1 — Total Filtration Area

Each frame provides **2 faces** of filtration.

$$
A = 2 \times N \times A_{\text{plate}} = 2 \times 20 \times 0.5 = 20 \text{ m}^2
$$

#### Step 2 — Cake Volume per Frame

$$
V_{\text{frame}} = A_{\text{plate}} \times \frac{L}{2} = 0.5 \times \frac{0.040}{2} = 0.010 \text{ m}^3/\text{frame}
$$

#### Step 3 — Total Cake Volume for the Press

$$
V_{\text{cake}} = N \times V_{\text{frame}} = 20 \times 0.010 = 0.200 \text{ m}^3
$$

#### Step 4 — Mass of Solids in Cake

$$
M_s = V_{\text{cake}} \times \rho_s \approx 0.200 \times 1500 = 300 \text{ kg} \quad \text{(assuming }\rho_s = 1500 \text{ kg/m}^3\text{)}
$$

#### Step 5 — Total Filtrate Volume per Cycle

Using the material balance: solids in slurry = solids in cake.

$$
V_{\text{cycle}} = \frac{M_s}{c} = \frac{300}{50} = 6.0 \text{ m}^3
$$

#### Step 6 — Compute $K$ and $V_e$

$$
K = \frac{2 \Delta P}{\mu \alpha c} = \frac{2 \times 3.5 \times 10^5}{1.1 \times 10^{-3} \times 2.0 \times 10^{11} \times 50}
$$

Numerator: $7.0 \times 10^5$  
Denominator: $1.1 \times 10^{-3} \times 10^{13} = 1.1 \times 10^{10}$

$$
K = \frac{7.0 \times 10^5}{1.1 \times 10^{10}} = 6.364 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}
$$

$$
V_e = \frac{R_m A}{\alpha c} = \frac{1.2 \times 10^{10} \times 20}{2.0 \times 10^{11} \times 50} = \frac{2.4 \times 10^{11}}{1.0 \times 10^{13}} = 0.024 \text{ m}^3
$$

#### Step 7 — Filtration Time $t_f$ from Eq.4

$$
V^2 + 2 V V_e = K A^2 t_f
$$

$$
(6.0)^2 + 2(6.0)(0.024) = K \cdot (20)^2 \cdot t_f
$$

$$
36 + 0.288 = 36.288 = 6.364 \times 10^{-5} \times 400 \cdot t_f
$$

$$
36.288 = 0.025456 \cdot t_f
$$

$$
t_f = \frac{36.288}{0.025456} \approx 1425.6 \text{ s} \approx 23.76 \text{ min}
$$

#### Step 8 — Total Cycle Time

$$
t_{\text{cycle}} = t_f + 0.25\,t_f + 600 = 1.25 \times 1425.6 + 600 = 1782 + 600 = 2382 \text{ s}
$$

$$
\boxed{t_{\text{cycle}} \approx 2382 \text{ s} \approx 39.7 \text{ min}}
$$

#### Step 9 — Throughput (Bonus Insight)

$$
Q = \frac{V_{\text{cycle}}}{t_{\text{cycle}}} = \frac{6.0}{2382} = 2.52 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 9.07 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Verdict:** This press delivers **9 m³/h** of filtrate. If the upstream demand exceeds this, either **add more frames** (raise $A$) or **operate two presses in parallel**.

---

### 3.4 Throughput Optimisation — "The Optimal Cycle"

For a PFFP, filtration time $t_f$ grows quadratically with $V$ (Eq.4). Washing + discharging time $t_w + t_d$ is roughly **constant** per cycle. Therefore:

- **Short cycle → many batches** but each gives tiny $V$ → poor throughput.
- **Long cycle → huge batch** but downtime dominates → poor throughput.

The **optimal cycle time** occurs when $t_{\text{downtime}} = t_f$ (a celebrated textbook result). This is often a **3-mark short question** in KTU papers.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Architecture — Plate & Frame Filter Press

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PressBody["PLATE AND FRAME FILTER PRESS ASSEMBLY"]
        direction LR
        HeadPlate["HEAD PLATE / FIXED END"]
        FrameUnit["FRAME - HOLLOW CHAMBER"]
        PlateFlush["FLUSH PLATE / DRAIN PLATE"]
        FrameUnit2["FRAME - CHAMBER 2"]
        PlateWash["WASH PLATE / WASHING PLATE"]
        FrameUnitN["FRAME - CHAMBER N"]
        TailPlate["TAIL PLATE / MOVABLE END"]
    end

    SlurryPump["SLURRY PUMP\nCENTRIFUGAL / DIAPHRAGM"] -->|Feed inlet| FeedChannel["CENTRAL FEED CHANNEL\nruns through all frames"]
    FeedChannel --> FrameUnit
    FeedChannel --> FrameUnit2
    FeedChannel --> FrameUnitN

    WashPump["WASH WATER PUMP"] -->|Wash inlet| WashChannel["WASH WATER CHANNEL\ndrilled in wash plates only"]
    WashChannel --> PlateWash

    FrameUnit -->|Cake accumulates| CakeAccum["FILTER CAKE\n20 to 60 percent solids"]
    PlateFlush -->|Filtrate drains| FiltrateOut["FILTRATE OUTLET\nclear liquid to receiver"]
    PlateWash -->|Strong liquor out| FiltrateOut
    FrameUnit2 --> CakeAccum
    FrameUnitN --> CakeAccum

    CakeAccum --> DischargeStage["DISCHARGE STAGE\npress opens, frames separate,\ncake slabs drop by gravity"]
    DischargeStage --> ClothWash["CLOTH WASH STATION\nbackwash or spray nozzles"]
    ClothWash --> Reassembly["REASSEMBLY\nhydraulic ram closes press"]
    Reassembly --> SlurryPump

    ClosingRam["HYDRAULIC CLOSING RAM"] -->|Clamping force| TailPlate
    ClosingRam --> Reassembly
```

---

### 4.2 Schematic of Slurry Flow Inside the Press

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["SLURRY INLET\n3 to 10 bar"] --> B["CENTRAL\nFEED\nCHANNEL"]
    B --> C1["FRAME 1\n(cake)"]
    B --> C2["FRAME 2\n(cake)"]
    B --> C3["FRAME N\n(cake)"]
    C1 --> D1["FILTER CLOTH\n(polypropylene)"]
    C2 --> D2["FILTER CLOTH\n(polypropylene)"]
    C3 --> D3["FILTER CLOTH\n(polypropylene)"]
    D1 --> E1["GROOVED PLATE\nFILTRATE"]
    D2 --> E2["GROOVED PLATE\nFILTRATE"]
    D3 --> E3["GROOVED PLATE\nFILTRATE"]
    E1 --> F["FILTRATE\nOUTLET"]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    C1 -.->|WASH\nWATER| G1["WASH PLATE\nINLET"]
    C2 -.->|WASH\nWATER| G2["WASH PLATE\nINLET"]
    G1 --> H["STRONG LIQUOR\nOUTLET"]
    G2 --> H
```

---

### 4.3 Functional Component Architecture

| Subsystem | Components | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Clamping System** | Fixed head, tail plate, hydraulic ram, tie-rods | Holds stack against ΔP |
| **Filtration Chamber** | Frames + filter cloths + grooved plates | Cake formation + filtrate drainage |
| **Slurry Distribution** | Central feed channel, inlet elbow | Uniform feed to all chambers |
| **Washing Subsystem** | Wash plates, wash water channel, distribution valves | Soluble impurity removal |
| **Discharge Subsystem** | Plate shifter, cake scrapers, drop tray | Cake removal without manual handling |
| **Automation** | Pressure transmitters, flow transmitters, PLC | End-of-cycle detection |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024]**

**Q1. Differentiate between a recessed-plate filter press and a plate-and-frame filter press.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Recessed-Plate Press | Plate-and-Frame Press |
|---|---|---|
| Cake chamber | Formed by recesses in the plates | Formed by separate hollow frames |
| Cloth coverage | Single cloth per plate face | Separate cloths on both sides of each frame |
| Cake washing | Poor (no dedicated channel) | Excellent (uses wash plates) |
| Operating ΔP | Up to 30 bar | Up to 10 bar |
| Cake thickness | 25–50 mm | 10–50 mm |
| Discharge | Easier (cake sticks to cloth) | Manual scraping of each frame |

**(Marks: 1 mark for stating the chamber difference + 1 mark washing + 1 mark ΔP/operation.)**

---

**Q2. What is "specific cake resistance" $\alpha$? Why is it relevant for PFFP design?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Definition (1.5 marks):** Specific cake resistance $\alpha$ is defined as the resistance offered per unit mass of dry cake solids, having units of **m/kg**. It quantifies the inherent resistance of the particulate bed.
- **Design relevance (1.5 marks):** $\alpha$ directly determines the slope of the $t/V$ vs $V$ plot and hence the **filtration time** for a given volume. It is essential for **(i) sizing the press** (number of frames), **(ii) predicting throughput**, and **(iii) optimising the operating pressure** since $\alpha$ depends on $\Delta P$ through the compressibility relation $\alpha = \alpha_0 \Delta P^{1-s}$.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model 2024 Scheme, Module 2 Internal Choice Pattern]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Filtration Theory & Numerical**

**(a)** Derive the **constant-pressure filtration equation** $V^2 + 2VV_e = KA^2 t$ starting from Darcy's law. Clearly define every term. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Plate & Frame Filter Press with **15 frames**, each of effective area **0.6 m²** per face and frame thickness **35 mm**, handles a slurry of concentration **60 kg/m³** at constant ΔP = **4.0 bar**. Given: $\mu = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s, $\alpha = 1.5 \times 10^{11}$ m/kg, $R_m = 8 \times 10^9$ m⁻¹. Determine: **(i)** total filtrate volume per batch, **(ii)** filtration time, and **(iii)** optimum cycle throughput if downtime is 20 % of filtration time. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**
Full derivation as shown in **Section 3.1** above. Allocate:
- 2 marks for Darcy equation (Eq.1)
- 2 marks for substitution of $R_c = \alpha c V/A$ (Eq.2)
- 2 marks for integration and definition of $K$, $V_e$
- 1 mark for final boxed equation.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** $A = 2 \times 15 \times 0.6 = 18 \text{ m}^2$

**Step 2:** $V_{\text{cake/frame}} = 0.6 \times (0.035/2) = 0.0105 \text{ m}^3$

**Step 3:** $V_{\text{cake,total}} = 15 \times 0.0105 = 0.1575 \text{ m}^3$

Assuming $\rho_s \approx 1600 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $M_s = 0.1575 \times 1600 = 252 \text{ kg}$

**Step 4:** $V_{\text{cycle}} = M_s / c = 252 / 60 = 4.2 \text{ m}^3$

**Step 5:**
$$
K = \frac{2 \times 4.0 \times 10^5}{1.0 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.5 \times 10^{11} \times 60} = \frac{8.0 \times 10^5}{9.0 \times 10^9} = 8.89 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}
$$

$$
V_e = \frac{8 \times 10^9 \times 18}{1.5 \times 10^{11} \times 60} = \frac{1.44 \times 10^{11}}{9.0 \times 10^{12}} = 0.016 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 6:** Filtration time

$$
V^2 + 2 V V_e = K A^2 t_f
$$
$$
(4.2)^2 + 2 \times 4.2 \times 0.016 = 8.89 \times 10^{-5} \times (18)^2 \times t_f
$$
$$
17.64 + 0.1344 = 17.7744 = 0.0288 \cdot t_f
$$
$$
t_f = 617.2 \text{ s} \approx 10.29 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 7:** Optimum cycle time (when downtime = filtration time)

$$
t_{\text{cycle,opt}} = 2 t_f = 1234.4 \text{ s}
$$

$$
Q_{\text{opt}} = \frac{V}{t_{\text{cycle,opt}}} = \frac{4.2}{1234.4} = 3.40 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 12.24 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout**
> **Three common mistakes that cost 2–3 marks:**
> 1. Forgetting the **factor of 2** in $A = 2 N A_{\text{plate}}$ (frames filter on **both** sides).
> 2. Using the **wrong pressure units** — always convert bar → Pa (multiply by $10^5$).
> 3. Computing $V_e$ with $A$ instead of $A/\alpha c$ — recheck the definition carefully.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Construction, Operation & Washing**

**(a)** With a neat schematic, explain the **construction and working of a Plate and Frame Filter Press** with dedicated wash plates. Describe the **slurry flow path**, **filtrate path**, and **wash water path**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **mechanism of cake washing** in a PFFP. Define **wash efficiency $\eta_w$** and derive the relationship between washing time and filtration time. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution Outline

**(a) Construction (7 marks):**
- **1 mark:** Plate-frame alternation sequence (3 types of plates — flush, wash, frame).
- **2 marks:** Path tracing with arrows:
  - Slurry: pump → central channel → frames (cake).
  - Filtrate: cloth → grooves of flush plate → outlet manifold.
  - Wash water: separate channel → wash plate → through entire cake → flush plate → strong liquor outlet.
- **2 marks:** Closing mechanism (hydraulic ram), filter cloth material (polypropylene/nylon), and supports.
- **2 marks:** Cycle of operations: closure → priming → filtration → washing → opening → cake discharge → cloth cleaning → reassembly.

**(b) Washing (7 marks):**
- **1 mark:** Definition — washing is the **displacement** of mother liquor from the cake pores by fresh solvent/water.
- **2 marks:** Two stages of washing:
  - **Displacement washing** — wash water simply displaces the filtrate; no mixing.
  - **Diffusion washing** — solute diffuses out from within the cake particles.
- **2 marks:** Wash efficiency formula:

$$
\eta_w = \frac{\text{Recovered solute}}{\text{Total solute in cake}} = \frac{V_w}{V_w + V_{\text{displacement}}}
$$

where $V_{\text{displacement}}$ is the fraction of wash water that emerges at filtrate concentration.

- **2 marks:** Time relation — if wash flow rate $\dot{V}_w$ equals filtration rate at end $\dot{V}_f$, then $t_w / t_f = V_w / V$ (with correction factor $1/\eta_w$).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap — "Important Things to Remember" (Last-Minute Revision)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bullet Checklist for Last-Day Revision**
> - [x] **PFFP is a BATCH, CONSTANT-PRESSURE** filter — not continuous.
> - [x] Two variants: **Recessed-plate** (high ΔP, no washing) vs. **Plate-and-Frame with Wash Plates** (efficient washing).
> - [x] **Three types of plates:** Flush (drain), Wash (wash inlet), Frame (chamber).
> - [x] **Mother equation:** $V^2 + 2VV_e = KA^2 t$ — derived from Darcy's law.
> - [x] **Linear form:** $t/V = \frac{\mu\alpha c}{2A^2\Delta P} \cdot V + \frac{\mu R_m}{A\Delta P}$ — used for pilot data analysis.
> - [x] **Total area:** $A = 2 N A_{\text{plate}}$ (factor of 2 — both faces filter).
> - [x] **Cake volume per frame:** $V_{\text{frame}} = A_{\text{plate}} \cdot L / 2$.
> - [x] **Cycle = filtration + washing + discharge + assembly** — always add downtime.
> - [x] **Optimum cycle:** $t_{\text{cycle,opt}} = 2 t_f$ (when downtime equals filtration).
> - [x] **Compressibility:** $\alpha = \alpha_0 (\Delta P)^{1-s}$ — $s = 0$: incompressible; $s = 1$: fully compressible.
> - [x] **Pressure unit conversion:** 1 bar = **10⁵ Pa** (NOT $10^6$).
> - [x] **Wash efficiency:** $\eta_w = V_w / (V_w + V_{\text{displacement}})$.
> - [x] **Throughput formula:** $Q = V_{\text{cycle}} / t_{\text{cycle}}$ — key performance metric.
> - [x] **Industrial use:** polymer dewatering, dye cake, sugar mud, antibiotic mycelium, STP sludge.

---

### 5.4 Final KTU Examiner's Verdict

> [!WARNING]
> **Most Repeated KTU Question Themes (High-Probability for 2024 Exam)**
> 1. *Derivation of $V^2 + 2VV_e = KA^2 t$* — almost guaranteed.
> 2. *Numerical on filtration time + cycle throughput* — every paper since 2019.
> 3. *Working + slurry/filtrate path diagram* — at least 7 marks every cycle.
> 4. *Wash efficiency derivation* — Module 2 favourite.
> 5. *Compare recessed vs plate-frame* — guaranteed 3-marker.
>
> **Mark-Loss Hot-Spots:**
> - Wrong pressure units (bar vs. Pa)
> - Missing factor of 2 in area calculation
> - Not drawing arrows in the slurry/filtrate path
> - Confusing $V$ (filtrate) with cake volume

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT752 - POLYMERS AND SEPARATION PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Tubular

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

A **Tubular Reactor**, also known as a **Plug Flow Reactor (PFR)** or **Continuous Tubular Reactor (CTR)**, is a continuous-flow, cylindrical vessel in which the reacting fluid moves through as a "plug" — with **no axial mixing** but typically complete radial mixing. Each fluid element travels through the reactor with an identical residence time $\tau$, behaving like a batch reactor in motion along the coordinate axis.

In the context of **PEPOT752 – Polymers and Separation Processes**, tubular reactors hold special industrial prominence because:

- High-pressure free-radical polymerizations (e.g., **LDPE**, **PVC**) are conducted in tubular reactors.
- The narrow residence-time distribution favours tight **molecular-weight distribution (MWD)** control.
- Excellent heat-transfer capability through large surface-area-to-volume ratios.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition Box:** A tubular reactor is a steady-state, ideally non-backmixed continuous reactor characterized by a one-dimensional differential mass balance along its length $L$, governed by the design equation $F_{A0}\,dX/dV = -r_A$.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine a long, narrow hallway through which a crowd of people must walk from entrance to exit. **No one is allowed to overtake anyone else**, and **no one is allowed to turn back**. Every person who enters at the same instant also exits at the same instant. This is the essence of plug flow.

- The hallway = the **tube**.
- People walking in single-file = **fluid elements acting as coherent "plugs"**.
- The time taken to walk through = the **space time $\tau$**.

In polymer terms, every "plug" of monomer sees the same temperature profile and the same reaction history — which means **all polymer chains grow under identical conditions**, yielding a uniform product.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Examples** for KTU context:
> - **LDPE synthesis** (≈ 1000–3000 atm, 130–300 °C) — ICI-type tubular process.
> - **Nylon-6,6 melt polycondensation** — final finishing in tubular reactors.
> - **Polystyrene bulk polymerization** — continuous tubular pre-polymerizer.
> - **Poly(vinyl chloride)** suspension polymerization — jacket-cooled tubular loop.

### 1.3 Key Physical & Operational Metrics

- **Space Time** $\tau = V / v_0$ — measured in **seconds (s)** or **hours (h)**.
- **Space Velocity** $SV = v_0 / V = 1/\tau$ — measured in **s⁻¹** or **h⁻¹**.
- **Residence Time Distribution (RTD)** — for ideal PFR: $E(t) = \delta(t - \tau)$.
- **Superficial Velocity** $u_s = v_0 / A_c$ where $A_c$ is the cross-sectional area.
- **L/D Ratio** — typically $L/D \geq 100$ for ideal plug-flow approximation.
- **Reactor Péclet Number** $Pe_L = uL/D_{ax}$ — must be $\gg 1$ for plug-flow behaviour.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Assumptions of the Ideal Tubular Reactor

For KTU derivations, the following idealizations are mandatory:

1. **Steady state**: $\partial(\cdot)/\partial t = 0$ everywhere.
2. **Radial uniformity**: Concentration and temperature are uniform across any cross-section.
3. **No axial mixing (back-mixing)**: Each fluid element proceeds as a discrete plug.
4. **Constant physical properties** unless otherwise stated (density $\rho$, heat capacity $C_p$).
5. **One-dimensional flow** along the axial coordinate $z$ (or $V$).

### 2.2 Why and How — Operational Logic

**Step 1 — Inlet Boundary**: At $V = 0$ (reactor inlet), concentration of reactant A is $C_{A0}$ and molar flow rate is $F_{A0} = v_0 C_{A0}$ (for constant-density liquids) or $F_{A0} = v_0 C_{A0}$ (with $C_{A0}$ defined at inlet).

**Step 2 — Differential Element**: A thin slice of thickness $dz$ (volume $dV = A_c\,dz$) acts as a differential batch reactor over an infinitesimal contact time $dt = dz/u_s$.

**Step 3 — Mass Balance on Element**: $\text{In} - \text{Out} + \text{Generation} = 0$, leading to $F_{A0}\,dX = -r_A\,dV$.

**Step 4 — Integration**: Cumulative effect obtained by integrating from inlet ($X = 0$) to outlet ($X = X_f$), giving the **PFR Design Equation**.

**Step 5 — Heat Balance**: For non-isothermal operation, an energy differential balance is coupled with the mass balance, yielding simultaneous ODEs in $X$ and $T$.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates **all high-yield equations** for tubular reactors. Memorize the integral form and the explicit solutions for zero, first, and second-order kinetics — these are recurring 7-mark and 14-mark items.

| # | Equation Name | Mathematical Form | Variables / Units | Valid For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | PFR Design Equation (differential) | $F_{A0}\,\dfrac{dX}{dV} = -r_A$ | $F_{A0}$ [kmol/h], $V$ [m³] | General |
| 2 | PFR Design Equation (integral) | $V = F_{A0}\displaystyle\int_{0}^{X_f}\!\dfrac{dX}{-r_A}$ | $V$ [m³] | Steady isothermal PFR |
| 3 | Space-time form | $\tau = C_{A0}\displaystyle\int_{0}^{X_f}\!\dfrac{dX}{-r_A}$ | $\tau$ [s] | Constant $\rho$ |
| 4 | Space Velocity | $SV = \dfrac{v_0}{V} = \dfrac{1}{\tau}$ | $SV$ [s⁻¹] | General |
| 5 | First-order integral | $X_f = 1 - \exp(-k\tau)$ | Dimensionless | $-r_A = kC_A$ |
| 6 | Second-order integral | $\dfrac{X_f}{1-X_f} = k C_{A0}\,\tau$ | Dimensionless | $-r_A = kC_A^2$ |
| 7 | Zero-order integral | $X_f = k\tau / C_{A0}$ | Dimensionless | $-r_A = k$ |
| 8 | Variable-volume (gas) | $V = F_{A0}\displaystyle\int_{0}^{X_f}\!\dfrac{(1+\varepsilon X)\,dX}{-r_A}$ | [m³] | $\varepsilon = y_{A0}\,\delta$ |
| 9 | Non-isothermal energy balance | $\dfrac{dT}{dV} = \dfrac{(-\Delta H_r)\,(-r_A)}{\sum F_i C_{pi}}$ | K / m³ | Adiabatic/Non-adiabatic |
| 10 | Ergun pressure drop (packed) | $\dfrac{dP}{dz} = -\dfrac{150\,\mu(1-\varepsilon_p)^2}{\varepsilon_p^3\,d_p^2}\,u_s - \dfrac{1.75\,\rho(1-\varepsilon_p)}{\varepsilon_p^3\,d_p}\,u_s^2$ | Pa / m | Packed tubular |
| 11 | Fanning pressure drop (empty) | $\dfrac{dP}{dz} = -\dfrac{2 f \rho u_s^2}{D}$ | Pa / m | Empty tube |
| 12 | Péclet number (axial) | $Pe_L = \dfrac{u_s L}{D_{ax}}$ | Dimensionless | RTD criterion |
| 13 | Damköhler number | $Da = k\tau = \dfrac{k L}{u_s}$ | Dimensionless | Reactor benchmarking |
| 14 | Polymer MWD (ideal batch/PFR) | $\dfrac{\bar{X}_n}{1} = 1 + DP_n\,p$ where $p \approx X_f$ | Number avg. DP | Step-growth |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Polymer industry** uses PFRs because they minimize residence-time spread, producing **narrow polydispersity index (PDI ≈ 1.5–2.0)** for free-radical polymerization versus CSTR's broad distribution.
- **Petrochemical cracking** furnaces — large tubular reactors with external radiant heating.
- **Pharmaceutical & specialty chemicals** — chosen where selectivity to intermediate product matters (favoured for reactions with order $n > 0$).

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Tubular Reactor Design Equation (Mass Balance)

**Statement**: At steady state, derive the differential mole balance on reactant A for a tubular reactor.

Consider an elemental slice of volume $dV$ at axial position $z$.

$$
\text{Molar flow in at } z:\quad F_A \big\vert_z
$$

$$
\text{Molar flow out at } z + dz:\quad F_A \big\vert_{z+dz} = F_A \big\vert_z + \dfrac{dF_A}{dz}\,dz
$$

$$
\text{Rate of disappearance by reaction}:\quad -r_A\,dV
$$

At steady state with no accumulation:

$$
F_A\big\vert_z \;-\; \left(F_A\big\vert_z + \dfrac{dF_A}{dz}\,dz\right) \;-\; r_A\,dV \;=\; 0
$$

Simplify:

$$
-\dfrac{dF_A}{dz}\,dz = r_A\,dV
$$

Since $dV = A_c\,dz$, we get:

$$
-\dfrac{dF_A}{dV} = r_A
$$

Introducing fractional conversion $X$ via $F_A = F_{A0}(1 - X)$, so $dF_A = -F_{A0}\,dX$:

$$
-\dfrac{(-F_{A0}\,dX)}{dV} = r_A \quad\Longrightarrow\quad F_{A0}\,\dfrac{dX}{dV} = -r_A
$$

Separating variables:

$$
dV = \dfrac{F_{A0}\,dX}{-r_A}
$$

Integrating from $V=0$, $X=0$ to $V=V$, $X=X_f$:

$$
\boxed{\,V = F_{A0}\int_{0}^{X_f}\!\dfrac{dX}{-r_A}\,}
$$

For **constant density** ($v_0 = \text{const}$), $F_{A0} = v_0 C_{A0}$, and $\tau = V/v_0$, giving the equivalent space-time form:

$$
\boxed{\,\tau = C_{A0}\int_{0}^{X_f}\!\dfrac{dX}{-r_A}\,}
$$

---

### 3.2 Explicit Solutions for Common Kinetic Orders

**Case A — First-order irreversible reaction:** $A \rightarrow \text{Products}$, $\quad -r_A = kC_A$, with $C_A = C_{A0}(1-X)$.

Substituting into the integral form:

$$
\tau = C_{A0}\int_{0}^{X_f}\!\dfrac{dX}{k\,C_{A0}(1-X)}
$$

The $C_{A0}$ cancels:

$$
\tau = \dfrac{1}{k}\int_{0}^{X_f}\!\dfrac{dX}{1-X} = \dfrac{1}{k}\bigl[-\ln(1-X)\bigr]_{0}^{X_f}
$$

$$
\tau = -\dfrac{\ln(1 - X_f)}{k}
$$

Inversion gives the conversion:

$$
\boxed{\,X_f = 1 - e^{-k\tau}\,}
$$

**Case B — Second-order irreversible reaction:** $2A \rightarrow \text{Products}$, $\quad -r_A = kC_A^2$.

$$
\tau = C_{A0}\int_{0}^{X_f}\!\dfrac{dX}{k\,C_{A0}^2(1-X)^2}
$$

Pulling out constants:

$$
k C_{A0}\tau = \int_{0}^{X_f}\!\dfrac{dX}{(1-X)^2}
$$

Standard integral:

$$
\int\!\dfrac{dX}{(1-X)^2} = \dfrac{1}{1-X} + C
$$

Evaluating limits:

$$
k C_{A0}\tau = \left[\dfrac{1}{1-X}\right]_{0}^{X_f} = \dfrac{1}{1-X_f} - 1
$$

Solving for $X_f$:

$$
\boxed{\,\dfrac{X_f}{1-X_f} = k C_{A0}\tau\,}
$$

**Case C — Zero-order reaction:** $-r_A = k$.

$$
\tau = C_{A0}\int_{0}^{X_f}\!\dfrac{dX}{k} = \dfrac{C_{A0}X_f}{k}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,X_f = \dfrac{k\tau}{C_{A0}}\,}
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the Energy Balance for a Non-Isothermal Tubular Reactor

For an adiabatic PFR (no heat exchange with surroundings), a steady-state energy balance on the differential element gives:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{n} F_i C_{pi}\,\dfrac{dT}{dV} = -(-\Delta H_r)\cdot(-r_A) = (-\Delta H_r)\,r_A
$$

Re-arranging:

$$
\boxed{\,\dfrac{dT}{dV} = \dfrac{(-\Delta H_r)\,(-r_A)}{\sum F_i C_{pi}}\,}
$$

For a single reactant A with constant inlet flow and approximate constant heat capacity:

$$
F_{A0} C_{p,\text{tot}}\,\dfrac{dT}{dV} = (-\Delta H_r)\,(-r_A)
$$

Using $F_{A0}\,dX = -r_A\,dV$, this simplifies to a $T$–$X$ relation:

$$
\dfrac{dT}{dX} = \dfrac{(-\Delta H_r)\,F_{A0}}{F_{A0} C_{p,\text{tot}}} = \dfrac{(-\Delta H_r)}{C_{p,\text{tot}}}
$$

Integrating:

$$
\boxed{\,T - T_0 = \dfrac{(-\Delta H_r)}{C_{p,\text{tot}}}\,X\,}
$$

where $C_{p,\text{tot}} = \sum \theta_i C_{pi}$ (with $\theta_i = F_i/F_{A0}$), the **heat-capacity parameter**.

This is the **adiabatic temperature rise relation** — a cornerstone of PFR non-isothermal design.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Quick Comparison for KTU Exams

| Feature | PFR (Tubular) | CSTR (Back-mix) | Batch |
|---|---|---|---|
| RTD | $\delta(t-\tau)$ | $E(t) = \dfrac{1}{\tau}e^{-t/\tau}$ | $\delta(t-t_{\text{batch}})$ |
| $X$ for first-order | $1-e^{-k\tau}$ | $\dfrac{k\tau}{1+k\tau}$ | $1 - e^{-kt}$ |
| Volume for same $X$ | Smaller | Larger | — |
| Best for $n>0$ reactions | ✓ Higher $X$ | ✗ Lower $X$ | — |
| Best for $n<0$ reactions | ✗ Lower $X$ | ✓ Higher $X$ | — |
| Polymer PDI tendency | Narrower | Broader | Narrowest |
| Heat removal capability | Excellent (large $A/V$) | Good | Limited at scale |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** For $n$-th order kinetics with $n > 0$, a PFR always gives **higher conversion than a CSTR** of equal volume at steady state. This is a frequent 3-mark conceptual question.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Tubular Reactor System

The following Mermaid block renders the **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** for an industrial tubular polymerization reactor (e.g., LDPE high-pressure process). It captures the interaction between inlet streams, reactor body, jacket heat exchange, recycle, and downstream separation.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Monomer Feed Tank] --> A2[Pre-Heater]
    A2 --> A3[Compressor or Pump]
    A3 --> B1[Reactor Inlet Header]
    B1 --> B2[Tubular Reactor Section 1]
    B2 --> B3[Jacket Cooling In]
    B3 --> B2
    B2 --> B4[Reactor Section 2]
    B4 --> B5[Initiator Injection Port]
    B5 --> B6[Reactor Section 3]
    B6 --> B7[Side Stream Cooler Recycle]
    B7 --> B1
    B6 --> C1[Pressure Letdown Valve]
    C1 --> C2[High Pressure Separator]
    C2 --> C3[Low Pressure Separator]
    C3 --> C4[Polymer Extruder and Pelletizer]
    C4 --> C5[Product Storage Silo]
    C2 --> D1[Unreacted Monomer Recycle Compressor]
    D1 --> A3
    C3 --> D1
```

**Reading the diagram**:
- The **left-side loop** (B1 → B2 → B4 → B6 → B1 via recycle) is the reaction zone with multi-zone initiator injection — characteristic of LDPE high-pressure tubular process.
- The **bottom loop** (D1 → A3) represents monomer recycle — essential for economic viability when conversion per pass is 15–25%.
- Heat management is by **jacket cooling** and **side-stream coolers** — the defining feature enabling high-pressure exothermic free-radical polymerization.

---

### 4.2 Schematic of an Ideal Tubular Reactor Element

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    In1[Inlet at z = 0, X = 0, T = T0] --> Elem[Tubular Element of Volume dV = Ac dz]
    Elem -->|Mass balance: FA0 dX = -rA dV| Conv[Local Conversion X at z]
    Elem -->|Energy balance: dT/dV form| Temp[Local Temperature T at z]
    Conv --> Out1[Outlet at z = L, X = Xf, T = Tf]
    Temp --> Out1
    Elem -.Jacket heat duty UA T-Tj.-> Elem
```

**Interpretation**:
- Every differential element of volume $dV$ performs a differential mass + energy balance.
- The cumulative integration along $z$ gives the **outlet conversion $X_f$** and **temperature $T_f$**.
- Jacket heat duty $UA(T - T_j)$ represents the **cooling/heating flux** crossing the tube wall.

---

### 4.3 Conversion Profile Along the Reactor Length

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P0[z = 0, X = 0] --> P1[z = L/4, X rising rapidly]
    P1 --> P2[z = L/2, X moderate]
    P2 --> P3[z = 3L/4, X high but slope decreasing]
    P3 --> P4[z = L, X = Xf asymptote]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for KTU:** Conversion in an isothermal PFR **increases monotonically** with $z$ and approaches the equilibrium or stoichiometric limit asymptotically. The slope $dX/dz$ is largest at the inlet (high $C_A$) and tapers toward the outlet.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*State the assumptions made in the design of an ideal tubular reactor. Derive the space-time expression $\tau = C_{A0}\displaystyle\int_{0}^{X}\!\dfrac{dX}{-r_A}$ for a constant-density system.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The five canonical assumptions of an ideal PFR are:

1. **Steady-state** operation — $\partial(\cdot)/\partial t = 0$.
2. **No axial mixing** (pure plug flow).
3. **Radial uniformity** — flat velocity and concentration profiles.
4. **One-dimensional flow** along the tube axis $z$.
5. **Constant density** for liquid systems (or known $\varepsilon$ for gases).

From the differential mass balance $F_{A0}\,dX = -r_A\,dV$, with $V = v_0\,\tau$:

$$
F_{A0}\,dX = -r_A\,v_0\,d\tau
$$

For constant density, $F_{A0} = v_0 C_{A0}$. Dividing both sides by $v_0$:

$$
C_{A0}\,dX = -r_A\,d\tau
$$

Rearranging and integrating:

$$
\tau = C_{A0}\int_{0}^{X_f}\!\dfrac{dX}{-r_A}
$$

This is the **space-time design equation** of an ideal PFR. ✔

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Compare the performance of a PFR and a CSTR for an irreversible first-order reaction $A \rightarrow \text{Products}$. Show that for equal volumes, the PFR achieves a higher conversion.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

For first-order $-r_A = kC_A$:

- **PFR:** $X_{\text{PFR}} = 1 - e^{-k\tau}$ ⟹ $\dfrac{C_{A,\text{PFR,out}}}{C_{A0}} = e^{-k\tau}$
- **CSTR:** $\dfrac{C_{A,\text{CSTR,out}}}{C_{A0}} = \dfrac{1}{1+k\tau}$

Comparing the two expressions:

$$
e^{-k\tau} \;<\; \dfrac{1}{1+k\tau} \quad\text{for all } k\tau > 0
$$

Therefore, $C_{A,\text{PFR,out}} < C_{A,\text{CSTR,out}}$, which means **PFR conversion > CSTR conversion** for equal space time. ✔

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### **Question A — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the integral design equation for an isothermal tubular reactor in which the irreversible liquid-phase reaction $A + B \rightarrow \text{Products}$ proceeds with rate $-r_A = k C_A C_B$, where $C_{A0} = 2\,C_{B0}$. Express the conversion $X_f$ as a function of $k C_{A0}\tau$ and $\Theta_B = C_{B0}/C_{A0} = 0.5$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A PFR is to be designed for the gas-phase decomposition $A \rightarrow 2B + C$ obeying first-order kinetics with $k = 0.05\;\text{s}^{-1}$ at 1 atm and 600 K. Pure A enters at $v_0 = 10\;\text{L/s}$ and $C_{A0} = 0.1\;\text{mol/L}$. Compute (i) the reactor volume for 80% conversion, and (ii) the space time and space velocity.

---

##### **Solution to A(a) — Stoichiometry and Integral**

For $A + B \to \text{Products}$, with $\Theta_B = C_{B0}/C_{A0}$:

$$
C_A = C_{A0}(1 - X),\qquad C_B = C_{B0}(1 - X) = C_{A0}\Theta_B(1 - X)
$$

Wait — if stoichiometry is 1:1, then:

$$
C_B = C_{B0} - C_{A0}X = C_{A0}\Theta_B - C_{A0}X = C_{A0}(\Theta_B - X)
$$

With $\Theta_B = 0.5$:

$$
-r_A = k\,C_A C_B = k\,C_{A0}^2(1 - X)(\Theta_B - X)
$$

Plug into the PFR integral:

$$
\tau = C_{A0}\int_{0}^{X_f}\!\dfrac{dX}{k\,C_{A0}^2(1-X)(\Theta_B - X)}
$$

$$
kC_{A0}\tau = \int_{0}^{X_f}\!\dfrac{dX}{(1-X)(\Theta_B - X)}
$$

Using partial fractions (with $\Theta_B = 0.5 \neq 1$):

$$
\dfrac{1}{(1-X)(0.5-X)} = \dfrac{A}{1-X} + \dfrac{B}{0.5-X}
$$

Solving: $1 = A(0.5 - X) + B(1 - X)$. Setting $X = 1 \Rightarrow 1 = -0.5A \Rightarrow A = -2$. Setting $X = 0.5 \Rightarrow 1 = 0.5B \Rightarrow B = 2$.

$$
kC_{A0}\tau = \int_{0}^{X_f}\!\left(\dfrac{-2}{1-X} + \dfrac{2}{0.5-X}\right)dX
$$

Integrate:

$$
kC_{A0}\tau = 2\Bigl[\ln(1-X) - \ln(0.5-X)\Bigr]_{0}^{X_f}
$$

$$
kC_{A0}\tau = 2\ln\!\left(\dfrac{1-X_f}{0.5-X_f}\cdot\dfrac{0.5}{1}\right)
$$

Final implicit relation:

$$
\boxed{\,kC_{A0}\tau = 2\ln\!\left(\dfrac{1 - X_f}{2(0.5 - X_f)}\right)\,}
$$

##### **Solution to A(b) — Numerical Volume and Space Time**

**(i)** For first-order kinetics in a PFR: $X_f = 1 - e^{-k\tau}$

With $X_f = 0.80$ and $k = 0.05\;\text{s}^{-1}$:

$$
0.80 = 1 - e^{-0.05\tau}\;\Longrightarrow\;e^{-0.05\tau} = 0.20
$$

$$
\tau = -\dfrac{\ln(0.20)}{0.05} = \dfrac{1.6094}{0.05} = 32.19\;\text{s}
$$

Reactor volume:

$$
V = v_0 \tau = 10\;\text{L/s} \times 32.19\;\text{s} = 321.9\;\text{L} \approx 0.322\;\text{m}^3
$$

**(ii)** Space time: $\tau = 32.19\;\text{s}$. Space velocity:

$$
SV = \dfrac{1}{\tau} = \dfrac{1}{32.19} = 0.0311\;\text{s}^{-1}
$$

**Final Answers:** $V \approx 322\;\text{L}$, $\tau \approx 32.2\;\text{s}$, $SV \approx 0.031\;\text{s}^{-1}$. ✔

---

#### **Question B — [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Develop the steady-state energy balance for a non-isothermal tubular reactor and obtain the adiabatic temperature rise equation $T - T_0 = \dfrac{(-\Delta H_r)}{C_{p,\text{tot}}}\,X$. Discuss its significance in polymer reactor design.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A liquid-phase polymerization $A \to \text{Polymer}$ follows second-order kinetics with $k = 2.5 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{L/(mol·s)}$ at the operating temperature. Pure monomer A enters at $C_{A0} = 2.0\;\text{mol/L}$ with $v_0 = 0.5\;\text{L/s}$. Compute the PFR volume required to achieve 70% conversion.

---

##### **Solution to B(a) — Energy Balance Derivation**

Starting from a steady-state energy balance on a PFR element of volume $dV$:

$$
\sum_i F_i C_{pi}\,\dfrac{dT}{dV} = (-\Delta H_r)\,(-r_A)
$$

For constant $F_{A0}$ and using $F_{A0}\,dX/dV = -r_A$:

$$
F_{A0}\,C_{p,\text{tot}}\,\dfrac{dT}{dV} = (-\Delta H_r)\,F_{A0}\,\dfrac{dX}{dV}
$$

Cancel $F_{A0}$:

$$
C_{p,\text{tot}}\,\dfrac{dT}{dV} = (-\Delta H_r)\,\dfrac{dX}{dV}
$$

Convert to $T$–$X$ form:

$$
\dfrac{dT}{dX} = \dfrac{(-\Delta H_r)}{C_{p,\text{tot}}}
$$

Integrate from $X = 0$ at $T = T_0$ to $X = X_f$ at $T = T_f$:

$$
\boxed{\,T - T_0 = \dfrac{(-\Delta H_r)}{C_{p,\text{tot}}}\,X\,}
$$

**Significance in polymer reactor design:**
- For highly exothermic free-radical polymerizations (e.g., LDPE, $\Delta H_r \approx -95\;\text{kJ/mol}$), even small $X$ causes large $\Delta T$ — risk of **thermal runaway** and **MWD broadening**.
- Sets the upper bound on achievable conversion in adiabatic operation.
- Drives the design of multi-injection initiator zones in industrial LDPE tubular reactors.
- Couples reaction kinetics with temperature, often requiring simultaneous solution of mass and energy ODEs.

##### **Solution to B(b) — Volume for 70% Conversion**

For second-order kinetics in a PFR:

$$
\dfrac{X_f}{1 - X_f} = k C_{A0}\tau
$$

Substituting $X_f = 0.70$, $k = 2.5\times 10^{-3}\;\text{L/(mol·s)}$, $C_{A0} = 2.0\;\text{mol/L}$:

$$
\dfrac{0.70}{0.30} = 2.333
$$

$$
\tau = \dfrac{2.333}{(2.5\times 10^{-3})(2.0)} = \dfrac{2.333}{5.0\times 10^{-3}} = 466.7\;\text{s}
$$

Reactor volume:

$$
V = v_0 \tau = 0.5\;\text{L/s} \times 466.7\;\text{s} = 233.3\;\text{L} \approx 0.233\;\text{m}^3
$$

**Final Answer:** $V \approx 233\;\text{L}$. ✔

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls** — Where Students Typically Lose Marks:
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units** of $k$ before substituting. For second-order kinetics, $k$ has units of L/(mol·s) or m³/(kmol·s) — mismatch with $C_{A0}$ leads to wrong $\tau$. **(−2 marks)**
> 2. **Sign error in $(-\Delta H_r)$**: Using a positive value when the convention demands a negative sign for exothermic reactions causes the adiabatic temperature to drop instead of rise. **(−1 mark)**
> 3. **Confusing space-time $\tau$ with residence time $t$**: For an ideal PFR they are equal, but for non-ideal reactors they differ by the RTD integral. **(−1 mark)**
> 4. **Skipping the assumption statement** in derivations — KTU examiners routinely deduct **1 mark** if assumptions are not listed before the mass balance.
> 5. **Forgetting to write $C_{p,\text{tot}}$ as a weighted sum** $\sum \theta_i C_{pi}$ — many students write just $C_p$ without justification. **(−1 mark)**
> 6. **Misapplying the variable-volume form** for gas-phase reactions: forgetting the $(1+\varepsilon X)$ factor in the rate expression. **(−2 marks)**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [ ] **Ideal PFR** = no axial mixing, complete radial mixing, steady state.
- [ ] **Design Equation**: $V = F_{A0}\displaystyle\int_{0}^{X_f}\!\dfrac{dX}{-r_A}$ — the cornerstone identity.
- [ ] **Space-time form** for liquids: $\tau = C_{A0}\displaystyle\int_{0}^{X_f}\!\dfrac{dX}{-r_A}$.
- [ ] **First-order** → $X_f = 1 - e^{-k\tau}$.
- [ ] **Second-order** → $X_f/(1 - X_f) = k C_{A0}\tau$.
- [ ] **Zero-order** → $X_f = k\tau / C_{A0}$.
- [ ] **PFR > CSTR** in conversion for $n > 0$; opposite for $n < 0$.
- [ ] **Adiabatic temperature rise**: $\Delta T_{\text{ad}} = (-\Delta H_r) X / C_{p,\text{tot}}$.
- [ ] **Polymer industry** uses tubular reactors for **narrow MWD** (free-radical polymerization).
- [ ] **Multi-injection initiator zones** characteristic of industrial LDPE tubular process.
- [ ] **Pressure drop** computed via Ergun equation (packed) or Fanning equation (empty).
- [ ] **Péclet criterion** $Pe_L \gg 1$ justifies the plug-flow assumption.
- [ ] **Conversion asymptote**: $X_f \to X_{\text{eq}}$ monotonically; never overshoots equilibrium.
- [ ] Always **state assumptions** before writing any mass/energy balance — KTU 2024 scheme rewards methodology.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Spiral Wound and Hollow Fibre Membrane Reactors and Their Relative Merits
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT752 - POLYMERS AND SEPARATION PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Spiral Wound and Hollow Fibre Membrane Reactors and Their Relative Merits

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Membrane Modules — The Engineering Reality

A **membrane module** is the smallest functional, commercially replaceable unit that houses the membrane in a structurally supported configuration, enabling practical handling of feed, permeate, and retentate streams under defined pressure, temperature, and flow conditions. Without modules, raw membranes are mechanically fragile and geometrically unusable in industrial plants.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (verbatim):**
> Membrane modules are devices that house membrane elements and provide the structural framework, flow distribution, and pressure containment required to perform a membrane-based separation. The two most widely used configurations in chemical and biochemical engineering are the **Spiral Wound (SW)** module and the **Hollow Fibre (HF)** module.

### 1.2 What is a Membrane Reactor (MR)?

A **Membrane Reactor** is a process intensifying device that combines a chemical reaction with an *in-situ* membrane-based separation in a single physical unit. The membrane plays one of three functional roles inside the reactor:

| MR Type | Membrane Function | Engineering Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Extractor MR** | Selective product removal | Shifts equilibrium; overcomes thermodynamic limitation |
| **Distributor MR** | Controlled reactant dosing | Improves selectivity; prevents side reactions |
| **Contactor MR** | Interface between two phases | Enables two-phase reactions without emulsification |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Membrane Reactors Matter in KTU Context:**
> Conventional reactor + downstream separation = two units, two CAPEX items, intermediate storage, and a recycle loop. A Membrane Reactor collapses this into **one unit**, which is the core philosophy of **Process Intensification (PI)** — a recurring theme across PEPOT752 and the NEP 2020 outcome-based curriculum.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "Straws vs. Wrapped Sandwich"

To a student reading this for the first time:

- **Hollow Fibre Module** → Imagine bundling **thousands of drinking straws (each a membrane)** inside a pressure vessel. Fluid either flows *through* the straws (lumen-side) or *around* them (shell-side). One module may contain **>10,000 fibres**, giving an enormous surface area.

- **Spiral Wound Module** → Take a **flat "sandwich"** of two membrane sheets separated by a permeate spacer, glue three edges to form an **envelope**, open one edge to a central perforated tube, and then **roll the whole thing like a Swiss cake roll**. Feed flows axially across the membrane; permeate spirals inward to the central collection tube.

- **Membrane Reactor** → A **tea bag in a hot cup**. Tea leaves (catalyst) sit inside the porous bag (membrane); flavours (products) diffuse out into water (permeate) while tea leaves (catalyst) stay retained. One cup does both steeping AND filtration.

> [!TIP]
> **Memorisation Hook:** Hollow Fibre = **"many small tubes"**; Spiral Wound = **"flat sheets rolled up"**; Membrane Reactor = **"reaction + separation in one vessel."**

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following benchmarks are universal in membrane engineering and must be memorised:

- **Standard Spiral Wound element diameter:** *8 inches (≈ 200 mm)*
- **Standard Spiral Wound element length:** *40 inches (≈ 1016 mm)*
- **Hollow Fibre outer diameter range:** *100 µm to 2 mm*
- **Hollow Fibre inner diameter range:** *50 µm to 1 mm*
- **Packing density — Hollow Fibre:** up to **30,000 m²/m³**
- **Packing density — Spiral Wound:** typically **500 to 1000 m²/m³**
- **Operating pressure — Spiral Wound (RO):** up to **≈ 8 MPa (80 bar)**
- **Operating pressure — Hollow Fibre (RO):** typically **< 2.5 MPa (25 bar)**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Spiral Wound Module — Operational Anatomy

The spiral wound module is constructed in four distinct sub-components, layered in a precise sequence:

1. **Two membrane leaves** (a "leaf" = membrane skin + support layer)
2. **Feed-side spacer** (a plastic mesh that sits between the two leaves)
3. **Permeate-side spacer** (a porous tricot fabric glued to the inside of each membrane leaf)
4. **Permeate collection tube** (a perforated central PVC tube)

The envelope is glued on **three sides**; the **fourth open side** is glued to the perforated central tube. The whole assembly is then wrapped around the tube and sealed at the outer edge.

#### Flow Pattern (Step-by-Step)

- **Feed** enters axially at one end of the pressure vessel.
- Feed flows **parallel** to the central tube, **across** the membrane surface (crossflow operation).
- **Permeate** passes through the membrane skin, enters the porous permeate spacer, and spirals inward toward the central perforated tube.
- **Retentate (concentrate)** exits the other end of the vessel as brine or concentrate.

#### Why the Geometry Matters

The **crossflow velocity** is maintained high enough to scour foulants off the membrane surface. Without this, concentration polarisation and fouling would rapidly reduce the flux.

### 2.2 Hollow Fibre Module — Operational Anatomy

A hollow fibre module contains a large bundle of self-supporting hollow fibres potted at both ends with epoxy or polyurethane, sealed inside a cylindrical pressure shell. Two operational modes exist:

| Mode | Feed Location | Permeate Location | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Inside-Out (Lumen Feed)** | Inside fibre bore | Shell side | Reverse Osmosis, Gas Separation |
| **Outside-In (Shell Feed)** | Shell side | Inside fibre bore | Ultrafiltration, Microfiltration, Hemodialysis |

#### Critical Design Rule

> [!WARNING]
> In **outside-in** mode, the shell-side flow must be uniform across the bundle. If poorly distributed, fluid **channeling** occurs (preferential paths through low-resistance zones), drastically reducing effective membrane area utilisation.

### 2.3 The Concept of Packing Density

The packing density (φ) is the membrane area per unit module volume:

$$\varphi = \frac{A_{membrane}}{V_{module}} \quad [\text{m}^2/\text{m}^3]$$

This single metric captures how compactly a membrane configuration packages separation area. It is the dominant reason hollow fibre dominates UF/MF and spiral wound dominates RO.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Equations

| # | Formula | Symbols | Typical Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\varphi = A_{m}/V$ | $A_m$ membrane area, $V$ module volume | m²/m³ | Module compactness |
| 2 | $A_{HF} = \pi \, d_{o} \, L \, N$ | $d_o$ outer fibre dia, $L$ fibre length, $N$ number of fibres | m² | HF membrane area |
| 3 | $A_{SW} = 2 \, L \, W$ | $L$ leaf length, $W$ leaf width | m² | SW membrane area per leaf |
| 4 | $J = L_{p}\,(\Delta P - \Delta \pi)$ | $J$ flux, $L_p$ permeability, $\Delta P$ TMP, $\Delta \pi$ osmotic pressure diff | m/s, m/(s·Pa) | Solution-Diffusion flux |
| 5 | $\Delta P_{HF} = \dfrac{32\,\mu\,L\,v}{d_i^{2}}$ | $\mu$ viscosity, $v$ velocity, $d_i$ inner dia | Pa | Hagen-Poiseuille bore ΔP |
| 6 | $Re_{HF} = \dfrac{\rho\,v\,d_i}{\mu} = \dfrac{4\,\rho\,\dot{m}}{\pi\,\mu\,N\,d_i}$ | $\rho$ density, $\dot{m}$ total mass flow | — | Flow regime check |
| 7 | $TMP = \dfrac{P_{f} + P_{r}}{2} - P_{p}$ | $P_f, P_r, P_p$ feed/retentate/permeate pressures | Pa | Driving force calculation |
| 8 | $\eta_{MR} = \dfrac{X_{with\,MR}}{X_{eq\,PFR}}$ | $X$ conversion | — | Membrane reactor enhancement |
| 9 | $\dot{V}_{p} = J \cdot A_{m}$ | $\dot{V}_p$ permeate flow | m³/s | Permeate production rate |
| 10 | $K_{overall} = \dfrac{1}{\dfrac{1}{K_{feed}} + \dfrac{1}{K_{m}} + \dfrac{1}{K_{perm}}}$ | Resistances in series | m/s | Resistance-in-series model |

> [!TIP]
> **Formula 4 (Solution-Diffusion)** is the single most-tested flux relation across PEPOT752 Module 2 derivations. Memorise it as the KTU "default" flux model.

### 2.5 Why These Equations Matter in Engineering Practice

- Equations 1–3 are used during **module sizing** (CAPEX decisions).
- Equations 4 and 7 govern **operating point selection** (OPEX and energy).
- Equation 8 is the **figure of merit** for any membrane reactor analysis.
- Equation 10 is the **diagnostic tool** to localise fouling during plant troubleshooting.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I — Packing Density of a Hollow Fibre Module

Consider a cylindrical pressure vessel of inner diameter $D_{v}$ and length $L$. Inside it, $N$ hollow fibres of outer diameter $d_o$ are packed in a hexagonal close-packed arrangement.

**Step 1:** Total membrane area of all fibres combined.

$$A_{HF} = (\pi \, d_{o}) \cdot L \cdot N$$

*Logic:* Each fibre contributes surface area equal to its circumference ($\pi d_o$) times its length ($L$). Multiplying by the total fibre count $N$ yields total area.

**Step 2:** Volume occupied by the module.

$$V_{module} = \dfrac{\pi}{4}\,D_{v}^{2}\,L$$

*Logic:* The module is essentially a cylinder. Standard cylinder volume formula.

**Step 3:** Substitute and simplify.

$$\varphi_{HF} = \dfrac{A_{HF}}{V_{module}} = \dfrac{\pi\, d_{o}\, L\, N}{\dfrac{\pi}{4}\, D_{v}^{2}\, L}$$

$$\varphi_{HF} = \dfrac{4\, N\, d_{o}}{D_{v}^{2}}$$

**Step 4:** Express the maximum fibre count in terms of packing efficiency $\eta_{pack}$ (≈ 0.91 for hexagonal close packing).

$$N_{max} = \eta_{pack}\cdot\dfrac{\pi\,D_{v}^{2}}{2\sqrt{3}\,d_{o}^{2}}$$

*Logic:* For hexagonal close packing, each fibre occupies a hexagonal cell of area $2\sqrt{3}\,d_o^{2}/4$ projected on the cross-section. The shell cross-sectional area $\pi D_v^2/4$ divided by this cell area gives theoretical maximum fibre count, scaled by packing efficiency.

**Step 5:** Substitute back.

$$\varphi_{HF,max} = \dfrac{4\, d_{o}}{D_{v}^{2}} \cdot \eta_{pack}\cdot\dfrac{\pi\,D_{v}^{2}}{2\sqrt{3}\,d_{o}^{2}}$$

$$\varphi_{HF,max} = \dfrac{2\pi\,\eta_{pack}}{\sqrt{3}\,d_{o}}$$

**Step 6:** Insert $\eta_{pack} = 0.91$.

$$\varphi_{HF,max} = \dfrac{2\pi\,(0.91)}{\sqrt{3}\,d_{o}} = \dfrac{3.30}{d_{o}}$$

*Engineering Interpretation:* For a fibre of $d_o = 200 \, \mu m = 2\times10^{-4}\,m$:

$$\varphi_{HF,max} = \dfrac{3.30}{2\times 10^{-4}} = 16{,}500\, \text{m}^2/\text{m}^3$$

This justifies the industrial benchmark of **up to 30,000 m²/m³** (theoretical upper limit with thinner fibres of 100 µm).

---

### 3.2 Derivation II — Packing Density of a Spiral Wound Module

Each spiral wound envelope contains **two membrane leaves**. For one leaf of length $L$ and width $W$:

**Step 1:** Membrane area per leaf.

$$A_{leaf} = L \cdot W$$

**Step 2:** Membrane area per envelope (two leaves glued back-to-back).

$$A_{env} = 2 \cdot L \cdot W$$

**Step 3:** Volume of the rolled envelope (approximated as a cylinder of outer radius $R_o$ and inner radius $R_t$, where $R_t$ is the radius of the central permeate tube).

$$V_{env} = \pi\,(R_{o}^{2} - R_{t}^{2})\,L$$

*Logic:* The total length of the membrane sheet, when rolled, occupies an annular cylinder volume between the central tube and the outer jacket.

**Step 4:** The sheet length $S$ when unrolled relates to the spiral geometry as:

$$S = 2\pi\,\dfrac{(R_{o}^{2} - R_{t}^{2})}{W}$$

*Logic:* When the sheet of width $W$ is rolled, the projected annular area ($W \cdot S$) equals the annulus area $\pi(R_o^2 - R_t^2)$.

**Step 5:** Substituting $S = 2L$ (because two leaves each of length $L$ form the envelope perimeter):

$$2L = \dfrac{2\pi\,(R_{o}^{2} - R_{t}^{2})}{W}$$

$$L = \dfrac{\pi\,(R_{o}^{2} - R_{t}^{2})}{W}$$

**Step 6:** Membrane area of envelope:

$$A_{env} = 2\,L\,W = 2\pi\,(R_{o}^{2} - R_{t}^{2})$$

**Step 7:** Packing density.

$$\varphi_{SW} = \dfrac{A_{env}}{V_{env}} = \dfrac{2\pi\,(R_{o}^{2} - R_{t}^{2})}{\pi\,(R_{o}^{2} - R_{t}^{2})\,L} = \dfrac{2}{L}$$

**Step 8:** For a standard element length $L = 1.016\,m$ (40 inches):

$$\varphi_{SW} = \dfrac{2}{1.016} \approx 1.97\,\text{m}^2/\text{m}^3 \text{ per envelope}$$

*Engineering note:* Real elements stack multiple envelopes (typically 4–6) per element, giving effective packing densities of **500–1000 m²/m³** when the permeate spacer and feed spacer thicknesses are accounted for. The factor of ~1000× lower packing density versus hollow fibre is the central trade-off.

---

### 3.3 Derivation III — Membrane Reactor Conversion Enhancement (Extractor Mode)

Consider an equilibrium-limited reaction $A \rightleftharpoons B + C$. In a conventional Plug Flow Reactor (PFR), the maximum achievable conversion is $X_{eq,PFR}$.

When product $B$ is continuously removed via a selective membrane, Le Chatelier's principle drives the reaction forward.

**Step 1:** Molar balance on species $B$ in a differential slice $dV$:

$$F_{B0}\,dX = r_{B}\,dV - J_{B}\,dA_{m}$$

*Logic:* Production rate of $B$ by reaction minus removal rate of $B$ through membrane equals accumulation.

**Step 2:** At steady state, define enhancement factor:

$$\eta_{MR} = \dfrac{r_{B,\,MR}}{r_{B,\,PFR}}$$

For first-order reversible kinetics with rapid membrane removal, the Damköhler number analogy gives:

$$\eta_{MR} = 1 + Da_{m}, \quad Da_{m} = \dfrac{k_{m}\,A_{m}}{F_{B0}}$$

where $k_m$ is the membrane mass-transfer coefficient.

**Step 3:** Conversion with membrane (approximation for thin membrane, high permeability):

$$X_{MR} \approx X_{eq,PFR}\,(1 + Da_{m})$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Limiting case:** As $Da_m \to \infty$ (perfect membrane), $X_{MR} \to 1$ (complete conversion), demonstrating the equilibrium-shift advantage of membrane reactors. This is the **headline result** of any extractor-type MR.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Spiral Wound vs. Hollow Fibre

| Parameter | Spiral Wound | Hollow Fibre |
|---|---|---|
| Packing density (m²/m³) | 500–1000 | 3,000–30,000 |
| Operating pressure (bar) | Up to 80 | Typically < 25 |
| Fouling resistance | Moderate (good crossflow) | Poor in inside-out; better outside-in |
| Cleaning ease | Easy (single element) | Difficult (fibres plug) |
| Replacement cost | Moderate | High per module |
| Plugging tolerance | High (open channel) | Low (fine bore) |
| Typical application | RO, NF, some UF | UF, MF, RO (DuPont B-9), gas separation |
| Feed pretreatment | Standard | Demanding |
| Backflush capability | Limited | Yes (outside-in mode) |
| Capital cost per m² | Low | Lowest |
| Pressure drop | Moderate | High (laminar in bore) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Spiral Wound Module — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Feed Inlet] --> B[Feed Spacer Mesh]
    B --> C[Membrane Leaf 1]
    C --> D[Permeate Spacer Tricot]
    D --> E[Membrane Leaf 2]
    E --> F[Crossflow Across Membrane]
    F --> G[Permeate Spiral Inward]
    G --> H[Central Perforated Tube]
    H --> I[Permeate Outlet]
    B --> J[Retentate Exits Axially]
    J --> K[Concentrate Outlet]

    subgraph "Pressure Vessel"
        B
        C
        D
        E
        F
        G
        H
    end
```

### 4.2 Hollow Fibre Module — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1[Feed In - Shell Side] --> Q1[Distribution Baffle]
    Q1 --> R1[Crossflow Around Fibres]
    R1 --> S1[Membrane Permeation Inward]
    S1 --> T1[Permeate Collection Manifold]
    T1 --> U1[Permeate Outlet]
    R1 --> V1[Retentate Outlet]

    subgraph "Fibre Bundle"
        F1[Fibre 1]
        F2[Fibre 2]
        F3[Fibre N]
    end

    R1 --> F1
    R1 --> F2
    R1 --> F3
```

### 4.3 Membrane Reactor — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    MR1[Reactant Feed A] --> MR2[Reactor Zone with Catalyst]
    MR2 --> MR3[Reaction: A to B plus C]
    MR3 --> MR4[Membrane Zone]
    MR4 --> MR5{Product Separation}
    MR5 --> MR6[Permeate Product B - Removed Selectively]
    MR5 --> MR7[Retentate Unreacted A - Recycle]
    MR2 -.-> MR8[Equilibrium Shifted Forward]
    MR8 -.-> MR3

    style MR8 stroke-dasharray: 5 5
```

### 4.4 Comparative Selection Logic — Which Module When?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ST1{Application Type?}
    ST1 -->|Reverse Osmosis High Pressure| SW1[Choose Spiral Wound]
    ST1 -->|UF MF Low Pressure| HF1[Choose Hollow Fibre]
    ST1 -->|Gas Separation| HF2[Choose Hollow Fibre]
    ST1 -->|High Fouling Feed| ST2{Suspended Solids Level?}
    ST2 -->|Low| SW2[Choose Spiral Wound]
    ST2 -->|High| ST3{Backflush Required?}
    ST3 -->|Yes| HF3[Choose Hollow Fibre Outside-In]
    ST3 -->|No| SW3[Choose Spiral Wound with Pretreatment]

    SW1 --> END1[End: Spiral Wound Selection]
    HF1 --> END2[End: Hollow Fibre Selection]
    SW2 --> END1
    HF2 --> END2
    HF3 --> END2
    SW3 --> END1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the diagrams:** All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `ST1`, `MR4`, `END1`) and free of reserved Mermaid keywords (`end`, `graph`, `subgraph`, `style`). Labels use plain uppercase text for clean rendering.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Pattern]**
*"Differentiate between spiral wound and hollow fibre membrane modules with respect to packing density and pressure handling capacity."*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Spiral Wound | Hollow Fibre |
|---|---|---|
| Packing Density | 500–1000 m²/m³ | 3000–30,000 m²/m³ |
| Pressure Handling | Up to 80 bar (RO duty) | Typically < 25 bar |

**Conclusion:** Spiral wound is preferred for high-pressure RO applications due to robust mechanical containment; hollow fibre is preferred for high-area, low-pressure UF/MF due to superior packing density. **(3 marks)**

---

#### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper Pattern]**
*"What is a membrane reactor? List any three types with one example each."*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A membrane reactor integrates a chemical reaction with a membrane-based separation inside a single unit, enabling **in-situ** product removal, reactant distribution, or interfacial contacting.

1. **Extractor MR** — e.g., dehydrogenation of cyclohexane to benzene with Pd membrane removing H₂.
2. **Distributor MR** — e.g., controlled O₂ dosing via dense ceramic membrane in oxidative coupling of methane.
