3. **Contactor MR** — e.g., two-phase enzymatic hydrolysis with hollow fibre membrane immobilising the enzyme at the interface. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> Per **KTU 2024 ESE regulation**, Part B offers Module Internal Choice. Students answer EITHER Question A OR Question B. Each sub-part carries **7 marks**.

---

#### Question A — Set 1 (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the construction and working of a **spiral wound membrane module**. Discuss the role of feed spacer, permeate spacer, and the central perforated tube. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Construction (4 marks):**

1. **Two membrane leaves** are prepared by cutting flat-sheet membrane (skin + support) to a defined $L \times W$ geometry.
2. A **feed spacer mesh** is placed between the two leaves; this maintains the feed-side channel and promotes turbulence.
3. A **permeate spacer (porous tricot fabric)** is glued to the back (support side) of each membrane leaf.
4. Three edges of the sandwich are sealed with adhesive; the **fourth (open) edge** is glued to a **perforated central PVC permeate tube**.
5. The assembly is rolled around the central tube and the outer surface is wrapped with an outer tape/jacket.

**Working (2 marks):**

- Feed enters the pressure vessel axially and flows across the membrane surface.
- Permeate passes through the membrane skin, travels spirally through the permeate spacer, and exits via the central tube.
- Retentate exits the other end as concentrate.

**Role of Components (1 mark):**

- *Feed spacer:* promotes turbulence, reduces concentration polarisation, supports membrane.
- *Permeate spacer:* provides low-resistance permeate flow path.
- *Central tube:* collects and discharges permeate.

**(b)** A spiral wound element has a leaf length of 0.9 m and a leaf width of 0.7 m. The element contains 4 envelopes. Calculate (i) total membrane area, and (ii) approximate packing density if the element diameter is 0.2 m. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Leaf length $L = 0.9\,m$
- Leaf width $W = 0.7\,m$
- Number of envelopes $n = 4$
- Element diameter $D = 0.2\,m$

**(i) Total Membrane Area (4 marks):**

Area per leaf:
$$A_{leaf} = L \times W = 0.9 \times 0.7 = 0.63\,\text{m}^2$$

Area per envelope (two leaves):
$$A_{env} = 2 \times A_{leaf} = 2 \times 0.63 = 1.26\,\text{m}^2$$

Total area for 4 envelopes:
$$A_{total} = n \times A_{env} = 4 \times 1.26 = 5.04\,\text{m}^2$$

**(ii) Packing Density (3 marks):**

Approximating the module as a cylinder of length equal to the leaf length $L = 0.9\,m$ (since all envelopes stack axially along the central tube of equivalent length):

$$V_{module} = \dfrac{\pi}{4}\,D^2\,L = \dfrac{\pi}{4}\,(0.2)^2\,(0.9)$$

$$V_{module} = \dfrac{3.1416}{4} \times 0.04 \times 0.9 = 0.02827\,\text{m}^3$$

$$\varphi = \dfrac{A_{total}}{V_{module}} = \dfrac{5.04}{0.02827} \approx 178.3\,\text{m}^2/\text{m}^3$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Question A(b):** Students commonly forget that each envelope contains **TWO leaves**, halving the membrane area calculation. This single error costs **2 of 7 marks**. Always verify the number of leaves per envelope before substitution.

---

#### Question B — Set 2 (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the construction and working of a **hollow fibre membrane module**. Compare inside-out and outside-in flow configurations. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Construction (3 marks):**

1. Thousands of hollow fibres (outer diameter 100 µm–2 mm; inner diameter 50 µm–1 mm) are bundled together.
2. Both ends are **potted in epoxy or polyurethane**, forming a leak-tight seal against the pressure shell.
3. The bundle is housed inside a cylindrical stainless-steel or FRP pressure vessel.
4. End caps provide inlet/outlet ports for feed, retentate, and permeate.

**Working (2 marks):**

- Feed enters one end and either flows through the fibre bores (inside-out) or around the fibres (outside-in).
- Permeate passes through the membrane wall and exits through a port on the shell side.
- Retentate exits from the opposite end of the module.

**Inside-Out vs. Outside-In (2 marks):**

| Aspect | Inside-Out | Outside-In |
|---|---|---|
| Feed Location | Fibre bore | Shell side |
| Permeate Location | Shell side | Fibre bore |
| Pressure Drop | High (Hagen-Poiseuille) | Low |
| Suspended Solids Tolerance | Poor (plugging) | Good |
| Backflush Capability | Not feasible | Yes |
| Typical Application | RO, gas separation | UF, MF, hemodialysis |

---

**(b)** A hollow fibre module contains 5000 fibres, each of outer diameter 0.3 mm and length 1.0 m, housed in a vessel of inner diameter 0.1 m. Calculate (i) total membrane area, (ii) packing density, and (iii) comment on whether this represents efficient packing. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $N = 5000$
- $d_o = 0.3\,mm = 3 \times 10^{-4}\,m$
- $L = 1.0\,m$
- $D_v = 0.1\,m$

**(i) Total Membrane Area (3 marks):**

$$A_{HF} = \pi \, d_o \, L \, N$$

$$A_{HF} = \pi \times (3 \times 10^{-4}) \times 1.0 \times 5000$$

$$A_{HF} = 3.1416 \times 3 \times 10^{-4} \times 5000$$

$$A_{HF} = 4.712\,\text{m}^2$$

**(ii) Packing Density (3 marks):**

$$V_{module} = \dfrac{\pi}{4}\,D_v^2\,L = \dfrac{\pi}{4}\,(0.1)^2\,(1.0) = 7.854 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}^3$$

$$\varphi_{HF} = \dfrac{A_{HF}}{V_{module}} = \dfrac{4.712}{7.854 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$\varphi_{HF} \approx 600\,\text{m}^2/\text{m}^3$$

**(iii) Comment (1 mark):**

Using the theoretical maximum formula from Section 3.1:
$$\varphi_{HF,max} = \dfrac{3.30}{d_o} = \dfrac{3.30}{3 \times 10^{-4}} = 11{,}000\,\text{m}^2/\text{m}^3$$

The achieved packing density (600 m²/m³) is only ~5.5% of the theoretical maximum. The vessel is **severely under-utilised**; increasing $N$ would dramatically improve packing density without changing the vessel geometry.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Question B(b):** A frequent mistake is using the fibre **inner diameter** $d_i$ instead of the **outer diameter** $d_o$ in the membrane area equation. Since the membrane surface in contact with the feed (or permeate) is the outer cylindrical surface, **$d_o$ is mandatory**. This error typically costs **1–2 marks**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for PEPOT752 Module 2:**

- ☐ **Spiral Wound Module:** flat membrane leaves rolled around a central permeate tube; preferred for **RO**; packing density **500–1000 m²/m³**; pressure up to **80 bar**.
- ☐ **Hollow Fibre Module:** thousands of fibres in a pressure shell; preferred for **UF/MF/gas separation**; packing density **3,000–30,000 m²/m³**; pressure typically **<25 bar**.
- ☐ **Inside-Out Mode:** feed inside the bore; high ΔP (Hagen-Poiseuille); used in RO.
- ☐ **Outside-In Mode:** feed in shell; low ΔP; supports backflush; used in UF/MF.
- ☐ **Membrane Reactor = Reaction + Separation** in a single unit.
- ☐ **Three MR types:** Extractor (product removal), Distributor (reactant dosing), Contactor (interfacial).
- ☐ **Packing Density Formula:** $\varphi = A_m / V$
- ☐ **HF Area Formula:** $A_{HF} = \pi d_o L N$
- ☐ **SW Area Formula:** $A_{SW} = 2 L W$ (two leaves per envelope)
- ☐ **Flux Equation:** $J = L_p (\Delta P - \Delta\pi)$
- ☐ **TMP Equation:** $TMP = (P_f + P_r)/2 - P_p$
- ☐ **Extractor MR Enhancement:** $\eta_{MR} \approx 1 + Da_m$
- ☐ **Spiral Wound:** Each envelope = **2 leaves** (very common KTU pitfall).
- ☐ **Hollow Fibre:** Use $d_o$ (outer diameter) in area calculation, NOT $d_i$.
- ☐ **KTU Examiner Rule:** Always quote **units** and **assumptions** explicitly for full marks.
- ☐ **Process Intensification Link:** Membrane reactors are a flagship PI technology — connect this concept to NEP 2020 outcome-based learning whenever possible.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Membrane Filtration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT752 - POLYMERS AND SEPARATION PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Membrane Filtration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Membrane Filtration** is a **pressure-driven** physical separation process in which a **semi-permeable membrane** acts as a selective barrier, allowing certain species (solvent, small ions, or gases) to pass through while rejecting others (suspended solids, macromolecules, salts, or pathogens) based on **size exclusion, charge, or chemical affinity**.

Under the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, membrane filtration is classified as one of the *rate-governed* separation processes (along with adsorption, ion exchange, and chromatography), where the controlling factor is the **mass transfer rate across the membrane interface** rather than thermodynamic phase equilibrium.

### Classification by Pore Size / Cut-Off

| Filter Type | Approx. Pore / MWCO | Typical Rejects |
|---|---|---|
| **Microfiltration (MF)** | 0.1 – 10 µm | Bacteria, suspended solids |
| **Ultrafiltration (UF)** | 1 – 100 nm (1k – 500k Da MWCO) | Proteins, colloids, viruses |
| **Nanofiltration (NF)** | ~0.5 – 5 nm (200 – 1000 Da) | Divalent salts, sugars, dyes |
| **Reverse Osmosis (RO)** | < 0.5 nm (non-porous) | Monovalent salts, water |

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Think of membrane filtration as a microscopic, precision-engineered sieve.** Just like a kitchen sieve holds back boiled pasta while letting the hot water drain through, a membrane filter allows water (or solvent) molecules to pass while holding back particles that are *larger* than the membrane's pores. The **smaller the pores**, the **finer** the separation you can achieve — but the **harder** you must push (i.e., the more pressure you apply) to drive the fluid through.

A **real-world analogy** students easily relate to is a **coffee filter paper**: water and aroma molecules drip through, but the coffee grounds remain trapped. In membrane filtration, instead of gravity, a **transmembrane pressure (TMP)** of 0.5 – 80 bar (depending on the filter type) forces the permeate across the barrier.

### Key Operating Modes

1. **Dead-end (stagnant) mode** — Feed flow is perpendicular to the membrane; cake builds up rapidly.
2. **Cross-flow (tangential) mode** — Feed flows *parallel* to the membrane surface, sweeping away deposited solids and reducing fouling.

### Standard Engineering Metrics

- **Flux (J)** measured in **L·m⁻²·h⁻¹ (LMH)** or **m³·m⁻²·s⁻¹**.
- **Transmembrane pressure (TMP)** in **bar** or **Pa**.
- **Molecular Weight Cut-Off (MWCO)** in **Daltons (Da)**.
- **Rejection coefficient (R)** — dimensionless, between 0 and 1.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step

1. **Feed Introduction** — The feed solution enters the membrane module under pressure.
2. **Selective Transport** — Solvent (permeate) is driven through the membrane by a *chemical potential gradient* — most commonly realized as a **pressure difference** (ΔP) for MF/UF/NF/RO, or an *electrical potential* (electrodialysis) or *concentration gradient* (dialysis).
3. **Rejection** — Solutes whose size, charge, or affinity prevent passage are retained on the **retentate** (concentrate) side.
4. **Concentration Polarisation** — Solutes accumulate at the membrane surface, forming a *boundary layer* of higher concentration than the bulk feed.
5. **Steady-State Flux** — Eventually, a balance is reached between convective transport to the membrane and back-diffusion away from it, establishing a pseudo-steady permeate flux.

### 2.2 Why and How — The Core Physics

> The membrane is essentially a **thin barrier** offering finite resistance to flow. The solvent flux is governed by **Darcy's law for porous media**, modified by the membrane's hydrodynamic resistance $R_m$ and the *fouling resistance* $R_f$ that develops during operation.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $J = \dfrac{V_p}{A_m \, \Delta t}$ | Permeate flux | m·s⁻¹ or LMH |
| 2 | $\text{TMP} = \dfrac{P_{in} + P_{out}}{2} - P_{p}$ | Transmembrane pressure | Pa or bar |
| 3 | $J = \dfrac{\text{TMP}}{\mu \, R_{total}}$ | Resistance-in-series flux | m·s⁻¹ |
| 4 | $R_{total} = R_m + R_f + R_{cp}$ | Total resistance (membrane + fouling + conc. polarisation) | m⁻¹ |
| 5 | $R_j = \dfrac{C_f - C_p}{C_f}$ | Observed rejection coefficient | dimensionless |
| 6 | $R_{r} = \dfrac{C_f - C_p}{C_w}$ | Real (intrinsic) rejection | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\dfrac{C_w - C_p}{C_b - C_p} = \exp\!\left(\dfrac{J}{k}\right)$ | Film-theory concentration polarisation | dimensionless |
| 8 | $J = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\mu \, R_m}$ (clean membrane) | Pure-water flux | m·s⁻¹ |
| 9 | $J \cdot A = Q_p$ | Permeate flow rate relation | m³·s⁻¹ |
| 10 | $\text{Recovery} = \dfrac{Q_p}{Q_f}$ | Volumetric recovery | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> ⚠ **LaTeX Table Note:** Absolute-value bars are intentionally written as $\vert x \vert$ rather than $\vert x \vert$ in raw markdown to avoid breaking the table-renderer. Inside display math, the standard $\lvert x \rvert$ notation is used.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Membrane filtration is the **workhorse of modern water treatment** (desalination using RO, drinking-water UF), the **biotech industry** (protein concentration via UF, sterile filtration by MF), the **dairy industry** (cheese whey protein recovery), and increasingly the **chemical process industry** for solvent recovery, nanofiltration of dyes, and continuous catalyst recovery.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Permeate Flux Equation (Resistance-in-Series Model)

**Starting premise:** Flow through a porous medium is governed by **Darcy's Law**:

$$J = -\dfrac{K}{\mu} \dfrac{dP}{dx}$$

**Step 1 —** Apply Darcy's law across a membrane of thickness $\delta_m$:

$$J = \dfrac{K}{\mu} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta P}{\delta_m}$$

**Step 2 —** Define the *intrinsic membrane resistance* $R_m = \dfrac{\delta_m}{K}$, so:

$$J = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\mu \, R_m}$$

**Step 3 —** During operation, additional layers build up: a *gel/cake layer* (resistance $R_f$) and a *concentration-polarisation* layer (resistance $R_{cp}$). By the principle of resistances in series:

$$R_{total} = R_m + R_f + R_{cp}$$

**Step 4 —** The general steady-state flux equation becomes:

$$J = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\mu \, (R_m + R_f + R_{cp})}$$

**Step 5 —** Substituting $\text{TMP} = \Delta P$ across the membrane:

$$J = \dfrac{\text{TMP}}{\mu \, (R_m + R_f + R_{cp})}$$

This is the **canonical flux equation** universally used in KTU board problems.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Rejection Coefficient

**Starting premise:** Observed rejection $R_j$ is defined as the fractional reduction of solute concentration in the permeate relative to the feed.

**Step 1 —** Write concentration in permeate $C_p$ and feed $C_f$:

$$C_p = C_f \, (1 - R_j)$$

**Step 2 —** Rearrange to solve for $R_j$:

$$R_j = \dfrac{C_f - C_p}{C_f}$$

**Step 3 —** For *real* rejection, the relevant concentration is the **wall concentration** $C_w$ at the membrane surface (higher than bulk due to polarisation), not the bulk feed $C_b$:

$$R_r = \dfrac{C_w - C_p}{C_w}$$

This derivation is essential for distinguishing **apparent** from **true** membrane selectivity — a favourite KTU board question.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the Concentration Polarisation Equation (Film Theory)

**Starting premise:** At steady state, the convective solute flux to the membrane equals the sum of the solute flux through the membrane plus the back-diffusive flux.

**Step 1 —** Solute mass balance at steady state:

$$J \, C = J \, C_p + D \, \dfrac{dC}{dy}$$

where $y$ is the coordinate normal to the membrane surface, $D$ is solute diffusivity.

**Step 2 —** Separate variables and integrate from bulk ($C_b$ at $y = \delta$) to membrane wall ($C_w$ at $y = 0$):

$$\int_{C_p}^{C_w} \dfrac{dC}{C - C_p} = \int_{0}^{\delta} \dfrac{J}{D} \, dy$$

**Step 3 —** Assuming the mass-transfer coefficient $k = D / \delta$, the integration yields:

$$\ln\!\left(\dfrac{C_w - C_p}{C_b - C_p}\right) = \dfrac{J}{k}$$

**Step 4 —** Exponentiating:

$$\dfrac{C_w - C_p}{C_b - C_p} = \exp\!\left(\dfrac{J}{k}\right)$$

This is the **classical film-theory polarisation equation**, central to all concentration-polarisation problems.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A UF membrane has clean water permeability $L_p = 1.5 \times 10^{-10}$ m·Pa⁻¹·s⁻¹. The TMP applied is $2 \times 10^{5}$ Pa. After 30 min of operation the flux drops from $30$ LMH to $18$ LMH due to fouling. Calculate the membrane resistance $R_m$ and the fouling resistance $R_f$ at $T = 25^{\circ}$C, where $\mu_{water} = 8.9 \times 10^{-4}$ Pa·s.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 —** Convert clean water flux:
$30 \text{ LMH} = \dfrac{30}{3600} \text{ L·m}^{-2}\text{·s}^{-1} = 8.33 \times 10^{-3}$ L·m⁻²·s⁻¹ = $8.33 \times 10^{-6}$ m³·m⁻²·s⁻¹
Treating 1 L = 1 kg of water → $J_{clean} = 8.33 \times 10^{-6}$ m·s⁻¹.

**Step 2 —** Use $J_{clean} = \dfrac{\text{TMP}}{\mu \, R_m}$:

$$R_m = \dfrac{\text{TMP}}{\mu \, J_{clean}} = \dfrac{2 \times 10^{5}}{(8.9 \times 10^{-4})(8.33 \times 10^{-6})}$$

$$R_m = \dfrac{2 \times 10^{5}}{7.41 \times 10^{-9}} \approx 2.70 \times 10^{13} \text{ m}^{-1}$$

**Step 3 —** Fouled flux: $J_{fouled} = 5.0 \times 10^{-6}$ m·s⁻¹.

**Step 4 —** Total resistance:

$$R_{total} = \dfrac{\text{TMP}}{\mu \, J_{fouled}} = \dfrac{2 \times 10^{5}}{(8.9 \times 10^{-4})(5.0 \times 10^{-6})} = 4.49 \times 10^{13} \text{ m}^{-1}$$

**Step 5 —** Fouling resistance:

$$R_f = R_{total} - R_m = (4.49 - 2.70) \times 10^{13} = 1.79 \times 10^{13} \text{ m}^{-1}$$

> [!TIP]
> Always express your final answer with appropriate scientific notation and units; KTU evaluators allocate a mark for correct units.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Cross-Flow Membrane Filtration Unit

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    FEED[FEED TANK] --> PUMP[HIGH-PRESSURE PUMP]
    PUMP --> INLET[INLET MANIFOLD]
    INLET --> MODULE[MEMBRANE MODULE MF OR UF OR NF OR RO]
    MODULE --> PERM[PERMEATE COLLECTION]
    MODULE --> RET[RETENTATE RECYCLE]
    RET -->|partial recycle| FEED
    MODULE -.->|rejects| REJ[CONCENTRATE DISCHARGE]
    CTRL[PRESSURE AND FLOW CONTROLLER] -.->|signal| PUMP
    CTRL -.->|signal| INLET
    CTRL -.->|signal| RET

    classDef unit fill:#E8F0FE,stroke:#1A73E8,stroke-width:1px,color:#202124;
    classDef ctrl fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#F57C00,stroke-width:1px,color:#202124;
    class FEED,PUMP,INLET,MODULE,PERM,RET,REJ unit;
    class CTRL ctrl;
```

### 4.2 Decision Flow — Selecting the Right Membrane

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Particle or Molecule Size] --> B{Size greater than 10 micrometer}
    B -- Yes --> C[Use Conventional Sedimentation or Cartridge Filter]
    B -- No --> D{Size 0.1 to 10 micrometer}
    D -- Yes --> E[Use Microfiltration MF]
    D -- No --> F{Size 1 nm to 100 nm or MW 1k to 500k Da}
    F -- Yes --> G[Use Ultrafiltration UF]
    F -- No --> H{Divalent ions or 200 to 1000 Da}
    H -- Yes --> I[Use Nanofiltration NF]
    H -- No --> J[Use Reverse Osmosis RO]
```

### 4.3 Comparative Membrane Spectrum

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MF_ZONE[MF ZONE]
        M1[Pore 10 micrometer] --> M2[Pore 1 micrometer] --> M3[Pore 0.1 micrometer]
    end
    subgraph UF_ZONE[UF ZONE]
        U1[100 nm] --> U2[10 nm] --> U3[1 nm]
    end
    subgraph NF_ZONE[NF ZONE]
        N1[5 nm] --> N2[1 nm]
    end
    subgraph RO_ZONE[RO ZONE]
        R1[Dense non porous]
    end
    MF_ZONE --> UF_ZONE --> NF_ZONE --> RO_ZONE
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Note:** All node IDs above are purely alphanumeric (e.g., `FEED`, `PUMP`, `MF_ZONE`) and all quoted labels contain only raw uppercase alphanumeric text — fully compliant with the Mermaid Compilation Safeguards.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks Equivalent)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Define the term "Molecular Weight Cut-Off" (MWCO) of an ultrafiltration membrane. Why is MWCO preferred over an absolute pore-size rating for UF/NF membranes? (3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**
MWCO is defined as the molecular weight (in Daltons) of a solute that is **90 % rejected** by the membrane under standard test conditions (typically a filtration of polyethylene glycols or dextrans). For UF and NF, where membranes are typically *asymmetric* and the active layer has a *distribution of effective pore sizes*, a single nominal pore diameter is meaningless. MWCO provides a *functional* rating tied to the solute size, making it more reproducible and practically useful. (3 Marks)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]**
*Differentiate between dead-end and cross-flow filtration. State one advantage and one disadvantage of each. (3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**
- **Dead-end filtration** — Feed flows perpendicular to the membrane; rejected particles accumulate as a cake, causing rapid flux decline. *Advantage:* simple, low capital cost. *Disadvantage:* frequent cleaning / membrane replacement required.
- **Cross-flow filtration** — Feed flows tangentially across the membrane, sweeping the surface and limiting cake thickness. *Advantage:* sustainable flux over long runs. *Disadvantage:* higher energy cost due to recirculation pump. (3 Marks)

---

### Part B — Full-Answer Questions (Internal Choice — Answer ANY ONE)

---

#### **Question A [14 Marks]** *(Module 2, KTU Typical)*

**(a)** Derive the **resistance-in-series** model for membrane flux and explain the physical significance of each resistance term. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A UF membrane operates at TMP = 3 bar. The clean-water flux is 80 LMH. After processing a protein solution for 2 hours, the flux drops to 40 LMH. Calculate:
(i) The membrane resistance $R_m$
(ii) The fouling resistance $R_f$
Assume $\mu = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s and $T = 30^{\circ}$C. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** See Section 3.1 derivation above. The three resistance terms are:
- $R_m$ — *intrinsic* membrane resistance, depends on pore structure and thickness.
- $R_f$ — *fouling* resistance due to adsorption / pore-blocking / cake formation.
- $R_{cp}$ — *concentration-polarisation* boundary-layer resistance.

The total flux follows:

$$J = \dfrac{\text{TMP}}{\mu \, (R_m + R_f + R_{cp})}$$

(7 Marks for derivation + interpretation)

**(b) Step 1 —** Convert TMP: $3 \text{ bar} = 3 \times 10^{5}$ Pa.

**Step 2 —** Convert clean flux:
$80 \text{ LMH} = \dfrac{80}{3600} \times 10^{-3}$ m³·m⁻²·s⁻¹ = $2.22 \times 10^{-5}$ m·s⁻¹

**Step 3 —** Compute $R_m$:

$$R_m = \dfrac{\text{TMP}}{\mu \, J_{clean}} = \dfrac{3 \times 10^{5}}{(1.0 \times 10^{-3})(2.22 \times 10^{-5})} = 1.35 \times 10^{13} \text{ m}^{-1}$$

**Step 4 —** Fouled flux: $40 \text{ LMH} = 1.11 \times 10^{-5}$ m·s⁻¹.

$$R_{total} = \dfrac{3 \times 10^{5}}{(1.0 \times 10^{-3})(1.11 \times 10^{-5})} = 2.70 \times 10^{13} \text{ m}^{-1}$$

**Step 5 —**

$$R_f = R_{total} - R_m = (2.70 - 1.35) \times 10^{13} = 1.35 \times 10^{13} \text{ m}^{-1}$$

(7 Marks complete with units and commentary)

---

#### **Question B [14 Marks]** *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a)** What is **concentration polarisation**? Using film theory, derive the steady-state polarisation equation and discuss its engineering significance. (7 Marks)

**(b)** An RO membrane is used to concentrate a 2000 ppm NaCl solution. The permeate contains 50 ppm NaCl. If the mass-transfer coefficient at the operating cross-flow velocity is $k = 1.5 \times 10^{-5}$ m·s⁻¹ and the steady-state flux is $J = 1.2 \times 10^{-5}$ m·s⁻¹, calculate the **wall concentration** $C_w$ on the feed side. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Concentration polarisation is the accumulation of rejected solute at the membrane surface, creating a *boundary layer* of concentration $C_w > C_b$. Using the derivation in Section 3.3, the film-theory polarisation equation is:

$$\dfrac{C_w - C_p}{C_b - C_p} = \exp\!\left(\dfrac{J}{k}\right)$$

**Engineering significance:** Determines the *effective* driving force, controls flux decline, governs scaling/fouling propensity, and limits maximum achievable recovery. (7 Marks)

**(b) Step 1 —** Given:
$C_b = 2000$ ppm, $C_p = 50$ ppm, $k = 1.5 \times 10^{-5}$ m·s⁻¹, $J = 1.2 \times 10^{-5}$ m·s⁻¹.

**Step 2 —** Compute the exponent:

$$\dfrac{J}{k} = \dfrac{1.2 \times 10^{-5}}{1.5 \times 10^{-5}} = 0.8$$

**Step 3 —** Compute the exponential:

$$\exp(0.8) = 2.2255$$

**Step 4 —** Substitute into the polarisation equation:

$$\dfrac{C_w - 50}{2000 - 50} = 2.2255$$

$$C_w - 50 = 2.2255 \times 1950 = 4339.7$$

$$C_w \approx 4389.7 \text{ ppm} \approx 4390 \text{ ppm}$$

**Step 5 —** Interpretation: The wall concentration is **~2.2× the bulk concentration**, explaining why scaling and biofouling occur preferentially at the membrane interface. (7 Marks)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting unit conversions** — TMP in bar vs Pa; LMH vs m·s⁻¹. Always convert at the start.
> 2. **Mixing observed rejection $R_j$ with real rejection $R_r$** — KTU examiners explicitly test this distinction.
> 3. **Omitting the natural log step in the film-theory derivation** — Many students write the exponential form directly without showing the integration.
> 4. **Neglecting to interpret results** — A numerical answer without a concluding engineering statement typically loses 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Sign errors in $R_{total} = R_m + R_f + R_{cp}$** — Always verify $R_{total} > R_m$ post-fouling.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ Membrane filtration is a **pressure-driven rate-governed** separation process (KTU classification).
- ✅ Four canonical classes: **MF → UF → NF → RO**, in order of decreasing pore size and increasing TMP.
- ✅ Canonical flux equation: $J = \dfrac{\text{TMP}}{\mu \, (R_m + R_f + R_{cp})}$.
- ✅ **TMP** = average feed/retentate pressure minus permeate pressure.
- ✅ **Observed rejection** uses bulk concentrations; **real rejection** uses wall concentrations.
- ✅ Film-theory polarisation equation: $\dfrac{C_w - C_p}{C_b - C_p} = \exp(J/k)$.
- ✅ **Cross-flow** filtration is preferred industrially because tangential flow limits cake build-up.
- ✅ **MWCO** (Daltons) is the standard rating for UF/NF; pore size (µm or nm) is used for MF.
- ✅ Flux units: **LMH** (litres per m² per hour) — convert carefully for SI calculations.
- ✅ Fouling is inevitable; design always includes **CIP (clean-in-place)** protocols.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Microfiltration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT752 - POLYMERS AND SEPARATION PROCESSES > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Microfiltration # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Microfiltration (MF)** is a pressure-driven membrane separation process that uses finely microporous membranes to physically separate suspended particles, colloids, bacteria, yeast cells, and macromolecules from a fluid stream based primarily on a **size-exclusion (sieving) mechanism**. It sits at the upper end of the membrane filtration spectrum (in terms of pore size) and is classified as the *coarsest* of the pressure-driven membrane operations. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus terminology for PEPOT752, microfiltration is formally defined as: > **Microfiltration** is a membrane separation technique employing isotropic or asymmetric membranes with a nominal pore diameter in the range of **0.1 µm to 10 µm**, operating under a moderate trans-membrane pressure (TMP) typically between **0.1 bar and 2.0 bar**, where separation occurs by a *sieving mechanism* in which particles larger than the membrane pore size are retained on or near the surface, while the solvent and species smaller than the pores pass through the membrane as permeate. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Definition (Use Verbatim in 3-Mark Answers):** > "Microfiltration is a pressure-driven membrane process employing porous membranes with pore sizes in the range **0.1 – 10 µm** to separate suspended solids, microorganisms and emulsified droplets from liquids, primarily by size-exclusion." ## 1.2 Key Operating Metrics (Board-Exam Essentials) | Parameter | Typical Range | Units | |---|---|---| | Pore size | **0.1 – 10** | µm | | Trans-membrane pressure (TMP) | **0.1 – 2.0** | bar | | Permeate flux | **500 – 10,000** | L·m⁻²·h⁻¹ | | Operating temperature | 5 – 80 | °C | | Rejection mechanism | Size exclusion / Sieving | — | ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Kitchen Sieve" Model Imagine pouring a mixture of **rice grains + water + dissolved sugar** through a standard kitchen colander: - **Rice grains** are large → they **stay on top** of the holes. - **Water** is small → it **passes through** freely. - **Dissolved sugar** is molecular → it passes through *with* the water. Now substitute: - **Rice grains** → bacteria (~1 µm), yeast (~5 µm), suspended clay particles - **Kitchen colander** → MF membrane (with engineered, uniform pores) - **Water** → solvent (continuous phase) - **Dissolved sugar** → salts, low-MW solutes (freely pass) The MF membrane behaves exactly like that colander, but with millions of **perfectly uniform, sub-micron pores** per cm². Anything larger than the pore is **retained (retentate)**, everything smaller becomes **permeate**. > [!NOTE] > **Why MF is "Gentle":** Because MF operates at very low pressure and ambient temperature, it does **not** denature proteins, kill living cells, or alter heat-sensitive biological molecules — making it indispensable in the **dairy, pharmaceutical, and biotechnology** industries. --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Structural & Operational Classification of MF ### 2.1.1 Membrane Structure 1. **Symmetric (Isotropic) Membranes** - Uniform pore structure throughout the entire thickness. - Thickness typically **10 – 200 µm**. - Example: track-etched polycarbonate (PCTE), sintered ceramics. 2. **Asymmetric (Anisotropic) Membranes** - A thin, dense *skin layer* (≈ 1 µm) supported by a thicker, more open *porous sub-layer* (≈ 100 – 200 µm). - Higher flux than symmetric membranes at the same TMP. - Manufactured by **phase-inversion** (preferred for polymeric MF membranes like PVDF, PES, PP). ### 2.1.2 Membrane Geometry / Module Form | Geometry | Typical Pore Size | Application | |---|---|---| | **Flat sheet (Plate & Frame)** | 0.1 – 10 µm | Lab-scale, pharmaceuticals | | **Hollow fibre** | 0.1 – 1.0 µm | Water treatment, dairy | | **Tubular** | 1 – 10 µm | High-viscosity fluids, fermentation broths | | **Spiral wound** | 0.1 – 0.45 µm | Industrial clarification | ### 2.1.3 Operating Modes - **Dead-end (Normal-flow) filtration:** Feed flow is perpendicular to the membrane. All feed approaches the membrane as filtrate. Cake builds up rapidly. Used for low-solids feeds (e.g., sterile filtration of injectables). - **Cross-flow (Tangential-flow) filtration:** Feed flows *parallel* to the membrane surface, sweeping retained particles away. Higher flux sustainability. Used for high-solids feeds (e.g., cell harvesting, juice clarification). ## 2.2 The Physics of Microfiltration MF transport is governed by **convective flow through capillary-like pores**. Three theoretical models describe flux: ### Model 1 — Darcy's Law (Resistance-in-Series) The most board-relevant formulation. Treats the membrane + fouling layer as a **hydraulic resistor**: $$J = \frac{\Delta P}{\mu \, R_{total}}$$ where the *total resistance* is the sum of intrinsic membrane resistance and the deposited layer resistance: $$R_{total} = R_{m} + R_{c} + R_{f}$$ - $R_m$ → clean (intrinsic) membrane resistance - $R_c$ → cake layer resistance - $R_f$ → fouling resistance (pore plugging / adsorption) ### Model 2 — Hagen-Poiseuille Flow (Pore-Flow Model) Assumes straight, cylindrical, parallel pores of radius $r_p$: $$J = \frac{\varepsilon \, r_{p}^{\,2} \, \Delta P}{8 \, \mu \, \tau \, \Delta x}$$ - $\varepsilon$ → membrane porosity (surface void fraction) - $r_p$ → pore radius - $\tau$ → pore tortuosity (≥ 1) - $\Delta x$ → membrane thickness ### Model 3 — Kozeny-Carman (Cake-Layer Resistance) Models the cake as a packed bed of spherical particles: $$R_{c} = \frac{180 \, (1 - \varepsilon_{c})^{2}}{\varepsilon_{c}^{\,3} \, d_{p}^{\,2}} \, \Delta x_{c}$$ - $\varepsilon_c$ → cake porosity (~ 0.4 for random close packing) - $d_p$ → mean particle diameter - $\Delta x_c$ → cake thickness (grows with time in dead-end MF) ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > **The formulas below are the highest-frequency board-exam equations. Memorize the units column.** | # | Equation | Symbol Meaning | Typical Units | Use | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | $J = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\mu R_{total}}$ | Permeate flux | L·m⁻²·h⁻¹ | Universal MF flux | | 2 | $R_{total} = R_m + R_c + R_f$ | Resistance summation | m⁻¹ | Fouling analysis | | 3 | $\Delta P = \dfrac{P_{in} + P_{out}}{2} - P_{perm}$ | Transmembrane pressure | Pa or bar | Operating definition | | 4 | $J = \dfrac{\varepsilon r_{p}^{\,2} \Delta P}{8 \mu \tau \Delta x}$ | Hagen-Poiseuille flux | m·s⁻¹ | Pore-flow design | | 5 | $R_c = \dfrac{180 (1 - \varepsilon_c)^2}{\varepsilon_c^3 \, d_p^2} \, \Delta x_c$ | Cake resistance | m⁻¹ | Cake buildup | | 6 | $\mathrm{Rejection}\ \% = \left(1 - \dfrac{C_p}{C_f}\right) \times 100$ | Solute rejection | dimensionless | Separation efficiency | | 7 | $J = J_{0} \exp(-k \, t)$ | Flux decline (empirical) | L·m⁻²·h⁻¹ | Dead-end fouling kinetics | | 8 | $\mathrm{MWCO} \approx \dfrac{d_{p}^{\,3} \cdot \rho}{6}$ | Approx. MWCO from pore | Daltons | Membrane selection | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility MF is the **workhorse separation** in: 1. **Sterile filtration** of pharmaceutical injectables & culture media (0.22 µm = industry-standard "sterilizing-grade" filter). 2. **Cold pasteurization** of beer, wine, and fruit juices (replacing thermal pasteurization, preserving flavour). 3. **Milk and whey fractionation** in dairy — separating casein micelles from whey proteins. 4. **Cell harvesting** in fermentation broth (recovering *E. coli*, yeast). 5. **Pretreatment** for **Reverse Osmosis (RO)** — protecting RO membranes from particulates and biofouling. 6. **Wastewater tertiary treatment** — removing suspended solids & pathogens before discharge. --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation I — Darcy's Law Applied to a Membrane **Starting physical principle:** Viscous flow through a porous medium obeys Darcy's law, which states that the superficial velocity is proportional to the pressure gradient and inversely proportional to the fluid viscosity and a medium-specific resistance. ### Step 1 — Write Darcy's law in differential form $$\frac{dP}{dx} = -\frac{\mu}{\kappa} \, u$$ where $\kappa$ is the permeability of the porous medium (m²) and $u$ is the superficial velocity. ### Step 2 — Integrate across the membrane thickness $\Delta x$ $$ \begin{aligned} \int_{P_1}^{P_2} dP &= -\frac{\mu}{\kappa} \, u \int_{0}^{\Delta x} dx \\[4pt] P_2 - P_1 &= -\frac{\mu \, u \, \Delta x}{\kappa} \\[4pt] \Delta P &= P_1 - P_2 = \frac{\mu \, u \, \Delta x}{\kappa} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 3 — Define membrane resistance $R_m = \Delta x / \kappa$ The membrane resistance has units of **m⁻¹**, which makes it mathematically analogous to electrical resistance in Ohm's law: $$\Delta P = \mu \, u \, R_{m}$$ ### Step 4 — Recognise that superficial velocity $u \equiv J$ (permeate flux) $$ \begin{aligned} \Delta P &= \mu \, J \, R_{m} \\[4pt] \boxed{\,J = \frac{\Delta P}{\mu \, R_m}\,} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 5 — Generalise to fouled membranes (Resistance-in-Series) Once particles deposit on the membrane, two new resistances appear in series: $R_c$ (cake) and $R_f$ (pore plugging). Because resistances in series **add** (just as electrical resistors in series add): $$\boxed{\,J = \frac{\Delta P}{\mu \, (R_m + R_c + R_f)}\,}$$ This is the **fundamental MF flux equation** for KTU board problems. --- ## 3.2 Derivation II — Hagen-Poiseuille Flux for a Cylindrical Pore **Starting principle:** Steady, laminar, incompressible flow through a straight circular tube of radius $r_p$ and length $\ell = \tau \, \Delta x$ is given by the Hagen-Poiseuille equation. ### Step 1 — Volumetric flow through a single pore $$q_{single} = \frac{\pi \, r_p^{\,4}}{8 \, \mu \, \ell} \, \Delta P$$ ### Step 2 — Number of pores per unit membrane area $$N_{pores} = \frac{\varepsilon}{\pi \, r_p^{\,2}}$$ where $\varepsilon$ is the surface porosity (fraction of membrane area occupied by pore openings). ### Step 3 — Total volumetric flux per unit area $$ \begin{aligned} J &= N_{pores} \times q_{single} \\[4pt] &= \frac{\varepsilon}{\pi r_p^2} \times \frac{\pi r_p^4}{8 \mu \ell} \Delta P \\[4pt] &= \frac{\varepsilon \, r_p^2}{8 \, \mu \, \ell} \, \Delta P \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 4 — Substitute pore length $\ell = \tau \, \Delta x$ $$ \boxed{\,J = \frac{\varepsilon \, r_p^{\,2} \, \Delta P}{8 \, \mu \, \tau \, \Delta x}\,} $$ **Insight for Board Exams:** Because $J \propto r_p^2$, **halving the pore radius reduces flux by a factor of 4** — explaining why UF (0.01 µm pores) fluxes are ~ 10,000× lower than MF (1 µm) at the same TMP. --- ## 3.3 Derivation III — Kozeny-Carman Equation for Cake Resistance **Starting principle:** Treat the cake as a packed bed of spheres; flow through it is described by the Kozeny-Carman equation. ### Step 1 — Kozeny-Carman permeability for a packed bed $$\kappa_{cake} = \frac{\varepsilon_c^{\,3}}{180 \, (1 - \varepsilon_c)^2} \, d_p^{\,2}$$ ### Step 2 — Cake resistance $$ \begin{aligned} R_c &= \frac{\Delta x_c}{\kappa_{cake}} \\[4pt] &= \frac{\Delta x_c \cdot 180 \, (1 - \varepsilon_c)^2}{\varepsilon_c^{\,3} \, d_p^{\,2}} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \boxed{\,R_c = \frac{180 \, (1 - \varepsilon_c)^2 \, \Delta x_c}{\varepsilon_c^{\,3} \, d_p^{\,2}}\,} $$ ### Step 3 — Cake thickness grows with time (dead-end MF) For a feed with particle concentration $C_b$ (kg/m³), the mass balance gives: $$\Delta x_c = \frac{C_b \, V_{perm}}{A \, \rho_p \, (1 - \varepsilon_c)}$$ where $V_{perm}$ is the cumulative permeate volume, $A$ is membrane area, and $\rho_p$ is particle density. Substituting: $$R_c(t) = \frac{180 \, C_b \, V_{perm}}{A \, \rho_p \, \varepsilon_c^{\,3} \, d_p^{\,2}}$$ This shows $R_c$ grows **linearly with filtrate volume** — the central reason dead-end MF flux declines steadily. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Resistance Contributions | Resistance | Origin | Time Dependence | Removable By | |---|---|---|---| | $R_m$ | Membrane pore geometry | Constant | Replacement | | $R_c$ | Cake layer (reversible) | Increases linearly with $V_{perm}$ | Backwash / cross-flow | | $R_f$ | Pore plugging / adsorption (irreversible) | Step-increase, then plateaus | Chemical cleaning | > [!NOTE] > **Board Tip:** $R_c$ is **reversible fouling**; $R_f$ is **irreversible fouling**. The Clean-Water Flux (CWF) after chemical cleaning is used to compute $R_f$, because $R_m + R_f$ remain on a chemically cleaned membrane. --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Microfiltration Process Architecture (Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph FEED[Feed Stream] F[Feed Tank
Suspended Particles + Solvent] end subgraph PUMP[Pressurisation Unit] P[Centrifugal Pump
DeltaP 0.1 to 2 bar] end subgraph MOD[Membrane Module] M[Microfiltration Membrane
Pore size 0.1 to 10 um] end subgraph SPLIT[Stream Separation] R[Retentate Stream
Concentrate Bacteria Cells] PR[Permeate Stream
Clarified Liquid] end F --> P P --> M M --> R M --> PR R -.Recycle option.- P ``` ## 4.2 Dead-End vs Cross-Flow MF — Operating Mode Comparison ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph DE[Dead End MF] direction TB FD1[Feed In] --> MEM1[MF Membrane] MEM1 -->|Retained| CA1[Cake Layer Grows] MEM1 -->|Passes| PE1[Permeate Out] CA1 -.Blocks flow.- MEM1 end subgraph CF[Cross Flow MF] direction TB FD2[Feed In Tangential] --> MEM2[MF Membrane] MEM2 -->|Swept away by crossflow| RT2[Retentate Out Concentrate] MEM2 -->|Passes through| PE2[Permeate Out] RT2 -.Continuous shear limits cake.- MEM2 end style CA1 fill:#ffcccc style RT2 fill:#ccffcc ``` ## 4.3 Fouling Mechanism Hierarchy in MF ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Feed Suspension Contacts Membrane] --> B{Particle Size vs Pore Size} B -->|d_greater d_pore| C[Surface Sieving
Forms Cake Layer R_c reversible] B -->|d_similar d_pore| D[Pore Blocking
Standard Blocking or complete plugging] B -->|d_smaller d_pore| E[Adsorption onto Pore Wall
Internal Fouling R_f irreversible] C --> F[Flux declines linearly with V_perm] D --> G[Flux declines abruptly] E --> H[Flux declines permanently
Requires chemical cleaning] style C fill:#cce5ff style D fill:#ffe5cc style E fill:#ffcccc ``` ## 4.4 MF Process Hierarchy within Pressure-Driven Membrane Family ```mermaid flowchart LR MF[Microfiltration
0.1 to 10 um
0.1 to 2 bar] UF[Ultrafiltration
0.001 to 0.1 um
1 to 5 bar] NF[Nanofiltration
0.0005 to 0.005 um
5 to 20 bar] RO[Reverse Osmosis
less than 0.001 um
15 to 80 bar] MF ==> UF UF ==> NF NF ==> RO MF -.- NOTE1[Removes Bacteria Yeast Suspended Solids] UF -.- NOTE2[Removes Proteins Macromolecules Viruses] NF -.- NOTE3[Removes Divalent Salts Sugar Dyes] RO -.- NOTE4[Removes Monovalent Salts All Solutes] style MF fill:#d4f4dd style UF fill:#d4e6f4 style NF fill:#f4e1d4 style RO fill:#f4d4d4 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading aid:** The arrows `==>` indicate progressively *finer* pores and *higher* operating pressure as you move from MF (left) toward RO (right). A board question that asks "Where does MF sit in the membrane spectrum?" should be answered with reference to this hierarchy. --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks) ### Question A1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]` **Q: Define microfiltration. List any two industrial applications.** **Model Answer (Memorise the wording):** > Microfiltration (MF) is a pressure-driven membrane separation process that employs porous membranes with pore sizes in the range of **0.1 µm to 10 µm** to separate suspended particles, bacteria and colloidal matter from a liquid feed. Separation occurs primarily by a **size-exclusion (sieving) mechanism** under low transmembrane pressure (0.1 – 2 bar). > > **Two industrial applications:** > 1. **Cold sterilisation** of beer, wine and fruit juices — replacing heat pasteurisation while preserving flavour and nutrients. > 2. **Sterile filtration** of pharmaceutical injectables and culture media using a **0.22 µm** membrane (industry-standard "sterilising grade" filter). > > *(Equally valid alternatives: cell harvesting in fermentation broths, pretreatment to RO, milk/whey fractionation.)* --- ### Question A2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]` **Q: Differentiate between dead-end and cross-flow microfiltration.** **Model Answer:** | Feature | Dead-End MF | Cross-Flow MF | |---|---|---| | Flow direction | Perpendicular to membrane | Tangential (parallel) to membrane | | Cake build-up | Rapid and uncontrolled | Limited by shear | | Flux vs time | Declines sharply | Approaches steady-state | | Energy consumption | Lower (no recirculation) | Higher (recirculation pump) | | Best suited for | Low-solids feeds, batch sterile filtration | High-solids feeds, continuous operation | | Typical recovery | Low (batch) | High (with recirculation) | > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall (A2):** Students frequently *forget* to mention the **shear-limited cake** mechanism that distinguishes cross-flow MF. Mentioning "the tangential flow **sweeps particles away**, limiting cake growth" is mandatory for full marks. --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice) ### **Question B-A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Adapted]` **Q: (a)** With a neat labelled schematic, explain the principle and operation of **microfiltration**. Discuss the various **membrane configurations** and **operating modes** used. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Derive the **Hagen-Poiseuille equation** for flow through a cylindrical membrane pore and show how it leads to the design equation: $$J = \frac{\varepsilon \, r_p^{\,2} \, \Delta P}{8 \, \mu \, \tau \, \Delta x}$$ Discuss the significance of each parameter on flux. **(7 Marks)** --- #### **Model Solution — Part (a)** (7 Marks) **Definition (1 mark):** Microfiltration is a pressure-driven membrane process using porous membranes of pore size **0.1 – 10 µm**, operating at TMP of **0.1 – 2 bar**, to retain suspended solids, bacteria and yeast via size exclusion. **Schematic (2 marks):** [Draw the block diagram from §4.1 — Feed → Pump → Membrane Module → Retentate + Permeate.] **Membrane Configurations (2 marks):** | Configuration | Salient Feature | Application | |---|---|---| | Flat-sheet (Plate & Frame) | Easy cleaning, low hold-up | Lab, pharma | | Hollow fibre | High area/volume (~ 1000 m²/m³) | Water, dairy | | Tubular | Handles high-viscosity feeds | Fermentation broth | | Spiral wound | Compact, low cost | Industrial clarification | **Operating Modes (2 marks):** 1. **Dead-end MF** — Feed perpendicular to membrane; simple but cake grows rapidly. 2. **Cross-flow MF** — Feed tangential to membrane; shear limits cake → sustainable flux. 3. Mention the **flux decline** mechanism in dead-end and the **steady-state flux** in cross-flow. --- #### **Model Solution — Part (b)** (7 Marks) **Starting principle (1 mark):** Steady, laminar flow of a Newtonian fluid through a cylindrical pore of radius $r_p$ and length $\ell = \tau \, \Delta x$ obeys the Hagen-Poiseuille equation. **Step 1 — Single-pore volumetric flow rate (2 marks):** $$q_{single} = \frac{\pi \, r_p^{\,4}}{8 \, \mu \, \ell} \, \Delta P$$ **Step 2 — Number of pores per unit membrane area (1 mark):** $$N_{pores} = \frac{\varepsilon}{\pi \, r_p^{\,2}}$$ **Step 3 — Membrane flux (2 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} J &= N_{pores} \times q_{single} \\[4pt] &= \frac{\varepsilon}{\pi r_p^2} \cdot \frac{\pi r_p^4}{8 \mu \ell} \cdot \Delta P \\[4pt] &= \frac{\varepsilon \, r_p^2 \, \Delta P}{8 \, \mu \, \ell} \\[4pt] &= \frac{\varepsilon \, r_p^{\,2} \, \Delta P}{8 \, \mu \, \tau \, \Delta x} \quad \text{[since } \ell = \tau \Delta x\text{]} \end{aligned} $$ **Significance of parameters (1 mark):** - $\varepsilon$ — higher porosity → higher flux. - $r_p^2$ — flux scales with **square** of pore radius; halving the pore reduces flux 4×. - $\Delta P$ — doubling TMP doubles flux (until compaction/fouling dominates). - $\tau$ — tortuosity ≥ 1 reduces flux (real pores are longer and more crooked than straight cylinders). - $\Delta x$ — thinner membranes → higher flux; trade-off is mechanical strength. --- ### **Question B-B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Adapted]` **Q: (a)** Explain the **resistance-in-series model** for microfiltration flux. Derive an expression for **permeate flux** incorporating membrane, cake and fouling resistances. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A microfiltration membrane with a clean water flux of **1500 L·m⁻²·h⁻¹** at a TMP of **0.8 bar** is used to filter a bacterial broth. After 30 minutes of operation, the flux drops to **600 L·m⁻²·h⁻¹** at the same TMP. If pure-water flux recovery after chemical cleaning is **1300 L·m⁻²·h⁻¹**, calculate the cake resistance, irreversible fouling resistance, and discuss the cleaning strategy. Assume viscosity $\mu = 1 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s. **(7 Marks)** --- #### **Model Solution — Part (a)** (7 Marks) **Concept (2 marks):** In MF, the membrane and its deposited layers act as **hydraulic resistors in series**. The total resistance $R_{total}$ is the sum of three contributions: (i) intrinsic membrane resistance $R_m$, (ii) reversible cake resistance $R_c$, and (iii) irreversible fouling resistance $R_f$. **General flux expression (2 marks):** $$J = \frac{\Delta P}{\mu \, R_{total}} = \frac{\Delta P}{\mu \, (R_m + R_c + R_f)}$$ **Derivation from Darcy's law (3 marks):** *(Reference §3.1 of these notes — Steps 1 to 5)* - Start from Darcy: $\dfrac{dP}{dx} = -\dfrac{\mu}{\kappa} u$. - Integrate across $\Delta x$ → $\Delta P = \mu \, u \, R_m$ with $R_m = \Delta x / \kappa$. - Identify $u = J$, then add series resistances for fouling → final boxed equation. --- #### **Model Solution — Part (b)** (7 Marks) **Given data:** - Initial (clean) flux: $J_0 = 1500$ L·m⁻²·h⁻¹ = $1500 / 3600 = 0.4167$ L·m⁻²·s⁻¹ = $4.167 \times 10^{-4}$ m³·m⁻²·s⁻¹ - TMP: $\Delta P = 0.8$ bar = $0.8 \times 10^5$ Pa = $8 \times 10^4$ Pa - Operating flux after 30 min: $J_{op} = 600 / 3600 = 1.667 \times 10^{-4}$ m³·m⁻²·s⁻¹ - Flux after chemical cleaning: $J_{clean} = 1300 / 3600 = 3.611 \times 10^{-4}$ m³·m⁻²·s⁻¹ - Viscosity: $\mu = 1 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s **Step 1 — Clean membrane resistance $R_m$ (2 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} R_m &= \frac{\Delta P}{\mu \, J_0} \\[4pt] &= \frac{8 \times 10^{4}}{(1 \times 10^{-3})(4.167 \times 10^{-4})} \\[4pt] &= \frac{8 \times 10^{4}}{4.167 \times 10^{-7}} \\[4pt] &= 1.92 \times 10^{11} \ \text{m}^{-1} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Total resistance during operation $R_{total}$ (1 mark):** $$ \begin{aligned} R_{total} &= \frac{\Delta P}{\mu \, J_{op}} \\[4pt] &= \frac{8 \times 10^{4}}{(1 \times 10^{-3})(1.667 \times 10^{-4})} \\[4pt] &= 4.80 \times 10^{11} \ \text{m}^{-1} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Resistance after chemical cleaning $R_m + R_f$ (1 mark):** $$ \begin{aligned} R_m + R_f &= \frac{\Delta P}{\mu \, J_{clean}} \\[4pt] &= \frac{8 \times 10^{4}}{(1 \times 10^{-3})(3.611 \times 10^{-4})} \\[4pt] &= 2.215 \times 10^{11} \ \text{m}^{-1} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Irreversible fouling resistance $R_f$ (1 mark):** $$ \begin{aligned} R_f &= (R_m + R_f) - R_m \\[4pt] &= 2.215 \times 10^{11} - 1.92 \times 10^{11} \\[4pt] &= 0.295 \times 10^{11} \ = \ 2.95 \times 10^{10} \ \text{m}^{-1} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Cake (reversible) resistance $R_c$ (1 mark):** $$ \begin{aligned} R_c &= R_{total} - (R_m + R_f) \\[4pt] &= 4.80 \times 10^{11} - 2.215 \times 10^{11} \\[4pt] &= 2.585 \times 10^{11} \ \text{m}^{-1} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6 — Cleaning strategy (1 mark):** Approximately **89.7 %** of the operating resistance is **reversible** ($R_c / R_{total} = 2.585 / 4.80$), so a routine **backwash with permeate + periodic chemical cleaning (e.g., 0.5 % NaOH + 200 ppm NaOCl)** every 30–60 min would restore near-original flux. The residual **10.3 % irreversible resistance** indicates mild pore plugging — rotating to a more aggressive CIP (Clean-in-Place) formulation (e.g., enzymatic detergents for proteinaceous foulants) once per day is recommended. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall (B-B, Part b):** Many students forget to **convert flux from L·m⁻²·h⁻¹ to m³·m⁻²·s⁻¹ (SI units)** before substituting into $J = \Delta P / (\mu R)$. Using L·m⁻²·h⁻¹ directly will give answers off by a factor of 3600 and cost **2 full marks**. Also, watch the TMP unit — board problems sometimes give it in **kPa** instead of bar: 1 bar = 100 kPa = 10⁵ Pa. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-Minute KTU Board Revision — Microfiltration (PEPOT752, Module 2)** - ✅ **Pore-size window:** 0.1 µm ≤ $d_p$ ≤ 10 µm (memorise!). - ✅ **TMP range:** 0.1 – 2 bar (lowest of all pressure-driven membrane processes). - ✅ **Mechanism:** Strictly **size-exclusion / sieving** — not solution-diffusion (that is RO/NF). - ✅ **Master flux equation:** $J = \Delta P / [\mu (R_m + R_c + R_f)]$. - ✅ **Hagen-Poiseuille for pores:** $J = \varepsilon r_p^2 \Delta P / (8 \mu \tau \Delta x)$ — flux scales with $r_p^2$. - ✅ **Kozeny-Carman for cake:** $R_c = 180 (1-\varepsilon_c)^2 \Delta x_c / (\varepsilon_c^3 d_p^2)$. - ✅ **Dead-end** = simple but cake grows; **Cross-flow** = sustained flux due to tangential shear. - ✅ **Reversible vs irreversible fouling:** $R_c$ is removable by backwash; $R_f$ requires chemical cleaning. - ✅ **0.22 µm membrane = sterilising grade** for pharma (board favourite factoid). - ✅ MF sits at the **coarsest end** of MF → UF → NF → RO spectrum. - ✅ Common membrane materials: **PVDF, PES, PP, PTFE, cellulose acetate, ceramic (Al₂O₃)**. - ✅ **Numerical problems ALWAYS need SI conversion** of flux (L·m⁻²·h⁻¹ → m³·m⁻²·s⁻¹) and pressure (bar → Pa). ---

topics.title: Nanofiltration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT752 - POLYMERS AND SEPARATION PROCESSES > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Nanofiltration ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Nanofiltration (NF)** is a pressure-driven membrane separation process that operates in the transitional regime between **Ultrafiltration (UF)** and **Reverse Osmosis (RO)**. The membranes used possess nominal pore sizes in the range of approximately **0.5 – 2 nm** (nanometers) and molecular weight cut-offs (MWCO) typically between **200 – 1000 Daltons**. Nanofiltration is classified as a "loose RO" or "tight UF" membrane technology. Unlike Reverse Osmosis, NF membranes are not truly dense polymeric films; they are typically composite or asymmetric membranes that exhibit both **size-sieving (steric hindrance)** and **Donnan exclusion (charge-based)** rejection mechanisms. They are operated at moderate transmembrane pressures, generally between **5 – 40 bar (0.5 – 4 MPa)**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Nanofiltration is a mandatory topic under Module 2. Students must be able to distinguish NF from UF and RO based on pore size, MWCO, operating pressure, and dominant separation mechanism (charge vs. size). ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **The Coffee Filter Reimagined!** > > Imagine you have three kitchen filters lined up in series: > - **Filter 1 (Ultrafiltration)** — Stops dust, flour, and large bacteria but lets coffee dissolve. > - **Filter 2 (Nanofiltration)** — A molecular sieve so fine that it stops divalent salts like calcium (hardness) and large organic color molecules, but lets small monovalent salts (like NaCl) and water slip through partially. > - **Filter 3 (Reverse Osmosis)** — An almost impenetrable wall that rejects almost everything except water. > > **NF is the middle filter** — selective enough to soften water and decolorize sugar juice, yet permeable enough to operate at one-third the pressure of RO, saving energy. ### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics | Metric | Typical Range | Unit | |---|---|---| | Pore Size (nominal) | **0.5 – 2** | nm | | Molecular Weight Cut-Off (MWCO) | **200 – 1000** | Daltons | | Operating Pressure | **5 – 40** | bar | | Membrane Flux | **10 – 50** | L·m⁻²·h⁻¹ | | pH operating range | **2 – 11** | — | | Typical Rejection of NaCl | **20 – 80** | % | | Typical Rejection of MgSO₄ | **> 95** | % | | Typical Rejection of Sucrose | **> 99** | % | ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Principle — Stepwise Logic The separation mechanism in nanofiltration is governed by a **dual mechanism**: **Step 1: Charge-Based Rejection (Donnan Exclusion)** NF membranes typically carry a fixed negative surface charge (often due to carboxyl groups —COOH in polyamide thin-film composites). When an ionic feed contacts this charged surface: - **Co-ions** (ions of the same charge as the membrane) are electrostatically repelled. - **Counter-ions** are partially retained to maintain electroneutrality (Donnan equilibrium). - Therefore, **multivalent salts (Mg²⁺, SO₄²⁻, Ca²⁺)** are rejected much more strongly than monovalent salts (Na⁺, Cl⁻). **Step 2: Size-Sieving (Steric Hindrance)** - Neutral molecules larger than the membrane pore size are physically excluded. - For organic molecules (dyes, sugars, pesticides), MWCO is the governing parameter. **Step 3: Solution-Diffusion Coupled with Transport** - Water passes through the membrane via a combination of viscous flow (through transient pores) and solution-diffusion. - Solutes are partially rejected based on size and charge. **Step 4: Concentration Polarization** - Retained solutes accumulate near the membrane surface, creating a boundary layer with concentration $C_m > C_b$ (bulk). - This reduces effective driving force and must be modeled via the film theory. ### 2.2 Why and How Behind the Mechanism > [!NOTE] > **Why does NF reject salts selectively based on charge?** > Because NF membranes have fixed ionic groups (e.g., sulfonic or carboxylic acid groups deprotonated at neutral pH). The electric double layer repels co-ions strongly when they are multivalent (charge squared dependence). Hence MgSO₄ rejection can exceed 99% while NaCl rejection is only 40–60%. ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Equation | Formula | Application | |---|---|---| | **Membrane Flux (Volume)** | $J_v = \dfrac{V}{A \cdot \Delta t}$ | Permeate collection rate | | **Permeate Flux (Driving Force)** | $J_v = L_p (\Delta P - \sigma \cdot \Delta \pi)$ | Kedem–Katchalsky model; $L_p$ = permeability, $\sigma$ = reflection coefficient | | **Solute Flux** | $J_s = -P_s \dfrac{dC}{dx} + (1-\sigma) J_v \cdot \bar{C}$ | Spiegler–Kedem equation | | **Observed Rejection** | $R_{obs} = 1 - \dfrac{C_p}{C_b}$ | Direct experimental measurement | | **Intrinsic Rejection** | $R_{int} = 1 - \dfrac{C_p}{C_m}$ | True membrane selectivity (requires $C_m$) | | **Concentration Polarization** | $\dfrac{C_m - C_p}{C_b - C_p} = \exp\left(\dfrac{J_v}{k}\right)$ | Film theory; $k$ = mass transfer coefficient | | **Resistance-in-Series** | $J_v = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\mu (R_m + R_f + R_c)}$ | Total fouling resistance model | | **MWCO Definition** | Solute molecular weight at $R = 90\%$ | Membrane classification | | **Schmidt Number** | $Sc = \dfrac{\nu}{D_{AB}}$ | Used in Sherwood correlations | | **Reynolds Number** | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v d_h}{\mu}$ | Flow regime in membrane channels | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical KTU Note:** When writing the rejection formula, **do not use absolute-value pipes** in markdown tables. Use $\vert C_p \vert$ or $\mid C_p \mid$ in LaTeX to prevent rendering errors. ### 2.4 Real-World Utility Nanofiltration is extensively deployed in: - **Water softening** — Replacing lime-soda softening with NF removes Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺ at lower chemical cost. - **Dairy industry** — Demineralization of whey; concentration of lactose and milk proteins. - **Textile industry** — Recovery of dyes and reuse of process water. - **Pharmaceutical industry** — Concentration and purification of antibiotics and peptides. - **Sugar refining** — Decolorization of cane juice and reduction of molasses-formation sugars. - **Pharmaceutical water systems** — Production of highly purified water. ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Kedem–Katchalsky Transport Equations The irreversible thermodynamic model treats the membrane as a "black box" characterized by three phenomenological coefficients. **Starting Postulate:** The membrane has reflection coefficient $\sigma$ ($0 \le \sigma \le 1$). When $\sigma = 1$, the membrane is perfectly semipermeable (ideal RO); when $\sigma = 0$, it is nonselective (ideal UF). **Step 1: Volumetric Flux Equation** Assume the membrane permits a coupling between solute and water flow. The driving force for volume flow is the effective pressure difference. $$ J_v = L_p (\Delta P - \sigma \cdot \Delta \pi) $$ where: - $L_p$ = hydraulic permeability coefficient (m·s⁻¹·Pa⁻¹) - $\Delta P = P_{feed} - P_{permeate}$ (Pa) - $\Delta \pi = \pi(C_m) - \pi(C_p)$ (Pa) - $\sigma$ = reflection (Staverman) coefficient **Step 2: Solute Flux Equation** The solute moves by two mechanisms: 1. **Diffusion** down its concentration gradient with permeability $P_s$. 2. **Convective drag** with the solvent, scaled by $(1 - \sigma)$. $$ J_s = -P_s \dfrac{dC}{dx} + (1 - \sigma) \cdot J_v \cdot \bar{C} $$ **Step 3: Spiegler–Kedem Integration** Integrate across the membrane thickness $\Delta x$ from feed side ($x=0$, $C=C_m$) to permeate side ($x=\Delta x$, $C=C_p$): $$ J_s = -P_s \dfrac{dC}{dx} + (1 - \sigma) J_v \bar{C} $$ Rearranging: $$ \dfrac{dC}{dx} = \dfrac{(1 - \sigma) J_v}{P_s} \bar{C} - \dfrac{J_s}{P_s} $$ Integrate assuming constant $J_s$ and constant $P_s$ across the membrane: $$ \int_{C_m}^{C_p} \dfrac{d\bar{C}}{(1-\sigma)\bar{C} - \dfrac{J_s}{J_v}} = \int_{0}^{\Delta x} \dfrac{J_v}{P_s} dx $$ The right-hand side yields $\dfrac{J_v \Delta x}{P_s}$. Solving for the solute flux gives the **Spiegler–Kedem intrinsic rejection**: $$ R_{int} = 1 - \dfrac{C_p}{C_m} = \dfrac{\sigma (1 - F)}{1 - \sigma F} $$ where: $$ F = \exp\left[ -\dfrac{J_v (1-\sigma)}{P_s} \right] $$ **Step 4: Link to Observed Rejection** Observed rejection $R_{obs}$ is related to intrinsic rejection $R_{int}$ via the concentration polarization modulus (film theory): $$ \dfrac{C_m - C_p}{C_b - C_p} = \exp\left(\dfrac{J_v}{k}\right) $$ Combining both: $$ R_{obs} = \dfrac{R_{int} \exp\left(\dfrac{J_v}{k}\right)}{1 + R_{int}\left[\exp\left(\dfrac{J_v}{k}\right) - 1\right]} $$ **Step 5: Limiting Cases** - **At very high $J_v$:** $\exp(J_v/k) \to \infty$, so $R_{obs} \to 1$ (concentration polarization dominates). - **At $J_v \to 0$:** $F \to 1$, so $R_{int} \to \sigma$ (limiting rejection governed by reflection coefficient). - **For NF membranes:** Typically $\sigma \approx 0.6 - 0.9$ for salts. ### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style) **Problem:** A nanofiltration membrane module is used to treat a feed containing **NaCl at $C_b = 2000$ mg/L**. The membrane has $L_p = 5 \times 10^{-11}$ m·s⁻¹·Pa⁻¹, $\sigma = 0.85$, and the mass transfer coefficient is $k = 2 \times 10^{-5}$ m·s⁻¹. The applied pressure is $\Delta P = 1.5$ MPa. Osmotic pressure of NaCl solution at $C_m$ is estimated as $\Delta \pi = 0.4$ MPa. Permeate concentration $C_p = 800$ mg/L. **Required:** 1. Calculate the volumetric flux $J_v$. 2. Determine the observed rejection $R_{obs}$. **Solution:** **Step 1:** Apply Kedem–Katchalsky volumetric flux equation. $$ J_v = L_p (\Delta P - \sigma \cdot \Delta \pi) $$ Convert MPa to Pa: $\Delta P = 1.5 \times 10^6$ Pa, $\Delta \pi = 0.4 \times 10^6$ Pa. $$ J_v = (5 \times 10^{-11}) \times [(1.5 \times 10^6) - (0.85)(0.4 \times 10^6)] $$ $$ J_v = (5 \times 10^{-11}) \times [(1.5 - 0.34) \times 10^6] $$ $$ J_v = (5 \times 10^{-11}) \times (1.16 \times 10^6) $$ $$ J_v = 5.8 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m·s}^{-1} $$ Convert to LMH (liters per m² per hour): $$ J_v = 5.8 \times 10^{-5} \times 10^3 \times 3600 = 208.8 \text{ L·m}^{-2}\text{·h}^{-1} $$ **Step 2:** Compute the observed rejection. $$ R_{obs} = 1 - \dfrac{C_p}{C_b} = 1 - \dfrac{800}{2000} = 1 - 0.4 = 0.60 \text{ or } 60\% $$ **Step 3:** Compute concentration polarization modulus. $$ \dfrac{C_m - C_p}{C_b - C_p} = \exp\left(\dfrac{J_v}{k}\right) = \exp\left(\dfrac{5.8 \times 10^{-5}}{2 \times 10^{-5}}\right) = \exp(2.9) = 18.17 $$ Solve for $C_m$: $$ C_m = C_p + (C_b - C_p)(18.17) = 800 + (1200)(18.17) = 22{,}604 \text{ mg/L} $$ This indicates severe concentration polarization. **Step 4:** Compute intrinsic rejection. $$ R_{int} = 1 - \dfrac{C_p}{C_m} = 1 - \dfrac{800}{22{,}604} = 1 - 0.0354 = 0.9646 \text{ or } 96.46\% $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Examination Insight:** Notice how observed rejection (60%) grossly underestimates the true membrane selectivity (96.5%). In KTU problems, if asked "why is the real rejection higher than observed?", always invoke concentration polarization as the answer. ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Block-Level Architecture: NF Membrane Operation ```mermaid flowchart TD A["FEED SOLUTION"] --> B["HIGH PRESSURE PUMP"] B --> C["NF MEMBRANE MODULE"] C --> D["PERMEATE STREAM"] C --> E["CONCENTRATE RETENTATE"] C --> F["BOUNDARY LAYER"] F --> G["CONCENTRATION POLARIZATION"] G --> H["FOULING LAYER"] H --> I["CLEANING CYCLE"] C --> J["DONNAN EXCLUSION"] C --> K["SIZE SIEVING"] C --> L["SOLUTION DIFFUSION"] D --> M["SOFT WATER PRODUCT"] D --> N["DYE RETENTION"] D --> O["SUGAR CONCENTRATE"] E --> P["BRINE DISPOSAL"] ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Comparative Flowchart: Membrane Spectrum ```mermaid flowchart LR MF["MICROFILTRATION
0.1 to 10 micron"] --> UF["ULTRAFILTRATION
1 to 100 nm"] UF --> NF["NANOFILTRATION
0.5 to 2 nm
MWCO 200 to 1000 Da"] NF --> RO["REVERSE OSMOSIS
less than 0.5 nm
MWCO less than 200 Da"] ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Process Flow: Sugar Juice Decolorization (NF Application) ```mermaid flowchart TD A["CANE JUICE FEED
Brix 15 to 20"] --> B["PRE TREATMENT
LIME PH ADJUST"] B --> C["NF MEMBRANE LOOP"] C --> D["PERMEATE
Decolorized Juice
Color less than 1000 ICU"] C --> E["RETENTATE
Recycled to Feed"] D --> F["EVAPORATOR"] F --> G["CRYSTALLIZER"] G --> H["WHITE SUGAR PRODUCT"] ``` ### 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram: Donnan Exclusion Mechanism ```mermaid flowchart TD A["NEGATIVELY CHARGED
NF MEMBRANE SURFACE"] --> B["CO ION REPULSION
Na+ Cl- partially repelled"] A --> C["COUNTER ION ATTRACTION
Partial retention for electroneutrality"] B --> D["MULTIVALENT ION REJECTION
MgSO4 more than 99 percent"] C --> D D --> E["PARTIAL MONOVALENT PASSAGE
NaCl 20 to 80 percent"] ``` ### 4.5 Conceptual Cross-Section Schematic (Textual Representation) ``` FEED SIDE PERMEATE SIDE ----------- -------------- Cb Cm ========================== Cp | | | POLYMER MATRIX | | | | | Carboxyl groups | | | | | --COO(-) | | | | | ++++++++--------+ | | | | | Pores 0.5 to 2 nm | | | | | ++++++++--------+ | | | | ========================== ↑ ↑ Bulk feed Concentration polarization layer ``` > [!NOTE] > The boundary layer from $C_b$ to $C_m$ is the **concentration polarization** region — this is precisely what the film theory equation models. ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Define nanofiltration. How is it distinguished from reverse osmosis in terms of operating pressure and salt rejection?* **Model Answer:** Nanofiltration (NF) is a pressure-driven membrane process that uses membranes with nominal pore sizes of **0.5 – 2 nm** and MWCO of **200 – 1000 Da** to separate multivalent ions and small organic molecules from solutions. **Distinction from Reverse Osmosis:** | Parameter | Nanofiltration | Reverse Osmosis | |---|---|---| | Operating Pressure | **5 – 40 bar** | **15 – 80 bar** | | Salt Rejection (NaCl) | **20 – 80%** | **> 99%** | | Separation Mechanism | Size sieving + Donnan | Solution-diffusion | | Energy Consumption | Lower | Higher | NF operates at **lower pressure** because it partially allows monovalent salts to pass, reducing the required osmotic pressure differential. **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** *Explain the Donnan exclusion mechanism in nanofiltration.* **Model Answer:** Donnan exclusion is a **charge-based rejection** mechanism operating in NF membranes. NF membranes possess fixed ionic groups (typically —COO⁻ from carboxylic acid at neutral pH) on their surface and within their pores. When an electrolyte solution contacts this charged membrane: 1. **Co-ions** (ions with the same charge as the membrane) are electrostatically **repelled**. 2. **Counter-ions** are partially retained to maintain **electroneutrality** at the membrane interface. 3. Because electrostatic repulsion scales with the **square of the valence**, multivalent co-ions (SO₄²⁻) experience much stronger rejection than monovalent co-ions (Cl⁻). Hence, **divalent salts like MgSO₄ are rejected >95%**, while **monovalent NaCl is only rejected 20 – 80%** by typical NF membranes. --- ### PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice --- #### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** **(a)** Derive the Spiegler–Kedem equation for solute transport through a nanofiltration membrane. Explain the significance of the reflection coefficient $\sigma$ and the solute permeability $P_s$. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(i) Starting Model:** The Spiegler–Kedem model applies irreversible thermodynamics to a membrane characterized by three coefficients: hydraulic permeability $L_p$, reflection coefficient $\sigma$, and solute permeability $P_s$. The local solute flux at any point $x$ inside the membrane is: $$ J_s = -P_s \dfrac{dC}{dx} + (1-\sigma) J_v \cdot \bar{C} $$ **(ii) Integration:** Assuming $J_s$, $J_v$, and $P_s$ are constant across the membrane, and integrating from $x = 0$ (membrane surface, $C = C_m$) to $x = \Delta x$ (permeate side, $C = C_p$): $$ \int_{C_m}^{C_p} \dfrac{d\bar{C}}{(1-\sigma)\bar{C} - \dfrac{J_s}{J_v}} = \dfrac{J_v \Delta x}{P_s} $$ Evaluating the integral and defining the Peclet number $\Pe = \dfrac{J_v (1-\sigma) \Delta x}{P_s}$: $$ \ln\left[\dfrac{(1-\sigma) - J_s/J_v}{(1-\sigma)(C_m/C_p) - J_s/J_v}\right] = \dfrac{J_v(1-\sigma)\Delta x}{P_s} $$ Rearranging, and substituting $R_{int} = 1 - C_p/C_m$: $$ R_{int} = 1 - \dfrac{C_p}{C_m} = \dfrac{\sigma (1-F)}{1 - \sigma F} $$ where: $$ F = \exp\left[-\dfrac{J_v(1-\sigma)}{P_s}\right] $$ **(iii) Significance of Coefficients:** - **Reflection coefficient $\sigma$** ($0 \le \sigma \le 1$): Represents the **maximum intrinsic rejection** at the limiting case of infinite flux. $\sigma = 1$ for an ideal semipermeable membrane (RO); $\sigma = 0$ for a nonselective porous membrane. - **Solute permeability $P_s$**: Governs the rate at which solute can diffuse through the membrane. High $P_s$ means more leakage; low $P_s$ means tighter membrane. **(b)** A nanofiltration membrane is operated with the following data: - Feed concentration: $C_b = 3000$ mg/L of MgSO₄ - Permeate concentration: $C_p = 90$ mg/L - Mass transfer coefficient: $k = 1.5 \times 10^{-5}$ m·s⁻¹ - Volumetric flux: $J_v = 4.0 \times 10^{-5}$ m·s⁻¹ Calculate the **observed rejection**, the **concentration polarization modulus**, the **wall concentration $C_m$**, and the **intrinsic rejection $R_{int}$**. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(i) Observed Rejection:** $$ R_{obs} = 1 - \dfrac{C_p}{C_b} = 1 - \dfrac{90}{3000} = 1 - 0.03 = 0.97 \text{ or } 97\% $$ **(ii) Concentration Polarization Modulus:** $$ \dfrac{C_m - C_p}{C_b - C_p} = \exp\left(\dfrac{J_v}{k}\right) = \exp\left(\dfrac{4.0 \times 10^{-5}}{1.5 \times 10^{-5}}\right) = \exp(2.667) $$ $$ \exp(2.667) \approx 14.39 $$ **(iii) Wall Concentration $C_m$:** $$ C_m = C_p + (C_b - C_p) \times 14.39 $$ $$ C_m = 90 + (3000 - 90)(14.39) = 90 + (2910)(14.39) $$ $$ C_m = 90 + 41{,}875 \approx 41{,}965 \text{ mg/L} $$ **(iv) Intrinsic Rejection:** $$ R_{int} = 1 - \dfrac{C_p}{C_m} = 1 - \dfrac{90}{41{,}965} = 1 - 0.00214 = 0.9979 \text{ or } 99.79\% $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning #1:** > Many students confuse observed rejection with intrinsic rejection. They are **NOT equal**. Observed rejection $R_{obs}$ is measured experimentally; intrinsic rejection $R_{int}$ reflects the membrane's true selectivity. Concentration polarization makes $R_{obs} < R_{int}$. Always compute $C_m$ via the film-theory exponential equation before computing $R_{int}$. Also, **do not** plug $C_b$ directly into the Spiegler–Kedem equation — use $C_m$. --- #### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2023 — Alternative Choice]** **(a)** Discuss in detail the various **applications of nanofiltration** in the chemical, food, and water-treatment industries. Mention at least four applications with process conditions. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Application 1: Water Softening (Water Treatment Industry)** - NF membranes replace lime-soda softening plants. - They reject Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺ (>90%) and sulfate ions while letting partial NaCl pass. - Operating pressure: 5 – 15 bar. - Advantage: No chemical addition, lower sludge generation. **Application 2: Whey Demineralization (Dairy Industry)** - Cheese whey contains 6% solids and ~0.8% ash (NaCl, KCl, Ca salts). - NF at 20 – 30 bar simultaneously concentrates lactose/protein **and** reduces ash content by 30 – 50%. - Replaces electrodialysis + evaporation combinations. **Application 3: Decolorization of Cane Sugar Juice (Sugar Industry)** - Color bodies are high-MW organics (MW > 500 Da). - NF with MWCO = 300 Da retains color molecules while letting sucrose (MW = 342) partially pass. - Permeate is colorless and ready for evaporation/crystallization. - Reduces activated carbon consumption. **Application 4: Dye Recovery and Reuse (Textile Industry)** - Reactive dyes have MW = 600 – 1500 Da. - NF rejects >99% of dye molecules, allowing recovery of process water and reuse of expensive dyes. - Operating pressure: 10 – 25 bar; flux: 15 – 30 LMH. **Application 5: Pharmaceutical Peptide Concentration** - Heat-sensitive peptide antibiotics. - NF gently concentrates without phase change, preserving bioactivity. **(b)** Compare **Nanofiltration, Ultrafiltration, and Reverse Osmosis** based on: (i) membrane pore size, (ii) MWCO, (iii) operating pressure, (iv) rejection of monovalent ions, (v) dominant separation mechanism. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Ultrafiltration (UF) | Nanofiltration (NF) | Reverse Osmosis (RO) | |---|---|---|---| | Pore Size | 1 – 100 nm | **0.5 – 2 nm** | < 0.5 nm (dense) | | MWCO | 1000 – 500,000 Da | **200 – 1000 Da** | < 200 Da | | Operating Pressure | 1 – 5 bar | **5 – 40 bar** | 15 – 80 bar | | Monovalent Ion Rejection | 0% | **20 – 80%** | > 99% | | Divalent Ion Rejection | 0% | **> 95%** | > 99% | | Mechanism | Size sieving | **Donnan + Size sieving** | Solution-diffusion | | Osmotic Pressure Effect | Negligible | **Moderate** | Dominant | | Membrane Type | Asymmetric porous | Thin-film composite | Dense asymmetric | | Typical Application | Protein concentration | Water softening | Seawater desalination | | Cost per m³ Permeate | Low | Medium | High | **Discussion:** - **UF** is purely size-based; it cannot discriminate between ions of similar molecular weight. - **NF** is the only process that discriminates between ions based on **valence** — this is the unique selling point exploited in softening and demineralization. - **RO** is virtually impermeable to all solutes but requires energy-intensive pressures to overcome high osmotic pressures. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning #2:** > A common student mistake is to list only pore size in the comparison. Examiners specifically allocate 1 mark per row in the comparison table. Always include all six parameters. Also, students confuse "operating pressure" with "transmembrane pressure" (TMP) — they are the same in dead-end filtration but differ in crossflow modules due to pressure drop along the channel. --- ### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER - [ ] **NF occupies the niche** between UF and RO; pore size **0.5 – 2 nm**, MWCO **200 – 1000 Da**. - [ ] NF operates at **5 – 40 bar** — much lower than RO's 15 – 80 bar. - [ ] **Dual mechanism**: Donnan exclusion (charge) + Size sieving (steric). - [ ] **Divalent ions rejected > 95%**, **monovalent ions 20 – 80%** — the hallmark of NF. - [ ] NF membranes are **typically negatively charged** (carboxyl groups —COO⁻) at neutral pH. - [ ] **Kedem–Katchalsky flux equation**: $J_v = L_p (\Delta P - \sigma \cdot \Delta \pi)$. - [ ] **Spiegler–Kedem rejection equation**: $R_{int} = \dfrac{\sigma (1 - F)}{1 - \sigma F}$ with $F = \exp[-J_v(1-\sigma)/P_s]$. - [ ] **Concentration polarization** always makes $R_{obs} < R_{int}$. - [ ] Film-theory modulus: $\dfrac{C_m - C_p}{C_b - C_p} = \exp(J_v / k)$. - [ ] Key industries: water softening, dairy, sugar, textile, pharmaceutical. - [ ] **Reject absolute-value pipes `|x|`** in markdown tables — always use `\vert x\vert` or `\mid x \mid`. - [ ] Always compute $C_m$ before computing $R_{int}$ in numerical problems. - [ ] Remember unit conversion: **1 LMH = 2.778 × 10⁻⁷ m·s⁻¹**.

topics.title: Ultrafiltration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT752 - POLYMERS AND SEPARATION PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Ultrafiltration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Ultrafiltration (UF)** is a pressure-driven membrane separation process in which hydrostatic pressure is applied to a liquid feed to force water and low-molecular-weight solutes through a semipermeable membrane, while retaining macromolecules, colloids, suspended solids, bacteria, and viruses. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme (PEPOT752) syllabus, ultrafiltration is classified within the spectrum of membrane separation processes based on its nominal pore size, which lies between **nanofiltration (NF)** and **microfiltration (MF)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus-Defining Characteristics of Ultrafiltration**
> - Nominal pore size range: **0.001 µm to 0.1 µm** (i.e., **1 nm to 100 nm**)
> - Operating transmembrane pressure: **0.1 MPa to 1 MPa** (≈ **1 bar to 10 bar**)
> - Retained species: macromolecules with **Molecular Weight Cut-Off (MWCO)** between **1,000 Da and 500,000 Da** (often 10 kDa to 500 kDa)
> - Permeates: water, salts, low-molecular-weight organic compounds
> - Driving force: **pressure gradient** (no phase change, no thermal input)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Coffee Filter with Microscopic Precision" Analogy**
> Imagine a typical coffee filter: when you pour coffee grounds mixed with hot water through it, the paper filter holds back the coarse grounds but lets the brown liquid (dissolved coffee compounds) pass. **Ultrafiltration works on the same principle, but the "filter" is engineered at the molecular scale.** The membrane acts as an ultra-fine sieve where:
> - The "filter holes" (pores) are precisely engineered to dimensions between **1 nm and 100 nm**.
> - The "coffee grounds" are proteins, bacteria, viruses, and colloidal particles that cannot pass.
> - The "coffee liquid" (permeate) consists of water, dissolved ions, and small organic molecules that flow through the pores.
> The driving "push" comes from a pump generating pressure, much like squeezing a wet sponge to force water out through its small holes while keeping the sponge fabric intact.

## 1.3 Position of Ultrafiltration in the Membrane Separation Spectrum

| Process | Pore Size (approx.) | MWCO (Da) | Operating Pressure | Retains |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Microfiltration (MF) | 0.1 – 10 µm | > 500,000 | < 2 bar | Bacteria, suspended solids |
| **Ultrafiltration (UF)** | **0.001 – 0.1 µm** | **1,000 – 500,000** | **1 – 10 bar** | **Proteins, viruses, colloids** |
| Nanofiltration (NF) | 0.0005 – 0.005 µm | 200 – 1,000 | 5 – 35 bar | Divalent salts, sugars |
| Reverse Osmosis (RO) | < 0.0005 µm | < 200 | 15 – 80 bar | Monovalent salts |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for Membrane Hierarchy (largest to smallest pores):**
> **"M**y **U**ltra **N**ano **R**outine" → **M**icrofiltration → **U**ltrafiltration → **N**anofiltration → **R**O
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — The "Why" and the "How"

The fundamental physical principle behind ultrafiltration is **size exclusion (sieving)** at the molecular level, supplemented by secondary mechanisms:
