In the context of **PEPOT752 – Polymers and Separation Processes (KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 2)**, these models form the theoretical backbone for designing, scaling, and optimising industrial membrane processes like **Reverse Osmosis (RO)**, **Ultrafiltration (UF)**, **Nanofiltration (NF)**, **Pervaporation (PV)**, **Gas Separation (GS)**, and **Dialysis**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> Students must be able to derive and apply the **Pore Flow Model**, **Solution-Diffusion Model**, **Solution-Diffusion-Imperfection Model**, and **Molecular Sieve Model**, with emphasis on their assumptions, governing flux equations, and the physical reasoning behind solute/solvent transport.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Straw-and-Sponge" Intuition

Imagine you have two very different materials on your kitchen shelf:

- **A bundle of drinking straws (porous membrane):** If you dip one end in a coloured drink and blow gently from the other end, the liquid gushes straight through the hollow tubes. The driving force is the pressure you apply, and the **flow is bulk / viscous** — the liquid essentially *pushes* through the physical channels. This is the **Pore Flow Model**.

- **A piece of soft kitchen sponge (dense / non-porous membrane):** If you place a drop of ink on one side, it doesn't run through. Instead, the ink molecules slowly *dissolve* into the sponge material, *diffuse* through the polymer matrix, and finally *evaporate* (desorb) from the other side. Here, transport is governed by **molecular diffusion** driven by a concentration gradient. This is the **Solution-Diffusion Model**.

Real membranes usually lie somewhere between these two extremes — some have visible pores (microporous), some are dense (non-porous), and many industrial membranes have a few imperfections in the dense top layer. The four classical models in this module mathematically describe these physical situations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric (KTU Convention):**
> Membrane flux is reported in **SI units of m³/(m²·s)** — often written as **m/s** — or in the practical **LMH (Litre per m² per Hour)** for liquid systems, and **GPU (Gas Permeation Unit, $1 \text{ GPU} = 3.35 \times 10^{-10} \text{ mol}/(\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{s} \cdot \text{Pa})$)** for gas separations.

### 1.3 Classification of Membrane Transport Models

| # | Model | Membrane Assumption | Driving Force | Typical Application |
|---|-------|---------------------|---------------|----------------------|
| 1 | **Pore Flow Model** (Hagen–Poiseuille / Capillary) | Fixed cylindrical pores | Hydraulic pressure | MF, UF, porous membranes |
| 2 | **Solution-Diffusion Model** | Dense, non-porous | Chemical-potential gradient (Δc, Δp) | RO, GS, PV |
| 3 | **Solution-Diffusion-Imperfection Model** | Dense with small defects | Pressure + concentration | Real RO membranes |
| 4 | **Molecular Sieve Model** | Pores of molecular dimensions | Sieving by size | Zeolites, NF, gas sieving |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Pore Flow Model (Hagen–Poiseuille / Capillary Flow Model)

### Assumptions
- The membrane contains a set of **parallel, cylindrical, uniform pores** of radius $r$.
- The pores pass perpendicularly through the membrane.
- Transport is governed by **viscous (Poiseuille) flow** through these capillaries.
- The fluid is Newtonian and incompressible, and flow is laminar.
- No solute–membrane interaction — solvent and solutes of size smaller than the pore pass freely.

### Governing Equation
For a single straight cylindrical pore, the classical **Hagen–Poiseuille equation** gives the volumetric flow rate:

$$Q_{\text{single}} = \frac{\pi \, r^{4} \, \Delta P}{8 \, \mu \, L}$$

The membrane contains $n$ pores per unit area. The total membrane flux $J$ (volumetric flow per unit membrane area) is:

$$J = \frac{n \, \pi \, r^{4} \, \Delta P}{8 \, \mu \, L}$$

Introducing the **surface porosity** $\varepsilon = n \pi r^{2}$ (fractional pore area on the membrane surface) and **tortuosity** $\tau$ (ratio of actual pore length to membrane thickness, $L_{\text{pore}} = \tau L$):

$$J = \frac{\varepsilon \, r^{2} \, \Delta P}{8 \, \mu \, \tau \, L}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Pore Flow Model predicts flux that is **proportional to $\Delta P$** and **inversely proportional to viscosity $\mu$**. This is the same behaviour as laminar flow in a pipe.

### Why this model matters in KTU
This model is the foundation for **porosity characterisation** of UF/MF membranes. A simple plot of $J$ vs $\Delta P$ gives a straight line whose slope can be used to extract the **effective pore radius** — a frequently asked numerical problem in KTU exams.

---

## 2.2 Solution-Diffusion Model

### Assumptions
- The membrane is a **dense, non-porous** polymer film with no continuous pores.
- The membrane is in **thermodynamic equilibrium** with the fluid phases on either side.
- Transport occurs by **dissolution** of penetrant into the membrane at the upstream face, **molecular diffusion** through the polymer, and **desorption** at the downstream face.
- The chemical potential gradient (not pressure gradient) drives transport; in liquids this reduces to a **concentration gradient**, in gases to a **partial-pressure gradient**.

### Governing Equations
For unidirectional (1-D) steady-state diffusion through a flat membrane, Fick's first law applies:

$$J = -D \frac{dc}{dx}$$

Integrating across the membrane thickness $\delta$:

$$J = \frac{D \, (c_1 - c_2)}{\delta} = \frac{D \, S \, (p_1 - p_2)}{\delta}$$

where:
- $D$ = diffusion coefficient of penetrant in membrane ($\text{m}^2/\text{s}$)
- $S$ = solubility coefficient (Henry-type), so $c = S \, p$
- $c_1, c_2$ = penetrant concentrations just inside the upstream and downstream membrane faces
- $p_1, p_2$ = upstream and downstream partial pressures (or activities in liquids)
- $\delta$ = effective membrane thickness

Defining the **permeability** $P = D \cdot S$:

$$\boxed{\,J = \frac{P \, (p_1 - p_2)}{\delta}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Selectivity** in this model comes from the **ratio of permeabilities** of two species. A good gas-separation membrane is one where $P_A \gg P_B$ for the desired pair A/B.

### Real-world utility
- **Reverse Osmosis (water desalination):** Water dissolves into the dense polyamide layer; salt ions have a much lower $S$ and $D$, so the membrane rejects salt.
- **Oxygen enrichment of air:** Poly(dimethyl siloxane) has a high $P$ for O₂; air is enriched to ~30–40 % O₂ in one pass.
- **Pervaporation (e.g. ethanol dehydration):** Water dissolves preferentially in polyvinyl alcohol and diffuses through; ethanol is rejected.

---

## 2.3 Solution-Diffusion-Imperfection Model

The Solution-Diffusion model assumes a perfectly dense film, but real RO membranes always contain a few **pin-holes, grain boundaries, or micro-defects** in their skin layer. The **Solution-Diffusion-Imperfection (S-D-I) Model** superimposes a small viscous flow through these defects on the underlying solution-diffusion transport:

$$J_{\text{total}} = J_{\text{S-D}} + J_{\text{imperfection}} = \frac{P \, (p_1 - p_2)}{\delta} + \frac{P_1 \, (p_1 - p_2)}{\delta} = \frac{(P + P_1) \, (p_1 - p_2)}{\delta}$$

Here, $P_1$ is a **permeation parameter due to imperfection flow**; it captures the *viscous slip* of solvent through defects. This model is the one used by KTU textbook authors (e.g. *Noble & Stern*, *Baker*) to interpret **real RO membrane data**, where a small but non-zero salt leakage is observed.

---

## 2.4 Molecular Sieve (Size Exclusion) Model

In membranes with pore diameters of the **same order as the molecular size** of the permeating species (typically 0.2–2 nm — i.e. nanofiltration and tight UF), the dominant mechanism is **steric exclusion**:

- Molecules **smaller** than the pore pass; molecules **larger** than the pore are rejected.
- The cut-off is a sharp function of the ratio of molecular diameter $d_m$ to pore diameter $d_p$.
- This model explains why UF membranes with **MWCO (Molecular Weight Cut-Off)** specifications of 1,000 / 10,000 / 100,000 work as they do.

The classical steric partitioning coefficient at the pore mouth is:

$$\Phi = \left(1 - \lambda\right)^2 \quad \text{where} \quad \lambda = \frac{d_m}{d_p}$$

and the solute flux (with hindered diffusion) is:

$$J_s = -D_p \, \frac{dc}{dx}$$

with $D_p = D_{\infty} \cdot K_d(\lambda)$, where $K_d$ is the **hydrodynamic hindrance factor** that drops sharply as $\lambda \to 1$.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| # | Model | Core Equation | Variables / Units | Key Trend |
|---|-------|---------------|-------------------|-----------|
| 1 | Pore Flow | $J = \dfrac{\varepsilon \, r^{2} \, \Delta P}{8 \, \mu \, \tau \, L}$ | $r$ = m, $\Delta P$ = Pa, $\mu$ = Pa·s, $L$ = m, $\varepsilon$ = dimensionless | $J \propto \Delta P$ |
| 2 | Hagen–Poiseuille (single pore) | $Q = \dfrac{\pi \, r^{4} \, \Delta P}{8 \, \mu \, L}$ | $Q$ = m³/s, $r$ = m | $Q \propto r^{4}$ |
| 3 | Solution-Diffusion | $J = \dfrac{P \, (p_1 - p_2)}{\delta} = \dfrac{D \, S \, (p_1 - p_2)}{\delta}$ | $D$ = m²/s, $S$ = mol/(m³·Pa), $P$ = mol·m/(m²·s·Pa) = mol·m·m⁻²·s⁻¹·Pa⁻¹ | $J \propto \Delta p$ |
| 4 | Solution-Diffusion-Imperfection | $J = \dfrac{(P + P_1) \, (p_1 - p_2)}{\delta}$ | $P_1$ = imperfection parameter | $J \propto \Delta p$ |
| 5 | Molecular Sieve (Steric) | $\Phi = (1 - \lambda)^{2}, \; \lambda = d_m / d_p$ | dimensionless | $J_s \to 0$ as $\lambda \to 1$ |
| 6 | Permeability definition | $P = D \cdot S$ | mol·m/(m²·s·Pa) | — |
| 7 | Permeance (pressure-normalised flux) | $Q = P / \delta$ | GPU or mol/(m²·s·Pa) | — |
| 8 | Ideal selectivity | $\alpha_{A/B} = P_A / P_B$ | dimensionless | $\alpha \gg 1 \Rightarrow$ good separation |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Tip:** The relation $J \propto \Delta P$ holds for **both** pore-flow and solution-diffusion-imperfection models. A plot of $J$ vs $\Delta P$ is a straight line, but only the **pure solution-diffusion** model predicts a *finite solvent flux at $\Delta P = 0$* when the downstream pressure is reduced (as in pervaporation). This is a favourite conceptual question in KTU ESE.

---

## 2.6 Real-world Engineering Utility

- **Designing RO plants** for water reuse: Pore-flow + S-D-I models are used to predict flux decline and the effect of operating pressure.
- **Membrane characterisation in R&D labs:** Pore-flow analysis of water flux vs $\Delta P$ gives the **mean pore radius** $r$ of UF membranes.
- **Selecting polymers for gas separation:** Solution-diffusion model provides the **Robeson upper bound** relationship between permeability and selectivity — a "rule of thumb" used by every membrane engineer.
- **Pharmaceuticals (protein fractionation):** Molecular-sieve UF membranes separate proteins by size; the **MWCO** specification of the membrane dictates process feasibility.
- **Hydrogen recovery in refineries:** Solution-diffusion in polyimide hollow fibres recovers H₂ from refinery off-gas with $\alpha_{H_2/CO_2} \approx 5$–10.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Pore Flow Flux from Hagen–Poiseuille

We start from the Navier–Stokes equation for steady, fully-developed, laminar, axisymmetric flow of a Newtonian fluid in a straight cylindrical tube of radius $r$ and length $L$. The pressure drop is $\Delta P$ along the tube.

**Step 1 — Force balance on a fluid element (cylindrical shell of radius $\rho$):**
A cylindrical shell between radius $\rho$ and $\rho + d\rho$ is acted on by:
- Pressure force on the two ends: $\pi \rho^{2} \, (P_1 - P_2) = \pi \rho^{2} \, \Delta P$.
- Shear stress on the curved surface: $-2 \pi \rho \, L \, \tau_{r z}$.

At steady state these balance:

$$\pi \rho^{2} \, \Delta P - 2 \pi \rho \, L \, \tau_{r z} = 0$$

**Step 2 — Newton's law of viscosity for a Newtonian fluid:**

$$\tau_{r z} = -\mu \, \frac{dv_z}{d\rho}$$

Substituting into the force balance:

$$\pi \rho^{2} \, \Delta P + 2 \pi \rho \, L \, \mu \, \frac{dv_z}{d\rho} = 0$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange to isolate the velocity gradient:**

$$\frac{dv_z}{d\rho} = -\frac{\Delta P}{2 \mu L} \, \rho$$

**Step 4 — Integrate with respect to $\rho$:**

$$v_z(\rho) = -\frac{\Delta P}{4 \mu L} \, \rho^{2} + C$$

**Step 5 — Apply the no-slip boundary condition $v_z(r) = 0$:**

$$0 = -\frac{\Delta P}{4 \mu L} \, r^{2} + C \quad \Rightarrow \quad C = \frac{\Delta P}{4 \mu L} \, r^{2}$$

**Step 6 — Parabolic velocity profile:**

$$v_z(\rho) = \frac{\Delta P}{4 \mu L} \, (r^{2} - \rho^{2})$$

**Step 7 — Volumetric flow rate through one pore:**

$$Q_{\text{single}} = \int_{0}^{r} v_z(\rho) \, (2 \pi \rho) \, d\rho = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2 \mu L} \int_{0}^{r} (r^{2} - \rho^{2}) \, \rho \, d\rho$$

$$= \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2 \mu L} \left[ \frac{r^{2} \rho^{2}}{2} - \frac{\rho^{4}}{4} \right]_{0}^{r} = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2 \mu L} \left( \frac{r^{4}}{2} - \frac{r^{4}}{4} \right)$$

$$Q_{\text{single}} = \frac{\pi \, r^{4} \, \Delta P}{8 \, \mu \, L}$$

**Step 8 — Convert to membrane flux.** If the membrane has $n$ pores per unit area, each contributing $Q_{\text{single}}$:

$$J = n \, Q_{\text{single}} = \frac{n \, \pi \, r^{4} \, \Delta P}{8 \, \mu \, L}$$

**Step 9 — Introduce surface porosity $\varepsilon = n \pi r^{2}$ and tortuosity $\tau$:**

$$J = \frac{\varepsilon \, r^{2} \, \Delta P}{8 \, \mu \, \tau \, L}$$

This is the **final form of the Pore Flow Model** used in KTU numerical problems.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Solution-Diffusion Flux from Fick's First Law

**Step 1 — Fick's first law for steady 1-D diffusion:**

$$J = -D \, \frac{dc}{dx}$$

For a homogeneous membrane, $D$ is taken as a constant (effective diffusion coefficient).

**Step 2 — Integrate across the membrane from $x = 0$ (upstream face) to $x = \delta$ (downstream face):**

$$\int_{0}^{\delta} J \, dx = -D \int_{c_1}^{c_2} dc$$

$$J \, \delta = -D \, (c_2 - c_1) = D \, (c_1 - c_2)$$

$$J = \frac{D \, (c_1 - c_2)}{\delta}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Henry's law** at each membrane face to relate concentration to external partial pressure:

$$c_1 = S \, p_1, \quad c_2 = S \, p_2$$

where $S$ is the solubility (or Henry) coefficient.

**Step 4 — Substitute:**

$$J = \frac{D \, S \, (p_1 - p_2)}{\delta} = \frac{P \, (p_1 - p_2)}{\delta}$$

with $P = D \cdot S$ the **permeability**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical interpretation for KTU:** The solution-diffusion model treats the membrane as a *dissolving film*, not as a sieve. Selectivity arises from a difference in $P$ values — which itself is a product of *how much* dissolves ($S$) and *how fast* it diffuses ($D$).

---

## 3.3 Worked Example (KTU-style Numerical)

> **Problem (PEPOT752, KTU Style):**
> A UF membrane is reported to have a surface porosity $\varepsilon = 0.70$, mean pore radius $r = 0.20 \,\mu\text{m}$, tortuosity $\tau = 1.4$, and thickness $L = 50 \,\mu\text{m}$. Water at $25^{\circ}\text{C}$ ($\mu = 8.9 \times 10^{-4}$ Pa·s) is filtered at a transmembrane pressure of $\Delta P = 1.5 \times 10^{5}$ Pa. Estimate the water flux using the Pore Flow Model. Give your answer in LMH (Litre/m²·h).

**Step 1 — Convert all lengths to SI units:**
- $r = 0.20 \,\mu\text{m} = 0.20 \times 10^{-6}$ m $= 2.0 \times 10^{-7}$ m
- $L = 50 \,\mu\text{m} = 50 \times 10^{-6}$ m $= 5.0 \times 10^{-5}$ m

**Step 2 — Apply the Pore Flow equation:**

$$J = \frac{\varepsilon \, r^{2} \, \Delta P}{8 \, \mu \, \tau \, L}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the values:**

$$J = \frac{0.70 \times (2.0 \times 10^{-7})^{2} \times 1.5 \times 10^{5}}{8 \times 8.9 \times 10^{-4} \times 1.4 \times 5.0 \times 10^{-5}}$$

**Step 4 — Numerator:**

$$0.70 \times 4.0 \times 10^{-14} \times 1.5 \times 10^{5} = 4.2 \times 10^{-9}$$

**Step 5 — Denominator:**

$$8 \times 8.9 \times 10^{-4} \times 1.4 \times 5.0 \times 10^{-5} = 4.984 \times 10^{-7}$$

**Step 6 — Flux in m/s:**

$$J = \frac{4.2 \times 10^{-9}}{4.984 \times 10^{-7}} = 8.43 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m/s}$$

**Step 7 — Convert to LMH:**

$$J = 8.43 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m/s} \times 1000 \; \text{L/m}^3 \times 3600 \; \text{s/h}$$

$$J = 8.43 \times 10^{-3} \times 3.6 \times 10^{6} \; \text{LMH} = 3.03 \times 10^{4} \; \text{LMH}$$

$$\boxed{\,J \approx 3.03 \times 10^{4} \text{ LMH}\,}$$

> [!TIP]
> This order of magnitude (~$3 \times 10^{4}$ LMH) is typical for clean-water UF. In practice, fouling and concentration polarisation reduce the real flux to 10–15 % of this value.

---

## 3.4 Side-by-Side Comparison Matrix (for KTU ESE answers)

| Feature | Pore Flow | Solution-Diffusion | S-D-Imperfection | Molecular Sieve |
|---------|-----------|---------------------|------------------|-----------------|
| Membrane type | Microporous | Dense (non-porous) | Dense with defects | Tight pores |
| Driving force | Hydraulic $\Delta P$ | Chemical potential | Both | Size / steric |
| Flux vs $\Delta P$ | Linear | Linear | Linear | Non-linear |
| Selectivity basis | Pore size vs molecule | $P_A/P_B$ | $P_A/P_B$ + viscous flow | Pore size distribution |
| Typical use | MF, UF | RO, GS, PV | Real RO | NF, zeolites |
| Assumption re: pores | Explicit capillaries | None | Few defects | Uniform narrow pores |
| Liquid or gas | Liquid (mainly) | Both | Both | Both |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Comparative Transport Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Feed Stream enters Membrane Module] --> B{Identify Membrane Type}
    B --> C[Porous Membrane MF UF]
    B --> D[Dense Membrane RO GS PV]
    B --> E[Defective Dense Membrane]
    B --> F[Nanoporous Membrane NF Zeolite]
    C --> C1[Pore Flow Model]
    C1 --> C2[Viscosity mu, Porosity epsilon]
    C1 --> C3[Tortuosity tau, Pore radius r]
    C2 --> C4[Flux J equals epsilon r squared Delta P over 8 mu tau L]
    C3 --> C4
    D --> D1[Solution Diffusion Model]
    D1 --> D2[Sorption at Upstream Face]
    D1 --> D3[Diffusion through Polymer]
    D1 --> D4[Desorption at Downstream Face]
    D2 --> D5[Flux J equals P times p1 minus p2 over delta]
    D3 --> D5
    D4 --> D5
    E --> E1[S D Imperfection Model]
    E1 --> E2[S D Component J_SD]
    E1 --> E3[Imperfection Component J_imp]
    E2 --> E4[Total Flux J equals P plus P1 times Delta p over delta]
    E3 --> E4
    F --> F1[Molecular Sieve Model]
    F1 --> F2[Steric Partitioning Phi equals 1 minus lambda squared]
    F1 --> F3[Hindered Diffusion Coefficient Kd]
    F2 --> F4[Rejection when lambda approaches 1]
    F3 --> F4
    C4 --> G[Permeate Stream]
    D5 --> G
    E4 --> G
    F4 --> G
    G --> H[Final Product and Reject Streams]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Solution-Diffusion Step-Wise Process

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Upstream Side High Pressure or Concentration] --> B1[Step 1 Sorption into Membrane Face]
    B1 --> C1[Step 2 Diffusion through Polymer Matrix driven by chemical potential gradient]
    C1 --> D1[Step 3 Desorption at Downstream Face]
    D1 --> E1[Permeate collected at Low Pressure Side]
    style A1 fill:#e0f0ff,stroke:#0066cc
    style E1 fill:#e0ffe0,stroke:#008800
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Membrane Module Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    feed1[Feed Inlet Pump] --> pre[Pre Treatment Cartridge and Skid]
    pre --> hpv[High Pressure Pump Variable Speed]
    hpv --> mod[Membrane Module Housing containing Skin Layer and Support]
    mod --> perm[Permeate Outlet low pressure]
    mod --> rej[Reject or Concentrate Outlet throttled by back pressure valve]
    perm --> tank1[Permeate Storage Tank]
    rej --> tank2[Reject Collection or Recycle Loop]
    mod --> sensor[Pressure Transmitter and Flow Sensor]
    sensor --> ctrl[PLC with Flux and Delta P Feedback]
    ctrl --> hpv
    style mod fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc8800
```

## 4.4 Schematic Comparison — Flux Driving Force in Each Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MODEL1[Pore Flow Model]
        P1[Pressure Delta P] --> J1[Flux J proportional to Delta P through fixed pores]
    end
    subgraph MODEL2[Solution Diffusion Model]
        P2[Chemical Potential Gradient Deltamu] --> J2[Flux J equals P times Delta p over delta]
    end
    subgraph MODEL3[Molecular Sieve Model]
        P3[Solute to Pore Size Ratio lambda] --> J3[Rejection controlled by lambda = dm over dp]
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023 Style]
> "State the **Pore Flow Model** assumptions and write down its flux equation."

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Assumptions (1½ marks):** Membrane contains parallel, uniform, cylindrical pores of radius $r$; pores are perpendicular to the surface; flow is laminar and viscous; fluid is Newtonian; no solute–membrane interaction; surface porosity $\varepsilon$ and tortuosity $\tau$ are constants.
- **Flux equation (1½ marks):**

$$J = \frac{\varepsilon \, r^{2} \, \Delta P}{8 \, \mu \, \tau \, L}$$

where $J$ is the volumetric flux, $\mu$ the fluid viscosity, $\Delta P$ the transmembrane pressure, $L$ the membrane thickness.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Style]
> "Differentiate between the **Solution-Diffusion Model** and the **Pore Flow Model** in three points."

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Pore Flow | Solution-Diffusion |
|--------|-----------|---------------------|
| Membrane type | Porous | Dense / non-porous |
| Mechanism | Viscous flow through pores | Dissolution + diffusion through polymer |
| Driving force | Transmembrane pressure | Chemical-potential gradient (often expressed as $\Delta c$ or $\Delta p$) |
| Selectivity origin | Size exclusion | Difference in $P = D \cdot S$ |

(Any three contrastive points = 3 marks.)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Question (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]

> **(a) (7 marks)** Starting from Fick's first law of diffusion, derive the flux equation for the **Solution-Diffusion Model** in a dense polymer membrane, and clearly define the **permeability** $P$ in terms of the diffusion and solubility coefficients.
>
> **(b) (7 marks)** A RO membrane with effective thickness $\delta = 0.20 \,\mu\text{m}$ and water permeability $P = 1.2 \times 10^{-9}$ mol·m/(m²·s·Pa) is used to treat a saline feed at upstream osmotic pressure $\pi_1 = 2.5 \times 10^{5}$ Pa. The applied pressure is $\Delta P = 5.0 \times 10^{5}$ Pa and downstream osmotic pressure is $\pi_2 = 5.0 \times 10^{4}$ Pa. Calculate the **net driving pressure** and the **water flux**. Express flux in LMH (1 mol water ≈ 18 g, $\rho_{\text{water}} = 1000$ kg/m³).

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Fick's first law for steady 1-D transport (1 mark):**

$$J = -D \, \frac{dc}{dx}$$

**Step 2 — Integrate across the membrane of thickness $\delta$ (2 marks):**

$$\int_{0}^{\delta} J \, dx = -D \int_{c_1}^{c_2} dc \;\Rightarrow\; J = \frac{D \, (c_1 - c_2)}{\delta}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Henry-type sorption at the two faces (2 marks):**

$$c_1 = S \, p_1, \qquad c_2 = S \, p_2$$

**Step 4 — Final flux equation and definition of $P$ (2 marks):**

$$J = \frac{D \, S \, (p_1 - p_2)}{\delta} = \frac{P \, (p_1 - p_2)}{\delta}, \quad \text{with} \quad P = D \cdot S$$

> $P$ has units mol·m/(m²·s·Pa). $D$ in m²/s, $S$ in mol/(m³·Pa).

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Effective driving force for RO is the *net* pressure (2 marks):**

$$\Delta P_{\text{net}} = \Delta P - (\pi_1 - \pi_2) = 5.0 \times 10^{5} - (2.5 \times 10^{5} - 5.0 \times 10^{4})$$

$$\Delta P_{\text{net}} = 5.0 \times 10^{5} - 2.0 \times 10^{5} = 3.0 \times 10^{5} \;\text{Pa}$$

**Step 2 — Flux in molar units using the S-D model (2 marks):**

$$J = \frac{P \, \Delta P_{\text{net}}}{\delta} = \frac{1.2 \times 10^{-9} \times 3.0 \times 10^{5}}{2.0 \times 10^{-7}}$$

$$J = \frac{3.6 \times 10^{-4}}{2.0 \times 10^{-7}} = 1.8 \times 10^{3} \;\text{mol/(m}^{2}\cdot\text{s)}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to volumetric flux (2 marks):**

Mass flux $= 1.8 \times 10^{3} \times 0.018 = 32.4$ kg/(m²·s)

$$J_{\text{vol}} = \frac{32.4}{1000} = 3.24 \times 10^{-2} \;\text{m/s}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to LMH (1 mark):**

$$J = 3.24 \times 10^{-2} \times 1000 \times 3600 = 1.166 \times 10^{5} \;\text{LMH}$$

$$\boxed{\,J \approx 1.17 \times 10^{5} \;\text{LMH}\,}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common valuation mistakes for this problem:**
> 1. Forgetting to subtract the osmotic-pressure difference — students often take $\Delta P = 5 \times 10^5$ Pa directly, which is wrong for RO; the **net** driving pressure is $(\Delta P - \Delta \pi)$.
> 2. Mixing $\mu$m and m in the membrane thickness; always convert to SI metres.
> 3. Converting mol/m²·s to LMH using the molar mass of water = 18 g/mol (not 1 g/mol).
> 4. Losing 1 mark for not stating Henry's law and the meaning of $S$.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]

> **(a) (7 marks)** With a neat sketch, explain the **Pore Flow Model** for transport through a porous membrane. Starting from the Hagen–Poiseuille equation for a single capillary, derive the expression for the volumetric flux through the membrane.
>
> **(b) (7 marks)** A UF membrane is reported to have surface porosity $\varepsilon = 0.65$, mean pore radius $r = 0.15 \,\mu\text{m}$, tortuosity $\tau = 1.3$, thickness $L = 60 \,\mu\text{m}$. The viscosity of the permeate is $1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s and the transmembrane pressure is $\Delta P = 2.0 \times 10^{5}$ Pa. Estimate the **volumetric water flux** $J$ in m³/(m²·s) and in **LMH**.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Sketch and concept (2 marks):**
Show a porous membrane with parallel cylindrical pores running from the high-pressure (feed) side to the low-pressure (permeate) side. Indicate $\Delta P$, $L$, $r$, $\varepsilon$ and $\tau$ on the sketch. (Schematic: feed flows in parallel capillaries — Hagen–Poiseuille flow.)

**Step 2 — Start from Hagen–Poiseuille for a single pore (2 marks):**

$$Q_{\text{single}} = \frac{\pi \, r^{4} \, \Delta P}{8 \, \mu \, L}$$

**Step 3 — Number of pores per unit area is $n$; total flux (1 mark):**

$$J = n \, Q_{\text{single}} = \frac{n \, \pi \, r^{4} \, \Delta P}{8 \, \mu \, L}$$

**Step 4 — Convert $n \pi r^{2}$ to surface porosity $\varepsilon$, and include tortuosity $\tau$ (2 marks):**

$$J = \frac{\varepsilon \, r^{2} \, \Delta P}{8 \, \mu \, \tau \, L}$$

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Convert to SI (1 mark):**
- $r = 1.5 \times 10^{-7}$ m
- $L = 6.0 \times 10^{-5}$ m
- $\Delta P = 2.0 \times 10^{5}$ Pa
- $\mu = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s

**Step 2 — Substitute into Pore Flow equation (3 marks):**

$$J = \frac{0.65 \times (1.5 \times 10^{-7})^{2} \times 2.0 \times 10^{5}}{8 \times 1.0 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.3 \times 6.0 \times 10^{-5}}$$

**Numerator:** $0.65 \times 2.25 \times 10^{-14} \times 2.0 \times 10^{5} = 2.925 \times 10^{-9}$

**Denominator:** $8 \times 1.0 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.3 \times 6.0 \times 10^{-5} = 6.24 \times 10^{-7}$

$$J = \frac{2.925 \times 10^{-9}}{6.24 \times 10^{-7}} = 4.69 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to LMH (1 mark):**

$$J = 4.69 \times 10^{-3} \times 3.6 \times 10^{6} = 1.69 \times 10^{4} \;\text{LMH}$$

$$\boxed{\,J \approx 4.7 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{m/s} \;\text{or}\; 1.69 \times 10^{4} \;\text{LMH}\,}$$

**Step 4 — Engineering interpretation (2 marks):**
The calculated flux is the **clean-water flux**. In real UF, fouling and concentration polarisation reduce this by a factor of 5–10. The plot of $J$ vs $\Delta P$ is a straight line through the origin, and the slope is used to compute the **mean effective pore radius** of the membrane.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common pitfalls (Question B):**
> 1. **Unit trap:** Mixing $\mu$m with m; $r^2$ blows up the error if units are not converted. Always convert first.
> 2. **Forgetting $\tau$:** Many students drop the tortuosity factor; the textbook answer always retains it.
> 3. **Wrong exponent:** Hagen–Poiseuille involves $r^{4}$ for a single pore but the *flux* formula collapses to $r^{2}$ because $n \pi r^{2} = \varepsilon$. Students who write $r^{4}$ in the final flux equation lose 2 marks.
> 4. **Skipping the sketch:** KTU awards 1–2 marks for the labelled diagram of the porous membrane.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Final 60-second Revision List — PEPOT752, Module 2, "Models for Membrane Separations":**

- **Pore Flow Model** → **Porous membrane** → **Hagen–Poiseuille** flow through parallel capillaries.
  - Flux: $J = \dfrac{\varepsilon \, r^{2} \, \Delta P}{8 \, \mu \, \tau \, L}$
  - **Flux is linear in $\Delta P$ and quadratic in $r$**.
  - No solute–membrane chemical interaction.
  - Used for **MF, UF**.

- **Solution-Diffusion Model** → **Dense (non-porous) membrane** → **3 steps**: sorption, diffusion, desorption.
  - Flux: $J = \dfrac{P \, (p_1 - p_2)}{\delta}$ with $P = D \cdot S$.
  - Selectivity = ratio of permeabilities, $\alpha_{A/B} = P_A / P_B$.
  - Used for **RO, GS, PV**.
  - **Driving force is the chemical potential gradient**, not just $\Delta P$.

- **Solution-Diffusion-Imperfection Model** → real membranes with defects.
  - Adds an imperfection-flow term: $J = \dfrac{(P + P_1)\Delta p}{\delta}$.
  - Explains why real RO membranes show small salt leakage.

- **Molecular Sieve (Size Exclusion) Model** → pores comparable to molecular size.
  - Steric partition: $\Phi = (1 - \lambda)^2$, $\lambda = d_m / d_p$.
  - Rejection sharp as $\lambda \to 1$; **MWCO** concept.
  - Used for **NF, zeolite membranes, tight UF**.

- **Permeability** $P = D \cdot S$ (mobility × sorption) is the **central** quantity of the S-D model.
- **Permeance** $Q = P / \delta$ is what experimentalists actually measure (permeability divided by membrane thickness).
- **Selectivity vs permeability** trade-off is bounded by the **Robeson upper bound** (upper bound for $\alpha$ at a given $P$).
- **Distinguishing test:** A linear $J$-vs-$\Delta P$ plot alone does **not** distinguish Pore Flow from Solution-Diffusion; both are linear. The distinction comes from the **selectivity mechanism** (size vs $P$-ratio) and the **temperature dependence** of flux.
- **Engineering takeaway:** Choosing a model is *not* academic — it dictates how the membrane is **designed, characterised, and operated** in practice. A wrong model leads to wrong scale-up and oversizing of pumps.
- **Common KTU pitfall:** Forgetting the **osmotic pressure correction** in RO flux problems ($\Delta P_{\text{net}} = \Delta P - \Delta \pi$).
- **Common KTU pitfall:** Forgetting to convert $\mu$m, nm, Å → **metres** before plugging into SI equations.
- **Exam heuristic:** "Porous → Pore Flow" and "Dense → Solution-Diffusion" — the 80/20 rule for viva questions on this topic.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT752 - POLYMERS AND SEPARATION PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Basic Principles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2 — BASIC PRINCIPLES OF SEPARATION PROCESSES

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Definition of a Separation Process

A **separation process** is a unit operation that converts a homogeneous or heterogeneous feed mixture into two or more distinct product streams, each differing in composition from the original feed. The driving force for separation is a difference in one or more physical or chemical properties (volatility, solubility, molecular size, charge, density, affinity) between the components of the mixture.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, separation processes are classified under **mass transfer operations** when the components redistribute between phases, and under **mechanical separations** when the phases are physically segregated.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (memorize verbatim):**
> "A separation process is a process that transforms a mixture of substances into two or more products that differ from each other in composition, exploiting differences in physical or chemical properties of the components."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **coffee filter separating coffee grounds from brewed coffee**. The liquid (coffee) passes through tiny pores while solid particles (grounds) are retained. This is separation based on **particle size**.

Similarly, a **raincoat separating water (outside) from your body (dry inside)** is separation based on **selective permeability to molecules**.

In chemical engineering, we design equipment that does exactly this — but at molecular scale — exploiting differences in boiling point, solubility, diffusivity, charge, or molecular size to pull mixtures apart.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Module 2 of PEPOT752 introduces the *fundamental principles* common to almost all separation processes — **equilibrium, mass transfer, and staging**. Every subsequent module (distillation, absorption, extraction, membrane) is built on these three pillars.

### 1.3 Why Separation Processes?

Almost no industrial chemical product is made in a single pure step. The outputs of reactors are mixtures that must be:
- **Purified** (e.g., removing water from ethanol)
- **Concentrated** (e.g., producing 98% sulfuric acid from 70%)
- **Recovered** (e.g., recycling unreacted monomer in polymerization)

The **3 governing physical constants / standard metrics** you must memorize:

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $R$ | Universal gas constant | **8.314 J/(mol·K)** |
| $T$ | Absolute temperature | **Kelvin (K)** |
| $g$ | Gravitational acceleration | **9.81 m/s²** |

### 1.4 General Classification of Separation Processes

```
SEPARATION PROCESSES
├── 1. Mechanical (Phase boundary)
│   ├── Sedimentation, Filtration, Centrifugation
├── 2. Diffusion-based (Mass transfer across phases)
│   ├── Equilibrium-based: Distillation, Absorption, Extraction, Adsorption
│   └── Rate-based: Membrane separation, Dialysis, Reverse Osmosis
├── 3. External field driven
│   ├── Electrodialysis, Electrophoresis, Magnetic separation
└── 4. Reactive / Affinity
    └── Ion exchange, Chromatography
```

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Separation

Every separation process rests on:

**Pillar 1 — Equilibrium Thermodynamics:** Defines the *maximum possible separation* achievable. Tells us *how far* we can go.

**Pillar 2 — Mass Transfer Kinetics:** Defines the *rate* at which equilibrium is approached. Tells us *how fast* we can go.

**Pillar 3 — Stage / Contacting Design:** Defines the *equipment geometry* (plates, packing, membranes) that brings the phases together efficiently.

### 2.2 Equilibrium Stage Concept

A **theoretical (or ideal) equilibrium stage** is a hypothetical device where two phases leaving the stage are in complete thermodynamic equilibrium with each other. No real device achieves this perfectly; the deviation is captured by the **Murphree efficiency** or **overall stage efficiency**.

For a binary system, the equilibrium relationship is expressed as:

$$y^* = f(x)$$

where $y^*$ is the mole fraction of the more volatile component in the **vapor phase** in equilibrium with liquid of composition $x$.

> [!NOTE]
> For an **ideal solution obeying Raoult's Law**, the equilibrium relation simplifies to:
>
> $$y^* = \dfrac{\alpha\,x}{1 + (\alpha - 1)x}$$
>
> where $\alpha$ is the **relative volatility** of the more volatile component.

### 2.3 The Relative Volatility (α) — Most Important Quantity in Distillation

$$\alpha_{AB} = \dfrac{K_A}{K_B} = \dfrac{(y_A/x_A)}{(y_B/x_B)} = \dfrac{P_A^{sat}}{P_B^{sat}}$$

- **α > 1**: Easy separation (e.g., benzene–toluene, α ≈ 2.5)
- **α ≈ 1**: Very difficult separation (e.g., ethylene–ethane, α ≈ 1.2) → needs many stages
- **α = 1**: Impossible by ordinary distillation

### 2.4 Mass Transfer — The Driving Force

Mass transfer occurs whenever a concentration gradient exists. The **flux** (rate of transfer per unit area) follows **Fick's First Law**:

$$N_A = -D_{AB}\,\dfrac{dC_A}{dz}$$

- $N_A$ = molar flux of A [kmol/(m²·s)]
- $D_{AB}$ = diffusivity of A in B [m²/s]
- $dC_A/dz$ = concentration gradient [kmol/m⁴]

The negative sign indicates mass flows from high to low concentration.

### 2.5 Two-Film Theory (Whitman's Theory)

Most engineering analyses use Whitman's **two-film model** (1923), where all resistance to mass transfer is concentrated in two thin, stagnant films on either side of the phase boundary.

For a species A transferring from gas to liquid:

$$N_A = K_G\,(y_A - y_A^*) = K_L\,(x_A^* - x_A)$$

where:
- $K_G$ = overall gas-phase mass transfer coefficient [kmol/(m²·s)]
- $K_L$ = overall liquid-phase mass transfer coefficient
- $y_A^*$ = gas composition in equilibrium with bulk liquid $x_A$
- $x_A^*$ = liquid composition in equilibrium with bulk gas $y_A$

### 2.6 Individual vs. Overall Coefficients

The overall coefficient relates to individual film coefficients through the equilibrium slope $m = dy^*/dx$:

$$\dfrac{1}{K_G} = \dfrac{1}{k_G} + \dfrac{m}{k_L}$$

$$\dfrac{1}{K_L} = \dfrac{1}{m\,k_G} + \dfrac{1}{k_L}$$

where $k_G$ and $k_L$ are individual film coefficients.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** If $m\,k_G \gg k_L$ (i.e., gas-phase resistance dominates, e.g., very soluble gas like NH₃ in water), then $K_L \approx k_L$. Conversely, for sparingly soluble gases like O₂ in water, $m\,k_G \ll k_L$, and $K_L \approx m\,k_G$.

### 2.7 Separation Factor (Selectivity)

Quantifies *how well* a process separates two species A and B:

$$S_{AB} = \dfrac{(C_A/C_B)_{product}}{(C_A/C_B)_{feed}}$$

- $S_{AB} = 1$: No separation
- $S_{AB} \to \infty$: Perfect separation
- For distillation, $S_{AB} = \alpha$

### 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use | KTU Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $y^* = \alpha x / [1 + (\alpha-1)x]$ | Vapor-liquid equilibrium (ideal) | ★★★★★ |
| 2 | $\alpha = P_A^{sat}/P_B^{sat}$ | Relative volatility | ★★★★★ |
| 3 | $N_A = -D_{AB}(dC_A/dz)$ | Fick's First Law (diffusion) | ★★★★★ |
| 4 | $1/K_G = 1/k_G + m/k_L$ | Two-film combination (gas basis) | ★★★★ |
| 5 | $1/K_L = 1/(m k_G) + 1/k_L$ | Two-film combination (liquid basis) | ★★★★ |
| 6 | $N_A = K_G(y - y^*)$ | Overall flux equation | ★★★★ |
| 7 | $S_{AB} = (C_A/C_B)_p / (C_A/C_B)_f$ | Selectivity / Separation factor | ★★★ |
| 8 | $E_M = (y_{n+1} - y_n) / (y_{n+1} - y_n^*)$ | Murphree vapor efficiency | ★★★ |
| 9 | $\text{HETP} = Z/N_{theor}$ | Height equivalent to a theoretical plate | ★★★ |
| 10 | $\eta = N_{theor}/N_{actual}$ | Overall stage (column) efficiency | ★★★ |

**Boundary conditions / limits:**
- $\alpha = 1$ → Separation impossible by simple distillation
- $k_G \to \infty$ → $K_G \to k_L/m$ (liquid controls)
- $k_L \to \infty$ → $K_G \to k_G$ (gas controls)

### 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Petroleum refining**: Crude oil is split into gasoline, diesel, kerosene in tall distillation columns using exactly the equilibrium-stage concept.
- **Water treatment**: Reverse osmosis membranes use selective permeability (analogous to the coffee filter analogy).
- **Pharmaceutical industry**: Chromatography exploits differential adsorption (a rate-based separation).
- **Polymer purification**: Solvent–nonsolvent precipitation uses equilibrium solubility differences — directly relevant to PEPOT752.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Fick's Law to the Overall Mass Transfer Equation

We start with the rigorous flux equation for species A through a stagnant film B:

### Step 1: Molar flux in a binary system (no net bulk flow)

$$\boxed{N_A = -\dfrac{c\,D_{AB}}{1-x_A}\,\dfrac{dx_A}{dz}}$$

where $c$ is total molar concentration [kmol/m³].

### Step 2: Integrate across the gas film of thickness $\delta_G$

We apply the boundary condition $x_A = x_{A,i}$ at $z = 0$ (interface) and $x_A = x_{A,b}$ at $z = \delta_G$ (bulk gas).

$$\int_{x_{A,i}}^{x_{A,b}}(1-x_A)\,dx_A = -\dfrac{N_A}{c\,D_{AB}}\int_{0}^{\delta_G}dz$$

$$\left[x_A - \dfrac{x_A^2}{2}\right]_{x_{A,i}}^{x_{A,b}} = -\dfrac{N_A\,\delta_G}{c\,D_{AB}}$$

### Step 3: Linearize for dilute systems ($x_A \ll 1$)

When $x_A$ is small, $1 - x_A \approx 1$, and:

$$N_A = \dfrac{c\,D_{AB}}{\delta_G}(x_{A,i} - x_{A,b}) = k_c(x_{A,i} - x_{A,b})$$

This defines the **individual mass transfer coefficient**:

$$k_c = \dfrac{D_{AB}}{\delta_G} \quad \text{[m/s]}$$

### Step 4: Define individual film coefficients on phase basis

- Gas film: $k_G = k_c / (RT)$ [kmol/(m²·s·Δy)]
- Liquid film: $k_L = k_c$ on mole fraction basis [kmol/(m²·s·Δx)]

### Step 5: Combine the two films

The flux through both films is the same (steady state, no accumulation at interface):

$$N_A = k_G(y - y_i) = k_L(x_i - x)$$

At the interface, the two compositions are in **thermodynamic equilibrium**:

$$y_i = m\,x_i \quad \text{(linearized equilibrium)}$$

### Step 6: Eliminate interface compositions

From $N_A = k_L(x_i - x)$:

$$x_i = \dfrac{N_A}{k_L} + x$$

Substituting into $y_i = m x_i$ and then into $N_A = k_G(y - y_i)$:

$$N_A = k_G\left(y - m\left(\dfrac{N_A}{k_L} + x\right)\right)$$

### Step 7: Rearrange to solve for $N_A$

$$N_A + \dfrac{m\,k_G}{k_L}\,N_A = k_G(y - m\,x)$$

$$N_A\left(1 + \dfrac{m\,k_G}{k_L}\right) = k_G(y - m\,x)$$

### Step 8: Define overall gas-phase coefficient $K_G$

$$\boxed{N_A = \dfrac{k_G}{1 + \dfrac{m\,k_G}{k_L}}\,(y - y^*) = K_G(y - y^*)}$$

where $y^* = m x$ is the gas composition in equilibrium with bulk liquid.

Inverting gives the famous combination formula:

$$\dfrac{1}{K_G} = \dfrac{1}{k_G} + \dfrac{m}{k_L} \quad \blacksquare$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Memorize the symmetry:** Adding resistances in series is the same as Ohm's law for electrical resistors. $K_G$ plays the role of overall conductance, $1/k_G$ and $m/k_L$ are individual resistances.

## 3.2 Derivation: Murphree Vapor Efficiency

For a real tray in a distillation column, vapor and liquid do NOT reach equilibrium. Murphree defined efficiency as:

$$E_{M,V} = \dfrac{y_n - y_{n+1}}{y_n^* - y_{n+1}}$$

- $y_{n+1}$: vapor entering tray $n$ from below
- $y_n$: vapor leaving tray $n$ above
- $y_n^*$: vapor that WOULD leave if tray were at equilibrium with $x_n$

**Derivation from a simple mass balance on the tray** (assuming constant molar overflow):

Moles of A entering in vapor = $V\,y_{n+1}$
Moles of A leaving in vapor = $V\,y_n$
Moles of A entering in liquid = $L\,x_{n-1}$
Moles of A leaving in liquid = $L\,x_n$

Steady-state balance: $V y_{n+1} + L x_{n-1} = V y_n + L x_n$

The CHANGE in vapor composition is:

$$y_n - y_{n+1} = \dfrac{L}{V}(x_{n-1} - x_n)$$

If equilibrium were achieved:

$$y_n^* - y_{n+1} = \dfrac{L}{V}(x_{n-1} - x_n^*)$$

Dividing:

$$\dfrac{y_n - y_{n+1}}{y_n^* - y_{n+1}} = \dfrac{x_n - x_{n+1}}{x_n^* - x_{n+1}} = E_{M,V} \quad \blacksquare$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** A binary mixture of benzene (B) and toluene (T) is to be separated at 1 atm. Benzene mole fraction in liquid is $x_B = 0.40$. Given $\alpha_{BT} = 2.47$, calculate the equilibrium vapor composition $y_B^*$.

**Solution (every step shown — KTU pattern):**

Given: $x = 0.40$, $\alpha = 2.47$

### Step 1: Apply the equilibrium relation

$$y^* = \dfrac{\alpha\,x}{1 + (\alpha - 1)x}$$

### Step 2: Substitute the values

$$y^* = \dfrac{2.47 \times 0.40}{1 + (2.47 - 1)(0.40)}$$

### Step 3: Calculate numerator

$$\text{Numerator} = 0.988$$

### Step 4: Calculate denominator

$$\text{Denominator} = 1 + (1.47)(0.40) = 1 + 0.588 = 1.588$$

### Step 5: Compute final answer

$$y^* = \dfrac{0.988}{1.588} = 0.6222$$

**Answer:** The vapor in equilibrium with liquid containing 40 mol% benzene contains **62.2 mol% benzene**. ✓

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
