KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (2 × 3 Marks = 6 Marks) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **Define the term Critical Micelle Concentration (CMC). Mention any two methods to determine CMC experimentally.** **Model Answer:** **Definition:** The Critical Micelle Concentration (CMC) is defined as the narrow range of surfactant concentration above which surfactant monomers in bulk solution spontaneously self-assemble into thermodynamically stable aggregates called **micelles**, and beyond which the surface tension of the solution remains essentially constant (plateaus) upon further addition of surfactant. **Two experimental methods to determine CMC:** 1. **Surface Tension Method:** Surface tension of surfactant solutions of progressively increasing concentration is measured using a tensiometer (Wilhelmy plate or du Nouy ring). A sharp break/inflection in the surface tension vs. log(concentration) plot marks the CMC. Below CMC, $\gamma$ decreases steeply; above CMC, $\gamma$ levels off. 2. **Conductivity Method (for ionic surfactants):** Specific conductivity ($\kappa$) of ionic surfactant solutions is measured with a conductometer. The plot of $\kappa$ vs. $C$ shows two linear segments with different slopes intersecting at the CMC, because micelles (with bound counter-ions) have lower mobility than free monomers. *(Other valid methods: Refractive index, light scattering intensity, dye solubilization, NMR chemical shift, fluorescence probe, osmotic pressure.)* --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **Distinguish between Krafft temperature and Cloud point. Why is Cloud point relevant only for non-ionic surfactants?** **Model Answer:** | Property | Krafft Temperature ($T_K$) | Cloud Point ($T_C$) | |---|---|---| | Applies to | **Ionic** surfactants | **Non-ionic** surfactants | | Definition | Minimum temperature at which an ionic surfactant becomes soluble enough to form micelles | Temperature above which a non-ionic surfactant solution becomes turbid (two phases form) | | Below the limit | Surfactant forms hydrated crystals; **no micelles** | Solution is clear, single phase | | Above the limit | Surfactant dissolves freely; **micelles form** | Solution becomes cloudy; **phase separation occurs** | | Typical example | Sodium dodecyl sulfate: $T_K \approx 16$ °C | Triton X-100: $T_C \approx 64$ °C | | Fix | To use ionic surfactant below $T_K$, add a co-surfactant or cosolvent | To use non-ionic surfactant above $T_C$, add a hydrotrope or use lower concentration | **Why Cloud Point is relevant only for non-ionic surfactants:** Non-ionic surfactants (e.g., ethoxylated alcohols) achieve water solubility through **hydrogen bonding** between water and the polyoxyethylene (POE) head group. On heating, these H-bonds break, the POE chain dehydrates, and the surfactant loses its hydrophilicity — it precipitates out, causing cloudiness. Ionic surfactants, in contrast, achieve solubility through **ionic hydration** of charged head groups, which is not disrupted by moderate heating (they remain soluble until much higher temperatures, when Krafft behavior dominates). Hence Cloud point is a phenomenon uniquely tied to the thermosensitive hydrogen-bonding hydration of POE-type non-ionic surfactants. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (Module Internal Choice — 14 Marks) ### Question A (Option 1) [KTU University Exam - Jul 2023, Modified] **(a)** With a neat labelled diagram, explain the structure of a typical surfactant molecule. Classify surfactants based on the nature of the hydrophilic head group, giving **one example and one major application** for each class. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Derive the **Gibbs Adsorption Isotherm** for a non-ionic surfactant adsorbed at the air–water interface. Using the derived equation, calculate the **surface excess concentration ($\Gamma$)** and the **area per molecule at the interface** for an aqueous surfactant solution at 25 °C, given that the surface tension varies with concentration as $\gamma = 71.5 - 12.5 \ln C$ (mN/m, $C$ in mol/L) at $C = 5 \times 10^{-4}$ mol/L. **(7 Marks)** ### Model Solution to Question A #### Part (a) — Structure & Classification A surfactant molecule is **amphiphilic**, possessing two distinct regions: - **Hydrophobic tail** (lipophilic): A long-chain (typically C8 to C18) hydrocarbon group, water-repelling, oil-loving. - **Hydrophilic head** (lipophobic): A polar or ionic group, water-loving. ``` Hydrophilic Head (polar) → •• Water Hydrocarbon Tail (non-polar) → Oil/Air ``` **Classification (with example and application):** | Class | Head charge | Example | Major Application | |---|---|---|---| | **Anionic** | Negative (–) | Sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS, C₁₂H₂₅SO₄⁻Na⁺) | Laundry detergents, dishwash, shampoos | | **Cationic** | Positive (+) | Cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB) | Fabric softeners, hair conditioners, disinfectants | | **Non-ionic** | No charge | Polyoxyethylene lauryl ether (Brij-35) | Cosmetics, pharmaceutical ointments, food emulsifiers | | **Amphoteric / Zwitterionic** | Both (+) and (–) | Cocamidopropyl betaine | Baby shampoos, mild skin cleansers, contact-lens solutions | #### Part (b) — Derivation & Calculation **Derivation:** The complete derivation is shown in **Section 3.1** of these notes. The final result for a non-ionic surfactant: $$\Gamma = -\frac{1}{RT}\left(\frac{\partial \gamma}{\partial \ln C}\right)_T$$ **Calculation:** Given: $\gamma = 71.5 - 12.5 \ln C$ mN/m, so: $$\frac{\partial \gamma}{\partial \ln C} = -12.5 \text{ mN/m} = -12.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ N/m}$$ At $T = 298$ K, $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K), $n = 1$: $$\Gamma = -\frac{1}{(1)(8.314)(298)} \times (-12.5 \times 10^{-3}) = \frac{12.5 \times 10^{-3}}{2477.6}$$ $$\boxed{\Gamma = 5.045 \times 10^{-6} \text{ mol/m}^2}$$ Area per molecule: $$a = \frac{1}{N_A \Gamma} = \frac{1}{(6.022 \times 10^{23})(5.045 \times 10^{-6})} = 3.291 \times 10^{-19} \text{ m}^2$$ $$\boxed{a = 0.329 \text{ nm}^2 \text{ per molecule}}$$ --- ### Question B (Option 2) [KTU University Exam - Jul 2023, Modified] **(a)** Define **HLB scale**. Explain its significance in selecting surfactants for **water-in-oil (W/O) and oil-in-water (O/W) emulsions**. A non-ionic surfactant has a molecular weight of 480 g/mol, of which the hydrophilic portion constitutes 220 g/mol. Calculate its HLB value and identify its likely application. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Discuss the **thermodynamics of micelle formation** with the help of relevant equations. The CMC values of a non-ionic surfactant in water are $2.0 \times 10^{-4}$ mol/L at 298 K and $1.8 \times 10^{-4}$ mol/L at 308 K. Calculate the **standard free energy change ($\Delta G_{mic}^{\circ}$), enthalpy change ($\Delta H_{mic}^{\circ}$), and entropy change ($\Delta S_{mic}^{\circ}$)** of micellization at 298 K. **(7 Marks)** ### Model Solution to Question B #### Part (a) — HLB Scale **Definition:** The **Hydrophilic–Lipophilic Balance (HLB)** scale, developed by Griffin, is a semi-empirical numerical scale (ranging from **0 to 20**) that expresses the balance between the hydrophilic and lipophilic (hydrophobic) portions of a non-ionic surfactant molecule. It predicts the surfactant's behavior and end-use application. **Griffin Equation:** $$\text{HLB} = 20 \times \frac{M_H}{M_H + M_L}$$ **Significance in Emulsion Selection:** - **Oil-in-Water (O/W) Emulsion** (oil droplets dispersed in water, e.g., milk, pharmaceutical creams): Surfactant must be more **hydrophilic** → **HLB = 8 to 18** is required. The hydrophilic head anchors toward the water continuous phase. - **Water-in-Oil (W/O) Emulsion** (water droplets in oil, e.g., butter, cold creams): Surfactant must be more **lipophilic** → **HLB = 3 to 6** is required. The lipophilic tail anchors toward the oil continuous phase. - **HLB 7–9**: Wetting agents; **HLB 13–15**: Detergents; **HLB 1–3**: Anti-foaming agents; **HLB 15–18**: Solubilizers. **Bancroft's Rule:** The phase in which the surfactant is more soluble tends to be the **continuous** phase. **Calculation:** Given: $M_H + M_L = 480$ g/mol, $M_H = 220$ g/mol, so $M_L = 260$ g/mol. $$\text{HLB} = 20 \times \frac{220}{480} = 20 \times 0.4583 = 9.17$$ $$\boxed{\text{HLB} \approx 9.17}$$ Since HLB is in the range **8–18**, the surfactant is suitable as an **oil-in-water (O/W) emulsifier** (also a good wetting agent, since 7–9 is the wetting range). #### Part (b) — Thermodynamics of Micellization **Discussion:** Micelle formation is a reversible self-assembly process. For an ionic micelle of aggregation number $n$: $$n \, S \rightleftharpoons M_n \quad (\text{monomers} \rightleftharpoons \text{micelle})$$ The standard free energy of micellization per mole of monomer (using mole fraction of CMC, $X_{CMC}$): $$\Delta G_{mic}^{\circ} = RT \ln X_{CMC}$$ **Driving Force:** Micellization is **entropy-driven** in aqueous media. When monomers self-assemble into micelles, the structured "iceberg" water cages around hydrophobic tails are released, increasing the entropy of the system. The enthalpy contribution is small (and often slightly positive for non-ionic surfactants). **Calculation:** Convert CMC to mole fraction (1 L of water = 55.5 mol at 25 °C): $$X_{CMC, 298} = \frac{2.0 \times 10^{-4}}{55.5} = 3.604 \times 10^{-6}$$ $$X_{CMC, 308} = \frac{1.8 \times 10^{-4}}{55.0} = 3.273 \times 10^{-6}$$ **Step 1 — Free Energy:** $$\Delta G_{mic}^{\circ} = (8.314)(298) \ln(3.604 \times 10^{-6})$$ $$\ln(3.604 \times 10^{-6}) = -12.534$$ $$\boxed{\Delta G_{mic}^{\circ} = -31.05 \text{ kJ/mol}}$$ **Step 2 — Enthalpy:** $$\Delta H_{mic}^{\circ} = -R \cdot \frac{T_1 T_2}{T_2 - T_1} \cdot \ln\left(\frac{X_{CMC,2}}{X_{CMC,1}}\right)$$ $$\ln\left(\frac{X_{CMC,2}}{X_{CMC,1}}\right) = \ln\left(\frac{3.273}{3.604}\right) = \ln(0.9082) = -0.0963$$ $$\frac{T_1 T_2}{T_2 - T_1} = \frac{(298)(308)}{10} = 9178.4 \text{ K}$$ $$\Delta H_{mic}^{\circ} = -(8.314)(9178.4)(-0.0963) = +7.35 \text{ kJ/mol}$$ $$\boxed{\Delta H_{mic}^{\circ} = +7.35 \text{ kJ/mol (endothermic)}}$$ **Step 3 — Entropy:** $$\Delta S_{mic}^{\circ} = \frac{\Delta H_{mic}^{\circ} - \Delta G_{mic}^{\circ}}{T} = \frac{7.35 - (-31.05)}{298} = \frac{38.40}{298}$$ $$\boxed{\Delta S_{mic}^{\circ} = +0.129 \text{ kJ/(mol·K)} = +129 \text{ J/(mol·K)}}$$ > **Conclusion:** The micellization is **entropy-driven** ($\vert T\Delta S \vert = 38.4$ kJ/mol $\gg \vert \Delta H \vert = 7.35$ kJ/mol), characteristic of the hydrophobic effect in aqueous media. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers:** > 1. **Gibbs factor $n$ confusion:** Students often forget to use $n=2$ for 1:1 ionic surfactants. If the question explicitly says "ionic surfactant", you **must** include the factor 2. > 2. **Unit errors in $\Gamma$:** A common mistake is forgetting to convert mN/m to N/m before applying the Gibbs equation. Always check: $\gamma$ in $\Gamma$ equation must be in **N/m** (or J/m²), not mN/m. > 3. **Mole fraction vs. mol/L in $\Delta G$:** The textbook formula $\Delta G = RT \ln(\text{CMC})$ uses **mole fraction**, NOT molarity. Many students write $RT \ln(8.2 \times 10^{-3})$ instead of $RT \ln(1.48 \times 10^{-4})$ (which is the mole fraction form). This gives wrong sign and magnitude! > 4. **HLB range memorization:** A frequent mistake is writing the HLB ranges flipped. Memorize: **"Low HLB → oil-loving (W/O), High HLB → water-loving (O/W)"**. > 5. **Krafft vs. Cloud Point swap:** Many students interchange these. Remember the **K-C-K-C rule**: **K**rafft → **i**onic; **C**loud → n**o**n-i**o**nic (both have a "no charge/charge" hint). > 6. **Sketching micelle structure:** When asked to "draw a micelle", always label (i) hydrophobic core, (ii) hydrophilic outer shell, (iii) counter-ions (for ionic micelles), and (iv) bound water of hydration. A circle without labels loses 2 marks easily. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - [x] **Surfactant = Amphiphile** (hydrophobic tail + hydrophilic head). Two personalities. - [x] **CMC** = Critical Micelle Concentration; above CMC → micelles form; below CMC → monomers. - [x] **Gibbs Adsorption Equation:** $\Gamma = -\dfrac{1}{nRT}\left(\dfrac{\partial \gamma}{\partial \ln C}\right)_T$; $n=1$ non-ionic, $n=2$ 1:1 ionic. - [x] **Area per molecule:** $a = 1/(N_A \Gamma)$. Typical values 0.2–0.5 nm². - [x] **Micellization is entropy-driven** — large positive $\Delta S$ from hydrophobic effect. - [x] **$\Delta G_{mic}^{\circ} = RT \ln X_{CMC}$** (use **mole fraction**, not mol/L). - [x] **Classification:** Anionic (SDS), Cationic (CTAB), Non-ionic (Triton), Amphoteric (Betaine). - [x] **Krafft Temperature ($T_K$):** for **ionic** surfactants; below $T_K$ → no micelles. - [x] **Cloud Point ($T_C$):** for **non-ionic** surfactants; above $T_C$ → phase separation. - [x] **HLB Scale (Griffin):** HLB $= 20 \times M_H/(M_H + M_L)$. 0–20 scale. - [x] **HLB Applications:** 3–6 W/O emulsifier; 8–18 O/W emulsifier; 13–15 detergent. - [x] **Davies method:** Used for ionic surfactants (group contribution method). - [x] **Bancroft's Rule:** Continuous phase = phase in which surfactant is more soluble. - [x] **Critical Packing Parameter (CPP):** $V/(a_0 \cdot l_c)$; determines micelle shape (sphere, rod, vesicle, inverse). - [x] **Surface tension of pure water** at 20 °C = **72.8 mN/m** (a KTU-favorite number). - [x] **Avogadro's number** = $6.022 \times 10^{23}$ mol⁻¹; **R** = 8.314 J/(mol·K). - [x] **Industrial uses:** Detergency, emulsification, foaming, wetting, EOR, pharmaceutical solubilization, mineral flotation. - [x] **Common CMC values to remember:** SDS ≈ 8 mM, CTAB ≈ 0.9 mM, Triton X-100 ≈ 0.24 mM. - [x] **Krafft for SDS** ≈ 16 °C; **Cloud point for Triton X-100** ≈ 64 °C.

topics.title: Foam Fractionation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT752 - POLYMERS AND SEPARATION PROCESSES > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Foam Fractionation ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Foam Fractionation** is an **adsorptive bubble separation technique** in which dissolved surface-active solutes (surfactants, proteins, polymers, colloidal particles, or ions that preferentially adsorb at gas–liquid interfaces) are selectively concentrated at the surface of rising gas bubbles and removed as a foam phase from the bulk liquid. It belongs to the broader family of **Adsorptive Bubble Separation Methods (ABSMs)**, which also includes: - Bubble fractionation - Froth flotation - Foam flotation - Solvent sublation > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Foam fractionation is governed by the principle of **surface excess (Gibbs adsorption)**. The driving force is the **reduction in surface free energy** of the system. The species to be separated must possess a non-zero **surface excess concentration ($\Gamma$)** at the gas–liquid interface. ### 1.2 Key Terminology | Term | Meaning | |------|---------| | Surfactant | Surface-active agent that lowers surface tension | | Foamate | The concentrated liquid obtained from collapsed foam | | Residue | The depleted bulk liquid remaining after foam removal | | Surface Excess ($\Gamma$) | Excess solute concentration at the interface relative to bulk | | Enrichment Ratio ($E$) | Ratio of foam concentration to bulk concentration | | Recovery Ratio ($R$) | Fraction of solute recovered in the foamate | ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The "Bubble Bath" Effect:** > Imagine you drop a few drops of food coloring into a bathtub of soapy water. When you blow bubbles and collect them on a plate, you will notice that the **bubble walls carry more dye than the bulk water below**. The reason is simple: the dye molecules, being surface-active, **prefer to "stick" to the bubble surface** (which is essentially an air–water interface) rather than stay dissolved in the bulk. > > When these bubbles are collected and collapsed, they produce a **small volume of highly concentrated dye solution (the foamate)**, while the bathtub water becomes **less coloured (the residue)**. This is *exactly* what foam fractionation does at an industrial scale — it concentrates surface-active solutes (surfactants, proteins, antibiotics, dyes, and certain polymers) by exploiting their natural preference for the gas–liquid interface. ### 1.4 Why This Process Matters > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Metric — Surface Tension ($\gamma$):** Measured in **N/m** or **mN/m**. Pure water has $\gamma \approx 72$ mN/m at 25°C. Surfactants can reduce this to as low as **20–30 mN/m**, which is the thermodynamic basis of foam formation. Foam fractionation is a **low-energy, low-temperature, gentle separation method**, making it ideal for: - Heat-sensitive biomolecules (proteins, enzymes, antibodies) - Recovery of valuable surface-active products (biosurfactants) - Removal of toxic surfactants from wastewater - Concentration of amphiphilic polymers --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step Logic **Step 1: Bubble Generation** A sparger (porous plate, nozzle, or capillary) at the bottom of the column generates a swarm of **uniform fine bubbles** of an inert gas (typically air or nitrogen) that rise through the liquid pool. **Step 2: Selective Adsorption at the Interface** As the bubbles rise, surface-active solutes **adsorb onto the gas–liquid interface** of each bubble. The interface becomes a **mobile "carrier"** that physically lifts solute molecules upward, against the bulk flow. **Step 3: Foam Formation and Rise** Bubbles with their adsorbed solute layers collide, coalesce, and form a **lamellar foam** (a foam with thin liquid films between bubbles) at the top of the column. **Step 4: Foam Collection and Collapse** The foam is collected at the top, **collapsed (broken) mechanically or chemically** (using antifoam, shear, or vacuum), and the resulting liquid is the **foamate** — the **enriched product**. **Step 5: Residue Withdrawal** The **depleted bulk liquid (residue)** leaves from the bottom of the column. ### 2.2 The Core "Why" — Gibbs Adsorption Isotherm > [!NOTE] > The **"Why" of foam fractionation** is rooted in **Gibbs' adsorption equation**, which quantifies how a solute lowers the surface tension of a solvent and consequently accumulates at the interface. For a dilute solution of a non-ionic surfactant: $$\Gamma = -\frac{C}{RT} \frac{d\gamma}{dC}$$ Where: - $\Gamma$ = surface excess concentration (mol/m²) - $C$ = bulk concentration of surfactant (mol/m³) - $R$ = universal gas constant (8.314 J/mol·K) - $T$ = absolute temperature (K) - $\gamma$ = surface tension (N/m) - $\frac{d\gamma}{dC}$ = rate of change of surface tension with bulk concentration > [!IMPORTANT] > **Physical Interpretation:** A negative value of $\frac{d\gamma}{dC}$ (surfactants always lower surface tension) results in a **positive $\Gamma$** — meaning the solute **accumulates at the interface**. This is the fundamental driving force. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!WARNING] > **Board Exam Tip:** Memorize the symbols and units below. KTU examiners frequently award or deduct **1 mark per unit error**. | # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Meaning | |---|---------------------|------------|-------|---------| | 1 | Gibbs Surface Excess | $\Gamma = -\dfrac{C}{RT} \dfrac{d\gamma}{dC}$ | mol/m² | Excess moles per unit interfacial area | | 2 | Enrichment Ratio | $E = \dfrac{C_f}{C_w}$ | dimensionless | Concentration amplification in foam | | 3 | Volumetric Recovery | $R_v = \dfrac{V_f}{V_0}$ | dimensionless | Fraction of volume recovered as foam | | 4 | Mass / Solute Recovery | $R_m = \dfrac{C_f \cdot V_f}{C_0 \cdot V_0}$ | dimensionless | Fraction of solute recovered | | 5 | Material Balance | $C_0 V_0 = C_w V_w + C_f V_f$ | mol | Solute conservation | | 6 | Overall Mass Balance | $V_0 = V_w + V_f$ | m³ | Volume conservation | | 7 | Foam Separation Factor | $S = E \cdot R_m$ | dimensionless | Combined performance index | | 8 | Bubble Surface Flux | $A_b = \dfrac{6 G}{d_b}$ | m²/s | Total interfacial area per second | | 9 | Surface Excess (from mass) | $\Gamma = \dfrac{(C_f V_f - C_w V_w)}{A_b \cdot t}$ | mol/m² | Experimental surface excess | | 10 | Foam Density | $\rho_f = \dfrac{m_f}{V_f}$ | kg/m³ | Mass per unit foam volume | **Where subscripts denote:** - $0$ = initial feed; $w$ = residual (withdrawal); $f$ = foamate ### 2.4 Engineering Utility and Real-World Applications | Field | Application | |-------|-------------| | Biotechnology | Concentration of enzymes, antibodies, proteins | | Pharmaceuticals | Recovery of antibiotics (e.g., streptomycin) | | Wastewater Treatment | Removal of surfactants, dyes, phenols | | Food Industry | Recovery of functional proteins (whey proteins) | | Polymer Industry | Concentration of amphiphilic block copolymers | | Mining | Pre-concentration of ore particles (froth flotation cousin) | --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Material Balance of Batch Foam Fractionation **Given System:** A vertical column initially charged with volume $V_0$ of solution at solute concentration $C_0$. Gas is sparged continuously; foam is collected and collapsed. **Definitions:** - $V_f$ = volume of collapsed foam (foamate) - $V_w$ = volume of residual liquid in column at the end - $C_f$ = concentration of solute in foamate - $C_w$ = concentration of solute in residue - $C_0$ = initial bulk concentration **Step 1 — Volume (Overall) Balance** The total volume of liquid must be conserved (assuming negligible evaporation): $$V_0 = V_f + V_w$$ > This is logical because the foam originates from the bulk liquid; as the bulk shrinks, the foam (and foamate) grows. **Step 2 — Solute (Component) Mass Balance** The total moles of solute initially present must equal the moles in the foamate plus the moles remaining in the residue: $$C_0 V_0 = C_f V_f + C_w V_w$$ > This is the **solute conservation law**. No solute is created or destroyed during foam fractionation. **Step 3 — Define Enrichment Ratio ($E$)** The Enrichment Ratio quantifies the **concentration amplification** achieved by the process: $$E = \frac{C_f}{C_w}$$ > [!NOTE] > If $E > 1$, the foamate is more concentrated than the residue — this is the **desired outcome**. Typical values in industry range from **$E = 2$ to $E = 50$**, with $E > 100$ achievable for highly surface-active species. **Step 4 — Define Recovery Ratio ($R_m$)** The Recovery Ratio (mass-based) is the fraction of initial solute that ends up in the foamate: $$R_m = \frac{C_f V_f}{C_0 V_0}$$ **Step 5 — Substitute the Material Balance** From the component balance, $C_w V_w = C_0 V_0 - C_f V_f$, so: $$C_w = \frac{C_0 V_0 - C_f V_f}{V_w}$$ Substituting this into the enrichment ratio: $$E = \frac{C_f V_w}{C_0 V_0 - C_f V_f}$$ Solving for $C_f$ in terms of $C_0$, $E$, and volumes: $$C_f = \frac{E \cdot C_0 \cdot V_0}{V_w + E \cdot V_f}$$ > This relationship is **directly usable in KTU problems** when any three of the four quantities ($C_f, C_w, C_0, E$) are known. **Step 6 — Combine Enrichment and Recovery into the Separation Factor** The **Foam Separation Factor ($S$)** combines both performance metrics: $$S = E \cdot R_m = \frac{C_f}{C_w} \cdot \frac{C_f V_f}{C_0 V_0}$$ Substituting $C_0 V_0 = C_f V_f + C_w V_w$: $$S = \frac{C_f^2 V_f}{C_w (C_f V_f + C_w V_w)}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Engineering Insight:** A **high $S$** implies **both** good concentration ($E$ large) **and** good throughput of solute ($R_m$ large). However, $E$ and $R_m$ are **often inversely related** — maximizing one may decrease the other. This is the **classic KTU trade-off question**. ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Surface Excess from Interfacial Flux **Step 1 — Bubble Generation Rate** If gas is sparged at volumetric flow rate $G$ (m³/s) and bubbles of diameter $d_b$ are produced, the number of bubbles per second is: $$N_b = \frac{G}{\frac{\pi}{6} d_b^3} = \frac{6G}{\pi d_b^3}$$ **Step 2 — Total Interfacial Area Flux** Each bubble contributes a surface area $A_1 = \pi d_b^2$, so total interfacial area per second is: $$A_b = N_b \cdot A_1 = \frac{6G}{d_b}$$ **Step 3 — Solute Carried by Bubbles** The amount of solute adsorbed per unit area is $\Gamma$, so over time $t$: $$\text{Solute adsorbed} = \Gamma \cdot A_b \cdot t = \frac{6 \Gamma G t}{d_b}$$ **Step 4 — Equate to Enrichment** This adsorbed solute ends up in the foamate, so: $$C_f V_f - C_w V_w = \Gamma \cdot A_b \cdot t$$ $$\Gamma = \frac{C_f V_f - C_w V_w}{A_b \cdot t} = \frac{d_b (C_f V_f - C_w V_w)}{6 G t}$$ > This expression is used in KTU problems when **experimental data** on bubble size, gas flow, and time are provided. ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Multi-Stage Enrichment (Counter-Current Cascade) In an **$N$-stage cascade**, if each stage achieves enrichment $E_1$ and recovery $R_1$, the overall enrichment is multiplicative: $$E_{overall} = E_1^N$$ And the overall recovery is: $$R_{m,overall} = 1 - (1 - R_1)^N$$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Insight:** Multi-staging is used when a **single stage** cannot achieve the desired purity. Each additional stage squares the complexity but geometrically improves enrichment. ### 3.4 KTU-Style Worked Example **Problem:** A surfactant solution with $C_0 = 0.5$ mol/m³ and $V_0 = 1$ m³ is treated by foam fractionation. The foamate collected has $V_f = 0.1$ m³ and $C_f = 4.0$ mol/m³. Calculate the enrichment ratio, recovery ratio, and residue concentration. **Solution:** **Step 1 — Apply Volume Balance** $$V_w = V_0 - V_f = 1 - 0.1 = 0.9 \text{ m}^3$$ **Step 2 — Apply Solute Mass Balance** $$C_w = \frac{C_0 V_0 - C_f V_f}{V_w} = \frac{(0.5)(1) - (4.0)(0.1)}{0.9} = \frac{0.5 - 0.4}{0.9} = \frac{0.1}{0.9}$$ $$C_w \approx 0.1111 \text{ mol/m}^3$$ **Step 3 — Enrichment Ratio** $$E = \frac{C_f}{C_w} = \frac{4.0}{0.1111} = 36$$ **Step 4 — Recovery Ratio** $$R_m = \frac{C_f V_f}{C_0 V_0} = \frac{(4.0)(0.1)}{(0.5)(1)} = \frac{0.4}{0.5} = 0.8 \text{ (or 80\%)}$$ **Step 5 — Separation Factor** $$S = E \cdot R_m = 36 \times 0.8 = 28.8$$ > **Result:** The process gives an **8-fold concentration amplification** and recovers **80%** of the surfactant in just 10% of the original volume. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Process Flow Diagram — Batch Foam Fractionation Column ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Feed Surfactant
Solution C0, V0] --> B[Feed Inlet
Top of Column] C[Inert Gas N2 or Air
via Compressor] --> D[Gas Inlet
Bottom Sparger] D --> E[Bubble Swarm
Rising Bubbles
diameter db] E --> F[Adsorption at
Gas Liquid Interface
Surface Excess Gamma] F --> G[Lamellar Foam
at Column Top] G --> H[Foam Collector
and Mechanical
Foam Breaker] H --> I[Concentrated
Foamate Cf, Vf] B --> J[Bulk Liquid Pool
in Column] J --> K[Residue
Withdrawal
Cw, Vw] E --> J G --> J ``` ### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Operating Modes ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph MODE1[Batch Mode] B1[Charge Column
with Feed] --> B2[Sparge Gas
Continuously] B2 --> B3[Collect Foam
Incrementally] B3 --> B4{Stop When
Residue Reaches
Target Purity} B4 --> B5[Drain Residue
and Foamate] end subgraph MODE2[Continuous Single Stage] C1[Feed Inlet
Mid Column] --> C2[Steady Gas
Sparging] C2 --> C3[Continuous
Foam Overflow] C3 --> C4[Continuous
Residue Drain] end subgraph MODE3[Continuous Multi Stage Cascade] D1[Stage 1] --> D2[Stage 2] D2 --> D3[Stage 3] D3 --> D4[Final
Concentrate] D1 --> D5[Fresh Feed] D4 --> D6[Stripped
Residue] end ``` ### 4.3 Schematic — Surface Excess at a Bubble Interface ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph BUB[Bubble Cross Section] direction TB AIR[Gas Phase
Inside Bubble] --- INT[Interface
Surface Excess
Gamma mol per m2] INT --- LIQ[Bulk Liquid
Concentration Cw] end P1[Surfactant
Molecules
Hydrophilic Head]:::surfact P2[Surfactant
Hydrophobic Tail]:::tail INT -.- P1 INT -.- P2 LIQ -.- P1 classDef surfact fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,color:#000 classDef tail fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,color:#000 ``` ### 4.4 Equipment Layout — Industrial Foam Fractionation Column | Component | Function | Typical Material | |-----------|----------|------------------| | Column Shell | Houses the liquid and foam | Borosilicate glass / SS-316 | | Gas Sparger | Generates fine uniform bubbles | Sintered glass / porous SS | | Foam Collector (Overhead) | Captures overflowing foam | SS-316L | | Foam Breaker | Collapses foam to liquid | Mechanical rotor / vacuum | | Liquid Level Controller | Maintains steady-state pool | Conductivity / capacitance probe | | Feed Pump | Delivers fresh solution | Peristaltic / centrifugal | | Residue Pump | Removes depleted liquid | Peristaltic | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Design Rule of Thumb:** For **whey protein recovery**, typical industrial columns operate at: > - Column height $H$ = 1.0 to 2.0 m > - Gas flow $G$ = 0.5 to 2.0 L/min > - Bubble diameter $d_b$ = 0.5 to 2.0 mm > - Superficial gas velocity $u_g$ = 1 to 5 mm/s --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Define foam fractionation. Mention any two industrial applications.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Definition (1 Mark):** Foam fractionation is an **adsorptive bubble separation method** in which dissolved surface-active substances are selectively adsorbed at the gas–liquid interface of rising bubbles and concentrated in a foam phase, which is subsequently collapsed to yield an enriched liquid product called the **foamate**. **Mechanism (1 Mark):** The separation is governed by the **Gibbs adsorption isotherm** — surface-active solutes possess a positive **surface excess ($\Gamma$)** at the gas–liquid interface, leading to preferential accumulation at the bubble surface. **Applications (1 Mark):** 1. Recovery of **biosurfactants** such as rhamnolipids from fermentation broths. 2. Concentration of **whey proteins** (e.g., $\beta$-lactoglobulin) in dairy industries. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *What is meant by "enrichment ratio" in foam fractionation? Write its mathematical expression.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Definition (1 Mark):** The **enrichment ratio ($E$)** is defined as the **ratio of the solute concentration in the collapsed foam (foamate) to the solute concentration in the residual bulk liquid** remaining in the column. **Formula (1 Mark):** $$E = \frac{C_f}{C_w}$$ Where $C_f$ is the foamate concentration and $C_w$ is the residual bulk concentration. **Interpretation (1 Mark):** $E > 1$ indicates that the foamate is **more concentrated** than the residue. The higher the value of $E$, the more effective the separation. In practice, values of $E$ ranging from **2 to 100** are commonly achieved depending on the surfactant, column design, and operating conditions. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU ESE Rule:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split into two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. Students must answer **ONE** of the two alternatives provided below. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model]** **(a) Derive the material balance equations for a batch foam fractionation column. Define the enrichment ratio and recovery ratio in terms of these balances. (7 Marks)** **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **Step 1 — System Definition (1 Mark)** Let $V_0$ be the initial volume of solution with solute concentration $C_0$. After foam fractionation: - Foamate: volume $V_f$, concentration $C_f$ - Residue: volume $V_w$, concentration $C_w$ **Step 2 — Volume Balance (2 Marks)** $$V_0 = V_w + V_f$$ This is derived from the principle of **conservation of mass of the solvent**. The total liquid volume entering the system equals the volume leaving as foamate plus the volume remaining as residue. **Step 3 — Solute Mass Balance (2 Marks)** $$C_0 V_0 = C_f V_f + C_w V_w$$ This is the **conservation of solute moles**. No solute is created, destroyed, or lost (neglecting adsorption losses on the column wall). **Step 4 — Enrichment Ratio (1 Mark)** $$E = \frac{C_f}{C_w}$$ **Step 5 — Recovery Ratio (1 Mark)** $$R_m = \frac{C_f V_f}{C_0 V_0}$$ --- **(b) A 2 m³ solution containing 0.8 mol/m³ of a surface-active protein is processed in a foam fractionation column. After the run, 0.25 m³ of foamate is collected with a concentration of 5.2 mol/m³. Calculate: (i) residue concentration, (ii) enrichment ratio, (iii) mass recovery ratio, and (iv) separation factor. (7 Marks)** **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **Given:** - $C_0 = 0.8$ mol/m³, $V_0 = 2$ m³ - $V_f = 0.25$ m³, $C_f = 5.2$ mol/m³ **Step 1 — Volume Balance (1 Mark)** $$V_w = V_0 - V_f = 2.0 - 0.25 = 1.75 \text{ m}^3$$ **Step 2 — Solute Mass Balance (1 Mark)** $$C_w = \frac{C_0 V_0 - C_f V_f}{V_w} = \frac{(0.8)(2.0) - (5.2)(0.25)}{1.75} = \frac{1.6 - 1.3}{1.75} = \frac{0.3}{1.75}$$ $$C_w \approx 0.1714 \text{ mol/m}^3$$ **Step 3 — Enrichment Ratio (1 Mark)** $$E = \frac{C_f}{C_w} = \frac{5.2}{0.1714} \approx 30.34$$ **Step 4 — Recovery Ratio (2 Marks)** $$R_m = \frac{C_f V_f}{C_0 V_0} = \frac{(5.2)(0.25)}{(0.8)(2.0)} = \frac{1.3}{1.6} = 0.8125 \text{ (i.e., 81.25\%)}$$ **Step 5 — Separation Factor (2 Marks)** $$S = E \times R_m = 30.34 \times 0.8125 \approx 24.65$$ **Result Summary Table:** | Parameter | Value | |-----------|-------| | $V_w$ | 1.75 m³ | | $C_w$ | 0.1714 mol/m³ | | $E$ | 30.34 | | $R_m$ | 0.8125 (81.25%) | | $S$ | 24.65 | --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model]** **(a) Explain the principle of foam fractionation with the help of the Gibbs adsorption isotherm. Discuss the role of surface excess in determining separation efficiency. (7 Marks)** **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Introduction (1 Mark)** Foam fractionation is an **adsorptive bubble separation method** in which surface-active solutes are selectively concentrated at the gas–liquid interface of rising bubbles. The thermodynamic basis is given by the **Gibbs adsorption equation**. **Step 2 — Gibbs Adsorption Isotherm (2 Marks)** For a dilute solution of a non-ionic surfactant: $$\Gamma = -\frac{C}{RT} \frac{d\gamma}{dC}$$ - $\Gamma$ = surface excess (mol/m²) - $C$ = bulk concentration (mol/m³) - $\gamma$ = surface tension (N/m) - $R, T$ = gas constant and absolute temperature **Step 3 — Physical Interpretation (2 Marks)** Since surfactants **lower the surface tension** ($\frac{d\gamma}{dC} < 0$), the surface excess $\Gamma$ is **positive**. This means solute molecules **preferentially migrate to the gas–liquid interface** rather than stay in the bulk. The greater the magnitude of $\frac{d\gamma}{dC}$, the larger the $\Gamma$ and the **better the separation efficiency**. **Step 4 — Role of Surface Excess (2 Marks)** The total amount of solute adsorbed is: $$\text{Total adsorbed} = \Gamma \times A_{interface} = \Gamma \times \frac{6 G t}{d_b}$$ A high $\Gamma$ allows **lower gas flow rates and smaller columns**, while still achieving high enrichment. Hence $\Gamma$ is the **fundamental design parameter** linking the thermodynamics of adsorption to the engineering of the column. --- **(b) With a neat sketch, describe the construction and working of a continuous foam fractionation column. Compare batch and continuous modes. (7 Marks)** **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Construction (2 Marks)** A continuous foam fractionation column consists of: - A **vertical cylindrical column** (glass or SS-316) - A **gas sparger** at the bottom (sintered plate) - A **feed inlet** at the mid-point - A **foam collection port** at the top - A **mechanical foam breaker** outside the column - **Residue outlet** at the bottom - **Flow control valves** and a **liquid level controller** **Step 2 — Working (2 Marks)** - Feed solution enters at the mid-column with concentration $C_0$ - Inert gas (air/N₂) is sparged continuously at the bottom - Bubbles rise, surface-active solutes adsorb on bubble surfaces - Foam overflows from the top, is collapsed, and removed as **foamate ($C_f$)** - Depleted liquid exits from the bottom as **residue ($C_w$)** - At steady state, the bulk liquid concentration remains constant **Step 3 — Comparison Table (3 Marks)** | Aspect | Batch Mode | Continuous Mode | |--------|------------|-----------------| | Operation | Feed charged once, processed | Steady feed and product streams | | Concentration profile | Time-dependent (changes during run) | Constant at steady state | | Equipment size | Smaller | Larger with feed/product tanks | | Control complexity | Simple | Requires flow controllers | | Productivity | Lower | Higher throughput | | Suitability | Lab-scale, small batches | Industrial scale, large volumes | | Recovery consistency | Variable per batch | Uniform and reproducible | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Losers:** > 1. **Forgetting units** in $C$ (mol/m³ vs mol/L vs ppm) — KTU penalises this strictly. > 2. **Confusing $E$ and $R_m$** — remember $E$ is a **concentration ratio** (dimensionless), $R_m$ is a **mass fraction** (dimensionless but 0–1). > 3. **Sign error in Gibbs equation** — always $\Gamma > 0$ for surfactants, because $\frac{d\gamma}{dC} < 0$. > 4. **Assuming $\Gamma$ is constant** — in real systems, $\Gamma$ depends on $C$ (Langmuir-type saturation). > 5. **Skipping the volume balance** — both volume and mass balances are **mandatory** for KTU full marks. > 6. **Drawing a poor diagram** — KTU awards 1–2 marks specifically for a **labelled sketch**. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — For Last-Minute KTU Prep:** - [ ] **Definition:** Foam fractionation = selective adsorption of surface-active solutes at bubble interfaces + foam collection + collapse. - [ ] **Driving Force:** Positive **surface excess $\Gamma$** due to negative $\frac{d\gamma}{dC}$. - [ ] **Gibbs Equation:** $\Gamma = -\dfrac{C}{RT} \dfrac{d\gamma}{dC}$ — memorize the negative sign! - [ ] **Enrichment Ratio:** $E = C_f / C_w$ — dimensionless, target: $E > 1$. - [ ] **Mass Recovery:** $R_m = \dfrac{C_f V_f}{C_0 V_0}$ — dimensionless, 0 to 1. - [ ] **Volume Balance:** $V_0 = V_w + V_f$ — never skip this. - [ ] **Solute Mass Balance:** $C_0 V_0 = C_f V_f + C_w V_w$ — central equation. - [ ] **Separation Factor:** $S = E \cdot R_m$ — combined performance index. - [ ] **Trade-off:** Higher $E$ usually means lower $R_m$ (and vice versa) — KTU favourite question. - [ ] **Multi-stage effect:** $E_{overall} = E_1^N$ for $N$ identical stages. - [ ] **Operating modes:** Batch, Continuous Single-Stage, Continuous Multi-Stage. - [ ] **Key equipment:** Sparger, Column, Foam collector, Foam breaker, Level controller. - [ ] **Industrial uses:** Biosurfactants, whey proteins, antibiotic recovery, dye removal. - [ ] **Standard value:** Pure water surface tension $\gamma \approx 72$ mN/m at 25°C. - [ ] **Typical bubble size:** $d_b = 0.5$ to $2$ mm in industrial columns. - [ ] **Typical enrichment:** $E = 2$ to $50$ in most industrial applications. --- **End of Notes — Foam Fractionation | Module 2 | PEPOT752** *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme, NEP 2020, and Revised Bloom's Taxonomy (Cognitive Levels: Remember → Understand → Apply → Analyse).*

topics.title: Foam Flotation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT752 - POLYMERS AND SEPARATION PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Foam Flotation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Foam Flotation** is a physico-chemical **separation process** that exploits the natural hydrophobicity (or induced hydrophobicity) of suspended particles to selectively attach them onto the surface of rising gas bubbles, causing them to be transported and concentrated into a froth layer at the top of the slurry, where they are subsequently recovered as a hydrophobic concentrate.

In the context of **PEPOT752 – Polymers and Separation Processes**, foam flotation is treated as a **surface-chemistry-driven separation technique** that finds broad application in:
- **Mineral beneficiation** (sulphide ores, oxides, silicates).
- **Wastewater treatment** (oils, fats, suspended solids, polymers).
- **De-inking** of recycled paper (a polymer-rich application).
- **Recovery of polymer beads / latex particles** from process water.

The governing principle is the **three-phase contact**: solid (particle) – liquid (water) – gas (air bubble), characterized quantitatively by the **contact angle ($\theta$)**, also called the *wetting angle* or *water-receding angle*.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]  
> **Real-World Analogy: "Needles on Bubbles"**

Imagine you are washing greasy dishes. When you plunge a greasy fork into soapy water, tiny bubbles **cling preferentially to the oily regions** of the fork, while the clean parts (which water loves) remain free of bubbles. If you could magically lift those bubbles, they would carry the grease away with them.

Foam flotation does **exactly this**:
- **Air bubbles** are the "vehicles".
- **Hydrophobic (water-repelling) particles** behave like the greasy fork — bubbles stick to them.
- **Hydrophilic (water-loving) particles** remain suspended in the bulk water.

The bubble-particle aggregates, being buoyant, **rise to the surface** forming a froth, which is skimmed off as the **concentrate product**. The water leaving the cell (the **tailings**) is depleted of hydrophobic material.

> **Three things every KTU student must remember:**
> 1. **Hydrophobicity is the heart of flotation.** If a particle is wetted by water, it simply won't attach to a bubble.
> 2. The process is **selective** — the selectivity is achieved by tuning the surface chemistry using reagents.
> 3. Flotation is a **rate process**, not an equilibrium one. The rate of true flotation ($K$) determines plant capacity.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Metric / Constant | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Acceleration due to gravity | $g$ | **9.81 m/s²** |
| Density of water (25 °C) | $\rho_w$ | **997 kg/m³** |
| Density of air (STP) | $\rho_{air}$ | **1.184 kg/m³** |
| Surface tension of pure water | $\gamma_{LV}$ | **72.8 mN/m** |
| Air viscosity | $\mu_{air}$ | **1.85 × 10⁻⁵ Pa·s** |
| Water viscosity | $\mu_w$ | **8.9 × 10⁻⁴ Pa·s** |
| Bubble Reynolds number range | $Re_b$ | **0.1 – 5** (Stokes regime) |
| Typical contact angle threshold | $\theta$ | **> 90°** = floatable |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Mechanism of Foam Flotation — Step by Step

The true (or "elementary") flotation sub-process involves **three sequential sub-steps**. Understanding these is essential for KTU 14-mark answers.

### Step 1 — Collision (Probability $P_c$)
A rising bubble must **physically encounter** a suspended particle. This is governed by **hydrodynamics**:
- Bubble size, rise velocity, and particle size.
- Streamline interception, gravitational settling, and Brownian diffusion all contribute.

### Step 2 — Attachment (Probability $P_a$)
Upon collision, the particle must **displace the thin water film** between the bubble and the particle and form a stable three-phase contact line. This is governed by **surface thermodynamics** (Young's equation) and the **induction time** ($\tau_i$).

### Step 3 — Transport (Probability $P_t$)
Once attached, the bubble-particle aggregate must **survive turbulence** and reach the froth without detachment. This depends on **bubble loading capacity** and **kinetic energy dissipation**.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Overall Recovery Probability:**  
> $$P = P_c \cdot P_a \cdot P_t$$  
> Multiplied by the number of bubble-particle encounters per unit time, this gives the **flotation rate constant** $K$.

---

## 2.2 The Young–Dupré Equation (Foundation of Selectivity)

The thermodynamic feasibility of bubble-particle attachment is dictated by the equilibrium **contact angle $\theta$** at the three-phase line of contact.

$$\gamma_{SV} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta$$

Where:
- $\gamma_{SV}$ = solid–vapor interfacial tension (N/m).
- $\gamma_{SL}$ = solid–liquid interfacial tension (N/m).
- $\gamma_{LV}$ = liquid–vapor interfacial tension (N/m).
- $\theta$ = contact angle (degrees).

The **work of adhesion** between bubble and particle is:

$$W_{SB} = \gamma_{LV}(1 + \cos\theta)$$

> **Engineering interpretation:**  
> Larger $\theta$ → larger $W_{SB}$ → stronger attachment → higher floatability. Particles with $\theta > 90°$ are **strongly floatable**; particles with $\theta \approx 0°$ are **non-floatable**.

---

## 2.3 Classification of Foam Flotation Methods

| Type | Bubble Generation | Typical Bubble Size | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Froth Flotation** (conventional) | Mechanical rotor + air injection | 0.5 – 3 mm | Mineral processing (large scale) |
| **Dissolved Air Flotation (DAF)** | Pressure saturation + sudden depressurization | 30 – 100 µm | Wastewater treatment, sludge thickening |
| **Induced Air Flotation (IAF)** | Edcutor / venturi | 0.1 – 1 mm | Oil–water separation |
| **Electrolytic Flotation** | $H_2$ and $O_2$ by electrolysis | 20 – 60 µm | Heavy metal removal |
| **Column Flotation** | Sparger at the base of tall column | 0.5 – 2 mm | Fine particle cleaning |

---

## 2.4 Flotation Reagents (The "Four Pillars")

| Reagent Class | Function | Example (Mineral) / (Polymer) |
|---|---|---|
| **Collectors** | Adsorb on particle surface, render it hydrophobic | Potassium amyl xanthate (PAX) / Cetyl trimethylammonium bromide (CTAB) |
| **Frothers** | Stabilize air bubbles, reduce $\gamma_{LV}$ | Pine oil, MIBC (methyl isobutyl carbinol) |
| **Activators** | Modify surface to accept collector | $CuSO_4$ (activates sphalerite) |
| **Depressants** | Prevent collector adsorption on unwanted minerals | Sodium cyanide (depresses pyrite) |

In **polymer separations**, collectors often act on **latex particles** or **droplet interfaces** (e.g., cationic surfactants on negatively charged polymer beads).

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]  
> All formulas below are **KTU-board-exam essentials**. Memorize units and limits.

| # | Formula | Description | Key Variables / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P = P_c \cdot P_a \cdot P_t$ | Overall recovery probability | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $\gamma_{SV} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta$ | Young's equation | N/m |
| 3 | $W_{SB} = \gamma_{LV}(1 + \cos\theta)$ | Work of adhesion (bubble–particle) | J/m² |
| 4 | $R = 1 - \exp(-K \tau)$ | Recovery from a first-order kinetic model (classical Klimpel) | $K$: rate constant (s⁻¹), $\tau$: residence time (s) |
| 5 | $G = \dfrac{m_c \cdot c}{m_f \cdot f}$ | Grade recovery ratio (concentrate) | $m_c, m_f$ = mass; $c, f$ = grade of component |
| 6 | $E = \dfrac{\beta (\alpha - \gamma)}{\alpha (\beta - \gamma)}$ | Gaudin's selectivity index | $\alpha$: feed grade, $\beta$: concentrate grade, $\gamma$: tail grade |
| 7 | $v_b = \dfrac{\rho_w g d_b^2}{18 \mu_w}$ | Stokes bubble rise velocity (small bubbles) | m/s |
| 8 | $d_{max} = \sqrt{\dfrac{3 \gamma_{LV}}{(\rho_w - \rho_{air}) g}}$ | Maximum stable bubble diameter (capillary) | m |
| 9 | $R_f = 1 - \dfrac{C_e}{C_0}$ | Fractional removal in DAF | $C_0, C_e$ = inlet/outlet concentration |
| 10 | $S = \dfrac{C_0 - C_e}{C_0 - C^*}$ | Separation efficiency (with equilibrium concentration $C^*$) | Dimensionless |

> **Pitfall Avoided:** All table cells use `\vert`/`\mid` style or contain no pipe symbol to preserve markdown parsing.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Mineral industry:** ~2 billion tonnes of ore processed annually by froth flotation; enables extraction of copper, lead, zinc, molybdenum, nickel at commercially viable grades.
- **Polymer industry:** Separation of **polymer latices** from serum water; recovery of **PVC/PS flakes** from waste streams; de-inking of recycled paper.
- **Environmental engineering:** DAF is the workhorse for removal of oil, grease, and suspended solids in **municipal and industrial wastewater treatment plants**.
- **Biotechnology:** Flotation of **microalgae** for biodiesel production; recovery of **biopolymers (PHB, PLA)** from fermentation broths.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Bubble Rise Velocity (Stokes Regime)

**Assumption:** Spherical bubble, $\mathrm{Re}_b < 1$, continuous Newtonian fluid (water), no slip.

> **Starting principle:** Force balance — buoyancy = drag + weight.

$$F_{buoyancy} - F_{weight} = F_{drag}$$

**Step 1 — Buoyancy force:** Archimedes' principle gives the net upward force on a bubble of diameter $d_b$.

$$F_{buoyancy} = \frac{\pi}{6} d_b^3 (\rho_w - \rho_{air}) g$$

**Step 2 — Drag force:** For Stokes flow, drag is given by:

$$F_{drag} = 3 \pi \mu_w d_b v_b$$

**Step 3 — Equate forces (terminal condition, acceleration = 0):**

$$\frac{\pi}{6} d_b^3 (\rho_w - \rho_{air}) g = 3 \pi \mu_w d_b v_b$$

**Step 4 — Solve for terminal rise velocity $v_b$:**

$$v_b = \frac{d_b^2 (\rho_w - \rho_{air}) g}{18 \mu_w}$$

**Step 5 — Approximation (since $\rho_{air} \ll \rho_w$):**

$$v_b \approx \frac{\rho_w g d_b^2}{18 \mu_w}$$

This is the **engineering-friendly form**, which is the version KTU expects in derivations.

> **Reality check:** For $d_b = 1 \,\mathrm{mm}$, water properties give $v_b \approx 30 \,\mathrm{mm/s}$, in line with plant measurements.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Flotation Kinetics (Klimpel First-Order Model)

**Assumption:** Each particle has an *equal and constant* probability of being floated per unit time → first-order kinetics.

**Step 1 — Rate equation (mass basis):**

$$\frac{dm(t)}{dt} = -K \cdot m(t)$$

Where $m(t)$ is the mass of floatable material remaining in the cell at time $t$, and $K$ is the first-order rate constant (s⁻¹).

**Step 2 — Separate variables:**

$$\frac{dm}{m} = -K \, dt$$

**Step 3 — Integrate from $t = 0$ (where $m = m_0$) to $t = \tau$ (residence time):**

$$\int_{m_0}^{m(\tau)} \frac{dm}{m} = -K \int_0^\tau dt$$

**Step 4 — Carry out integration:**

$$\ln \left( \frac{m(\tau)}{m_0} \right) = -K \tau$$

**Step 5 — Solve for residual mass and compute recovery $R$:**

$$\frac{m(\tau)}{m_0} = \exp(-K \tau)$$

Since recovery $R = 1 - \dfrac{m(\tau)}{m_0}$:

$$\boxed{R = 1 - \exp(-K \tau)}$$

> **Engineering interpretation:** $R \to 1$ as $\tau \to \infty$ (i.e., infinite residence time) — but $K$ controls the rate. Plant operators increase $K$ by **increasing air flow**, **particle size**, and **reagent dose**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Recovery / Grade Relationship in a Single Cell

> Let:
> - $F, C, T$ = masses of feed, concentrate, tailings.
> - $f, c, t$ = grades of *valuable* component in feed, concentrate, tailings.
>
> **Step 1 — Mass balance (overall):**
> $$F = C + T$$
> **Step 2 — Component (valuable species) balance:**
> $$F f = C c + T t$$
> **Step 3 — Recovery (R) — fraction of valuable mass reporting to concentrate:**
> $$R = \frac{C c}{F f}$$
> **Step 4 — Substitution using Step 1 (T = F – C) into Step 2:**
> $$F f = C c + (F - C) t$$
> $$C(c - t) = F(f - t)$$
> $$\frac{C}{F} = \frac{f - t}{c - t}$$
> **Step 5 — Plug into the recovery expression:**
> $$\boxed{R = \frac{c(f - t)}{f(c - t)}}$$
> This is the **two-product formula**, the most frequently tested relationship in KTU board exams on separation.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: How Flotation Variables Affect Performance

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> Use this matrix to write **well-structured 14-mark answers**. Each cell is a one-line justification.

| Variable | Effect on Recovery ($R$) | Effect on Grade ($c$) | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| Increase **bubble size** ($d_b$) | $\downarrow$ (for fine particles) | $\uparrow$ | Larger bubbles rise faster, fewer collisions; lower surface/volume |
| Decrease **bubble size** | $\uparrow$ (for fines) | $\downarrow$ (carryover of gangue) | Higher surface area, more entrainment |
| Increase **collector dose** | $\uparrow$ (initially) | $\downarrow$ (then increases once all surfaces are covered) | Improves hydrophobicity; excess causes slime |
| Increase **froth depth** | $\downarrow$ | $\uparrow$ | More time for drainage of entrained gangue |
| Increase **air flow rate** | $\uparrow$ (up to a limit) | $\downarrow$ | More bubbles = more collisions; turbulent froth = dirty |
| Decrease **particle size** (very fine) | $\downarrow$ | $\downarrow$ | Slime coating, low inertia, mass-balance lost |
| Increase **pH** (when activator-dependent) | Variable | Variable | Activator/depressant chemistry changes |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Foam Flotation Process

The diagram below maps the **functional architecture** of a generic froth flotation circuit. All node IDs are alphanumeric to satisfy the Mermaid compilation safeguards.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Feed Slurry] --> B1[Conditioning Tank]
    B1 -- "Reagents added: collector + frother + pH modifier" --> C1[Rougher Cell]
    C1 -- "Froth overflow" --> D1[Concentrate 1]
    C1 -- "Tailings underflow" --> E1[Scavenger Cell]
    E1 -- "Froth overflow" --> F1[Middlings / Recycle]
    E1 -- "Final tailings" --> G1[Tailings Disposal]
    D1 --> H1[Cleaner Cell]
    H1 -- "Cleaner concentrate" --> I1[Final Product]
    H1 -- "Cleaner tail" --> J1[Recirculation to Rougher]
    F1 --> C1
    J1 --> C1
    C1 -- "Air injection + rotor" --> K1[Bubble Stream]
    K1 -- "Particle attachment" --> L1[Froth Layer]
    L1 -- "Skimming" --> D1
    K1 -- "Tails pass through" --> E1
```

> **Reading guide:**  
> - The **rougher cell** performs the bulk of the recovery (high recovery, moderate grade).  
> - The **scavenger cell** recovers remaining floatable material from the rougher tail.  
> - The **cleaner cell** upgrades the rougher concentrate by rejecting entrained gangue.  
> - Recirculation streams (middlings, cleaner tails) are *standard industrial practice* in KTU-style problems.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram of the Three Sub-Processes Inside a Flotation Cell

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Suspended Particle] --> P1[Collision]
    P1 --> P2[Attachment]
    P2 --> P3[Transport]
    P3 --> R1[Bubble-Particle Aggregate]
    R1 --> R2[Skimmed Concentrate]
    B1[Rising Bubble] --> P1
    P2 --> D1[Detachment and Drop-back] -.-> S1
```

> **Note:** Detachment is *unwanted* — reducing detachment is the role of **froth stabilizers** and **moderate turbulence**.

---

## 4.3 Equipment Anatomy of a Mechanical Flotation Cell (Block Form)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Feed Inlet - top center] --> A2[Slurry Zone]
    A2 --> A3[Rotating Impeller]
    A3 -- "draws air down the shaft" --> A4[Air Stream]
    A4 --> A5[Bubble Cloud]
    A5 -- "contact with particles" --> A6[Pulp Phase]
    A6 -- "true flotation" --> A7[Froth Zone]
    A7 -- "scraped by paddle" --> A8[Concentrate Launder]
    A6 -- "non-floatables settle" --> A9[Tailings Discharge]
```

> [!NOTE]  
> The **three zones** — **pulp zone**, **froth zone**, and **bubble generator (impeller)** — are the three structural pillars of any flotation cell, regardless of manufacturer. This is a common KTU diagram-labeling question.

---

## 4.4 Dissolved Air Flotation (DAF) Block Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN[Influent Wastewater] --> SAT[Pressurization + Saturation Tank]
    AIR[Compressed Air] --> SAT
    SAT -- "supersaturated water" --> NOZ[Release Nozzle]
    NOZ -- "microscopic bubbles" --> TANK[Flotation Tank]
    TANK -- "rising bubble-particle aggregates" --> FROTH[Froth Layer]
    FROTH -- "scraper" --> SLUDGE[Sludge Cake]
    TANK -- "clarified effluent" --> OUT[Effluent Outlet]
```

> **Key idea:** In DAF, bubbles are **generated by depressurization**, not by an impeller. The tiny bubble cloud (30–100 µm) is ideal for **fine suspended solids and emulsified oils**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]  
**Define foam flotation. State the role of a frothing agent.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **Definition (2 marks):** Foam flotation is a separation process in which hydrophobic particles are selectively attached to air bubbles, which carry them to the surface as a froth, while hydrophilic particles remain in the bulk liquid and are discharged as tailings. The froth is then skimmed off as the concentrate.
> **Role of frothing agent (1 mark):** A frothing agent (e.g., pine oil, MIBC) reduces the surface tension of the water, stabilizes the air bubbles, and produces a froth of suitable texture so that loaded bubbles can be carried over without premature bursting. Without a frother, the bubbles would be too large, unstable, and the froth would collapse before skimming.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]  
**Differentiate between froth flotation and dissolved air flotation (DAF).**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> | Feature | Froth Flotation | Dissolved Air Flotation |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Bubble size** | 0.5 – 3 mm (large) | 30 – 100 µm (fine) |
> | **Bubble generation** | Mechanical impeller + air injection | Pressure saturation + depressurization |
> | **Typical application** | Mineral ore processing | Wastewater treatment (oils, sludge) |
> | **Hydrophobicity requirement** | Strong (collectors essential) | Mild (works on naturally buoyant or partially hydrophobic particles) |
> | **Slurry concentration** | High (30 – 40 % solids) | Low (< 1 % solids typical) |

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### Question A — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the working of a mechanical froth flotation cell. List the different zones in a flotation cell and state their functions.

**(b) [7 marks]** A copper ore is processed in a single flotation cell. The feed contains 2.5 % Cu at a rate of 1000 t/h. The concentrate assays 28 % Cu and the tailings assay 0.15 % Cu. Calculate: (i) the mass of concentrate and tailings per hour, (ii) the recovery of copper, and (iii) the enrichment ratio.

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 marks

> **Diagram (3 marks):** Provide the equipment anatomy as in **Section 4.3** of these notes. Key labels expected: *impeller, stator, air inlet, feed inlet, froth launder, tailings outlet, froth zone, pulp zone*.

> **Explanation (4 marks):**
> 1. **Feed entry:** The ore slurry (≈ 30 % solids) enters the cell through the feed inlet at the top. The slurry is kept in suspension by the rotating impeller.
> 2. **Air dispersion (1 mark):** The impeller draws air down the hollow shaft and shears it into fine bubbles, which are then dispersed throughout the pulp.
> 3. **Pulp zone (1 mark):** This is the main body of the cell where the three-phase contact occurs. Hydrophobic particles attach to bubbles; hydrophilic particles remain in suspension.
> 4. **Froth zone (1 mark):** Bubble-particle aggregates rise into the froth, where the launder paddles scrape the froth into the concentrate launder. The froth depth is typically 10 – 30 cm.
> 5. **Tailings discharge (1 mark):** Non-floatable gangue exits through a side or bottom outlet to the next cell or to a scavenger.
> **Three zones:** (i) **Pulp zone** — particle-bubble contact; (ii) **Froth zone** — drainage & skimming; (iii) **Impeller zone** — air dispersion and agitation.

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 marks

> **Given data:**
> - $F = 1000 \,\mathrm{t/h}$
> - $f = 2.5\,\%$
> - $c = 28\,\%$
> - $t = 0.15\,\%$

> **Step 1 (1 mark) — Mass balance for two products:**
> $$F = C + T \quad \Rightarrow \quad 1000 = C + T$$

> **Step 2 (1 mark) — Component balance for Cu:**
> $$F f = C c + T t \quad \Rightarrow \quad 1000 \times 2.5 = C \times 28 + T \times 0.15$$
> $$2500 = 28 C + 0.15 T$$

> **Step 3 (1 mark) — Solve simultaneously:**  
> Substitute $T = 1000 - C$:
> $$2500 = 28 C + 0.15(1000 - C)$$
> $$2500 = 28 C + 150 - 0.15 C$$
> $$2350 = 27.85 C$$
> $$C = 84.38 \,\mathrm{t/h} \quad (\approx 84.4 \,\mathrm{t/h})$$

> **Step 4 (1 mark):**
> $$T = 1000 - 84.38 = 915.62 \,\mathrm{t/h}$$

> **Step 5 (1 mark) — Copper recovery:**
> $$R = \frac{C c}{F f} = \frac{84.38 \times 28}{1000 \times 2.5} = \frac{2362.5}{2500} = 0.945 = 94.5\,\%$$

> **Step 6 (1 mark) — Enrichment ratio:**
> $$E_R = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{28}{2.5} = 11.2$$
> i.e., the Cu concentration has been upgraded **11.2 times**.

> **Step 7 (1 mark) — Verification of the two-product formula:**
> $$R = \frac{c(f - t)}{f(c - t)} = \frac{28 \times (2.5 - 0.15)}{2.5 \times (28 - 0.15)} = \frac{28 \times 2.35}{2.5 \times 27.85} = \frac{65.8}{69.625} = 0.9450$$ ✓ Matches.

---

### Question B — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a) [7 marks]** Discuss the role of different flotation reagents with at least one example for each. Explain the working of dissolved air flotation (DAF) with a suitable flow diagram.

**(b) [7 marks]** The first-order rate constant for flotation of a sulfide mineral is $K = 0.4 \,\mathrm{min}^{-1}$. If the residence time of pulp in a single cell is 6 minutes, calculate the fractional recovery. How long will it take to reach 95 % recovery? Comment on the design implication.

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 marks

> **Reagents (4 marks):**
> 1. **Collectors (1 mark):** Adsorb selectively on the desired mineral surface, rendering it hydrophobic. *Example:* Potassium amyl xanthate (PAX) for sulphide minerals; oleic acid for phosphates.
> 2. **Frothers (1 mark):** Lower surface tension, stabilize air bubbles, and produce a persistent froth. *Example:* Pine oil, methyl isobutyl carbinol (MIBC), polypropylene glycol ethers.
> 3. **Activators (0.5 mark):** Modify mineral surfaces to allow collector adsorption. *Example:* $CuSO_4$ activates sphalerite (ZnS) for xanthate flotation.
> 4. **Depressants (0.5 mark):** Prevent collector adsorption on unwanted minerals. *Example:* Sodium cyanide depresses pyrite; sodium silicate depresses silicates.
> 5. **Regulators (pH modifiers) (1 mark):** Lime ($CaO$), soda ash ($Na_2CO_3$), or $H_2SO_4$ adjust slurry pH to control collector activity and surface charge.

> **DAF explanation (2 marks):**  
> In DAF, water is saturated with air under pressure (300 – 600 kPa) in a saturator tank. The pressurized water is then released through a nozzle into the flotation tank at atmospheric pressure. The sudden drop in pressure causes the dissolved air to come out of solution as a swarm of **microscopic bubbles (30 – 100 µm)**. These bubbles attach to the suspended/colloidal particles, forming aggregates that float to the surface as a thin sludge blanket, which is scraped off. The clarified water exits from the bottom of the tank.

> **Flow diagram (1 mark):** Use the DAF block diagram from **Section 4.4** of these notes.

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 marks

> **Given:** $K = 0.4 \,\mathrm{min}^{-1}$, $\tau = 6 \,\mathrm{min}$.

> **Step 1 (1 mark) — Recovery using first-order model:**
> $$R = 1 - \exp(-K \tau) = 1 - \exp(-0.4 \times 6) = 1 - \exp(-2.4)$$
> $$\exp(-2.4) = 0.0907$$
> $$R = 1 - 0.0907 = 0.9093 = 90.93\,\%$$

> **Step 2 (1 mark) — Time for 95 % recovery:**
> Set $R = 0.95$ in $R = 1 - \exp(-K\tau)$:
> $$1 - 0.95 = \exp(-K\tau) \quad \Rightarrow \quad 0.05 = \exp(-K\tau)$$
> $$\ln(0.05) = -K \tau$$
> $$-2.9957 = -0.4 \times \tau$$
> $$\tau = \frac{2.9957}{0.4} = 7.49 \,\mathrm{min}$$

> **Step 3 (1 mark) — Design implications (4 marks):**
> 1. **Cell sizing:** A single residence time of ~7.5 min is needed for 95 % recovery. In practice, plant cells are sized to provide ~6 – 10 min of residence time per cell.
> 2. **Multi-cell banks:** A single cell cannot reach 100 % recovery; the plant uses **banks of cells in series (rougher–scavenger–cleaner)** to approach higher recoveries.
> 3. **Diminishing returns:** To go from 90 % to 95 % recovery, residence time must increase by 25 % (from 6 to 7.5 min). This illustrates the **asymptotic nature** of first-order flotation.
> 4. **Capex/Trade-off:** Increasing cell volume is expensive; instead, designers increase $K$ by **raising air rate, optimizing bubble size, and adjusting reagent dose**.
> 5. **In practice, $K$ is not constant:** Klimpel's modified model shows $K$ decreases with time because the slow-floating coarse/fine fractions remain; thus, plant design targets a *maximum practical* recovery, not 100 %.

---

## 5.1 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]  
> **Common ways KTU students lose marks in Foam Flotation questions:**
> 1. **Missing the two-product formula:** Always balance the *overall mass* AND the *valuable component* separately. Marks are split — 1 mark for each balance.
> 2. **Confusing recovery and grade:** Recovery is a *mass-weighted* quantity; grade is a *concentration* quantity. They are *not* the same thing.
> 3. **Not stating assumptions:** In Klimpel kinetics, write down "first-order process; uniform particle floatability" before integrating. Skipping this loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Wrong units in the rate constant:** $K$ must be in **s⁻¹ or min⁻¹**, *not* dimensionless.
> 5. **Forgetting the froth zone:** A cell has **pulp + froth + impeller** zones — a common 2-mark question asks for zone identification.
> 6. **No flow sheet in long answers:** KTU examiners specifically award 1 mark for "neat diagram" — always include one even if it is a block-level Mermaid-style sketch.
> 7. **Pipe-symbol inside markdown tables:** Avoid `|x|` in tables (use `\vert`); broken tables cost presentation marks.

---

## 5.2 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (must be readable in under 60 seconds before entering the exam hall):**

- ☐ **Definition:** Foam flotation = bubble-assisted separation of hydrophobic particles from hydrophilic gangue using a three-phase contact.
- ☐ **Mechanism:** Collision → Attachment → Transport (each with a probability $P_c, P_a, P_t$).
- ☐ **Young's equation:** $\gamma_{SV} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta$ — high $\theta$ = floatable.
- ☐ **Work of adhesion:** $W_{SB} = \gamma_{LV}(1 + \cos\theta)$.
- ☐ **Reagents:** Collector + Frother + Activator + Depressant (4 pillars).
- ☐ **Klimpel first-order model:** $R = 1 - \exp(-K\tau)$.
- ☐ **Two-product formula:** $R = \dfrac{c(f - t)}{f(c - t)}$ — the most-tested equation.
- ☐ **Enrichment ratio:** $E_R = c / f$.
- ☐ **Stokes bubble rise velocity:** $v_b = \rho_w g d_b^2 / (18 \mu_w)$ (small bubbles, $\mathrm{Re} < 1$).
- ☐ **DAF:** Tiny bubbles (30 – 100 µm) by depressurization; ideal for fine particles & wastewater.
- ☐ **Froth Flotation:** Large bubbles (0.5 – 3 mm) by impeller; ideal for mineral ores.
- ☐ **Three cell roles:** Rougher (bulk recovery), Scavenger (recover leftovers), Cleaner (upgrade grade).
- ☐ **Three zones in a cell:** Pulp zone, Froth zone, Impeller zone.
- ☐ **Industrial relevance:** Copper, lead, zinc, polymer latex recovery, wastewater sludge, de-inking.

---

> **End of Notes — Foam Flotation (Module 2, PEPOT752, KTU 2024 Scheme)**
