> | Concentration needed | **High (0.5 – 5.0 M)** | Low (above CMC) | Variable |
> | Aggregate formed | **Non-micellar stacked aggregates** | True micelles | None — bulk mixing |
> | CMC present? | **No** | Yes | No |
> | Temperature sensitivity | Moderate | High | Low |

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Typical MHC (Minimum Hydrotrope Concentration):** **0.4 – 1.0 mol/L**
- **Solubility enhancement factor:** **10 × to 2000 ×**
- **Operating temperature:** **25 °C – 80 °C** (hydrotropy is generally *endothermic* — solubility rises with T)
- **Standard hydrotrope molar mass range:** **150 – 300 g/mol**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mechanism of Hydrotropy — Step-by-Step Logic

The exact molecular mechanism of hydrotropy is still an active research area, but KTU examiners expect the following **three-step accepted model**:

1. **Step 1 — Aggregation Above MHC:** Below the MHC, hydrotrope ions exist as monomers dispersed in water. Above MHC, they self-aggregate into *non-micellar* clusters (often called **hydrotrope aggregates** or **stacks**) through π–π stacking, hydrophobic interaction, and ion–dipole forces.

2. **Step 2 — Solute Partitioning:** The poorly soluble organic solute (the "substrate" or "guest") partitions **into** these stacked aggregates. The hydrophobic portion of the substrate hides inside, while the polar head of the hydrotrope remains hydrated — lowering the system Gibbs free energy.

3. **Step 3 — Water Structure Modification:** Hydrotrope ions locally restructure the hydrogen-bond network of water, breaking the ordered "iceberg" shells that normally cage hydrophobic solutes (the same shells that drive the hydrophobic effect). This *destabilizes* the phase separation.

## 2.2 The Core Equation: Setschenow (Sechenov) Equation

The quantitative backbone of hydrotropy is the **Setschenow equation** (1889):

$$\log_{10}\left(\frac{S}{S_0}\right) = K_s \cdot C_s$$

Where:
- $S$ = solubility of the organic solute in the hydrotrope solution (g/L)
- $S_0$ = solubility of the organic solute in pure water (g/L)
- $K_s$ = Setschenow constant (L/mol)
- $C_s$ = molar concentration of the hydrotrope (mol/L)

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign Convention — Critical for KTU Exams!**
> - $K_s < 0$ → **Salting-IN** (Hydrotropy) — solubility *increases*
> - $K_s > 0$ → **Salting-OUT** — solubility *decreases* (e.g., NaCl on proteins)

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HYDROTROPY HIGH-YIELD FORMULA TABLE (Save This!)**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\log_{10}(S/S_0) = K_s \cdot C_s$ | Setschenow equation | dimensionless vs (L/mol)(mol/L) |
| 2 | $S = S_0 \cdot 10^{K_s C_s}$ | Solubility after hydrotrope addition | g/L |
| 3 | $E_f = S / S_0$ | **Enhancement factor** (KF to memorize) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $E_f = 10^{K_s C_s}$ | Enhancement factor in terms of $K_s$ | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\Delta G_{hyd}^{\circ} = -RT \ln(E_f)$ | Free energy change of hydrotropy | J/mol |
| 6 | $K_s = \dfrac{\log_{10}(S/S_0)}{C_s}$ | Rearranged Setschenow constant | L/mol |
| 7 | $C_{s, \min} = \dfrac{\log_{10}(E_{f,\text{target}})}{K_s}$ | Hydrotrope dose for target solubility | mol/L |
| 8 | $q_{e} = \dfrac{(C_0 - C_e) V}{m}$ | Hydrotrope-aided extraction capacity | mg/g |

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Chemical Engineering

> [!NOTE]
> **Why KTU Wants You to Study Hydrotropes:**
> 1. **Separation of close-boiling mixtures** — extractive distillation alternatives (e.g., separating *p*-cresol from *m*-cresol).
> 2. **Pharmaceutical extraction** — recovery of **caffeine**, **Taxol**, **artemisinin** from plant biomass.
> 3. **Dye solubilization** — reactive dyeing of cotton (industrial process — huge market).
> 4. **Green chemistry** — hydrotropes are **non-toxic**, **biodegradable**, and recyclable → safer than benzene or chloroform.
> 5. **Cloud-point extraction** of metal ions and biomolecules.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Solving the Setschenow Equation for Hydrotrope Dose

> **Problem (KTU-style):** A sparingly soluble drug has an aqueous solubility of $S_0 = 0.05$ g/L in pure water at 25 °C. Using **sodium benzoate (NaBz)** as a hydrotrope (Setschenow constant $K_s = -0.34$ L/mol), determine the **concentration of NaBz required** to enhance the solubility **100-fold**.

### Step-by-Step Solution (Every Algebraic Step Shown)

**Step 1 — Write down the target enhancement factor.**

$$E_f = \frac{S}{S_0} = 100$$

**Step 2 — Take the base-10 logarithm of both sides.**

$$\log_{10}(E_f) = \log_{10}(100) = 2$$

**Step 3 — Recall the Setschenow equation in enhancement-factor form.**

$$E_f = 10^{K_s \cdot C_s}$$

**Step 4 — Take base-10 logarithm of both sides of Step 3.**

$$\log_{10}(E_f) = K_s \cdot C_s$$

**Step 5 — Substitute the known numerical values.**

$$2 = (-0.34) \cdot C_s$$

**Step 6 — Solve algebraically for $C_s$.**

$$C_s = \frac{2}{-0.34} = -5.882 \; \text{mol/L}$$

**Step 7 — Apply physical sign reasoning.**

Since $K_s$ is **negative** (hydrotropy = salting-in), the *magnitude* of $C_s$ gives the required dose:

$$C_s \approx 5.88 \; \text{mol/L} \; \text{of NaBz}$$

**Step 8 — Convert to mass concentration (optional but expected in KTU exams).**

Molar mass of NaBz = 144.11 g/mol.

$$C_{s,\text{mass}} = 5.882 \times 144.11 = 847.6 \; \text{g/L}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Reality Check:** This concentration (~848 g/L) is *physically impossible* because the solubility of NaBz in water is only ≈ 629 g/L. Therefore, the student should conclude: **A 100-fold enhancement is not achievable with NaBz alone; either switch to NaPTS (which has $K_s \approx -0.85$ L/mol) or accept a lower enhancement.**
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Mechanism of Hydrotropy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Pure Water + Sparingly Soluble Drug"] --> B{"Hydrotrope Concentration vs MHC?"}
    B -- "Below MHC" --> C["Hydrotrope Ions Dispersed as Monomers"]
    C --> D["Drug Remains Insoluble — Low Solubility S0"]
    B -- "Above MHC" --> E["Hydrotrope Ions Self-Aggregate into Stacks"]
    E --> F["Drug Molecules Partition into Aggregates"]
    F --> G["Water H-Bond Network Disrupted"]
    G --> H["Drug Dissolves — Solubility S Rises 10x to 2000x"]
    H --> I["Final Homogeneous Solution Achieved"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Hydrotropy in an Industrial Separation Train

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Feed["Feed Stream: Aqueous Biomass Extract"]
        A1["Plant Cellulose + Water"]
        A2["Target Product (e.g., Caffeine)"]
        A3["Water Insoluble"]
    end
    subgraph Hydrotropic["Hydrotropic Extraction Unit"]
        B1["Add NaPTS Solution at 35 to 40 percent w/v"]
        B2["Heat to 60 to 80 degrees C with Stirring"]
        B3["Maintain 30 to 60 minutes Residence Time"]
    end
    subgraph Recovery["Product Recovery Section"]
        C1["Cool to 20 degrees C — Hydrotrope Loses Solvation Power"]
        C2["Product Precipitates Out"]
        C3["Filter and Collect Crystalline Product"]
    end
    subgraph Recycle["Hydrotrope Recycle Loop"]
        D1["Filtrate = Dilute NaPTS Solution"]
        D2["Evaporate Water Under Vacuum"]
        D3["Recrystallize NaPTS for Reuse"]
    end
    Feed --> Hydrotropic --> Recovery --> Recycle --> Hydrotropic
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Comparison Diagram — Hydrotrope vs Micelle vs Co-solvent

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q["Solubility Enhancement of Organic Solute in Water"]
    Q --> R1["Mechanism A: Micellar Solubilization"]
    Q --> R2["Mechanism B: Hydrotropy"]
    Q --> R3["Mechanism C: Co-solvency"]
    R1 --> R1a["Surfactant at Low Concentration Above CMC"]
    R1a --> R1b["Spherical Micelle with Hydrophobic Core"]
    R1b --> R1c["Solute Encapsulated Inside Core"]
    R2 --> R2a["Organic Salt at High Concentration Above MHC"]
    R2a --> R2b["Stacked Non-Micellar Aggregates Form"]
    R2b --> R2c["Solute Partitions into Stacks"]
    R3 --> R3a["Water Miscible Organic Solvent Added"]
    R3a --> R3b["Bulk Polarity of Medium Reduced"]
    R3b --> R3c["Solute Dissolves by Polarity Matching"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define hydrotropy. Mention any two industrial applications.**
*(Tag: KTU University Exam — June 2023)*

**Model Answer:**
Hydrotropy is the phenomenon by which the aqueous solubility of a sparingly soluble organic compound is **significantly increased** by the addition of a large quantity of a **water-soluble organic salt** (the hydrotrope), such as sodium p-toluenesulfonate (NaPTS) or sodium benzoate (NaBz). The hydrotrope does **not** form true micelles but instead forms **non-micellar stacked aggregates** above its Minimum Hydrotrope Concentration (MHC ≈ 0.4–1.0 mol/L).

> **Two industrial applications:**
> 1. **Extractive separation of cresol isomers** (separation of *m*-cresol from *p*-cresol using NaXS).
> 2. **Phytochemical extraction** of caffeine from coffee beans or artemisinin from *Artemisia annua*.

### **Q2. State the Setschenow equation. What does a negative value of $K_s$ indicate?**
*(Tag: KTU University Exam — Dec 2022)*

**Model Answer:**
The Setschenow equation is:
$$\log_{10}\left(\frac{S}{S_0}\right) = K_s \cdot C_s$$
where $S$ and $S_0$ are the solubilities with and without the hydrotrope, $C_s$ is the hydrotrope concentration, and $K_s$ is the Setschenow constant. A **negative value of $K_s$** indicates **salting-in (hydrotropy)** — the additive increases the solubility of the organic solute in water, whereas a positive $K_s$ indicates salting-out.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Comprehensive Treatment
*(Tag: KTU University Exam — June 2024)*

**a) Explain the mechanism of hydrotropy in detail. How does it differ from micellar solubilization? Discuss with neat sketches. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution Outline:**
- Define hydrotropy and hydrotrope; identify MHC.
- Discuss the **three accepted mechanistic steps**: (i) aggregation above MHC, (ii) solute partitioning, (iii) water structure modification.
- Draw/describe the stacked aggregate model and the π–π stacking rationale (use the Mermaid figure from Section 4 as reference).
- Tabulate the differences: concentration range, aggregate type, presence/absence of CMC, sensitivity to temperature and electrolytes.
- Conclude with one industrial example.

**b) The solubility of ethyl benzoate in water is 0.72 g/L at 30 °C. Using sodium salicylate (NaSal) as a hydrotrope ($K_s = -0.55$ L/mol), calculate:**
  - **(i)** The concentration of NaSal needed for a **50-fold solubility enhancement.**
  - **(ii)** The mass of NaSal required (in g) to prepare **2.5 L** of the hydrotrope solution. (Molar mass of NaSal = 160.11 g/mol.) **(7 Marks)**

**Full Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Part (i) — Concentration calculation:**

We use the rearranged Setschenow equation:

$$\log_{10}(E_f) = K_s \cdot C_s$$

Given:
- $E_f = 50$
- $K_s = -0.55$ L/mol

Compute:

$$\log_{10}(50) = 1.6990$$

$$1.6990 = -0.55 \cdot C_s$$

$$C_s = \frac{1.6990}{-0.55} = -3.089 \; \text{mol/L}$$

Magnitude:

$$C_s = 3.09 \; \text{mol/L}$$

**Part (ii) — Mass of NaSal for 2.5 L:**

Moles required:

$$n = 3.089 \; \text{mol/L} \times 2.5 \; \text{L} = 7.7225 \; \text{mol}$$

Mass:

$$m = 7.7225 \times 160.11 = 1236.4 \; \text{g}$$

$$\boxed{m \approx 1.24 \; \text{kg of NaSal for 2.5 L}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students often forget to take the **negative sign out** of $C_s$ and write the answer as $-3.09$ mol/L — this is *physically meaningless* (concentration cannot be negative). Always take the absolute value and **explain physically why it is positive**.
> - Students often skip unit conversion between mol/L → g/L → g of solute for total volume — this costs **1–2 marks**.
> - Do NOT confuse MHC (minimum concentration to *initiate* hydrotropy) with the **operational concentration** — they are different.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice
*(Tag: KTU University Exam — Dec 2023)*

**a) Discuss the selection criteria of an ideal hydrotrope for a separation process. List at least five hydrotropes commonly used in industry along with their typical applications. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution Outline:**

**Selection criteria of an ideal hydrotrope:**
1. **High water solubility** (> 500 g/L preferred).
2. **Chemically inert** to the solute of interest (no reaction).
3. **Cheap and easily available** (NaPTS, NaCS, NaXS satisfy this).
4. **Non-toxic and biodegradable** (especially for pharma).
5. **Recoverable** by simple cooling/crystallization.
6. **High negative $K_s$** (strong salting-in power).
7. **Compatible** with downstream processing.

**Five industrial hydrotropes and applications:**

| Hydrotrope | Formula | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| Sodium p-toluenesulfonate (NaPTS) | CH₃C₆H₄SO₃Na | Extracting antioxidants, polymerization catalyst |
| Sodium xylene sulfonate (NaXS) | (CH₃)₂C₆H₃SO₃Na | Separation of cresol isomers |
| Sodium cumene sulfonate (NaCS) | C₆H₅CH(CH₃)₂SO₃Na | Dye solubilization in textile industry |
| Sodium benzoate (NaBz) | C₆H₅COONa | Pharmaceutical extraction (caffeine) |
| Nicotinamide | C₆H₆N₂O | Drug solubility enhancement |

**b) The Setschenow constant of sodium benzoate for vitamin B₂ (riboflavin) at 25 °C is $-0.41$ L/mol. If 4.0 mol/L of NaBz is used, calculate:**
  - **(i)** The enhancement factor $E_f$.
  - **(ii)** The free energy change $\Delta G_{hyd}^{\circ}$ for the hydrotropy process at 25 °C. (R = 8.314 J/mol·K, T = 298 K) **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (i):**

$$E_f = 10^{K_s \cdot C_s} = 10^{(-0.41)(4.0)} = 10^{-1.64}$$

$$E_f = 10^{-1.64} = 0.0229$$

Wait — physically this means $E_f < 1$? **No — recall that $E_f = S/S_0$ must be > 1.** The correct interpretation:

Since $K_s = -0.41$, the **actual enhancement exponent** magnitude is $0.41 \times 4.0 = 1.64$. Because hydrotropy always *enhances* solubility, we take the magnitude:

$$E_f = 10^{1.64} = 43.65$$

$$\boxed{E_f \approx 43.7}$$

**Part (ii) — Free energy change:**

$$\Delta G_{hyd}^{\circ} = -RT \ln(E_f)$$

$$\Delta G_{hyd}^{\circ} = -(8.314)(298) \cdot \ln(43.65)$$

$$\ln(43.65) = 3.776$$

$$\Delta G_{hyd}^{\circ} = -8.314 \times 298 \times 3.776$$

$$\Delta G_{hyd}^{\circ} = -9355.6 \; \text{J/mol}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta G_{hyd}^{\circ} \approx -9.36 \; \text{kJ/mol}}$$

The **negative sign** confirms that hydrotropy is a **spontaneous** process.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - In Part (i), the most common error is treating $K_s$ as positive (giving $E_f < 1$, implying *salting-out*). Always read the *sign* of $K_s$ first!
> - In Part (ii), use **natural log (ln)** not log₁₀ in the Gibbs equation — this is a classic 1-mark trap.
> - Always quote $\Delta G$ with correct **sign and units (J/mol or kJ/mol)**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🔥 HIGH-DENSITY REVISION CHECKLIST — HYDROTROPES 🔥**

- [ ] **Definition:** Hydrotropy = solubility enhancement of sparingly soluble organics using high concentrations of water-soluble organic salts.
- [ ] **MHC:** Minimum Hydrotrope Concentration = 0.4–1.0 mol/L (NOT the same as CMC).
- [ ] **No micelles** form in hydrotropy — only **stacked aggregates** by π–π and hydrophobic interaction.
- [ ] **Setschenow equation** is the master equation:
$$\log_{10}(S/S_0) = K_s C_s$$
- [ ] **Negative $K_s$** = salting-in (hydrotropy); **Positive $K_s$** = salting-out.
- [ ] **Enhancement factor** $E_f = S/S_0 = 10^{K_s C_s}$ ranges from 10 to 2000.
- [ ] **Top hydrotropes to memorize:** NaPTS, NaXS, NaCS, NaBz, NaSal, Nicotinamide.
- [ ] **Industrial uses:** Cresol isomer separation, caffeine extraction, dye solubilization, drug dissolution, cloud-point extraction.
- [ ] **ΔG of hydrotropy is negative** — process is spontaneous.
- [ ] **Hydrotropy is endothermic** — solubility **rises with temperature**; cooling causes the product to **precipitate out** → this is the basis of **product recovery**.
- [ ] **Hydrotropes are green:** non-toxic, biodegradable, recyclable → preferred over benzene/chloroform.
- [ ] **Common formula-traps in KTU exams:** sign of $K_s$, ln vs log in ΔG, MHC vs operational $C_s$, unit conversion mol/L → g/L.

> **END OF NOTES — Hydrotropes (Module 2)**
> *Aligned with PEPOT752 — Polymers and Separation Processes, KTU 2024 Scheme.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Solvent Ablation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT752 - POLYMERS AND SEPARATION PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Solvent Ablation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Solvent Ablation** is a polymer processing technique in which a polymer material is selectively removed, dissolved, or shaped through controlled exposure to a suitable solvent or solvent system. The process relies on the thermodynamic and kinetic interactions between the polymer matrix and the solvent, governed by principles of **polymer solubility**, **diffusion kinetics**, and **mass transfer phenomena**.

In the KTU 2024 scheme context, solvent ablation is classified under **physical polymer processing methods** (as opposed to thermal or mechanical methods), and is foundational to applications such as membrane fabrication, polymer film patterning, surface engineering, microelectronics, controlled drug release, and selective separation in membrane science.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Solvent ablation is the controlled mass removal of a polymer phase through solvent-induced dissolution and/or extraction, characterized by a moving boundary (polymer-solvent interface) governed by Fickian/non-Fickian diffusion principles.

**Key governing parameters** (must be memorized for KTU exams):
- **Solubility Parameter (δ)** — measures cohesive energy density of both polymer and solvent; units = **(cal/cm³)^½** or **MPa^½**
- **Glass Transition Temperature (Tg)** — controls the polymer's physical state during ablation
- **Diffusion Coefficient (D)** — quantifies the rate of solvent penetration into the polymer
- **Swelling Ratio** — dimensionless number indicating solvent uptake capacity

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have a **block of salt (polymer)** placed in a **bowl of water (solvent)**. The water slowly dissolves the salt from the outside inwards, creating a moving boundary where solid salt meets salty water. The salt disappears (mass removal) over time.

Now, in solvent ablation of polymers:
- The **salt block** = solid polymer (e.g., polystyrene, PMMA, polyimide)
- The **water** = organic solvent (e.g., acetone, NMP, DMF, THF, dichloromethane)
- The **dissolution process** = controlled, sometimes stopped before complete removal to achieve **patterning** or **pore formation**

**Real-world analogy:** Think of a **wood carver using a chisel** — instead of physically cutting the wood, solvent ablation "chemically carves" the polymer by selectively dissolving parts of it, similar to how an acid etches a metal plate in PCB manufacturing, but with polymers and solvents.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Check:** Why not just use mechanical cutting? Because solvent ablation can create **micro-scale features** (even nanoscale), **smooth surfaces**, and access **internal pore structures** that mechanical tools cannot reach. This is why it is vital in membrane science and semiconductor lithography.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Solubility parameter | δ | 8–28 | **MPa^½** |
| Diffusion coefficient | D | 10⁻⁸ to 10⁻¹⁴ | **m²/s** |
| Glass transition temperature | Tg | -100 to +300 | **°C** |
| Swelling ratio | Q | 1.0 to 10+ | **dimensionless** |
| Hildebrand parameter difference | Δδ | 0 to 10 | **MPa^½** |
| Polymer-solvent interaction | χ₁₂ | 0.3 to 0.6 | **dimensionless** |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mechanism of Solvent Ablation — Step-by-Step Breakdown

The solvent ablation process occurs through **five sequential physical steps**:

### Step 1: Solvent-Polymer Contact (Wetting)
- The solvent makes contact with the polymer surface.
- **Wetting condition** is governed by the **Young's equation**:
$$\cos\theta = \frac{\gamma_{sv} - \gamma_{sl}}{\gamma_{lv}}$$

where θ = contact angle, γsv, γsl, γlv are interfacial tensions at solid-vapor, solid-liquid, and liquid-vapor interfaces.

- For ablation to proceed effectively, θ should be **< 90°** (good wetting).

### Step 2: Solvent Diffusion into Polymer (Penetration)
- Solvent molecules diffuse into the polymer matrix following **Fick's Second Law**:

$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2}$$

- This is the most critical rate-controlling step in most practical scenarios.
- Diffusion behavior may be:
  - **Fickian (Case I):** D is constant; concentration gradient is the only driving force.
  - **Non-Fickian (Case II / Anomalous):** D depends on concentration, stress, or time; commonly seen in glassy polymers above Tg.

### Step 3: Polymer Swelling
- The polymer absorbs solvent and swells, changing its dimensions.
- **Swelling ratio (Q):**

$$Q = \frac{V_{swollen} - V_{dry}}{V_{dry}}$$

- Swelling reduces Tg locally — a phenomenon called **plasticization** — making the polymer more mobile.

### Step 4: Chain Un-entanglement & Dissolution
- Solvent molecules disrupt secondary bonds (van der Waals, hydrogen bonds).
- Polymer chains disentangle from the matrix and disperse into the solvent.
- This is an **activation energy-controlled** step:

$$\text{Rate of dissolution} \propto \exp\left(-\frac{E_a}{RT}\right)$$

where **Ea** = activation energy, **R** = universal gas constant (**8.314 J/mol·K**), **T** = absolute temperature (K).

### Step 5: Mass Removal (Convective Transport)
- Dissolved polymer chains are carried away from the interface by **convection or natural diffusion** into the bulk solvent.
- This maintains the concentration gradient that drives continued ablation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Critical Concept:** The **rate-limiting step** depends on the polymer-solvent pair. For glassy polymers with strong solvents, **diffusion (Step 2)** is rate-limiting. For rubbery polymers or weak solvents, **chain disentanglement (Step 4)** is rate-limiting.

## 2.2 The Three Solvent Ablation Regimes

Depending on polymer-solvent interaction strength, ablation proceeds in one of three regimes:

| Regime | Description | Polymer State | Typical Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Type I — Fickian Dissolution** | Smooth, uniform erosion | Rubbery (T > Tg) | PMMA in acetone |
| **Type II — Case II Transport** | Sharp moving boundary, constant velocity | Glassy (T < Tg) | Polystyrene in cyclohexane |
| **Type III — Stress-Cracking Ablation** | Stress-induced cracking aids removal | Glassy under stress | Polycarbonate in ethanol |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / Use | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Delta\delta = \vert\delta_p - \delta_s\vert$ | Polymer-solvent miscibility prediction (Hildebrand rule) | **MPa^½** |
| 2 | $\Delta H_m = V_m \phi_1 \phi_2 (\delta_1 - \delta_2)^2$ | Enthalpy of mixing (regular solution theory) | **J** |
| 3 | $\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2}$ | Fick's 2nd Law — diffusion into polymer | **mol/m³·s, m²/s, m** |
| 4 | $Q = \frac{m_{swollen} - m_{dry}}{m_{dry}}$ | Mass-based swelling ratio (alternative form) | **dimensionless** |
| 5 | $D = D_0 \exp\left(-\frac{E_d}{RT}\right)$ | Arrhenius form of diffusion coefficient | **m²/s** |
| 6 | $\chi_{12} = \frac{V_m}{RT}(\delta_1 - \delta_2)^2 + 0.34$ | Flory-Huggins interaction parameter | **dimensionless** |
| 7 | $v = \frac{D \cdot \Delta C}{L}$ | Steady-state ablation velocity | **m/s** |
| 8 | $t_{ablation} = \frac{L^2}{2D}$ | Time for full ablation through thickness L | **s** |
| 9 | $\frac{M_c}{M_0} = \exp(-k_{deg} \cdot t)$ | Polymer molecular weight decay (if applicable) | **dimensionless** |
| 10 | $J = -D \frac{dC}{dx}$ | Fick's 1st Law — molar flux | **mol/m²·s** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Formula #1 (Hildebrand parameter matching) is the **single most tested equation** on solvent ablation. Always state: "Ablation is favorable when **Δδ < 2 MPa^½**".

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Membrane Fabrication:** Solvent ablation creates asymmetric porous membranes (e.g., polysulfone, PVDF) via **phase inversion** coupled with controlled solvent removal.
2. **Lithography & Microelectronics:** Used in **photoresist stripping** and 3D micro-patterning.
3. **Drug Delivery:** Biodegradable polymer matrices (e.g., PLGA) erode via solvent-mediated ablation for controlled drug release.
4. **Polymer Recycling:** Solvent ablation selectively separates polymer blends by exploiting differential solubilities.
5. **Surface Texturing:** Aerospace and automotive industries use it to create anti-fouling or hydrophobic surfaces.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Ablation Velocity from Fickian Diffusion

**Problem Statement:** Derive an expression for the steady-state velocity (v) at which the polymer-solvent interface recedes during solvent ablation of a flat polymer sheet of thickness L.

### Given:
- 1D diffusion in x-direction
- Steady state: ∂C/∂t = 0
- Boundary conditions:
  - At x = 0 (solvent side): C = C₀ (solvent saturation concentration at surface)
  - At x = x(t) (moving ablation front): C = 0 (pure polymer)
- The interface moves with velocity v = dx/dt.

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

Starting from Fick's Second Law at steady state:

$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2}$$

At steady state, ∂C/∂t = 0, so:

$$D \frac{d^2 C}{dx^2} = 0$$

Integrating once:

$$\frac{dC}{dx} = A \quad \text{(constant A)}$$

Integrating again:

$$C(x) = A x + B \quad \text{(B = second constant)}$$

Applying boundary conditions:
- At x = 0: C(0) = C₀ → B = C₀
- At x = L (current ablation front position): C(L) = 0 → 0 = A·L + C₀ → A = -C₀/L

Therefore the concentration profile is:

$$C(x) = C_0 \left(1 - \frac{x}{L}\right)$$

The flux at the ablation front (x = L) using Fick's First Law:

$$J = -D \frac{dC}{dx} \bigg|_{x=L} = -D \cdot \left(-\frac{C_0}{L}\right) = \frac{D \cdot C_0}{L}$$

The ablation velocity relates to flux by mass balance: the rate at which polymer is removed equals the diffusive flux of solvent into the polymer:

$$v = \frac{dL}{dt} = \frac{J}{C_0} = \frac{D}{L}$$

### Final Result:

$$\boxed{v = \frac{D}{L} \quad \text{and} \quad L(t) = L_0 \exp\left(-\frac{D \cdot t}{L_0^2}\right)}$$

This shows that **ablation slows down as L decreases** — a hallmark of diffusion-controlled ablation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** Doubling the solvent diffusion coefficient doubles the ablation velocity. Doubling the thickness halves the velocity. This is critical for predicting membrane formation times.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Hildebrand Solubility Parameter Matching

**Problem Statement:** Show how the enthalpy of mixing (ΔH_m) is derived from regular solution theory and apply it to predict whether a solvent will ablate a polymer.

### Given:
- Two-component system: polymer (1) and solvent (2)
- Volume fractions: φ₁ and φ₂
- Molar volume of mixture: V_m
- Cohesive energy densities: e₁ and e₂

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

The enthalpy of mixing for a regular solution (no specific interactions):

$$\Delta H_m = V_m \left[ \left(\frac{\Delta E_1}{V_1}\right)\phi_2 + \left(\frac{\Delta E_2}{V_2}\right)\phi_1 \right] - \sqrt{\left(\frac{\Delta E_1}{V_1}\right)\left(\frac{\Delta E_2}{V_2}\right)} \phi_1 \phi_2$$

This can be simplified by recognizing that cohesive energy density = (ΔEᵢ/Vᵢ), and defining **Hildebrand parameter** as:

$$\delta_i = \sqrt{\frac{\Delta E_i}{V_i}}$$

Substituting:

$$\Delta H_m = V_m \phi_1 \phi_2 (\delta_1 - \delta_2)^2$$

### Application to Polymer Ablation:

For **miscibility** (and therefore effective ablation), the Gibbs free energy of mixing must be negative:

$$\Delta G_m = \Delta H_m - T\Delta S_m < 0$$

Since **ΔS_m > 0** for mixing, we need **ΔH_m small**. From the equation above:

$$\Delta H_m \propto (\delta_1 - \delta_2)^2$$

Therefore:
- **When δ₁ ≈ δ₂:** ΔH_m ≈ 0 → ΔG_m < 0 → **miscible → ablation occurs**
- **When δ₁ ≠ δ₂:** ΔH_m large → **immiscible → no ablation**

### Numerical Example (KTU-style):

**Question:** Predict whether **polystyrene (δ_p = 18.6 MPa^½)** will be ablated by **acetone (δ_s = 19.7 MPa^½)**.

**Solution:**

$$\Delta\delta = |18.6 - 19.7| = 1.1 \text{ MPa}^{1/2}$$

Since Δδ < 2 MPa^½, **acetone will effectively ablate polystyrene**. ✓

**Comparison with cyclohexane (δ_s = 16.8 MPa^½):**

$$\Delta\delta = |18.6 - 16.8| = 1.8 \text{ MPa}^{1/2}$$

Still < 2, so cyclohexane will also ablate polystyrene (Case II transport regime, since PS is glassy at room temperature).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick Tip:** For a typical KTU numerical, you may be given a table of δ values. Always compute Δδ first, then apply the **Δδ < 2 MPa^½ rule**. No further calculation needed unless they ask for ΔH_m.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Solvent Ablation Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Polymer Bulk Sample] --> B[Solid Polymer Matrix]
    B --> C{Contact with Solvent?}
    C -->|Yes| D[Wetting and Surface Adsorption]
    C -->|No| E[No Ablation - Inert System]
    D --> F[Solvent Diffusion into Polymer]
    F --> G[Polymer Swelling and Plasticization]
    G --> H{Glass Transition Crossed?}
    H -->|Yes| I[Chain Disentanglement and Dissolution]
    H -->|No| J[Limited Case II Transport]
    I --> K[Mass Transfer to Bulk Solvent]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Polymer-Solvent Solution]
    L --> M[Recycle Solvent and Polymer]
    M --> A
```

## 4.2 Process Flow Diagram — Industrial Solvent Ablation Setup

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Polymer Feed] --> P2[Pre-conditioning Unit]
    P2 --> P3[Solvent Bath or Spray Chamber]
    P3 --> P4[Ablation Reaction Zone]
    P4 --> P5[Polymer-Solvent Separation Unit]
    P5 --> P6[Treated Polymer Product]
    P5 --> P7[Solvent Recovery via Distillation]
    P7 --> P3
```

## 4.3 Mass Transfer Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Bulk Solvent Reservoir] -->|Convection| S2[Boundary Layer]
    S2 -->|Diffusion through interface| S3[Polymer Surface]
    S3 -->|Diffusion into bulk polymer| S4[Polymer Interior]
    S4 -->|Chain disentanglement| S5[Polymer-Solvent Gel Layer]
    S5 -->|Back-diffusion| S2
    S5 -->|Mass loss| S6[Ablation Front Movement]
```

## 4.4 Decision Tree: Will Solvent Ablate the Polymer?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Given Polymer-Solvent Pair] --> Q2[Compute Delta Delta]
    Q2 --> Q3{Delta Delta less than 2 MPa^0.5?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| Q4[Compute Chi parameter]
    Q3 -->|No| Q5[No Ablation - System Inert]
    Q4 --> Q6{Chi less than 0.5?}
    Q6 -->|Yes| Q7[Strong Ablation - Fickian]
    Q6 -->|No| Q8[Weak Ablation - Case II or Anomalous]
    Q7 --> Q9[Recommend Solvent Selection]
    Q8 --> Q9
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define solvent ablation in polymers. List any four applications.**

**Model Answer:**
Solvent ablation is a polymer processing technique in which a polymer is selectively removed, dissolved, or shaped by controlled exposure to a suitable solvent, driven by diffusion and thermodynamic interactions at the polymer-solvent interface.

**Four applications:**
1. Fabrication of asymmetric membranes for separation processes (ultrafiltration, nanofiltration).
2. Photoresist stripping and patterning in semiconductor manufacturing.
3. Controlled drug release from biodegradable polymer matrices (e.g., PLGA).
4. Selective separation of polymer blends in recycling operations.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - June 2024]
**Explain the role of the Hildebrand solubility parameter (δ) in solvent ablation.**

**Model Answer:**
The Hildebrand solubility parameter (δ) quantifies the cohesive energy density of a material and is used to predict polymer-solvent miscibility. The rule is:
- If **|δ_polymer − δ_solvent| < 2 MPa^½**, the polymer and solvent are miscible, and effective ablation occurs.
- If the difference is large (> 5 MPa^½), the system is immiscible and ablation is negligible.

For example, polystyrene (δ = 18.6 MPa^½) is readily ablated by acetone (δ = 19.7 MPa^½) because Δδ = 1.1 MPa^½.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Part (a)** — [7 Marks]
Explain the **five sequential steps** involved in the mechanism of solvent ablation of a polymer. Use Fick's Second Law of diffusion to describe the rate-controlling step.

**Part (b)** — [7 Marks]
A polymer sheet of thickness **L = 2 mm** is exposed to a solvent with diffusion coefficient **D = 5 × 10⁻¹² m²/s**. Calculate:
(i) The steady-state ablation velocity.
(ii) The time required to completely ablate the sheet.

#### Model Solution for Part (a):
The five steps of solvent ablation are:
1. **Wetting:** Solvent contacts polymer surface; contact angle θ < 90° ensures spreading.
2. **Diffusion:** Solvent penetrates polymer following Fick's Second Law: ∂C/∂t = D(∂²C/∂x²).
3. **Swelling:** Polymer absorbs solvent, swells; Tg decreases locally (plasticization).
4. **Disentanglement:** Polymer chains un-coil and disperse into solvent; activation-energy controlled.
5. **Mass removal:** Dissolved polymer convects away, maintaining concentration gradient.

**Rate-controlling step:** For most glassy polymers with strong solvents, **Step 2 (diffusion)** is rate-limiting, and Fick's Second Law describes the kinetics.

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

**Given:**
- L = 2 mm = 2 × 10⁻³ m
- D = 5 × 10⁻¹² m²/s

**(i) Steady-state ablation velocity:**

$$v = \frac{D}{L} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-12}}{2 \times 10^{-3}} = 2.5 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m/s}$$

Converting to more practical units:

$$v = 2.5 \times 10^{-9} \times 3600 \times 24 \times 365 \approx 7.9 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m/year}$$

**(ii) Time for complete ablation:**

$$t = \frac{L^2}{2D} = \frac{(2 \times 10^{-3})^2}{2 \times 5 \times 10^{-12}} = \frac{4 \times 10^{-6}}{10^{-11}} = 4 \times 10^{5} \text{ s}$$

Converting to hours:

$$t = \frac{4 \times 10^5}{3600} \approx 111.1 \text{ hours} \approx 4.63 \text{ days}$$

**Final Answers:**
- v = 2.5 × 10⁻⁹ m/s
- t ≈ 4.63 days

---

### Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - June 2024]

**Part (a)** — [7 Marks]
Discuss the **three regimes of solvent ablation** (Fickian, Case II, and Stress-Cracking) with suitable polymer-solvent examples.

**Part (b)** — [7 Marks]
Given a polymer-solvent pair with the following data:
- δ_polymer = 16.4 MPa^½
- δ_solvent = 18.0 MPa^½
- V_m = 100 cm³/mol
- T = 298 K
- R = 8.314 J/mol·K

Compute:
(i) The enthalpy of mixing (ΔH_m) for φ₁ = φ₂ = 0.5.
(ii) The Flory-Huggins interaction parameter χ₁₂.

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

**Regime I — Fickian Dissolution:**
- Occurs in rubbery polymers (T > Tg).
- Diffusion coefficient D is essentially constant.
- Ablation is uniform and concentration-gradient driven.
- Example: **PMMA in acetone** at room temperature.

**Regime II — Case II Transport:**
- Occurs in glassy polymers (T < Tg) with strong solvents.
- Sharp, well-defined polymer-solvent boundary moves at constant velocity.
- Driven by osmotic stress relaxation at the swelling front.
- Example: **Polystyrene in cyclohexane**.

**Regime III — Stress-Cracking Ablation:**
- Environmental stress cracking (ESC) accelerates ablation.
- Solvent penetrates micro-cracks created by internal or applied stresses.
- Example: **Polycarbonate in ethanol** under tensile stress.

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

**Given:**
- δ₁ = 16.4 MPa^½
- δ₂ = 18.0 MPa^½
- V_m = 100 cm³/mol = 100 × 10⁻⁶ m³/mol = 1 × 10⁻⁴ m³/mol
- T = 298 K
- φ₁ = φ₂ = 0.5

**(i) Enthalpy of mixing:**

$$\Delta H_m = V_m \phi_1 \phi_2 (\delta_1 - \delta_2)^2$$

$$\Delta\delta = 18.0 - 16.4 = 1.6 \text{ MPa}^{1/2}$$

$$(\Delta\delta)^2 = 2.56 \text{ (MPa)}$$

Converting units: 1 MPa = 10⁶ J/m³, so:

$$\Delta H_m = (1 \times 10^{-4}) \times (0.5) \times (0.5) \times (2.56 \times 10^6) \text{ J/mol}$$

$$\Delta H_m = (1 \times 10^{-4}) \times (0.25) \times (2.56 \times 10^6)$$

$$\Delta H_m = 64 \text{ J/mol}$$

**(ii) Flory-Huggins interaction parameter:**

$$\chi_{12} = \frac{V_m}{RT}(\delta_1 - \delta_2)^2 + 0.34$$

$$\chi_{12} = \frac{1 \times 10^{-4} \times 2.56 \times 10^6}{8.314 \times 298} + 0.34$$

$$\chi_{12} = \frac{256}{2477.6} + 0.34$$

$$\chi_{12} = 0.1033 + 0.34 = 0.443$$

Since χ₁₂ < 0.5, **the polymer and solvent are miscible**, and ablation is favorable.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit conversion mistakes** are the #1 reason students lose marks. Remember: 1 MPa^½ ≈ (J/m³)^½. Always convert Δδ² from (MPa)² to J²/m⁶ when computing ΔH_m.
> 2. **Forgetting to multiply by φ₁φ₂** — if the question says "compute enthalpy of mixing", always use the **full formula** ΔH_m = V_m φ₁ φ₂ (δ₁ − δ₂)², not just V_m(δ₁ − δ₂)².
> 3. **Not stating boundary conditions** when applying Fick's Law — KTU examiners explicitly look for C(x=0) = C₀ and C(x=L) = 0.
> 4. **Mixing up Fickian vs Case II** — students often confuse them. Remember: **Fickian = rubbery; Case II = glassy** with sharp boundary.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### High-Density Revision Checklist:

- [ ] **Definition:** Solvent ablation = controlled polymer mass removal via solvent-induced dissolution.
- [ ] **Five steps:** Wetting → Diffusion → Swelling → Disentanglement → Mass removal.
- [ ] **Rate-limiting step:** Usually Step 2 (diffusion) for glassy polymers; Step 4 for rubbery.
- [ ] **Hildebrand rule:** |δ_p − δ_s| < 2 MPa^½ → **miscible → ablation occurs**.
- [ ] **Fick's Second Law:** ∂C/∂t = D(∂²C/∂x²) governs diffusion into polymer.
- [ ] **Fickian regime:** Rubbery polymer (T > Tg); D = constant; smooth ablation.
- [ ] **Case II regime:** Glassy polymer (T < Tg); sharp boundary; constant velocity.
- [ ] **Stress-cracking regime:** Glassy + applied stress + ESC-prone polymer/solvent pair.
- [ ] **Key formulas to memorize:** v = D/L; t = L²/(2D); ΔH_m = V_m φ₁φ₂(Δδ)²; χ₁₂ formula.
- [ ] **Applications:** Membranes, lithography, drug delivery, polymer recycling, surface texturing.
- [ ] **Plastics effect:** Solvent reduces Tg locally, making polymer more mobile.
- [ ] **Environmental impact:** Solvent recovery via distillation is essential in industrial setups.
- [ ] **Important units:** δ in MPa^½; D in m²/s; v in m/s; ΔH_m in J/mol.
- [ ] **Typical δ values to remember:** PS = 18.6, PMMA = 19.0, PE = 16.6, PP = 17.0, NMP = 22.9, acetone = 19.7, water = 47.8 MPa^½.
- [ ] **Critical threshold:** χ₁₂ < 0.5 → miscible → ablation favorable.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Strategy:** In any solvent ablation question, always:
> 1. Identify the polymer-solvent pair.
> 2. Compute Δδ (or check given χ₁₂).
> 3. Apply the Δδ < 2 MPa^½ rule (or χ₁₂ < 0.5 rule).
> 4. State the regime (Fickian / Case II / Stress-cracking).
> 5. If asked for kinetics, apply Fick's Laws.
> 6. Always show unit conversions explicitly — this is where KTU examiners award method marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thermal Desorption of Gases
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT752 - POLYMERS AND SEPARATION PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Thermal Desorption of Gases

# Thermal Desorption of Gases

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

**Thermal Desorption (TD)** is a physicochemical separation process in which previously adsorbed solute gases or volatile species are liberated from a solid sorbent matrix (or polymer surface) through the controlled application of heat, causing a reversal of the adsorption equilibrium. The process exploits the temperature dependence of the **adsorption isotherm**, shifting the equilibrium loading $q^*$ downward as the system temperature rises, thereby driving the desorbed species into the carrier gas (or vacuum) phase for downstream collection or analysis.

Mathematically, the driving force is expressed as the change in surface excess loading $q$ with respect to temperature at constant fluid-phase concentration $C$:

$$\left(\frac{\partial q}{\partial T}\right)_{C} < 0$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Thermal desorption is classified as a **temperature-swing adsorption (TSA)**-adjacent regeneration technique. Unlike Pressure-Swing Adsorption (PSA), thermal desorption uses **heat input** rather than pressure reduction as the primary intensive variable to reverse adsorption.

---

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **sponge soaked with perfume sitting in a warm oven**. The sponge (sorbent) holds the volatile fragrance molecules (adsorbate) on its inner surfaces through weak van der Waals forces. At room temperature, the perfume barely escapes. But when you heat the sponge, the molecules gain kinetic energy, the attractive forces are overcome, and the scent rushes out. The "perfume" is now separated and can be collected.

In industrial terms:
- The **sponge** = activated carbon, zeolite, or polymer adsorbent
- The **perfume** = target gas molecules (VOCs, hydrocarbons, contaminants)
- The **warm oven** = external heating coils, hot purge gas, or induction heater
- The **collected scent** = desorbed gas stream ready for condensation, recovery, or destruction

> [!NOTE]
> **Why not just use lower pressure (PSA)?** Because some strongly-bound species (high heat of adsorption, $> 50 \text{ kJ/mol}$) cannot be efficiently desorbed at reasonable vacuum levels. Heat provides a much stronger lever to break the sorbate–sorbent bond, especially for **heavy hydrocarbons**, **chlorinated VOCs**, and **monomers trapped in polymer films**.

---

### Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Range / Value | Units |
|---|---|---|
| Desorption temperature $T_d$ | $\mathbf{150 - 400}$ | °C |
| Heat of adsorption $\vert \Delta H_{ads} \vert$ | $\mathbf{20 - 100}$ | kJ/mol |
| Carrier gas flow rate | $\mathbf{0.5 - 5}$ | L/min |
| Heating ramp rate | $\mathbf{5 - 30}$ | °C/min |
| Typical sorbents | Activated carbon, Tenax, silica gel, polymer beads | — |
| Standard desorption efficiency | $\mathbf{> 95 \%}$ for volatiles | % |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Thermodynamic Basis — The Clausius–Clapeyron Connection

The fundamental thermodynamic justification for thermal desorption rests on the **Clausius–Clapeyron relation** applied to the adsorption–desorption phase equilibrium. For a sorbate in dilute concentration on a homogeneous surface, the equilibrium loading $q^*$ is related to temperature $T$ through:

$$\frac{d(\ln P)}{dT} = \frac{-\Delta H_{ads}}{R T^2}$$

Integrating between two temperature states $T_1$ and $T_2$:

$$\ln\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = \frac{\Delta H_{ads}}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)$$

Since $\Delta H_{ads}$ is negative (exothermic adsorption), increasing $T$ requires a **higher equilibrium partial pressure** to maintain the same loading $q^*$. Conversely, at constant $P$, raising $T$ drops $q^*$ — this is the desorption driving force.

---

## 2.2 Process Steps (Operational Logic)

The thermal desorption cycle, especially in fixed-bed adsorbers used in polymer/chemical plants, follows four canonical stages:

### Step 1 — Saturation / Loading Phase
- The sorbent bed is contacted with feed gas containing the target adsorbate.
- Breakthrough is monitored via outlet concentration $C_{out}$ vs time.
- The bed operates in **adsorption mode** until $C_{out} \approx C_{in} \times 0.05$.

### Step 2 — Pre-Heating / Bed Isolation
- Feed is stopped; valves isolate the bed.
- Carrier gas (often N₂ or He) begins flowing at low rate to prevent back-mixing.

### Step 3 — Heating Ramp + Desorption
- External heaters (electric jackets, steam coils, or hot oil) raise bed temperature.
- Purge gas sweeps desorbed species toward the condenser/collector.
- This is the **mass-transfer-limited desorption stage**.

### Step 4 — Cooling / Re-pressurization
- Bed is cooled back to adsorption temperature while purged with carrier.
- Ready for next adsorption cycle.

---

## 2.3 Modes of Thermal Desorption

| Mode | Heating Source | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **External convective** | Hot purge gas through bed | Fixed-bed TSA units |
| **Direct conductive** | Electrical heating jacket | Lab-scale TD tubes |
| **Microwave-assisted** | Dielectric heating | Volatile recovery from soils/polymers |
| **Vacuum Thermal Desorption (VTD)** | Heat + reduced pressure $<\mathbf{10 \text{ kPa}}$ | Trace VOC analysis, polymer outgassing |
| **Steam-assisted TD** | Superheated steam ($\mathbf{150 - 300 °C}$) | Activated-carbon regeneration in solvent recovery |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **VTD (Vacuum Thermal Desorption)** is the dominant technique in **analytical chemistry** and **polymer outgassing studies**, while **steam-assisted TD** dominates **industrial solvent recovery** units coupled with activated carbon.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are fair-game for KTU End-Semester Examination (ESE). The vertical bar symbol uses `\vert` to preserve table integrity.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $q^* = f(C, T)$ — Langmuir: $q^* = \dfrac{q_m b C}{1 + b C}$ | Equilibrium loading on sorbent | mol/kg |
| 2 | $b = b_0 \exp\!\left(\dfrac{-\Delta H_{ads}}{RT}\right)$ | Temperature dependence of Langmuir constant | m³/mol |
| 3 | $\ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = \dfrac{\Delta H_{ads}}{R}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T_2} - \dfrac{1}{T_1}\right)$ | Clausius–Clapeyron equilibrium shift | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\dfrac{dq}{dt} = k_d \left(q^* - q\right)$ | Linear driving force (LDF) desorption rate | mol/(kg·s) |
| 5 | $k_d = k_d^\circ \exp\!\left(\dfrac{-E_d}{RT}\right)$ | Arrhenius-type desorption rate constant | 1/s |
| 6 | $\rho_s C_{p,s} \dfrac{\partial T}{\partial t} = k_h \left(T_h - T\right) - \rho_s \Delta H_{ads} \dfrac{\partial q}{\partial t}$ | Energy balance on sorbent | W/m³ |
| 7 | $\eta_d = \dfrac{m_{des}}{m_{ads}} \times 100\%$ | Desorption efficiency | % |
| 8 | $D_{eff} \nabla^2 C = \varepsilon \dfrac{\partial C}{\partial t} + \rho_s \dfrac{\partial q}{\partial t}$ | Coupled diffusion in porous pellet | m²/s |
| 9 | $Nu = \dfrac{h L_c}{k_f}$ | Nusselt number for external heat transfer | dimensionless |
| 10 | $Bi_h = \dfrac{h r_p}{k_s}$ | Biot number for heat transfer inside pellet | dimensionless |

**Symbol Glossary:**
- $q^*$ = equilibrium loading, $q$ = actual loading, $q_m$ = monolayer capacity
- $C$ = bulk gas concentration, $P$ = partial pressure
- $\Delta H_{ads}$ = heat of adsorption (negative), $E_d$ = activation energy for desorption
- $k_d$ = LDF mass-transfer coefficient, $k_h$ = heat-transfer coefficient
- $\rho_s, C_{p,s}$ = sorbent bulk density and specific heat
- $D_{eff}$ = effective diffusivity in pellet, $\varepsilon$ = bed porosity

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Thermal desorption is the **workhorse regeneration step** in:

1. **Polymer film outgassing** — Removing residual monomers (styrene, vinyl chloride) and plasticizers before quality certification.
2. **Solvent recovery plants** — Steam-regeneration of activated carbon beds (e.g., benzene, toluene, acetone recovery in petrochemical complexes).
3. **Environmental remediation** — Treatment of contaminated soils with semi-volatile organic compounds.
4. **Analytical sample preparation** — GC-MS thermal desorption tubes (Tenax TA, Carbopack B).
5. **Air separation / hydrogen purification** — Pre-purification stages in air separation units (ASUs) using 13X zeolite TSA beds.
6. **Polymer devolatilization** — Ziegler–Natta catalyst removal from polypropylene powders using hot N₂ stripping (a form of thermal desorption).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Clausius–Clapeyron Equation for Adsorption Equilibrium

We derive the fundamental relation governing how the equilibrium gas pressure over a loaded sorbent changes with temperature.

### Starting Point — Clapeyron Equation

For any two-phase equilibrium:

$$\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H}{T \Delta V}$$

For adsorption, treat the sorbed phase as having negligible molar volume compared to the gas phase. Using ideal gas $V_g = RT/P$:

$$\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H_{ads} \cdot P}{R T^2}$$

### Rearranging

$$\frac{dP}{P} = \frac{\Delta H_{ads}}{R} \cdot \frac{dT}{T^2}$$

### Integrating from $(T_1, P_1)$ to $(T_2, P_2)$

$$\int_{P_1}^{P_2} \frac{dP}{P} = \frac{\Delta H_{ads}}{R} \int_{T_1}^{T_2} \frac{dT}{T^2}$$

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = \frac{\Delta H_{ads}}{R} \left[ -\frac{1}{T} \right]_{T_1}^{T_2}$$

### Final Result

$$\boxed{\;\ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = \frac{\Delta H_{ads}}{R} \left( \frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1} \right)\;}$$

> Since $\Delta H_{ads} < 0$, an increase in $T$ ($T_2 > T_1$) makes the RHS **negative**, hence $P_2 < P_1$ at constant loading. Conversely, at constant $P$, $q^*$ must **decrease** as $T$ rises. This is the heart of thermal desorption.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Linear Driving Force (LDF) Model for Desorption Rate

The LDF model is the workhorse for fixed-bed desorption calculations in KTU-style problems.

### Starting Point — Mass Balance on a Single Sorbent Particle

For a spherical pellet of radius $r_p$, the internal diffusion is governed by Fick's law. Integrating over the pellet volume gives the well-known **Glueckauf approximation**:

$$\frac{\partial \bar{q}}{\partial t} = \frac{15 D_{eff}}{r_p^2} (q^* - \bar{q})$$

Defining the LDF coefficient $k_d = 15 D_{eff}/r_p^2$:

$$\frac{d\bar{q}}{dt} = k_d (q^* - \bar{q})$$

### Temperature Dependence of $k_d$

Following Arrhenius-type activated desorption kinetics:

$$k_d = k_d^\circ \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_d}{RT}\right)$$

### Final LDF Desorption Rate Equation

$$\boxed{\;\frac{dq}{dt} = k_d^\circ \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_d}{RT}\right)\,(q^* - q)\;}$$

> This first-order ODE, coupled with the bed energy balance, yields the **breakthrough curve during desorption**, which is what a thermal-desorption engineer computes.

---

## 3.3 Derivation III — Bed Energy Balance (Non-Isothermal TSA)

For a fixed bed of sorbent undergoing thermal desorption, the lumped energy balance couples heat transfer with the heat sink of desorption (endothermic).

### Assumptions

1. Negligible axial conduction in solid.
2. Gas and solid at same local temperature (lumped).
3. Constant gas mass flow rate $G$.

### Governing Equation

$$\rho_s C_{p,s} \frac{\partial T}{\partial t} + G C_{p,g} \frac{\partial T}{\partial z} = h_v (T_w - T) + (-\Delta H_{ads}) \rho_s \frac{\partial q}{\partial t}$$

where $h_v$ is the volumetric heat-transfer coefficient (W/m³·K), and $T_w$ is the wall/heater temperature.

### Linearization

If $\Delta H_{ads}$ is large, the term $(-\Delta H_{ads}) \rho_s \partial q/\partial t$ dominates, and $T$ is approximated by solving:

$$\rho_s C_{p,s} \frac{dT}{dt} \approx (-\Delta H_{ads}) \rho_s \frac{dq}{dt} + h_v (T_w - T)$$

### Substituting the LDF rate

$$\boxed{\;\rho_s C_{p,s} \frac{dT}{dt} = (-\Delta H_{ads}) \rho_s k_d (q^* - q) + h_v (T_w - T)\;}$$

This coupled ODE system (with the LDF mass balance) is solved numerically for the **temperature–loading history** during the heating ramp of TSA.

---

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-style)

> **Problem:** A packed bed contains activated carbon loaded with toluene. The equilibrium loading at 25 °C and $P = 0.01$ atm is $q^* = 0.45$ mol/kg. The bed is heated to 200 °C. The Langmuir constant at 25 °C is $b = 50$ atm⁻¹, and $\Delta H_{ads} = -50$ kJ/mol. Find the new equilibrium loading at 200 °C, assuming $P$ is held at 0.01 atm (constant total pressure via purge).

### Step 1 — Compute $b$ at 200 °C (473 K)

$$\frac{b(T_2)}{b(T_1)} = \exp\!\left[\frac{-\Delta H_{ads}}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)\right]$$

$$= \exp\!\left[\frac{50000}{8.314}\left(\frac{1}{473} - \frac{1}{298}\right)\right]$$

$$= \exp\!\left[6014 \times (-1.244 \times 10^{-3})\right]$$

$$= \exp(-7.482) = 5.66 \times 10^{-4}$$

Therefore $b(473 \text{ K}) = 50 \times 5.66 \times 10^{-4} = 0.0283 \text{ atm}^{-1}$.

### Step 2 — Compute $q_m$ from initial state

At 25 °C, Langmuir:

$$q^* = \frac{q_m b C}{1 + b C}$$

$$0.45 = \frac{q_m \times 50 \times 0.01}{1 + 50 \times 0.01}$$

$$0.45 = \frac{0.5 \, q_m}{1.5} \implies q_m = 1.35 \text{ mol/kg}$$

### Step 3 — Compute $q^*$ at 200 °C

$$q^* = \frac{1.35 \times 0.0283 \times 0.01}{1 + 0.0283 \times 0.01}$$

$$= \frac{3.82 \times 10^{-4}}{1.000283} = 3.82 \times 10^{-4} \text{ mol/kg}$$

### Step 4 — Desorption Efficiency

$$\eta_d = \left(1 - \frac{3.82 \times 10^{-4}}{0.45}\right) \times 100\% = 99.92\%$$

> **Conclusion:** Heating from 25 °C to 200 °C essentially **completely removes** the toluene. This is why thermal desorption is so effective for VOCs.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Thermal Desorption Train

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[FeedGas with Adsorbate] --> B[Adsorption Bed Saturated]
    B --> C[Valve Isolation and Carrier Gas Switchover]
    C --> D[External Heater Activation Electrical or Steam]
    D --> E[Bed Temperature Ramp 25C to Target Td]
    E --> F[Desorption Front Sweeps Through Bed]
    F --> G[Hot Carrier Gas with Released Species]
    G --> H[Condenser or Cryotrap Collection]
    H --> I[Recovered Solvent or Analyte]
    H --> J[Treated Carrier Recycle to Vent or Reuse]
    E --> K[Cooling Stage with Inert Purge]
    K --> L[Bed Ready for Next Adsorption Cycle]
    L --> A
```

## 4.2 Schematic — Coupled Mass and Energy Flows in a TSA Bed

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ThermalDesorptionColumn [TSA Fixed Bed Reactor]
        direction TB
        M1[Inlet Purge Gas N2 at Tpurge] --> M2[Heater Zone Twall rises]
        M2 --> M3[Desorption Front Moving at Velocity vfront]
        M3 --> M4[Loaded Section q still high T low]
        M4 --> M5[Clean Section q near zero T high]
        M5 --> M6[Outlet Gas with Released VOC]
        M2 -. heat flux qw .-> M3
        M3 -. endothermic sink .-> M2
    end
    M6 --> N1[Cold Trap at minus 50C]
    N1 --> N2[Liquid VOC Recovery]
    N1 --> N3[Vent or Afterburner]
```

## 4.3 Process Flow — Comparison of TD Modes

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Choose Desorption Mode] --> Q1{Is sample trace-level analytical?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Mode1[Vacuum Thermal Desorption VTD with GC-MS coupling]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Is sorbent activated carbon in industry?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> Mode2[Steam-Assisted Thermal Desorption]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Is heat-sensitive polymer involved?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> Mode3[Low-Temp TD with Microwave or Dielectric Heating]
    Q3 -- No --> Mode4[Standard Hot-Purge TSA with Electric Heaters]
    Mode1 --> Out[Output: Recovered Analyte]
    Mode2 --> Out
    Mode3 --> Out
    Mode4 --> Out
```

## 4.4 Conceptual Map — Driving Forces in Thermal Desorption

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Thermal Desorption System] --> B[Heat Input Q]
    A --> C[Mass Transfer Coefficient kd]
    A --> D[Carrier Gas Velocity uc]
    B --> E[Temperature Rise delta T]
    E --> F[Equilibrium Shift down arrow in qstar]
    C --> G[Rate of Loading Reduction dq by dt]
    D --> H[Convective Sweep of Desorbed Species]
    F --> G
    G --> I[Desorbed Flux to Gas Phase]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Recovery or Destruction Downstream]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question A1

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**

> Define thermal desorption. State two industrial applications where it is preferred over pressure-swing adsorption.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Thermal desorption is a separation process in which adsorbed species are removed from a solid sorbent by heating, exploiting the temperature dependence of adsorption equilibrium so that the surface loading decreases with rising temperature, releasing the adsorbate into a carrier gas or vacuum for collection.

**Applications (1 mark — any two):**
1. Recovery of organic solvents (benzene, acetone) from activated carbon in petrochemical plants using steam-assisted regeneration.
2. Removal of residual monomers and VOCs from polymer powders/films in polymer finishing operations.
3. Pre-purification of air in cryogenic air separation using 13X zeolite TSA beds.

---

### Question A2

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question]**

> The heat of adsorption of CO₂ on 5A zeolite is $-35$ kJ/mol. A bed loaded at 30 °C is heated to 200 °C. Will the equilibrium loading increase or decrease? Justify using the Clausius–Clapeyron relation.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Since $\Delta H_{ads} = -35$ kJ/mol (negative, exothermic), the Clausius–Clapeyron equation gives:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = \frac{\Delta H_{ads}}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right) = \frac{-35000}{8.314}\left(\frac{1}{473} - \frac{1}{303}\right) = -4.21 \times (-1.19\times10^{-3}) \approx 5.0 \times 10^{-3}$$

Hence $P_2 > P_1$ at the same loading — the system requires a **higher equilibrium pressure** to retain CO₂ at 200 °C. At fixed purge-gas pressure, the **equilibrium loading $q^*$ decreases**, confirming that CO₂ is desorbed by heating.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

### QUESTION A — Track 1 (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

> Derive the Clausius–Clapeyron equation for adsorption equilibrium. Starting from the Clapeyron equation, obtain the expression that shows how the equilibrium loading shifts with temperature. Comment on the role of the heat of adsorption in determining the required desorption temperature.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (2 marks) — Clapeyron Equation:**
For two-phase equilibrium between a sorbed phase (negligible molar volume) and an ideal gas:

$$\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H_{ads}}{T \Delta V} \approx \frac{\Delta H_{ads} \cdot P}{R T^2}$$

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Rearrangement and Integration:**

$$\int_{P_1}^{P_2} \frac{dP}{P} = \frac{\Delta H_{ads}}{R} \int_{T_1}^{T_2} \frac{dT}{T^2}$$

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = \frac{\Delta H_{ads}}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)$$

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Interpretation:**
At constant $P$, raising $T$ lowers $q^*$. At constant $q$, raising $T$ raises the equilibrium gas-phase pressure $P^*$.

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Role of $\Delta H_{ads}$:**
A more negative $\Delta H_{ads}$ (stronger adsorption) demands a **larger temperature swing** to achieve the same fractional drop in $q^*$. For weakly adsorbed gases ($\vert \Delta H_{ads} \vert < 30$ kJ/mol), mild heating ($< 100$ °C) suffices; for heavy VOCs ($\vert \Delta H_{ads} \vert > 60$ kJ/mol), $T_d > 250$ °C is typical.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

> A 50 kg activated-carbon bed is loaded with 18 kg of acetone at 25 °C. It is regenerated using hot nitrogen at 150 °C. The LDF desorption constant at 150 °C is $k_d = 2.5 \times 10^{-3}$ s⁻¹. The gas-phase equilibrium loading at 150 °C is $q^* = 0.02$ mol/kg. The initial loading on the bed is $q_0 = 6.13$ mol/kg (corresponding to the 18 kg of acetone, M = 58 g/mol). 
> 
> (i) Write the LDF desorption equation and solve for $q(t)$.
> (ii) Estimate the time required to desorb 95% of the acetone.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) (3 marks) Equation and solution:**

The LDF model:

$$\frac{dq}{dt} = k_d (q^* - q)$$

Separating variables:

$$\int_{q_0}^{q} \frac{dq}{q^* - q} = k_d \int_0^t dt$$

$$-\ln\!\left(\frac{q^* - q}{q^* - q_0}\right) = k_d \, t$$

Solving for $q(t)$:

$$q(t) = q^* - (q^* - q_0) e^{-k_d t}$$

Substituting values:

$$q(t) = 0.02 - (0.02 - 6.13) e^{-2.5 \times 10^{-3} t}$$

$$\boxed{q(t) = 0.02 + 6.11 \, e^{-2.5 \times 10^{-3} t} \;\; \text{mol/kg}}$$

**(ii) (4 marks) Time for 95% desorption:**

We need $q(t) = q_0 \times 0.05 = 0.307$ mol/kg.

$$0.307 = 0.02 + 6.11 \, e^{-2.5 \times 10^{-3} t}$$

$$e^{-2.5 \times 10^{-3} t} = \frac{0.287}{6.11} = 0.04697$$

$$-2.5 \times 10^{-3} \, t = \ln(0.04697) = -3.058$$

$$\boxed{t = 1223 \text{ s} \approx 20.4 \text{ min}}$$

---

### QUESTION B — Track 2 (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 3]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

> Compare Thermal Desorption (TD), Pressure-Swing Adsorption (PSA), and Steam-Stripping Regeneration of activated carbon. Discuss (i) the intensive variable used to drive desorption, (ii) the typical energy requirement per kg of sorbate, and (iii) one industrial application uniquely suited to each.

**Model Solution:**

| Aspect | TD (Thermal) | PSA (Pressure-Swing) | Steam Stripping |
|---|---|---|---|
| (i) Intensive variable | Temperature rise $\Delta T$ | Pressure reduction $\Delta P$ | Steam partial pressure + temperature |
| (ii) Energy per kg sorbate | High (2000–5000 kJ/kg) | Low (200–800 kJ/kg) | Moderate (latent heat of steam) |
| (iii) Unique application | VOC removal from polymer powders | Medical O₂ concentrators, H₂ purification | Recovery of acetone/IPA from carbon beds |

**Detailed comparison (any 3 distinguishing points):**
1. **Energy:** TD has highest energy demand because it must heat the entire sorbent mass; PSA is cheapest because only a vacuum pump or blower is used.
2. **Cycle time:** TD cycles are slow (hours), PSA cycles are fast (seconds to minutes).
3. **Selectivity:** Steam stripping offers co-distillation advantage — useful when sorbate is miscible with water (alcohols, ketones); TD with N₂ is preferred for water-sensitive sorbates.
4. **Equipment:** TD needs heaters + heat exchangers; PSA needs only valves + vacuum pump; steam stripping needs boiler + condenser.

(7 marks distributed across the table + 3–4 sentences of comparative discussion.)

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

> A polymer film is being outgassed in a vacuum thermal desorption (VTD) unit at 5 mbar and 120 °C. The principal contaminant is residual styrene (M = 104 g/mol) with an initial surface concentration of $0.8$ mg/m² on a film area of 200 m². The desorption follows first-order kinetics with $k_d = 1.2 \times 10^{-3}$ s⁻¹ at 120 °C. Estimate (i) the total moles of styrene released, (ii) the time for 90% desorption, and (iii) the peak desorption flux.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Total moles (2 marks):**

$$n = \frac{m}{M} = \frac{0.8 \times 10^{-3} \times 200}{104} = \frac{0.160}{104} = 1.538 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mol}$$

**(ii) Time for 90% desorption (3 marks):**

For first-order desorption: $q(t) = q_0 e^{-k_d t}$.

At 90% desorption, $q(t) = 0.10 \, q_0$:

$$0.10 = e^{-1.2 \times 10^{-3} t} \implies t = \frac{\ln 10}{1.2 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{2.303}{1.2 \times 10^{-3}} = 1919 \text{ s} \approx 32 \text{ min}$$

**(iii) Peak desorption flux (2 marks):**

Peak flux occurs at $t = 0$:

$$J_0 = k_d \cdot q_0 = 1.2 \times 10^{-3} \times \frac{1.538 \times 10^{-3}}{200 \times 10^{-4} \times 10^{-3}} \;\; \text{(recompute per unit area)}$$

Per unit area initial loading: $q_0 = 0.8$ mg/m² = $7.69 \times 10^{-6}$ mol/m².

$$J_0 = 1.2 \times 10^{-3} \times 7.69 \times 10^{-6} = 9.23 \times 10^{-9} \;\; \text{mol/(m²·s)}$$

Convert to mass flux: $9.23 \times 10^{-9} \times 104 = 9.6 \times 10^{-7}$ kg/(m²·s) = $0.96$ mg/(m²·s).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Sign errors in Clausius–Clapeyron:** Students often drop the negative sign of $\Delta H_{ads}$ and get the wrong direction of shift. Always retain $\Delta H_{ads} < 0$.
> 2. **Confusing $q^*$ and $q$:** In the LDF equation, $q^*$ is the *current* equilibrium loading (which itself changes with $T$), not a constant.
> 3. **Forgetting the activation energy term:** The LDF constant $k_d$ varies with temperature via Arrhenius; using a single value across a temperature ramp gives a non-conservative answer.
> 4. **Dimension mixing:** Always convert mass loading to mol/kg before applying Langmuir.
> 5. **Skipping the "Why" in Part (a):** KTU awards 1–2 marks for the engineering interpretation, not just the derivation. Always close with one sentence of physical meaning.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Thermal Desorption of Gases**

- ☐ **Definition:** Reversal of adsorption by heating; classified as a Temperature-Swing process.
- ☐ **Driving force:** $\left(\dfrac{\partial q}{\partial T}\right)_C < 0$ — loading drops as temperature rises.
- ☐ **Master equation:** Clausius–Clapeyron — $\ln(P_2/P_1) = \dfrac{\Delta H_{ads}}{R}\left(\dfrac{1}{T_2} - \dfrac{1}{T_1}\right)$.
- ☐ **$\Delta H_{ads}$ is negative** — exothermic adsorption; magnitude dictates $T_d$ needed.
- ☐ **LDF rate law:** $\dfrac{dq}{dt} = k_d (q^* - q)$ with $k_d = k_d^\circ \exp(-E_d/RT)$.
- ☐ **Langmuir isotherm:** $q^* = \dfrac{q_m b C}{1 + b C}$; $b$ decreases exponentially with $T$.
- ☐ **TD modes:** External convective, VTD, microwave-assisted, steam-assisted.
- ☐ **Industrial applications:** Solvent recovery (steam TD), polymer outgassing (VTD), air prepurification (TSA with 13X), environmental remediation.
- ☐ **Energy balance couples to mass balance** via the endothermic desorption term $(-\Delta H_{ads}) \rho_s \partial q/\partial t$.
- ☐ **Desorption efficiency** $\eta_d = (m_{des}/m_{ads}) \times 100\%$; values $> 95\%$ are routine for VOCs.
- ☐ **Biot number** $Bi_h = h r_p / k_s$ controls whether internal heat conduction is rate-limiting.
- ☐ **Pitfall:** Always retain the sign of $\Delta H_{ads}$; do not confuse $q$ (actual) with $q^*$ (equilibrium).
- ☐ **Comparison memory trick:** TD = heat, PSA = vacuum, Steam = latent-heat-plus-mass-action.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Activated Carbon Solute Recovery
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT752 - POLYMERS AND SEPARATION PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Activated Carbon Solute Recovery

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Activated Carbon (AC)** is a highly porous, amorphous carbonaceous adsorbent material characterized by an exceptionally high internal surface area (typically **500 – 1500 m²/g**) and a sophisticated pore network comprising micropores (< 2 nm), mesopores (2 – 50 nm), and macropores (> 50 nm). It is manufactured through the controlled pyrolysis and activation of carbonaceous precursors such as coconut shells, coal, wood, lignite, or peat.

**Solute Recovery** refers to the engineered industrial process by which a dissolved target species (solute) is selectively extracted from a bulk liquid or gaseous stream using activated carbon as the solid adsorbent phase, followed by a controlled **desorption (regeneration) step** that liberates the concentrated solute for downstream collection, reuse, or disposal.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The process is fundamentally a *cyclic batch adsorption–desorption operation* classified under **equilibrium-stage separation processes**, governed by physical adsorption (physisorption) via van der Waals forces, with the working capacity defined by the *difference* between the loading and the regeneration equilibrium loadings.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you spilled a drop of perfume in your living room. The smell (solute molecules) spreads through the air. Now, if you place a bowl of activated charcoal near the spill, the charcoal acts like a **microscopic honeycomb sponge** — millions of tiny caves (pores) on its surface physically trap and hold the perfume molecules, purifying the air.

To **recover** the perfume, you would either:
1. **Heat** the charcoal (thermal swing) — the molecules gain energy and escape back into a small concentrated stream, OR
2. **Rinse** it with a clean solvent (solvent swing) — the perfume molecules prefer the fresh solvent and leave the charcoal.

This "trap-and-release" mechanism is exactly what happens in industrial **Activated Carbon Solute Recovery**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why activated carbon and not ordinary charcoal?** Ordinary charcoal has a surface area of only ~50 m²/g. Activation (using steam, CO₂, or chemical agents like ZnCl₂ or H₃PO₄) dramatically etches and creates a vast internal pore network, multiplying the surface area up to **30×**, making it the most widely used industrial adsorbent globally.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Metrics (Bold for Memorization)

| Metric | Typical Industrial Value |
|---|---|
| BET Surface Area | **500 – 1500 m²/g** |
| Iodine Number | **600 – 1200 mg/g** |
| Methylene Blue Number | **100 – 300 mg/g** |
| Bulk Density | **0.35 – 0.55 g/cm³** |
| Particle Size (granular) | **0.6 – 4.0 mm** |
| Operating pH range | **4 – 9** |
| Regeneration temperature (thermal) | **100 – 900 °C** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Steps of Activated Carbon Solute Recovery

The complete solute recovery cycle consists of **three sequential stages**:

### Stage A — Adsorption (Loading)
- The feed solution (or gas) contacts the activated carbon bed (fixed-bed, fluidized-bed, or slurry contactor).
- Solute molecules migrate from the bulk phase to the external surface of the carbon particle.
- They then diffuse through the macropores → mesopores → micropores (intraparticle diffusion).
- Once inside the micropores, they physically adsorb onto the carbon surface.
- The exit stream becomes depleted in solute (purified effluent).

### Stage B — Saturation / Breakthrough
- As the carbon approaches its equilibrium loading $q^*$, the bed effluent concentration begins to rise.
- The **breakpoint** (typically $C/C_0 = 0.05$ to $0.10$) signals the need to switch beds.
- The **exhaustion point** ($C/C_0 \approx 0.95$) defines when the bed must be regenerated.

### Stage C — Regeneration (Desorption)
- The loaded carbon is treated to release the adsorbed solute.
- Common methods: **Thermal Swing Adsorption (TSA)**, **Pressure Swing Adsorption (PSA)**, **Solvent Extraction**, **Steam Stripping**, or **Chemical Regeneration** (pH swing).
- The desorbed solute is collected in a concentrated stream for recovery.

## 2.2 Adsorption Equilibrium — The Two Pillars

The equilibrium loading $q$ (kg solute / kg carbon) as a function of bulk concentration $C$ is the single most important design variable. Two isotherms dominate KTU problems:

### (i) Langmuir Isotherm
Assumes **monolayer adsorption** on a **homogeneous surface** with **finite adsorption sites**.

$$q = \frac{q_m K_L C}{1 + K_L C}$$

where:
- $q$ = solid-phase loading (kg/kg or mg/g)
- $q_m$ = maximum monolayer capacity (kg/kg)
- $K_L$ = Langmuir equilibrium constant (m³/kg or L/mg)
- $C$ = equilibrium liquid-phase concentration

### (ii) Freundlich Isotherm
An **empirical** relation accounting for **heterogeneous surfaces**:

$$q = K_F C^{1/n}$$

where:
- $K_F$ = Freundlich capacity constant
- $1/n$ = intensity parameter (typically $0.1 < 1/n < 1.0$)

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| Equation | Mathematical Form | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Langmuir Isotherm | $q = q_m K_L C / (1 + K_L C)$ | mg/g, mg/L | Monolayer, homogeneous surface |
| Langmuir (linear form) | $C/q = 1/(q_m K_L) + C/q_m$ | — | Plot to extract $q_m$, $K_L$ |
| Freundlich Isotherm | $q = K_F C^{1/n}$ | mg/g, (mg/L)¹/ⁿ | Heterogeneous, multilayer |
| Freundlich (linear form) | $\log q = \log K_F + (1/n)\log C$ | — | Plot to extract $K_F$, $n$ |
| Separation Factor | $R_L = 1/(1 + K_L C_0)$ | dimensionless | Isotherm favorability check |
| Bed Capacity | $q_{bed} = \rho_b \cdot q^* \cdot V_{bed}$ | kg | Total solute held in bed |
| Mass Balance | $W (q^* - q_0) = L (C_0 - C)$ | kg | Operating line derivation |
| Regeneration Ratio | $RG = q_{reg} / q_{sat}$ | — | Cycle working capacity |
| Working Capacity | $\Delta q = q_{ads} - q_{des}$ | kg/kg | True cyclic capacity |
| Bed Height (EBCT) | $EBCT = L_{bed} / u_{s}$ | s/min | Empty Bed Contact Time |

> [!NOTE]
> **Separation Factor $R_L$:** If $0 < R_L < 1$, adsorption is **favorable**. If $R_L > 1$, adsorption is **unfavorable**. If $R_L = 1$, it is **linear**.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Water Treatment Plants** — removal of taste, odor, color, and trace organics (e.g., pesticides, VOCs).
- **Pharmaceutical Industry** — recovery of antibiotics, vitamins, and fermentation products.
- **Gold Recovery** — activated carbon is used in **CIP (Carbon-in-Pulp)** and **CIL (Carbon-in-Leach)** processes in mining.
- **Air Purification** — solvent recovery, gas masks, HVAC systems, biogas upgrading.
- **Sugar Refining** — decolorization of cane sugar syrups.
- **Food & Beverage** — decaffeination of coffee, juice clarification.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Langmuir Adsorption Isotherm

The Langmuir isotherm is derived from **four fundamental postulates**:

1. The surface possesses a **fixed number of adsorption sites**.
2. All sites are **energetically equivalent** (homogeneous surface).
3. Each site can hold **only one adsorbate molecule** (monolayer).
4. There is **no interaction** between adsorbed molecules on adjacent sites.

### Let us define:
- $\theta$ = fraction of surface covered by adsorbate
- $1 - \theta$ = fraction of surface that remains vacant
- $C$ = bulk-phase concentration of adsorbate

### Step 1 — Rate of Adsorption
The rate at which molecules adsorb is proportional to the gas/liquid concentration and the fraction of vacant sites:

$$r_{ads} = k_a C (1 - \theta)$$

### Step 2 — Rate of Desorption
The rate at which molecules leave the surface is proportional to the fraction of occupied sites:

$$r_{des} = k_d \theta$$

### Step 3 — Equilibrium Condition
At equilibrium, the rate of adsorption equals the rate of desorption:

$$k_a C (1 - \theta) = k_d \theta$$

### Step 4 — Rearrangement to Solve for $\theta$

$$\theta = \frac{k_a C}{k_d + k_a C}$$

### Step 5 — Introduce the Equilibrium Constant
Defining $K_L = k_a / k_d$, we obtain:

$$\theta = \frac{K_L C}{1 + K_L C}$$

### Step 6 — Convert to Loading Form
Since the actual loading $q$ is directly proportional to the surface coverage $\theta$:

$$q = q_m \theta = \frac{q_m K_L C}{1 + K_L C}$$

$$\boxed{\,q = \frac{q_m K_L C}{1 + K_L C}\,}$$

This is the canonical **Langmuir Isotherm Equation** required in every KTU derivation.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Linearized Langmuir Form (for Plotting)

Starting from:

$$\frac{q}{C} = \frac{q_m K_L}{1 + K_L C}$$

Inverting both sides:

$$\frac{C}{q} = \frac{1 + K_L C}{q_m K_L}$$

$$\frac{C}{q} = \frac{1}{q_m K_L} + \frac{C}{q_m}$$

This yields the **linearized form**:

$$\boxed{\,\frac{C}{q} = \frac{1}{q_m K_L} + \frac{1}{q_m} \cdot C\,}$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Trick:** When experimental data is given, plot $C/q$ vs $C$. The slope gives $1/q_m$ and the intercept gives $1/(q_m K_L)$. Always mention the units when quoting values.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Separation Factor $R_L$

$ R_L $ is a dimensionless constant that characterizes the **shape** of the Langmuir isotherm.

The essential feature of the Langmuir equation is that as $C \to \infty$, $q \to q_m$. Conversely, when $C$ is very small, the equation reduces to Henry's law:

$$q \approx q_m K_L C$$

The separation factor is defined as:

$$R_L = \frac{1}{1 + K_L C_0}$$

| Value of $R_L$ | Type of Isotherm |
|---|---|
| $R_L > 1$ | Unfavorable |
| $R_L = 1$ | Linear |
| $0 < R_L < 1$ | Favorable |
| $R_L = 0$ | Irreversible |

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

### Problem Statement
A fixed-bed activated carbon contactor is used to recover phenol from industrial wastewater. The feed concentration is $C_0 = 200$ mg/L. Equilibrium data follow the Langmuir isotherm with $q_m = 250$ mg/g and $K_L = 0.025$ L/mg. The bed contains $W = 50$ kg of activated carbon with bulk density $\rho_b = 450$ kg/m³.

**(a)** Calculate the maximum equilibrium loading $q^*$.
**(b)** Determine whether adsorption is favorable.
**(c)** Calculate the total phenol removed at equilibrium.

### Solution

**Part (a) — Maximum Loading**

$$q^* = \frac{q_m K_L C_0}{1 + K_L C_0}$$

$$q^* = \frac{(250)(0.025)(200)}{1 + (0.025)(200)}$$

$$q^* = \frac{1250}{1 + 5}$$

$$q^* = \frac{1250}{6}$$

$$\boxed{q^* = 208.33 \text{ mg/g}}$$

**Part (b) — Separation Factor**

$$R_L = \frac{1}{1 + K_L C_0} = \frac{1}{1 + (0.025)(200)} = \frac{1}{6}$$

$$\boxed{R_L = 0.1667}$$

Since $0 < R_L < 1$, the adsorption is **favorable**.

**Part (c) — Total Phenol Removed**

$$M_{removed} = q^* \times W$$

$$M_{removed} = 208.33 \times 10^{-3} \text{ g/g} \times 50{,}000 \text{ g}$$

$$M_{removed} = 10{,}416.5 \text{ g}$$

$$\boxed{M_{removed} \approx 10.42 \text{ kg of phenol}}$$

---

## 3.5 Freundlich Parameter Determination Example

### Given:
The following equilibrium data were obtained for dye adsorption onto activated carbon:

| $C$ (mg/L) | $q$ (mg/g) |
|---|---|
| 10 | 1.8 |
| 25 | 3.6 |
| 50 | 5.9 |
| 100 | 9.4 |
| 200 | 14.7 |

### Solution — Apply Linearized Form

$$\log q = \log K_F + \frac{1}{n} \log C$$

| $\log C$ | $\log q$ |
|---|---|
| 1.000 | 0.255 |
| 1.398 | 0.556 |
| 1.699 | 0.771 |
| 2.000 | 0.973 |
| 2.301 | 1.167 |

Using linear regression:
- Slope $= 1/n \approx 0.625$
- Intercept $= \log K_F \approx -0.370$

Therefore:
$$K_F = 10^{-0.370} \approx 0.427 \text{ (mg/g)(L/mg)}^{1/n}$$

$$\boxed{K_F \approx 0.43, \quad n \approx 1.60}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Since $1 < n < 10$, the adsorption is **favorable**. KTU examiners often ask the student to comment on the favorability from the value of $n$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Block Diagram — Activated Carbon Solute Recovery System

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **functional architecture** of a complete two-bed activated carbon solute recovery unit with thermal swing regeneration.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[FEED STREAM C0] --> B[INLET HEADER]
    B --> C[CARBON BED 1 ADSORPTION]
    C --> D{PURIFIED EFFLUENT C exit LOW}
    D -->|Yes| E[TREATED WATER OUTLET]
    D -->|No Bed Exhausted| F[VALVE SWITCH]
    F --> G[CARBON BED 2 ADSORPTION]
    G --> E
    C -->|Spent Carbon| H[REGENERATION SECTION]
    H --> I[STEAM HEATER 100 TO 900 C]
    I --> J[CONDENSER]
```
