```mermaid
    J --> K[RECOVERED SOLUTE CONCENTRATED PRODUCT]
    K --> L[STORAGE TANK]
    H -->|Regenerated Carbon| M[COOLING ZONE]
    M --> N[REACTIVATED CARBON RETURN TO BED 1]
    N --> C
```

## 4.2 Detailed Functional Block Diagram — Regeneration Subsystem

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LOADED_CARBON_IN
        SP[Spent Carbon from Bed]
    end
    SP --> ST[Steam Stripping Column]
    ST --> HX[Heat Exchanger Preheater]
    HX --> FR[Furnace 600 C]
    FR --> RG[Regeneration Reactor]
    RG --> QN[Quench Cooler]
    QN --> CL[Clean Carbon Storage Hopper]
    CL --> RT[Return to Adsorption Bed]
    RG --> CN[Condenser]
    CN --> DT[Decanter Phase Separator]
    DT --> PR[Pure Solute Product]
    DT --> W[Water Recycle]
```

## 4.3 Comparative Architecture — TSA vs PSA vs Solvent Swing

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Selection of Regeneration Method] --> B{Type of Solute}
    B -->|Volatile Organics| C[Thermal Swing Adsorption TSA]
    B -->|Gases e.g. CO2 CH4| D[Pressure Swing Adsorption PSA]
    B -->|Heat Sensitive Compounds| E[Solvent Extraction Swing]
    C --> C1[100 to 900 C]
    C --> C2[Low energy for trace VOCs]
    D --> D1[High Pressure to Low Pressure]
    D --> D2[Fast cycles seconds]
    E --> E1[Low temperature]
    E --> E2[Selectivity via solvent choice]
```

## 4.4 Adsorption Isotherm Curve Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ISOTHERM_FAMILY
        A1[Low KL Unfavorable] --> A2[Concave Upward]
        B1[KL Intermediate] --> B2[Linear]
        C1[High KL Favorable] --> C3[Concave Downward]
    end
```

## 4.5 Breakthrough Curve Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Time t0 Inlet to Bed] --> B[All Solute Adsorbed C exit 0]
    B --> C[Mass Transfer Zone MTZ Travels Down Bed]
    C --> D[Breakpoint C exit equals 0.05 C0]
    D --> E[Steeper Rise in S-shaped Breakthrough Curve]
    E --> F[Exhaustion C exit equals 0.95 C0]
    F --> G[Switch Bed Begin Regeneration]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Mermaid Diagrams:** Each block represents a *process unit operation*. The arrows represent *mass or material flows*. The decision diamonds (e.g., $C_{exit}$ low?) represent *process logic* used in automated control schemes.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**List any four desirable properties of activated carbon that make it suitable for industrial solute recovery applications.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Extremely high internal surface area** (500 – 1500 m²/g), providing abundant adsorption sites.
2. **Highly developed pore structure** with controlled distribution of micropores, mesopores, and macropores.
3. **High adsorption capacity** for a wide range of organic and inorganic species.
4. **Chemically inert** and mechanically stable across a broad pH (4 – 9) and temperature range.
5. **Easily regenerable**, allowing multiple adsorption-desorption cycles with minimal capacity loss.

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between physical adsorption and chemisorption.**

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Physisorption | Chemisorption |
|---|---|---|
| Nature of bonding | Van der Waals forces | Covalent / ionic bonds |
| Enthalpy of adsorption | 20 – 40 kJ/mol | 80 – 400 kJ/mol |
| Specificity | Non-specific | Highly specific |
| Reversibility | Easily reversible | Often irreversible |
| Activation energy | Low | High |
| Layers | Multilayer possible | Strictly monolayer |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Derive the Langmuir adsorption isotherm from first principles. State all assumptions clearly. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The derivation is based on the kinetic equilibrium between adsorption and desorption rates.

**Assumptions:**
1. Fixed number of identical adsorption sites.
2. Homogeneous surface (all sites energetically equivalent).
3. Monolayer coverage.
4. No interaction between adjacent adsorbed molecules.

**Derivation:**

Rate of adsorption is proportional to the concentration of solute and the fraction of vacant sites:

$$r_{ads} = k_a C (1 - \theta)$$

Rate of desorption is proportional to the fraction of occupied sites:

$$r_{des} = k_d \theta$$

At equilibrium $r_{ads} = r_{des}$:

$$k_a C (1 - \theta) = k_d \theta$$

Solving for $\theta$:

$$\theta = \frac{k_a C}{k_d + k_a C}$$

Defining $K_L = k_a / k_d$:

$$\theta = \frac{K_L C}{1 + K_L C}$$

Since $q = q_m \theta$, we obtain:

$$\boxed{q = \frac{q_m K_L C}{1 + K_L C}}$$

This is the Langmuir isotherm equation.

**(b)** The following data were obtained in an experiment to study the adsorption of acetic acid on activated carbon at 25 °C. Verify whether the data fit the Langmuir isotherm and determine the constants $q_m$ and $K_L$. **(7 marks)**

| $C$ (mmol/L) | $q$ (mmol/g) |
|---|---|
| 1.0 | 0.230 |
| 2.5 | 0.456 |
| 5.0 | 0.700 |
| 10.0 | 0.940 |
| 25.0 | 1.190 |

**Model Solution:**

Use the linearized form:

$$\frac{C}{q} = \frac{1}{q_m K_L} + \frac{C}{q_m}$$

| $C$ | $q$ | $C/q$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1.0 | 0.230 | 4.348 |
| 2.5 | 0.456 | 5.482 |
| 5.0 | 0.700 | 7.143 |
| 10.0 | 0.940 | 10.638 |
| 25.0 | 1.190 | 21.008 |

Performing linear regression on $C/q$ vs $C$:

Slope $\approx 0.667$ → $1/q_m = 0.667$ → $\boxed{q_m = 1.500 \text{ mmol/g}}$

Intercept $\approx 3.85$ → $1/(q_m K_L) = 3.85$ → $\boxed{K_L = 0.173 \text{ L/mmol}}$

The data fit the Langmuir isotherm well (linearity confirmed by high $R^2$).

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks)

**(a)** With neat sketches, explain the working of a **Thermal Swing Adsorption (TSA)** cycle for activated carbon solute recovery. Mention the engineering advantages and limitations. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Principle:** TSA exploits the exothermic nature of adsorption. Adsorption is carried out at low temperature; desorption (regeneration) is forced by heating the bed to 100 – 900 °C with steam or hot inert gas.

**Cycle Steps:**
1. **Adsorption:** Feed at 25 – 40 °C; solute adsorbs onto carbon.
2. **Hot Regeneration:** Steam at 100 – 900 °C passes through the bed; adsorbed solute desorbs.
3. **Cooling:** Bed is cooled by ambient air or water to adsorption temperature.
4. **Re-pressurization (if needed) and Standby.**

**Advantages:**
- High working capacity (large swing).
- Suitable for strongly adsorbed species (high $K_L$).
- Product is highly concentrated (low desorbent volume).
- Mature, well-established technology.

**Limitations:**
- High energy consumption (heat + steam).
- Long cycle times (hours).
- Not suitable for thermally labile solutes (e.g., proteins).
- Possible thermal degradation of carbon at very high T.

**(b)** A wastewater stream of $10$ m³/h containing $50$ mg/L of an organic pollutant is to be treated using a fixed-bed activated carbon adsorber. The Langmuir parameters are $q_m = 200$ mg/g and $K_L = 0.05$ L/mg. The bed contains $100$ kg of carbon. Estimate:
   (i) The equilibrium loading at the inlet concentration.
   (ii) The breakthrough time if the bed operates at 50 % of equilibrium loading.
   (iii) Total pollutant mass adsorbed per cycle. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Equilibrium loading at $C_0 = 50$ mg/L:**

$$q^* = \frac{q_m K_L C_0}{1 + K_L C_0} = \frac{(200)(0.05)(50)}{1 + (0.05)(50)}$$

$$q^* = \frac{500}{1 + 2.5} = \frac{500}{3.5}$$

$$\boxed{q^* = 142.86 \text{ mg/g}}$$

**(ii) Breakthrough time at 50 % saturation:**

Operating loading: $q_{op} = 0.50 \times 142.86 = 71.43$ mg/g

Mass of solute adsorbed:
$$M_{ads} = q_{op} \times W = 71.43 \times 10^{-3} \text{ g/g} \times 100{,}000 \text{ g} = 7143 \text{ g} = 7.143 \text{ kg}$$

Mass flow rate of pollutant in feed:
$$\dot{M}_{feed} = 10 \text{ m}^3/\text{h} \times 50 \text{ mg/L} = 10 \times 50 \text{ g/h} = 500 \text{ g/h}$$

Breakthrough time:
$$t_B = \frac{M_{ads}}{\dot{M}_{feed}} = \frac{7143 \text{ g}}{500 \text{ g/h}}$$

$$\boxed{t_B \approx 14.29 \text{ hours}}$$

**(iii) Total pollutant adsorbed per cycle:**
$$\boxed{M_{cycle} = 7.143 \text{ kg per cycle}}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cause mark deductions in KTU exams:**
> 1. **Units inconsistency** — KTU examiners deduct 1 full mark if you mix mg/L with mg/g without unit conversion. Always convert.
> 2. **Forgetting to linearize the Langmuir isotherm** — KTU 2024 papers specifically ask for plotting and determining parameters from the linear form $C/q$ vs $C$.
> 3. **Ignoring the separation factor $R_L$** — Many students skip calculating $R_L$. KTU key answers explicitly require the favorability comment.
> 4. **Confusing $q^*$ with $q_m$** — $q_m$ is the *maximum monolayer capacity* (theoretical). $q^*$ is the *actual equilibrium loading* at the operating concentration $C_0$. They are equal **only** when $K_L C \to \infty$.
> 5. **Mass balance errors in cycle calculations** — Always work in consistent units (e.g., mg → g, L → m³) before substituting.
> 6. **Skipping assumptions in derivations** — Listing the four Langmuir assumptions explicitly is a **mandatory 2-mark item** in KTU scripts.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Rapid-Revision Checklist

- **Activated carbon** is a **porous carbonaceous adsorbent** with surface area up to **1500 m²/g**, made by pyrolysis + activation of biomass or coal.
- The **adsorption cycle** consists of three stages: **adsorption → breakthrough → regeneration**.
- **Langmuir isotherm** $q = q_m K_L C / (1 + K_L C)$ — monolayer, homogeneous, **finite sites**.
- **Freundlich isotherm** $q = K_F C^{1/n}$ — empirical, **heterogeneous surface**, multilayer.
- **Linearized forms** are used for plotting and parameter extraction:
  - Langmuir: $C/q$ vs $C$ → slope $= 1/q_m$, intercept $= 1/(q_m K_L)$.
  - Freundlich: $\log q$ vs $\log C$ → slope $= 1/n$, intercept $= \log K_F$.
- **Separation factor** $R_L = 1/(1 + K_L C_0)$; favorable when $0 < R_L < 1$.
- **Regeneration methods**: TSA, PSA, solvent swing, electrochemical, microwave, ultrasonic.
- **TSA** is used for VOC/organic recovery; **PSA** for gases; **solvent swing** for heat-sensitive compounds.
- **Working capacity** $\Delta q = q_{ads} - q_{des}$ is the true cyclic capacity (not $q_m$).
- **EBCT** (Empty Bed Contact Time) is the most cited design parameter for fixed beds.
- **Industrial applications**: water treatment, gold CIP/CIL, pharmaceuticals, sugar refining, solvent recovery, air purification.

### Key Equations to Memorize (One-Line Recall)

1. Langmuir: $\;q = q_m K_L C / (1 + K_L C)$
2. Freundlich: $\;q = K_F C^{1/n}$
3. Separation Factor: $\;R_L = 1/(1 + K_L C_0)$
4. Linear Langmuir: $\;C/q = 1/(q_m K_L) + C/q_m$
5. Linear Freundlich: $\;\log q = \log K_F + (1/n)\log C$
6. Breakthrough Time: $\;t_B = q_{op} W / (Q C_0)$
7. Working Capacity: $\;\Delta q = q_{ads} - q_{des}$
8. Mass Balance: $\;W(q^* - q_0) = L(C_0 - C)$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Final Tip:** Questions on this topic often integrate **isotherm verification** + **bed design calculation** + **regeneration selection** into a single 14-mark problem. Practice combining these three skills in a single timed answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Processing Liquid Using Thermal Regeneration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT752 - POLYMERS AND SEPARATION PROCESSES > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Processing Liquid Using Thermal Regeneration # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Processing Liquid Using Thermal Regeneration** refers to a class of separation operations in which a *loaded separating agent* — typically a solvent (in liquid–liquid extraction), an ion-exchange resin (in solid–liquid ion exchange), or a polymeric adsorbent — is contacted with a feed liquid to selectively capture a target solute, and is subsequently **regenerated by the application of heat** rather than by chemical displacement or pressure swing. The thermal input reverses the thermodynamic driving force for sorption, desorbs the solute, and restores the separating medium to its active (lean) state for recycle. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology for **PEPOT752**, this falls under *regenerative liquid-separation processes* and is most commonly associated with: 1. **Thermal (Hot) Regeneration of Ion-Exchange Resins** — displacement of sorbed ions by raising temperature of the regenerant, exploiting the negative enthalpy of ion exchange for weakly basic/exchange resins. 2. **Thermal Swing Adsorption (TSA) of Liquids** — desorption of organic solutes from polymeric macroporous resins (e.g., styrene–divinylbenzene, XAD, Amberlite) by hot water, steam, or hot inert gas. 3. **Solvent Recovery by Distillative (Thermal) Regeneration in Liquid–Liquid Extraction** — the extract phase is heated; the volatile solvent is vaporized and condensed overhead, leaving the solute as bottoms product. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PEPOT752, Module 3):** Thermal regeneration is treated as a *unit operation complement* to solvent extraction and adsorption, where the **regeneration step itself constitutes the governing design problem**, not the loading step. Examiner focus is on **energy balance, regeneration curve, and breakthrough-resurgence analysis**. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Sponge and Hot Rinse Imagine a **sponge soaked in ink** (the loaded separating medium). To recover the sponge: - A *cold rinse* would slowly leach the dye out — slow and inefficient. - A **squeeze** (mechanical) works but is incomplete. - A **hot rinse** rapidly drives the ink out because heat breaks the molecular bonds holding the dye to the sponge fibres. This is precisely what thermal regeneration does: **heat weakens the solute–medium interaction, freeing the solute and reviving the medium in one combined step**. In polymeric resins, the polymer matrix swells with temperature, the partition coefficient drops, and the solute is released — much like the sponge fibres relaxing in hot water. ## 1.3 Why Thermal Regeneration is Used (Engineering Rationale) - **Avoids chemical waste:** No need for stoichiometric acid/alkali regenerants that produce brine waste. - **Solute recovery as concentrated product:** The desorbate is recovered in concentrated form (e.g., volatile fragrance, pharmaceutical intermediate). - **Suitable for heat-sensitive, high-value solutes** trapped on polymeric adsorbents (e.g., XAD-4 for phenols, XAD-7 for antibiotics). - **Polymer-protected systems:** Modern *thermally regenerable ion-exchange resins* (e.g., **Amberlite XD-2**, **Mitsubishi Diaion**) embed anion-exchange sites in a thermosensitive polymer (N-isopropylacrylamide-based) — a polymer engineering application that sits naturally inside PEPOT752. > [!NOTE] > **Standard Metric Used in KTU Problems:** The **Regeneration Efficiency (η)** is normally expressed as a percentage, while the **Heat Duty (Q)** is reported in **kJ per kg of solute recovered** or **kcal per m³ of resin treated**. The reference regenerant temperature for hot ion-exchange work is **323 K to 333 K (50 °C to 60 °C)**, and for TSA of organics is **373 K to 473 K (100 °C to 200 °C)**. ## 1.4 Classification of Thermal Regeneration Modes | Mode | Medium | Regenerant | Temperature Range | Typical Application | |------|--------|------------|-------------------|---------------------| | **Hot Acid/Base** | Cation / Anion resin | Dilute H₂SO₄ / NaOH (heated) | 323–333 K | Water demineralization, dealkalization | | **Hot Water Strip** | Polymeric adsorbent (XAD) | Hot water | 343–373 K | Phenol, chlorinated organics recovery | | **Steam Stripping** | Activated carbon / polymeric resin | Saturated steam | 373–473 K | VOC recovery, solvent recovery | | **Vacuum + Heat** | Molecular sieves (liquid feed) | Reduced P + heat | 423–573 K | Specialty chemical purification | | **Distillative Regeneration** | Solvent (extract phase) | Reboiler heat | Solvent b.p. + ΔT | LLE with high-boiling solute | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Underlying Thermodynamic Driving Force Thermal regeneration is fundamentally possible when the **equilibrium loading of the solute on the separating medium is a decreasing function of temperature**. Mathematically: $$\left(\frac{\partial q^*}{\partial T}\right)_{P,\,C} < 0$$ where $q^*$ is the equilibrium solid-phase loading (kg solute / kg dry resin or kg solvent / kg solvent), $T$ is temperature, and $C$ is the equilibrium liquid-phase concentration. For an ion-exchange reaction of the type: $$\text{R–A} + \text{B}^+ \rightleftharpoons \text{R–B} + \text{A}^+$$ the van't Hoff relation gives: $$\left(\frac{\partial \ln K_{eq}}{\partial T}\right)_{P} = \frac{\Delta H^{\circ}_{rxn}}{R T^2}$$ If $\Delta H^{\circ}_{rxn} < 0$ (exothermic loading), then **increasing T shifts equilibrium back** to the left, releasing B⁺ and reloading the resin with A⁺. This is the *thermodynamic license* for thermal regeneration. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Insight:** A *negative enthalpy of exchange* is the **necessary condition** for thermal regeneration. For strong-acid cation exchangers, $\Delta H^{\circ}$ is mildly positive → thermal regeneration is **infeasible**; for weak-base/weak-acid exchangers and many polymeric adsorbents, it is **feasible**. ## 2.2 Mechanism of Thermal Regeneration in Polymeric Resins (Step Logic) 1. **Loading stage (cold):** Solute partitions into the swollen polymer gel; binding is exothermic and favoured at low T. 2. **Heating initiation:** Hot regenerant contacts the bed; bed temperature rises; water/regenerant enters the polymer matrix. 3. **Polymer swelling transition:** For thermosensitive polymers (e.g., poly-NIPAM), the matrix undergoes a *lower critical solution temperature* (LCST ≈ **305 K / 32 °C**), causing the polymer to **collapse and expel bound water + solute** — an "auto-desorption" gate. 4. **Desorption front advances:** A sharp thermal front moves through the bed, behind which the resin is in the lean (regenerated) state. 5. **Equilibration & rinse:** A small cool rinse rehydrates the bed, restores swelling, and the cycle restarts. ## 2.3 Mass-Balance Framework Define a single-stage regenerator (mixer-settler with thermal regeneration) operating at steady state: - **Solvent (or resin) flow rate:** $S$ (kg/h) - **Extract (solute-rich) flow to regenerator:** $Y_{in}$ (kg solute / kg lean solvent) - **Regenerator outlet (solvent returned to extractor):** $Y_{out}$ - **Recovered solute (distillate/bottoms):** $X_R$ (kg solute / h) Overall solute balance: $$S \, Y_{in} = S \, Y_{out} + X_R$$ The **regeneration ratio** is: $$\beta = \frac{Y_{out}}{Y_{in}} \quad \text{(target: } \beta \to 0 \text{ for complete regeneration)}$$ ## 2.4 Energy-Balance Framework For **distillative regeneration** (most common in LLE), let: - Solvent: latent heat of vaporization $λ$ (kJ/kg) - Mass of solvent vaporized: $V$ (kg/h) - Bottoms sensible heating: from $T_F$ to $T_b$ (bubble point) - Reflux ratio: $R = L/D$ - Column heat duty (reboiler): $$Q_{reb} = V \, \lambda = D (R+1) \, \lambda$$ Overall energy per kg of solute recovered: $$\frac{Q}{X_R} = \frac{D (R+1) \lambda}{X_R}$$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Shortcut:** If the problem gives the *solvent-to-solute mass ratio* in the extract, $Z = S \cdot Y_{in} / X_R$, and the reflux ratio $R$, then directly: > > $$\frac{Q}{X_R} = Z \, (R+1) \, \lambda$$ > > This is the **formula KTU examiners love** — it bundles mass and energy balances in one line. ## 2.5 Equilibrium and Operating Line for Hot Regeneration For a packed regenerator (TSA-style), the **regeneration curve** is the isothermal (or thermal-front) analogue of the McCabe–Thiele construction: Operating line (mass balance on a stage of the regenerator): $$Y_{n+1} = \frac{L}{V} \, X_n + \frac{V \, Y_{in} - L \, X_{in}}{V}$$ Equilibrium (loaded solvent ↔ hot regenerant): $$Y^* = f(X, T_{regen})$$ Number of theoretical regeneration stages: stepped off between operating and equilibrium lines — **identical procedure** to a standard extraction cascade. ## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > All formulas below are tested repeatedly in KTU 2024 Scheme ESE and are **the only set a student should memorize** for this topic. The vertical bar for absolute value is rendered as `\vert` to keep the markdown table parser happy. | # | Concept | Formula | Units | Typical Range / Note | |---|---------|---------|-------|----------------------| | 1 | Equilibrium temperature sensitivity | $\left(\dfrac{\partial \ln K_{eq}}{\partial T}\right) = \dfrac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{R T^{2}}$ | K⁻¹ | Sign of $\Delta H^{\circ}$ governs feasibility | | 2 | Langmuir isotherm (resin loading) | $q^{*} = \dfrac{q_{m} b C}{1 + b C}$ | kg/kg | $b$ is $T$-dependent | | 3 | Langmuir $b(T)$ | $b(T) = b_{0} \exp\!\left(\dfrac{-\Delta H_{ads}}{R T}\right)$ | m³/kg | $\Delta H_{ads} < 0$ for exothermic sorption | | 4 | Regeneration efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{q_{loaded} - q_{residual}}{q_{loaded}} \times 100$ | % | Target $\eta \geq 90\%$ | | 5 | Regen ratio (single stage) | $\beta = Y_{out} / Y_{in}$ | – | Lower is better | | 6 | Reboiler duty (distillative) | $Q_{reb} = D (R+1) \, \lambda$ | kW | $D$ = distillate rate | | 7 | Specific energy | $Q / X_{R} = Z (R+1) \lambda$ | kJ/kg solute | KTU-favourite result | | 8 | Kremser regeneration stages | $N = \dfrac{\ln\!\left[\left(Y_{in} - Y^{*}\right) / \left(Y_{out} - Y^{*}\right)\right]}{\ln(E)}$ | – | $E$ = extraction factor | | 9 | Solvent-to-solute ratio | $Z = S \cdot Y_{in} / X_{R}$ | kg/kg | Inputs to energy balance | | 10 | Thermal front velocity (TSA) | $u_{T} = \dfrac{u_{L} \rho_{L} c_{p,L}}{\rho_{s} c_{p,s} + \rho_{L} c_{p,L} \varepsilon}$ | m/s | Axial front movement | | 11 | Breakthrough time | $t_{b} = \dfrac{\rho_{s} q_{sat} L}{u_{L} C_{0}}$ | s | Packed-bed analog | | 12 | Hot-regen equilibrium constant (Kremser) | $E = \dfrac{m \cdot S}{W}$ | – | $m$ = slope of equil. line, $W$ = solvent rate | ## 2.7 Real-World Utility - **Pharmaceutical industry:** Recovery of antibiotics (e.g., tetracycline) from fermentation broth using **XAD-7 resin + methanol thermal regeneration** — polymer-supported separation. - **Water treatment:** Removal of phenols from coke-oven effluent via hot caustic regeneration of weak-base resins. - **Petrochemical:** Glycol–water thermal regeneration in *triethylene glycol (TEG) dehydration units* — the heat-regenerated solvent is recycled to the absorber. - **Polymer manufacturing:** Removal of unreacted monomer (styrene, vinyl chloride) from polymer slurry by steam-stripping — direct polymer-process application relevant to PEPOT752. - **Aroma/flavour industry:** Thermal desorption of vanilla, citrus, and hop extracts from macroporous resins without thermal degradation of the product. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: van't Hoff Driving Force for Thermal Regeneration **Starting point:** general ion-exchange equilibrium $$\text{R}^-\text{A}^{+} + \text{B}^{+} \rightleftharpoons \text{R}^-\text{B}^{+} + \text{A}^{+}$$ **Step 1.** Write the equilibrium constant in terms of the equivalent fractions $x_A, x_B$ on the resin and $y_A, y_B$ in solution: $$K_{AB} = \frac{x_B \, y_A}{x_A \, y_B}$$ **Step 2.** Apply the van't Hoff equation relating $K_{AB}$ to temperature: $$\frac{d \ln K_{AB}}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{R T^{2}}$$ **Step 3.** Rearrange to obtain the temperature sensitivity of selectivity: $$d \ln K_{AB} = \frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{R} \cdot \frac{dT}{T^{2}}$$ **Step 4.** Integrate between reference temperature $T_{1}$ (loading) and regenerator temperature $T_{2}$: $$\int_{T_{1}}^{T_{2}} d \ln K_{AB} = \frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{R} \int_{T_{1}}^{T_{2}} \frac{dT}{T^{2}}$$ **Step 5.** Evaluate the right-hand integral: $$\int_{T_{1}}^{T_{2}} \frac{dT}{T^{2}} = \left[ -\frac{1}{T} \right]_{T_{1}}^{T_{2}} = \frac{1}{T_{1}} - \frac{1}{T_{2}}$$ **Step 6.** Obtain the closed-form result: $$\ln\!\left(\frac{K_{AB}(T_{2})}{K_{AB}(T_{1})}\right) = \frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_{1}} - \frac{1}{T_{2}}\right)$$ **Step 7.** Interpretation: if $\Delta H^{\circ} < 0$ (loading exothermic) and $T_{2} > T_{1}$, then the bracket is positive, the logarithm is **negative**, and $K_{AB}(T_{2}) < K_{AB}(T_{1})$ — selectivity for B⁺ drops, so B⁺ is **released** by the resin. This is the thermodynamic proof of feasibility. $\blacksquare$ ## 3.2 Derivation: Specific Heat Duty for Distillative Regeneration (LLE Solvent) **Given:** A liquid–liquid extractor delivers an extract of flow $E$ (kg/h) containing solvent $S_E$ and solute $X_E$ (kg/h). This extract is fed to a distillation column; solvent is recovered as distillate $D$ and recycled. **Step 1.** Mass balance on the extract stream: $$E = S_E + X_E$$ **Step 2.** Mass balance on the column (total): $$F = E = D + B$$ where $B$ is the bottoms (solute-rich) product of rate $X_R$ (kg/h). **Step 3.** Solvent balance: virtually all solvent exits as distillate, so $D \approx S_E$. **Step 4.** Define the solvent-to-solute ratio in the feed: $$Z = \frac{S_E}{X_E} = \frac{D}{X_R}$$ **Step 5.** Energy balance on the reboiler: vaporization of solvent plus sensible heat of bottoms: $$Q_{reb} = D \, \lambda + B \, c_{p,B} (T_b - T_F)$$ **Step 6.** For a dilute bottoms stream, the sensible term is small compared with $D \lambda$; neglect it for a first estimate: $$Q_{reb} \approx D \, \lambda$$ **Step 7.** Substituting $D = Z \cdot X_R$: $$\boxed{\,Q_{reb} \approx Z \, X_R \, \lambda\,} \quad \text{or} \quad \boxed{\,\dfrac{Q_{reb}}{X_R} = Z \, \lambda\,}$$ **Step 8.** If the problem specifies a **reflux ratio** $R$ (i.e., the column is not operating at total reflux), the vapor rate is $V = D(R+1)$: $$Q_{reb} = V \lambda = D(R+1) \lambda = Z \, X_R \, (R+1) \lambda$$ $$\boxed{\,\dfrac{Q_{reb}}{X_R} = Z \, (R+1) \, \lambda\,}$$ **Step 9.** Numerical sanity check (KTU-style): - $Z = 10$ kg solvent / kg solute - $R = 1.5$ - $\lambda = 400$ kJ/kg (typical organic solvent, e.g., toluene) $$Q / X_R = 10 \times (1 + 1.5) \times 400 = 10 \times 2.5 \times 400 = 10{,}000 \text{ kJ/kg solute}$$ This is the order of magnitude a KTU student should expect for distillative solvent regeneration. $\blacksquare$ ## 3.3 Derivation: Number of Theoretical Regeneration Stages (Kremser Equation) For a multi-stage hot regenerator, treat it as an extraction cascade running *in reverse*. The Kremser equation (separation analogue) gives: $$N = \frac{\ln\!\left[\left(\dfrac{X_{in} - X_{in}/m}{X_{out} - X_{in}/m}\right)\right]}{\ln(E)}$$ where $E = m S / W$ is the extraction factor, $m$ is the slope of the equilibrium line at $T_{regen}$, $S$ is the solvent flow, and $W$ is the regenerant (water/steam) flow. **Simplification for thermal regen (operating line ≈ equilibrium line at high T, i.e., $m \to 0$):** As $T \to T_{regen}$, the loading capacity collapses, so $m \to 0$ and the denominator diverges. Physically, this means **one equilibrium stage of hot regenerant is enough** if the temperature swing is large — explaining why thermal regeneration is often a *single-stage* operation in industrial practice. ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Examination Style) **Problem.** A pharmaceutical plant uses **XAD-4 resin** to adsorb an antibiotic from a fermentation broth. The loaded resin has a solute loading of $q_{loaded} = 0.082$ kg/kg dry resin. Hot methanol at 333 K is used as regenerant. After regeneration, the residual loading is $q_{residual} = 0.006$ kg/kg. If the column treats 1200 kg of dry resin per cycle, calculate: (a) the regeneration efficiency, (b) the mass of solute recovered per cycle, and (c) the minimum hot-methanol volume required if the equilibrium concentration in methanol at 333 K is $C^* = 8.0$ kg/m³ and the partition coefficient at 333 K is $K_d = 0.92$. **Solution.** **(a) Regeneration efficiency:** $$\eta = \frac{q_{loaded} - q_{residual}}{q_{loaded}} \times 100 = \frac{0.082 - 0.006}{0.082} \times 100$$ $$\eta = \frac{0.076}{0.082} \times 100 = 92.68\%$$ **(b) Mass of solute recovered:** $$m_{recovered} = (q_{loaded} - q_{residual}) \times m_{resin} = 0.076 \times 1200 = 91.2 \text{ kg per cycle}$$ **(c) Minimum methanol volume:** Total solute in residual regenerant = $m_{recovered} = 91.2$ kg (if all goes into methanol). At equilibrium: $$C^* = \frac{m_{recovered}}{V_{methanol}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_{methanol} = \frac{m_{recovered}}{C^*}$$ $$V_{methanol} = \frac{91.2 \text{ kg}}{8.0 \text{ kg/m}^3} = 11.4 \text{ m}^{3}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Examination Tip:** If the partition coefficient $K_d$ is given, examiners sometimes ask for the *minimum thermodynamic solvent volume*: $$V_{min} = \frac{m_{recovered}}{K_d \, C_{0} + C^*}$$ > where $C_0$ is the inlet regenerant concentration (usually 0 for fresh solvent). ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Loading vs Regeneration Comparison | Parameter | Loading Stage (Cold) | Regeneration Stage (Hot) | |-----------|---------------------|--------------------------| | Temperature | 298–308 K | 333–473 K | | Equilibrium constant $K_{eq}$ | Large (favours sorption) | Small (favours desorption) | | Polymer state | Swollen (gel-like) | Partially collapsed (LCST) | | Solvent capacity $q^*$ | High | Low (often < 10% of $q_{loaded}$) | | Enthalpy of sorption $\Delta H_{ads}$ | Negative (exothermic) | Reverse direction | | Mass-transfer driving force | $C - C^*$ high | $q^* - q$ small → kinetics matter | | Limiting mechanism | External film diffusion | Pore diffusion + thermal front | | Typical residence time | 5–30 min | 10–60 min (slower desorption) | | Energy input | Minimal (pumping) | Significant (Q ~ Z(R+1)λ) | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Liquid Processing with Thermal Regeneration Loop ```mermaid flowchart LR feedA[Liquid Feed
Containing Solute] --> mixerA[Extraction Column
or Adsorber
Cold Stage T1] raf1[Raffinate
Lean Liquid] --> mixerA mixerA --> loadedB[Loaded Solvent
or Loaded Resin] loadedB --> heaterA[Preheater / Heat Exchanger
Q Preheat] heaterA --> regenA[Thermal Regenerator
Distillation Column
or Hot Stripper T2] regenA --> vap1[Solvent Vapour
Overhead] vap1 --> cond1[Condenser
Q Condenser] cond1 --> lean1[Lean Solvent
Recycled to Extractor] lean1 --> mixerA regenA --> prod1[Recovered Solute
Product Bottoms] ``` ## 4.2 Thermal Swing Adsorption (TSA) Cycle for Liquid Feed ```mermaid flowchart TD start([Cycle Start]) --> loadValve{Feed Valve} loadValve --> adsorbStage[Adsorption Stage
Cold Liquid Feed
T = 303 K] adsorbStage --> loadCheck{Bed Saturated?} loadCheck -->|No| adsorbStage loadCheck -->|Yes| switchValve[Switch to Hot Regenerant] switchValve --> heatStage[Heating Stage
Hot Water / Steam
T = 353 to 423 K] heatStage --> desorbStage[Desorption Stage
Concentrated Solute Eluted] desorbStage --> coolStage[Cooling / Re-equilibration
Rinse with Cold Solvent] coolStage --> readyCheck{Bed Ready?} readyCheck -->|No| coolStage readyCheck -->|Yes| loadValve ``` ## 4.3 Energy-Flow Schematic of Distillative Regeneration ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Distillation_Column reboiler1[Reboiler
Q Reb = D R+1 lambda] --> col1[Column Trays or Packing] col1 --> cond2[Condenser
Q Cond = D R lambda] end cond2 --> refluxSplit{Reflux Split} refluxSplit --> refluxBack[Reflux L = R times D] refluxBack --> col1 refluxSplit --> distillate[Distillate D
Lean Solvent] reboiler1 --> bottoms1[Bottoms B
Recovered Solute] feedIn[Extract Feed
Solute + Solvent] --> col1 ``` ## 4.4 Decision Flowchart: When is Thermal Regeneration the Right Choice? ```mermaid flowchart TD q1{Is the solute
heat stable?} q1 -->|No| chem[Use Chemical Regeneration
Acid or Alkali] q1 -->|Yes| q2{Is enthalpy of sorption
negative?} q2 -->|No| press[Consider Pressure Swing
or Vacuum Desorption] q2 -->|Yes| q3{Is the solute
volatile?} q3 -->|Yes| distill[Distillative Thermal Regen
LLE Solvent Recovery] q3 -->|No| q4{Is steam or hot water
available on site?} q4 -->|Yes| tsa[Thermal Swing Adsorption
Polymeric Resin] q4 -->|No| micro[Consider Microwave or
Ultrasound-Assisted Regen] ``` ## 4.5 Component-Interaction Map (Polymer-Engineering Emphasis) ```mermaid flowchart LR polymerResin[Polymer Resin Bead
e.g. XAD-4 or NIPAM-based] --> activeSite[Active Sorption Site
Functional Group] activeSite --> solute[Solute Molecule
Bound] hotTrigger[Hot Regenerant
T > LCST or T > T critical] --> polymerCollapse[Polymer Matrix Collapse
Volume Phase Transition] polymerCollapse --> soluteRelease[Solute Released
into Regenerant Stream] soluteRelease --> leanResin[Lean Resin
Ready for Recycle] leanResin --> polymerResin ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Define thermal regeneration as applied to liquid separation processes. State two situations where it is preferred over chemical regeneration.** **Model Answer:** Thermal regeneration is a separation-regeneration technique in which the separating medium (solvent, ion-exchange resin, or polymeric adsorbent) is restored to its active state by the **application of heat** rather than by a chemical regenerant. The heat input raises the system temperature, decreasing the equilibrium loading $q^*$ and driving the desorption of the trapped solute back into a recovery stream. It is preferred over chemical regeneration when: 1. The solute is **heat-stable and high-value** (e.g., pharmaceuticals, flavours) and chemical regenerants would contaminate the product. 2. The enthalpy of sorption is **negative** (exothermic loading), so heating reverses the equilibrium economically — typical of weak-acid/weak-base ion-exchange resins and macroporous polymeric adsorbents like XAD-4. --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Explain the role of the lower critical solution temperature (LCST) in thermally regenerable ion-exchange resins.** **Model Answer:** The **lower critical solution temperature (LCST)** is the temperature at which a thermosensitive polymer (e.g., poly-N-isopropylacrylamide, LCST ≈ **305 K / 32 °C**) undergoes a sharp *volume phase transition* — switching from a swollen, hydrophilic state to a collapsed, hydrophobic state. In thermally regenerable ion-exchange resins, the ion-exchange functional groups are attached to such a polymer backbone. **Below** the LCST, the polymer is hydrated and swollen, exposing the exchange sites to the aqueous solution and **capturing** the target ions. **Above** the LCST, the polymer collapses, expelling water and **releasing** the bound ions into the regenerant stream. This auto-gating behaviour eliminates the need for chemical regenerants and is the polymer-engineering cornerstone of thermal regeneration. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) > **ESE Module Internal Choice:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024] **(a)** Derive an expression for the **specific heat duty** (kJ per kg of solute recovered) for the distillative regeneration of solvent in a liquid–liquid extraction process, starting from the reboiler energy balance. State clearly the assumptions. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A single-stage extractor uses **toluene** to recover **acetone** from an aqueous feed. The extract phase contains 0.18 kg acetone / kg toluene and is fed at 800 kg/h to a distillation column. The column operates at reflux ratio $R = 2.5$. Data: latent heat of vaporisation of toluene $\lambda = 363$ kJ/kg. Calculate: **(i)** the distillate rate, **(ii)** the reboiler heat duty, and **(iii)** the specific energy consumption in kJ per kg of acetone recovered. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution.** **(a) Derivation (see Section 3.2 for full working):** The required expression is: $$\frac{Q_{reb}}{X_R} = Z \, (R+1) \, \lambda$$ **Assumptions:** 1. Steady-state, continuous operation. 2. Total condenser (vapour fully condensed; distillate composition = vapour composition). 3. Negligible heat losses to surroundings (adiabatic column). 4. Bottoms sensible-heat term is small compared to latent heat of vaporisation. 5. Solute is non-volatile relative to solvent (all solute exits in the bottoms). 6. Constant molar overflow / constant latent heat across the column. **(b) Numerical solution:** Given: - Extract feed $F = 800$ kg/h - Acetone fraction in extract: $0.18$ kg acetone / kg toluene → $S_E = 0.82 \times 800 = 656$ kg toluene/h, $X_E = 0.18 \times 800 = 144$ kg acetone/h - $R = 2.5$, $\lambda = 363$ kJ/kg **(i) Distillate rate:** Toluene is the volatile component; virtually all toluene leaves overhead: $$D = S_E = 656 \text{ kg/h}$$ **(ii) Reboiler heat duty:** $$Q_{reb} = D \, (R+1) \, \lambda = 656 \times (2.5 + 1) \times 363$$ $$Q_{reb} = 656 \times 3.5 \times 363 = 833{,}508 \text{ kJ/h} \approx 231.5 \text{ kW}$$ **(iii) Specific energy consumption:** Recovered acetone $X_R = X_E = 144$ kg/h: $$\frac{Q_{reb}}{X_R} = \frac{833{,}508}{144} = 5788 \text{ kJ/kg acetone}$$ Alternative: $Z = S_E / X_E = 656 / 144 = 4.556$ kg toluene / kg acetone $$\frac{Q_{reb}}{X_R} = Z (R+1) \lambda = 4.556 \times 3.5 \times 363 = 5788 \text{ kJ/kg} \quad \checkmark$$ --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024] **(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the principle of **thermal swing adsorption (TSA)** for processing a liquid feed using a polymeric adsorbent. Discuss the role of the **thermal front velocity** $u_T$ in the regeneration cycle time. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A fixed bed of **XAD-7 resin** (bulk density $\rho_s = 650$ kg/m³, bed porosity $\varepsilon = 0.40$) is used to remove a phenol derivative from wastewater. The bed depth is $L = 1.2$ m, superficial liquid velocity $u_L = 0.002$ m/s, $\rho_L c_{p,L} = 4.18 \times 10^{6}$ J/(m³·K), and $\rho_s c_{p,s} = 1.30 \times 10^{6}$ J/(m³·K). Calculate: **(i)** the thermal front velocity, and **(ii)** the time required for the thermal front to traverse the entire bed during regeneration. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution.** **(a) Principle of TSA (sketch already provided in Section 4.2):** In TSA of liquids, the polymeric adsorbent is first contacted with the cold feed liquid; the solute partitions into the resin (loading). Once the resin approaches saturation, the feed is switched off and a **hot regenerant** (hot water, steam, or hot solvent) is passed through the bed. The thermal front moves through the bed; behind the front the resin is at high temperature and the equilibrium loading collapses, releasing the solute. A concentrated desorbate exits the column. The cycle ends with a cool rinse to rehydrate the resin. The **thermal front velocity** $u_T$ governs the cycle time: $$u_T = \frac{u_L \, \rho_L c_{p,L}}{\rho_s c_{p,s} + \rho_L c_{p,L} \, \varepsilon}$$ A low $u_T$ means a slow-moving front, which is *good for regeneration quality* (more time for desorption equilibrium to be reached) but *bad for cycle time*. The designer must balance residence time against throughput. **(b) Numerical solution:** **(i) Thermal front velocity:** Numerator: $u_L \cdot \rho_L c_{p,L} = 0.002 \times 4.18 \times 10^{6} = 8360$ W/m² (or J/(m²·s)) Denominator: $\rho_s c_{p,s} + \rho_L c_{p,L} \varepsilon = 1.30 \times 10^{6} + 4.18 \times 10^{6} \times 0.40$ $$= 1.30 \times 10^{6} + 1.672 \times 10^{6} = 2.972 \times 10^{6} \text{ J/(m}^{3}\text{·K)}$$ $$u_T = \frac{8360}{2.972 \times 10^{6}} = 2.812 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m/s}$$ **(ii) Time to traverse the bed:** $$t = \frac{L}{u_T} = \frac{1.2}{2.812 \times 10^{-3}} = 426.7 \text{ s} \approx 7.11 \text{ min}$$ --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Thermal-Regeneration Problems (KTU 2024 Scheme):** > 1. **Forgetting the $(R+1)$ term** in the reboiler duty formula. Students often write $Q = D \lambda$ instead of $Q = D(R+1)\lambda$. This is the **#1 mark-loser** — the reflux ratio adds 30–50% extra duty and examiners allocate a full sub-step to it. > 2. **Sign confusion in van't Hoff:** Students write $\partial \ln K / \partial T = \Delta H / RT$ instead of $/RT^2$. The square on $T$ is non-negotiable. > 3. **Wrong solvent/solute identification:** In LLE thermal regeneration, the **solvent** is the volatile component (distillate), and the **solute** is the bottoms. Mixing these up flips $D$ and $X_R$ and wrecks both the mass and energy balance. > 4. **Units in the thermal-front formula:** Forgetting that $\rho_L c_{p,L}$ and $\rho_s c_{p,s}$ have *different reference volumes*. Liquid uses volumetric heat capacity; solid uses volumetric heat capacity of the packed bed (not per kg of dry solid). KTU examiners have marked down half the question for this unit slip. > 5. **Neglecting the LCST concept** when the question is about thermally regenerable *ion-exchange resins*. LCST is the polymer-engineering soul of the topic and a guaranteed 2–3 mark sub-question in Part A. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Thermal regeneration** = heat-driven desorption that restores the separating medium for recycle, without chemical regenerants. - **Thermodynamic prerequisite:** $\Delta H_{sorption} < 0$ (exothermic loading) so that increasing $T$ shifts equilibrium back. - **Van't Hoff equation** is the analytical licence: $\ln(K_{T_2}/K_{T_1}) = (\Delta H^\circ/R)(1/T_1 - 1/T_2)$. - **Three principal modes:** (i) hot ion-exchange regeneration, (ii) thermal swing adsorption with polymeric resins (XAD, Amberlite, poly-NIPAM), (iii) distillative solvent regeneration in LLE. - **Polymer-engineering angle (PEPOT752-specific):** thermosensitive polymers with LCST ≈ 305 K enable *auto-gated* regeneration by collapse of the polymer matrix above the LCST. - **Master formulas (memorize):** - Specific heat duty: $Q/X_R = Z(R+1)\lambda$ - Reboiler duty: $Q_{reb} = D(R+1)\lambda$ - Regeneration efficiency: $\eta = (q_{loaded} - q_{residual})/q_{loaded} \times 100$ - Thermal front velocity: $u_T = u_L \rho_L c_{p,L} / [\rho_s c_{p,s} + \rho_L c_{p,L}\varepsilon]$ - Langmuir isotherm with $b(T) = b_0 \exp(-\Delta H_{ads}/RT)$ - **Design heuristics:** - Hot-regeneration temperature for weak-base resins: **323–333 K**. - TSA regenerant temperature: **343–473 K** with steam. - Distillative regeneration: choose solvent with $\lambda$ low and b.p. well separated from solute. - Acceptable regeneration efficiency in industry: **≥ 90%**. - Acceptable regeneration ratio $\beta$ in single stage: **< 0.15**. - **Order-of-magnitude check for specific energy:** $Q/X_R$ in distillative LLE regeneration typically falls in the range **5,000 – 50,000 kJ/kg solute**. - **Industrial exemplars:** TEG dehydration (gas processing), XAD-4 antibiotic recovery (pharma), hot-NaOH regeneration of weak-base resins (water treatment), steam stripping of VOCs (environmental). - **KTU-typical question patterns:** (i) derive the van't Hoff driving force, (ii) compute reboiler duty for a given LLE system, (iii) explain LCST-based resin, (iv) sketch the TSA cycle, (v) compute thermal front velocity and regeneration time. - **Conversion factors to keep handy:** 1 kcal = 4.186 kJ; 1 RT = 0.0821 L·atm/(mol·K) ≈ 8.314 J/(mol·K); LCST of poly-NIPAM ≈ 32 °C / 305 K.

topics.title: Pressure Swing and Vacuum Swing Adsorption
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT752 - POLYMERS AND SEPARATION PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Pressure Swing and Vacuum Swing Adsorption

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3 — Pressure Swing and Vacuum Swing Adsorption

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Adsorption?
**Adsorption** is a surface-based separation operation in which molecules of a fluid phase (gas or liquid) selectively adhere to the internal pore surface of a solid **adsorbent** (e.g., activated carbon, zeolite 5A, 13X, silica gel, activated alumina). The species that sticks to the surface is the **adsorbate**, and the solid providing the surface is the **adsorbent**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Distinction the examiner expects**
> - **Adsorption** → surface accumulation (2-D, exothermic, reversible).
> - **Absorption** → bulk penetration into a liquid (3-D, e.g., scrubbing).
> - At KTU, the term is always **adsorption** for the unit operations covered in this module.

### 1.2 Pressure Swing Adsorption (PSA) — Formal KTU Definition
**Pressure Swing Adsorption (PSA)** is a cyclic, non-cryogenic gas separation process in which a **gaseous mixture is selectively adsorbed at an elevated (high) pressure** on a porous solid, and the **adsorbent is regenerated by lowering the pressure to near-atmospheric or sub-atmospheric levels**. The separation driving force is the **difference in adsorption loading at high pressure versus low pressure** for the components in the feed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pressure metric anchor (KTU Board Favourite)**
> - **High pressure (adsorption step):** typically **0.7 – 10 atm** (industrial units use **4 – 8 atm** for air separation, **10 – 30 atm** for H₂ purification).
> - **Low pressure (regeneration step, PSA):** approximately **1.0 – 1.2 atm** (just above atmospheric, achieved by depressurization).
> - **Vacuum pressure (regeneration step, VSA):** typically **0.05 – 0.3 atm** absolute (achieved by a vacuum pump).
> - The term **"swing"** refers to this **periodic pressure oscillation** between two pressure levels.

### 1.3 Vacuum Swing Adsorption (VSA) — Formal KTU Definition
**Vacuum Swing Adsorption (VSA)** is a special case of PSA in which the **adsorption occurs at or near atmospheric pressure** and **regeneration is achieved by pulling vacuum (sub-atmospheric pressure)** using a vacuum pump. VSA is preferred when the strongly adsorbed component is desired as the **product** (e.g., oxygen from air) and when the feed is already at low pressure and cannot be economically compressed.

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — The "Sponge and the Hand" Picture
Imagine a kitchen sponge that, when **squeezed dry (low pressure)**, can soak up colored water. Then, when you **release the sponge (high pressure)**, it holds the colored water tightly. Finally, you **press it again (low pressure or vacuum)**, and pure water drips out.
- **High pressure (open sponge)** → adsorbent grabs impurity (or the desired product, depending on cycle design).
- **Low pressure / vacuum (squeezed sponge)** → adsorbent releases the trapped molecules, regenerating itself.
- The sponge works in **cycles** because the same piece of adsorbent is reused again and again. Two sponges working out of phase (one soaking, the other squeezing) provide **continuous** separation — this is the heart of multi-bed PSA.

> [!TIP]
> In a KTU answer, drawing this 2-bed out-of-phase picture in words (Bed A: Adsorbing → Bed B: Regenerating) earns full process-understanding marks even without a complex figure.

### 1.5 Why PSA/VSA Matter in Industry
- **Air drying** (removing H₂O with activated alumina or silica gel).
- **Oxygen enrichment** from air (medical O₂, steel plants) using zeolite 5A or LiLSX.
- **Nitrogen generation** (food packaging, electronics) using carbon molecular sieve (CMS).
- **Hydrogen purification** in refineries (removing CO, CO₂, CH₄ from syngas using activated carbon + zeolite layered beds).
- **Biogas upgrading** (CO₂ / CH₄ separation).
- **Trace VOC recovery** from vent streams.

The key advantage is **low energy consumption** (only pressure energy, no phase change), **modular scalability**, and **ambient-temperature operation**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Underlying Physics — Why Pressure Controls Loading
The **adsorption equilibrium loading** $q$ of a component on a solid is a strong function of the partial pressure $p$ and the temperature $T$. The most commonly tested equilibrium expression is the **Langmuir Isotherm**:

$$q = \frac{q_m \, b \, p}{1 + b \, p}$$

where:
- $q$ = equilibrium loading (kg adsorbate / kg adsorbent),
- $q_m$ = monolayer saturation capacity,
- $b$ = Langmuir affinity constant (function of $T$),
- $p$ = partial pressure of the adsorbable component.

**Why PSA works (the "swing"):**
- At **high pressure** ($p \uparrow$), $q \rightarrow q_m$ (bed is heavily loaded — the impurity is captured).
- At **low pressure / vacuum** ($p \downarrow$), $q \rightarrow 0$ (bed releases the captured molecules — the bed is regenerated).
- The **difference in loading $\Delta q$** between the two pressures is the actual separation capacity per cycle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Operating rule of thumb (for KTU problems):** the working capacity $\Delta q$ is typically **10 – 20 %** of $q_m$ in PSA and can rise to **40 – 60 %** of $q_m$ in VSA because of the larger pressure ratio.

### 2.2 The Four Step Building Blocks of a PSA Cycle
Every PSA/VSA cycle is composed of **four fundamental steps** (though industrial cycles combine them):

1. **Adsorption (high pressure)**: Feed gas enters the bed at high $P$. The strongly adsorbed component is captured; the weakly adsorbed (or non-adsorbed) component exits as the **product**.
2. **Depressurization (blowdown)**: Bed pressure is reduced — the captured component begins to desorb. In VSA, this step may be replaced or assisted by **vacuum pulling**.
3. **Regeneration (purge or vacuum)**: At low $P$, a purge gas (part of the product) or a vacuum pump strips the desorbed component out. This produces the **tail gas** or **desorbate stream**.
4. **Repressurization**: Bed is brought back to the high adsorption pressure using either feed gas or product gas, ready for the next cycle.

### 2.3 Why a Single Bed Cannot Work — The Multi-Bed Logic
A single bed must sequentially Adsorb → Depressurize → Regenerate → Repressurize. **During regeneration, no product is produced.** To deliver a **continuous product stream**, KTU problems almost always assume a **two-bed** (or **three-bed**) configuration, where:
- **Bed A** is on **Adsorption step** → product exits.
- **Bed B** is on **Regeneration step** → tail gas exits.
- When Bed A saturates, the valves switch → Bed A regenerates, Bed B adsorbs.

> [!TIP]
> A typical cycle time is **2 – 6 minutes per bed**. Industrial air-separation PSA plants often use **3 beds** to gain efficiency: Bed 1 adsorbs, Bed 2 provides product during pressure equalization, Bed 3 regenerates.

### 2.4 Key Engineering Variables the Examiner Loves
- **Bed length** $L$ (m): typically 1 – 5 m.
- **Bed diameter** $D$ (m): typically 0.05 – 2 m.
- **Superficial velocity** $u$ (m/s): 0.1 – 0.5 m/s.
- **Particle diameter** $d_p$ (mm): 0.5 – 3 mm (smaller → better kinetics, higher pressure drop).
- **Interparticle porosity** $\varepsilon_b$: 0.35 – 0.45.
- **Bulk density** $\rho_b$ (kg/m³): 500 – 800 kg/m³ for typical zeolites.
- **Cycle time** $t_c$ (s): 120 – 600 s.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol / Units | Engineering Meaning |
|---|-------------------|----------------|---------------------|
| 1 | Langmuir Isotherm: $q = \dfrac{q_m b p}{1 + b p}$ | $q$ [kg/kg], $p$ [Pa] | Equilibrium loading vs. pressure |
| 2 | Working capacity: $\Delta q = q_{high} - q_{low}$ | $\Delta q$ [kg/kg] | Actual separation capacity per cycle |
| 3 | Pressure ratio: $\Pi = P_{high} / P_{low}$ | dimensionless | Driver of separation; PSA $\Pi \approx 4 - 10$, VSA $\Pi \approx 5 - 20$ |
| 4 | Mass balance on bed: $\varepsilon_b \dfrac{\partial C}{\partial t} + \rho_b \dfrac{\partial q}{\partial t} + u \dfrac{\partial C}{\partial z} = 0$ | per m³ of bed | Governs the breakthrough wave |
| 5 | Breakthrough time: $t_B = \dfrac{L}{u}\left(1 + \dfrac{\rho_b \, \Delta q}{\varepsilon_b \, C_0}\right)$ | $t_B$ [s] | Time when product purity falls below spec |
| 6 | Stoichiometric (ideal) time: $t^* = \dfrac{L}{u} \left(1 + \dfrac{\rho_b \, q_0}{\varepsilon_b \, C_0}\right)$ | $t^*$ [s] | Time if the wave were a perfect shock |
| 7 | Length of unused bed: $\text{LUB} = u (t^* - t_B)$ | LUB [m] | Bed length sacrificed to mass-transfer resistance |
| 8 | Cycle time: $t_c \le t_B$ | $t_c$ [s] | Must switch beds before breakthrough |
| 9 | Pressure drop (Ergun): $\dfrac{\Delta P}{L} = \dfrac{150 \mu u}{d_p^{2}}\dfrac{(1-\varepsilon_b)^{2}}{\varepsilon_b^{3}} + \dfrac{1.75 \rho u^{2}}{d_p}\dfrac{(1-\varepsilon_b)}{\varepsilon_b^{3}}$ | $\Delta P$ [Pa] | Pumping cost vs. bed design |
| 10 | Recovery: $\text{Rec} = \dfrac{y_{prod} \cdot V_{prod}}{y_{feed} \cdot V_{feed}}$ | dimensionless | Fraction of feed recovered as product |

> [!NOTE]
> In a KTU answer, write the **Langmuir equation**, the **working capacity $\Delta q$**, and the **breakthrough time** expression. These three alone solve 80 % of numerical sub-parts.

### 2.6 Real-World Utility
- **Medical O₂ concentrators**: a 2-bed VSA with zeolite LiLSX, cycle time ≈ 6 s, delivers 90 – 95 % O₂.
- **Hydrogen PSA in refineries**: layered bed (activated carbon for CO₂, zeolite 5A for CH₄/CO), recovers 70 – 90 % H₂ at 99.99 % purity.
- **Industrial N₂ plants**: carbon molecular sieve (CMS) separates O₂ (small, fast) from N₂ (slow) using kinetic selectivity, not equilibrium.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Bed Mass Balance (1-D, Isothermal, Plug Flow)
Consider a differential slice of the adsorbent bed of thickness $\mathrm{d}z$ and cross-section $A$. The slice contains gas in the voids (volume $\varepsilon_b A\, \mathrm{d}z$) and adsorbent (mass $\rho_b A\, \mathrm{d}z$).

**Step 1 — Accumulation in gas phase (voids):**
$$\text{Accum}_{\text{gas}} = \varepsilon_b A\, \mathrm{d}z \cdot \dfrac{\partial C}{\partial t}$$

**Step 2 — Accumulation on solid (adsorbate):**
$$\text{Accum}_{\text{solid}} = \rho_b A\, \mathrm{d}z \cdot \dfrac{\partial q}{\partial t}$$

**Step 3 — Net convective input (gas in at $z$, out at $z + \mathrm{d}z$):**
$$\text{Conv} = u A \cdot C \big\vert_{z} - u A \cdot C \big\vert_{z+\mathrm{d}z} = -u A\, \dfrac{\partial C}{\partial z}\,\mathrm{d}z$$

**Step 4 — Add and divide by $A\, \mathrm{d}z$:**
$$\varepsilon_b \dfrac{\partial C}{\partial t} + \rho_b \dfrac{\partial q}{\partial t} + u \dfrac{\partial C}{\partial z} = 0$$

This is the **governing PDE** of an adiabatic, isothermal, plug-flow PSA bed.

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU board answers, examiners accept this derivation with the statement *"by a material balance on a differential slice"*. The key is to show **what goes in − what goes out = accumulation in gas + accumulation on solid**.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Stoichiometric Breakthrough Time $t^*$
Integrate the PDE across the whole bed under the assumption of a **sharp (shock) adsorption front** — the simplest possible model. For a step input of feed concentration $C_0$ and an initially clean bed ($q = 0$), the mass balance over the full bed for a time interval $\Delta t$ is:

**Step 1 — Total moles of adsorbate delivered to bed in $\Delta t$:**
$$N_{\text{in}} = u A C_0 \Delta t$$

**Step 2 — Moles stored in gas voids of the bed (when fully saturated):**
$$N_{\text{gas}} = \varepsilon_b A L C_0$$

**Step 3 — Moles stored on the solid (when fully saturated):**
$$N_{\text{solid}} = \rho_b A L q_0$$

**Step 4 — Set $N_{\text{in}} = N_{\text{gas}} + N_{\text{solid}}$ and solve for $\Delta t \equiv t^*$:**
$$u A C_0 t^* = A L \left(\varepsilon_b C_0 + \rho_b q_0\right)$$

**Step 5 — Isolate $t^*$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t^* &= \dfrac{L}{u} \left(1 + \dfrac{\rho_b \, q_0}{\varepsilon_b \, C_0}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> $t^*$ is the **ideal breakthrough time** assuming a perfect shock wave. In reality, axial dispersion and finite mass-transfer resistance **broaden the wave**, so the actual breakthrough time $t_B < t^*$.

### 3.3 Derivation of the Length of Unused Bed (LUB)
The **Length of Unused Bed (LUB)** is the portion of the bed that, at the moment of breakthrough, is still not fully utilized. The mass-transfer zone (MTZ) propagates like a wave; when the trailing edge of the MTZ exits the bed, breakthrough occurs.

**Step 1 — Define the MTZ velocity:**
$$u_{\text{MTZ}} = \dfrac{L}{t^*}$$

**Step 2 — The MTZ has length $\ell$ (proportional to $L$ in the simplest model). The breakthrough time satisfies:**
$$t_B = t^* - \dfrac{\ell}{u_{\text{MTZ}}}$$

**Step 3 — Replace $u_{\text{MTZ}}$ and rearrange:**
$$u_{\text{MTZ}} (t^* - t_B) = \ell \equiv \text{LUB}$$

**Step 4 — Since $u_{\text{MTZ}} = L / t^*$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{LUB} &= \dfrac{L}{t^*}\,(t^* - t_B) = L\left(1 - \dfrac{t_B}{t^*}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design rule:** the **bed length must exceed LUB**, otherwise the product purity target is impossible regardless of cycle time. Typical industrial LUB is **15 – 25 % of the total bed length**.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem (typical Part b sub-question):**
> A PSA bed of length $L = 2.0$ m and cross-section $A = 0.05$ m² is packed with zeolite 5A ($\rho_b = 700$ kg/m³, $\varepsilon_b = 0.40$). The feed is air at $P = 5$ atm containing nitrogen (the *strongly adsorbed* component in this CMS analogy). Feed superficial velocity $u = 0.20$ m/s, $C_0 = 40$ mol/m³, equilibrium loading at feed conditions $q_0 = 1.5$ mol/kg.
> (a) Calculate the stoichiometric breakthrough time.
> (b) If the actual breakthrough time is $t_B = 480$ s, find the LUB.
> (c) Is the bed length adequate if the LUB must be ≤ 0.4 m?

**Solution (a):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t^* &= \dfrac{L}{u}\left(1 + \dfrac{\rho_b \, q_0}{\varepsilon_b \, C_0}\right) \\
&= \dfrac{2.0}{0.20}\left(1 + \dfrac{700 \times 1.5}{0.40 \times 40}\right) \\
&= 10 \times \left(1 + \dfrac{1050}{16}\right) \\
&= 10 \times (1 + 65.625) \\
&= 10 \times 66.625 \\
&= 666.25 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Solution (b):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{LUB} &= L\left(1 - \dfrac{t_B}{t^*}\right) = 2.0\left(1 - \dfrac{480}{666.25}\right) \\
&= 2.0 \times (1 - 0.7205) = 2.0 \times 0.2795 \\
&= 0.559 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Solution (c):**
Since $\text{LUB} = 0.559$ m **>** 0.4 m, the bed length is **not adequate**. To meet the constraint, either:
- **Increase $L$** (higher capital cost), or
- **Reduce $t_B$** (longer cycle → smaller $u$ → larger $t^*$), or
- **Use a finer adsorbent** (lower LUB through reduced mass-transfer resistance).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a 2-Bed PSA System
The following Mermaid block diagram traces the **flow of feed, product, and tail gas** through the two beds and the four-way valve logic of a classic 2-bed PSA cycle.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    feedA[FEED GAS COMPRESSOR] --> inA[INLET MANIFOLD]
    inA --> v1[VALVE 1]
    v1 --> bedA[BED A ADSORBING HIGH PRESSURE]
    bedA --> v3[VALVE 3]
    v3 --> prod[PRODUCT STREAM WEAK ADSORBATE]
    v3 --> repress[REPRESSURIZE BED B]
    repress --> v4[VALVE 4]
    v4 --> bedB[BED B REGENERATING LOW PRESSURE]
    bedB --> v2[VALVE 2 BLOWDOWN]
    v2 --> tail[TAIL GAS STRONG ADSORBATE]
    tail --> vacpump[VACUUM PUMP FOR VSA]
    controller[SEQUENCING TIMER PLC] -. controls .-> v1
    controller -. controls .-> v2
    controller -. controls .-> v3
    controller -. controls .-> v4
```

> [!TIP]
> When a KTU question asks "draw a 2-bed PSA schematic", a labelled block diagram with the four valves, the controller (PLC/timer), the feed, product, and tail-gas streams is sufficient for full marks. Adding the **vacuum pump** shows you can distinguish VSA from PSA.

### 4.2 Functional State Diagram of the 4 PSA Cycle Steps
The following diagram shows the **state transition** of a single bed through the four canonical steps, which is the most common conceptual sketch in KTU viva questions.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> STEP1
    STEP1[STEP 1 ADSORPTION HIGH P FEED ENTERS PRODUCT OUT] --> STEP2
    STEP2[STEP 2 DEPRESSURIZATION OR BLOWDOWN P DROPS] --> STEP3
    STEP3[STEP 3 REGENERATION PURGE OR VACUUM TAIL GAS OUT] --> STEP4
    STEP4[STEP 4 REPRESSURIZATION BACK TO HIGH P] --> STEP1
    STEP4 --> [*]
```

### 4.3 Comparative Block Architecture — PSA vs VSA
A side-by-side block view of the **pressure and stream differences** between PSA and VSA, which examiners use to test whether the student can distinguish the two processes.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PSA[PSA SYSTEM]
        feedA1[FEED AT 5 TO 10 ATM] --> compA[COMPRESSOR]
        compA --> bedA1[BED AT HIGH P]
        bedA1 --> lowA1[BED DEPRESSURIZED TO 1 ATM]
        lowA1 --> purgeA[PURGE WITH PRODUCT FRACTION]
        purgeA --> bedA1
    end
    subgraph VSA[VSA SYSTEM]
        feedB1[FEED AT 1 ATM] --> bedB1[BED AT 1 ATM]
        bedB1 --> lowB1[BED PULLED TO 0.1 TO 0.3 ATM]
        lowB1 --> vacB[VACUUM PUMP]
        vacB --> lowB1
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **only hardware difference** between PSA and VSA is the regeneration step: PSA uses a **blowdown valve** to reach near-atmospheric pressure; VSA uses a **vacuum pump** to reach sub-atmospheric pressure. The rest of the cycle is identical.

### 4.4 Breakthrough Curve — Typical Shape
For a step change in feed concentration at $t = 0$, the outlet concentration vs. time follows the **breakthrough curve**. Although this is a 2-D graph (not a Mermaid), KTU examiners accept a hand-sketched curve in the answer script. The shape is:

- **0 → $t_B$**: outlet $C \approx 0$ (pure product, bed is adsorbing).
- **$t_B$ → $t^*$**: outlet $C$ rises in an **S-shape** from 0 to $C_0$ (mass-transfer zone moving through bed).
- **After $t^*$**: outlet $C = C_0$ (bed is fully saturated, no further separation).

> [!TIP]
> In the answer script, label the **breakpoint $t_B$**, the **stoichiometric time $t^*$**, the **mass-transfer zone (MTZ)** width, and the **area above the curve** (which represents the working capacity of the bed).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between Pressure Swing Adsorption (PSA) and Vacuum Swing Adsorption (VSA).** Mention the typical pressure ranges used in each.

**Model Answer:**
PSA operates at elevated adsorption pressure (typically **5 – 10 atm**) and regenerates by depressurization to near-atmospheric pressure (**≈ 1 atm**). VSA operates at near-atmospheric adsorption pressure (**≈ 1 atm**) and regenerates by pulling vacuum (**0.05 – 0.3 atm**). PSA is preferred for **high-pressure feed streams** (e.g., refinery H₂); VSA is preferred when the **feed is already at low pressure and the strongly adsorbed component is the desired product** (e.g., medical O₂ from air). Both are cyclic, both rely on the equilibrium-loading difference between two pressure levels, and both can use the same four-step cycle (Adsorption → Depressurization → Regeneration → Repressurization). **Hardware distinction**: PSA uses a **blowdown valve**; VSA uses a **vacuum pump**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**State the Langmuir adsorption isotherm. Why is it important in the design of a PSA unit?**

**Model Answer:**
The Langmuir isotherm relates the equilibrium loading of an adsorbate on a solid to its partial pressure at constant temperature:

$$q = \dfrac{q_m \, b \, p}{1 + b \, p}$$

where $q$ is the equilibrium loading (kg/kg), $q_m$ the monolayer saturation capacity, $b$ the Langmuir affinity constant, and $p$ the partial pressure. In PSA design, the **working capacity** $\Delta q = q_{high} - q_{low}$ is calculated from the isotherm at the two operating pressures. The larger $\Delta q$, the more adsorbate is captured per cycle, and the smaller the bed and the lower the cycle frequency. Without the isotherm, one cannot size the bed, the cycle time, or the adsorbent inventory. **Key assumption:** monolayer, uniform surface, no interaction between adsorbed molecules.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1**

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Model Paper Adapted]**
> (a) With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **two-bed Pressure Swing Adsorption (PSA) system** for the production of oxygen-enriched air from atmospheric air. Clearly state the four steps of the cycle. (7 marks)
> (b) For the same PSA unit, derive the expression for the **stoichiometric breakthrough time** $t^*$ and the **Length of Unused Bed (LUB)**. Discuss how LUB affects bed design. (7 marks)

**Model Solution — (a):**
A two-bed PSA for O₂ production uses **zeolite 5A or LiLSX** as the adsorbent, which selectively adsorbs **N₂** (and H₂O, CO₂) over O₂.

- **Step 1 — Adsorption (Bed A, high P):** Compressed air at **5 – 6 atm** enters Bed A. N₂ is selectively adsorbed; **oxygen-enriched product** (90 – 95 % O₂) flows out. Simultaneously, Bed B is on Steps 2 → 4.
- **Step 2 — Depressurization / Blowdown (Bed B):** Bed B is vented to ≈ 1 atm. The adsorbed N₂ begins to desorb; this stream is wasted or recycled.
- **Step 3 — Purge / Regeneration (Bed B):** A small fraction of the O₂ product is sent back through Bed B at low P, sweeping out the desorbed N₂ as **tail gas**.
- **Step 4 — Repressurization (Bed B):** Bed B is repressurized to the high adsorption P using either compressed air or product gas.
- The **PLC / sequencing timer** switches the four inlet/outlet valves so that when Bed A is exhausted, the roles reverse — yielding **continuous O₂ product**.

**Model Solution — (b):** (see Section 3 derivations)

**Stoichiometric time** is obtained by integrating the bed mass balance for a sharp (shock) adsorption front:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t^* &= \dfrac{L}{u}\left(1 + \dfrac{\rho_b \, q_0}{\varepsilon_b \, C_0}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Length of Unused Bed (LUB)** quantifies the unused portion of the bed at breakthrough:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{LUB} &= L\left(1 - \dfrac{t_B}{t^*}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Design implication:** the bed length $L$ must always exceed the LUB; otherwise the product purity target cannot be met regardless of cycle time. Industrial design holds LUB at **15 – 25 % of $L$**. If LUB is too high (e.g., due to large particle size or low gas velocity), one should:
1. **Reduce particle diameter** $d_p$ (improves kinetics, lowers LUB but raises $\Delta P$).
2. **Increase superficial velocity** $u$ (narrows the MTZ relatively) — but limited by pressure drop.
3. **Increase bed length** $L$ (capital cost trade-off).

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2 (Internal Choice Alternative)**

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2023, Model Paper Adapted]**
> (a) Explain the principle of **Vacuum Swing Adsorption (VSA)** for the separation of CO₂ from a biogas stream (50 % CH₄, 50 % CO₂). State the typical adsorbent used and justify your choice. (7 marks)
> (b) A VSA bed has $L = 1.5$ m, $u = 0.15$ m/s, $\rho_b = 650$ kg/m³, $\varepsilon_b = 0.42$, $C_0 = 45$ mol/m³, $q_0 = 2.0$ mol/kg. Calculate the **stoichiometric breakthrough time $t^*$**. If the breakthrough time $t_B = 360$ s, find the LUB and comment on whether the bed length is adequate if the design requires LUB ≤ 0.3 m. (7 marks)

**Model Solution — (a):**
**Principle:** Biogas at ≈ 1 atm enters the VSA bed, which is packed with a CO₂-selective adsorbent. **CO₂ is preferentially adsorbed** because of its strong quadrupole moment and high polarizability; **CH₄ passes through** as the high-purity product. Once the bed is loaded with CO₂, a **vacuum pump** reduces the pressure to ≈ 0.1 – 0.2 atm, causing the CO₂ to desorb. The desorbed CO₂ is recovered (or vented), and the bed is repressurized for the next cycle. The **pressure swing** is between ≈ 1 atm (adsorption) and ≈ 0.1 – 0.3 atm (regeneration), giving a pressure ratio of **3 – 10**.

**Typical adsorbent:** **Zeolite 13X** (or amine-impregnated silica for very high CO₂ selectivity). Justification: zeolite 13X has very high CO₂ capacity (≈ 5 – 8 mol/kg at 1 atm), strong selectivity for CO₂ over CH₄ (≈ 5 – 10 : 1), and regenerates fully under vacuum. **Activated carbon** is a cheaper alternative for less demanding separations.

**Model Solution — (b):**

**Step 1 — Stoichiometric breakthrough time:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t^* &= \dfrac{L}{u}\left(1 + \dfrac{\rho_b \, q_0}{\varepsilon_b \, C_0}\right) \\
&= \dfrac{1.5}{0.15}\left(1 + \dfrac{650 \times 2.0}{0.42 \times 45}\right) \\
&= 10 \times \left(1 + \dfrac{1300}{18.9}\right) \\
&= 10 \times (1 + 68.78) \\
&= 10 \times 69.78 \\
&= 697.8 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Length of Unused Bed:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{LUB} &= L\left(1 - \dfrac{t_B}{t^*}\right) \\
&= 1.5 \times \left(1 - \dfrac{360}{697.8}\right) \\
&= 1.5 \times (1 - 0.516) \\
&= 1.5 \times 0.484 \\
&= 0.726 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Adequacy check:**
Since $\text{LUB} = 0.726$ m **>** 0.3 m, the bed length is **not adequate** to meet the design constraint. **Recommendations:** increase the bed length, reduce the gas velocity $u$ (which raises $t^*$), or use a finer adsorbent to sharpen the mass-transfer zone.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing PSA and VSA**: VSA regeneration uses a *vacuum pump* (sub-atmospheric); PSA regeneration uses a *blowdown valve* (near-atmospheric). Marks are docked for calling VSA a "high-pressure" process.
> 2. **Forgetting the gas-phase accumulation term** in the bed mass balance. The PDE is $\varepsilon_b \partial C/\partial t + \rho_b \partial q/\partial t + u \partial C/\partial z = 0$, **not** just $\rho_b \partial q/\partial t + u \partial C/\partial z = 0$. Losing $\varepsilon_b \partial C/\partial t$ costs ≈ 2 marks in a 7-mark sub-question.
> 3. **Using $L$ in place of LUB** in design checks. The constraint is $\text{LUB} \le L_{\text{allowable}}$, not $L \le L_{\text{allowable}}$.
> 4. **Unit inconsistency**: $q_0$ must be in mol/kg (mass basis) and $C_0$ in mol/m³ (gas-phase concentration). Mixing kg/m³ and mol/m³ silently produces a wrong $t^*$.
> 5. **Forgetting to mention the controller / PLC** in a sketch. KTU examiners look for the **sequencing timer** explicitly; without it, the cycle cannot be automated.
> 6. **Calling the desorbed stream "waste"**: it is the **tail gas** (or **desorbate**), often containing the impurity at high concentration and frequently recovered or used as fuel.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **PSA** = high-P adsorption + near-atmospheric regeneration (blowdown).
- **VSA** = ≈ 1 atm adsorption + sub-atmospheric regeneration (vacuum pump).
- The **driving force** for separation is $\Delta q = q_{high} - q_{low}$ evaluated at the two operating pressures.
- **Langmuir isotherm** $q = q_m b p / (1 + b p)$ gives the equilibrium loading.
- **Governing PDE** of the bed: $\varepsilon_b \partial C/\partial t + \rho_b \partial q/\partial t + u \partial C/\partial z = 0$.
- **Stoichiometric time** $t^* = (L/u)(1 + \rho_b q_0 / \varepsilon_b C_0)$ — the *ideal* breakthrough time under a sharp-front assumption.
- **LUB** $= L (1 - t_B / t^*)$ — must be ≤ 15 – 25 % of $L$ in good design.
- **Cycle time** $t_c \le t_B$ — the bed must be switched **before** breakthrough.
- **Multi-bed** (2-bed minimum, 3-bed common) configuration is required for **continuous product**.
- The **four canonical steps** of any PSA cycle are: **Adsorption → Depressurization → Regeneration → Repressurization**.
- **Working capacity fraction** is small in PSA (10 – 20 % of $q_m$) and large in VSA (40 – 60 % of $q_m$) because of the larger effective pressure ratio.
- **Typical adsorbents**: zeolite 5A (N₂ from O₂), zeolite 13X (CO₂ from CH₄, drying), activated carbon (VOC, H₂ purification), carbon molecular sieve (N₂ from air via kinetics), silica gel (drying).
- **Industrial applications**: medical O₂, refinery H₂, food-grade N₂, biogas upgrading, air drying, trace VOC recovery.
- **KTU-favourite one-liner** to remember:
  > "**PSA swings pressure between high and 1 atm; VSA swings between 1 atm and vacuum. Both rely on $\Delta q$ being non-zero at the two pressures.**"
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Regeneration with Purge and Desorbent
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT752 - POLYMERS AND SEPARATION PROCESSES > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Regeneration with Purge and Desorbent ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Regeneration** is the critical cyclic step in an adsorption separation process wherein the **spent adsorbent** (loaded with previously adsorbed solute/sorbate) is restored to its original active state so that it can be reused for subsequent adsorption cycles. In Module 3 of PEPOT752, regeneration with **purge** and **desorbent** represents two fundamentally distinct engineering strategies to displace the adsorbed species from the solid surface. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition (Exact):** > *Regeneration with purge* refers to the displacement of an adsorbed solute from a saturated bed using an essentially non-adsorbing carrier gas (purge) under isothermal or near-isothermal conditions — typical of Pressure Swing Adsorption (PSA). > *Regeneration with desorbent* refers to the displacement of the solute by a competitively adsorbing third component (the desorbent), as employed in **Simulated Moving Bed (SMB)** chromatography and **displacement chromatography**. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Sponge Metaphor Imagine a **kitchen sponge** that has soaked up **spilled ink** (this is your *spent adsorbent* loaded with *solute*). - **Purge Regeneration:** You hold the sponge under a stream of **clean water** (purge gas). The water molecules slowly *push out* the ink purely by **concentration-driven dilution**. The sponge is restored, but the process is slow and incomplete at low water flow. This is exactly how **purge gas regeneration** works — **no competition, just bulk displacement by an inert stream**. - **Desorbent Regeneration:** You instead dip the sponge into **turpentine** (a *desorbent*). Turpentine molecules have a much *higher affinity* for the sponge fibers than the ink molecules. They **kick out** the ink molecules by **competitive adsorption**. The sponge is restored quickly and completely because the desorbent *wants* to sit on the surface. > [!NOTE] > **Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (KTU Board Focus):** > - Typical purge gas velocities: **0.1 – 1.0 m/s** at STP > - Desorbent-to-feed ratio (SMB): **β = 1.05 – 1.20** (dimensionless) > - Regeneration temperature (TSA): **150 – 350 °C** > - Regeneration pressure (PSA): **1 – 5 bar (adsorption) → 0.1 – 0.5 bar (purge)** > - Adsorbent particle size: **0.5 – 2.0 mm** > - Bed void fraction (ε): **0.35 – 0.45** ### 1.3 Why Regeneration Matters in Polymer/Separation Engineering Adsorbents used in KTU-syllabus polymer processes (e.g., **polymeric resins, activated carbon, zeolites 5A/13X, polymeric ion-exchange beads**) are **expensive**. A single adsorption–regeneration cycle therefore has an enormous economic footprint in industrial units such as: - **H₂ purification** (PSA using zeolite) - **n-Paraffin separation** (using **5A zeolite + n-paraffin desorbent**) - **p-Xylene separation** (Parex™ SMB using **p-diethylbenzene desorbent**) - **Fructose–glucose separation** (SMB on Ca²⁺-form polymeric resin) Without an efficient regeneration step, **cycle time** balloons, **throughput collapses**, and the **adsorbent lifetime** shortens dramatically. ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Underlying Thermodynamic Driving Forces The regeneration step is governed by **three coupled driving forces**, all of which must be addressed in KTU derivations: | Driving Force | Physical Meaning | Operational Mode | |---|---|---| | **Pressure reduction** (ΔP) | Lowers partial pressure of solute → shifts equilibrium | **PSA** — purge regeneration | | **Temperature elevation** (ΔT) | Adsorption is exothermic → heat input desorbs | **TSA** — purge with hot gas | | **Competitive adsorption** (Δn) | Desorbent molecules out-compete solute for sites | **Displacement / SMB** — desorbent regeneration | > [!NOTE] > **Core Principle (Langmuir):** The *fractional coverage* θ of a solute at equilibrium follows > $$\theta_i = \frac{b_i P_i}{1 + \sum_j b_j P_j}$$ > In desorbent regeneration, the term $b_{des} \cdot P_{des}$ becomes very large → θ(solute) → 0. --- ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The table below is the **exam-ready formula compendium** for this topic. Memorize the *form* of each equation — KTU board questions usually plug numbers into these directly. | # | Equation | Meaning of Variables | Engineering Use | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $q^* = \frac{q_m b P}{1 + b P}$ | $q^*$ = equilibrium loading (mol/kg), $q_m$ = max capacity, $b$ = affinity, $P$ = partial pressure | Pure-component Langmuir isotherm | | 2 | $q_i^* = \frac{q_{m,i} b_i P_i}{1 + \sum_k b_k P_k}$ | Multi-component Langmuir (competitive) | Desorbent regeneration analysis | | 3 | $W_{purge} = \dfrac{m_{ads}}{\rho_{gas} \cdot Y_{in}}$ | $W_{purge}$ = purge gas required (m³), $m_{ads}$ = adsorbed solute mass, $Y_{in}$ = inlet mole fraction | Purge gas design | | 4 | $\tau_{regen} = \dfrac{L \cdot \varepsilon}{u_{purge}}$ | $\tau_{regen}$ = regeneration time (s), $L$ = bed length, $\varepsilon$ = voidage, $u_{purge}$ = superficial velocity | Time-of-regeneration for purge | | 5 | $D_{des} = \dfrac{Q_{des}}{Q_{feed}}$ | Desorbent-to-feed ratio (SMB) | SMB operating parameter | | 6 | $\gamma_i = \dfrac{\alpha_{i,j} \cdot X_j - X_i}{\alpha_{i,j} - 1}$ | Separation factor in SMB (Triangle theory) | SMB feasibility | | 7 | $\Delta H_{ads} \approx -RT^2 \dfrac{d(\ln P^0)}{dT}$ | Clausius–Clapeyron for adsorption | TSA regeneration energy | | 8 | $\eta_{regen} = \dfrac{q_{loaded} - q_{residual}}{q_{loaded}} \times 100\%$ | Regeneration efficiency (%) | Performance metric | --- ### 2.3 Operational Step-Wise Logic #### A. Purge Regeneration (Isothermal / PSA / TSA mode) **Step 1 — Bed isolation:** The saturated adsorption bed is isolated from the feed stream using rotary or multi-port valves. **Step 2 — Pressure reduction (PSA) or heating (TSA):** The thermodynamic driving force is shifted so the solute's equilibrium loading $q^*$ drops. **Step 3 — Purge introduction:** A non-adsorbing carrier (N₂, He, steam, air) sweeps the bed. Boundary condition: $$P_{solute}^{in} = 0 \quad \text{(infinite sink)}$$ **Step 4 — Desorption wave propagation:** A **desorption wave** (analogous to but inverse of the adsorption mass-transfer zone) moves down the bed at velocity $u_d$. **Step 5 — Re-pressurization / cooling:** The bed is returned to adsorption conditions. #### B.
