\frac{y_A^{\,z_B} \, x_B^{\,z_A}}{y_B^{\,z_A} \, x_A^{\,z_B}}\;}$$ - If $\alpha_B^A > 1$ → resin **prefers A** (A is preferentially held on resin). - If $\alpha_B^A < 1$ → resin **prefers B**. - For **monovalent–monovalent** ($z_A = z_B = 1$), the expression simplifies to: $$\alpha_B^A \;=\; \frac{y_A \, x_B}{y_B \, x_A}$$ ### 2.2 Equilibrium Isotherm Relationship Re-arranging the selectivity expression to give the equilibrium curve $y_A = f(x_A)$: $$y_A^{\,z_B} \,(1 - x_A)^{\,z_A} \;=\; \alpha_B^A \, y_B^{\,z_A} \, x_A^{\,z_B}$$ For the special case $z_A = z_B = 1$: $$y_A \;=\; \frac{\alpha_B^A \, x_A}{1 \;+\; (\alpha_B^A - 1)\, x_A}$$ This is the **Langmuir-type equilibrium isotherm** for ion exchange, plotted as $y_A$ vs $x_A$ on the KTU McCabe–Thiele diagram. ### 2.3 Material Balance (Operating Line) Consider a counter-current or co-current contactor with: - $L$ = solution flow rate (eq/s or L/s) - $H$ = resin flow rate (eq/s or L/s) - $x_F$ = equivalent fraction of A in feed solution - $x$ = equivalent fraction of A in treated solution leaving stage - $y_0$ = equivalent fraction of A on entering (regenerated) resin - $y$ = equivalent fraction of A on exiting (loaded) resin A **stage-wise mass balance on A** gives the operating line: $$\boxed{\;H\,(y - y_0) \;=\; L\,(x_F - x)\;}$$ or in slope-intercept form: $$y \;=\; y_0 \;+\; \frac{L}{H}\,(x_F - x)$$ This is a **straight line** on the $y$–$x$ diagram with slope $-L/H$ (negative because the operating line goes from upper-left to lower-right in a stripping-type representation). > [!NOTE] > For KTU problems, $L$ and $H$ are usually expressed in **equivalents per unit time**, ensuring the material balance is consistent in equivalents (not moles or kilograms). ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Equation | Meaning | Typical Units / Notes | |---|----------|---------|----------------------| | 1 | $x_A = \dfrac{z_A C_A}{z_A C_A + z_B C_B}$ | Equivalent fraction in solution | dimensionless, 0–1 | | 2 | $y_A = \dfrac{z_A q_A}{z_A q_A + z_B q_B}$ | Equivalent fraction on resin | dimensionless, 0–1 | | 3 | $K_B^A = \dfrac{\bar{a}_A^{z_B} a_B^{z_A}}{a_A^{z_B} \bar{a}_B^{z_A}}$ | True thermodynamic equilibrium constant | based on activities | | 4 | $\alpha_B^A = \dfrac{y_A^{z_B} x_B^{z_A}}{y_B^{z_A} x_A^{z_B}}$ | Selectivity coefficient (practical) | dimensionless | | 5 | $y_A = \dfrac{\alpha_B^A x_A}{1 + (\alpha_B^A - 1) x_A}$ | Equilibrium isotherm ($z_A = z_B = 1$) | valid for 1:1 exchange | | 6 | $H(y - y_0) = L(x_F - x)$ | Stage material balance | eq/time consistent | | 7 | $y = y_0 + (L/H)(x_F - x)$ | Operating line | slope $-L/H$ | | 8 | $q_A = y_A \cdot Q$ | Resin loading | eq/L of wet resin | | 9 | $C_A = x_A \cdot N$ | Solution concentration | eq/L of solution | | 10 | Number of stages via McCabe–Thiele stepping | Graphical method | between op-line and eq-curve | > [!TIP] > **Why it matters in engineering:** Binary equilibrium tells the designer **how much resin is needed (mass-transfer zone length)**, **how long a column will run before breakthrough**, and **what regeneration level is required** to restore the resin. These three answers drive the entire CAPEX and OPEX of a water-treatment plant. ### 2.5 Physical Meaning of Selectivity Reversal For a **divalent ion competing with a monovalent ion** (e.g., Ca²⁺ vs Na⁺ on a SAC resin), the equilibrium expression becomes: $$\alpha_{Na}^{Ca} \;=\; \frac{y_{Ca}\, x_{Na}^{\,2}}{y_{Na}^{\,2}\, x_{Ca}}$$ Note the exponents — divalent ion concentration in solution is **squared** in the denominator of the equivalent fraction term, which makes the selectivity **strongly concentration-dependent**: - At **high total normality (N)**, monovalent ions win. - At **low total normality (N)**, divalent ions win. - This reversal is why **water softeners must be regenerated with concentrated NaCl (≈ 10%)** — to push the equilibrium back toward the Na-form resin. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Equilibrium Isotherm for 1:1 Exchange **Starting Point:** Mass-action selectivity coefficient for $A^+ + \overline{B^+} \rightleftharpoons \overline{A^+} + B^+$: $$\alpha_B^A \;=\; \frac{y_A \, x_B}{y_B \, x_A}$$ **Step 1 — Substitute the binary constraints:** For a binary system, $y_B = 1 - y_A$ and $x_B = 1 - x_A$. $$\alpha_B^A \;=\; \frac{y_A \,(1 - x_A)}{(1 - y_A)\, x_A}$$ **Step 2 — Cross-multiply to isolate $y_A$:** $$\alpha_B^A \,(1 - y_A)\, x_A \;=\; y_A \,(1 - x_A)$$ **Step 3 — Expand the left-hand side:** $$\alpha_B^A x_A \;-\; \alpha_B^A x_A y_A \;=\; y_A - y_A x_A$$ **Step 4 — Collect all $y_A$ terms on the right-hand side:** $$\alpha_B^A x_A \;=\; y_A - y_A x_A + \alpha_B^A x_A y_A$$ **Step 5 — Factor $y_A$ on the right:** $$\alpha_B^A x_A \;=\; y_A \left[\,1 - x_A + \alpha_B^A x_A\,\right]$$ **Step 6 — Solve for $y_A$:** $$y_A \;=\; \frac{\alpha_B^A x_A}{1 - x_A + \alpha_B^A x_A} \;=\; \frac{\alpha_B^A x_A}{1 + (\alpha_B^A - 1) x_A}$$ This is the **famous Langmuir-type ion-exchange isotherm** for 1:1 exchange — a result that KTU examiners love because it directly mirrors the Langmuir adsorption isotherm and connects the two unit operations. --- ### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Monovalent Exchange) **Problem:** A strong-acid cation resin in the H⁺ form is contacted with a feed solution containing 0.06 eq/L Na⁺ and 0.04 eq/L H⁺. The selectivity coefficient $\alpha_H^{Na} = 1.6$. Calculate the equilibrium equivalent fraction of Na⁺ on the resin. **Step 1 — Compute $x_{Na}$ (feed composition):** $$x_{Na} \;=\; \frac{C_{Na}}{C_{Na} + C_{H}} \;=\; \frac{0.06}{0.06 + 0.04} \;=\; 0.60$$ **Step 2 — Apply the equilibrium isotherm:** $$y_{Na} \;=\; \frac{\alpha_H^{Na} \, x_{Na}}{1 + (\alpha_H^{Na} - 1) x_{Na}} \;=\; \frac{1.6 \times 0.60}{1 + (1.6 - 1)(0.60)}$$ **Step 3 — Compute numerator and denominator separately:** $$\text{Numerator} \;=\; 0.960$$ $$\text{Denominator} \;=\; 1 + 0.6 \times 0.60 \;=\; 1 + 0.36 \;=\; 1.36$$ **Step 4 — Final value:** $$y_{Na} \;=\; \frac{0.960}{1.36} \;=\; 0.706$$ **Interpretation:** Although the feed is only 60% Na⁺, the resin holds 70.6% Na⁺ at equilibrium because $\alpha_H^{Na} > 1$ — confirming the resin's **preference for Na⁺** over H⁺. --- ### 3.3 Derivation of the Operating-Line Equation (Cross-Current / Counter-Current Stage) **Assumption:** A single ideal equilibrium stage where solution of composition $x$ leaves in equilibrium with resin of composition $y$, and the entering streams are $x_F$ (solution) and $y_0$ (resin). **Mass balance on A** (steady state, no reaction, no accumulation): $$\text{A in (solution)} + \text{A in (resin)} \;=\; \text{A out (solution)} + \text{A{A out (resin)}}$$ $$L \, x_F + H \, y_0 \;=\; L \, x + H \, y$$ **Rearrange:** $$L \, x_F - L \, x \;=\; H \, y - H \, y_0$$ $$\boxed{\;L\,(x_F - x) \;=\; H\,(y - y_0)\;}$$ **Slope–intercept form:** $$y \;=\; y_0 + \frac{L}{H}(x_F - x)$$ > [!NOTE] > This is the **operating line** drawn between the points $(x_F, y_0)$ on the diagonal $y = x$ and the intersection of the operating-line equation with the equilibrium curve. The number of theoretical stages is found by **stepping** between the operating line and the equilibrium isotherm, exactly as in distillation McCabe–Thiele analysis. --- ### 3.4 Number of Theoretical Stages — McCabe–Thiele Construction Procedure **Procedure for KTU problems:** 1. **Plot the equilibrium curve** $y_A$ vs $x_A$ using the isotherm equation. 2. **Plot the operating line** joining $(x_F, y_0)$ to $(x, y)$ with slope $-L/H$. 3. **Locate the rectifying/stripping intersection** (for multi-stage problems with feed and regeneration). 4. **Step off stages** from the operating line to the equilibrium curve, alternating horizontal and vertical moves. 5. **Count the number of "steps"** — this gives $N_{th}$, the number of theoretical stages. 6. The **L/V analogy** from distillation: $L/H$ plays the role of $L/V$, and the operating line is analogous to the rectifying/stripping section line. --- ### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Effect of Selectivity on Stage Requirement | $\alpha_B^A$ | Resin Preference | Equilibrium Curve Shape | Implication on Stages | |---------------|------------------|--------------------------|------------------------| | $\alpha_B^A = 1$ | No preference (ideal) | Straight diagonal line $y = x$ | **Infinite stages** required for separation | | $\alpha_B^A > 1$ | Prefers A | Curve **bows above** the diagonal | Fewer stages; easy separation | | $\alpha_B^A < 1$ | Prefers B | Curve **bows below** the diagonal | Roles of A and B are reversed | | $\alpha_B^A \to \infty$ | Strongly prefers A | Curve hugs the top edge | **One stage** sufficient | | $\alpha_B^A \to 0$ | Strongly prefers B | Curve hugs the bottom edge | Effectively **no uptake** of A | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Conceptual Process Flow: Binary Ion Exchange Equilibrium ```mermaid flowchart LR node1["FEED SOLUTION
Contains ion A plus counter ion B
Composition xF equiv fraction of A"] node2["MIXING ZONE
Solution and resin in
intimate contact"] node3["ION EXCHANGE RESIN
Fixed negative sites with
counter ion B initially"] node4["EXITING SOLUTION
Depleted in A
Composition x"] node5["EXITING RESIN
Loaded with A
Composition y"] node6["REGENERATED RESIN
Return to ion B form
Composition y0"] node7["REGENERANT
Strong acid or salt
Restores B form"] node1 --> node2 node3 --> node2 node2 --> node4 node2 --> node5 node5 --> node6 node7 --> node6 node6 --> node3 style node1 fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0b5ed7 style node2 fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#cc9a06 style node3 fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#b02a37 style node4 fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#146c43 style node5 fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#b02a37 style node6 fill:#e2e3e5,stroke:#41464b style node7 fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0b5ed7 ``` ### 4.2 McCabe–Thiele Construction for Binary Ion Exchange ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA["STEP 1
Draw y versus x axes
Both from 0 to 1"] nodeB["STEP 2
Plot equilibrium isotherm
y = alpha x divided by 1 plus alpha minus 1 times x"] nodeC["STEP 3
Plot operating line
from point xF y0
slope negative L over H"] nodeD["STEP 4
Start at point xF y0
on operating line"] nodeE["STEP 5
Move vertically to
equilibrium curve"] nodeF["STEP 6
Move horizontally back
to operating line"] nodeG["STEP 7
Repeat stepping until
target x is reached"] nodeH["STEP 8
Count number of steps
equals Nth theoretical stages"] nodeA --> nodeB nodeB --> nodeC nodeC --> nodeD nodeD --> nodeE nodeE --> nodeF nodeF --> nodeG nodeG --> nodeH style nodeA fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#146c43 style nodeB fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#cc9a06 style nodeC fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0b5ed7 nodeD --> nodeE style nodeE fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#b02a37 style nodeF fill:#e2e3e5,stroke:#41464b style nodeG fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0b5ed7 style nodeH fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#146c43 ``` ### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Binary Exchange System ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph INPUT["INPUT STREAMS"] inp1["Solution feed
Equivalent fraction xF
Normality N"] inp2["Resin feed
Equivalent fraction y0
Capacity Q"] end subgraph CORE["EQUILIBRIUM STAGE"] eq1["Selectivity coefficient
alpha B to the A
defines y as function of x"] eq2["Mass balance
H times y minus y0 equals L times xF minus x"] end subgraph OUTPUT["OUTPUT STREAMS"] out1["Treated solution
Equivalent fraction x
Normality unchanged"] out2["Loaded resin
Equivalent fraction y
Working capacity delta Q"] end inp1 --> eq1 inp1 --> eq2 inp2 --> eq2 eq1 --> out1 eq2 --> out2 style inp1 fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0b5ed7 style inp2 fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#b02a37 style eq1 fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#cc9a06 style eq2 fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#cc9a06 style out1 fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#146c43 style out2 fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#b02a37 ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define **selectivity coefficient** for a binary ion exchange system. Explain its significance in designing ion exchange columns. **Model Answer:** > The **selectivity coefficient** $\alpha_B^A$ for the binary exchange $A^{z_A+} / B^{z_B+}$ is defined as: > $$\alpha_B^A \;=\; \frac{y_A^{z_B}\, x_B^{z_A}}{y_B^{z_A}\, x_A^{z_B}}$$ > where $y$ and $x$ are equivalent fractions on the resin and in solution, respectively. A value of $\alpha_B^A > 1$ indicates that the resin preferentially holds ion A; a value less than unity indicates preference for B. Its significance lies in: > 1. Predicting the **equilibrium loading** of the resin at any solution composition. > 2. Determining the **shape of the equilibrium isotherm** used in McCabe–Thiele analysis. > 3. Estimating the **regenerant dosage** required to reverse the loading. > 4. Selecting the **type of resin** (strong vs weak acid/base) for a given separation task. *(3 marks — definition 1, significance 2)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** Distinguish between **equivalent fraction** and **mole fraction** in the context of binary ion exchange. Why is the equivalent fraction used in equilibrium calculations? **Model Answer:** > **Equivalent fraction** uses the product of concentration and valence (i.e., $z_i C_i$), while **mole fraction** uses only the molar concentration. The equivalent fraction is preferred because: > 1. It automatically accounts for the **stoichiometry of the exchange** (a Ca²⁺ ion displaces two Na⁺ ions). > 2. It maintains the **electroneutrality constraint** in both phases. > 3. It directly gives the **loading on the resin** in eq/L, which adds up to the total capacity $Q$. > 4. The material balance in a stage becomes a simple linear equation in equivalent fractions. *(3 marks — distinction 1, reasons 2)* --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** > - **Mark Loss Pitfall 1:** Students often confuse the **selectivity coefficient** with the **equilibrium constant**. The former is a practical ratio of equivalent fractions; the latter is a true thermodynamic constant based on activities. KTU examiners deduct 1–2 marks if these are used interchangeably. > - **Mark Loss Pitfall 2:** Forgetting to **square or cube** the equivalent fractions when the ions are divalent or trivalent. Always write the exponents $z_B$ and $z_A$ explicitly. > - **Mark Loss Pitfall 3:** Mixing up the **operating-line slope** sign. The slope is $-L/H$ in the $y$–$x$ plane; some students plot it positive, which reverses the McCabe–Thiele stepping. > - **Mark Loss Pitfall 4:** Not specifying whether the resin is in the **A-form or B-form** at the start — this affects $y_0$ and hence the operating line. > - **Mark Loss Pitfall 5:** Leaving the answer as an unsimplified algebraic expression when a numerical answer is asked. Always substitute numbers in the **final step**. --- #### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 — Model Paper]** **(a)** Derive the **equilibrium isotherm equation** for a 1:1 binary ion exchange starting from the mass-action selectivity coefficient. Discuss the limiting cases of $\alpha = 1$, $\alpha \to \infty$, and $\alpha \to 0$. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A strong-acid cation resin in the H⁺ form is used to soften a water supply containing 0.005 eq/L Ca²⁺ and 0.015 eq/L Na⁺. The selectivity coefficient $\alpha_{Na}^{Ca} = 3.0$ and the total resin capacity $Q = 1.8$ eq/L. Calculate the **equilibrium loading of Ca²⁺ on the resin** (in eq/L) using the appropriate binary equilibrium expression. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution — Part (a):** **Step 1 — Write the mass-action expression for 1:1 exchange:** $$A^+ + \overline{B^+} \rightleftharpoons \overline{A^+} + B^+$$ $$\alpha_B^A \;=\; \frac{y_A \, x_B}{y_B \, x_A}$$ **Step 2 — Substitute binary constraints** $y_B = 1 - y_A$ and $x_B = 1 - x_A$: $$\alpha_B^A \;=\; \frac{y_A (1 - x_A)}{(1 - y_A) x_A}$$ **Step 3 — Cross-multiply and expand:** $$\alpha_B^A (1 - y_A) x_A \;=\; y_A (1 - x_A)$$ $$\alpha_B^A x_A - \alpha_B^A x_A y_A \;=\; y_A - y_A x_A$$ **Step 4 — Collect $y_A$ terms:** $$\alpha_B^A x_A \;=\; y_A [1 - x_A + \alpha_B^A x_A]$$ **Step 5 — Solve:** $$y_A \;=\; \frac{\alpha_B^A x_A}{1 + (\alpha_B^A - 1) x_A} \quad \text{...(final isotherm)}$$ **Step 6 — Limiting cases:** - **$\alpha_B^A = 1$:** $y_A = x_A$ — equilibrium curve is the diagonal; **no separation possible**. - **$\alpha_B^A \to \infty$:** $y_A \to \dfrac{x_A}{x_A} = 1$ — resin is fully in A-form at any finite $x_A$; **one equilibrium stage suffices**. - **$\alpha_B^A \to 0$:** $y_A \to 0$ — resin rejects A entirely; **A remains in solution**. *(7 marks — derivation 4, limiting cases 3)* --- **Model Solution — Part (b):** **Step 1 — Identify the valences:** $z_{Ca} = 2$, $z_{Na} = 1$. **Step 2 — Compute the equivalent fraction of Ca²⁺ in solution:** $$x_{Ca} \;=\; \frac{z_{Ca} C_{Ca}}{z_{Ca} C_{Ca} + z_{Na} C_{Na}} \;=\; \frac{2 \times 0.005}{2 \times 0.005 + 1 \times 0.015} \;=\; \frac{0.010}{0.025} \;=\; 0.40$$ **Step 3 — Write the general binary equilibrium expression with exponents:** $$\alpha_{Na}^{Ca} \;=\; \frac{y_{Ca}^{1} \, x_{Na}^{2}}{y_{Na}^{2} \, x_{Ca}} \;=\; \frac{y_{Ca}\,(1 - x_{Ca})^2}{(1 - y_{Ca})^2 \, x_{Ca}}$$ **Step 4 — Solve for $y_{Ca}$ algebraically first.** Let $u = y_{Ca}$ and substitute the known values: $$3.0 \;=\; \frac{u \,(0.60)^2}{(1 - u)^2 \,(0.40)}$$ $$3.0 \times 0.40 \times (1 - u)^2 \;=\; u \times 0.36$$ $$1.2 (1 - 2u + u^2) \;=\; 0.36 u$$ $$1.2 - 2.4 u + 1.2 u^2 \;=\; 0.36 u$$ $$1.2 u^2 - 2.76 u + 1.2 \;=\; 0$$ **Step 5 — Solve the quadratic $1.2 u^2 - 2.76 u + 1.2 = 0$:** $$u \;=\; \frac{2.76 \pm \sqrt{(2.76)^2 - 4 \times 1.2 \times 1.2}}{2 \times 1.2}$$ $$u \;=\; \frac{2.76 \pm \sqrt{7.6176 - 5.76}}{2.4} \;=\; \frac{2.76 \pm \sqrt{1.8576}}{2.4}$$ $$\sqrt{1.8576} \;\approx\; 1.363$$ $$u_1 \;=\; \frac{2.76 + 1.363}{2.4} \;=\; \frac{4.123}{2.4} \;\approx\; 1.718 \;\;(\text{rejected, since } y_{Ca} \leq 1)$$ $$u_2 \;=\; \frac{2.76 - 1.363}{2.4} \;=\; \frac{1.397}{2.4} \;\approx\; 0.582$$ **Step 6 — Compute the equilibrium loading of Ca²⁺ on the resin:** $$q_{Ca} \;=\; y_{Ca} \times Q \;=\; 0.582 \times 1.8 \;=\; 1.048 \;\text{eq/L}$$ **Result:** The resin holds approximately **1.05 eq/L of Ca²⁺** at equilibrium, despite the solution being only 40% Ca²⁺ on an equivalent basis — confirming the strong preference of the SAC resin for the divalent ion. *(7 marks — setup 2, quadratic 3, computation and interpretation 2)* --- #### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) **[KTU University Exam – June 2023 — Past Paper]** **(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **McCabe–Thiele construction** for determining the number of theoretical stages in a binary ion exchange cascade. Clearly indicate the operating line and the equilibrium curve. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A counter-current ion exchange contactor treats a feed containing 0.04 eq/L Cu²⁺ and 0.06 eq/L Zn²⁺. The entering regenerated resin is in the H⁺ form ($y_0 = 0$ for Cu²⁺). The solution-to-resin flow ratio is $L/H = 1.5$. The selectivity coefficient $\alpha_H^{Cu} = 2.5$. Determine the **number of theoretical stages** required to achieve a raffinate composition of $x_{Cu} = 0.10$. Assume the equilibrium is given by $y_{Cu} = \dfrac{\alpha x_{Cu}}{1 + (\alpha - 1) x_{Cu}}$. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution — Part (a):** **Sketch description (to be drawn on answer sheet):** - **X-axis:** $x_{Cu}$ (equivalent fraction in solution), 0 to 1. - **Y-axis:** $y_{Cu}$ (equivalent fraction on resin), 0 to 1. - **Diagonal line:** $y = x$ (reference). - **Equilibrium curve:** drawn from $y = 0$ at $x = 0$ to $y = 1$ at $x = 1$, lying above the diagonal because $\alpha_H^{Cu} = 2.5 > 1$. - **Operating line:** straight line joining $(x_F, y_0) = (x_F, 0)$ on the x-axis to the intersection point on the equilibrium curve, with slope $-L/H = -1.5$. **Stepping procedure:** 1. Start at the point $(x_F, y_0)$ on the operating line. 2. Move **vertically** to the equilibrium curve to find the equilibrium resin composition $y_1$. 3. Move **horizontally** to the operating line to find the new solution composition $x_1$. 4. Repeat the process, alternating vertical and horizontal moves, until the solution composition reaches the desired raffinate value $x = 0.10$. 5. Count the number of "triangular steps" — this is $N_{th}$. **Key points to mention in the answer:** - The operating line is a consequence of the **material balance** on a stage. - The equilibrium curve is a consequence of the **mass-action selectivity** expression. - For $N_{th} = 1$, the operating line and equilibrium curve intersect exactly at the desired raffinate point. - The construction is **analogous to binary distillation**, with $L/H$ playing the role of $L/V$. *(7 marks — sketch 3, procedure 2, analogy 2)* --- **Model Solution — Part (b):** **Step 1 — Compute $x_{Cu,F}$ in the feed:** $$x_{Cu,F} \;=\; \frac{z_{Cu} C_{Cu}}{z_{Cu} C_{Cu} + z_{Zn} C_{Zn}} \;=\; \frac{2 \times 0.04}{2 \times 0.04 + 2 \times 0.06} \;=\; \frac{0.08}{0.20} \;=\; 0.40$$ (Note: since both ions are divalent, equivalent fraction equals mole fraction.) **Step 2 — Operating line equation.** With $y_0 = 0$: $$y \;=\; 0 + 1.5 \times (0.40 - x) \;=\; 0.60 - 1.5 x$$ **Step 3 — Equilibrium isotherm (using the given simplified form):** $$y_{Cu} \;=\; \frac{2.5\, x_{Cu}}{1 + 1.5\, x_{Cu}}$$ **Step 4 — Tabulate $y_{eq}$ at successive $x$ values for McCabe–Thiele stepping:** | $x_{Cu}$ | $y_{eq} = \dfrac{2.5 x}{1 + 1.5 x}$ | $y_{op} = 0.60 - 1.5 x$ | |-----------|--------------------------------------|--------------------------| | 0.00 | 0.000 | 0.600 | | 0.05 | 0.116 | 0.525 | | 0.10 | 0.213 | 0.450 | | 0.15 | 0.294 | 0.375 | | 0.20 | 0.364 | 0.300 | | 0.25 | 0.417 | 0.225 | | 0.30 | 0.476 | 0.150 | | 0.35 | 0.519 | 0.075 | | 0.40 | 0.571 | 0.000 | **Step 5 — Perform the stepping construction logically** (described below for a written answer): - **Stage 1:** Start at $(x = 0.40, y = 0)$ on the operating line. Move vertically up to the equilibrium curve: $y_{eq}(0.40) = 0.571$. - **Stage 1 cont'd:** Move horizontally from $y = 0.571$ back to the operating line. The operating line gives $x = (0.60 - 0.571)/1.5 = 0.0193$. So after one stage, the raffinate is $x \approx 0.02$, which is **already below the target of 0.10**. **Step 6 — Interpretation:** A single theoretical stage is more than sufficient to bring the raffinate from 0.40 down to 0.10 because the selectivity is favorable ($\alpha = 2.5$) and the flow ratio is also favorable ($L/H = 1.5$). - In practice, **$N_{th} \approx 1$** is reported, with a small safety factor to account for stage inefficiency. - If the question demands counting sub-steps: even with very close inspection, the target is crossed within the **first step**, so $N_{th} = 1$. *(7 marks — operating line 2, equilibrium 2, stepping 2, conclusion 1)* --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Binary Ion Exchange Equilibrium** - [x] **Binary exchange** involves exactly **two counter-ions** swapping between resin and solution. - [x] The **stoichiometric reaction** is $z_B A^{z_A+} + z_A \bar{B}^{z_B+} \rightleftharpoons z_B \bar{A}^{z_A+} + z_A B^{z_B+}$. - [x] **Equivalent fraction** $x_A = z_A C_A / (z_A C_A + z_B C_B)$ is the preferred composition variable. - [x] **Selectivity coefficient** $\alpha_B^A = (y_A^{z_B} x_B^{z_A}) / (y_B^{z_A} x_A^{z_B})$ — dimensionless, practical (not thermodynamic). - [x] **$\alpha > 1$** → resin prefers A; **$\alpha < 1$** → resin prefers B; **$\alpha = 1$** → no preference. - [x] For 1:1 exchange: $y_A = \alpha x_A / [1 + (\alpha - 1) x_A]$ — **Langmuir-type isotherm**. - [x] For 2:1 or 1:2 exchange, **exponents** matter — do not forget to square concentration-like terms. - [x] **Material balance:** $H(y - y_0) = L(x_F - x)$ — operating line in $y$–$x$ plane. - [x] **Slope** of operating line is $-L/H$; passes through $(x_F, y_0)$. - [x] **McCabe–Thiele construction** gives number of theoretical stages — analogous to distillation. - [x] **Divalent ions** are preferred at **low normality**; **monovalent ions** preferred at **high normality** → explains why regeneration uses concentrated NaCl. - [x] Selectivity is **temperature-dependent**, **concentration-dependent**, and **resin-type-dependent**. - [x] Always specify the **valences** $z_A$ and $z_B$ before writing the equilibrium expression. - [x] For KTU numerical problems, **show all algebraic steps** — partial credit is given for correct setup. - [x] **Final numerical answer** must be a clean number with correct units (eq/L for loading, dimensionless for fractions).

topics.title: Ion Movement Theory
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT752 - POLYMERS AND SEPARATION PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Ion Movement Theory

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Ion Movement Theory** in the context of polymers and separation processes refers to the body of physico-chemical principles governing the transport, exchange, and equilibrium distribution of ions across **ion-exchange polymer membranes** and **ion-exchange resin matrices**. It encompasses three interconnected sub-theories:

1. **Donnan Membrane Equilibrium Theory** – governs the steady-state ionic distribution at the polymer–solution interface.
2. **Nernst–Planck Transport Theory** – governs the flux of ions under combined gradients of concentration and electrical potential.
3. **Selectivity & Mass-Transfer Theory** – governs the preferential uptake of one ionic species over another and the rate-limiting step of the overall process.

Mathematically, the driving force for ion movement is described as the negative gradient of the **electrochemical potential**, $\tilde{\mu}_i$, of the ionic species $i$.

$$\tilde{\mu}_i = \mu_i^0 + RT \ln a_i + z_i F \phi$$

where:
- $\mu_i^0$ = standard chemical potential (J/mol)
- $R$ = universal gas constant = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)
- $a_i$ = activity of species $i$
- $z_i$ = valence (charge number) of ion $i$
- $F$ = Faraday constant = **96,485 C/mol**
- $\phi$ = electrical potential (V)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Highlight:** Whenever an examiner asks "What drives ion movement?", the one-word answer is **electrochemical potential gradient**. Do not write only "concentration gradient" — that costs you 1 mark.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine a **currency exchange counter at an airport**. You walk in with US Dollars (your counter-ion) and want Indian Rupees (your target ion). The counter has a fixed pool of Rupees stuck in its drawer (the **fixed ion** on the polymer backbone), and it will only release them if you hand over an equivalent charge of Dollars (or any foreign currency of matching value).

- The **drawer full of Rupees** = the immobile fixed charge group (e.g., $-\text{SO}_3^-$ on a cation-exchange resin).
- **You and your Dollars** = the mobile counter-ion in solution.
- **The exchange rate** posted at the window = the **selectivity coefficient**.
- **How fast the teller counts your money** = the **mass-transfer / kinetic rate**.
- **The glass window itself** = the **Donnan membrane** — only certain ions can physically cross it, dictated by charge balance and electroneutrality.

This is precisely what happens inside an **ion-exchange polymer**: charged functional groups anchored to the polymer chain act as a "currency vault," and ions in solution trade places based on electrostatic and chemical affinity rules.

---

## 1.3 The Polymer Backbone — Why Polymers?

Ion-exchange materials are almost always **cross-linked polymers** (e.g., polystyrene-divinylbenzene, polyacrylate, perfluorosulfonic acid like Nafion™) because:

- The **polymer matrix** provides mechanical strength.
- The **fixed ionic groups** grafted to the backbone cannot leave — they create the Donnan exclusion field.
- The **cross-link density** controls pore size and therefore selectivity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Hook:** KTU 2024 Scheme Module 3 explicitly links **polymer structure → fixed-ion concentration → Donnan exclusion → selectivity**. Memorize this chain — it appears in 14-mark questions.

---

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants (Bold Reference)

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Value / Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $F$ | Faraday constant | **96,485 C/mol** |
| $R$ | Gas constant | **8.314 J/(mol·K)** |
| $RT/F$ | Thermal voltage at 25 °C | **0.02569 V** |
| $\epsilon$ | Dielectric constant of water | **78.5 (dimensionless)** |
| $z_i$ | Valence | typically +1, +2, –1, –2 |
| $\bar{C}_R$ | Fixed ion conc. in resin | **1–5 eq/L** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Ion Movement Theory

### Pillar 1 — Donnan Membrane Equilibrium

When an ion-exchange polymer (containing fixed anionic groups, e.g., $-\text{SO}_3^-$ with mobile counter-ions $\text{H}^+$ or $\text{Na}^+$) is immersed in an electrolyte solution (e.g., $\text{NaCl}$), equilibrium demands **electroneutrality in both phases** AND equality of electrochemical potentials for each diffusible species.

The condition derived (full derivation in Section 3) is the **Donnan product rule**:

$$\left( \bar{a}_{\text{Na}^+} \right)\left( \bar{a}_{\text{Cl}^-} \right) = \left( a_{\text{Na}^+} \right)\left( a_{\text{Cl}^-} \right)$$

where overbar denotes the resin (polymer) phase.

This simple equation produces two dramatic consequences:

1. **Donnan Exclusion of Co-ions:** Because the resin has fixed negative charges ($\bar{C}_R$), electroneutrality forces $\bar{a}_{\text{Cl}^-} \ll a_{\text{Cl}^-}$. Co-ions (ions of the same sign as the fixed group) are largely **excluded** from the polymer phase.
2. **Selective Enrichment of Counter-ions:** The counter-ion ($\text{Na}^+$) concentration inside the resin becomes much **higher** than in the bulk solution — typically 5–50× for a strongly acidic cation resin in dilute NaCl.

### Pillar 2 — Nernst–Planck Transport Equation

Once the system is perturbed (concentration gradient, applied voltage, or both), ions move. The molar flux $J_i$ of species $i$ is:

$$J_i = -D_i \frac{dC_i}{dx} - \frac{z_i F D_i C_i}{RT}\frac{d\phi}{dx} + C_i v$$

The three terms represent, respectively:
- **Molecular diffusion** (Fick's first law term).
- **Electromigration** (driven by the electric field, $d\phi/dx$).
- **Convection** (bulk flow with velocity $v$; usually zero in ion exchange).

For an ion-exchange resin where convection is absent:

$$J_i = -D_i \left( \frac{dC_i}{dx} + \frac{z_i F C_i}{RT}\frac{d\phi}{dx} \right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Trick Question:** "Is ion movement in an ion-exchange resin governed by Fick's law alone?"
> **Correct Answer:** No. It is governed by the **Nernst–Planck equation**, which combines diffusion AND migration. Fick's law alone is valid only when the electric field term vanishes (e.g., in symmetric tracer diffusion or at high ionic strength where electrostatic effects are screened).

### Pillar 3 — Selectivity Theory

When two counter-ions $\text{A}^{z_A}$ and $\text{B}^{z_B}$ compete for the same fixed sites, the **selectivity coefficient** $K_{\text{B}}^{\text{A}}$ is defined as:

$$K_{\text{B}}^{\text{A}} = \frac{\bar{a}_{\text{A}}^{1/z_A} \, a_{\text{B}}^{1/z_B}}{\bar{a}_{\text{B}}^{1/z_B} \, a_{\text{A}}^{1/z_A}}$$

A value $K > 1$ means the resin prefers ion A over ion B. Selectivity generally follows:

| Rule of Thumb | Explanation |
| :--- | :--- |
| Higher valence preferred | $\text{Th}^{4+} > \text{Al}^{3+} > \text{Ca}^{2+} > \text{Na}^+$ |
| Smaller hydrated radius preferred | $\text{Li}^+ < \text{Na}^+ < \text{K}^+ < \text{Cs}^+$ (after hydration correction) |
| More polarizable preferred | Organic ions on hydrophobic resins |

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield Table)

> [!NOTE]
> The vertical bar symbol has been replaced with `\vert` and `\mid` so the markdown table renders correctly.

| # | Equation Name | Formula | Key Variables | Engineering Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Electrochemical potential | $\tilde{\mu}_i = \mu_i^0 + RT\ln a_i + z_i F \phi$ | $a_i$, $z_i$, $\phi$ | Defines driving force |
| 2 | Donnan product rule | $\bar{a}_+ \bar{a}_- = a_+ a_-$ | activities | Predicts co-ion exclusion |
| 3 | Donnan distribution | $\frac{\bar{C}_+}{C_+} = \left( \frac{\bar{C}_R + \sqrt{\bar{C}_R^2 + 4C^2}}{2C} \right)^{z_+/ \vert z_- \vert}$ | $\bar{C}_R$, $C$ | Quantifies counter-ion uptake |
| 4 | Nernst–Planck flux | $J_i = -D_i\left( \dfrac{dC_i}{dx} + \dfrac{z_i F C_i}{RT}\dfrac{d\phi}{dx} \right)$ | $D_i$, $C_i$, $\phi$ | Predicts ion flux in resin |
| 5 | Selectivity coefficient | $K_{\text{B}}^{\text{A}} = \dfrac{\bar{a}_{\text{A}}^{1/z_A} a_{\text{B}}^{1/z_B}}{\bar{a}_{\text{B}}^{1/z_B} a_{\text{A}}^{1/z_A}}$ | activities, valences | Predicts ion preference |
| 6 | Equilibrium constant (binary) | $K_{ab} = \dfrac{\bar{C}_a^{1/z_a} C_b^{1/z_b}}{\bar{C}_b^{1/z_b} C_a^{1/z_a}}$ | resin & soln concs. | Design of IX columns |
| 7 | Mass-transfer rate (linear driving force) | $\dfrac{dq}{dt} = k_s a_p (q^* - q)$ | $k_s$, $a_p$, $q^*$ | Rate-limited step design |
| 8 | Film diffusion control | $\dfrac{dq}{dt} = \dfrac{3D_f}{r_p \delta \, \bar{C}_R}(C - C^*)$ | $D_f$, $\delta$ | Dilute-solution regime |
| 9 | Particle diffusion control | $\dfrac{dq}{dt} = \dfrac{15 D_p}{r_p^2 \, \bar{C}_R}(q^* - q)$ | $D_p$ | Concentrated-solution regime |
| 10 | Nernst–Einstein relation | $D_i = \dfrac{u_i RT}{\vert z_i \vert F}$ | mobility $u_i$ | Links mobility ↔ diffusivity |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Mechanism Used | Polymer Used |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Domestic water softening** | Selectivity $\text{Ca}^{2+} > \text{Na}^+$ | Polystyrene-DVB sulfonate resin |
| **Demineralization in power plants** | Cation + anion resin pair | Strong-acid + strong-base resin |
| **Hemodialysis (artificial kidney)** | Donnan exclusion of proteins | Cellulose acetate / polysulfone membranes |
| **Fuel cell proton exchange** | Nernst–Planck migration of $\text{H}^+$ | Nafion™ (perfluorosulfonic acid) |
| **Lithium extraction from brines** | Selective binding + Donnan | Crown-ether grafted polymers |
| **Wastewater heavy-metal removal** | High-valence preference | Chelating ion-exchange resins |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Donnan Membrane Equilibrium (Full, Exhaustive)

### Setup

Consider a cation-exchange resin (polymer phase, overbar) in equilibrium with an aqueous NaCl solution. The resin contains fixed anionic groups at concentration $\bar{C}_R$ (eq/L) and mobile $\text{Na}^+$ at $\bar{C}_+$, mobile $\text{Cl}^-$ at $\bar{C}_-$.

### Step 1: Apply Electroneutrality in Both Phases

**Solution phase:**

$$C_+ = C_- = C$$

**Resin phase:**

$$\bar{C}_+ = \bar{C}_R + \bar{C}_-$$

This is because the fixed $-\text{SO}_3^-$ groups contribute negative charge that must be balanced by the net mobile positive charge.

### Step 2: Equate Electrochemical Potentials at Equilibrium

For the diffusing species $\text{Na}^+$ and $\text{Cl}^-$, at the membrane–solution interface, $\tilde{\mu}_+^{\text{resin}} = \tilde{\mu}_+^{\text{soln}}$ and $\tilde{\mu}_-^{\text{resin}} = \tilde{\mu}_-^{\text{soln}}$.

For $\text{Na}^+$:

$$\mu_+^0 + RT \ln \bar{a}_+ + (+1)F \bar{\phi} = \mu_+^0 + RT \ln a_+ + (+1)F \phi$$

For $\text{Cl}^-$:

$$\mu_-^0 + RT \ln \bar{a}_- + (-1)F \bar{\phi} = \mu_-^0 + RT \ln a_- + (-1)F \phi$$

### Step 3: Subtract the Two Equations

Subtracting the $\text{Cl}^-$ equation from the $\text{Na}^+$ equation, the $\mu^0$ terms cancel, and the potential terms combine as:

$$RT \ln \bar{a}_+ + F\bar{\phi} - RT \ln \bar{a}_- + F\bar{\phi} = RT \ln a_+ + F\phi - RT \ln a_- + F\phi$$

Wait — careful with signs. Let $\Delta\phi = \bar{\phi} - \phi$. Then:

$$RT \ln \bar{a}_+ - RT \ln \bar{a}_- + F(\bar{\phi} - \phi) + F(\bar{\phi} - \phi) = RT \ln a_+ - RT \ln a_- + 2F(\bar{\phi} - \phi)$$

Actually the cleanest path is to **add** the two equations (since $z_+ = +1$, $z_- = -1$):

$$RT \ln \bar{a}_+ + F\bar{\phi} - F\bar{\phi} + RT \ln \bar{a}_- - F\bar{\phi} + F\bar{\phi} \;= \; \text{(solution side equivalent)}$$

The $\phi$ terms cancel exactly upon addition, leaving:

$$RT \ln \bar{a}_+ + RT \ln \bar{a}_- = RT \ln a_+ + RT \ln a_-$$

### Step 4: Simplify to the Donnan Product Rule

$$\ln(\bar{a}_+ \bar{a}_-) = \ln(a_+ a_-)$$

$$\boxed{\bar{a}_+ \bar{a}_- = a_+ a_-}$$

### Step 5: Combine with Electroneutrality to Find Counter-ion Concentration

Assume ideal solutions so $a = C$. Then $\bar{C}_+ \bar{C}_- = C^2$.

Substitute $\bar{C}_- = \bar{C}_+ - \bar{C}_R$:

$$\bar{C}_+ (\bar{C}_+ - \bar{C}_R) = C^2$$

$$\bar{C}_+^2 - \bar{C}_R \bar{C}_+ - C^2 = 0$$

Solving the quadratic in $\bar{C}_+$ (taking the positive root since concentration must be positive):

$$\bar{C}_+ = \frac{\bar{C}_R + \sqrt{\bar{C}_R^2 + 4C^2}}{2}$$

### Step 6: Analyze Two Limiting Cases

**Case A — High external concentration ($C \gg \bar{C}_R$):**

$$\sqrt{\bar{C}_R^2 + 4C^2} \approx 2C \sqrt{1 + \frac{\bar{C}_R^2}{4C^2}} \approx 2C \left(1 + \frac{\bar{C}_R^2}{8C^2}\right)$$

$$\bar{C}_+ \approx \frac{\bar{C}_R}{2} + C \left(1 + \frac{\bar{C}_R^2}{8C^2}\right) \approx C$$

So in concentrated solutions, the resin behaves like the bulk — **Donnan exclusion breaks down**.

**Case B — Low external concentration ($C \ll \bar{C}_R$):**

$$\sqrt{\bar{C}_R^2 + 4C^2} \approx \bar{C}_R \left(1 + \frac{2C^2}{\bar{C}_R^2}\right)$$

$$\bar{C}_+ \approx \frac{\bar{C}_R}{2} + \frac{\bar{C}_R}{2}\left(1 + \frac{2C^2}{\bar{C}_R^2}\right) = \bar{C}_R + \frac{C^2}{\bar{C}_R}$$

Since $\bar{C}_R \gg C$, we get $\bar{C}_+ \approx \bar{C}_R$ — the counter-ion concentration inside is essentially fixed by the polymer's own charge. This is the basis for **selective ion removal from dilute streams** — the engineering reason ion exchange is so effective for trace heavy-metal cleanup.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Showing both limiting cases earns full marks. Most students stop after solving the quadratic.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Nernst–Planck Equation from First Principles

### Step 1: Start from the Driving Force

The flux $J_i$ of species $i$ is proportional to the gradient of its electrochemical potential:

$$J_i = -u_i C_i \frac{d\tilde{\mu}_i}{dx}$$

where $u_i$ is the ionic mobility (m²/(V·s)).

### Step 2: Expand the Electrochemical Potential

$$\frac{d\tilde{\mu}_i}{dx} = RT \frac{d \ln a_i}{dx} + z_i F \frac{d\phi}{dx}$$

For dilute solutions, $a_i \approx C_i$, so $\frac{d\ln a_i}{dx} = \frac{1}{C_i}\frac{dC_i}{dx}$.

### Step 3: Substitute Back

$$J_i = -u_i C_i \left( \frac{RT}{C_i}\frac{dC_i}{dx} + z_i F \frac{d\phi}{dx} \right)$$

$$J_i = -u_i RT \frac{dC_i}{dx} - u_i z_i F C_i \frac{d\phi}{dx}$$

### Step 4: Apply the Nernst–Einstein Relation

The mobility $u_i$ is related to the molecular diffusivity $D_i$ via:

$$D_i = u_i RT \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad u_i = \frac{D_i}{RT}$$

### Step 5: Final Nernst–Planck Form

$$\boxed{J_i = -D_i \frac{dC_i}{dx} - \frac{z_i F D_i C_i}{RT}\frac{d\phi}{dx}}$$

This is the **master transport equation** for ions in a polymer phase under isothermal, convection-free conditions.

### Step 6: Why the Potential Gradient Appears Even Without External Voltage

In a binary electrolyte like NaCl diffusing through a cation resin, **both** $\text{Na}^+$ and $\text{Cl}^-$ must diffuse at equal rates to prevent charge buildup (electroneutrality). Because $D_+ \neq D_-$, an internal **Donnan potential gradient $d\phi/dx$** spontaneously develops to slow down the faster ion and speed up the slower one. This is called the **Dufour-like coupling**.

If $J_+ = J_-$ and $C_+ = C_-$ locally:

$$-D_+ \left( \frac{dC}{dx} + \frac{FC}{RT}\frac{d\phi}{dx} \right) = -D_- \left( \frac{dC}{dx} - \frac{FC}{RT}\frac{d\phi}{dx} \right)$$

Solving:

$$\frac{d\phi}{dx} = \frac{(D_- - D_+)}{(D_+ + D_-)} \cdot \frac{RT}{FC} \frac{dC}{dx}$$

This **internal electric field** is what makes single-ion permeability possible through an otherwise neutral polymer film — a key principle behind **electrodialysis**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Mass-Transfer Rate Equation (Linear Driving Force)

For an ion-exchange resin bead of radius $r_p$, mass transfer is governed by either **film diffusion** (external liquid film) or **particle diffusion** (inside the bead). The linear driving force (LDF) approximation gives:

### Film-Diffusion Control

$$\frac{d\bar{q}}{dt} = \frac{3 D_f}{r_p \delta \, \bar{C}_R}(C - C^*)$$

where:
- $D_f$ = diffusivity in the liquid film (m²/s)
- $\delta$ = film thickness (m)
- $r_p$ = particle radius (m)
- $\bar{q}$ = average loading in resin (eq/kg)
- $C^*$ = solution concentration at particle surface in equilibrium with $\bar{q}$

### Particle-Diffusion Control

$$\frac{d\bar{q}}{dt} = \frac{15 D_p}{r_p^2 \, \bar{C}_R}(\bar{q}^* - \bar{q})$$

where $D_p$ is the diffusivity inside the polymer and $\bar{q}^*$ is the equilibrium loading corresponding to bulk solution $C$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Helfferich's Criterion (KTU-favorite exam fact):**
> - **Film diffusion controls** when solution concentration is **low** (e.g., $< 0.01$ M) and resin particle is **large**.
> - **Particle diffusion controls** when solution concentration is **high** (e.g., $> 1$ M) and resin is **small / highly cross-linked**.

This dichotomy is why industrial IX columns are sized very differently for trace removal vs. bulk softening.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: Define Donnan membrane equilibrium and state its significance in ion-exchange separation processes.**

**Model Answer:**

Donnan membrane equilibrium describes the **steady-state distribution of ions** between an ion-exchange polymer phase (containing fixed ionic groups) and an external electrolyte solution. It arises from the simultaneous application of **electroneutrality** in both phases and the **equality of electrochemical potentials** for diffusible species.

The governing relation is the Donnan product rule:

$$\bar{a}_+ \, \bar{a}_- = a_+ \, a_-$$

**Significance in ion-exchange separations:**
1. Explains why **co-ions are excluded** from the resin (basis of selectivity).
2. Explains why **counter-ions are enriched** inside the polymer (basis of concentration).
3. Provides the **thermodynamic basis for designing ion-exchange columns** and electrodialysis membranes.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for equation, 1 for significance.)*

---

### Question A2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: Distinguish between film diffusion control and particle diffusion control in ion-exchange kinetics.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Film Diffusion Control | Particle Diffusion Control |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Location of resistance | External liquid film | Inside the resin bead |
| Concentration regime | Dilute solutions ($C < 0.01$ M) | Concentrated solutions ($C > 1$ M) |
| Particle size | Large beads favor it | Small beads favor it |
| Key variable | $D_f$ (film diffusivity) | $D_p$ (particle diffusivity) |
| Effect of agitation | Strongly affected | Little affected |
| Temperature dependence | Lower activation energy | Higher activation energy |

A useful rule: the **Helfferich number** $He = \dfrac{C \bar{C}_R D_p r_p}{C D_f \delta}$ decides which mechanism dominates. $He \ll 1$ ⇒ film control; $He \gg 1$ ⇒ particle control.

---

## PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]`

**(a)** Derive the Donnan distribution equation for a cation-exchange resin of fixed ion concentration $\bar{C}_R = 2.0$ eq/L in equilibrium with a 0.05 M NaCl solution. Calculate the concentrations of $\text{Na}^+$ and $\text{Cl}^-$ inside the resin and comment on the **Donnan exclusion efficiency**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Nernst–Planck equation** in detail. How does it differ from Fick's first law? Derive the expression for the **internal electric field gradient** that develops when NaCl diffuses through a cation-exchange membrane, assuming $D_+ = 1.33 \times 10^{-9}$ m²/s and $D_- = 2.03 \times 10^{-9}$ m²/s. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Use the Donnan product rule.** For ideal solutions ($a = C$):

$$\bar{C}_+ \bar{C}_- = C^2$$

with $\bar{C}_+ = \bar{C}_R + \bar{C}_-$.

**Step 2 — Substitute.** Let $\bar{C}_- = x$. Then $\bar{C}_+ = 2.0 + x$.

$$(2.0 + x)(x) = (0.05)^2 = 0.0025$$

$$x^2 + 2.0 x - 0.0025 = 0$$

**Step 3 — Solve the quadratic.**

$$x = \frac{-2.0 + \sqrt{4.0 + 0.01}}{2} = \frac{-2.0 + \sqrt{4.01}}{2}$$

$$\sqrt{4.01} = 2.00250$$

$$x = \frac{-2.0 + 2.00250}{2} = \frac{0.00250}{2} = 0.00125 \text{ eq/L}$$

**Step 4 — Compute counter-ion concentration.**

$$\bar{C}_+ = 2.0 + 0.00125 = 2.00125 \text{ eq/L}$$

**Step 5 — Compute partition coefficient and rejection.**

$$\bar{C}_- = 0.00125 \text{ eq/L}, \quad C_- = 0.05 \text{ eq/L}$$

$$\text{Rejection} = \left(1 - \frac{\bar{C}_-}{C_-}\right) \times 100\% = \left(1 - \frac{0.00125}{0.05}\right) \times 100\% = 97.5\%$$

$$\text{Enrichment of Na}^+ = \frac{\bar{C}_+}{C_+} = \frac{2.00125}{0.05} \approx 40\times$$

**Conclusion:** The resin excludes 97.5% of chloride co-ions and enriches sodium counter-ions by a factor of ~40, demonstrating efficient Donnan selectivity.

*(7 marks: 2 derivation, 3 calculation, 2 interpretation.)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — State the Nernst–Planck equation:**

$$J_i = -D_i \frac{dC_i}{dx} - \frac{z_i F D_i C_i}{RT}\frac{d\phi}{dx}$$

**Step 2 — Contrast with Fick's law.**

Fick's first law: $J_i = -D_i \dfrac{dC_i}{dx}$ — only handles **concentration gradients**.

Nernst–Planck adds a **migration term** accounting for ion movement under an electric field. It is needed when (i) the polymer carries fixed charges (Donnan potential exists), or (ii) an external voltage is applied.

**Step 3 — Set up the no-net-current condition.**

For electroneutral diffusion through a cation membrane, $J_+ = J_-$ and $C_+ = C_- = C$ locally:

$$-D_+ \left( \frac{dC}{dx} + \frac{FC}{RT}\frac{d\phi}{dx} \right) = -D_- \left( \frac{dC}{dx} - \frac{FC}{RT}\frac{d\phi}{dx} \right)$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $d\phi/dx$.**

$$\frac{d\phi}{dx} = \frac{(D_- - D_+)}{(D_+ + D_-)} \cdot \frac{RT}{FC} \frac{dC}{dx}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute numerical values.**

At $T = 298$ K, $RT/F = 0.02569$ V.

$$D_- - D_+ = (2.03 - 1.33) \times 10^{-9} = 0.70 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$$

$$D_+ + D_- = 3.36 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$$

$$\frac{d\phi}{dx} = \frac{0.70}{3.36} \cdot \frac{0.02569}{C} \frac{dC}{dx} = \frac{0.2083}{C} \cdot \frac{0.02569}{1} \cdot \frac{dC}{dx}$$

For a typical $C = 0.05$ mol/m³ and $\dfrac{dC}{dx} = 1$ mol/m⁴:

$$\frac{d\phi}{dx} = 0.2083 \times \frac{0.02569}{0.05} \times 1 = 0.107 \text{ V/m}$$

This small internal field is what makes the **membrane permselective** — passing cations while rejecting anions.

*(7 marks: 2 for stating Nernst–Planck, 2 for contrast with Fick, 3 for derivation + calculation.)*

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]`

**(a)** A strong-acid cation-exchange resin (capacity 4.5 eq/kg, particle radius 0.5 mm) is used to remove $\text{Ca}^{2+}$ from water. If the resin is initially in $\text{Na}^+$ form and is contacted with water containing $\text{Ca}^{2+}$ at $10^{-3}$ M, derive and apply the **selectivity coefficient expression** to predict whether the resin prefers $\text{Ca}^{2+}$ or $\text{Na}^+$. Use $K_{\text{Na}}^{\text{Ca}} = 5.2$ (literature value for polystyrene-DVB sulfonate). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **linear driving force (LDF)** rate equation for film-diffusion-controlled ion exchange, and discuss how it is used to **design the minimum contact time** in an industrial IX column. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Write the exchange reaction.**

$$2 \bar{\text{Na}}^+ + \text{Ca}^{2+} \rightleftharpoons \bar{\text{Ca}}^{2+} + 2 \text{Na}^+$$

**Step 2 — Define the selectivity coefficient.**

$$K_{\text{Na}}^{\text{Ca}} = \frac{\bar{a}_{\text{Ca}}^{1/2} \cdot a_{\text{Na}}}{\bar{a}_{\text{Na}} \cdot a_{\text{Ca}}^{1/2}}$$

**Step 3 — Identify the equilibrium.**

Given $K_{\text{Na}}^{\text{Ca}} = 5.2 > 1$, the resin **preferentially uptakes $\text{Ca}^{2+}$** over $\text{Na}^+$.

**Step 4 — Quantify the preference using the equivalent fraction form.**

For a divalent/monovalent exchange, mass action gives:

$$K_{\text{Na}}^{\text{Ca}} = \frac{\bar{X}_{\text{Ca}} \cdot (1 - X_{\text{Ca}})^2 \cdot C}{X_{\text{Ca}}^2 \cdot \bar{C}_R \cdot (1 - \bar{X}_{\text{Ca}})^2 \cdot \bar{C}_R \cdot C}$$

where $X$ is the equivalent fraction of $\text{Ca}^{2+}$ in solution and $\bar{X}$ is the fraction on the resin.

**Step 5 — Apply with given data.** $\bar{C}_R = 4.5$ eq/kg, $C = 10^{-3}$ M.

Letting $\bar{X}_{\text{Ca}} = 0.8$ (80% loading — typical design target):

$$5.2 = \frac{(0.8)(1 - X_{\text{Ca}})^2 \cdot 10^{-3}}{X_{\text{Ca}}^2 (4.5)^2 (1 - 0.8)^2 \cdot \text{(ratio term)}}$$

Solving iteratively gives $X_{\text{Ca}} \approx 0.06$, meaning only 6% of the total cations in water need be $\text{Ca}^{2+}$ to achieve 80% loading of the resin. This confirms the strong preference.

**Conclusion:** Since $K > 1$ and the higher-valence ion is preferred, the resin effectively **softens water** by binding $\text{Ca}^{2+}$ from very dilute solutions.

*(7 marks: 2 reaction + coefficient, 2 numerical, 3 design interpretation.)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Define film-diffusion flux through the liquid film.**

The flux $N_A$ of counter-ion $A$ through the external film around a spherical bead is:

$$N_A = \frac{D_f}{\delta}(C_A - C_{A,i})$$

where $C_{A,i}$ is the concentration at the bead surface.

**Step 2 — Express in terms of resin loading.**

The rate of uptake per bead of radius $r_p$:

$$\frac{d\bar{q}_A}{dt} = \frac{3 D_f}{r_p \delta \bar{C}_R}(C_A - C_A^*)$$

where $C_A^*$ is the solution concentration in equilibrium with the resin loading $\bar{q}_A$.

**Step 3 — Identify the LDF form.**

$$\boxed{\frac{d\bar{q}}{dt} = k_s a_p (q^* - q)}$$

with $k_s$ = mass-transfer coefficient (m/s), $a_p$ = specific surface area (m²/m³), $q^*$ = equilibrium loading, $q$ = current loading.

**Step 4 — Solve the ODE (constant $q^*$).**

Integrating from $q_0$ to $q$:

$$q(t) = q^* - (q^* - q_0) \exp(-k_s a_p t)$$

**Step 5 — Apply to column design.**

Set a target loading $q_{\text{target}} = 0.95 q^*$. Then:

$$t_{\min} = -\frac{\ln(0.05)}{k_s a_p} = \frac{3.0}{k_s a_p}$$

For $k_s = 10^{-5}$ m/s, $a_p = 10^4$ m²/m³:

$$t_{\min} = \frac{3.0}{10^{-5} \times 10^4} = 30 \text{ s}$$

This is the **minimum residence time** needed per stage in a multi-stage IX contactor.

*(7 marks: 2 derivation, 3 integration, 2 design application.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**
>
> **Common mistakes that cost marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the units of $F$ and $R$** — many students write $F = 96{,}485$ but then forget to convert from J to V·C. Always check: $F$ has units of **C/mol**, not J/mol.
> 2. **Sign errors in Donnan derivation** — the term $z_i F \phi$ carries the sign of $z_i$. Mixing up the signs of $\text{Na}^+$ and $\text{Cl}^-$ is the #1 cause of wrong Donnan products.
> 3. **Ignoring the activity coefficient** — at concentrations above ~0.1 M, using $a \approx C$ breaks down. Examiners appreciate when students state the **ideal-solution assumption explicitly**.
> 4. **Stating Fick's law instead of Nernst–Planck** for resin-phase transport — this loses 2 marks outright.
> 5. **Forgetting the convective term** when the question mentions a flowing stream. Write $J_i = -D_i(\ldots) + C_i v$ even if you then say $v = 0$.
> 6. **Missing the $\bar{C}_R$ fixed-ion contribution** in the electroneutrality equation of the resin. This single omission flips every subsequent calculation.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Driving force** for ion movement = gradient of **electrochemical potential**, not just concentration.
- ✅ **Donnan product rule** $\bar{a}_+\bar{a}_- = a_+a_-$ is the master equation for ion-exchange equilibrium.
- ✅ **Counter-ion concentration in resin** $\bar{C}_+ = \dfrac{\bar{C}_R + \sqrt{\bar{C}_R^2 + 4C^2}}{2}$.
- ✅ **Nernst–Planck** = Fick diffusion + electromigration. Use it for charged species in polymers.
- ✅ **Internal Donnan field** $\dfrac{d\phi}{dx} = \dfrac{(D_- - D_+)}{(D_+ + D_-)} \cdot \dfrac{RT}{FC}\dfrac{dC}{dx}$ arises without any external voltage.
- ✅ **Selectivity rules**: higher valence > lower valence; smaller hydrated radius > larger; more polarizable > less.
- ✅ **Film diffusion dominates** in **dilute** solutions with **large** particles; **particle diffusion dominates** in **concentrated** solutions with **small** particles (Helfferich criterion).
- ✅ **Constants**: $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol; $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K); $RT/F$ at 25 °C = 0.02569 V.
- ✅ **Linear driving force (LDF)** rate: $\dfrac{dq}{dt} = k_s a_p (q^* - q)$; exponential approach to equilibrium with time constant $1/(k_s a_p)$.
- ✅ **Engineering uses**: water softening, electrodialysis, fuel cells, hemodialysis, heavy-metal removal.
- ✅ **Polymer connection**: the **fixed ionic groups** on the polymer backbone (e.g., $-\text{SO}_3^-$, $-\text{NR}_3^+$) are what enable Donnan exclusion and selectivity — without them, the polymer is just an inert film.
- ✅ **For KTU answers**, always state the **assumption** (ideal solution, isothermal, no convection) and then the equation — this earns ½ mark each time.

# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **PEPOT752 – Polymers and Separation Processes**, **Module 3: Applications** refers to the industrial, environmental, and biomedical deployment of **polymer-based separation systems**—most importantly **polymer membranes** and **polymer-supported sorbents/ion exchangers**—used to perform unit operations such as **microfiltration (MF)**, **ultrafiltration (UF)**, **nanofiltration (NF)**, **reverse osmosis (RO)**, **gas separation (GS)**, **pervaporation (PV)**, **dialysis**, and **electrodialysis**.

A **polymer membrane** is formally defined as a *selective barrier* made from an organic macromolecular material (e.g., polysulfone, polyamide, cellulose acetate, PVDF, PTFE, polyimide) that permits the preferential transport of certain species under a thermodynamic driving force (pressure, concentration, temperature, or electrical potential).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The term "Applications" here is NOT about polymer products in general. It is narrowly focused on **how polymers (especially as membranes and sorbents) are used to achieve separations in industry, environment, and medicine**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Sieve" Model

Imagine pouring a mixture of sand, gravel, and water into a kitchen colander. The water drains out, but the solids stay. Now imagine a *super-smart* sieve that:
- Lets only water molecules pass (RO),
- Lets only water pass but not salt (NF),
- Lets only certain gases pass (GS),
- Lets only ethanol through, blocking water (PV).

A **polymer membrane** is precisely this smart sieve. The polymer chains are arranged in a way that creates *pores* or *free-volume channels* of controlled size, shape, and chemical affinity. Molecules that fit, dissolve, or interact favorably pass through; others are rejected.

| Separation Mode | "Sieve" Character | Real-World Analogy |
|---|---|---|
| **Microfiltration (MF)** | Coarse strainer | Tea strainer |
| **Ultrafiltration (UF)** | Fine strainer | Coffee filter |
| **Nanofiltration (NF)** | Molecular sieve | Fine sand filter |
| **Reverse Osmosis (RO)** | Salt-excluding barrier | Waterproof cloth |
| **Gas Separation (GS)** | Size-sieving at molecular level | Molecular doorway |
| **Pervaporation (PV)** | Affinity-based sorter | Oil-and-water separator |

> [!NOTE]
> **Driving Force Reminder:** Each membrane process is governed by a specific driving force. KTU examiners love to test this:
> - Pressure → MF, UF, NF, RO
> - Concentration (partial pressure) → GS, PV
> - Electrical potential → Electrodialysis
> - Concentration gradient → Dialysis

## 1.3 Why This Module Matters in KTU Examinations

This module is the **culmination of the theory** taught in Modules 1 and 2. Where Modules 1 and 2 cover *what polymers are* and *how separations work theoretically*, Module 3 asks the crucial question: **"Where is all of this actually used?"** Examiners frequently ask 14-mark descriptive questions on:
- Selection of a membrane process for a given industrial problem,
- Justification of operating conditions,
- Comparison between two competing processes (e.g., RO vs. NF for water softening),
- Case-study style problems from dairy, pharma, or petrochemical industries.

---

> [!TIP]
> **Standard Metric to Memorize:** Flux $(J)$ is universally reported in **L·m⁻²·h⁻¹ (LMH)** for liquid systems and **GPU (Gas Permeation Unit)** for gas systems, where $1 \text{ GPU} = 3.348 \times 10^{-10} \text{ mol·m}^{-2}\text{·s}^{-1}\text{·Pa}^{-1}$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Classification of Polymer-Based Separation Applications

The applications of polymers in separation processes can be classified into **four broad industrial domains**:

1. **Water & Wastewater Treatment** — Desalination, brackish water purification, industrial effluent treatment, domestic water supply.
2. **Gas & Vapor Separations** — O₂/N₂ enrichment, H₂ recovery, CO₂ capture, natural gas sweetening, vapor recovery.
3. **Food & Dairy Industry** — Milk protein concentration, whey processing, fruit juice clarification, wine/beer stabilization, sugar refining.
4. **Biomedical & Pharmaceutical** — Hemodialysis, blood oxygenation, controlled drug release, sterile filtration, protein purification.

## 2.2 Operational Principles — The "Why" Behind Each Process

### 2.2.1 Microfiltration (MF)
- **Pore size:** $0.1 \text{ to } 10\ \mu m$
- **Driving force:** Pressure difference ($0.1$ to $2$ bar)
- **Rejects:** Suspended solids, bacteria, yeasts.
- **Common polymers:** PVDF, polyethersulfone (PES), cellulose acetate, polypropylene.
- **Mechanism:** Sieving (size exclusion).

### 2.2.2 Ultrafiltration (UF)
- **Pore size:** $1 \text{ to } 100 \text{ nm}$ (MWCO $1000$ to $500{,}000$ Da)
- **Driving force:** Pressure difference ($1$ to $10$ bar)
- **Rejects:** Macromolecules, proteins, viruses, colloids.
- **Common polymers:** Polysulfone, polyethersulfone, regenerated cellulose, polyacrylonitrile.
- **Mechanism:** Size exclusion at the macromolecular level.

### 2.2.3 Nanofiltration (NF)
- **Pore size:** $0.5 \text{ to } 5 \text{ nm}$ (MWCO $200$ to $1000$ Da)
- **Driving force:** Pressure difference ($5$ to $35$ bar)
- **Rejects:** Divalent salts (Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺, SO₄²⁻), organic molecules $> 200$ Da.
- **Common polymers:** Polyamide thin-film composites, cellulose acetate.
- **Mechanism:** Combination of size exclusion and Donnan exclusion (charge-based rejection).

### 2.2.4 Reverse Osmosis (RO)
- **Pore size:** $< 0.5 \text{ nm}$ (effectively non-porous)
- **Driving force:** Pressure difference ($10$ to $80$ bar), exceeding osmotic pressure.
- **Rejects:** Monovalent ions (Na⁺, Cl⁻) and virtually all solutes.
- **Common polymers:** Aromatic polyamide thin-film composites.
- **Mechanism:** Solution–diffusion.

### 2.2.5 Gas Separation (GS)
- **Pore size:** Non-porous / ultra-microporous
- **Driving force:** Partial pressure difference
- **Selectivity basis:** Solubility selectivity and/or diffusivity selectivity.
- **Common polymers:** Polysulfone, polyimide, cellulose acetate, silicone rubber (PDMS).
- **Mechanism:** Solution–diffusion.
- **Examples:** O₂/N₂, CO₂/CH₄, H₂/N₂.

### 2.2.6 Pervaporation (PV)
- **Driving force:** Partial pressure (vacuum on permeate side)
- **Selectivity basis:** Differential sorption and diffusion through polymer.
- **Common polymers:** PDMS (hydrophobic, removes organics), PVA (hydrophilic, removes water).
- **Applications:** Dehydration of ethanol, removal of VOCs from water.

### 2.2.7 Dialysis
- **Driving force:** Concentration gradient
- **Common polymers:** Regenerated cellulose (Cuprophan), hemophan.
- **Applications:** Hemodialysis (artificial kidney).

### 2.2.8 Electrodialysis
- **Driving force:** Electrical potential
- **Common polymers:** Cation- and anion-exchange membranes (sulfonated polystyrene, aminated polystyrene).
- **Applications:** Desalination of brackish water, salt production.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear in nearly every KTU board exam on this module.**

| Concept | Equation | Description / Units |
|---|---|---|
| Permeate Flux | $J = \dfrac{V_p}{A \cdot \Delta t}$ | $V_p$ = permeate volume (L), $A$ = membrane area (m²), $\Delta t$ = time (h). Result: LMH. |
| Darcy-type Flux (for porous MF/UF) | $J = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\mu R_m}$ | $R_m$ = membrane resistance, $\mu$ = viscosity. |
| Solution-Diffusion Flux | $J_i = \dfrac{P_i}{l} \left( p_{i,\text{feed}} - p_{i,\text{permeate}} \right)$ | $P_i$ = permeability, $l$ = thickness. |
| Permeability | $P_i = D_i \cdot S_i$ | $D_i$ = diffusivity, $S_i$ = solubility coefficient. |
| Selectivity (Gas) | $\alpha_{A/B} = \dfrac{P_A}{P_B} = \dfrac{D_A}{D_B} \cdot \dfrac{S_A}{S_B}$ | Ratio of permeabilities. |
| Rejection | $R_i = \left( 1 - \dfrac{C_{i,p}}{C_{i,f}} \right) \times 100\%$ | $C_p$ = permeate, $C_f$ = feed concentration. |
| Osmotic Pressure (van't Hoff) | $\pi = i C R T$ | For dilute solutions. |
| Recovery | $\text{Recovery} = \dfrac{Q_p}{Q_f} \times 100\%$ | $Q_p$ = permeate flow, $Q_f$ = feed flow. |
| TMP (Transmembrane Pressure) | $\Delta P = \dfrac{P_f + P_c}{2} - P_p$ | For crossflow systems. |
| Separation Factor (PV) | $\alpha_{A/B} = \dfrac{y_A / y_B}{x_A / x_B}$ | Vapor–liquid ratio. |
| Cut (Stage Cut) | $\theta = \dfrac{Q_p}{Q_f}$ | For gas separation modules. |

> [!NOTE]
> In the table above, notice the **two different definitions of selectivity**: $\alpha = P_A / P_B$ for **gases** (where "permeability" is the controlling parameter) versus $\alpha = (y_A / y_B) / (x_A / x_B)$ for **pervaporation** (a true vapor–liquid thermodynamic ratio). KTU examiners often mix these up to trap students.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Seawater Desalination:** Modern plants (e.g., Sorek, Israel) use spiral-wound polyamide RO modules producing $>\!600{,}000 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$.
- **Natural Gas Sweetening:** Cellulose acetate membranes remove H₂S and CO₂ from CH₄, protecting pipelines.
- **Artificial Kidney:** A hemodialyzer uses $>\!10{,}000$ hollow fibers of regenerated cellulose, providing $>\!2 \text{ m}^2$ of membrane area.
- **Green Solvents:** Pervaporation with PDMS membranes replaces energy-intensive azeotropic distillation in ethanol plants.
- **CO₂ Capture:** Polyimide hollow fibers are deployed in pilot projects for post-combustion carbon capture.

---

> [!TIP]
> **Why polymers and not ceramics or metals?** Polymers offer **tailorable chemistry** (functional groups), **low cost**, **easy processability** (spinning, casting), and **low-temperature operation**. Their weakness is **thermal/chemical stability**, which is why ceramic membranes are used in extreme conditions.

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Solution-Diffusion Model (Foundation for RO, GS, PV)

The **Solution-Diffusion model** is the cornerstone of dense (non-porous) polymer membrane transport. Every KTU examiner expects you to derive this.

### Step 1 — Recognize the Three Sequential Steps
A permeating species $i$ must:
1. **Dissolve** (sorb) into the upstream face of the membrane.
2. **Diffuse** through the polymer matrix under a chemical potential gradient.
3. **Desorb** (evaporate/exit) on the downstream face.

### Step 2 — Express the Flux in Terms of Chemical Potential
The flux of species $i$ through a membrane of thickness $l$ is given by Fick's first law, with concentration $C_i$ as the variable:

$$
J_i = - D_i \frac{dC_i}{dx}
$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Thermodynamic Relationship Between $C_i$ and Chemical Potential
At equilibrium at each face of the membrane:

$$
C_i = S_i \cdot p_i
$$

where $S_i$ is the solubility coefficient and $p_i$ is the partial pressure.

### Step 4 — Boundary Conditions
At the upstream face $(x = 0)$: $C_i(0) = S_i \cdot p_{i,\text{feed}}$
At the downstream face $(x = l)$: $C_i(l) = S_i \cdot p_{i,\text{permeate}}$

### Step 5 — Integration
Assuming $D_i$ is constant across the membrane (idealized), integrate from $x=0$ to $x=l$:

$$
\int_0^l J_i \, dx = - D_i \int_{C_i(0)}^{C_i(l)} dC_i
$$

$$
J_i \cdot l = - D_i \left[ C_i(l) - C_i(0) \right] = D_i \left[ C_i(0) - C_i(l) \right]
$$

### Step 6 — Substitute the Boundary Values

$$
J_i \cdot l = D_i \cdot S_i \left( p_{i,\text{feed}} - p_{i,\text{permeate}} \right)
$$

### Step 7 — Define Permeability and Finalize
The **permeability** is $P_i = D_i \cdot S_i$, giving:

$$
\boxed{\,J_i = \frac{P_i}{l} \left( p_{i,\text{feed}} - p_{i,\text{permeate}} \right)\,}
$$

> **Interpretation:** The flux is **proportional to the partial pressure difference** and **inversely proportional to membrane thickness**. This is why membrane manufacturers strive for *ultra-thin selective layers* (e.g., $0.1\ \mu m$ polyamide on a porous support).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Ideal Selectivity of a Gas Separation Membrane

The **ideal selectivity** of species $A$ over species $B$ is the ratio of their permeabilities.

### Step 1 — Write Permeabilities for Both Species

$$
P_A = D_A \cdot S_A \quad ; \quad P_B = D_B \cdot S_B
$$

### Step 2 — Form the Ratio

$$
\alpha_{A/B} = \frac{P_A}{P_B} = \frac{D_A}{D_B} \cdot \frac{S_A}{S_B}
$$

### Step 3 — Interpret the Two Terms
- $\dfrac{D_A}{D_B}$ is the **diffusivity selectivity** — governed by size (smaller molecules diffuse faster).
- $\dfrac{S_A}{S_B}$ is the **solubility selectivity** — governed by polymer–penetrant interaction (more condensable gases dissolve more).

### Step 4 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)
> **Problem:** A polyimide membrane has $D_{O_2} = 1.2 \times 10^{-8} \text{ cm}^2/\text{s}$, $D_{N_2} = 3.0 \times 10^{-9} \text{ cm}^2/\text{s}$, $S_{O_2} = 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ cm}^3(\text{STP})/(\text{cm}^3 \cdot \text{cmHg})$, $S_{N_2} = 8.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ cm}^3(\text{STP})/(\text{cm}^3 \cdot \text{cmHg})$. Calculate the ideal O₂/N₂ selectivity.

**Solution:**

$$
\frac{D_{O_2}}{D_{N_2}} = \frac{1.2 \times 10^{-8}}{3.0 \times 10^{-9}} = 4.0
$$

$$
\frac{S_{O_2}}{S_{N_2}} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^{-3}}{8.0 \times 10^{-4}} = 1.875
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\alpha_{O_2/N_2} = 4.0 \times 1.875 = 7.5\,}
$$

> **Interpretation:** This membrane is **7.5× more permeable to O₂ than to N₂** — acceptable for medical oxygen enrichment (typically $\alpha \geq 5$).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Rejection and Recovery in an RO System

### Step 1 — Define Rejection
For solute $i$:

$$
R_i = \left( 1 - \frac{C_{i,p}}{C_{i,f}} \right) \times 100\%
$$

### Step 2 — Define Recovery
For the volumetric flows:

$$
\text{Recovery} = \frac{Q_p}{Q_f} \times 100\%
$$

### Step 3 — Mass Balance
The feed splits into permeate (product) and retentate (concentrate):

$$
Q_f = Q_p + Q_r
$$

$$
Q_f C_f = Q_p C_p + Q_r C_r
$$

### Step 4 — Worked Numerical Example
> **Problem:** An RO unit treats $100 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$ of brackish water containing $3000 \text{ ppm}$ NaCl. The permeate contains $200 \text{ ppm}$ NaCl and the recovery is $60\%$. Calculate: (a) Rejection, (b) Permeate flow, (c) Retentate concentration.

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — Rejection:**

$$
R = \left( 1 - \frac{200}{3000} \right) \times 100\% = 93.33\%
$$

**Part (b) — Permeate flow:**

$$
Q_p = 0.60 \times 100 = 60 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}
$$

$$
Q_r = 100 - 60 = 40 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}
$$

**Part (c) — Retentate concentration:**
From the solute mass balance:

$$
C_r = \frac{Q_f C_f - Q_p C_p}{Q_r} = \frac{(100)(3000) - (60)(200)}{40}
$$

$$
C_r = \frac{300{,}000 - 12{,}000}{40} = \frac{288{,}000}{40} = 7200 \text{ ppm}
$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Selecting a Membrane Process

| Industrial Problem | Recommended Process | Key Polymer | Driving Force |
|---|---|---|---|
| Removing bacteria from water | **MF** | PVDF, PES | $\Delta P$ ($< 2$ bar) |
| Concentrating whey proteins | **UF** | Polysulfone | $\Delta P$ ($1$ to $10$ bar) |
| Water softening (Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺ removal) | **NF** | Polyamide TFC | $\Delta P$ ($5$ to $35$ bar) |
| Seawater desalination | **RO** | Aromatic polyamide | $\Delta P$ ($50$ to $80$ bar) |
| O₂ enrichment for medical use | **GS** | Polyimide, PDMS | $\Delta p$ partial |
| CO₂ capture from flue gas | **GS** | Polyimide, PEBAX | $\Delta p$ partial |
| Ethanol dehydration (breaking azeotrope) | **PV** | PVA, NaAlg | $\Delta p$ (vacuum) |
| Artificial kidney | **Dialysis** | Cuprophan, hemophan | $\Delta C$ |
| Brackish water desalination (low energy) | **Electrodialysis** | Ion-exchange polymers | $\Delta E$ |
| VOC removal from wastewater | **PV** | PDMS | $\Delta p$ |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** When asked "Which process?", always justify with **two reasons** — (1) the size of the species to be separated, and (2) the desired purity/recovery. Mentioning a specific polymer name (e.g., polysulfone) scores bonus marks.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 4: Osmotic Pressure and Minimum RO Pressure

### Step 1 — van't Hoff Equation for Dilute Solutions

$$
\pi = i C R T
$$

where $i = 2$ for NaCl, $C$ is in mol/L, $R = 0.0821 \text{ L·atm/(mol·K)}$.

### Step 2 — Worked Example
> **Problem:** Seawater contains $0.6 \text{ M}$ NaCl at $25\ ^\circ\text{C}$. What is the osmotic pressure? What is the minimum applied pressure to reverse the flow?

**Solution:**

$$
\pi = (2)(0.6)(0.0821)(298) = 29.36 \text{ atm} \approx 29.7 \text{ bar}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,P_{\min} \geq 29.7 \text{ bar (theoretical); practical systems use } 55\text{ to }80\text{ bar.}\,}
$$

# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Master Block Diagram: Polymer Membrane Separation Process

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **functional architecture** of a generic membrane separation system used across all the applications discussed in this module.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    feedIn[FEED STREAM\nCrude mixture] --> pump[FEED PUMP\nor Compressor]
    pump --> preT[PRE-TREATMENT\nFilter, Softener, pH adjust]
    preT --> modH[MEMBRANE MODULE\nSpiral / Hollow Fiber / Plate]
    modH --> permOut[PERMEATE\nDesired product]
    modH --> retOut[RETENTATE\nConcentrate reject]
    retOut --> recirc[RECYCLE LOOP\nOptional]
    recirc --> preT
    modH --> ctrl[CONTROL UNIT\nPressure, Flow, TMP]
    ctrl --> pump
    permOut --> prod[PRODUCT COLLECTION\nor Downstream Use]

    classDef block fill:#E8F4F8,stroke:#2C3E50,color:#1A1A1A,stroke-width:2px
    class feedIn,pump,preT,modH,permOut,retOut,recirc,ctrl,prod block
```

**Interpretation of the diagram:**

- The **FEED STREAM** carries the mixture to be separated.
- The **FEED PUMP** provides the driving force (pressure, electrical, or partial pressure).
- **PRE-TREATMENT** is *critical* — suspended solids, scale-formers, and oxidizers must be removed to prevent membrane fouling and degradation.
- The **MEMBRANE MODULE** is the heart of the system, where the actual separation occurs.
- The **PERMEATE** is the desired product; the **RETENTATE** is the concentrate that is either recycled, disposed, or sent for further processing.
- The **CONTROL UNIT** continuously monitors TMP, flux, and quality to ensure stable operation.

## 4.2 Application Mapping Diagram

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **four industrial domains** discussed in Section 2.1 to specific membrane processes and polymer types.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    root[POLYMER MEMBRANE APPLICATIONS]

    water[WATER AND WASTEWATER]
    gas[GAS AND VAPOR SEPARATION]

    root --> water
    root --> gas
    root --> food
    root --> biom

    water --> mF[Microfiltration MF]

    water --> nF[Nanofiltration NF]
    water --> rO[Reverse Osmosis RO]
    water --> eD[Electrodialysis ED]

    gas --> gS[Gas Separation GS]
    gas --> pV1[Pervaporation PV]

    food --> uFapp[UF for protein concentration]
    food --> nFapp[NF for sugar refining]
    food --> rOapp[RO for juice concentration]

    biom --> dI[Dialysis Artificial Kidney]
    biom --> mFapp[MF for sterile filtration]
    biom --> pVapp[PV for solvent recovery]

    mF --> pvdf[PVDF PES Polypropylene]
    uF --> psf[Polysulfone Cellulose Acetate]
    nF --> paNF[Polyamide TFC]
    rO --> paRO[Aromatic Polyamide TFC]
    gS --> pim[Polyimide Silicone PDMS]
    pV1 --> pdms[PDMS PVA]
    eD --> iem[Ion Exchange Membranes]
    dI --> cup[Cuprophan Hemophan]
```

## 4.3 Crossflow vs. Dead-End Configuration

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DE["DEAD END CONFIGURATION"]
        dF[Feed] --> dPerp[Perpendicular to membrane]
        dPerp --> dM[Membrane]
        dM --> dP[Permeate]
    end

    subgraph CF["CROSSFLOW CONFIGURATION"]
        cF[Feed] --> cT[Tangential flow]
        cT --> cM[Membrane]
        cM --> cP[Permeate passes through]
        cT --> cR[Retentate continues]
    end

    classDef deadCfg fill:#FAEBD7,stroke:#8B4513,color:#000
    classDef crossCfg fill:#E0F2E0,stroke:#006400,color:#000
    class dF,dPerp,dM,dP deadCfg
    class cF,cT,cM,cP,cR crossCfg
```

> **Why crossflow is preferred industrially:** In a **dead-end** system, foulants accumulate on the membrane, reducing flux. In a **crossflow** system, the tangential flow sweeps the foulants away, enabling sustained operation with periodic cleaning. **All large-scale plants use crossflow.**

## 4.4 Membrane Module Architectures

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    flat[FLAT SHEET\nPlate and Frame]
    spiral[SPIRAL WOUND\nMost common for RO NF]
    hollow[HOLLOW FIBER\nLargest area per volume]
    tubular[TUBULAR\nFor high fouling streams]

    flat --> use1[Lab scale, small batch]
    spiral --> use2[Water treatment, desalination]
    hollow --> use3[Hemodialysis, gas separation]
    tubular --> use4[Food, dairy, industrial effluent]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Note:** Spiral-wound and hollow-fiber modules dominate industrial applications. The exam often asks: *"Why is the hollow-fiber module preferred for hemodialysis?"* — Answer: **High surface area per unit volume (>$10{,}000 \text{ m}^2/\text{m}^3$), low pressure drop, and good mass transfer.**

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Each Part-A question carries 3 marks and tests direct conceptual recall, definitions, and one-line reasoning.**

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**List any three industrial applications of polymer membranes and specify the polymer used in each application. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Polymer membranes are widely used in separation processes across industries. Three major applications are:

1. **Seawater desalination by Reverse Osmosis (RO):** Aromatic polyamide thin-film composite (TFC) membranes are used to produce potable water from seawater.
2. **Hemodialysis (Artificial Kidney):** Regenerated cellulose (Cuprophan) hollow fibers are used to remove urea, creatinine, and other metabolic waste from blood.
3. **Oxygen enrichment for medical use by Gas Separation:** Polyimide or polyphenylene oxide (PPO) membranes separate O₂ from N₂ in air.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between Ultrafiltration (UF) and Reverse Osmosis (RO) with respect to (a) pore size, (b) operating pressure, and (c) mechanism of separation. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Ultrafiltration (UF) | Reverse Osmosis (RO) |
|---|---|---|
| **(a) Pore size** | $1 \text{ to } 100 \text{ nm}$ (porous) | $< 0.5 \text{ nm}$ (effectively non-porous, dense) |
| **(b) Operating pressure** | $1 \text{ to } 10 \text{ bar}$ | $10 \text{ to } 80 \text{ bar}$ (must exceed osmotic pressure) |
| **(c) Separation mechanism** | Size exclusion (sieving) of macromolecules, colloids, and proteins | Solution–diffusion through a dense polymer; rejects all solutes including ions |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Part B features a Module Internal Choice. Two fully independent alternatives (A or B) are provided below. Each has two sub-parts of 7 marks each. Choose ONE and answer.**

---

### **🔹 QUESTION A — Industrial Case Study on Water Treatment [14 Marks]**

> **Q.A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**
> A coastal city plans to produce $10{,}000 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$ of potable water from seawater containing $35{,}000 \text{ ppm}$ total dissolved solids (TDS). The proposed scheme uses **pretreatment by UF** followed by **RO using spiral-wound aromatic polyamide membranes**.

#### **(a) Justify the choice of UF as pretreatment and RO as the main desalination step. Discuss the role of polymer chemistry in each step. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Justification of UF as Pretreatment (3.5 Marks):**

Seawater contains suspended solids, organic matter, microorganisms (bacteria, algae), and colloidal silica. These foulants would rapidly degrade an RO membrane by:
- **Pore plugging** (irreversible fouling),
- **Biofouling** (bacterial colonization),
- **Scaling** (when concentrated).

UF provides a **physical barrier** with pore sizes of $1$ to $100 \text{ nm}$, removing nearly all suspended solids, bacteria ($> 99.9\%$), and viruses. The **polyethersulfone (PES)** UF membrane is preferred because it offers:
- High mechanical strength,
- Wide pH tolerance ($1$ to $13$),
- Resistance to chlorine (short-term).

**Justification of RO for Desalination (3.5 Marks):**

Seawater TDS of $35{,}000 \text{ ppm}$ corresponds to an osmotic pressure of approximately $25$ to $30 \text{ bar}$. To overcome this and produce permeate of $< 500 \text{ ppm}$ TDS, RO is mandatory. **Aromatic polyamide thin-film composite (TFC) membranes** are the industry standard because:
- They have an **ultrathin ($< 0.1\ \mu m$) dense polyamide layer** providing high salt rejection ($> 99.5\%$),
- The **porous polysulfone support layer** provides mechanical strength,
- The **polyester non-woven backing** provides handling strength,
- They tolerate operating pressures up to $80$ bar,
- They offer high flux ($> 15 \text{ LMH}$) at standard test conditions.

**The two-step UF + RO scheme** is the global benchmark for seawater desalination because UF protects the expensive RO membranes, extending their life from $< 2$ years to $> 5$ years.

---

#### **(b) Calculate the osmotic pressure of the feed, the minimum applied pressure, and the daily membrane area required if the membrane flux is $12 \text{ LMH}$ and recovery is $45\%$. State the formula used. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Feed TDS $= 35{,}000 \text{ ppm} \approx 35{,}000 \text{ mg/L}$ of NaCl (≈ $0.60 \text{ mol/L}$)
- Temperature $T = 25\ ^\circ\text{C} = 298 \text{ K}$
- Recovery $= 45\%$
- Membrane flux $J = 12 \text{ LMH}$
- Daily production $Q_p = 10{,}000 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$

**Step 1 — Osmotic Pressure (van't Hoff):**

$$
\pi = i C R T
$$

For NaCl, $i = 2$:

$$
