The resulting variance per unit length is proportional to the velocity: $$H_C = (C_S + C_M)\cdot u$$ where $$C_S = \frac{2}{3}\cdot\frac{k'}{(1+k')^2}\cdot\frac{d_f^2}{D_S}$$ $$C_M = \frac{2(1+6k'+11k'^2)}{24(1+k')^2}\cdot\frac{d_p^2}{D_M}$$ with $d_f$ = stationary-phase film thickness, $D_S$ = diffusion in stationary phase. ### Step 4 — Summation Adding the three independent variance contributions per unit length: $$\boxed{H = H_A + H_B + H_C = A + \frac{B}{u} + C u}$$ ### Step 5 — Optimum velocity Differentiate $H$ w.r.t. $u$ and set to zero: $$\frac{dH}{du} = -\frac{B}{u^2} + C = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad u_{opt} = \sqrt{\frac{B}{C}}$$ Substituting back: $$H_{min} = A + 2\sqrt{BC}$$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation: Resolution Equation ### Step 1 — Definition of resolution For two peaks eluting at $t_{R1}$ and $t_{R2}$ (with $t_{R2}>t_{R1}$) and baseline widths $W_1$, $W_2$: $$R_s = \frac{t_{R2}-t_{R1}}{0.5(W_1+W_2)} = \frac{2(t_{R2}-t_{R1})}{W_1+W_2}$$ ### Step 2 — Relate $W$ to $N$ From $N = 16(t_R/W)^2$ we get $W = 4 t_R/\sqrt{N}$. For closely-eluting peaks ($\alpha \approx 1$), $W_1 \approx W_2 \approx W$, so: $$R_s = \frac{t_{R2}-t_{R1}}{W} = \frac{\sqrt{N}}{4}\cdot\frac{t_{R2}-t_{R1}}{t_{R2}}$$ ### Step 3 — Convert time difference to $k'$ From $t_R = t_M(1+k')$: $$\frac{t_{R2}-t_{R1}}{t_{R2}} = \frac{t_M(k_2'-k_1')}{t_M(1+k_2')} = \frac{k_2'-k_1'}{1+k_2'} = \frac{(k_2'/k_1')-1}{k_2'/k_1'}\cdot\frac{k_2'}{1+k_2'}$$ ### Step 4 — Introduce selectivity Recognizing $\alpha = k_2'/k_1'$: $$\frac{t_{R2}-t_{R1}}{t_{R2}} = \frac{\alpha-1}{\alpha}\cdot\frac{k_2'}{1+k_2'}$$ ### Step 5 — Combine $$\boxed{R_s = \frac{\sqrt{N}}{4}\cdot\frac{\alpha-1}{\alpha}\cdot\frac{k_2'}{1+k_2'}}$$ ### Step 6 — Useful sub-result From the resolution equation, solving for the required $N$ for a desired $R_s$: $$N_{req} = 16 R_s^2 \left(\frac{\alpha}{\alpha-1}\right)^2\left(\frac{1+k_2'}{k_2'}\right)^2$$ This is the standard **method-development equation** in KTU Part B problems. --- ## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Style) **Problem (typical KTU Part B):** A reversed-phase HPLC column ($L = 25$ cm, $V_S = 0.5$ mL, $V_M = 2.5$ mL) gives retention times $t_M = 2.0$ min, $t_{R1} = 5.0$ min, $t_{R2} = 8.0$ min, and baseline widths $W_1 = W_2 = 1.0$ min. Compute (i) $k_1', k_2'$, (ii) $\alpha$, (iii) $N$, (iv) $R_s$. ### Solution **(i) Retention factors:** $$k_1' = \frac{t_{R1}-t_M}{t_M} = \frac{5.0-2.0}{2.0} = 1.5$$ $$k_2' = \frac{t_{R2}-t_M}{t_M} = \frac{8.0-2.0}{2.0} = 3.0$$ **(ii) Selectivity:** $$\alpha = \frac{k_2'}{k_1'} = \frac{3.0}{1.5} = 2.0$$ **(iii) Plate count (use peak 2):** $$N_2 = 16\left(\frac{t_{R2}}{W_2}\right)^2 = 16\left(\frac{8.0}{1.0}\right)^2 = 16 \times 64 = 1024$$ **(iv) Resolution:** $$R_s = \frac{2(t_{R2}-t_{R1})}{W_1+W_2} = \frac{2(8.0-5.0)}{1.0+1.0} = \frac{6.0}{2.0} = 3.0$$ > [!NOTE] > Cross-check with master equation: > > $$R_s = \frac{\sqrt{1024}}{4}\cdot\frac{2-1}{2}\cdot\frac{3.0}{1+3.0} = \frac{32}{4}\cdot\frac{1}{2}\cdot\frac{3}{4} = 8 \cdot 0.5 \cdot 0.75 = 3.0 \checkmark$$ # SECTION 4 — STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ## 4.1 Schematic of a Generic Chromatographic System ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Reservoir
Mobile Phase] --> B[Pump
Constant Flow] B --> C[Injector
Sample Introduction] C --> D[Column Oven
Temperature Control] D --> E[Chromatographic Column
Stationary + Mobile Phases] E --> F[Detector
UV or RI] F --> G[Data System
Recorder or PC] G --> H[Chromatogram
tR, W, Area] classDef hw fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#0a1f44; classDef proc fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#0a3622; class A,B,C,D,F hw; class E,G,H proc; ``` > *Block-Level Functional Architecture:* mobile phase is pressurized by the **pump**, sample injected via the **injector**, separated in the **column** (where retention acts), sensed by the **detector**, and reduced to a chromatogram by the **data system**. --- ## 4.2 The Retention Process Inside the Column ```mermaid flowchart TD S0[Sample at column inlet
t = 0] --> S1[Molecules distribute
Cs = K Cm] S1 --> S2[Strongly retained
large k prime
long tR] S1 --> S3[Weakly retained
small k prime
short tR] S2 --> S4[Elutes late
peak 2] S3 --> S5[Elutes early
peak 1] S5 --> S6[tM marker
dead time] S4 --> S7[Detector records
chromatogram] classDef therm fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#3a2e00; classDef kine fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#842029,color:#3b0a0a; class S1 therm; class S2,S3 kine; ``` --- ## 4.3 Van Deemter Curve — Conceptual Map ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph VD [Van Deemter H vs u] direction LR Aterm["A term
Eddy diffusion
constant in u"] --> Sum((H = A + B/u + Cu)) Bterm["B/u term
Longitudinal diffusion
dominates at low u"] --> Sum Cterm["C u term
Mass transfer
dominates at high u"] --> Sum end Sum --> Opt["u_opt = sqrt B over C
H_min = A + 2 sqrt BC"] Sum --> Curve["Hyperbola-shaped H u curve
U-shaped minimum"] classDef lowu fill:#e2e3e5,stroke:#41464b; classDef midu fill:#d1ecf1,stroke:#0c5460; classDef highu fill:#f5c2c7,stroke:#842029; class Bterm lowu; class Aterm midu; class Cterm highu; ``` > *Interpretation*: at low mobile-phase velocity, $B/u$ dominates (long residence time allows diffusion); at high velocity, $Cu$ dominates (insufficient time for equilibration). The minimum $H$ — best efficiency — lies between the two regimes. --- ## 4.4 Resolution Engineer's Optimization Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD R[Low Resolution Rs] --> Q1{Selectivity alpha acceptable?} Q1 -- No --> Fix1[Change column chemistry
or mobile phase composition
increase alpha] Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Retention k prime in 2 to 10 range?} Q2 -- No --> Fix2[Adjust mobile-phase strength
bring k prime to optimum] Q2 -- Yes --> Fix3[Increase N
lengthen column
reduce particle size] Fix1 --> Check[Recompute Rs] Fix2 --> Check Fix3 --> Check classDef bad fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#842029; classDef good fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#0f5132; class R bad; class Check good; ``` # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP --- ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model] **Define retention factor (k′) and selectivity factor (α). A compound has $t_R = 8.0$ min and the unretained marker elutes at $t_M = 1.5$ min on a 25 cm column. Compute k′. If a second compound has $k_2' = 4.0$, calculate α.** **Model Answer:** - **Retention factor** is the ratio of the time spent in the stationary phase to the time spent in the mobile phase; it measures how strongly a compound is retained. - **Selectivity factor** is the ratio of retention factors of two compounds, indicating the column's ability to discriminate between them. For the first compound: $$k' = \frac{t_R - t_M}{t_M} = \frac{8.0 - 1.5}{1.5} = \frac{6.5}{1.5} = 4.33$$ For the second compound $k_2' = 4.0$: $$\alpha = \frac{k_2'}{k_1'} = \frac{4.0}{4.33} = 0.92 \;\; (\text{swap labels so } \alpha > 1)$$ Re-labelling $k_1' = 4.0$ and $k_2' = 4.33$: $$\alpha = \frac{4.33}{4.0} = 1.08$$ > The retention factor is dimensionless; $\alpha$ is also dimensionless and must be reported $> 1$. --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model] **State the Van Deemter equation and explain the physical significance of each term. What is the optimum velocity?** **Model Answer:** $$H = A + \frac{B}{u} + C\cdot u$$ - **A term (Eddy diffusion):** arises because solute molecules take different paths of different lengths through the packed bed; depends on particle size $d_p$ and packing uniformity. - **B/u term (Longitudinal diffusion):** caused by molecular diffusion along the column axis during the solute's residence time; dominant at low mobile-phase velocity. - **C·u term (Mass-transfer resistance):** arises from the finite rate of equilibration of solute between mobile and stationary phases; dominant at high velocity. **Optimum velocity** (where $H$ is minimum) is obtained by setting $dH/du = 0$: $$u_{opt} = \sqrt{\frac{B}{C}}, \quad H_{min} = A + 2\sqrt{BC}$$ --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### **Question A** [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model] **(a) [7 Marks]** Derive an expression for the retention factor $k'$ in terms of the distribution constant $K$, the volume of stationary phase $V_S$, and the volume of mobile phase $V_M$. Show that $t_R = t_M(1 + k')$. **(b) [7 Marks]** A 30 cm long HPLC column has $V_M = 2.0$ mL, $V_S = 0.4$ mL. A solute A has $K_A = 8.0$ and solute B has $K_B = 12.0$. If the mobile-phase linear velocity is 0.5 cm/s, compute the retention times of A and B, the selectivity factor, and the resolution assuming $N = 5000$ for both peaks. --- #### Solution to Question A **(a) Derivation of $k'$ and $t_R$:** Consider a thin slice of the column with mobile-phase volume $dV_M$ and stationary-phase volume $dV_S$. At equilibrium, by definition of $K$: $$C_S = K\cdot C_M$$ The moles of solute in the slice are: $$dn = C_M\, dV_M + C_S\, dV_S = C_M\, dV_M + K C_M\, dV_S = C_M(dV_M + K\, dV_S)$$ Moles in stationary phase: $d n_S = C_S\, dV_S = K C_M\, dV_S$. Moles in mobile phase: $d n_M = C_M\, dV_M$. Hence, the retention factor: $$k' = \frac{d n_S}{d n_M} = \frac{K C_M\, dV_S}{C_M\, dV_M} = K\cdot\frac{dV_S}{dV_M}$$ Integrating over the full column: $$\boxed{k' = K\cdot\frac{V_S}{V_M}}$$ An analyte spends only a fraction $1/(1+k')$ of its time in the mobile phase, so its average velocity is $\bar{u} = u/(1+k')$. With $t_R = L/\bar{u}$ and $t_M = L/u$: $$\boxed{t_R = t_M(1+k')}$$ **(b) Numerical solution:** Phase ratio: $$\beta = \frac{V_S}{V_M} = \frac{0.4}{2.0} = 0.20$$ Retention factors: $$k_A' = K_A \beta = 8.0 \times 0.20 = 1.60$$ $$k_B' = K_B \beta = 12.0 \times 0.20 = 2.40$$ Dead time (column length / linear velocity): $$t_M = \frac{L}{u} = \frac{30\text{ cm}}{0.5\text{ cm/s}} = 60\text{ s} = 1.0\text{ min}$$ Retention times: $$t_{R,A} = t_M(1+k_A') = 1.0 \times (1 + 1.60) = 2.60\text{ min}$$ $$t_{R,B} = t_M(1+k_B') = 1.0 \times (1 + 2.40) = 3.40\text{ min}$$ Selectivity: $$\alpha = \frac{k_B'}{k_A'} = \frac{2.40}{1.60} = 1.50$$ Resolution (master equation with $N = 5000$): $$R_s = \frac{\sqrt{N}}{4}\cdot\frac{\alpha-1}{\alpha}\cdot\frac{k_B'}{1+k_B'} = \frac{\sqrt{5000}}{4}\cdot\frac{1.50-1}{1.50}\cdot\frac{2.40}{1+2.40}$$ $$R_s = \frac{70.71}{4}\cdot\frac{0.50}{1.50}\cdot\frac{2.40}{3.40} = 17.68 \times 0.3333 \times 0.7059 \approx 4.16$$ > Such a high $R_s$ implies baseline separation; in practice, one could shorten the column. --- ### **Question B (Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model] **(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a labelled plot, describe the Van Deemter equation. Explain how optimum velocity is obtained and the role of each term. **(b) [7 Marks]** Two compounds have $k_1' = 1.5$ and $k_2' = 4.5$ on a column with $N = 4000$. Calculate (i) selectivity factor, (ii) resolution using the master equation, and (iii) the number of plates required to achieve a baseline resolution of $R_s = 1.5$. --- #### Solution to Question B **(a) Van Deemter Equation:** The Van Deemter equation expresses plate height $H$ as a function of mobile-phase linear velocity $u$: $$H = A + \frac{B}{u} + C u$$ *Plot description:* a U-shaped (hyperbolic) curve of $H$ (y-axis) vs. $u$ (x-axis). The left branch is dominated by $B/u$ (longitudinal diffusion); the right branch by $Cu$ (mass transfer). At the minimum: $$u_{opt} = \sqrt{B/C}, \qquad H_{min} = A + 2\sqrt{BC}$$ **Roles of the terms:** - $A$: Eddy diffusion — packing-dependent. - $B/u$: longitudinal diffusion — increases as residence time lengthens. - $Cu$: mass-transfer resistance — increases with velocity. Reducing $d_p$ lowers $A$ and $C$; using lower-viscosity mobile phase lowers $C$. **(b) Numerical solution:** **(i) Selectivity:** $$\alpha = \frac{k_2'}{k_1'} = \frac{4.5}{1.5} = 3.00$$ **(ii) Resolution (master equation with $N = 4000$):** $$R_s = \frac{\sqrt{4000}}{4}\cdot\frac{3.0-1}{3.0}\cdot\frac{4.5}{1+4.5}$$ $$R_s = \frac{63.25}{4}\cdot\frac{2.0}{3.0}\cdot\frac{4.5}{5.5} = 15.81 \times 0.6667 \times 0.8182 \approx 8.62$$ (Note: This is far above baseline. In practice, the column is over-specified for this separation — efficiency could be reduced to save time.) **(iii) Required plate count for $R_s = 1.5$:** Solve for $N$ from the master equation: $$N_{req} = 16 R_s^2 \left(\frac{\alpha}{\alpha-1}\right)^2 \left(\frac{1+k_2'}{k_2'}\right)^2$$ Plug in $R_s = 1.5$, $\alpha = 3.0$, $k_2' = 4.5$: $$N_{req} = 16 (1.5)^2 \left(\frac{3.0}{2.0}\right)^2 \left(\frac{5.5}{4.5}\right)^2$$ $$N_{req} = 16 \times 2.25 \times 2.25 \times 1.4938 \approx 121 \text{ plates}$$ Cross-check via direct substitution: $$\sqrt{N_{req}} = \frac{4 R_s}{(\alpha-1)/\alpha \cdot k_2'/(1+k_2')} = \frac{4 \times 1.5}{(2.0/3.0)\times(4.5/5.5)}$$ $$= \frac{6.0}{0.6667 \times 0.8182} = \frac{6.0}{0.5455} = 11.0$$ $$N_{req} = 11.0^2 = 121 \checkmark$$ --- ## ⚠ KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where students lose marks in Module 4:** > 1. **Forgetting that $\alpha > 1$** by definition — swap the labels if your $k_2'/k_1' < 1$. > 2. **Mixing $t_R$ and $t_R'$** in formulas. Always subtract $t_M$ first when computing $k'$. > 3. **Using $k_1'$ in the retention term** of the resolution equation — it is **$k_2'$** (the more-retained peak). > 4. **Reporting $N$ without units** — $N$ is dimensionless, but $H$ needs units (cm or µm). > 5. **Confusing $W$ (baseline width)** with $w_{1/2}$ (half-height width) — they use different constants (16 vs 5.54). > 6. **Forgetting the phase-ratio step** in derivation — examiners want $k' = K V_S/V_M$ explicitly shown. --- ## 📝 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - **$t_R = t_M(1 + k')$** — the single most-tested relation in Module 4. - **$k' = K \beta = K (V_S/V_M)$** — links retention to thermodynamics. - **$\alpha = k_2'/k_1'$** — always report $> 1$. - **$N = 16(t_R/W)^2 = 5.54(t_R/w_{1/2})^2$** — two plate-count formulas. - **$H = L/N$** — small $H$ → efficient column. - **Van Deemter**: $H = A + B/u + Cu$ with $u_{opt} = \sqrt{B/C}$. - **Master Resolution Equation**: $R_s = (\sqrt{N}/4)\cdot[(\alpha-1)/\alpha]\cdot[k_2'/(1+k_2')]$. - **$R_s$ scaling rules** (very common KTU sub-question): - $R_s \propto \sqrt{L}$ (double the column length → 41% more resolution) - $R_s \propto \sqrt{N}$ - $R_s$ independent of $u$ (in the ideal flat region of the Van Deemter plot) - **Optimum $k'$** for best resolution lies in the range **2 < k' < 10**; below 2 → peaks too fast; above 10 → broad, late peaks. - **A-term** is fixed by packing; **B/u** can be reduced by higher velocity; **Cu** reduced by smaller particle size and thinner stationary-phase film. - **For SEC (size-exclusion)**, retention is governed by *hydrodynamic volume*, not $K$; $k'$ values are typically $< 1$. - **Real KTU trap**: distinguish *thermodynamic* ($K$, $k'$, $\alpha$) from *kinetic* ($N$, $H$, $u$) variables — never mix them in the same equation without justification.

topics.title: Band Broadening and Separation Efficiency
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT752 - POLYMERS AND SEPARATION PROCESSES > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Band Broadening and Separation Efficiency # Module 4: Band Broadening and Separation Efficiency ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Band Broadening** is the phenomenon in chromatographic separations where an initially narrow solute band progressively spreads (widens) as it migrates through the column, producing a Gaussian-shaped detector signal instead of a sharp spike. It is quantitatively measured by the **Height Equivalent to a Theoretical Plate (HETP, denoted H)** and the **number of theoretical plates (N)**. > [!NOTE] > **Separation Efficiency** refers to the ability of a chromatographic system to resolve (separate) two adjacent solute bands into distinct peaks. It is governed by three independent parameters: **column efficiency (N)**, **selectivity (α)**, and **retention/capacity factor (k′)** — combined into the master **Resolution Equation**. ### 1.2 Real-World Intuitive Analogy Imagine a **marathon race with 1,000 runners** who start together at a single gun shot. By the time they finish: - **Fast runners** arrive early in a tight cluster (sharp peak, narrow band). - **Slow runners** arrive late in a scattered cluster (broad peak). - The runners who zig-zag through different **side streets** (multiple paths) arrive even more scattered. The **finish-line spread** is exactly analogous to **band broadening** in chromatography. The **gap between the front-runner cluster and the next-best cluster** is the **separation efficiency / resolution**. > [!NOTE] > Three things cause the spread (broadening): > 1. Runners taking **different routes** (Eddy Diffusion — **A term**) > 2. Runners naturally **drifting forward/backward** even when pacing the same (Longitudinal Diffusion — **B term**) > 3. Runners **tiring and slowing inconsistently** when going uphill vs. flat road (Mass Transfer Resistance — **C term**) ### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (Bold) - **HETP (H)** — Height Equivalent to a Theoretical Plate, **units: cm or mm** - **N** — Number of theoretical plates, **dimensionless** - **u** — Linear mobile phase velocity, **units: cm/s** - **Resolution (Rs)** — **dimensionless**, ideally ≥ 1.5 for baseline separation - **Selectivity (α)** — ratio of capacity factors, **α > 1** - **Capacity factor (k′)** — dimensionless, **0 < k′ < 10** typical - **Diffusion coefficient (Dm)** — **units: cm²/s** - **Particle diameter (dp)** — **units: μm** --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Band Broadening Band broadening in any chromatographic column arises from **three independent kinetic processes** that occur simultaneously. Each process contributes additively to the overall plate height — this is the foundation of the **Van Deemter Rate Theory**. ### Pillar A — Eddy Diffusion (Multiple Flow Paths) - The packed column is a **random bed of irregular particles**. - Solute molecules take **many different paths** around the particles. - Some paths are short and fast; others are long and slow. - This produces a **statistical spread** independent of flow rate. - **Contribution: A = 2λdp**, where λ = packing irregularity factor, dp = particle diameter. ### Pillar B — Longitudinal (Molecular) Diffusion - Solute molecules naturally **diffuse** from high-concentration zones to low-concentration zones (Fick's Law). - In the axial (flow) direction, this causes **spreading along the band**. - Dominant at **low flow velocities** (long residence times). - **Contribution: B/u = 2γDm/u**, where γ = obstruction factor (0.6–0.8), Dm = solute diffusion in mobile phase. ### Pillar C — Mass Transfer Resistance Two sub-processes: - **Cs (stationary phase):** Slow diffusion inside the stagnant liquid film on particle surfaces or within pores. - **Cm (mobile phase):** Finite time required for solute to equilibrate between stagnant eddies near particles and the bulk mobile phase. - Both dominate at **high flow velocities**. - **Contribution: C·u**, where C = Cs + Cm. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table contains **all high-yield formulas** that the KTU board examiner expects students to reproduce in ESE (End Semester Examination). Memorize symbols and units precisely. | # | Formula Name | Equation | Symbols / Units | Notes | |---|--------------|----------|-----------------|-------| | 1 | Number of theoretical plates (Gaussian) | $N = 16 \left(\dfrac{t_R}{w}\right)^2$ | tR = retention time (s); w = baseline peak width (s) | Most widely used | | 2 | Number of plates (tangential / half-width) | $N = 5.54 \left(\dfrac{t_R}{w_{1/2}}\right)^2$ | w₁/₂ = peak width at half height (s) | Used when w not measurable | | 3 | HETP (Plate Height) | $H = \dfrac{L}{N}$ | L = column length (cm); H = cm | Lower H → better column | | 4 | Reduced plate height | $h = \dfrac{H}{d_p}$ | dp = particle diameter (cm); dimensionless | h < 10 = efficient | | 5 | Reduced velocity | $\nu = \dfrac{u \, d_p}{D_m}$ | dimensionless | Used in Knox equation | | 6 | **Van Deemter Equation** | $H = A + \dfrac{B}{u} + C \, u$ | A, B, C are constants; u = cm/s | **Master rate equation** | | 7 | Eddy diffusion term | $A = 2\lambda \, d_p$ | λ = dimensionless | Independent of u | | 8 | Longitudinal diffusion term | $B = 2\gamma \, D_m$ | γ = dimensionless; Dm = cm²/s | Dominant at low u | | 9 | Mass transfer term | $C = C_s + C_m$ | Both have time units | Dominant at high u | | 10 | Capacity (retention) factor | $k' = \dfrac{t_R - t_M}{t_M}$ | tM = dead time (s) | Measures retention strength | | 11 | Selectivity factor | $\alpha = \dfrac{k'_2}{k'_1}$ | dimensionless | α > 1 required for separation | | 12 | Resolution (full Purnell eqn.) | $R_s = \dfrac{\sqrt{N}}{4} \cdot \dfrac{\alpha - 1}{\alpha} \cdot \dfrac{k'_2}{1 + k'_2}$ | dimensionless | **Master resolution eqn.** | | 13 | Resolution (basic form) | $R_s = \dfrac{t_{R2} - t_{R1}}{0.5(w_1 + w_2)}$ | times in seconds | Direct measurement | | 14 | Knox equation (reduced) | $h = A \, \nu^{1/3} + \dfrac{B}{\nu} + C \, \nu$ | A, B, C dimensionless | Modern alternative to Van Deemter | | 15 | Optimum linear velocity | $u_{opt} = \sqrt{\dfrac{B}{C}}$ | cm/s | Minimizes H | | 16 | Minimum plate height | $H_{min} = A + 2\sqrt{BC}$ | cm | Plug uopt into Van Deemter | > [!NOTE] > **CRITICAL EXAM TIP:** When writing $R_s$ on the answer sheet, **always write all three factors** (efficiency × selectivity × retention). Examiners specifically deduct marks if you only write the $R_s = (t_{R2}-t_{R1})/0.5(w_1+w_2)$ formula without explaining its relationship to $N$, $\alpha$, $k'$. ### 2.3 Why This Matters in Real Engineering - **Pharmaceutical industry**: A 0.1 unit drop in resolution can mean a contaminant is co-eluted with an API → batch rejection. - **Polymer MW characterization (SEC/GPC)**: Band broadening directly distorts the molecular weight distribution, biasing Mw and Mn. - **Petroleum refining**: GC column efficiency determines how many hydrocarbon isomers can be baseline-separated within a reasonable analysis time. - **Environmental monitoring**: Trace-level PAH or pesticide analysis demands Rs ≥ 1.5 and HETP < 0.01 cm. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Number of Theoretical Plates (N) ### Theoretical Background The **Plate Theory** (Martin and Synge, 1941) treats a chromatographic column as a series of **N discrete, perfectly mixed stages (theoretical plates)**, analogous to a distillation column. Each plate establishes instantaneous equilibrium between mobile and stationary phases. The column's overall separation power is captured by N. ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1** — Consider a solute eluting as a Gaussian peak. The probability density function (PDF) for the elution time is: $$ C(t) = \dfrac{C_{max}}{\sigma \sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\left[-\dfrac{(t - t_R)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right] $$ where $\sigma$ is the standard deviation of the Gaussian peak and $t_R$ is the retention time. **Step 2** — Recognize that for a Gaussian peak, the **baseline width** $w$ is the distance between the two tangent intersection points with the baseline. Statistically: $$ w = 4\sigma $$ **Step 3** — The **variance per unit length** of the column is defined as $\sigma^2 / L$. By analogy with distillation plates, define HETP as: $$ H = \dfrac{\sigma^2}{L} $$ **Step 4** — Rearranging the definition of HETP: $$ L = N \cdot H \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sigma^2 = N \cdot H \cdot L \quad \text{... wait, let me reformulate cleanly} $$ Let me redo this with cleaner logic: $$ H = \dfrac{L}{N} \quad \text{(definition of HETP)} $$ $$ \sigma^2 = H \cdot L \quad \text{(statistical link between variance and plate height)} $$ **Step 5** — Substitute $H = L/N$ into $\sigma^2 = H \cdot L$: $$ \sigma^2 = \dfrac{L}{N} \cdot L = \dfrac{L^2}{N} $$ $$ N = \dfrac{L^2}{\sigma^2} $$ **Step 6** — Use $w = 4\sigma \Rightarrow \sigma = w/4$: $$ N = \dfrac{L^2}{(w/4)^2} = \dfrac{16 L^2}{w^2} = 16\left(\dfrac{L}{w}\right)^2 $$ **Step 7** — Express in terms of **retention time** $t_R$ (which is proportional to L through the linear velocity $u$): $$ t_R = \dfrac{L}{u} \quad \Rightarrow \quad L = u \, t_R $$ $$ N = 16 \left(\dfrac{u \, t_R}{w}\right)^2 $$ Since $w/u$ is also a time, and because **peak width scales with retention time at constant efficiency**, the form universally quoted is: $$ N = 16 \left(\dfrac{t_R}{w}\right)^2 $$ > [!NOTE] > This derivation is **highly exam-relevant**. Practice writing each substitution explicitly. KTU examiners reward clean algebraic transitions. --- ## 3.2 Derivation of the Van Deemter Equation ### Step 1 — Stochastic (Random-Walk) Starting Point Each solute molecule experiences **random axial displacements** due to the three independent broadening mechanisms. By the principle of **additivity of variances**: $$ \sigma_{total}^2 = \sigma_{eddy}^2 + \sigma_{long}^2 + \sigma_{mass}^2 $$ **Step 2 — Eddy Diffusion (A term)** The variance grows linearly with distance L because each particle layer encountered adds an independent random displacement: $$ \sigma_{eddy}^2 = 2\lambda \, d_p \cdot L $$ Dividing by L to get the plate height contribution: $$ H_A = \dfrac{\sigma_{eddy}^2}{L} = 2\lambda \, d_p = A $$ **Step 3 — Longitudinal Diffusion (B term)** From Einstein's diffusion equation, $\sigma_{long}^2 = 2 D_m \, t_{res}$ where $t_{res} = L/u$ is the residence time: $$ \sigma_{long}^2 = \dfrac{2 D_m \, L}{u} $$ $$ H_B = \dfrac{\sigma_{long}^2}{L} = \dfrac{2 D_m}{u} = \dfrac{2\gamma D_m}{u} = \dfrac{B}{u} $$ (γ is the **obstruction factor** that accounts for the tortuous path; γ ≈ 0.6) **Step 4 — Mass Transfer (C term)** The finite rate of equilibration between mobile and stationary phases adds another distance-proportional spreading. For a single rate constant $k$: $$ \sigma_{mass}^2 \propto \dfrac{u \, L}{k} $$ $$ H_C = C \cdot u \quad \text{where } C = C_s + C_m $$ **Step 5 — Combine all three contributions:** $$ \boxed{H(u) = A + \dfrac{B}{u} + C \, u} $$ This is the celebrated **Van Deemter Equation** (1956). --- ## 3.3 Derivation of Optimum Velocity To find $u_{opt}$, differentiate H with respect to u and set to zero: $$ \dfrac{dH}{du} = -\dfrac{B}{u^2} + C = 0 $$ $$ C = \dfrac{B}{u^2} $$ $$ u_{opt} = \sqrt{\dfrac{B}{C}} $$ Substituting back into H: $$ H_{min} = A + \dfrac{B}{\sqrt{B/C}} + C \sqrt{B/C} $$ $$ H_{min} = A + \sqrt{BC} + \sqrt{BC} $$ $$ \boxed{H_{min} = A + 2\sqrt{BC}} $$ --- ## 3.4 Derivation of the Master Resolution Equation **Step 1** — Fundamental definition of resolution between peaks 1 and 2: $$ R_s = \dfrac{t_{R2} - t_{R1}}{(w_1 + w_2)/2} $$ **Step 2** — For closely eluting peaks, assume $w_1 \approx w_2 \approx w$ and $w \approx w_2$: $$ R_s = \dfrac{t_{R2} - t_{R1}}{w_2} $$ **Step 3** — Introduce $k'$ definition: $t_R = t_M(1 + k')$: $$ t_{R2} - t_{R1} = t_M(k'_2 - k'_1) $$ **Step 4** — Recall $w_2 = 4\sigma_2 = 4 t_{R2}/\sqrt{N}$: $$ R_s = \dfrac{t_M(k'_2 - k'_1)}{4 t_{R2}/\sqrt{N}} $$ **Step 5** — Use $t_{R2} = t_M(1+k'_2)$ and define $\alpha = k'_2/k'_1$: $$ R_s = \dfrac{\sqrt{N}}{4} \cdot \dfrac{k'_2 - k'_1}{1 + k'_2} $$ $$ k'_2 - k'_1 = k'_2 - \dfrac{k'_2}{\alpha} = k'_2 \left(1 - \dfrac{1}{\alpha}\right) = k'_2 \cdot \dfrac{\alpha - 1}{\alpha} $$ **Step 6 — Final Master Equation:** $$ \boxed{R_s = \underbrace{\dfrac{\sqrt{N}}{4}}_{\text{Efficiency}} \cdot \underbrace{\dfrac{\alpha - 1}{\alpha}}_{\text{Selectivity}} \cdot \underbrace{\dfrac{k'_2}{1 + k'_2}}_{\text{Retention}}} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Triple-product form**: examiners MUST see all three factors written explicitly. Drop any one factor and lose 1–2 marks. --- ## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix | Mechanism | Symbol | Dependency on u | Practical Control | Typical Magnitude (HPLC) | |-----------|--------|-----------------|-------------------|--------------------------| | Eddy diffusion | A | Independent | Smaller dp, better packing | 2λdp ≈ 5–50 μm | | Longitudinal diffusion | B/u | Decreases as 1/u | Higher u, lower Dm | Dominant at u < 0.1 cm/s | | Stationary mass transfer | Cs·u | Increases linearly | Thin stationary film | Dominant at u > 0.5 cm/s | | Mobile mass transfer | Cm·u | Increases linearly | Smaller dp, low viscosity | Dominant at u > 0.5 cm/s | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Causes and Effects of Band Broadening ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Solute Injection
(Sharp Pulse)"] --> B["Chromatographic Column
(Packed Bed)"] B --> C{"Band Broadening
Sources"} C --> D["Eddy Diffusion
(A term)"] C --> E["Longitudinal Diffusion
(B/u term)"] C --> F["Mass Transfer Resistance
(C*u term)"] D --> G["Multiple Flow Paths
Around Particles"] E --> H["Molecular Diffusion
Along Flow Axis"] F --> I["Slow Equilibration
Mobile-Stagnant Phases"] G --> J["Detected Peak
(Broadened Gaussian)"] H --> J I --> J J --> K["Quantified by:
HETP, N, Variance sigma^2"] style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc style B fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600 style C fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#cc0000 style J fill:#e1ffe1,stroke:#009900 style K fill:#f0e1ff,stroke:#6600cc ``` ## 4.2 Functional Flow — Resolution Engineering Decisions ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Target Separation
Need Rs >= 1.5"] --> B{"Choose Strategy"} B -->|"Increase N"| C["Lengthen Column
OR
Reduce dp
OR
Smaller H"] B -->|"Increase alpha"| D["Change Stationary Phase
OR
Change Mobile Phase
OR
Change Temperature"] B -->|"Optimize kprime"| E["Adjust Solvent Strength
Target kprime in 2 to 10"] C --> F["Verify Rs via Eqn"] D --> F E --> F F -->|"Rs OK"| G["Validated Method"] F -->|"Rs NO"| B style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc style G fill:#e1ffe1,stroke:#009900 style B fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#cc0000 ``` ## 4.3 Schematic — Van Deemter Plot (H vs u) ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Van Deemter Plot
H vs u"] --> B["At LOW u
B/u term dominates
Hyperbolic rise"] A --> C["At OPTIMUM u
H = Hmin = A + 2*sqrt BC
Curve minimum"] A --> D["At HIGH u
C*u term dominates
Linear rise"] C --> E["u_opt = sqrt B/C"] style A fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600 style B fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc style C fill:#e1ffe1,stroke:#009900 style D fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#cc0000 style E fill:#f0e1ff,stroke:#6600cc ``` > [!NOTE] > The **flat region** around $u_{opt}$ explains why HPLC is run at velocities 2–5× the optimum (small penalty in H for a major gain in analysis speed). --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Define HETP. How is it related to the number of theoretical plates and column length?** **Model Answer:** - **HETP (Height Equivalent to a Theoretical Plate)** is the length of column required for one theoretical equilibration stage. - Relationship: $H = L/N$, where L = column length, N = number of theoretical plates. - A column with smaller H is more efficient. --- ### Question 2 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **List the three contributions to band broadening in the Van Deemter equation and state how each varies with linear velocity.** **Model Answer:** 1. **Eddy Diffusion (A term):** Independent of u. 2. **Longitudinal Diffusion (B/u):** Decreases as 1/u. 3. **Mass Transfer Resistance (C·u):** Increases linearly with u. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice) ### QUESTION A — (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 | KTU Model Paper 2024]** **(a)** Derive the Van Deemter equation for plate height H as a function of linear velocity u. Clearly identify the physical origin of each term. **(7 marks)** **(b)** For a packed GC column, the Van Deemter constants are A = 0.05 cm, B = 0.30 cm²/s, C = 0.05 s. Calculate: (i) the optimum linear velocity, (ii) the minimum plate height, (iii) the number of plates if the column length is 25 m. **(7 marks)** --- ### Model Solution for Question A #### Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks) The Van Deemter equation arises from additivity of variances from three independent broadening mechanisms: **Step 1 — Variance additivity:** The total variance of an eluted band is the sum of variances from each mechanism: $$ \sigma_{total}^2 = \sigma_{eddy}^2 + \sigma_{long}^2 + \sigma_{mass}^2 $$ **Step 2 — Eddy diffusion term:** In a packed bed, solute molecules traverse paths of varying lengths around irregular particles. Each layer contributes independently: $$ \sigma_{eddy}^2 = 2 \lambda \, d_p \, L \quad \Rightarrow \quad H_A = \dfrac{\sigma_{eddy}^2}{L} = 2\lambda d_p = A $$ where λ = packing irregularity factor, dp = particle diameter. **Step 3 — Longitudinal diffusion term:** Using Einstein's diffusion relation, $\sigma^2 = 2 D_m t$, with residence time $t = L/u$: $$ \sigma_{long}^2 = \dfrac{2 D_m L}{u} \quad \Rightarrow \quad H_B = \dfrac{2\gamma D_m}{u} = \dfrac{B}{u} $$ where γ = obstruction factor. **Step 4 — Mass transfer term:** Finite equilibration kinetics add: $$ H_C = C \, u $$ where $C = C_s + C_m$. **Step 5 — Combine:** $$ \boxed{H(u) = A + \dfrac{B}{u} + C \, u} $$ --- #### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks) **Given:** - A = 0.05 cm - B = 0.30 cm²/s - C = 0.05 s - L = 25 m = 2500 cm **(i) Optimum linear velocity:** $$ u_{opt} = \sqrt{\dfrac{B}{C}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{0.30}{0.05}} = \sqrt{6} = 2.449 \text{ cm/s} $$ **(ii) Minimum plate height:** $$ H_{min} = A + 2\sqrt{BC} = 0.05 + 2\sqrt{0.30 \times 0.05} $$ $$ \sqrt{0.015} = 0.1225 \text{ cm} $$ $$ H_{min} = 0.05 + 2(0.1225) = 0.05 + 0.245 = 0.295 \text{ cm} $$ **(iii) Number of theoretical plates:** $$ N = \dfrac{L}{H_{min}} = \dfrac{2500}{0.295} = 8474.6 \approx 8475 \text{ plates} $$ --- ### QUESTION B — (14 Marks) [Internal Choice Alternative] **[KTU University Exam – June 2023 | KTU Supplementary Aug 2024]** **(a)** What is Resolution? Derive the fundamental resolution equation showing the role of efficiency (N), selectivity (α), and retention factor (k′). **(7 marks)** **(b)** Two solutes A and B elute with retention times $t_{R,A} = 4.0$ min and $t_{R,B} = 4.8$ min. Baseline peak widths are $w_A = 0.40$ min and $w_B = 0.48$ min. Dead time $t_M = 0.5$ min. Calculate: (i) the resolution Rs, (ii) the number of plates N for peak B, (iii) the new column length needed to achieve Rs = 1.5 by increasing N alone. **(7 marks)** --- ### Model Solution for Question B #### Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks) **Definition:** Resolution $R_s$ is a quantitative measure of the degree of separation between two adjacent chromatographic peaks. **Step 1** — Basic definition: $$ R_s = \dfrac{t_{R2} - t_{R1}}{(w_1 + w_2)/2} $$ **Step 2** — Approximation for close peaks ($w_1 \approx w_2$): $$ R_s = \dfrac{t_{R2} - t_{R1}}{w_2} $$ **Step 3** — Use $w = 4\sigma = 4 t_R/\sqrt{N}$: $$ R_s = \dfrac{t_{R2} - t_{R1}}{4 t_{R2}/\sqrt{N}} = \dfrac{\sqrt{N}(t_{R2} - t_{R1})}{4 t_{R2}} $$ **Step 4** — Substitute $t_R = t_M(1+k')$ and $\alpha = k'_2/k'_1$: After algebraic manipulation (shown in §3.4): $$ \boxed{R_s = \underbrace{\dfrac{\sqrt{N}}{4}}_{\text{Efficiency term}} \cdot \underbrace{\dfrac{\alpha - 1}{\alpha}}_{\text{Selectivity term}} \cdot \underbrace{\dfrac{k'_2}{1 + k'_2}}_{\text{Retention term}}} $$ --- #### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks) **Given:** - $t_{R,A} = 4.0$ min, $t_{R,B} = 4.8$ min - $w_A = 0.40$ min, $w_B = 0.48$ min - $t_M = 0.5$ min **(i) Resolution:** $$ R_s = \dfrac{2(t_{R,B} - t_{R,A})}{w_A + w_B} = \dfrac{2(4.8 - 4.0)}{0.40 + 0.48} $$ $$ R_s = \dfrac{1.6}{0.88} = 1.818 $$ **(ii) Number of plates for peak B:** $$ N_B = 16 \left(\dfrac{t_{R,B}}{w_B}\right)^2 = 16 \left(\dfrac{4.8}{0.48}\right)^2 = 16 \times 100 = 1600 \text{ plates} $$ **(iii) New column length for Rs = 1.5 (using N alone):** Since $R_s \propto \sqrt{N}$ (with α and k′ unchanged), and $N \propto L$: $$ \dfrac{R_{s,new}}{R_{s,old}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{L_{new}}{L_{old}}} $$ $$ \dfrac{1.5}{1.818} = \sqrt{\dfrac{L_{new}}{L_{old}}} $$ $$ \dfrac{L_{new}}{L_{old}} = \left(\dfrac{1.5}{1.818}\right)^2 = 0.681 $$ So **shorter** column suffices: $L_{new} = 0.681 \times L_{old}$. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > 1. **Formula sign errors**: $N = 16(t_R/w)^2$ uses **w** (baseline width), not $w_{1/2}$. If you use $5.54(t_R/w_{1/2})^2$, you MUST specify half-height width — otherwise full marks are deducted. > 2. **Unit consistency in $u_{opt}$**: B has units of length·velocity (cm²/s in some notations) and C has units of time (s). The ratio B/C then correctly gives cm²/s², and √ gives cm/s. Mixing B and C with mismatched units is a frequent error. > 3. **Resolution equation — three factors**: Always write all three multiplicative factors. Writing only $R_s = \sqrt{N}/4 \cdot (\alpha - 1)/\alpha$ without the k′ term = **−2 marks**. > 4. **$N \propto L$**: In the third sub-part, students often write $N_{new}/N_{old} = L_{new}/L_{old}$ correctly but then forget the square root from $R_s \propto \sqrt{N}$. This is a 1-mark penalty trap. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-Minute Revision Bullets — Memorize These** - ✅ **Van Deemter**: $H = A + B/u + C \cdot u$ — three additive contributions. - ✅ **A term** = Eddy diffusion = $2\lambda d_p$, flow-rate independent. - ✅ **B/u term** = Longitudinal molecular diffusion, dominant at LOW u. - ✅ **C·u term** = Mass transfer resistance, dominant at HIGH u. - ✅ **Optimum velocity**: $u_{opt} = \sqrt{B/C}$. - ✅ **Minimum HETP**: $H_{min} = A + 2\sqrt{BC}$. - ✅ **Number of plates**: $N = 16(t_R/w)^2 = 5.54(t_R/w_{1/2})^2 = L/H$. - ✅ **Capacity factor**: $k' = (t_R - t_M)/t_M$ — measures retention strength; ideal range 2–10. - ✅ **Selectivity**: $\alpha = k'_2/k'_1 > 1$ — fixed by chemistry (stationary + mobile phase + temperature). - ✅ **Master Resolution**: $R_s = (\sqrt{N}/4) \cdot ((\alpha-1)/\alpha) \cdot (k'_2/(1+k'_2))$ — must show all three factors. - ✅ **Baseline separation**: $R_s \geq 1.5$. - ✅ **$R_s \propto \sqrt{N} \propto \sqrt{L}$** — doubling L increases $R_s$ by factor $\sqrt{2}$, not 2. - ✅ **$R_s \propto \alpha$ only through the $(\alpha-1)/\alpha$ term** — α must change to alter separation, not N alone. - ✅ **Knox equation** (advanced): $h = A\nu^{1/3} + B/\nu + C\nu$ — dimensionless/reduced form; use when comparing columns of different dp. - ✅ **Practically**: Reduce dp, optimize packing, use thin stationary film, and run at $u \approx u_{opt}$ for best efficiency. - ✅ **In SEC/GPC of polymers**, band broadening MUST be corrected (via instrument calibration) before computing Mw and Mn — uncorrected broadening inflates polydispersity. > [!NOTE] > **FINAL EXAM STRATEGY**: In any 14-mark question on this topic, allocate ~3 marks to the **derivation**, ~3 marks to **physical interpretation of each term**, and ~1 mark for a **clean final boxed equation**. Numerical parts typically test $u_{opt}$, $H_{min}$, $N$, and $R_s$ — practice all four. ---

topics.title: Types of Chromatography
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT752 - POLYMERS AND SEPARATION PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Types of Chromatography

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Chromatography** is a versatile **physical separation technique** in which the components of a mixture are distributed between two immiscible phases: a **stationary phase** (which remains fixed) and a **mobile phase** (which moves through or over the stationary phase). The separation is achieved due to **differential migration rates** of the individual solutes arising from their differing affinities (adsorption, partition, ion-exchange, or size-exclusion interactions) for the two phases.

In the context of **PEPOT752 – Polymers and Separation Processes (KTU 2024 Scheme)**, chromatography is positioned as a **high-resolution molecular separation method** that complements classical operations like distillation, extraction, and membrane separation. It is especially critical for **high-value, thermally-sensitive biopolymers, pharmaceutical polymers, and trace analytical separations** where conventional unit operations fail.

### Key Terminology Anchor Points

| Term | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Stationary Phase (SP)** | The fixed bed/surface/immobilized liquid that exerts the selective force on the analyte. |
| **Mobile Phase (MP)** | The fluid (gas or liquid) that percolates through the stationary phase carrying the sample. |
| **Analyte** | The substance being separated/identified. |
| **Eluent** | The mobile phase entering the column. |
| **Eluate** | The mobile phase leaving the column (containing separated fractions). |
| **Retention Time ($t_R$)** | The time an analyte spends inside the chromatographic system. |
| **Dead Time ($t_M$)** | The time an unretained component (mobile phase marker) takes to traverse the system. |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Marble Race in a Sand Track"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Pump (Plain-English Analogy):** Imagine a long, horizontal transparent tube filled with **wet sand** (the stationary phase). You pour a mixture of differently sized and sticky marbles (your sample) at one end and gently flush water (the mobile phase) through the tube. The **smooth, round, non-sticky marbles** glide through quickly because they don't interact with the sand — they exit first. The **sticky, jagged, smaller marbles** get trapped in the sand pores, lag behind, and exit later. The **output order** of marbles is therefore determined by their **affinity to the stationary phase versus their solubility/mobility in the mobile phase**. **This physical sorting by "stickiness-vs-mobility" IS the essence of chromatography.**

The "stickiness" in real chromatography can be **physical adsorption, chemical affinity, charge interaction, or molecular size** — and this variation is precisely what gives rise to the **different types of chromatography** that we will systematically cover in this module.

## 1.3 Why Chromatography? (Engineering Motivation)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Chromatography delivers **resolving power at the molecular level**. A single 25 cm HPLC column can resolve **>100,000 theoretical plates per meter**, which is the equivalent of running a **2 million-plate distillation column** at analytical scale. For polymer engineers, it is the **only** reliable technique to:
> - Determine **molecular weight distribution (MWD)** of synthetic polymers (GPC/SEC).
> - Separate **oligomers, monomers, and additives** in polymer formulations.
> - Quantify **residual monomers / unreacted species** in finished polymer products.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Master Classification Architecture

Chromatography is classified along **two independent axes**:

1. **Shape/Format of the Bed** → (Planar vs Columnar)
2. **Physical State of the Mobile Phase** → (Gas vs Liquid vs Supercritical Fluid)
3. **Mechanism of Separation / Physico-chemical Principle** → (Adsorption, Partition, Ion-Exchange, Size-Exclusion, Affinity)

> [!NOTE]
> A given instrument can sit at the **intersection of these axes**. For example, **HPLC** = Columnar + Liquid Mobile Phase + (Adsorption/Partition/Ion-Exchange mechanism). GPC = Columnar + Liquid + Size-Exclusion.

## 2.2 Type-by-Type Engineering Analysis

### A. Paper Chromatography (PC)
- **Stationary Phase:** Water trapped in cellulose fibers of filter paper.
- **Mobile Phase:** An organic solvent or solvent mixture (e.g., butanol:acetic acid:water).
- **Mechanism:** **Partition** of solutes between the aqueous SP and organic MP.
- **Format:** Planar (ascending, descending, or radial).
- **Quantitative Metric:** **$R_f$ (Retention Factor / Rate of flow)**

### B. Thin Layer Chromatography (TLC)
- **Stationary Phase:** A thin layer (≈ 0.25 mm) of silica gel ($SiO_2$), alumina ($Al_2O_3$), or cellulose coated on a glass/aluminum plate.
- **Mobile Phase:** A suitable solvent (the *eluent*).
- **Mechanism:** **Adsorption** (silica/alumina) or **partition** (cellulose).
- **Detection:** UV lamp, iodine vapors, staining reagents.
- **Use Case:** Quick purity checks of reaction mixtures in polymer synthesis labs.

### C. Column Chromatography (CC)
- **Stationary Phase:** Silica/alumina packed in a glass column (gravity flow) or under pressure.
- **Mobile Phase:** A solvent gradient of increasing polarity.
- **Use Case:** **Preparative-scale** purification of monomers, oligomers, or natural products.

### D. Gas Chromatography (GC)
- **Stationary Phase:** High-boiling liquid film coated on an inert solid support (packed column) or on the inner wall of a capillary (WCOT/SCOT columns).
- **Mobile Phase:** An **inert carrier gas** — typically **Hydrogen ($H_2$), Helium ($He$), or Nitrogen ($N_2$)**.
- **Mechanism:** **Partition** between gas MP and liquid SP.
- **Constraint:** Sample must be **volatile and thermally stable** below ≈ 400 °C.
- **Detectors:** FID (Flame Ionization), TCD (Thermal Conductivity), MS.

### E. High Performance Liquid Chromatography (HPLC)
- **Stationary Phase:** Microscopic (3–5 µm) silica particles densely packed in a stainless-steel column under high pressure (50–400 bar).
- **Mobile Phase:** A liquid (isocratic or gradient mode) — water, methanol, acetonitrile, buffers.
- **Variants:** **Normal Phase (NP)** & **Reverse Phase (RP, the most common — C18 bonded silica)**.
- **Use Case:** Polymer additive analysis, pharmaceutical QC, and separating non-volatile compounds that GC cannot handle.

### F. Ion Exchange Chromatography (IEC)
- **Stationary Phase:** Charged resin beads (cation-exchange: –$SO_3^-H^+$; anion-exchange: –$N(CH_3)_3^+OH^-$).
- **Mobile Phase:** An aqueous buffer of increasing ionic strength (salt gradient) or pH.
- **Mechanism:** **Electrostatic Coulombic interaction** between charged analytes and oppositely charged resin sites.
- **Use Case:** Purification of **charged biopolymers** (proteins, nucleic acids, polyelectrolytes).

### G. Gel Permeation Chromatography (GPC) / Size Exclusion Chromatography (SEC)
- **Stationary Phase:** Porous polymer beads (e.g., crosslinked polystyrene-divinylbenzene for organic solvents; crosslinked polyacrylamide or agarose for aqueous work).
- **Mobile Phase:** THF (for PS, PMMA), DMF, water, or aqueous buffers.
- **Mechanism:** **Steric / Size Exclusion** — molecules too large to enter the pores elute first; small molecules enter the pores and elute last. Intermediate sizes elute in order of decreasing hydrodynamic size.
- **Critical Importance in PEPOT752:** This is the **workhorse** technique for determining the **Molecular Weight Distribution (MWD)** of polymers — outputs **$M_n$, $M_w$, and Polydispersity Index (PDI = $M_w/M_n$)**.

### H. Affinity Chromatography
- **Stationary Phase:** A ligand (e.g., Protein A, lectin, antibody, metal ion like $Ni^{2+}$ for His-tags) covalently immobilized on a support.
- **Mobile Phase:** A physiological buffer; elution by competitive ligand, pH shift, or denaturant.
- **Mechanism:** **Biospecific, lock-and-key recognition**.
- **Use Case:** Purifying tagged recombinant proteins, antibodies, enzymes.

### I. Supercritical Fluid Chromatography (SFC)
- **Mobile Phase:** A supercritical fluid — almost always **supercritical $CO_2$** ($T_c = 31.1 \,°C$, $P_c = 73.8 \, bar$).
- **Advantage:** Combines the **low viscosity of gas** with the **high solvating power of liquid** → fast analysis + ability to handle a wider analyte polarity range than GC.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are **examination-critical**. Memorize the form, the variable definitions, and the units.

| # | Equation | Variable Definitions & Units | Engineering Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **1** | $R_f \;=\; \dfrac{d_{solute}}{d_{solvent}} \;=\; \dfrac{\text{distance moved by spot center}}{\text{distance moved by solvent front}}$ | Dimensionless; range $0 \le R_f \le 1$ | Identification of compounds in **PC / TLC** |
| **2** | $t_R \;=\; t_M \,(1 + k')$ | $t_R$: retention time (s); $t_M$: dead time (s); $k'$: retention (capacity) factor (dimensionless) | Defines **how long a solute is held** by the stationary phase |
| **3** | $k' \;=\; \dfrac{t_R - t_M}{t_M} \;=\; \dfrac{K \cdot \rho_s}{\beta}$ | $K$: distribution coefficient; $\rho_s$: density of SP; $\beta$: phase ratio | Measures **affinity** of solute for SP |
| **4** | $N \;=\; 16 \cdot \left(\dfrac{t_R}{w_b}\right)^2$ | $N$: number of theoretical plates; $w_b$: baseline peak width | **Column efficiency metric** |
| **5** | $HETP \;=\; \dfrac{L}{N}$ | $HETP$: Height Equivalent to a Theoretical Plate (m or cm); $L$: column length (m or cm) | Smaller $HETP$ = better column |
| **6** | $H \;=\; A \;+\; \dfrac{B}{u} \;+\; C \cdot u$ (**van Deemter equation**) | $H$: HETP (m); $u$: linear velocity of MP (m/s); $A$: eddy diffusion (m); $B$: longitudinal diffusion ($m^2/s$); $C$: mass transfer resistance (s) | **Optimizes the linear velocity** of the mobile phase for maximum efficiency |
| **7** | $\alpha \;=\; \dfrac{k'_2}{k'_1} \quad (k'_2 > k'_1)$ | $\alpha$: **selectivity / separation factor** (dimensionless, always $\geq 1$) | Measures **inherent separability** of two solutes |
| **8** | $R_s \;=\; \dfrac{\sqrt{N}}{4} \cdot \dfrac{(\alpha - 1)}{\alpha} \cdot \dfrac{k'}{1 + k'}$ (**Purnell equation**) | $R_s$: **resolution** (dimensionless; baseline separation at $R_s \ge 1.5$) | **Master equation** for column design |
| **9** | $V_R \;=\; V_M \,(1 + k')$ | $V_R$: retention volume (mL); $V_M$: mobile-phase hold-up volume (mL) | Used in **preparative / scale-up** |
| **10** | $[\eta] \cdot M \;\propto \; \text{elution volume}$ in GPC | $[\eta]$: intrinsic viscosity; $M$: molecular weight | Universal calibration in **GPC** |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> - In **polymer manufacturing**, GPC is the **release-test instrument** — no batch of commercial polystyrene, polyethylene, or PVC is shipped without its GPC trace being approved.
> - In **pharma**, HPLC is the gold standard for **drug purity, content uniformity, and dissolution profiling** (> 80 % of QC methods in pharmacopeias).
> - In **environmental engineering**, GC-MS quantifies **ppb-level pesticides, dioxins, and PAHs** in water and soil.
> - In **food & polymer additive industries**, ion chromatography (a subtype of IEC) measures **trace chloride, nitrate, and sulfate** in polymer formulations.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The derivations below are **exhaustive**. Every algebraic step, every substitution, and every physical justification is laid out explicitly. Do **not** skim — KTU 14-mark questions test the **chain of reasoning**, not just the final result.

---

## 3.1 Derivation of the Retention Factor ($k'$) and Retention Time ($t_R$)

### Step 1: Define the Distribution Coefficient $K$

For a solute partitioning between the stationary phase (volume $V_s$) and the mobile phase (volume $V_m$), the **thermodynamic distribution coefficient** at equilibrium is defined as:

$$ K \;=\; \frac{C_s}{C_m} $$

where $C_s$ is the concentration of solute in the stationary phase and $C_m$ is the concentration in the mobile phase. This is a **mole-fraction ratio** at the local isotherm (linear range assumed for analytical chromatography).

### Step 2: Define the Mass Distribution

The **total mass** of solute $m_{tot}$ is split between the two phases:

$$ m_{tot} \;=\; C_m \cdot V_m \;+\; C_s \cdot V_s $$

The **mass fraction in the stationary phase** is the probability that a randomly chosen molecule sits in the SP at any instant:

$$ \frac{m_s}{m_{tot}} \;=\; \frac{C_s \cdot V_s}{C_m \cdot V_m + C_s \cdot V_s} $$

### Step 3: Introduce the Retention (Capacity) Factor $k'$

Dividing numerator and denominator by $C_m \cdot V_m$:

$$ \frac{m_s}{m_{tot}} \;=\; \frac{\dfrac{C_s \cdot V_s}{C_m \cdot V_m}}{1 \;+\; \dfrac{C_s \cdot V_s}{C_m \cdot V_m}} \;=\; \frac{K \cdot \beta^{-1}}{1 + K \cdot \beta^{-1}} \quad \text{where} \quad \beta \;=\; \frac{V_m}{V_s} $$

Define $k' \;\equiv\; K / \beta \;=\; K \cdot V_s / V_m$. Then:

$$ \frac{m_s}{m_{tot}} \;=\; \frac{k'}{1 + k'} $$

### Step 4: Link $k'$ to Residence Time

The **fraction of time a molecule spends in the SP** equals the mass-fraction ratio (ergodic principle). The total residence time in the column is $t_R$ (the time at which the peak elutes). The mobile-phase (unretained) marker traverses in $t_M$. The time spent in MP is proportional to $t_M$, and the time spent in SP is the additional $t_R - t_M$. Therefore:

$$ \frac{t_R - t_M}{t_R} \;=\; \frac{k'}{1 + k'} $$

Cross-multiplying:

$$ t_R - t_M \;=\; \frac{k' \cdot t_R}{1 + k'} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad (t_R - t_M)(1 + k') \;=\; k' \cdot t_R $$

Expanding:

$$ t_R - t_M + k' t_R - k' t_M \;=\; k' t_R $$

Cancel $k' t_R$ from both sides:

$$ t_R - t_M - k' t_M \;=\; 0 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad t_R \;=\; t_M \,(1 + k') $$

This is the **fundamental retention-time equation** (Equation 2 in the cheat sheet). Rearranging:

$$ k' \;=\; \frac{t_R - t_M}{t_M} $$

which is **Equation 3** in the cheat sheet.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Number of Theoretical Plates ($N$) from a Gaussian Peak

### Step 1: Model the Elution Peak as a Gaussian Distribution

For a sufficiently narrow injection (Dirac pulse) and efficient column, the elution profile approximates:

$$ c(t) \;=\; c_{max} \cdot \exp\!\left[-\frac{(t - t_R)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right] $$

where $\sigma$ is the standard deviation (in time units) of the eluting band.

### Step 2: Define the Baseline Width

The peak intersects the baseline at $t = t_R \pm 2\sigma$ (since the Gaussian drops to 4.4 % of its max at $\pm 2\sigma$, which is a conventional approximation for baseline definition). The **baseline width** is therefore:

$$ w_b \;=\; 4\sigma $$

### Step 3: Connect $\sigma$ to the Plate Number

The **variance** of the elution time is the fundamental measure of band broadening. From the **plate model**, a column behaves as a series of $N$ discrete equilibrium stages, each adding variance $\sigma_1^2$. For $N$ stages in series:

$$ \sigma^2 \;=\; N \cdot \sigma_1^2 $$

The mean residence time scales linearly with $N$:

$$ t_R \;=\; N \cdot \tau $$

where $\tau$ is the mean residence time per plate. Combining these:

$$ \frac{\sigma^2}{t_R^2} \;=\; \frac{N \cdot \sigma_1^2}{N^2 \cdot \tau^2} \;=\; \frac{1}{N} \cdot \frac{\sigma_1^2}{\tau^2} $$

For a single ideal plate, $\sigma_1 / \tau = 1$ (a stochastic assumption for a well-mixed stage). Therefore:

$$ \frac{\sigma^2}{t_R^2} \;=\; \frac{1}{N} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad N \;=\; \left(\frac{t_R}{\sigma}\right)^2 $$

### Step 4: Substitute $w_b = 4\sigma$

$$ N \;=\; \left(\frac{t_R}{w_b/4}\right)^2 \;=\; 16 \cdot \left(\frac{t_R}{w_b}\right)^2 $$

This is **Equation 4** in the cheat sheet. For peak-width-at-half-height ($w_{0.5}$), the formula becomes $N = 5.54 \cdot (t_R / w_{0.5})^2$. (Note: $5.54 = 8 \ln 2$, derived from Gaussian normalization.)

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the van Deemter Equation

> [!NOTE]
> The **van Deemter equation** is the *most important* equation in chromatographic theory for KTU exams. Memorize the **three physical terms** and what each represents.

### Step 1: The Master $H$ – $u$ Relationship

The HETP $H$ depends on the linear velocity $u$ of the mobile phase. Three independent broadening mechanisms contribute, and (to first order) their variances add:

$$ H(u) \;=\; H_{eddy} + H_{long} + H_{transfer} $$

### Step 2: Eddy Diffusion Term ($A$)

As solute molecules navigate through the packed bed, they take **different paths of different lengths** around the particles. This **path-length distribution** is independent of $u$:

$$ H_{eddy} \;=\; A \;=\; 2\lambda d_p $$

where $\lambda$ is a packing-geometry factor and $d_p$ is the **particle diameter**. Result: $A$ is **constant** in $u$.

### Step 3: Longitudinal Diffusion Term ($B/u$)

Molecules in the band diffuse axially (along the flow direction) into clean mobile phase ahead and behind, broadening the band. The variance grows linearly with time, and the time spent in a slice of length $dz$ is $dz/u$:

$$ \sigma_{long}^2 \;=\; 2 D_m \cdot \frac{dz}{u} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad H_{long} \;=\; \frac{2 D_m}{u} \;=\; \frac{B}{u} $$

with $B = 2 D_m$, the **longitudinal diffusion coefficient** of the solute in the mobile phase (units: m²/s).

### Step 4: Mass-Transfer Resistance Term ($C \cdot u$)

Solute molecules must **diffuse in and out of the stationary phase film** and within the stationary phase film itself. Molecules closer to the interface do this faster; those deeper in lag behind. The combined effect gives:

$$ H_{transfer} \;=\; C_s \cdot u \;+\; C_m \cdot u $$

with the total $C = C_s + C_m$. In the simplified lumped form:

$$ H_{transfer} \;=\; C \cdot u $$

### Step 5: Sum the Three Contributions

$$ \boxed{\,H \;=\; A \;+\; \frac{B}{u} \;+\; C \cdot u\,} $$

### Step 6: Find the Optimum Velocity (KTU Hot Question)

Differentiate with respect to $u$ and set to zero:

$$ \frac{dH}{du} \;=\; -\frac{B}{u^2} \;+\; C \;=\; 0 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad u_{opt} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{B}{C}} $$

Substituting back:

$$ H_{min} \;=\; A \;+\; 2\sqrt{BC} $$

This is the **minimum plate height**, which determines the **maximum column efficiency**.

---

## 3.4 Derivation of the Resolution (Purnell) Equation

### Step 1: Define Resolution

The **resolution** $R_s$ between two adjacent peaks 1 and 2 (with $t_{R,2} > t_{R,1}$) is:

$$ R_s \;=\; \frac{t_{R,2} - t_{R,1}}{0.5 \cdot (w_1 + w_2)} $$

For closely spaced peaks of similar width ($w_1 \approx w_2 = w$):

$$ R_s \;\approx\; \frac{\Delta t_R}{w} $$

### Step 2: Use Plate-Count Identity

From Section 3.2, $w = 4 t_R / \sqrt{N}$. Substituting:

$$ R_s \;\approx\; \frac{\Delta t_R}{4 t_{R,2} / \sqrt{N}} \;=\; \frac{\sqrt{N}}{4} \cdot \frac{\Delta t_R}{t_{R,2}} $$

### Step 3: Express $\Delta t_R$ in Terms of $\alpha$ and $k'$

Since $t_R = t_M (1 + k')$ and $k'_2 = \alpha \cdot k'_1$:

$$ \Delta t_R \;=\; t_M(k'_2 - k'_1) \;=\; t_M \, k'_1(\alpha - 1) $$

$$ t_{R,2} \;=\; t_M(1 + k'_2) \;=\; t_M(1 + \alpha k'_1) $$

Therefore:

$$ \frac{\Delta t_R}{t_{R,2}} \;=\; \frac{k'_1(\alpha - 1)}{1 + \alpha k'_1} $$

### Step 4: Final Purnell Form

Multiplying by $\sqrt{N}/4$:

$$ \boxed{\,R_s \;=\; \frac{\sqrt{N}}{4} \cdot \frac{\alpha - 1}{\alpha} \cdot \frac{k'_2}{1 + k'_2}\,} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This equation **decouples the three levers of separation**:
> - **Efficiency term** $\dfrac{\sqrt{N}}{4}$ → controlled by column length, particle size, and velocity.
> - **Selectivity term** $\dfrac{\alpha - 1}{\alpha}$ → controlled by chemistry of SP, MP, and temperature.
> - **Retention term** $\dfrac{k'}{1 + k'}$ → controlled by MP strength and phase ratio $\beta$.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (GPC Calibration)

> [!NOTE]
> A GPC column (length 30 cm) is calibrated with polystyrene standards. For a peak eluting at $t_R = 6.0$ min, the baseline width is $w_b = 0.6$ min. The mobile-phase marker elutes at $t_M = 2.0$ min. Calculate $N$, $HETP$, and $k'$.

**Step A — Calculate $N$:**

$$ N \;=\; 16 \cdot \left(\frac{6.0}{0.6}\right)^2 \;=\; 16 \cdot 100 \;=\; 1600 \text{ plates} $$

**Step B — Calculate $HETP$:**

$$ HETP \;=\; \frac{L}{N} \;=\; \frac{30 \,\text{cm}}{1600} \;=\; 0.01875 \,\text{cm} \;=\; 187.5 \,\mu m $$

**Step C — Calculate $k'$:**

$$ k' \;=\; \frac{6.0 - 2.0}{2.0} \;=\; 2.0 $$

A $k'$ in the **range 1 – 10** is considered optimal — the solute is well-retained but not excessively so.

```mermaid
graph TD

    S2 --> S4{MEDIUM MOLECULES M between 1k and 100k typical}
    S2 --> S5{SMALL MOLECULES M less than 1k enters all pores}

    S3 --> S6[ELUTE FIRST VOID VOLUME VO]

```

## 4.4 Comparative Mapping of Major Chromatographic Types

| Technique | Mobile Phase | Stationary Phase | Separation Driver | Key Metric |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **TLC / PC** | Liquid (organic) | Silica / Cellulose | Adsorption / Partition | $R_f$ |
| **GC** | Carrier gas ($H_2$, $He$) | High-boiling liquid film | Partition (volatility) | $t_R$ |
| **HPLC (RP)** | Water / MeOH / ACN | C18 bonded silica | Hydrophobic partition | $t_R$, $R_s$ |
| **IEC** | Aqueous buffer | Charged resin | Coulombic charge | $t_R$ |
| **GPC / SEC** | THF / DMF / Water | Porous polymer beads | Hydrodynamic size | $M_w$, $M_n$ |
| **Affinity** | Buffer | Ligand-bound resin | Biospecific binding | Elution profile |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
> **Define chromatography and explain the role of the stationary and mobile phases with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer:**
Chromatography is a physical method of separation in which the components of a mixture are distributed between two immiscible phases — a **stationary phase** (which remains fixed) and a **mobile phase** (which moves through the stationary phase). The separation is achieved because each component has a **different affinity** for the two phases, and therefore migrates at a different rate. **Example:** In paper chromatography, water trapped in the cellulose paper acts as the stationary phase, while an organic solvent (e.g., butanol–acetic acid–water mixture) is the mobile phase. Pigments from a plant extract separate into distinct spots because of their differing partition coefficients between water and the organic solvent. (3 marks)

> **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
> **Differentiate between GPC and HPLC. Mention at least three points.**

**Model Answer:**

| # | GPC (Gel Permeation Chromatography) | HPLC (High-Performance Liquid Chromatography) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Separates molecules based on **hydrodynamic size** in solution. | Separates based on **adsorption / partition / ion-exchange**. |
| 2 | The stationary phase is **inert**; no chemical interaction with the analyte. | The stationary phase **chemically interacts** with the analyte. |
| 3 | Primary use: determination of **molecular weight distribution** of polymers. | Primary use: **qualitative and quantitative analysis** of non-volatile mixtures. |
| 4 | All solutes elute between the void volume $V_0$ and total permeation volume $V_t$. | Solutes can be eluted at any $t_R$ depending on mobile-phase strength. |

(3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4, Pattern A]`

**(a)** With a neat schematic, describe the principle, instrumentation, and working of **Gas Chromatography**. List its major detectors. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Principle (2 marks):** Gas chromatography separates volatile components of a mixture based on their **partition between a mobile gas phase and a stationary liquid phase** coated on an inert solid support. Components with **higher volatility and lower affinity for the stationary liquid** elute first; those with lower volatility and higher affinity elute later.

**Instrumentation Schematic (3 marks):**
A GC system consists of:
1. **Carrier gas supply** ($H_2$, $He$, or $N_2$) with pressure regulators and flow controllers.
2. **Sample injection port** — heated to vaporize the liquid sample instantly. Injection is via a micro-syringe through a **septum**, or via an **auto-sampler** with split/splitless modes.
3. **Column oven** — temperature-controlled (isothermal or programmed from 50 °C to 400 °C). Houses the **column**.
4. **Column** — either **packed** (1–3 m long, 2–4 mm i.d., filled with coated solid support) or **capillary** (5–60 m long, 0.1–0.5 mm i.d., wall-coated open tubular — **WCOT** — or support-coated — **SCOT**).
5. **Detector** — at the column exit; the signal is sent to a recorder/data system.

**Working (1 mark):** A micro-litre of the liquid sample is injected and instantly vaporized. The carrier gas sweeps the vapor through the column. Components partition repeatedly between the gas and the stationary liquid film. They emerge separated in time and are detected.

**Detectors (1 mark):**
- **TCD** (Thermal Conductivity Detector) — universal, non-destructive.
- **FID** (Flame Ionization Detector) — highly sensitive to organic compounds.
- **ECD** (Electron Capture Detector) — selective for halogens.
- **MS** (Mass Spectrometer) — gives structural identification.

**(b)** Explain the **van Deemter equation**. Sketch a typical $H$ vs $u$ plot and identify the terms $A$, $B/u$, and $C \cdot u$. Derive the expression for **optimum velocity** and **minimum plate height**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The **van Deemter equation** expresses the **Height Equivalent to a Theoretical Plate (HETP, $H$)** as a function of the linear mobile-phase velocity $u$:

$$ H \;=\; A \;+\; \frac{B}{u} \;+\; C \cdot u $$

**Term-by-term meaning (3 marks):**
- **$A$ — Eddy Diffusion:** Independent of velocity. Caused by the multiplicity of flow paths around the stationary-phase particles; smaller for smaller, more uniform particles. $A = 2\lambda d_p$.
- **$B/u$ — Longitudinal (Molecular) Diffusion:** Dominates at **low velocities**. Molecules diffuse axially along the concentration gradient. Smaller at higher $u$. $B = 2 D_m$.
- **$C \cdot u$ — Mass Transfer Resistance:** Dominates at **high velocities**. Solute lags in transferring between mobile and stationary phases. Increases linearly with $u$.

**Sketch of $H$ vs $u$ (2 marks):**
- A **U-shaped (hyperbola-like) curve**.
- At very low $u$: $B/u$ term dominates → $H$ is large.
- At very high $u$: $C \cdot u$ term dominates → $H$ rises again.
- A **minimum** at intermediate $u$.

**Derivation of Optimum Velocity (2 marks):**
Differentiate $H$ with respect to $u$ and set to zero:

$$ \frac{dH}{du} \;=\; -\frac{B}{u^2} \;+\; C \;=\; 0 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad u_{opt} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{B}{C}} $$

Substituting back:

$$ H_{min} \;=\; A \;+\; 2\sqrt{BC} $$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students frequently **forget the constant factor "2"** in $H_{min} = A + 2\sqrt{BC}$. They write $A + \sqrt{BC}$, which is wrong. Also, the **units of $u_{opt}$ are m/s** and **$H_{min}$ in m** — write them explicitly to score full marks.

---

> ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 4, Pattern B]`

**(a)** Describe the principle and working of **Gel Permeation Chromatography (GPC)**. Explain how a **molecular weight distribution (MWD)** of a polymer is obtained from a GPC trace. Define **$M_n$, $M_w$, and PDI**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Principle (2 marks):** GPC separates dissolved polymer molecules according to their **hydrodynamic volume** in solution. The column is packed with **porous beads** of carefully controlled pore sizes. Molecules **larger than the largest pores** cannot enter any pore and elute first at the **void volume $V_0$**. Molecules **smaller than the smallest pores** enter all pores and elute last at the **total permeation volume $V_t$**. Intermediate-sized molecules elute in decreasing order of size.

**Working and Instrumentation (2 marks):** A dilute polymer solution (e.g., 1–2 mg/mL in THF) is injected. The eluent is THF (or another appropriate solvent) at a constant flow rate (typically 1 mL/min). The eluate passes through a **refractive index (RI) detector** (universal) or a **UV detector** (for chromophoric polymers). The detector signal is plotted as a function of elution volume, giving the **GPC trace (chromatogram)**.

**Conversion to Molecular Weight Distribution (2 marks):** A **calibration curve** is constructed by running **narrow-MWD polystyrene standards** and plotting $\log M$ vs. elution volume $V_e$. This curve is then used to convert every point on the polymer's GPC trace to a molecular weight. By slicing the trace into narrow molecular-weight intervals and applying the standard definitions:

$$ M_n \;=\; \frac{\sum N_i M_i}{\sum N_i} \quad\quad (\text{number-average}) $$

$$ M_w \;=\; \frac{\sum N_i M_i^2}{\sum N_i M_i} \quad\quad (\text{weight-average}) $$

$$ PDI \;=\; \frac{M_w}{M_n} \quad\quad (\text{polydispersity index, always} \;\geq 1) $$

A **monodisperse** polymer has $PDI = 1.0$; commercial polymers typically have $PDI$ in the range 1.5 – 5; some natural polymers can be much broader. (1 mark)

**(b)** Define the **Retention Factor ($k'$)**, the **Separation Factor ($\alpha$)**, and the **Resolution ($R_s$)**. State and explain the **Purnell equation** for resolution. Discuss, with reasoning, how each of the three factors (efficiency, selectivity, retention) influences $R_s$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definitions (2 marks):**
- **$k'$ (Retention Factor / Capacity Factor):** $k' = (t_R - t_M)/t_M$. Measures how strongly a solute is retained by the stationary phase relative to an unretained marker. $k' = 0$ → no retention; $k' = \infty$ → infinite retention.
- **$\alpha$ (Separation Factor / Selectivity):** $\alpha = k'_2 / k'_1$ (with $k'_2 > k'_1$, so $\alpha \geq 1$). Measures the **inherent chemical separability** of two solutes by the chosen SP–MP combination.
- **$R_s$ (Resolution):** A dimensionless measure of how well two adjacent peaks are separated. $R_s = 1.0$ ≈ 94 % valley separation (touching at baseline); $R_s = 1.5$ ≈ **baseline resolution** (the KTU benchmark).

**Purnell Equation (3 marks):**

$$ R_s \;=\; \frac{\sqrt{N}}{4} \cdot \frac{\alpha - 1}{\alpha} \cdot \frac{k'_2}{1 + k'_2} $$

where $N$ is the number of theoretical plates, $\alpha$ is the selectivity, and $k'_2$ is the retention factor of the more-retained solute.

**Discussion (2 marks):**
- **Efficiency term** $\sqrt{N}/4$: Increasing $N$ (longer column, smaller particles, optimized velocity) broadens $R_s$ weakly (square-root dependence). Doubling $N$ only improves $R_s$ by a factor of $\sqrt{2} \approx 1.41$.
- **Selectivity term** $(\alpha - 1)/\alpha$: $\alpha$ has the **strongest** leverage on $R_s$ (it appears in both numerator and denominator). A 10 % increase in $\alpha$ (e.g., 1.10 → 1.21) **nearly doubles** $R_s$. Achieved by changing SP chemistry, MP composition, or temperature.
- **Retention term** $k'/(1 + k')$: Asymptotes to 1 at large $k'$. In practice, working in the range $k' = 1$ – 10 is optimal — too small and resolution suffers; too large and peaks become very broad and analysis time becomes excessive.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Writing $\alpha$ as a ratio with the larger number on the bottom** — always put the **larger $k'$ in the numerator** so $\alpha \geq 1$.
> 2. **Confusing $N$ (plates) with $L$ (length)** — they are not the same. $L$ is in cm/m, $N$ is dimensionless.
> 3. **Forgetting that resolution is a function of the *second* peak's $k'$** in the Purnell form, not the average. (Some textbooks use the average — read the question carefully and follow the convention stated.)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> Use this section as your **last-night revision** before the KTU exam.

- [x] **Chromatography** = separation by **differential migration** between a **stationary phase** and a **mobile phase**.
- [x] Classification axes: **mobile phase state** (Gas/Liquid/SFC), **mechanism** (Adsorption/Partition/IEC/SEC/Affinity), and **geometry** (Planar/Columnar).
- [x] **Paper/TLC** → $R_f$ is the diagnostic metric. $R_f = d_{solute}/d_{solvent}$.
- [x] **GC** → carrier gas ($H_2$, $He$, $N_2$); sample must be volatile and thermally stable.
- [x] **HPLC** → high pressure (50–400 bar); **Reverse Phase (C18)** is the workhorse; gradient elution is standard.
- [x] **GPC** → the **only routine method** to measure polymer **MWD**; $PDI = M_w/M_n$.
- [x] **IEC** → uses **charged resin**; elution by **salt gradient or pH**; ideal for charged biopolymers.
- [x] **Affinity** → **biospecific** ligand; elution by competitive binder or denaturant.
- [x] **$k' = (t_R - t_M)/t_M$** → the fundamental retention metric.
- [x] **$N = 16(t_R/w_b)^2$** → plate count from baseline width.
- [x] **$H = L/N$** → smaller is better.
- [x] **van Deemter:** $H = A + B/u + C \cdot u$ → **three physical broadening terms**; $u_{opt} = \sqrt{B/C}$, $H_{min} = A + 2\sqrt{BC}$.
- [x] **$\alpha = k'_2 / k'_1 \geq 1$** → selectivity (chemistry-driven).
- [x] **Purnell:** $R_s = (\sqrt{N}/4)\cdot[(\alpha-1)/\alpha]\cdot[k'/(1+k')]$ → **baseline separation at $R_s \geq 1.5$**.
- [x] **Selectivity** has the **strongest** leverage on $R_s$; **efficiency** has a square-root dependence; **retention** asymptotes.
- [x] Common detectors: **TCD, FID, ECD, MS** (GC); **UV-Vis, RI, ELSD, MS** (HPLC).
- [x] In **GPC**, the calibration curve is **$\log M$ vs. $V_e$**, typically built with **narrow-PS standards**.

---

**END OF MODULE 4 NOTES — TYPES OF CHROMATOGRAPHY**
*Prepared for KTU B.Tech 2024 Scheme — PEPOT752 — Polymers and Separation Processes*

topics.title: Large Scale Elution (Cyclic/Batch) Chromatography
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT752 - POLYMERS AND SEPARATION PROCESSES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Large Scale Elution (Cyclic/Batch) Chromatography

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Large Scale Elution (Cyclic / Batch) Chromatography** is a preparative, discontinuous chromatographic separation mode in which a finite volume of feed mixture is injected onto a packed chromatographic column at the start of a cycle, the components are retained on the stationary phase according to their differential affinities, and are subsequently recovered one after another by passing a mobile phase (eluent) through the column. Each cycle repeats the sequence *Load → Wash → Elute → Regenerate → Re-equilibrate*, and product is collected only during the **elution window** of each cycle.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, this is classified as a **cyclic steady-state batch contactor** because:

- The column behaves as a fixed bed (no mechanical agitation).
- Operations are periodic, not continuous.
- Performance parameters (purity, yield, productivity) are evaluated on a **per-cycle basis** and averaged over many cycles at *cyclic steady state*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Cyclic steady state** is reached when the elution profile of cycle *n* is identical to that of cycle *n+1*. Until this state is attained, the collected fractions may not meet purity specifications.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **marathon race with staggered checkpoints**:

1. All runners (feed components) enter the track (column) at the same time.
2. The track has several "rest stations" (stationary phase) where tired runners stop briefly.
3. Each runner rests a *different* amount of time depending on their stamina (affinity for stationary phase).
4. When the gun fires the *elution bell*, all runners resume the race, but they now emerge from the track **spaced apart in time**.
5. A photographer (detector/collector) at the finish line captures each runner separately.

The **time interval** between two runners crossing the finish line corresponds to their **chromatographic resolution**, and the **total number of runners captured per hour** corresponds to the **column productivity**. The whole process is then repeated for the next batch of runners — hence *cyclic / batch*.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Range (Preparative) |
|---|---|---|
| Linear velocity of mobile phase | $u$ | $1 \text{ – } 500 \text{ cm/h}$ |
| Column length | $L$ | $10 \text{ – } 100 \text{ cm}$ |
| Particle diameter | $d_p$ | $15 \text{ – } 200 \text{ μm}$ |
| Cycle time | $t_{cycle}$ | $5 \text{ – } 120 \text{ min}$ |
| Plate height | $H$ | $0.01 \text{ – } 0.5 \text{ mm}$ |
| Resin / gel density | $\rho_s$ | $1.0 \text{ – } 1.3 \text{ g/cm}^3$ |

---

## 1.4 Why Cyclic/Batch Mode? (Engineering Justification)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Cyclic/batch elution is the **workhorse of high-value biopharmaceutical manufacturing** because:
> - **High purity** (> 95 %) achievable in a single pass.
> - **Gentle conditions** preserve fragile biomolecules (proteins, mAbs, plasmids).
> - **Flexible** — the same column can purify different products by changing the elution gradient.
> - **Validated scalability** — small analytical columns scale up geometrically (same $L$, scaled $d_c$).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Cyclic Batch Operating Sequence

Every cycle of large-scale elution chromatography consists of **six sequential steps**:

1. **Equilibration** — Mobile phase conditions the bed to starting pH, ionic strength, and temperature.
2. **Loading** — Feed pulse (volume $V_{load}$, concentration $C_F$) is introduced at the column inlet.
3. **Wash** — Non-binding species and residual feed buffer are flushed out; weakly bound impurities may also be removed by a wash buffer.
4. **Elution** — A change in mobile-phase composition (pH, salt gradient, organic solvent, or temperature) desorbs the target product; product-rich eluate is collected.
5. **Cleaning-in-Place (CIP)** — Strong eluent (e.g., NaOH) removes fouling, lipids, endotoxins, and precipitated matter.
6. **Re-equilibration** — Column is conditioned for the next cycle.

> [!NOTE]
> Steps 1 and 6 are typically lumped together in productivity calculations because they involve identical mobile-phase composition.

---

## 2.2 Two Governing Theories

### A. Plate Theory (Martin & Synge)

The column is treated as a series of **$N$ discrete equilibrium stages** (theoretical plates). For Gaussian elution peaks:

$$N = 16 \left(\frac{t_R}{w}\right)^2 = 5.54 \left(\frac{t_R}{w_{1/2}}\right)^2$$

where $t_R$ is the retention time and $w$ is the baseline peak width.

### B. Rate Theory (Van Deemter)

Accounts for band-broadening kinetics along the column length:

$$H = A + \frac{B}{u} + C \cdot u$$

- $A$ — Eddy diffusion (flow inhomogeneity).
- $B/u$ — Molecular diffusion (longitudinal).
- $C \cdot u$ — Mass-transfer resistance (film + intra-particle).

The column plate count is:

$$N = \frac{L}{H}$$

---

## 2.3 Three Key Performance Indicators (KPIs)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners frequently test these three KPIs. Memorize the formulas and units.

**1. Productivity** ($P$, in $\text{g product / L resin / h}$):

$$P = \frac{m_{product}}{V_{col} \cdot t_{cycle}} = \frac{V_{load} \cdot C_F \cdot Y}{V_{col} \cdot t_{cycle}}$$

**2. Yield** ($Y$, dimensionless):

$$Y = \frac{\text{mass of target recovered}}{\text{mass of target loaded}}$$

**3. Purity** (also dimensionless):

$$\text{Purity} = \frac{\text{mass of target in collected fraction}}{\text{total mass in collected fraction}}$$

---

## 2.4 Resolution Between Two Adjacent Peaks

$$R_s = \frac{t_{R,2} - t_{R,1}}{0.5 \, (w_1 + w_2)}$$

For **baseline separation**, $R_s \geq 1.5$. For preparative work, $R_s \geq 1.0$ is often acceptable when fraction cutting is used.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $N = 16 \, (t_R / w)^2$ | Plate count from Gaussian peak | dimensionless |
| 2 | $H = A + B/u + C \, u$ | Van Deemter plate-height correlation | cm |
| 3 | $N = L / H$ | Total plates in column of length $L$ | dimensionless |
| 4 | $R_s = (t_{R,2} - t_{R,1}) / \left[0.5 \, (w_1 + w_2)\right]$ | Resolution of two peaks | dimensionless |
| 5 | $P = V_{load} \cdot C_F \cdot Y / (V_{col} \cdot t_{cycle})$ | Column productivity | g / (L · h) |
| 6 | $Y = m_{out} / m_{in}$ | Recovery yield | dimensionless |
