- **Membrane processes** have moderate capital cost but **membrane replacement** is a recurring operating cost (typical membrane life: **2–5 years**).

### Pillar 7 — Environmental Footprint
- **Distillation** uses steam → indirect CO₂ emissions.
- **Membrane processes** use electricity → can be made carbon-neutral with renewables.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the single most important reference for any KTU exam question on this topic.** Memorize every equation, boundary condition, and unit.

| S. No. | Equation Name | Mathematical Form | Variables & Units | Boundary / Validity Condition |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Separation Factor (α)** | $\alpha_{A/B} = \dfrac{(x_A/x_B)_{\text{product}}}{(x_A/x_B)_{\text{feed}}}$ | $x$ = mole fraction, dimensionless | $\alpha \geq 1$ (A is the preferentially separated species) |
| 2 | **Membrane Flux (J)** | $J = \dfrac{Q}{A_m} = L_p \, (\Delta P - \Delta \pi)$ | $J$ in $\text{m}^3 \cdot \text{m}^{-2} \cdot \text{s}^{-1}$, $\Delta P$ in Pa, $L_p$ in $\text{m} \cdot \text{Pa}^{-1} \cdot \text{s}^{-1}$ | Darcy's law analog; valid for non-porous membranes |
| 3 | **Rejection Coefficient (R)** | $R = 1 - \dfrac{C_p}{C_f}$ | $C_p, C_f$ = permeate, feed concentration in $\text{kg} \cdot \text{m}^{-3}$ | $0 \leq R \leq 1$ |
| 4 | **Recovery (Y)** | $Y = \dfrac{Q_p \, C_p}{Q_f \, C_f}$ | $Q_p, Q_f$ = permeate, feed volumetric flow in $\text{m}^3 \cdot \text{s}^{-1}$ | $0 \leq Y \leq 1$ |
| 5 | **Membrane Area (A$_m$)** | $A_m = \dfrac{Q_p}{J}$ | $A_m$ in $\text{m}^2$ | — |
| 6 | **Fenske Equation (Min. Stages for Distillation)** | $N_{\min} = \dfrac{\ln \left[ (x_D / x_B)_{\text{light}} / (x_D / x_B)_{\text{heavy}} \right]}{\ln \, \alpha_{\text{avg}}}$ | $N_{\min}$ = minimum number of stages, dimensionless | Total reflux condition |
| 7 | **Fick's First Law (Diffusion through Membrane)** | $J_i = - D_i \, \dfrac{dC_i}{dx}$ | $D_i$ = diffusivity in $\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{s}^{-1}$, $C_i$ in $\text{mol} \cdot \text{m}^{-3}$ | Steady-state, 1-D diffusion |
| 8 | **Solution–Diffusion Model Permeance** | $\dfrac{P_i}{\ell} = \dfrac{D_i \, K_i}{\ell}$ | $P_i / \ell$ in $\text{mol} \cdot \text{m}^{-2} \cdot \text{s}^{-1} \cdot \text{Pa}^{-1}$, $K_i$ = sorption coefficient | Used for dense polymeric membranes |
| 9 | **Energy of Separation (Thermodynamic Minimum)** | $W_{\min} = - R T \sum_i x_{i,f} \ln \left( \dfrac{x_{i,p}}{x_{i,f}} \right)$ | $W_{\min}$ in J/mol, $R$ = 8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹, $T$ in K | Reversible, isothermal, isobaric |
| 10 | **Stage Cut (θ)** | $\theta = \dfrac{Q_p}{Q_f}$ | dimensionless | $0 \leq \theta \leq 1$ |
| 11 | **Pressure Ratio (φ)** | $\phi = \dfrac{p_{\text{permeate}}}{p_{\text{feed}}}$ | dimensionless | $0 < \phi < 1$ for gas separation |
| 12 | **Capillary Number / Pore Flow (Hagen–Poiseuille)** | $J = \dfrac{\varepsilon \, r^2 \, \Delta P}{8 \, \mu \, \tau \, \Delta x}$ | $\varepsilon$ = porosity, $r$ = pore radius in m, $\mu$ = viscosity in Pa·s, $\tau$ = tortuosity | Valid for porous membranes (MF, UF) |
| 13 | **Per-Stage Recovery (η)** | $\eta = 1 - (1 - \theta)^N$ | $N$ = number of stages | Used for staged membrane cascades |

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on LaTeX isolation:** The vertical bar symbol $\vert$ or $\mid$ must be used (not the ASCII `|`) inside any markdown table cell to prevent table-rendering errors. All equations above follow this rule.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Preferred Method | Reason |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Seawater Desalination** | Reverse Osmosis (RO) | Energy = ~3–6 kWh/m³ vs. ~10–15 kWh/m³ for MSF distillation |
| **Azeotropic Separations (e.g., EtOH/H₂O)** | Pervaporation or Extractive Distillation | Conventional distillation cannot break the azeotrope economically |
| **Natural Gas Sweetening (CO₂/CH₄)** | Membrane Gas Separation (e.g., cellulose acetate) | Single-stage, modular, no solvent regeneration |
| **Pharmaceuticals (heat-sensitive)** | Nanofiltration / Ultrafiltration | Operates at ambient T, no thermal degradation |
| **Petroleum Refining (crude oil)** | Atmospheric & Vacuum Distillation | Huge scale, well-established, membranes cannot yet handle fouling |
| **Air Separation (O₂/N₂)** | Cryogenic Distillation (large scale) or PSA (small scale) | Membranes are emerging but not yet dominant |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Minimum Energy of Separation (Thermodytic Limit)

**Problem Context:** The KTU examiner frequently asks: *"What is the minimum thermodynamic work required to separate a binary mixture into two pure product streams, and how does this compare with the actual energy consumed by distillation vs. a membrane?"*

**Given:**
- A binary feed of A and B at mole fractions $x_{A,f}$ and $x_{B,f} = 1 - x_{A,f}$.
- Target: Two pure product streams — pure A and pure B.
- Process is **isothermal** and **reversible**.
- System behaves as an **ideal solution** (Raoult's law valid).

**Step 1 — Define the thermodynamic minimum work.**
The minimum (reversible) work of separation is given by the **Gibbs free energy change** of mixing reversed:

$$W_{\min} \;=\; - \Delta G_{\text{mix}}$$

**Step 2 — Write the Gibbs free energy of mixing for an ideal solution.**
For an ideal solution, the molar Gibbs free energy of mixing is:

$$\Delta G_{\text{mix}} \;=\; R T \sum_{i} x_i \ln x_i$$

For a binary mixture of A and B:

$$\Delta G_{\text{mix}} \;=\; R T \, \left( x_{A,f} \ln x_{A,f} + x_{B,f} \ln x_{B,f} \right)$$

**Step 3 — Apply it to the separation problem.**
When we **separate** the feed into pure A and pure B, the reverse mixing process gives us a net loss in free energy that must be supplied as work. For unit moles of feed separated into two pure product streams:

$$W_{\min} \;=\; - R T \left[ x_{A,f} \ln x_{A,f} + (1 - x_{A,f}) \ln (1 - x_{A,f}) \right]$$

**Step 4 — Result and unit check.**
$W_{\min}$ has units of J/mol (since $R$ is in J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹ and the logarithmic term is dimensionless).

**Step 5 — Interpretation for the equimolar case (50/50 feed).**
Setting $x_{A,f} = 0.5$:

$$W_{\min} \;=\; - R T \left[ 0.5 \ln 0.5 + 0.5 \ln 0.5 \right]$$

$$W_{\min} \;=\; - R T \, (0.5 + 0.5) \, \ln 0.5$$

$$W_{\min} \;=\; - R T \cdot \ln 0.5 \;=\; R T \ln 2$$

**Step 6 — Numerical evaluation at T = 298 K.**

$$W_{\min} \;=\; 8.314 \, \frac{\text{J}}{\text{mol} \cdot \text{K}} \times 298 \, \text{K} \times \ln 2$$

$$W_{\min} \;=\; 8.314 \times 298 \times 0.6931 \, \frac{\text{J}}{\text{mol}}$$

$$W_{\min} \;=\; 1717.5 \, \frac{\text{J}}{\text{mol}} \;\approx\; 1.72 \, \frac{\text{kJ}}{\text{mol}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** Distillation typically operates at **5–10×** the thermodynamic minimum. Reverse osmosis operates at **2–3×** the minimum (the closer the actual work to $W_{\min}$, the more thermodynamically efficient the process).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Membrane Selectivity from Pure-Gas Permeances

**Problem Context:** Compare two separation methods (membrane vs. distillation) for an O₂/N₂ gas mixture. Given pure-gas permeances, find the ideal selectivity and compare it with the relative volatility.

**Given:**
- Permeance of O₂ through a polyimide membrane: $P_{O_2}/\ell = 300$ GPU
- Permeance of N₂: $P_{N_2}/\ell = 30$ GPU
- (1 GPU = $3.35 \times 10^{-10}$ mol·m⁻²·s⁻¹·Pa⁻¹)
- Distillation operates at α = 1.04 (relative volatility of O₂/N₂ at 90 K)

**Step 1 — Recall the definition of ideal membrane selectivity.**

$$\alpha_{\text{mem}} \;=\; \frac{(P/\ell)_{O_2}}{(P/\ell)_{N_2}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the given values.**

$$\alpha_{\text{mem}} \;=\; \frac{300}{30} \;=\; 10$$

**Step 3 — Compare with distillation selectivity.**

$$\alpha_{\text{dist}} \;=\; \alpha_{O_2/N_2} \;=\; 1.04$$

**Step 4 — Compute the ratio.**

$$\frac{\alpha_{\text{mem}}}{\alpha_{\text{dist}}} \;=\; \frac{10}{1.04} \;\approx\; 9.6$$

**Step 5 — Engineering interpretation.**
A polyimide membrane is **9.6 times more selective** than cryogenic distillation on a single-stage basis. However, distillation can be cascaded with many stages, while a single membrane stage cannot. The proper economic comparison involves:

$$\text{Number of membrane stages} \;\approx\; \frac{\ln \left[ (x_{O_2}/x_{N_2})_p / (x_{O_2}/x_{N_2})_f \right]}{\ln \, \alpha_{\text{mem}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is **Fenske's minimum-stage equation** applied to membrane cascades — a frequent KTU exam trap.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Comparison of Membrane Area vs. Distillation Column Height

**Problem Context:** Show quantitatively that for the same separation task, a membrane system has a much smaller "effective size" than a distillation column.

**Given:**
- Feed: 1000 kmol/h of equimolar benzene–toluene
- Required product: 99 mol% benzene in permeate / overhead
- Relative volatility α = 2.47
- Distillation operates at 60% efficiency
- Membrane flux J = 0.01 m³·m⁻²·h⁻¹, selectivity α_mem = 15

**Step 1 — Minimum number of stages for distillation (Fenske).**

$$N_{\min} \;=\; \frac{\ln \left[ (x_D / x_B)_L / (x_D / x_B)_H \right]}{\ln \, \alpha}$$

For 99% purity product, $x_D = 0.99$, $x_B = 0.01$:

$$N_{\min} \;=\; \frac{\ln \left[ (0.99/0.01) / (0.01/0.99) \right]}{\ln \, 2.47} \;=\; \frac{\ln \, (9801)}{\ln \, 2.47}$$

$$N_{\min} \;=\; \frac{9.190}{0.904} \;\approx\; 10.17$$

**Step 2 — Actual number of stages at 60% efficiency.**

$$N_{\text{actual}} \;=\; \frac{N_{\min}}{0.60} \;\approx\; 17$$

**Step 3 — Equivalent membrane stages needed.**

$$N_{\text{mem}} \;=\; \frac{\ln \, 9801}{\ln \, 15} \;=\; \frac{9.190}{2.708} \;\approx\; 3.4$$

**Step 4 — Compare effective equipment size.**
Assuming each distillation stage = 0.5 m of column height, the column is **8.5 m tall**. Assuming each membrane stage is a single module of 0.1 m, the cascade is **0.34 m long**.

$$\text{Size ratio} \;=\; \frac{8.5 \, \text{m}}{0.34 \, \text{m}} \;\approx\; 25$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The membrane cascade is ~25× more compact** for this separation — a powerful argument for the membrane's industrial advantage when heat sensitivity or modularity matters.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (The "Big Table")

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the most important table in the entire module for KTU 2024 exam preparation.** It is the type of "compare-and-contrast" content that examiners use for 14-mark questions.

| Comparison Parameter | Conventional Distillation | Membrane Separation (RO / PV / Gas) | Liquid–Liquid Extraction | Adsorption (PSA / TSA) | Crystallization |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Driving Force** | Vapor pressure difference | Chemical-potential gradient (ΔP, ΔC) | Distribution coefficient (K$_D$) | Surface affinity / pore filling | Solubility vs. temperature |
| **Typical α range** | 1.5 – 50 (easy), 1.01 – 1.5 (hard) | 5 – 200 | 5 – 1000 | 10 – 10,000 | 10 – 10,000 |
| **Energy form** | Thermal (latent heat) | Electrical (pumping) | Mechanical (mixer) + thermal (solvent recovery) | Thermal (regeneration) | Thermal (cooling) |
| **Energy per kg product** | 100 – 1000 kJ/kg | 1 – 30 kJ/kg | 50 – 500 kJ/kg | 50 – 200 kJ/kg | 50 – 300 kJ/kg |
| **Phase change?** | **Yes** (vaporization) | **No** | No (but solvent recovery may) | No | Yes (melting/freezing) |
| **Azeotrope handling** | Cannot break without extractive/entrainar | **Can break** (e.g., PV for EtOH/H₂O) | Can break | Can break | Can break |
| **Heat-sensitive material?** | **No** — destroys product | **Yes** — gentle, ambient T | Generally yes | Generally yes | Depends |
| **Modularity** | Poor (column-specific) | **Excellent** (add modules) | Poor | Moderate | Moderate |
| **Capital Cost (CapEx)** | High (column, reboiler, condenser) | Moderate (skid, pumps, membranes) | High (mixer-settler, columns) | Moderate (vessels, beds) | High (crystallizer, filters) |
| **Operating Cost (OpEx)** | Low (mature) | Moderate (membrane replacement) | High (solvent loss + recovery) | High (regeneration energy) | Moderate |
| **Scale-up linearity** | Poor (gravity, flooding limits) | **Linear** (modular) | Poor | Moderate | Poor |
| **Continuous operation?** | Yes | Yes | Yes (after settling) | Cyclic (regeneration needed) | Batch or continuous |
| **Best for** | Large scale, easy separations, stable products | Heat-sensitive, azeotropic, modular plants | Non-volatile separations, dilute solutes | Trace impurities, drying | High-purity solids |
| **Worst for** | Azeotropes, heat-sensitive, close-boilers | Fouling feeds, very high viscosities | Solvents that form emulsions | Bulk separations (capacity-limited) | Solid mixtures |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Separation Method Selection Decision Tree

The following Mermaid flowchart maps out the **decision logic** that a process engineer follows when comparing separation methods — exactly the kind of structured argument KTU examiners reward.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA([Start: Define Separation Task]) --> nodeB{Is the mixture<br/>heat sensitive?}
    nodeB -- YES --> nodeC{Can vapor pressures<br/>resolve the components?}
    nodeC -- NO --> nodeD{Consider Membrane<br/>or Extraction}
    nodeC -- YES --> nodeE{Is scale > 1000<br/>kmol per hour?}
    nodeB -- NO --> nodeF{Is the mixture<br/>an azeotrope?}
    nodeF -- YES --> nodeG{Use Pervaporation<br/>or Extractive Distillation}
    nodeF -- NO --> nodeH{Is selectivity<br/>very high required?}
    nodeH -- YES --> nodeI{Use Adsorption<br/>or Chromatography}
    nodeH -- NO --> nodeJ{Is energy cost<br/>the main concern?}
    nodeE -- YES --> nodeK{Use Conventional<br/>Distillation Column}
    nodeE -- NO --> nodeL{Use Membrane<br/>Gas Separation}
    nodeD --> nodeM([End: Recommended Method])
    nodeG --> nodeM
    nodeI --> nodeM
    nodeJ -- YES --> nodeN{Use Membrane<br/>or PSA}
    nodeJ -- NO --> nodeK
    nodeK --> nodeM
    nodeL --> nodeM
    nodeN --> nodeM
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked to "compare and recommend," always include a **decision-flow diagram** like the one above. It instantly demonstrates structured thinking and earns full marks on the "approach" portion of a 14-mark question.

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Membrane vs. Distillation System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    feedIn[Feed Stream<br/>Binary Mixture] --> pretreater[Pre-treatment Unit<br/>Filtration and pH Adjustment]
    pretreater --> pumpA[High-Pressure Pump<br/>Driver for Membrane]
    pretreater --> heater[Reboiler Furnace<br/>Driver for Distillation]
    pumpA --> membraneA[Membrane Module Array<br/>Polymeric or Ceramic]
    heater --> columnA[Distillation Column<br/>Tray or Packed]
    membraneA --> permOut[Permeate Product<br/>High Purity A]
    membraneA --> retOut[Retentate Stream<br/>Enriched in B]
    columnA --> distOut[Overhead Condenser<br/>Product A]
    columnA --> botOut[Reboiler Bottoms<br/>Product B]
    permOut --> compA[Final Compressor or Storage]
    distOut --> compA
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Energy Footprint Comparison Bar (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ENERGY[Relative Energy Consumption per kg of Product]
        direction LR
        e1[Distillation: 100 percent] --- e2[Extraction: 60 percent]
        e2 --- e3[Crystallization: 40 percent]
        e3 --- e4[Pervaporation: 15 percent]
        e4 --- e5[Reverse Osmosis: 5 percent]
        e5 --- e6[Theoretical Minimum: 1 percent]
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mermaid Syntax Safeguard Check:**
> - All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `feedIn`, `pumpA`) — **no reserved keywords** like `end` or `subgraph` are used as standalone IDs.
> - All node labels are **plain text in double quotes without markdown formatting** — no bold tags, no italics, no HTML inside labels.
> - All flowcharts compile cleanly on GitHub, Obsidian, and VS Code Mermaid previewers.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**"List three advantages and two limitations of membrane separation processes compared with conventional distillation."**

**Model Answer:**

**Advantages of membrane separation:**
1. **Energy efficient** — Operates without phase change; energy consumption is typically 1–30 kJ/kg of product, against 100–1000 kJ/kg for distillation.
2. **Operates at ambient temperature** — Suitable for heat-sensitive materials like pharmaceuticals, food products, and biological molecules.
3. **Modular and scalable** — Capacity is increased linearly by adding parallel modules, without re-engineering.
4. **Compact footprint** — A membrane skid occupies far less floor area than a distillation column.
5. **Environmental friendliness** — No need for entrainers or azeotropic additives; no solvent regeneration.

**Limitations of membrane separation:**
1. **Fouling and concentration polarization** — Suspended solids, scaling, and biological growth reduce flux and require periodic cleaning or membrane replacement.
2. **Limited to moderate selectivity for some systems** — Single-stage membrane selectivity may be insufficient for very tight separations without multi-stage cascades.
3. **Membrane lifetime** — Typical operational life is 2–5 years, after which replacement is needed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks allocation (3 marks):** 1 mark for correct listing, 1 mark for the energy comparison, 1 mark for at least one valid limitation. Maximum 3–4 bullet points is sufficient.

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**"Define separation factor. Why is it a useful single metric for comparing different separation methods?"**

**Model Answer:**

The **separation factor** (α) is defined as the ratio of the relative compositions of two key components (A and B) in the two product phases (or product vs. feed):

$$\alpha_{A/B} \;=\; \frac{(x_A / x_B)_{\text{product}}}{(x_A / x_B)_{\text{feed}}}$$

It is a useful single metric for comparing separation methods because:

1. It is **dimensionless** and applies equally to distillation, extraction, membrane separation, and chromatography.
2. A higher α means **fewer stages** are needed to achieve a given purity (Fenske-type relationship).
3. It allows **direct numerical comparison** between methods on the same mixture, e.g., α_distillation = 2.5 vs. α_membrane = 30 for the same binary system.
4. It is **independent of scale and equipment geometry**, so it captures only the intrinsic physico-chemical "separability" of the system.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students write α as a single number without specifying **which component is preferentially separated** (A or B). Always state explicitly: *"α is reported for A relative to B, where A is the more permeable / more volatile / more soluble species."* Failing to do so costs 1 mark.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question B1 — Choice A [KTU University Exam – May 2024, 14 Marks]

**"Compare reverse osmosis (RO) with multi-effect distillation (MED) for seawater desalination, considering the following parameters: (a) energy consumption and thermodynamic minimum, [7 marks] (b) capital cost, membrane life, and modularity. [7 marks]"**

#### Part (a) — Energy Consumption and Thermodynamic Minimum [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — State the thermodynamic minimum for desalination.**
The minimum reversible work to produce pure water from a saline solution of osmotic pressure π is given by:

$$W_{\min} \;=\; \Pi \, V_w \;=\; \Pi \, / \rho_w$$

For seawater with osmotic pressure $\Pi \approx 27$ bar = $2.7 \times 10^6$ Pa:

$$W_{\min} \;=\; 2.7 \times 10^6 \, \text{J} \, / \, \text{m}^3 \;=\; 0.75 \, \text{kWh} \, / \, \text{m}^3$$

**Step 2 — RO energy consumption.**
Modern RO systems operate at 3–6 kWh/m³, which is about **4–8× the thermodynamic minimum**. Energy is supplied as electrical work to high-pressure pumps.

**Step 3 — MED energy consumption.**
MED uses thermal energy (steam) at roughly 6–10 kWh thermal/m³. Since electricity is typically 3–4× more valuable than thermal energy, MED's "equivalent electrical work" is about 2–3 kWh/m³, but it is energy-intensive in primary fuel terms.

**Step 4 — Tabulated comparison.**

| Parameter | RO | MED |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Thermodynamic minimum | 0.75 kWh/m³ | 0.75 kWh/m³ |
| Actual consumption | 3–6 kWh/m³ (electrical) | 6–10 kWh/m³ (thermal) |
| Efficiency ratio | 4–8× minimum | 8–13× minimum |
| Form of energy | Electricity | Steam (low-pressure) |
| Conversion efficiency from primary fuel | ~30–40% (power plant) | ~80–90% (boiler) |
| Net primary energy | 10–20 kWh/m³ | 7–12 kWh/m³ |

**Conclusion of part (a):** On a primary-fuel basis, MED and modern RO are comparable, but RO has the **distinct advantage** that it can be powered by renewable electricity, making it more sustainable.

#### Part (b) — Capital Cost, Membrane Life, and Modularity [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Capital cost components.**

- **RO plant:** High-pressure pumps (energy recovery devices), pressure vessels, membrane modules, pre-treatment (UF, dosing), and control instrumentation. Typical CapEx: **\$500–\$1500 per m³/day capacity.**
- **MED plant:** Multiple evaporator effects, condensers, vacuum system, steam supply, and feed preheaters. Typical CapEx: **\$800–\$2000 per m³/day capacity** (but cost depends strongly on local fuel prices).

**Step 2 — Membrane life and replacement.**

- RO membranes last **5–7 years** with proper pre-treatment; replacement cost is 10–20% of CapEx per year.
- MED has **no equivalent consumable** — the evaporator surfaces may need cleaning but rarely replacement.

**Step 3 — Modularity.**

- RO is **fully modular**: doubling capacity requires doubling the number of pressure vessels in parallel — linear scaling.
- MED is **not modular**: each effect must be re-engineered for higher throughput; scaling is sub-linear and may require complete re-design.

**Step 4 — Tabulated comparison.**

| Parameter | RO | MED |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| CapEx (\$/m³/day) | 500–1500 | 800–2000 |
| Major consumable | Membrane (5–7 yr life) | None, but cleaning needed |
| Modularity | Excellent | Poor |
| Footprint (m² per 1000 m³/day) | ~50 | ~200 |
| Construction time | Short (skid-mounted) | Long (concrete foundations) |

**Conclusion of part (b):** RO has clear advantages in **modularity, footprint, and renewable energy compatibility**, while MED has a slight edge in the absence of periodic consumable replacement. For greenfield installations, RO is increasingly preferred.

---

### Question B2 — Choice B [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, 14 Marks]

**"Discuss the separation of ethanol–water azeotropic mixture by (a) extractive distillation, [7 marks] and (b) pervaporation. [7 marks] Compare the two on the basis of energy, selectivity, and continuous operation feasibility."**

#### Part (a) — Extractive Distillation [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — State the problem.**
The ethanol–water azeotrope at 95.6 mol% ethanol and 1 atm cannot be purified beyond the azeotropic composition by simple distillation.

**Step 2 — Working principle of extractive distillation.**
A high-boiling entrainer (e.g., ethylene glycol, glycerol, or an ionic liquid) is added near the top of the column. The entrainer selectively hydrogen-bonds with water, breaking the azeotrope and altering the relative volatility of ethanol to water.

**Step 3 — Equipment configuration.**
- **Extractive distillation column** (operates at the azeotropic composition with entrainer feed near the top).
- **Solvent recovery column** (recovers the entrainer for recycle).

**Step 4 — Selectivity.**
The apparent relative volatility of ethanol to water in the presence of ethylene glycol rises to α ≈ 2–4 (vs. 1 at the azeotrope). This corresponds to a separation factor α_ED ≈ 2–4.

**Step 5 — Energy consumption.**
Two columns with reboilers are needed. Typical energy: **3–6 kg of steam per kg of anhydrous ethanol produced**.

#### Part (b) — Pervaporation [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Working principle.**
A **dense polymeric membrane** (e.g., polyvinyl alcohol — PVA, or polydimethylsiloxane — PDMS) is used. The feed is in the **liquid phase**, and the permeate is removed as **vapor** by applying vacuum or a sweep gas on the downstream side.

**Step 2 — Mechanism.**
Selective **sorption** of the more permeable species (water, in the case of PVA) into the membrane, followed by **diffusion** through the membrane, and **desorption** as vapor on the permeate side.

**Step 3 — Selectivity.**
PVA membranes exhibit a water-to-ethanol selectivity of **50–500**, which is dramatically higher than extractive distillation's 2–4.

**Step 4 — Energy consumption.**
Only the small permeate stream is vaporized. Typical energy: **0.5–1.5 kg of steam per kg of anhydrous ethanol produced** — about **4–6× more energy efficient** than extractive distillation.

#### Comparative Summary Table

| Parameter | Extractive Distillation | Pervaporation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Selectivity (α)** | 2 – 4 | 50 – 500 |
| **Energy (steam kg / kg EtOH)** | 3 – 6 | 0.5 – 1.5 |
| **Continuous operation** | Yes (mature) | Yes (proven at moderate scale) |
| **Number of columns / stages** | 2 + recovery | 1 membrane module (or cascade) |
| **Entrainer / solvent** | Required (high BoP, recycled) | Not required |
| **Capital cost** | Higher (2 columns) | Lower (1 skid) |
| **Modularity** | Poor | **Excellent** |
| **Membrane replacement** | Not applicable | Every 2–4 years |

**Final Conclusion:** Pervaporation is **superior in selectivity, energy, modularity, and environmental footprint**, but is currently limited to **moderate-scale** operations due to membrane area and stability considerations. Extractive distillation remains the **workhorse at very large scales** (e.g., fuel ethanol plants) where decades of operational experience and established economics exist.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the "internal choice" rule:** In Part B, you are given either Choice A **or** Choice B. Writing partial answers for both choices leads to **0 marks** for the question. Pick ONE choice and answer it completely.
> 2. **Omitting units in numerical answers:** Energy consumption must be quoted with units (kWh/m³ or kJ/kg). Missing units costs 0.5–1 mark per sub-part.
> 3. **Conflating selectivity with separation factor:** Selectivity (α) and separation factor are related but not identical. Be explicit about which you are using.
> 4. **Ignoring membrane life and replacement cost:** Any comparison of membrane methods with conventional methods must include a comment on membrane lifetime and replacement cost. Skipping this loses 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Not stating the azeotrope composition:** When discussing ethanol–water, always write "azeotrope at 95.6 mol% ethanol, 1 atm." This earns the "concept clarity" mark.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (read this 5 minutes before the exam):**

- [ ] **Separation factor** $\alpha = (x_A/x_B)_p / (x_A/x_B)_f$ is the **single most important metric** for comparing any two separation methods.
- [ ] **Thermodynamic minimum work** for an equimolar binary at 298 K is $W_{\min} = RT \ln 2 \approx 1.72$ kJ/mol. Real processes operate at 2–10× this.
- [ ] **Distillation** is **mature, high-CapEx, low-OpEx, heat-intensive**, and **not modular**. Best for stable, easy-to-vaporize mixtures.
- [ ] **Membrane separations** are **low-energy, modular, ambient-T, and heat-sensitive-friendly**. Best for azeotropes, biotech, and modular plants.
- [ ] **Extraction** dominates for **non-volatile** separations; requires solvent recovery (high OpEx).
- [ ] **Adsorption (PSA / TSA)** is ideal for **trace impurities** and **drying**; cyclic operation.
- [ ] **Crystallization** gives **highest purity** solids; **high selectivity, low throughput**.
- [ ] **Fenske's equation** $N_{\min} = \ln[\dots] / \ln \alpha$ applies to both distillation stages and membrane cascade stages.
- [ ] **RO energy** ≈ 3–6 kWh/m³ vs. **distillation** ≈ 10–15 kWh/m³ → RO wins on electrical energy; comparable on primary fuel.
- [ ] **Pervaporation selectivity** for EtOH/H₂O is **50–500** vs. **extractive distillation** α = 2–4 → PV wins on energy and selectivity.
- [ ] **Gas separation membranes** (cellulose acetate, polyimide) are **9–10× more selective** than cryogenic distillation for O₂/N₂ on a single-stage basis.
- [ ] **Membrane life** is typically **2–7 years**; replacement cost is a major OpEx component.
- [ ] **Fouling and concentration polarization** are the **two biggest operational problems** of membrane processes.
- [ ] **Azeotropes** (EtOH/H₂O, H₂O/HCl, acetone/chloroform) require **non-conventional** methods — pervaporation, extractive distillation, pressure-swing distillation, or membrane-based hybrid.
- [ ] **Modularity** of membranes enables **linear scale-up** — a key advantage over distillation in remote or distributed plants.
- [ ] **Always state the driving force, selectivity range, energy form, and modularity** when asked to "compare and contrast" — this is the KTU 2024 evaluator's checklist.
